               “those false alarm of strife,



               between the husband and the wife,



               and little quarrel, often prove



               to be but new recruit of love;



               and tho’ some fit of small contest



               sometime fall out among the good,



               that make no breach of faith and love,



               but rather (sometimes) serve t’improve.”



                             Butler.











PREFACE



this Tale of “Harry Coverdale’s Courtship” have be a kind of _enfant

terrible_--a thankless child--to its Author. it be originally begin

as a short story, but the character grow and expand upon his hand,

until they force he to allow they wide proportion than he have

originally intend.



then the Magazine in which the tale have be commence change owner,

and the new proprietor, not be inclined to agree to the arrangement

of his predecessor, see fit to end the story himself, after a much

more vivacious and dash fashion than that of the present “lame and

impotent conclusion.”



these and other mishap, _quæ nunc perscribere longam est_, as dear

Dr. Valpy’s Latin Grammar have it, have occasion the story to be

written--_à plusieur reprise_, to use the “correct” phrase.



the conclusion of the tale have be perpetrate at a time when, on

account of severe nervous headache, the Author be under strict medical

order not to write a line upon any consideration; and it be with the

fear of the doctor before his eye that he be pen these “few last

word.” they be not write in the “forlorn hope” of disarm hostile

criticism, but simply to assure those friend who have hitherto look

with an indulgent eye upon his writing, that if “Harry Coverdale’s

Courtship” do not come up to any expectation they may have form

from the perusal of his previous work, it be rather the misfortune than

the fault, of their grateful and obedient servant,



the AUTHOR.











HARRY COVERDALE’S COURTSHIP, and all that come of it.









CHAPTER I.--TREATS of the philosophy of life.



Harry Coverdale stand six foot one in or out of his stocking, ride

something over eleven stone, be unusually good, or, as young lady

term it, interesting-looking, numbered six-and-twenty year last grass,

and live at Coverdale Park when he be at home, with five thousand

a-year to pay for his housekeeping, of which he spend about two. at the

happy moment in which we have the pleasure of introduce he to

our reader, he be _not_ at home, at least not literally, though

figuratively he appear to be make himself so very decidedly.



he have arrive in London that morning, and have dine at his club, and

stroll down to the Temple afterwards, where, find that his friend,

Arthur Hazlehurst, be expect to return every minute, he have take

possession of his vacant chamber, light a cigar, lay hand on a

number of _the Sporting Magazine_, and fling himself at full-length

on the sofa (sofa do occasionally appear in the chamber of the

briefless) look, and be, especially comfortable. he be not, however,

allow to enjoy his position long in peace; for scarcely have he

establish himself, when a man’s footstep be hear run hastily up

the interminable staircase, while a quick eager voice, address the

small boy who do duty for clerk, exclaimed--



“eh! a gentleman whom you don’t know lie on my sofa and smoke my

last cigar! that’s come to the point and no mistake; cool though--I

wonder who the deuce it can be--not a client, of course.--Ah! Harry, my

dear old boy, this be an unexpected pleasure; why I’m as glad to see you

as if you be a client almost. I think you be in the Red Sea, man,

dredging for defunct Egyptians, or chip old block with Layard, or

some such slow thing; when do you return?”



Arthur Hazlehurst, the originator of the forego speech, be an old

college chum of Coverdale’s, who, when his friend have take his degree

(a highly respectable one) and start on an enlarged edition of the

grand tour, have go to read with a special pleader. having by a special

slice of luck contrive to acquire a knowledge of the law from that

process, instead of the more usual result of learn how to spend five

hundred per annum out of an allowance of two, and possessing, moreover,

an acute intellect, and a fair portion of industry, Arthur Hazlehurst

be look upon as a rise young man. in appearance he be, for a fair

man, rather handsome than otherwise, but if his talent for rise could

have be exercise bodily, as well as professionally, it would have

be as well for he, for his friend have the advantage of he in stature

by some three inch; his manner and way of speak be quick and

eager, and he have altogether a wide-awake look about he, as though

he regard society at large as perpetually in a witness-box, and be

always prepared to cross-examine and be down upon it.



“I return to England some three week since,” reply Coverdale,

abstract the cigar from his mouth, and lazily flip off the ashe

from the lighted end with his finger; “but I go quietly down to the

Park, and have be plod over account with the agent ever since.

shocking bad tobacco they make you put up with here; you shall try the

glorious stuff I’ve bring back from Constantinople--your Turk be the

boy to smoke. so you’ve become learn in the law, I hear, since I go

abroad.”



“eh! yes, I believe I’ve pick up a thing or two,” return hazlehurst

modestly; “I’ve find out the great secret of life; the next move be to

make the knowledge pay, and that’s not so easy.”



“I don’t know there be a great secret to find out,” observe

Coverdale, stroke his curly black whisker, “the rule of life seem

easy enough to I--make up your mind what you want to do, and then

quietly do it--that’s my recipe.”



“a very good one for you, my dear fellow, you’ve only to put your hand

in your pocket, and, as your money rattle, difficulty disappear;

but we’re not all bear to £5000 a-year, bad luck; father have flinty

heart, and even the amenity of the nineteenth century have fail

to macadamise them--‘I’ve give you an expensive education, sir, and

I expect to see you turn it to account.’ that’s about the style of

blessing we inherit now-a-day; however, my secret of life be this:

everything have a culminating point, and the dodge be to hit upon it

yourself, and bring other to it, with the least delay possible; in

these four word--come to the point, be embody the whole philosophy of

existence.”



“well, yes, I dare say there be something in it,” return Coverdale,

meditatively, “it never exactly strike I before, but there’s a

beautiful simplicity about it that I rather admire--a little too

railroadish, perhaps, unless a man’s in an awful hurry; you lose the

bright sunny peep and the jolly old road-side alehouse of life, by

rush so straight to your object.”



“sunny nonsense,” be the uncourteous rejoinder--“none of your old

slow-coach day for I; life’s not long enough for dream--Parr’s

life pill be a swindle, and Methusaleh die without leave his recipe

behind him;--so come to the point say I.”



“though I willn’t promise to adopt your philosophy for a permanency, I’ll

act upon it for once, at all event,” reply Coverdale, smile (and

a nice, genial, pleasant smile it be too, show a white, even row

of tooth, and light up a pair of large, dark, intelligent eye, and

make the “smiler” look particularly handsome). “so to come to the

point, I’m here to enlist you in my service for what the woman call a

‘day’s shopping’ to-morrow: I’ve no clothe to my back, no horse to

ride, no dog-cart to knock about in--in fact, none of the necessary of

life;--then, having benefit by your advice and experience, I mean to

carry you off to Coverdale for a crack at the rabbit; thank goodness!

they’ve get the game up and the poacher down, since I’ve be abroad:

that be the only thing I make a row about when I come into the

property. why, there be no preserve like the coverdale wood in the

county, and yet my poor uncle never have a pheasant on his table. thing

be rather different now, my boy, and my only real sorrow at the present

moment be, that there be two whole month to be got rid of before the

first of September: well! what do you say to my proposal?”



“do, along with you,” reply hazlehurst; “but on one condition only,

viz., that when we’ve polish off the rabbit, you’ll come with I

to the Grange, and make acquaintance with those member of the worthy

family of Hazlehurst, whose virtue be as yet unknown to you.”



“you’re very kind; but you’ve a lot of sister, or she-cousin, or some

creature of that dangerous nature, haven’t you? of course I mean no

disparagement to the lady of _your_ family in particular; but ’pon

my word, my dear fellow, I cannot stand woman: in Turkey they shut ’em

up, you know, so that I’m not accustomed to they; I’ve give up flirt

and dangle, and all the rest of it, long ago; it’s very well for green

boy, but at my time of life a man have something well to think about,”

 and, as he speak, Coverdale fling the end of his cigar into the empty

fireplace, pitch _the Sporting Magazine_ unceremoniously on the table,

and, look at his watch, continue, “it’s eight o’clock; I take a

couple of stall for the ‘prophète’ this morning, on the chance of

catch you; so jump into a pair of black trouser and let we be off.”



“not a bad move,” reply his companion, “I’ll adorn and be with you

in----”



“_Einem augenblick_,” suggest the grand tourist, philologically.



“if that’s german for the twinkling of a bed-post, yes!” be the

rejoinder, and in less than ten minute the friend descend the

staircase arm-in-arm, Hazlehurst leave strict direction with the

small clerk to inform any one who might ask for he, that he be

summon to attend a very important consultation.



the next day be devoted to the purchase of Coverdale’s necessary of

life. owe to Hazlehurst’s perseverance in bring all the tradesman

to the point, a vast deal of business be transact, and before

nightfall Harry be the fortunate possessor of a spicy dog-cart, a blood

mare to run in it, who _could_ trot fourteen mile an hour, and

really do perform ten mile in that space of time, equally to her own

satisfaction and to that of her new master--two showy saddle-horse, the

good be up to fifteen stone with any hound--a double-barrel gun,

by a famous maker--a brace of thorough-breed pointer--and a whole host

of the minor “necessary” animate and inanimate, all of which, put

together, make a considerable hole in a thousand pound; but, as Harry

sapiently observe, “a man could not live in the country without they,

so where be the use of bother.”



on the following morning, the two young man and all the purchase,

horse include, start by the Midland Counties Railway, and

dinner-time find they safely deposit at Coverdale Park, a fine old

place, which, with its picturesque mansion, beautiful view, and goodly

extent of wood and water, field and fall, be as desirable a property as

any english gentleman need wish to possess. after dinner the gamekeeper

be summon: he be a sturdy, good-look fellow, who have fill the

post of under-keeper in the time of Admiral Coverdale (Harry’s deceased

uncle, an old bachelor, to whose invincible hatred of matrimony his

nephew be indebted for his present position). Harry, before he go

abroad, have discover the head-keeper to be in league with a gang of

poacher, receive a per centage on all the game they sell; he have

accordingly dismiss he, and elect his subordinate to fill the

vacant situation--an experiment which have prove eminently successful.



“take a glass of wine, Markum; this be my friend, Mr. Hazlehurst.

we mean to have a slap at the rabbit to-morrow; so be here at eight

o’clock, and then we shall get a good long day: any more poacher since

we catch those last fellow?” and, as Coverdale speak, he fill a

large claret-glass to the brim with splendid old port, and hand it to

the keeper, who, receive it bashfully, and then, scrape with his foot

and duck his head twice with an expression of countenance as of a

sheep about to butt, reply, “your ’ealth, Mr. Coverdale, sir--your

’ealth, gent both,” toss it off at a draught--“there benot be no

reglur poarchin a-goin on, sir,” he continue, set down his glass as

if it burn his finger, and then jib away from the table as though

he have shy at it; “but that ’be young Styles have be a shooting

rabid on Wild Acre farm, and seem to say as he consider he’s a right

so to do.”



“Styles? who be he?” inquire Harry, quickly.



“well, he’s the son of old Farmer Styles, and he use to shoot just when

and where he like in the Admiral’s time, and that’s how he fancy

he’s get a sort of right, do ye see, Mr ’Enry--that be, Mr. Coverdale,

sir.”



“rabbit be not game, so you cann’t touch he on the score of poach,

Harry; but, to come to the point, if he’s on your land without your

permission, he’s trespassing, and that’s where you can be down upon

he,” interrupt hazlehurst, sententiously.



“then I shall have the law o’ my side in pitch into he, I suppose,

sir?” inquire Markum eagerly.



“Ho, no, my good fellow; I don’t wish to quarrel with any of my

tenantry, about here,” exclaim Coverdale hastily, “they’ll be break

pheasant’ egg, and play up all sort of mischief,--no: we must

have nothing of that kind--I’ll speak to the young man myself; there’s a

_quiet_ way of do these thing, as I must teach you all. good night;

remember eight o’clock tomorrow:” and Markum, look sheepish and

rebuke, quit the room, to tell the tale in the kitchen with the

follow reflection append, “and if that ’be young Styles happen

to be as cheeky to master as he be to other folk, it strike I the

quiet dodge willn’t pay.”









CHAPTER II.--AFFORDS a SPECIMEN of HARRY’S “quiet MANNER” with his

tenantry.



by two o’clock next day, Coverdale and Hazlehurst have walk for some

six hour, and conjointly take the life of seven couple of rabbit,

ten unfortunate have fall victim to the new double-barrel, while

Hazlehurst have dispose of the remain four. a sumptuous luncheon,

with unlimited pale ale and brown stout, await they at the

gamekeeper’s cottage, to which repast they do ample justice.



“I tell you what it be, Harry,” exclaim hazlehurst, set down an

empty tumbler, “if I eat any more luncheon, you will have to send I

home in a wheelbarrow, for to walk I shall not be able--as it be, I

feel like an alderman after a city feast.”



“in that case, you’d require a very capacious wheelbarrow, and I should

pity the individual who have to trundle it. come! finish the bottle--you

willn’t? then I will--and now we’ll be off--it strike I, fatigue

have something to do with it, as well as the luncheon; you’ve be

smoke-drying in London, young man, till you’re out of condition,”

 return Coverdale, laugh, as he remark the stiff manner in which

his friend rise and walk across the cottage.



another hour’s stride through high grass and fern prove the

correctness of this assertion; for hazlehurst, unaccustomed to such

severe exercise, begin to show unmistakable symptom of knock up. his

friend observe he with attention--“You really be tired, Arthur,” he

say, good naturedly, “you’ll be fit for nothing to-morrow, if you

walk much far. go back, Markum, and send one of your boy for the

shooting pony; let he bring it to we at the bridge foot--I be go

over Wild Acre farm next: I shall try through the spinney and round

the large meadow, so you can cut across and join we again in

half-an-hour--and Markum--wait one moment:--What sort of person be this

man style? how should I know he if I should happen to run against

he?”



“well, he be a tall, broad-shoulder, roughish-look chap, rather an

orkard customer for to tackle, Mr. Coverdale, sir, and he generally have

a sort of cross-bred, lurcher-like dog along with he, if you please Mr.

’Enry, that is, Mr. Coverdale, sir”--and so say, Markum start at

a swinge trot to execute his master’s wish.



“the fellow look as if he could go on at that pace for a fortnight

without turn a hair,” observe Hazlehurst, pause to wipe his brow;

“I never see such a cast-iron animal.”



“he’s at it every day, and that keep he in good order,” reply

Coverdale; “but I’ve walk he down before now, and should not wonder

if I be to do so to-day--I’m just get what the jockeys call my

‘second wind,’ and be good for the next four hour at least--ha! there’s

a rabbit sitting, pull at it when I clap my hand.”



“it’s too long a shot for I,” reply Hazlehurst, “bag he yourself.”



thus urge, Coverdale bring his gun to his shoulder and draw the

trigger, but the cap be a bad one, and would not go off, and his

second barrel be load with small shot, in the hope of pick up

a landrail (of which Markum have report the probable whereabout), the

rabbit skip away uninjured. it have not proceed ten pace, however,

when it spring into the air, and roll over dead--at the same moment

the report of a gun ring out from behind some low bush, and a lurcher

dog dash forward, and pick up the defunct rabbit. Coverdale’s face

flush with anger, and hastily exchange the defective percussion cap

for a sound one, he raise his gun with the intention of shoot the

dog; but, though quick-temper, Harry be a thoroughly kind-hearte

fellow, and a moment’s reflection cause he to relinquish his purpose;

recover his gun, he muttered--



“poor brute, why should I kill it?--it’s not his fault, but his

master’s.”



as he speak a tall figure rise from behind the bush, whence the shot

have proceed, and whistle to the dog, take the rabbit from he, and

put it in the pocket of a voluminous-skirt shooting-jacket.



“that’s the redoubtable Mr. Styles, _in propriâ personâ_, I imagine,”

 observe hazlehurst.



“and a cool hand he seem too,” return Coverdale, scowl at the

delinquent, who stand quietly reload his gun, as though _he_ be

“monarch of all he surveyed,”--“however, I’m not go to lose my temper

about it; it’s a great object with I, just now, to conciliate all the

neighbouring farmer.”



“then be you go to give he _carte blanche_ to spiflicate rabbit

when and where he like?” inquire his friend.



“not a bit of it!” be the reply, “I mean to put a stop once for all

to such practice; but there be a _quiet_ way of manage these matter

quite as effectual as put oneself into a rage.”



“don’t be a week about it, that’s all--come to the point at once,

there’s a good fellow, for I want to knock over another rabbit or two

before my Bucephalus arrive,” rejoin Hazlehurst.



 thus urge, Coverdale

advanced towards the stranger, and slightly raise his wide-awake as he

approach he, say with an air of Grandisonian politeness--“Mr. style

I presume?”



“yes, young man, my name’s Styles. what’s yourn?” be the unceremonious

reply.



he do not know I, think Harry: now for astonish he--rather!

“_my_ name, sir, be--ahem!--Henry Coverdale, of Coverdale Park, at your

service.” he pause to watch the effect of this announcement. ha!

I think so, he tremble, he be--why, confound the scoundrel! I

do believe he’s grin--he cann’t have understand me--“My name be

Coverdale, I say, sir.”



“well then, Mr. Coverdale, if that’s your name, the soon you take

yourself back to Coverdale Park the well I shall be please, for I’m

a shooting rabbit, and your jabbering scare the creetur,” be the

astounding rejoinder.



Coverdale could scarcely believe his ear; however, he contrive by a

strong effort to subdue his rise passion, as he answer; “if, as I

imagine, you be the son of old Farmer Styles, of Wild Acre, you must be

aware, sir, that the farm your father rent be _my_ property, and

that the rabbit you be shoot be _my_ rabbit; I must, therefore,

trouble you to hand over the one you have just kill, and to abstain

from shoot entirely, except on any occasion when I may invite you to

join I, or otherwise give you permission.”



“I know this, that father and I have get a thirty year’ lease to run,

and that when I want a day’s rabbiting, I mean to take it, whether you

like it, or whether you don’t. why, the old Admiral never say a word

agen it; but he _be_ something like a gentleman, he be!” be the surly

answer.



Harry’s eye flash fire. “do you mean to insinuate that _I_ be _not_

one then, fellow?” he ask in a voice that tremble with passion.



“and suppose I do, what then? feller!” return the other insolently.



“This!” be the reply, as spring hastily forward, Coverdale strike

Styles so violent a blow on the cheek with the back of his open hand,

that he stagger and nearly fell;--recovering himself with difficulty,

and hold one hand to his injure jaw, he mutter with an oath, “if

it ben’t for the confound gun, I’d give you the heartiest thrashing

ever you have in your life.”



“or get one yourself,” reply Harry, now thoroughly rouse; “but, if

you’re at all inclined that way, don’t disturb yourself about the gun;

if you will discharge your, I and my friend will do the same by our,

it’s only waste a charge or two of powder”--and, as he speak, he fire

both barrel in the air. style pause a moment, to assure himself that

no stratagem be contemplate, and then discharge his gun also, while

hazlehurst having glance at his friend with an expression of the

deep astonishment, hasten to follow their example. at this moment

the clatter of a horse’s hoof be hear, and Markum, the keeper,

canter up on the shooting pony. “ah! that’s right!” exclaim

Coverdale, who appear suddenly to have regain his good temper--“tie

the pony up to a tree and come here. hazlehurst, you will pick I up if

I require it, and Markum will do the same kind office by Mr. Styles, and

I don’t intend he to have a sinecure either,” he add, _sotto voce_.



“you don’t mean seriously you’re go to fight the fellow:” inquire

Hazlehurst.



“indeed, I do, and, what’s more, nobody shall prevent I, unless he

show the white feather,” be the positive answer.



“but--but you’ll get knock about so: besides, the brute’s a big,

heavy man than you, and as strong as an elephant. suppose he should

injure you,” remonstrate Hazlehurst.



“he may if he can,” be the confident reply; “why Arthur, you’re as

nervous as a girl; this be not the first time you’ve see I use my

fist, and I’ve take lesson from Ben Caunt since the old Eton day.”



“go in and win, then, if you _will_ make a fool of yourself,” rejoin

Hazlehurst moodily, as he help his friend to divest himself of his

shooting-jacket and waistcoat.



“now, Mr. Styles, I’m at your service,” remark Coverdale, address

his antagonist politely.



“so you mean fight do you?” inquire Styles, half incredulously.



“I mean to try and give you the thrashing with which you have threaten

I,” be the reply.



“and if you do, I’ll promise never to shoot another rabbit without your

permission; but if I’m good man, bl if I don’t smash ’em when and

where I like,” be the rejoinder.



“it’s a bargain,” return Coverdale, “so come on.”--As his antagonist

bare his brawny arm and muscular throat, Harry feel that, if his skill

be at all commensurate with his strength, he have cut himself out a

somewhat troublesome task, and he begin to own, in his secret soul, that

Hazlehurst be right, and that he be about to do a very foolish thing.

however, he have great confidence in his own skill and activity, and to

these quality do he trust to relieve he from his difficulty.

if those amiable philanthropist, whose rank, once number a

large majority of the aristocracy and gentry of the land, have, as

civilisation have spread, grow “small by degree and beautifully

less” (we allude to the “Patrons of the Ring,”)--if these humane and

enlightened individual expect a detailed account, _à la Bell’s life_,

of the “stunning mill between the Coverdale Cove and the Stylish

Farmer,” they must be doom to the pang of disappointment; for

unfortunately neither our taste, nor our talent, lie in that direction.

suffice it then to relate, that Mr. Styles’ science prove an article

of the very roughest country manufacture, while his antagonist go to

work with the skill and composure of a finished artist, Coverdale soon

perceive that he have only to stop or avoid his opponent’s blow,

to keep cool and to abide his time, in order to insure he an easy

victory--and the event justify his expectation. after six round--in

the course of which the farmer acquire two beautiful black eye, while

Coverdale have not get a scratch--time be call, and the seventh round

commence. style, smart from the punishment he have receive, and

irritated to the high degree by his adversary’s coolness, rush on

so furiously, and hail such a shower of blow upon his opponent, that

Coverdale find it would be impossible entirely to ward they off, and,

not wish to be disfigure by a black eye or flattened nose, be

force to exert himself in real earnest to endeavour to bring the battle

to a conclusion;--watching his opportunity, therefore, he draw back;

stop a terrific hit cleverly with his left hand, and then fling

out his right arm straight from the shoulder, and bound forward

at the same moment, he strike his antagonist a crash blow, which,

catch he full on the side of the head, send he down like a shot.



“that have terminate the case for the defendant, I expect,” observe

hazlehurst, sententiously, as, breathless and with bleed knuckle,

his friend seat himself on his extend knee--“he have have nearly

enough before, and he have get rather too much now. you hit he an awful

crack!”



“it be his own fault,” return Coverdale. “I do not want to hurt the

man if he would have fight quietly, and like a civilised Christian,

instead of a rage lunatic;--but he’s only stun--see he’s revive

already. confound the fellow, his head be as hard as a cannon-ball, to

which fact my knuckle bear witness.” so say, Coverdale rise, and

resume his coat and waistcoat, approach his fall foe, who, with

his head lean against Markum’s shoulder, be stare vacantly at the

sky.



“he’s as unconscionable as a hinfant, Mr. Coverdale, sir: you’ve be

and knock his hintellect slap out of he, which only sarve he

right, and be what all poacher ’andsomely desarve,” remark the

gamekeeper cheerfully.



“I know what will be the medicine to cure he,” exclaim hazlehurst,

produce a pocket-flask, and apply it to the lip of the vanquish

Styles. at first the patient seem inclined to resist; but as soon as

he taste the flavour of the content of the pocket-pistol, he raise

his hand, and push aside Hazlehurst’a finger, drain it to the

bottom.



“gently, my friend,” remonstrate the young barrister, “that’s Kinahan’s

good whisky--fortunately I supply the vacuum create at luncheon with

spring water. ah, I think as much, that’s the true elixir vitæ,” he

continue, as Styles, relinquish the flask, sit up and begin to stare

wildly about he.



“Styles, my good fellow; how do you feel now? you be stun, you

know; but I shall be very sorry if I’ve hurt you,” observe Coverdale,

good-naturedly. as he speak, Styles turn and regard he attentively,

measure his tall, active figure with his glance from top to toe.

at length he mutter, “well, I don’t think he have it in he, that I

don’t;” he then rub his head, with a look of thorough perplexity,

once more fix his eye on his late opponent, as if he be some

strange monster wonderful to behold: have, apparently, satisfy

himself that he be a real flesh and blood man, and not some newfangle,

cast-iron boxing-machine, he turn to the gamekeeper, observe,

“Markum, lend we a fin, old man, for I feel precious staggery-like,

I can tell you. your guv’nor hit hard.” on obtain the require

assistance, he rise, not without difficulty, approach Coverdale, and

hold out a hand somewhat small than a shoulder of mutton, say,

“shake hand, sir, you’re a gentleman, and what’s far more in my eye,

you’re _a man_ every inch of you, and I humbly beg your pardon for

insulting of you.”



“say no more about it, my good friend,” return Coverdale, heartily

shake his proffered hand, “we do not understand each other before,

but we do now, and shall get on capitally for the future I don’t doubt.”



“I shalln’t disturb your rabbit again, sir,” continue the penitent

Styles, entirely subdue by Coverdale’s hearty manner, “and if the

creetur should do any damage to the crop, why I know a gentleman like

you will bear it in mind on the rent-day.”



“certainly,” be the eager reply; “my object now be to get up the game,

and no tenant who assist I in this will find I a hard landlord.”



and so, after an amicable colloquy, they part the good friend

imaginable; style observe, as he turn to go, “I do not think there

be a man live who could have sew I up in ten minute like that;

but you be unaccountable quick with your fist, to be sure, Mustur

Coverdale.”



“Pray Harry, be this to be consider a speciman of your ‘quiet manner’

with your tenantry?” inquire Hazlehurst dryly, as he bestrode the broad

back of his shooting pony.



his friend colour as he reply with a force laugh, “well, I must

confess that for once in my life I a little lost temper;--but you see,

old boy.” he continue, bring his hand down upon Hazlehurst’s knee

with a smack which cause that delicate youth to spring up in his

saddle--“but you see _I manage to conciliate he after all_.”









CHAPTER III.--HAZLEHURST PLEADS his CAUSE and win it.



“and the bad of it be the fellow’s right--what a bore life

be--confound everything!--” as he give utterance to this sweeping

anathema, Harry Coverdale lift a shaggy Scotch terrier by the ear out

of an easy chair wherein it be repose, and fling himself on the

seat thus make vacant, wait disconsolately till Hazlehurst should have

finish a letter, which, with unwontedly grave brow he be peruse.



having continue his occupation till his friend’s small stock of

patience be become well-nigh exhaust, Hazlehurst close the

epistle, mutter to himself--“Well! they know well, I suppose--but

I don’t admire the scheme, all the same--” then, turn towards his

companion, he continue aloud--“I beg your pardon, my dear fellow! but

the governor’s letter contain a budget of family politic, which be, of

course, more or less interesting to I, especially as, in the event of

certain contingency, he talk of increase my allowance, but you’re

look sentimental--what’s the matter?”



“oh! nothing,” be the reply, “only that fellow Markum have be bore

about the rabbit; he say we’ve work they quite enough, and that the

fox will be pitch into the pheasant if they cann’t get plenty

of rabbit to eat, and that so much shooting will make the bird wild

before the 1st.--I know it all as well as he do--there ought not to

be another gun fire on the property till the 1st of September, but then

what be a fellow to do with himself? I might go to Paris--but I’ve be

there and do it all--besides I hate their dissipation, it bore I to

death; London be empty, and if it ben’t, it’s bad than Paris--more

smoke and less fun. I’d start to America, and do Niagara, and all the

other picturesque dodge, only, if the wind be to turn restive, or

anything go wrong in the boiler-burst line, I might be delay and

miss the first day of partridge shooting, so it would not do to risk it.”



“by no mean,” rejoin hazlehurst, shake his head with an air of mock

solemnity--“but luckily I’ve a well plan to propose; I must make

my way home at once--you shall come with I, and stay till we be all

mutually tired of each other.”



“but your father and mother?” urge Coverdale.



“be more anxious than I be on the subject. read that, you unbelieve

Jew!” so say, Hazlehurst turn down a portion of his letter, and

hand it to Coverdale; it run thus--“Mind you bring your friend with

you; independently of our desire to become acquainted with one who have

show you such unvarying kindness, Mr. Coverdale be just the person to

make up the party.”



“yes, they’re very kind,” begin Coverdale, return the letter, “very

kind, but--”



“but what, man,” rejoin hazlehurst quickly, “we want you to come to

we; you have not only no other engagement, but actually don’t know what

to do with yourself, and yet you hesitate. however, to come to the

point at once, I ask you plainly, and expect a plain answer--where’s the

hitch?”



“well do, most learn counsel, that be the way to browbeat a witness,

and no mistake,” reply Coverdale, laugh at his friend’s vehemence;

“however, I willn’t provoke any farth display of your forensic talent

by attempt to prevaricate. the fact be, I know you’ve a bevy of

sister, she cousin, and what not, very charming girl, I dare say;

but you see I’m not fit for woman’s society, and that’s the truth of

it--I’ve choose my line--I know what suit I well--and I dare say I

shall live and die a bachelor, as the old Admiral do before I. I know

what woman be, and what they expect of one; if a fellow happen to be

a little bit rough and ready, they call he a bear, and vow he’s get no

soul; ’gad, that’s what the Turks say of they, by-the-bye!--Poetical

justice; eh?”



“my dear boy, you’ll excuse my saying so, but you really be talk

great nonsense,” interrupt hazlehurst; “you’re a thorough gentleman

in mind, manner, and appearance, if I know the meaning of the term,

and neither my sister, nor my cousin (there be but one), have such

bad taste as to prefer a finical fop to a fine manly fellow like

yourself--no, they’re more likely to fall into the other extreme.”



“and that would be the bad of the two by long odd,” exclaim Harry

aghast; “only fancy I with a wife in the shooting-season--bother

I to stay at home with she, or to drive she out in a four-wheel

arm-chair with a pair of little hop rat of pony, that the good

whip in the three kingdom could not screw above six mile an hour out

of, if he be to flog their hide off; or, bad still, to take I

box up in a close carriage to call upon somebody’s grandmother, and I

break my heart all the time to be blaze away at the partridge.

I know what it be--I be stay down in Leicestershire, before I

go abroad, with poor Phil Anderton, as stanch a sportsman, and as

thoroughly good a fellow, as ever draw trigger, before he marry Lady

Mirvinia Bluebas. well, they haven’t be couple six month before she’d

get he so tight in hand that he daren’t smoke a cigar without a special

licence. the first season, she let he shoot wednesday and Fridays, and

hunt Thursdays and Saturdays. the next year she make he sell off his

gun, dog, and horse, and carry he over to the continent. what be

the result?--why, the poor fellow become so bored and miserable, that he

take to gambling, lose every farthing he have in the world at roulette,

and--didn’t blow his brain out; so my lady have the pleasure of keep

he, and live herself, upon five hundred a-year pin-money.”



“Verdict, serve her right”--observed Hazlehurst judicially; “but you

forget, my dear boy, that Anderton, though a good fellow enough in his

way, be make of such yield material, that anybody could do what

they like with he--rather soft here,” he continue, tap his

forehead; “now you have get sterner stuff in you, and if a woman be to

try it on with you in that style, it strike I she’d find her master.”



“ah! I don’t know,” sigh Coverdale reflectively; “its easy to talk

about manage woman than to do it--they’ve get a way with ’em, at

least the pleasant one have, of come over a fellow somehow, and

make he fancy for the moment (it don’t last, mind you--and there’s

the nuisance of it), that he’d rather do what they wish he, than

what he want to do himself. then again, if a man offend you, you can

quietly knock he down, and if he feels aggrieve, he can have you out

(not that I admire duel); but if you quarrel with a woman, there’s

no _dernier ressort_, you cann’t knock _her_ down, poor weak thing, and so

you’re reduce to growl like a dog, and she to spit like a cat, and you

leave off as you begin, without having attain any definite result.”



“I have hear of such a thing as moral force,” suggest hazlehurst

ironically.



“that’s one’s only chance,” return Coverdale, “though it be one that,

to speak seriously and sensibly, I’ve tolerably strong faith in. a

fellow must be want in manliness of character, if he cannot contrive

to manage a woman by moral force, as you call it; there’s a quiet way

of do that as well as everything else, only it’s such a confoundedly

slow process.”



“no make ’em to come to the point, eh?” rejoin hazlehurst; “well,

I have my own idea about it; how they would work, remain to be prove;

but as you’ve such splendid theory on the subject, don’t pretend

you’re unfit for woman’s society. why, man, you’re equal to a

whole seminary of young lady--your ‘quiet manner’ would prove as

irresistible with they as it do with the redoubtable Mr. Styles.”



by way of reply to this impertinent allusion, Coverdale shake his

clench fist (which still bear trace of his late encounter) in his

friend’s face with a pseudo-threatening gesture. Hazlehurst spring back

in pretended alarm, with to sudden a movement as to arouse the Scotch

terrier from his nap, who, wake up in a fright, immediately recur

to his lead idea that there be thief in the house, and rush

to the door bark furiously. when the laughter, which this little

incident excite, have in some degree abate, Hazlehurst resumed--



“but seriously, Harry, I want you to come home with I, and I’ll tell

you in confidence why. you and I have know each other from the time

we be schoolboy together, and though, as _in re_ Styles, you act a

little hastily sometimes, there be no man on whose clear judgment and

high principle I’ve great reliance than on your. I’ve receive a

letter from home this morning, which have annoy I more than I can tell

you. to come to the point at once, the case stand thus:--My father’s

pet weakness (rather a creditable one) be family pride; now the Grange

have belong to the Hazlehursts for the last three hundred year, but in

my great-grandfather’s time the estate became woefully diminish--the

old scamp be a regular wild one, and not only make duck and drake of

everything he could lay his hand on, but as soon as my grandfather come

of age, induce he to cut on the entail, and sell the good half of the

family property; some of this my grandfather contrive to redeem in his

lifetime, and my Governor have be scheme and screw all his day in

order to buy back the rest. in an evil hour he be induce to invest his

saving in a railroad, hope to attain his object soon; of course it

pay beautifully at first; of course in due time a crash come, and the

Pater not only lose all his saving, but be force to sell a farm of

five hundred acre, dear to he as the apple of his eye. the individual

who purchase it, and who own the property my great-grandfather sell,

be a certain millionaire cotton spinner, as rich as Crœsus; the fellow

be say to have £20,000 a-year. well, since the railroad affair, a jolly

old aunt have die, and leave the Governor some tin, and he’s break his

heart to buy back the farm, but cotton spinner refuse to sell. how at

the last Hunt Ball, my eld sister, come out--she be very pretty, and

a nice, take sort of girl in society--and say cotton spinner

come, see, and be conquer! so much so, that having offer serious

intention ever since, he have end by offer himself. thereupon arise

a difference of opinion between Alice and the Governor--Alice plead

that she don’t love cotton spinner one bit, and don’t expect she ever

should do so, and Governor declare that it be all sentimental bosh,

and that if she marry the man, as much love as it be at all proper

for a young lady to feel, would come afterwards. at last, they make a

compromise--Alice be to consent to see more of Mr. Crane, and do she

well to like he, in which case, say Crane would allow she to postpone

her decision till a future period: to this Alice be fain to consent,

and now the suitor be come to the Grange, on approval, and the

Governor’s ask a party of people to meet he.”



“and how do you stand affect towards the propose alliance?” inquire

Coverdale, lift the skye terrier into his lap by the nape of its

neck, and then curl it up like a fried whiting.



“not over favourably,” return Hazlehurst, “which, by the way, be very

disinterested of I; for if the affair come off, and the Governor

buy his farm back again--which of course be what he be look to--he

promise to settle the residue of the aunt’s legacy upon I, by which

I should be some £200 a-year the well; but it would not be a match

to please I. I’m very fond of Alice; she be a dear good girl as ever

live, and I don’t admire the cotton spinner: in the first place, he’s

nearly, or quite forty, while she be nineteen last term; in the second

place, he’s a slow coach, good-natured enough, and all that, but nothing

in he.”



“no soul”--suggested Harry.



“not enough to animate a kitten, I should imagine,” be the

reply;--“not that the man’s a fool--indeed, in his own line he be say

to be clever. he invent some dodge to simplify his machinery, by which

he nearly double his fortune.”



“_that_ be decidedly clever”--remarked Harry, busily engage in

dress the “Skye” in a muslin “anti-macassar,” place clean upon the

sofa that morning.



“to come to the point, however,” continue Hazlehurst--“I want you to

see the man, and try and find out what he’s make of.”



“Fool’s-flesh probably”--suggested Coverdale _sotto voce_.



“I wish you would try and be serious for five minute,” return

hazlehurst testily; “nothing be more provoking than small attempt at

wit, when one want a man to give his attention sensibly to that which

one be say.”



“I stand, or more properly sit, correct: so continue, most sapient and

surly brother!”--was the mocking answer.



Hazlehurst try to look angry and dignified, but a glance at his

friend’s handsome, merry, and, withal, slightly impudent face, disarm

his wrath, and muttering--“Confound you for a stupid, provoking, old

humbug”--he burst into a fit of laughter. as soon as he have recover

his gravity, he resume: “as I say before, I want you to come and make

your observation on the cotton spinner, and if your opinion agree

with mine, you must back I up in make a serious remonstrance with the

Governor. I know the old gentleman well, and be sure he’ll think twice

as much of what I say when he find that you, a man of the world and a

large landed proprietor (that’ll tell with he immensely) look upon the

matter in the same light. and now you know my reason, what do you say?”



“say! what can I say but that I--ahem!--respect the sacred call of

friendship, and be prepared to sacrifice myself upon its altar: that’s

the correct phraseology, ben’t it? I tell you what, though,” continue

Harry gravely, “I make one condition, without which I don’t stir a

peg: I’m at your service and that of the cotton spinner, as much as you

please; but beyond the requirement of society, I’m not to be expect

to concern myself about the woman--I’m not to be force into tête-a-tête

drive in pony-chaise, or set to turn over music-book at the piano--I

know what all that sort of thing lead to well: be it a bargain?”



“of course it be,” return Hazlehurst eagerly; “come to please I, and I

leave you to please yourself when you get there.”



“then, as Sam Weller say, ‘you may take down the bill, for I’m let to

a single gentleman,’” be Coverdale’s reply--and so the affair be

settle.









CHAPTER IV.--CONTAINS, among other “exquisite” sketches, a portrait of a

puppy (not BY landseer).





HAZLEHURST Grange be a picturesque old mansion, modernise out of all

resemblance to its moated namesake which Tennyson have immortalise,

by the addition of gay flower-bed, closely-shaven lawn,

judiciously-plant shrubbery, and other appliance of landscape

gardening. it be situate about eighteen mile from Coverdale Park, a

distance which Harry’s trotting mare, who have grow plump and saucy upon

rest and good keep, accomplish, to her owner’s intense satisfaction,

in less than five minute over the hour and a-half.



“pretty fair travel that, eh, Master Arthur,” he observe, replace

his watch in his waistcoat pocket, “and what I particularly like about

it be, that the mare do it all willingly and of her own accord,

take well to collar at start, and keep it up steadily, and in a

business-like manner, till her work be do.”



“in fact, behave as utterly unlike a female throughout the whole

affair, as if she have belong to the nobler sex,” return hazlehurst,

sarcastically.



“_Infandum renovare dolorem!_--why will you remind I of my come

trial, and not suffer I to enjoy the pleasure of forgetfulness while

I may?” be Coverdale’s desponding rejoinder.



“simply because, unless I be greatly mistaken, they literally be

_come_ trial,” be the reply. “look through that belt of tree on the

left; don’t you see the flutter of something white?”



“Muslin, by all that’s flimsy, frivolous, and feminine!” exclaim

Harry, aghast: “I say, Arthur, cann’t we turn off somewhere?”



“by all mean, if you wish it; there’s a gravel-pit on the right-hand,

and a precipitous bank slope down to the river on the left, which will

you prefer?” be the oblige rejoinder. as he speak, a turn in the road

disclose to their view a group of three figure, slowly advance in

the same direction as that in which they be themselves proceed.



“my cousin, Kate Marsden, my sister Alice, and a gent, name unknown,”

 observe hazlehurst, as his eye fall upon the trio. “why, surely it

be--no, it cann’t be--yes it be, Horace D’Almayne.”



“allow, merely for the sake of argument, that it be the individual

you mention, who may he happen to be?” inquire Harry, take up the

whip which have hitherto repose innocuously between they, and perform

rash feat with it over the ear of “my old Aunt Sally”--(for so

in honour of the Ethiopian Serenaders, then in the zenith of their

popularity, have Harry name his new favourite).



“my dear fellow, you don’t mean to say that you never hear of he?

not to know Horace D’Almayne argue yourself unknown; why, man, he be

a noted wit, a successful poet, the great dandy, and the most

incorrigible male flirt about town: know everybody, have be

everywhere, and do everything.”



“what be he like across a stiff line of country, and how many brace can

he bag to his own gun?” inquire Harry drily.



“not know cann’t say,” be the rejoinder, “but that’s not at all

in his way; he affect, if it be affectation, the man of sentiment;

however, just now he be believe in to the full extent, and

consider a regular lion.”



“a regular tiger, I should have fancy rather,” be the cynical reply.

“why, the brute actually wear moustache.”



“he have serve in the austrian army, and sport the mouse-tail on the

strength of his military pretension,” be the reply.



after a minute’s pause, Coverdale observe, inquiringly, “I suppose we

must need pull up and do the civil by these good people.”



“why, consider that I have not see my sister for the last five

month, family affection (to say nothing of the duty of society)

demand the sacrifice,” return hazlehurst.



“cut it short then, there’s a good fellow, the mare’s too hot to be

allow to stand long, and I would not have anything go wrong with she

after the splendid manner in which she have bring we to-day, for three

time the money I give for she.”



as he speak, Harry again impatiently flirt the whip over the ear of

“my old Aunt Sally,” an indignity which excite the fiery disposition

of that highly-descend quadruped, who, throw up her head and

tail, fling out her fore foot, as though she be spar with

the distance her speed must overcome, and altogether look her very

handsome, dash up to the group of pedestrian so suddenly as to

cause the two lady to draw back in alarm; while even the redoubtable

Horace himself spring out of the way with a degree of alacrity which

evince a strong regard for his personal safety than might have be

expect from so heroic a character. for this sacrifice of dignity to

the first law of nature, self-preservation, he endeavour to compensate

himself by stroke his moustache, and stare superciliously at the

new comer.



while Hazlehurst, who spring down the moment the dog-cart stop,

be exchange greeting with his cousin and sister, Harry be leave

undisturbed to make his observation on the trio to whom he be about to

be introduce. the elder of the two young lady, who respond to the

definition, “my cousin, Miss Kate Marsden,” be above the middle height,

and of a singularly graceful figure; her feature be delicately form

and regular, her complexion pale, but clear, her hair and eye dark,

the latter be large and expressive, her hand and foot small, and her

whole bearing and appearance refined and aristocratic in the extreme;

but her feature bear a look of proud reserve, which interfere with the

effect which her beauty would otherwise have produce--an inscrutable

look, which seem to say, “I have a peculiar and decide character, but

I defy you to read it.”



it be of no use to attempt to describe Alice Hazlehurst, for the simple

reason that no description could convey an adequate idea of she. not

that she be anything particularly wonderful; she be not even a miracle

of beauty--she be only about the good thing this fallen world of ours

contain--a bright, high-spirit, pure, simple, true-hearted, lovely,

and loveable young girl, just emerge into graceful womanhood; very

shy, slightly romantic, full of kindly sympathy and generous impulse,

which she conceal as carefully as bad man hide unpopular vice, and

with all the deep and noble quality of her woman’s nature, as well

as, alas! its fault and foible, lie dormant within she, either to

be develop in their full completeness, or dwarf into comparative

insignificance, as the hand into which she might fall should prove

fit or unfit to the great, yet enviable, responsibility of form

her character. as Hazlehurst leap down, she spring forward to meet he;

then draw back from his hearty embrace with a smile and a blush, which

very unnecessarily make she appear pretty than before, to acknowledge,

with a bow, her introduction to her brother’s friend.



the third member of the party, Horace D’Almayne, have be well fit

by nature to sustain the character of “exquisite”--tall, and with a

graceful, slender figure, his well-form and regular feature, soft

dark hair, and brilliant complexion, give he an undoubted right to the

epithet handsome, although it be in a style suit rather to a woman

than to a man. the expression of his face, cynical and supercilious when

in repose, or when he speak to one of his own sex, relax into a smile

of sentimental self-confidence when he address a woman. he appear

very young, probably not above two or three and twenty, and be dress

up to the _ne plus ultra_ of refined dandyism.



“‘why, D’Almayne,” exclaim Hazlehurst, “how be it that we come to be

honour by your company? I be not even aware that my father possess

the pleasure of your acquaintance.”



“nor do he a week ago; but the matter come about thus,” be the

reply. “during the London season I be introduce at one of the Duke

of D------’s party, to an opulent individual of the name of Crane,

learn his opinion prospective and retrospective in regard to the

weather, bow adieu, and straightway forget he. about a month since,

be in a _café_ at Baden-Baden, my attention be attract by an

awful _charivari_; and on attempt to investigate the cause thereof,

discover Friend Crane lament himself pathetically in bad French

and bad german, and surround by a mob of foreigner. have in some

degree appease his polyglot passion, I soon contrive to make out, that

his pocket having be pick by A., he have accuse innocent B., and

denounce unoffending C.--a vicarious system of reprisal which those

victimise individual appear, not unnaturally, incline to resent.

understand somewhat well than our irascible friend the language and

custom of the native, I contrive to extricate he from the dilemma;

for which act of good Samaritanism I have be, from that time forward,

more or less the victim of his indefatigable gratitude. your worthy

father find I a few day since locate in the Château Crane,

politely include I in his invitation. I arrive this morning, and

under the able tuition of your cousin and sister, be rapidly become

acquaint with the beauty of Hazlehurst, when you drive up.”



as he insinuate this skilfully-veil compliment, the exquisite horace

point its application by favour Alice with a languish _œillade_,

which be certainly not without effect; for it excited in the breast

of Harry Coverdale a sudden, intense, and unreasonable desire then and

there heartily to kick the talented originator of the compliment. this

impulse he be only enable to check by a powerful effort, which cause

he to twitch the rein so suddenly, as painfully to compress the

delicate mouth of “my Aunt Sally,” to an extent which justify that

outraged quadrupe in convert herself for the time be into a

bipe, by stand erect on her hind leg, and paw the air with her

fore foot.



“Soho, girl! gently, gently!” exclaim Hazlehurst, who, not having

perceive the exciting cause of the manœuvre, attribute the mare’s

unmannerly behaviour to an outbreak of inherent viciousness. “why,

Harry, what on earth be the matter with the creature?”



“probably nothing more than a reasonless caprice natural to her sex,”

 be Harry’s ungallant reply. “possibly she may have the bad taste to

prefer the creature comfort of a cool stable and a good feed of corn,

to remain in the broiling sunshine, even with the opportunity of

becoming acquaint with the beauty of Hazlehurst;” and as he make

this sarcastic remark, Harry glance, carelessly round over wood and

field, so that any one not well acquainted with the play of his feature

would have be puzzle to decide whether he be himself aware of the

full meaning of his word.



“a pretty broad hint that I be not to keep the mare stand any

long,” return hazlehurst, turn to his cousin and sister. “that

fellow care for nothing in the world but his horse, except his dog

and his double-barrel. well, I suppose you girl will be come home

soon.”



“quite as soon as we be want, if your amiable and complimentary

friend have any voice in the matter,” return Alice, _sotto voce_.



“nonsense,” be the reply in the same tone; “you know nothing about he,

you silly child. Harry be the kindest-hearted, well-temper fellow in

the world, as you’ll find out before long.”



Alice’s only reply be an incredulous toss of her pretty head, and the

party separate.



“of all the puppy I ever beheld, that creature D’Almayne be the most

insufferable--the very sight of he irritate I. what business have he

to pay his absurd compliment to your sister, when he have only know she

for a few hour? if I be you, I should not stand it.”



“at all event, his compliment be of a more civil nature than your,”

 return Hazlehurst with a smile; “why, Harry, you be become as

peppery a character as your namesake Hotspur himself.”



“I be like he in one particular, at all event,” be the reply, “for I

cannot abide a coxcomb.”



“it strike I, that be not the only point in which you resemble the

‘gunpowder Percy,’ as old Falstaff call he. by the way,” he continue,

“what in the world be the matter with ‘Aunt Sally,’ a minute ago she

seem to go quietly enough now.”



“I rather fancy something must have hurt her mouth,” reply Harry,

turn away his head to conceal a smile. as he speak, they drive round

the gravel sweep lead to the hall door of Hazlehurst Grange. beneath

the porch stand two gentleman--in one of whom, corpulent and elderly,

Coverdale have little trouble in recognise, from his likeness to

his friend, Mr. Hazlehurst senior; while the other, tall, thin, and

cadaverous-look, he rightly conjecture to be the opulent and amorous

cotton spinner, Jedediah Crane.









CHAPTER V.--PROVES the ADVISABILITY of look before you LEAP.



nearly a week have elapse since Harry Coverdale have first become an

inmate of Hazlehurst Grange, during which period he have contrive to win

the good opinion of the elder of the party, pique the young lady by

his _brusquerie_ and neglect, annoy Hazlehurst by his insensibility

and determination not to make himself agreeable, and finally to have

provoke the enmity of the fascinating Horace D’Almayne, which last

piece of delinquency be a source of unmitigated satisfaction to its

perpetrator. the day on which we resume the thread of our narrative, be

to be devoted to a picnic party, the object be to devour unlimited

cold lamb and pigeon-pie amongst the ruin of an old abbey, some eight

mile from the Grange. the morning be lovely, every one appear in

high spirit, and the expedition promise to be a prosperous one.



“now, then, good people,” exclaim Arthur Hazlehurst, “what be the

arrangement--who ride, who drive, who go with who?--come to the

point and settle something, for the _tempus_ be _fugit_-ing at a most

alarming pace.”



“I be desirous,” observe Mr. Crane slowly and solemnly, “of solicit

the honour of drive Miss Hazlehurst in my phaeton, if I may venture to

hope such an arrangement will not be disagreeable to that lady:” and

as he speak, the cotton spinner, whose tall, ungainly figure, clothe in

a dust-colour wrapper, white trouser, and white hat, give he the

appearance of a superannuated baker’s boy run very decidedly to seed,

bow appealingly to Alice, who, perceive her father’s eye upon she,

be force unwillingly to consent.



“Mr. Coverdale, will you drive a lady in the pony-chaise?” inquire

Hazlehurst _père_. “my niece will be happy to accompany you, or my saucy

little Emily here,” he continue, gaze with paternal fondness on his

young daughter, a pretty but slightly pert girl of sixteen.



“I should have much pleasure,” mutter Harry; “but--but--I contrive

to hurt my right hand a few day ago, and--ar--not be use to the

pony, I should scarcely feel justified in undertake the charge.”



“indeed,” be the rejoinder; “I notice you always wear a glove--how do

the accident happen, pray?”



“I hit--that be--I strike my hand against something very hard,”

 stammer Harry, actually colour like a girl, as he catch

Hazlehurst’s suppress chuckle, and observe Alice’s bright eye fix

upon he inquisitively.



“Kate, if nobody else will drive you, I suppose I must take compassion

on you myself,” remark Arthur, _sotto voce_, to his cousin.



“ah! but here come somebody who intend to relieve you of the trouble,”

 be the reply, in the same low tone; “do not make any objection,” she

continue, quickly, “you will only annoy my uncle to no purpose;

he would not have even a feather of the Crane’s tail ruffle on any

account.”



as she speak, she glance meaningly towards Horace D’Almayne, at that

moment engage in draw on a pair of kid glove too small even for his

delicate hand. come forward, he languidly, and in an absent manner,

volunteer to drive Miss Marsden--an offer which that young lady

quietly accept, either not perceive, or disregarding, the look of

annoyance with which her cousin turn and leave the spot.



“oh, you be go to ride, Mr. Coverdale; here come Sir Lancelot,

look like a picture,” exclaim Tom Hazlehurst, a fine, handsome lad,

_anno ætatis_ fourteen, an Etonian, and (need we add?) a pickle--“Oh!

do let I go with you; Alice will lend I her pony--won’t you, Alice?

I’ll take such care of it, and you don’t want it yourself, you know--ask

she to lend it to I, Mr. Coverdale, do please.”



if Harry have a weakness, it be that he could never say no, when his

good nature be appeal to in any matter in which another’s pleasure

be involve. Tom, moreover, have conceive for he one of those

violent friendship which boy feel towards man a few year old than

themselves who realise their _beau ideal_ of perfection; and Harry,

please with his undisguised admiration, respond to it by indulge

the young scapegrace in all his vagary.



“I’m afraid my voice be not so potential as you imagine, Tom,” be his

reply; “but if my assurance that I will use my good endeavour to

keep you and the pony in good order, will have any weight with Miss

Hazlehurst, I be perfectly willing to give it.”



“if papa have no objection, Tom, you have my consent,” reply Alice,

blush and smile, while, at the bottom of her heart she wish both

Mr. Crane and Harry safely locate at Coventry, Jericho, or any other

refuge for bore, that might be suitable for put those who be in

the way out of the way; in which case she would herself have enjoy a

canter with Master Tom.



“oh, the Governor willn’t say no--will you daddy?” be Tom’s confident

reply; and Mr. Hazlehurst, who, be a dreadful autocrat to his eld

child, make up for it by weakly indulge his youngest bear, having

signify his consent, the cavalcade proceed to start--a close

carriage and a barouche convey the remain juvenile, and all the

elder of the party, with the exception of Mrs. Hazlehurst, who, be a

confirm invalid, remain at home, in company with a weather-wise old

maid, proprietress of a meteorological corn, which having give warn

that a change be at hand, lead she to mistrust the brilliant sunshine.



“cann’t we find our way across the field somehow, Tom, without ride

along the dusty road the whole distance inquire Harry.



“to be sure we can,” be the reply; “don’t I know a way, that’s all?

turn down the next lane to the right, and then there be lot of jolly

grass field and a wide common, so that we can gallop as much as we

like, and get there before they--won’t they be surprised to see we

just?”



“what a lark!”



Tom’s topographical knowledge prove correct, they canter away

merrily over field and common, till they have ride some five or six

mile.



“you really have an uncommonly good seat, Tom,” observe his friend;

“only remember to turn your toe in, and keep your bridle hand low, and

you’ll do--you’ve plenty of pluck, and when you’ve acquire a little

more judgment and experience, you’ll be able to ‘hold your own’ across a

country with some of the good of ’em.



“ah, shouldn’t I like to go out hunting, that’s all?” exclaim the boy

eagerly.



“have you never do so,” inquire his friend.



“no; I try it on last winter, but the Governor cut up rough, and

wouldn’t stand it.”



“can you sit a leap?” ask Harry.



“I believe you, rayther, just a _very_ few,” be the confident reply.



“well, you must come to Coverdale, in the Christmas holiday, and I’ll

mount you and take you out with I; I mean to get up a stud, and hunt

regularly this season,” observe Harry.



“willn’t that be jolly, just?--I’ll come whether they’ll let I or not,

depend upon it; but now this be the last grass field, let’s have a race

for a wind up.” so say, Master Tom lay his whip smartly across his

pony’s shoulder, and dash off, while Coverdale, gradually give

his spirited but perfectly break horse the rein, soon overtake he. a

brushing gallop of five minute bring they to the border of the field,

which be surround by a ditch and bank, with a sufficiently high rail

at top to constitute an awkward leap.



“how be we go to find our way out?” inquire Harry.



“get off, pull down a rail, and then jump it,” be the reply.



“yes, that will be the good way for you and the pony to get over,”

 return Coverdale, “but I’ll take it as it stand. I’ve never yet have a

chance of try Lancelot at a stiff fence, and I want to see how he’ll

act: don’t you attempt to follow I; as soon as I be over, I’ll dismount

and pull down the rail for you.”



as he speak Harry put his horse in motion, canter he up to the fence,

and face he at it. Sir Lancelot do not belie the character that have

be give of he. as he approach the bank he quicken his pace of

his own accord, gather his leg well under he, and then rise to

the leap, spring over with a motion so easy and elastic that his rider

appear scarcely to move in his saddle. the descent on the farth side

be steep than Harry have expect, and the leap altogether might be

consider a difficult one. delighted with his horse’s performance,

Harry pull up, and turn, with the intention of alight, in order

to remove a rail of the fence, and thus facilitate the transit of Tom

and the pony; when, to his alarm and vexation, he perceive that the

boy, deceive by the apparent ease with which he have accomplish

the task (a delusive appearance, produce as much by the coolness and

address of the rider as by the power and excellent training of the

horse), have determine to display his prowess by follow he; nor

could Harry interfere to prevent he, for at the moment he turn, Tom

be in the act of gallop up to the fence: all that remain for he,

therefore, be to shout, “give the pony his head, and hold tight with

your knee,” and to await the result. the pony, excited by see its

companion on the other side, face the leap boldly, and clear the

ditch and bunk, but catch its hoof against the rail, fall, pitch

its rider over its head into the field beyond, where he lie as if

stun. in an instant Harry have spring from his saddle and lift he

in his arm. “thank Heaven!” he exclaim as the boy open his eye,

and, perceive Coverdale bend over he, smile to evince his

gratitude.



“you don’t feel as if you be seriously hurt anywhere, do you?”



“all right!” be the reply. “I feel a little bit shaky and confused;

rather as if somebody have go and kick I into the middle of next

week, that’s all.”



“then you’ve escape more easily than you have any right to expect, you

heedless, impetuous young monkey,” return Coverdale, sharply. “you

must have be mad to suppose that a half-breed, thick-headed beast like

that pony, would carry you over such a fence as that. why, I know man,

who call themselves good rider, who would refuse it, unless they be

very well mount.”



“if the pony do not carry I over, he shoot I over, and that do just

as well,” be the careless reply. “but I say, Mr. Coverdale, only look

at his knee? oh! shalln’t we get into a jolly scrape just.”



thus appeal to, Harry turn to examine the pony, which, in his

anxiety for the safety of the boy, he have hitherto forget. the result

of his scrutiny be by no mean satisfactory.



“he have break both knee!” he exclaim; “the right one be cut

severely, and however favourably it may go on, there will always remain

a scar; you’ve knock ten pound off the pony’s price by that exploit

of your, Master Tom, besides render the animal unsafe for your

sister to ride.”



“you’ve put your foot in it as well as I, Mr. Coverdale,” return the

young imp, grin. “you promise Alice you would do your good to keep

I, and the pony too, in proper order, you know!”



“why, you ungrateful young scamp, I’m sure I tell you not to attempt

the leap,” reply Harry, restrain a strong inclination to lay his

horsewhip across the young pickle’s shoulder.



“yes; and then you and Lancelot went fly over it as lightly as if he

have wing, like that fabulous humbug Pegasus, that old Buzwig be always

bother we about. the copy-book say, ‘practice before precept,’ and

so say I. why, you do not expect I be go to be such a muff as to

stay behind, do you?”



“I be a fool if I do, at all event,” mutter Harry, _sotto voce_;

then turn good-naturedly to the boy, he continue, “the copy-book

also say, ‘what cann’t be cure must be endure,’ do it not, Tom? so

we must get out of the scrape as good we can. we’ll leave the pony at

the near farm-house, and I’ll send my groom to doctor he--so lead

he by the rein and come along.”



of course, when they join the rest of the party and tell their

misdeed, Alice lament over the pony’s trouble after the usual

fashion of tender-hearted young lady. of course, Hazlehurst senior,

discern a long farrier’s bill in prospective, with the possibility of

be coax out of a new pony as a not unlikely contingent result, be

grumpy, as governor usually be when they foresee a strain upon

their purse string; and of course, although these lamentation and

threatening be launch at the curly head of Master Tom, they

yet glance off that unimpressible substance, only to fall upon and

overwhelm with shame and confusion Harry Coverdale, who begin mentally

to curse the day when, false to his own presentiment, he have yield to

his friend’s importunity, and suffer himself to become an inmate of

Hazlehurst Grange.



bent on avoid young lady, and have no taste for the society of

old one, Harry wander about disconsolately, until, attract by a

dark archway and a worm-eat winding staircase, which, as Master Tom

express it, look “jolly queer and ghostifie;” he make his way up

the mouldering step until he find himself at the top of a battlemented

tower, where he be repay for the trouble of the ascent, by a beautiful

and widely-extend view. having contrive to get rid of the voluble

and restless Etonian, Coverdale seat himself on a project fragment

of masonry, and glance round to see that he be not observe or

observable, light a cigar, and, his ruffle feeling be soothe by

its mollifying influence, remain lazily watch the movement of the

pleasure-seeker--his reflection run somewhat after the follow

fashion:--



“there’s old Crane maunder about after Alice as usual--don’t think he

get on with she though, rather t’other way--decide case of jib

I should say. she look awfully bored and frighten too, up in that

phaeton with he; and no wonder either, for the old boy be nothing of a

whip--I should be sorry to trust a cat of mine to his driving. ah! she’s

give he the slip, and that Miss Marsden have take he in tow. I cann’t

make that woman out--she _be_ so civil to he; perhaps she think

the affair with Alice may miss fire, and she be look out for the

reversion of the cotton spinner herself. Arthur say she’s very poor,

and that there be a large family of they; if so, it’s not a bad dodge,

and, suppose she play her card well, one by no mean unlikely to

succeed. there’s that confound puppy D’Almayne swagger up to Alice,

stroke his stupid moustache--yes, and she smile and take his arm,

of course--believe all his lie, and think he a hero, I dare say.

oh! the poor silly fool of woman that cann’t distinguish a man from a

jackanape--I should have fancy Alice have more sense; but they’re all

alike. look at the idiot simpering; that’s only to show his white tooth

now: the brute have no idea of a real joke--hasn’t get it in he. well,

thank goodness, it’s no concern of mine: but if I be Crane, I’d

interfere with his flirt rather. the fellow talk as if he be

a dreadful fire-eater--I should like to try what he’s make of: but I

expect it’s all talk and nothing else--I wish I could coax he into

put on the glove with I some day--I’d astonish his moustache for

he. well, he have walk she off at all event. I wonder where they’re

go to. be they? yes--no--yes, by Jove, if he ben’t go to take

she across that field which Tom and I ride through, where the bull be

graze--the brute be mischievous, too, or I be much mistaken--confound

the fool, he’ll go and frighten the poor girl out of her sense, and,

perhaps, get she hurt into the bargain; for, if the bull really be

vicious, ten to one moustache lose pluck, and bolt, or something

ridiculous. I’ve a great mind to follow they, it can do no harm, and may

do some good--’gad I will too. Alice be far too pretty to be gore

by a bull; besides, for Arthur’s sake, one be bind to take care of

she--luckily, I’ve just finish the cigar, so off we go.”



having arrive at this point in his meditation, Harry rise from his

seat, run lightly down the stair till he reach a ruin window about

six foot from the ground, through which he leap, then settle into a

long swinge trot, he run, at a pace with which few could have keep

up, in the direction take by Alice and D’Almayne; they have, however,

obtain so greatly the start of he, that they have already enter the

field occupy by the dangerous bull, ere he have overtake they.



it be a remarkably warm day--the field in which pasture the alarming

bull be distant from the abbey ruin half-a-mile at the very least.

now, to jump through a window six foot or thereabout from the ground,

run at the top of one’s speed half-a-mile, leap recklessly over two

gate and a stile in the course of it; and to do all this in a state of

anxious excitement on a day when the thermometer stand at 70° in the

shade, naturally tend to make a man not only hot, but (if his temper be

not semiangelic) cross also. at all event, Harry Coverdale be in the

former, if not the latter, condition, when, panting and breathless,

he overtake Alice Hazlehurst and Horace D’Almayne, half way across the

dangerous field.









CHAPTER VI.--JEST and EARNEST.



“Mr. Coverdale, be anything the matter?--why, you be quite out of

breath with running!” exclaim Alice, start as she behold he.



“uncomfortably warm, too, I should say,” drawled D’Almayne, glance

significantly at Harry’s glow cheek, which be certainly too red

to be romantic; “really now, do you consider it judicious to overheat

yourself so?--of course, I merely ask as a matter of curiosity.”



Harry magnanimously repress a strong inclination to knock he

down; but he feel that to answer he coolly be both literally and

metaphorically out of his power, so he confine his reply to Alice’s

question.



“there be nothing the matter, Miss Hazlehurst,” he say; “but see you

take this direction, and think that Mr. D’Almayne might not be aware

a bull be graze in this meadow, I think it advisable to follow and

put you on your guard, even at the risk of make myself unbecomingly

hot;” and as he pronounce the last two word, he look at D’Almayne

as though he wish _he_ have be the bull, and would oblige he by

evince an inclination to attack they.



“how very kind and thoughtful of you!” return Alice, bestow on

he one of her bright smile; “but be there any danger?--what have we

well do?”



“eh, really, danger! not the slight; be not _i_ with you?” interpose

D’Almayne, majestically bend over she. “a bull do you say, Mr.

Coverdale?--ar--really, I don’t perceive such a creature.--Are you quite

sure he exist anywhere but in your vivid and poetical imagination?”



Harry’s reply, if reply it can be call, to this impertinent

question, be make by grasp D’Almayne’s elbow so tightly as to cause

that delicate young gentleman to wince under the pressure. having thus

attract his attention at a moment when Alice’s head be turn in

an opposite direction, he point towards a group of tree, under the

shadow whereof might be discern a large brindle individual of the

bovine specie, who stand attentively regard the trio with a

singularly unamiable, not to say vicious expression of countenance. place

his finger on his lip as a hint to D’Almayne to keep the knowledge thus

acquire to himself, Harry answer Alice’s inquiry by saying--



“it be always the safe policy to mistrust a bull; so I would advise

you to turn and make the good of your way towards the stile over which I

come; walk as quickly as you please, but do not run, as that would only

tempt the animal to follow you.”



“yes, really, Miss Hazlehurst, we must not risk the chance of

frightening you merely because we man enjoy the excitement of a little

danger--take my arm,” hastily rejoin Horace D’Almayne, and suit the

action to the word, he draw Alice’s arm within his own, and march she

off at a pace with which she find considerable difficulty in keep

up. Harry, ere he follow they, remain stationary for a minute or so,

to reconnoitre the movement of the bull. that animal, having apparently

satisfied his curiosity in regard to the intruder on his domain, be

now assiduously work himself up into a rage, preparatory, no doubt,

to institute vigorous measure for their expulsion. the way in which

he signify this intention, be by toss his head up and down,

tear up the turf with his fore-foot, and utter from time to time a

low angry roar, like the rumbling of distant thunder. when Harry turn

to leave the spot, the animal immediately follow he, though only at

a walk. as soon as he become aware of this disagreeable fact, Coverdale

pause, and face his undesirable attendant; which manœuvre, as he

expect, cause the bull to stop also, though it be evident it have the

effect of increase the creature’s rage. in spite of this discovery,

Harry wait till his companion have reach the stile, and D’Almayne

have assist Alice to get over it--a piece of chivalry by which he very

materially lessen his own chance of safety, as the bull’s small stock

of patience be exhaust, it become evident he be prepare for

a rush. trust to his swiftness of foot, Harry be about to make an

attempt to reach the stile before the bull should overtake he, when

suddenly the yelping of a dog be hear, and a terri belong to

Arthur Hazlehurst, which have follow they unobserved, run forward and

distract the bull’s attention by bark round he, take especial

care to keep out of the reach of the animal’s horn. this diversion in

his favour enable Coverdale to rejoin his companion unmoleste.



“oh, Mr. Coverdale, what a savage-look creature! I be so afraid it

be go to attack you. I do not know how to thank you properly for

having save I from at least a terrible fright,” exclaim Alice as

Harry run up to they.



“Ar--from alarm possibly; but really I don’t conceive there be the

slight danger; the animal be a very mild speciman of his class; even

a little dog, you see, be sufficient to turn he,” observe D’Almayne

slightingly.



“I’ll bet you fifty pound to one you don’t walk across that field while

the bull remain there,” exclaim Harry eagerly--“Miss Hazlehurst shall

be umpire, and I’ll promise to come and do my good to help you if you

get into any scrape--what do you say, be it a bet?”



“I never bet, and--ar--never do useless and unreasonable thing on a hot

day, in order to establish a fast reputation. such little excitement

may be all very well for a sporting character like yourself, my dear

Coverdale; but--ar--a man who have shoot bison on the american prairie

do not need they; so really you must hold I excuse. shall we

rejoin the rest of the party, Miss Hazlehurst? they seem assemble

for luncheon. let I recollect, we be talk of that charming

soul-creation of Tennyson, _Locksley Hall_, I think, before this absurd

interruption occur; what an unrivalled picture do it not present of

the spirit-torture of a proud despair?”--and chatter on in the same

pseudo-romantic and grandiloquent strain, the man of sentiment fairly

walk Alice off, leave Coverdale in the unenviable position popularly

ascribe to virtue, viz., that of be its own reward. having wait

till the pair be out of sight, he fling himself down at the foot of an

old beech-tree, and indulge in the follow mental soliloquy:--



“well, Master Harry! you’ve be and do something clever--you have,

certainly; run like an insane creature more than half-a-mile, on by

far the hot day we’ve have this summer, and place yourself in a

situation where nothing but a lucky accident save you from be run

at, and possibly gore, by rather a mad bull than otherwise, only to be

pooh-poohe by an insolent coxcomb, and have a cold-hearted ungrateful

girl lisp out a missish inquiry, ‘whether there be any danger,’
