forsooth! ’gad, I almost wish I’d leave she and her swain to find out

for themselves.”



he pause, remove his hat to allow a slight breeze which have spring up

to cool his heated forehead, and then stretch himself resumed:--



“I hope I’m not really become morose and ill-temper, as Arthur

hint the other day. I must take care, or I shall be grow a savage

old brute, and have everybody hate I. it’s all that puppy D’Almayne; he

keep I in a constant state of suppress irritation with his affected

air of superiority;--but puppy will exist on the face of the earth,

I suppose, whether I like it or not, and must be endure; so we’ll

endeavour to look upon he as an appoint trial, and see if we can

turn he to good account in that way. there’s always the possibility of

horsewhip he as a _dernier ressort_, that’s one consolation.

now I’ll go to luncheon, and try whether I can put some of my good

intention into practice. Heigho! life’s hard work, and no mistake;

particularly in warm weather.” thus cogitating, Harry slowly gather

himself up, and betook himself to join the luncheon party, actuate

thereunto, amongst other reason, by the discovery of a serious attack

of appetite. in the meantime, a scene of a very different character be

be enact between two other of our _dramatis personæ_.



Arthur Hazlehurst, foil in his attempt to secure a _tête-à-tête_ drive

with his cousin, Kate Marsden, have, after his usual habit, bustle

about, settle everything for everybody, and make himself very generally

useful and agreeable, have contrive on arrive at the ruin to withdraw

himself from the rest of the party, and having watch the proceeding

of his cousin and Mr. Crane, wait until she separate from that

gentleman, when he join she, and induce she to stroll with he along

a shady, serpentine, romantic-look pathway lead through a wood.

agreeable as be external circumstance, however, neither the lady nor

the gentleman appear to be in a sympathetic frame of mind; for a

cloud hang on Arthur’s brow, while his cousin’s feature wear a cold,

uncompromise look of defiance. they proceed for some little distance

in silence; Hazlehurst be the first to speak.



“you find your companion amusing, I hope; pray what might he be talk

about so earnestly?”



“do you really care to know?” be the reply; “he be make I his

_confidante_ in regard to Alice. the poor man be at his wit’ end--if

a quality which he do not possess can be say to have an end; at all

event, he be _au désespoir_. even his obtuseness cannot be blind to the

fact that she dislike he, and the worthy soul be now begin to grow

mildly jealous of D’Almayne.”



“and what advice do you give he?” inquire her cousin, sternly; “tell

I the truth.”



as he speak the girl’s eye flash, and a slight colour burn for a

moment in her pale cheek.



“how _dare_ you say such a thing to I!” be her indignant rejoinder;

“have I ever attempt to deceive _you?_--you know I have not; but let

it pass. you ask I what advice I give he: I tell he to persevere,

remind he that a faint heart never win a fair lady, which I believe

he take to be an entirely original remark on my part, and gently

insinuate that no girl in her sense could refuse he.”



Arthur fix his pierce glance upon she, as he replied--



“and why do you say this? do you believe, indeed, that Alice will

eventually be prevail upon to marry him?--or do you say it to deceive

he for a purpose of your own?”



“I give he good sound advice,” be the answer; “I do _not_ believe

Alice will marry he; but that be no reason why he should not use his

good endeavour to obtain what he wish, or fancy he wish. I shall

advise he to prosecute his suit, and at the right moment to offer to

she in person.”



“in order that she may irritate he, and offend my father, by a refusal.

Kate, you be play some deep game in all this, and one of which you

know I should disapprove, or else you would not so studiously conceal it

from I,” return Hazlehurst gloomily.



there be a moment’s pause ere the young lady replied--



“let event unravel themselves, my worthy cousin; the result will appear

all in good time.”



they walk on in silence, till a turn in the path bring they before a

smooth moss-grow bank, on which the gnarl root of an old pollard-oak

form a natural rustic seat.



“let we rest here, and enjoy the sunshine while we may; there be not too

much of it in the world,” observe Kate, in a gentler tone than she have

hitherto use. there be a touch of sadness in her voice which Arthur

could not hear unmove, and merely wait till she have seat herself,

he place himself on a root of the tree at her foot. for some minute

neither of they speak, till as it be unconsciously, Kate allow she

hand to rest on his head, while her finger play with a lock of his

rich chesnut hair. as he feel her soft touch upon his brow, he raise

his eye to her countenance--the stern, hard expression have vanish, and

in its place appear that look which, once see, the recollection die

only with memory itself--the fond, wistful, tender gaze a love woman

turn on he she love. for a minute he remain silent and motionless,

subdue by the power of her rare beauty; then spring to his foot, he

exclaimed--



“you shall trifle with I thus no long; I be no petulant boy, to be

repulse one hour, and caress into good humour the next. what be

the meaning of this estrangement which you have choose shall spring

up between we? why do you?--but such question be useless--this shall

decide the point--once and for ever:--Do you love I, or do you not?”



for a moment she be silent; then turn her head to avoid his eager

scrutinizing glance, she murmured--



“have we not know each other from childhood, and love each other

always?”



“that be no answer; you only seek to evade my question,” be the angry

reply.



he stand for a moment, his lip quiver with emotion, and his hand

clench so tightly that the blood recede from the point of his

finger, leave they cold and colourless as marble. his companion

do not speak, but continue to regard he with a look half-pity,

half-implore pity. as their eye meet, his mood appear suddenly to

change, and spring to her side, he exclaim in a voice tremulous

with emotion--



“Kate, dearest, why will you thus torture yourself and I? hear I, dear

one; you know I love you well than any create thing--well than my

own soul. you say truly, that I have love you always--with the tender

unconscious love of the child, with the happy romantic love of the

boy, and, lastly, with the deep, earnest, absorb passion of mature

manhood; and you, Kate, you must--nay, you _do_ love I!”



as he speak, he draw she gently towards he, and unrepulsed press a

kiss upon her soft lip. she do not resist or respond to his caress,

but suffer her head to rest passively against his shoulder, as he

continued--



“I do not inquire--I hee not--what mad scheme you may have dream of;

but I ask--nay, I implore you, by all you hold sacred to put they away

from you, and to wait patiently for a few, a very few short year, until

I can claim you for my beloved, my honoured wife. Kate, you will do as I

desire?--speak to I, my own love!”



unheede his appeal, she remain for a minute silent, while a few

tear steal unchecked down her pale cheek, then rouse herself by an

effort, she wipe away the trace of her late emotion, gently remove

her cousin’s arm, which still encircle her waist, and draw herself

up, exclaimed--



“this be weakness--folly; I never intend it should have come to this;

but I be take by surprise--unprepared----”



she pause, struggle to regain self-possession, then in a calm voice

resumed:--



“my poor Arthur! I do, indeed, appreciate your noble, generous

self-sacrifice, and be I alone concern, would desire no happy fate

than to share and aid you in your struggle with the world; but it

may not be so; other have claim upon I--my father’s health be

fail--the care of that bitter curse, poverty, be wear out

my mother’s little remain strength, and blight the talent and

crush the youth and spirit of the child. Dear Arthur, forgive I

the pain I cost you when I tell you--I can never be your wife!”



“but, Kate,” interrupt her cousin, eagerly, “listen to I, dear one;

you do not suppose that I have forget all this; only agree to my

proposal, and I will be a son to your mother, a father--if, as you

fear, my uncle’s health be break--to her child. my practice be

increase every day; I shall soon be in the receipt of a good income;

Coverdale be rich, and love I as a brother; he will advance I money;

I will work day and night to repay he.”



“my husband destroy his health to support my family!--is this the

prospect of happiness you would offer me?--are these the argument you

would bring forward to induce I to agree?” be the reply. “no, Arthur,

I can never be your wife; you must from this moment forget that such an

idea have cross your mind.”



“but, Kate, only hear me!----” he exclaim passionately.



“I have already hear too much for your happiness, or for my own,”

 be the mournful reply; then, by a powerful effort resume her usual

manner, she exclaim, “come, no more of this folly, our path in life

lie separate; it be inevitable--therefore repine become bad

than useless; we be not boy and girl, to stand rehearse romantic

love-scene together; let we rejoin the other.”



for a moment hazlehurst remain silently gaze on the cold, immovable

expression of her feature; then, come close to she, he say in a low,

hoarse whisper, “I read your heart, and perceive the wickedness, for

such it be, you contemplate. I will give you till to-morrow morning

to reflect on what have pass between we; if then you adhere to your

determination, I leave you to the fate you have choose!” and as he

utter the last word, he turn and quit she.



Kate Marsden gaze after he with the same cold expression of defiance

on her feature till his retreating figure become no long visible,

then, sink back upon the rustic bench, she cover her face with her

hand and weep bitterly.









CHAPTER VII.--WHEREIN symptoms of HARRY’S COURTSHIP BEGIN to appear ON A

STORMY HORIZON.



the humour of a picnic have be too often describe to need

repetition; suffice it to say, that the picnic in question be decidedly

a favourable speciman of its class. of course everybody vote it to be

the summit of human felicity, to sit in an uncomfortable position upon

something never intend for a seat, beside a table-cloth spread upon

the grass, which, be elastic and uneven, cause everything that

should have remain perpendicular to assume a horizontal attitude. of

course, when the inevitable frog hop across the table-cloth, and,

lose its presence of mind on find itself so unexpectedly launch

into fashionable life, seek refuge in the pigeon-pie, the lady

scream little picturesque scream, which be increase

twentyfold when Tom Hazlehurst fish it out with a table-spoon, and

surreptitiously immerse it in the jug of beer, which liquid he artfully

incite Mr. Crane to pour out, thereby land the frog, decidedly

inebriated and most uncomfortably sticky, upon the elaborately

embroider shirt-front of Horace D’Almayne. of course the salt and the

sugar have fraternize, and the cayenne have elicit new and striking

effect by mingle indiscriminately with thing in general, and

the sweet in particular; and of course all these shocking disaster

irritate the few and delight the many, and add immensely to the

liveliness and hilarity of the party.



“Tom, you’re drink too much champagne!” exclaim an elderly maiden

sister of Mr. Hazlehurst, decidedly like a hippopotamus in face and

figure. “Mr. D’Almayne, may I trouble you to hand I his glass, the boy

will make himself poorly.”



thus appeal to, D’Almayne languidly extend his arm in the necessary

direction, but the Etonian be not to be so easily despoiled of his

beverage.



“_Mille pardon_, mounseer!” he exclaim, mimic the affected

half-foreign accent with which the exquisite Horace usually speak;

“_mais c’est tout à fait_--out of the question; _ne souhaitez-vous pas

que vous pouvez l’obtenir?_--don’t you wish you may get it? equally

oblige to you, but I’d rather do my own drinking myself. why, my dear

Aunt Betsy, how dreadfully ungrateful of you, just when I be go

to propose your health, too! silence, gentleman, for a toast! come,

Governor (to his father, who, delight with the young pickle’s ready

wit, be vainly endeavour to preserve an appearance of majestic

disapproval), fill up; D’Almayne, my boy, no heeltap; be you all

charge? ‘my Aunt Betsy, and the rest of her lovely sex!--hip! hip! hip!

hurrah!’” so say, and with a knowing wink at Coverdale, who, if the

truth must be tell, encourage he in his inclination to be impertinent

to D’Almayne, Master Tom toss down his glass of champagne amidst a

general chorus of laughter. and thus the _déjeûner_ pass off to all

appearance merrily enough; though in two, if not more, of the company a

smile exterior hide an aching heart.



“have you see the rabbit warren yet, Mr. Coverdale? do come, there be

such a lot of the beggar jump about! I find my way there before

luncheon, and it willn’t take long,” exclaim Tom Hazlehurst, grasp

Harry’s arm imploringly.



“it strike I I shall be consider especially rude if I again absent

myself,” be the reply.



“who by?--the woman?” inquire Tom, scornfully. “never mind they--poor,

weak-minded, fickle thing; there be nothing I consider a great

nuisance than to have a pack of silly girl dangle about one, that

willn’t leave a fellow alone; there, you needn’t toss your head and turn

up your nose about it, emily, beneficent nature’s do that for you

sufficiently already. now will you come, Mr. Coverdale? there be some

black rabbit among they, such rum shaver!”



“be there!” exclaim Harry, eagerly. “I wonder whether I could

contrive to buy a few couple of they; I want to get some black rabbit

at the park excessively: come along, for our time be grow short, I

expect.” and as he speak, Coverdale stride off, entirely forgetful of

the pretty emily, with whom, on the strength of her juvenility, he have

consider he might safely allow himself to laugh and talk, and to whom

he have, therefore, be unconsciously render himself very agreeable.



the warren be furth than he have expect it would be, and the black

rabbit be so long before they choose to show themselves, that Harry

begin to grow sceptical as to their existence; even when they do

appear, a gamekeeper have to be route out, and term for the transfer of

ten couple to Coverdale Park agree upon; so that by the time Tom and

his companion rejoin the pleasure-seeker, there be but few leave

to rejoin. these few consist of the old maiden aunt; a time-honour

female friend of the same--old, uglier, still more like a

hippopotamus, and with a double portion of the vinegar of inhuman

unkindness in her nature; and, lastly, a plain young lady, the daughter

of nobody in particular, who live with the time-honour friend

as companion, in a state of chronic martyrdom, for which perpetual

sacrifice she receive thirty pound a-year, and permission to cry

herself to sleep every night, in misty wonderment why so sad a creature

as she be, should ever have be bear into the world. besides this

uncomfortable trio, who compose the cargo of a brougham, and be

rather a tight fit, there remain Mr. Crane and Alice, who, it seem,

be wait for the phaeton, which have not yet make its appearance.



“upon my word, Miss Hazlehurst,” begin the sour friend, address the

acidulate aunt, “this be very provoking, ma’am; it’s six o’clock, and

it’s grow cold, and it will be quite dusk before we get home; and I

really believe Miss Cornetoe be right this morning, and that we shall

have a wet night after all.”



“shall I run down to the inn and see what cause the delay? I must go

there to get my horse,” inquire Coverdale, good naturedly.



“if you would be so kind, we really should be extremely oblige to

you,” return Miss Hazlehurst senior, with she most gracious and least

hippopotamic smile; and thus urge, Coverdale hurry off.



in the meantime poor Alice, who by no mean admire the position of

affair, and have moreover be considerably alarmed in the morning by

Mr. Crane’s unskilful driving, whisper a pathetic appeal to her aunt

to be allow to accompany the brougham party,--“she could sit on the

box, Wilson, the coachman, be so inconceivably respectable, and she be

almost sure it would not rain;”--but her aunt be a strong-minded woman,

and a warm advocate of the Crane alliance, and she would not hear of

such a change of plan. as soon as Coverdale arrive within sight of the

inn, he perceive the miss phaeton stand in front of the doorway,

the horse ready harness, and the groom seat on the driving-seat;

accordingly he make sign to he to come on, of which, for some

unaccountable reason, the man take not the slight notice. surprise

at this, Harry make the good of his way to the spot, and on reach it

discover, from the swollen, heated look of the fellow’s feature, and

the stupid, obstinate expression which characterize they, that he have

be drink to excess.



“why the man be intoxicate!” exclaim Coverdale, turn to the

ostler, who, with one or two hulking village lad, stand stare at the

coachman with a grin of amusement on their vacant face; “why do not

you make he get down, and bring the carriage yourself?”



“a do troy, but a woldn’t budge a inch--a be properly drunk to be

zure!”



“oh, he would not, eh?” inquire Coverdale; then, turn to the groom,

he continue, “get down directly, my friend, I want particularly to

speak to you.”



to this the groom contrive to stammer out on insolent refusal,

accompany by a recommendation to Coverdale to mind his own business,

and give order to his own servant.



“my business just at present be to make you get down from that phaeton,”

 return Harry, his eye flash.



“oh! it be, be it?--I should like to see you do it, that’s all!”

 rejoin the other, with a gesture of drunken defiance.



“you shall,” be the concise reply, as, direct the ostler to stand by

the horse’ head, Coverdale, ere the fellow be aware of his intention,

or could take measure to prevent he, spring lightly up, force the

rein from his uncertain grasp, twist he suddenly round, then place

his hand under his arm lift he by sheer strength, and drop he

to the ground. having perform this feat with the neatness and celerity

of some harlequinade trick, he glance round to see that the fellow have

fall clear of the wheel, and take the rein, drive off.



while this little affair have be proceed, the sky have become

overcast, and a few large drop of rain come patter heavily to

the ground; alarm by these symptom, the brougham party no soon

perceive the phaeton approach, than they scramble into their

vehicle and start. as their road lie in a direction opposite to that by

which Coverdale be advance, they be nearly out of sight by the time

he reach the spot where Alice and Mr. Crane await he. jump

down with the rein in his hand, he be explain to the owner of the

phaeton the plight in which he have find his servant, when a faint flash

of lightning glance across the sky, follow after an interval by

a clap of distant thunder, at which the horse, which be young and

spirit, begin to prick up their ear, and evince such unmistakable

sign of alarm, that their master, fear they be about to dash off,

run to lay hold of their head. Misfortune often bring about strange

association. if any one have that morning tell Alice Hazlehurst that

before the day should be over she would have appeal for protection to,

and confide in, “Arthur’s cross, disagreeable friend,” she would have

utterly disbelieve the statement--and yet so it be to be. the moment

Mr. Crane leave her side, she turn to Harry exclaiming--



“oh, Mr. Coverdale, I be so frightened! he will never be able to manage

those horse: he could scarcely hold they in this morning, and the groom

be force to get down to they twice--he do not know how to drive one

bit!”



Poor little Alice! she be tremble from head to foot, and look so

pretty and interesting in her alarm, that Harry feel peculiar, he don’t

exactly know how, about it.



“I’ll speak to Mr. Crane, and persuade he to let I drive you home,”

 he reply eagerly. (he would have knock he down without the small

hesitation, if Alice have in the slight degree prefer it.) “I’ve

be accustom to horse all my life, and have not a doubt of be

able to manage these, even if the thunder should startle they; so please

don’t look so frightened.”



and as Harry say this with his very bright, kind smile,



Alice wonder she have never before notice how handsome he be, and

begin to think he could not be so very cross after all.



when Harry urge his request, Mr. Crane be considerably embarrassed as

to the nature of his reply. in his secret soul he be delighted to be

relieve from the danger and responsibility of drive Alice and himself

home through a thunder-storm; but, on the other hand, he could not

disguise the fact, that by allow himself to be so relieved, he should

detract from the heroic style of character he wish Alice to impute to

he. have it be D’Almayne instead of Coverdale who seek to become his

substitute, he would probably, at the hazard of break his own neck

and that of his lady-love, have refuse to permit he; but he have

observe, as indeed he must have be blind if he have not do,

Harry’s marked avoidance of the young lady, and trust to these his

mysogynistic principle he, with many excuse and much circumlocution,

agree to Harry’s proposal that he should ride his horse, and allow he

to drive the phaeton.



“Ahem!--if the storm should come on violently,” observe the

cotton-spinner, as a second growl of thunder become audible, “I shall

wait till it have subside; so don’t let they expect I till they see I:

get wet always give I cold.”



“all right, sir,” return Harry, as he wrap Alice carefully up in

his own Macintosh; “take care of yourself by all mean--good people

be scarce. we shall see nothing more of friend Crane to-night,”

 he continue, as he drive off; “the old gentleman be very decidedly

alarmed--that be, I suppose I ought not to call he an old gentleman,”

 he stammer, suddenly recollect with whom he be converse.



“why should you not when he be so?” return Alice, innocently.



Harry turn his head away to conceal a smile which the _naïveté_ of the

reply have call forth, mutter to himself as he do so, “poor crane!”



after a few minute’ silence, Alice begin abruptly, and

apologetically,--



“I’m sure I ought to feel very much oblige to you, Mr. Coverdale--and

indeed I do; this be the second really good-natured thing you’ve do by

I to-day.”



the tone in which she speak so completely betray that surprise be the

feeling uppermost in her mind, that Harry, slightly pique, could not

help replying--



“you do not, then, give I credit for possess the least particle of

good-nature?”



Alice smile as she answered--



“if I have have a proper degree of faith in Arthur’s representation, I

need not have feel surprise.”



the delicate irony of this reply be not lose upon Coverdale; but he

know that he have deserve it, and, with the ready frankness which be

one of his good characteristic, he hasten to acknowledge it.



“I certainly have do little towards practically vindicate the

character your brother’s partiality have bestow upon I,” he say; “but

I must be allow to plead in justification, that I be quite aware of my

own deficiency, and tell Arthur that I have be rough it abroad

so long, that I be totally unfit for lady’ society. he would

not admit the excuse; but it be a full, true, and sufficient one,

nevertheless.”



as he utter the last word, a dazzle flash of lightning appear

almost to envelop they, follow instantaneously by a deafening peal

of thunder. half blind by the blaze of light, the frightened horse

stop abruptly, then, terrify at the prolonged thunder, try to

turn short round; foil in this attempt by the skill and promptitude of

their driver, they begin rear and plunge in a way which threaten

every moment to overturn the phaeton. fortunately the road happen to

be unusually wide at this point, and Harry, who never throughout the

affair in the slight degree lose his presence of mind, decide that

whatever might most effectually frighten the horse, would create the

impulse they would eventually obey, determine to try the effect of

a little judicious discipline. accordingly, stand up, he begin to

administer the whip to their sleek side with an amount of strength and

determination which, from the contrast it afford to the mild and timid

drive to which they be accustom, so astonish the animal,

that bound forward with a snatch which try the soundness of their

harness, they dash off at a furious gallop; at the same moment, a

second peal of thunder, even louder than the precede one, increase

their alarm to such a degree, that Coverdale, despite his utmost

effort, find it completely beyond his power to hold they in.









CHAPTER VIII.--HARRY condescend to play the AGREEABLE.



“Miss Hazlehurst!--Alice! be you mad? only sit still, don’t go and

scream or anything, and all will come right.”



thus appeal to, or rather command--for the tone of the speaker’s

voice be unmistakably imperative--Alice, who when the horse bolt have

half rise from her seat, and in an agony of terror glance round, as

though she meditate an attempt to jump out, shrink down again,

and cover her eye with her hand, remain perfectly still and

motionless, thus enable Coverdale to devote his whole attention to

the horse. the terrified animal, after gallop nearly a mile, their

fear be keep alive by repeat flash of lightning and peal of

thunder, while a perfect deluge of rain convert the dusty road beneath

their foot into a morass, at length begin to relax their speed. as soon

as Harry perceive this to be the case, he turn to his companion,

say, “there, Miss Hazlehurst, I have get they in hand again, they’re

quite under command now, and the bad of the storm be over too, so you

needn’t be frighten any long; you have behave like a”--(regular

brick be the simile that rise to his lip, but he refrain, and

substituted)--“complete heroine, since you overcome that slightly insane

impulse to commit suicide by jump out.”



reassure by his manner, Alice venture to open her eye, and the

first use she make of they be to fix they upon the countenance of her

companion, strive to read therein whether the hope with which he

seek to inspire she be true or false. but Harry’s be a face about

which there could be no mistake; truth and honesty be write in every

feature so legibly, that the veri tyro in physiognomy could not fail

at once to perceive they.



“how fortunate it be that you be drive, and not Mr. Crane!” be

the first word Alice utter; “we should have be overturn to a

certainty if the horse have behave so this morning. I’ll take good care

not to let he drive I again. how cleverly you manage the creature

when they be plunge and rear! I should never have dare to whip

they while they be in that furious state, but it answer capitally.”



“you observe _that_, do you?” inquire Harry in a tone of surprise.



Alice favour he with a quick glance, as she reply, half archly,

half petulantly, “of course I do; what a stupid silly little thing you

seem to consider I!”



Harry pause for a minute ere he rejoin, laughingly, “you know nothing

about what I consider you, Miss Hazlehurst, and therefore I advise

you not to form any theory whatsoever on the subject, as they be

tolerably certain to be wrong one.”



“I dare say you have never give yourself the trouble to reflect at all

on so frivolous a topic,” return Alice; “I know your heterodox notion

in regard to our sex; you consider we all simpleton.”



“I’m sure I never tell you so,” be all the denial Harry’s conscience

permit he to make.



“not _vivâ voce_, perhaps,” reply Alice; “but I have hear it

second-hand from Master Tom: the boy be uncomplimentary enough before

you come, but he have be fifty time bad since you’ve be here to

encourage he in his impertinence.”



“a young cub!” mutter Harry aside, “I’ll twist his neck if he tell

tale out of school in this way;” turn to Alice, he continue, “it be

never too late to mend, be it? if I confess my sin, promise never to

do so any more, and throw myself on the mercy of the court, be there any

chance of my obtaining forgiveness?”



“as far as I be concern, yes,” be the reply; “in consideration of

your service this afternoon, I graciously accord you a free pardon for

all past offence, and for the future we will try and be friend.” as

she speak she half playfully, half in earnest, hold out her hand. Harry

take it in his own, and shake it--even in a glove it be a nice, warm,

soft little hand, a kind of hand that it be impossible to relinquish

without give it a squeeze, at least such be Harry’s impression, and

he act upon it, although to do so be by no mean in accordance

with his principle; but he do not happen to be think about his

principle just then. by this time the storm, which have pretty well

exhaust itself by its violence, resign in favour of a lovely sunset;

and the horse having come to the conclusion that they have thoroughly

disgrace themselves, and behave with an equal disregard of principle

and propriety, trot steadily along under Coverdale’s skilful

guidance, like a pair of four-legged penitent, anxious to retrieve

their character. and Harry and Alice suddenly find a great deal to talk

about, and be quite surprised when they perceive themselves to be in

sight of the Grange; and the gentleman felt move by a sudden impulse

to declare that, despite its unpropitious commencement, he do not know

when he have have such a delightful drive, to which the lady reply that

it certainly have be very agreeable, an admission which she endeavour

to qualify by attribute her pleasurable sensation to the influence

of the set sun and the delicious coolness of the evening air--a

transparent attempt at deception that only render the truth more

obvious.



the next morning a groom bring back Sir Lancelot, together with a note

from Mr. Crane, say that he have contrive to get wet through on

his way to the inn, that he fear he have take cold, and therefore

consider it most prudent to return home for a day or two; add that

he should hope to be sufficiently convalescent to rejoin the party

at the Grange that day week, when a dinner be to be give by Mr.

Hazlehurst to some of the county magnate. his note wind up with an

elaborate inquiry as to whether Alice have experience any ill-effect

from the “atmospheric inclemency,” as he be pleased to style the

thunder-storm, accompany by an infallible specific against all

sore-throat, cold, hoarsenesse, and rheumatic affection, which that

young lady straightway commit to the waste-paper basket. there be

also a note for Horace D’Almayne, from which drop an inclosure that,

as the exquisite stoop to pick it up, look marvellously like a

cheque.



“A--really I find I must go to town--a--business of importance--can I

execute any little commission for you, Miss Hazlehurst? I’ve excellent

taste in riband, I assure you.”



“there, do you hear that!” observe Tom _sotto voce_ to Coverdale. “I

always think he’d be a counter-jumper!”



“Kate, must I accompany he?” inquire Arthur of his cousin, _sotto

voce_; “remember, if you send I from you now, we meet again as

stranger!” there be a moment’s struggle, and her colour go and

come--then in a cold, hard voice she answer, “yes, go!”



Arthur look at she; her feature might have be sculpture in marble,

so fixed and immovable be their expression. that look decide he; and

with set tooth and lower brow he rise and quit the room.



in less than half-an-hour he return, prepare for a journey; and

beckon Coverdale aside, begin, “Harry, I have a favour to ask of you.

I be oblige to go to town suddenly, in consequence of an affair which

have cause I some annoyance; but I shall come back for the dinner-party

on the ----th. crane will also return then; and from what I can make out,

Alice’s affair will be definitely settle one way or other. the more

I see of Crane, the more I perceive how thoroughly he and Alice be

unsuited; but my father appear obstinately bent on the match: and if

Alice be to refuse he, she will require all the support that can be

give she. my poor mother’s health be, as you be aware, so delicate,

that although she be as much averse to the match as any of we, we cannot

expect she to exert herself; indeed, our chief anxiety be to prevent she

attempt to do so. the whole thing will, therefore, fall upon I: and

your support and assistance will be invaluable. my father have take a

great fancy to you; and your opinion weigh with he more than you will

believe. I be sorry to perceive that you be bored to death here; but

I trust to your friendship to remain till after my return. be I tax

your kind feel too far?”



“my dear boy, don’t make pretty speech; for I can stand anything but

that,” be the reply. “as to stay here, I have no thought of go

away till you have do with I. in regard to be bore, I’m get

over that beautifully. your family be charming people. I’m becoming

use to woman’s society, and, in fact, find it’s not by any mean as

bad as imagination paint it; and when D’Almayne be fairly out of the

house, I really shall not care how long I remain in it; so will that

satisfy you?”



“my dear fellow,” rejoin hazlehurst, warmly, “there’s nobody like you

in the world! I’ve always say so, from the day that I first set eye on

you at Eton, when you thrash the bully of the form for strike I,

and then box my ear because I take a blow from a boy less than

myself, without return it. I shall never quite turn misanthrope while

I’ve you for a friend.”



“Misanthrope! no, why should you?” be the surprised rejoinder. “what

ail you, man?--you look ill and unhappy. it’s nothing in the money way,

be it? I’ve get a few odd thousand lie idle at my banker, that I

should really be oblige to you to make use of.”



Hazlehurst shake his friend’s hand heartily. “God bless you, old fellow!

I know you would.” he say; “but money cann’t help I: I must fight it

out alone. I shall be myself again by the time I return--till then,

good-by,” and wring Coverdale’s hand once more, he turn and be

go.



“Alice, here’s a treat! everybody’s go away except that horrid Harry

Coverdale!” exclaim Emily, in a tone of despair; “we shall have he

on our hand, talk stable, and wish we be dog and horse, for a

whole week! what be we to do with the creature?”



Alice turn her head to hide her heightened colour, as she reply,

in a tone of voice that be almost cross, “really, Emily, you should be

careful not to carry that absurd habit of your of laugh at everybody

too far. People will begin to call you flippant. Mr. Coverdale be so

good-natured that he be the easy person in the world to entertain.

surely, Arthur have a right to ask his friend to remain here without

consult you or I on the subject.”



“Phew!” whistle Emily, and a droll little parody of a whistle it be;

“the wind have change, have it? I suppose that be the thunder-storm

yesterday; not to mention a certain _tête-à-tête_ drive. take care,

Ally: recollect that sweet bird the Crane! what do the song say?” and

pop herself down at the pianoforte, she run her finger lightly over

the key, as she sing with mischievous archness:



                   “’tis good to be merry and wise,



                   ’tis good to be honest and true,



                   ’tis good to be off with the _old_ love



                   before you be on with the new.”



the party which sit down to dinner at Hazlehurst Grange on that day be

a very select one. Mr. Hazlehurst have drive over to the neighbouring

town on justice business, and having sentence certain deer-stealer to

undergo diver unpleasantnesse in the way of oakum-picking, solitary

confinement, and other such amenity of prison discipline, have

stay to reward virtue by dining with his brother-magistrate upon

orthodoxly-slaughter venison. accordingly, Mrs. Hazlehurst and the

three young lady, Harry Coverdale and Master Tom, sit down to

what Mrs. Malaprop would have term “quite a _tête-à-tête_ dinner”

 together;--a tame and docile curate, invite on the spur of the moment

to counterbalance Harry, having miss fire, owe to the untimely

repentance of a perverse old female parishioner, who be take poorly

and penitent simultaneously, have send her imperative compliment to

the Rev. B. A. A. Lambkin, and she would feel oblige by his come to

convert she at his very early possible convenience; to which serious

call he felt oblige to respond.



Coverdale have find himself in an unusual and peculiar frame of mind all

day; for perhaps the first time in his life he have feel disinclined to

active exertion; and have positively go the length of abstract from

the library a volume of Byron, and spend the afternoon lie under a

tree, read the _Bride of Abydos_. now his peculiarity take a new

turn; and, free from his incubus, D’Almayne, a sense of the domestic

and sociable suddenly spring up within he, and throw off all

reserve, he appear for the first time during his visit in his true

colour--that be, unaffected, courteous, kind-hearted, amusing, and

well-inform. in consequence possibly of this change, the dinner go

off most agreeably; and the absence of the Reverend Lambkin be mentally

decreed to be a subject of thanksgiving, by more than one member of the

party.



in the evening there be certain wasps’-nests to be destroy, about

which Harry have express much interest; but now he discover that he

have blister his heel on the previous day, by run in a tight boot;

and Tom, mightily discontent at his defection, be force to invade

the enemy’s country without the assistance of his ally. when Coverdale

rejoin the lady, Emily be read Tennyson’s _princess_ aloud, and

the moment he appear, she declare she be tired, and hand the book

to he, beg he to proceed; her mischievous intention be

thereby to overwhelm he with confusion, and derive amusement from his

consequent mistake. but she meet her match for once, as Harry, coolly

reply that he should have much pleasure, take the book and begin

read in a deep rich voice, with so much taste and feeling, that she

surprise soon change to admiration. after tea, music be propose, and

the moment Alice begin to sing Coverdale, for the first time since he

have be in the house, approach the piano, and actually turn over

the leave for she!



“that lovely _Là ci darem!_ ah, Alice! if we have but a gentleman’s voice

to take the second! why _don’t_ you sing, Mr. Coverdale?” exclaim

Emily, turn over the page of the duet.



“I’ll try what I can do if _you_ wish it,” be Coverdale’s quiet answer.



Alice, to whom he speak, glance at he in speechless surprise; but

emily, at once make up her mind that he be attempt a hoax, and

eager to turn the table upon he, resumed--



“Bravo! give I your seat, Alice, I’ll play the accompaniment for you

both.”



now the truth be, that Harry have be gift by nature with a rich

powerful voice and excellent ear, quality which the admiration of his

“set” at Cambridge have induce he to cultivate. when he first start

on his grand tour, he encounter at Florence the mother and sister of

an old college friend, and those be the day before he have foresworn

young lady’ society, he be let in for a mild flirtation with one

of the daughter. the “emphatic she” happen to be _fanatica per la

musica_. accordingly for three month Harry take lesson of the good

master in the place, and sing duet morning, noon, and night; at the end

of which period the “love one” bolt with a black-bearded native, who

call himself a count, and _be_ a courier. since which episode, Harry,

disgusted with the whole affair, and all connect with it, have chiefly

confine his singing to lyrical declaration that he would “not go home

till morning.” it will therefore be less a matter of surprise to the

reader, than it be to his audience at the Grange, that Coverdale

perform his part in the duet with equal taste and skill, and very much

well than Alice do her--that young lady pronounce her italian

with rather a midland-county accent than otherwise, although her sweet,

fresh, young voice, in great measure atone for this little peculiarity.



“why, Mr. Coverdale, what a charming voice you have, and how beautifully

you sing!” exclaim Emily, look at he as if she could not even yet

believe that it be possible he should have so distinguish himself.

“I think you be hoax we, and I sit down to play the duet for the

amiable purpose of expose your ignorance.”



“how do you acquire such a pure italian accent?” ask Mrs. Hazlehurst;

“it will be of the great advantage to my girl to sing with _you_.”



“I learn of an italian fellow when I be at Florence, and I suppose he

teach I to do the business all right,” be the careless reply.



“and you have be here more than a week,” continue Mrs. Hazlehurst,

“and allow Mr. D’Almayne to monopolise both the reading and singing

department, though he cannot fill either one quarter as efficiently as

you be able to do. you really be too diffident.”



“I don’t imagine diffidence to have have very much to do with it,”

 observe Kate Marsden, quietly raise her eye from her work (a crochet

purse with steel bead), and fix they on Coverdale.



Harry laugh slightly as with heighten colour he reply, “you be

too clever, Miss Marsden. I by no mean approve of be subject to

such subtle _clairvoyance_; however, I may as well honestly confess that

you be right, and that a feeling more akin to pride than to humility

have prevent my seeking to rival Mr. D’Almayne.”



“_we_ have find you out at last though,” return emily, “and I for

one will do my good to punish you for your idleness, by make you

sing every song I can think of. I don’t believe it be either pride or

humility which keep you silent--it be nothing but sheer idleness.”



“judge of her principle from her practice, I can readily believe Miss

Emily Hazlehurst must consider silence to result from some reprehensible

cause,” reply Coverdale, with a meaning smile.



of course Emily make a pert rejoinder, and of course Coverdale be

force to sing half-a-dozen more song, which, as he have by this time

get up the steam considerably, he do in a style which win he fresh

laurel but it be a remarkable fact, that from the moment in which

Harry begin to read aloud, Alice, although her attention have never

flag, have scarcely utter a single word--perhaps it be because she

think the more.









chapter IX.--CONTAINS little else SAVE MOONSHINE.



Mrs. Hazlehurst be so confirm an invalid as to be unable to walk,

even so short a distance as from the drawing-room to her own bed-room,

whither she be usually carry by either her husband or her son.

she be in the habit of retire at nine o’clock, but on the evening

refer to in the last chapter the clock chime the half-hour after

nine, and Mr. Hazlehurst have not return.



“Mamma, dear, you be look tired--you ought not to sit up so late!”

 exclaim Alice, who have be observe her mother attentively for some

minute. “do allow Evans to carry you up: papa be sometimes keep till

eleven o’clock at these magistrate’ meeting, you know.”



one great charm which Alice possess in Harry’s eye be her devotion

to her mother, for whom she entertain an affection which be, perhaps,

one of the strong feeling of her nature.



“I have rather wait, dear,” be the patient reply:--“the worthy Evans be

grow fat and old, and I be always afraid of his fall; and James be

very willing, poor lad, but he be so awkward that he rub I against

all the corner we pass, and only escape knock my brain out by a

succession of miracle.”



“if you would allow I to assist you, Mrs. Hazlehurst,” begin Coverdale,

in a hesitate voice, as though he be about to ask rather than to

confer a favour--“I be sure I could carry you safely; I have observe

exactly how Arthur hold you, and it would give I so much pleasure to

be of use to you.”



“you be very kind,” return Mrs. Hazlehurst, while a glow of grateful

surprise colour her pale cheek; “but I cannot bear to give you the

trouble--you do not know how heavy I be.”



“you do not know how strong I be, my dear madam,” be the good-natured

rejoinder; “allow I--that I think be right,” and raise the light

form of the invalid in his powerful arm he carry she, as easily and

tenderly as a mother would her child, to her room, where, carefully

deposit she in an easy-chair, he wish her good night, and leave she

without wait to receive she thank.



“Alice, love, Emily will stay and read to I--go down and tell Mr.

Coverdale how much oblige I be; he carry I as comfortably as if he

have be in the constant habit of do so for year. the kindness

of heart, and delicacy of feeling with which he make the offer, have

gratify I exceedingly; depend upon it he be an unusually amiable,

excellent young man.”



“he certainly appear in a new character to-night,” return emily,

laugh; “hitherto he have perform the modern Timon most naturally and

successfully. I wonder what make the creature take it into his head to

act the man--or rather the woman--hater! you’d well ask he, Alice,

perhaps he will tell _you!_--What go already!” she continue, glance

round the room. “well then, mamma dear, as there seem to be no more

fun forthcoming, let I give you your dose of Jeremy Taylor; that be our

present good book, I believe.”



a reproof for the levity with which Emily speak rise to her mother’s

lip; but Mrs. Hazlehurst be a sensible woman as well as a good

one, and so, be able to distinguish between the exuberance of high

spirit, and a scoffing turn of mind, she only murmur, “silly child,”

 and shake her head, with a reprove smile.



when Alice return to the drawing-room she at first imagine it to

be tenantless; but on look more attentively she perceive the tall

figure of Harry Coverdale stand with fold arm in the recess of one

of the window. so noiselessly do she enter that Harry, whose face be

turn away from the door, be not aware of her approach until she be

within a few yard of he. as with a sudden start he look round,

she be surprised to observe the trace of deep emotion visible on

his feature, which be usually characterise by an expression of so

completely opposite a nature. with a murmured apology for intrude on

he, Alice be about to withdraw, when Coverdale hasten to prevent

she.



“do not run away,” he say quickly, then continue, “you be surprised

to see I look sad; I think I should like, if you will permit I, to

tell you the cause. it be so seldom I meet with anybody to whom I can

talk about such thing--people in general would not understand I, but I

feel an instinctive certainty that you will. it be such a lovely night,

would you object to come out? your cousin, Miss Marsden, be already

enjoy the moonlight.” as he speak, he point to a white figure

pacing, with bent head and measure step, along a terrace-walk on the

further side of the lawn. throw a shawl over her head to protect

herself from the night dew, Alice signify her consent, and open one

of the french window, they descend into the garden. for some minute

they stroll on side by side without speak; the silence at length

become embarrass, Alice break it by observing--



“I must not forget to deliver mamma’s thank for your kindness. you

carry she so easily and carefully, she say, she could almost imagine

you must have be accustom to such an occupation before.”



Harry smile a melancholy smile. “that be what I be go to tell you

about,” he say, “only when it come to the point, I feel as if it be

impossible to begin. carry Mrs. Hazlehurst to-night bring back such

a flood of recollection!” he pause, then in a low tone continue:

“for many month before her death my own poor mother become perfectly

helpless, and I use to carry she like a child from room to room. I be

only seventeen when I lose she, and, except your brother, I have never

have any one to love since; and though Arthur be as good a fellow as ever

breathe, and all that one can wish a friend to be, yet somehow, whether

it be the difference between a man’s mind and a woman’s, or what, I

cannot tell, but there be thing I’ve never talk about with

anybody since my mother die, because I’ve feel that nobody else could

understand I. perhaps, if she have live, I might have be more what I

sometimes wish I be--less rough, and--but I do not know why I should

bore you with what must be singularly uninteresting to you.”



“pray go on,” reply Alice; “I have hear so much of you from Arthur,

that I always hope I should some day know you myself, and that we might

become friend; but--” here she stop, apparently embarrass how to

proceed.



Harry come to her assistance--“But when I do appear, I make myself so

disagreeable that you naturally repent ever having waste a thought

upon such an unamiable savage. be not that what you would have say?

well, you be quite right, I deserve that it should be so.”



there be a degree of regretful earnestness in his voice and manner

which touch Alice’s gentle heart, and she hasten to reply:--



“Nay, it be only that you do not know we; and--I think that silly Mr.

D’Almayne annoy you with his air and affectation; but I be sure you

will never be so--so--”



“brutish!” suggest Harry.



“so unjust to yourself again,” resume Alice.



“you be very kind--kind than I deserve by far,” reply Coverdale. he

pause, then continue, “I don’t think I be naturally such a bear; but

from childhood I have have to battle with the world on my own behalf. do

Arthur ever tell you any of my early history?”



“no; he often allude to it as curious, but say we ought to see you

first, and then we should understand you well and care more to hear

it,” be the simple reply.



Harry smile. “the only romantic episode in my career occur when I

be a very young boy,” he say, “so young, that if I have not hear

the story over and over again from the mouth of my late uncle, the

old Admiral, I should scarcely have remember it. to enable you to

comprehend the situation properly, I must trouble you with a few family

detail. my grandfather have two son--the Admiral the elder, and my

father the young. my father, when a lieutenant in a march regiment,

fall in love with a very pretty, amiable but portionless girl; my

grandfather desire he to marry an heiress; my father refuse, and

urge his affection for another; my grandfather grow imperative, my

father recusant; my grandfather storm, my father persist; and the

affair end by my father marry his lady-love, and my grandfather

disinherit he for so do. the natural consequence ensue: my

grandfather devote his fortune and influence to my uncle’s advancement,

and at the age of fifty he become an admiral; at the same age my father

find himself a captain, exist on half-pay, with a microscopic

pension and an incurable wound in his side, as reward for having

serve his country. ‘england expect every man to do his duty,’ and

occasionally recompense he for it with honourable starvation. as my

father’s health decrease his expense increase, unpaid doctor’

bill stare he in the face, and butcher and baker grow uncivil and

importunate.



“at my grandfather’s death he leave every farthing he possess to his

eld son. angry at the injustice, my father refuse his brother’s

offer of an allowance, and unwisely determine to dispute the will.

accordingly, he not only lose his cause, but irritate my uncle to

such a degree, that all communication cease between they. when I be

approach the august age of ten year, and affair seem to be come

to a crisis, by some chance I, play with and apparently absorb by

a regiment of tin soldier, happen to be present at a family committee

of way and mean. during this colloquy, the unfortunate disagreement

between the brother be talk over and lament by my mother; who

exert all her eloquence to persuade my father to write to this Admiral

and inform he of his fail health and ruin fortune, and trust to

his generosity to forgive and forget the past. but my father’s pride

stand in the way. he would willingly have be reconcile to his

brother, _if_ he have not require pecuniary assistance at his hand; but

the consciousness of this necessity render he inexorable. so find

his wife’s argument unanswerable, he adopt the usual resource in

such case--viz., he talk himself into a rage, and fling out of

the room, slam the door behind he, leave my mother and I

_tête-à-tête_.



“after a minute’s silence, I surprise she by ask, ‘papa’s very poor,

and my uncle’s very rich; and papa would ask uncle to give he some

money, only they quarrel when grandpapa stop papa’s pocket-money:

ben’t that it, mamma?’



“‘yes, my dear,’ be the reply; ‘but you must not talk about it to

anybody remember.’



“I nod assent, then resume, ‘Uncle’s a good, kind man, ben’t he?’



“‘yes, my love; a good man I know he to be, and he be kind once,’ be

the reply.



“‘then why don’t you go and tell he that papa’s very sorry he be

naughty, and want to make friend again; and if uncle be good and kind,

he will say yes; and when they be friend again, uncle will be sure to

give he some of his pocket-money without be ask, because they be

brother. willn’t that do, mamma?’



“my mother rise with tear in her eye, stroke the hair back from my

forehead, imprint a kiss on it, and murmur, ‘your papa would never

allow I to do so, darling,’ quit the room.



“well, I sit and cogitate the matter: even as a child I be of a

fearless nature, and confident in my own resource; and at last a plan

occur to I. at that time we live in London, and I attend a public

school as a day-scholar. at this school I have a friend--a boy some

two or three year old than myself. to he, in strict confidence, I

impart my scheme, which he be pleased graciously to approve of,

and in which he volunteer to aid I. accordingly, on the following

morning, when my parent imagine I be decline _hic, hæc, hoc_, I

be, under the able guidance of my school-fellow, make my way to the

office of a coach which pass within half a mile of Coverdale Park.

having see I set off in high health and spirit, my friend after

school-hour leave the follow note at our house:--



“‘dear Mamma,--I have go to see my uncle Coverdale, as you could

not do it. Papa never tell I not to--so he willn’t be angry with _I_.

Thompson see I off, and will leave this, so no more at present,



“‘from your dutiful son,



“‘H. C.’



“I reach Coverdale Park without adventure, and greatly astonishing a

solemn butler by demand to see my uncle forthwith, be usher into

a large oak-panel apartment, wherein sit a fine, portly-look

gentleman, eat his dinner in solitary dignity. as soon as his eye

fall upon my feature he start, exclaiming--



“‘bless my soul, boy! who be you?’



“‘your nephew Harry Coverdale, uncle,’ return I, look he full in

the face. my gaze seem rather to embarrass he, for his lip move

convulsively ere he be able to frame a reply. at length he exclaim

angrily--



“‘and pray, sir, what do you want here?’



“feel by no mean incline to enter abruptly upon family affair in

presence of the servant, I pause. but certain inward craving, arouse

by the sight of the good thing before I, soon furnish I with an

idea, and with a decidedly suggestive emphasis, I answer, ‘I have

_not_ have any _dinner_ yet.’ my uncle again look at I, to see whether

my observation be the result of impudence or simplicity--decide

apparently in favour of the latter, he desire the servant to place I

a chair, and give I a knife and fork. fortify by a good dinner, and

encourage by a kind twinkle in the corner of my uncle’s eye, which

belie all his attempt to look angry, I soon begin to chatter away

freely, and enlighten my newly-find relative as to my opinion of thing

in general. after the cloth be remove, and I have volunteer grace; at

which my uncle appear first surprised and then edify, he began--



“‘now, boy, tell I the truth--but first, you shall have a glass of

wine; which will you take?’



“‘I always tell the truth, uncle, even if it get I a thrashing; and

I’ll take port, for that’s the only wine fit for a gentleman,’ answer

I, which reply so delight my uncle, that he pour I out a bumper,

and pat I on the back exclaimed--



“‘Bravo, my boy! stick to truth and port wine through life, and you’ll

be a credit to your name!’



“that speech of mine win the day! I explain the object of my visit,

and that it have originate wholly with myself; and succeed so

well, that on the follow morning my uncle accompany I home, be

reconcile to my father, to whom, till the day of his death (which

occur within the next year), he show every kindness, and after that

event take my dear mother to reside with he at the Park, provide for

my education, and eventually make I his heir.”



to this recital, follow by a detail of many of those pure thought and

deep feeling which lie hide in the breast of every generous-hearted

man, till heaven bless he with a female friend worthy to receive such

sacred confidence, do Alice listen with grow interest and sympathy;

and when, two hour afterwards, Mr. Hazlehurst return home in a great

state of universal vinous philanthropy, Harry and his companion

could scarcely believe they have be walk together for more than

half-an-hour.



the week pass away like a dream. Harry walk, and drive, and sing,

and read poetry with the young lady, and find himself especially

happy and comfortable. moreover, he contrive to institute a system of

romantic ramble with Alice, during which they talk about all

those peculiar subject which can only be discuss comfortably in a

_tête-à-tête_--thoughts and feeling too delicate to be submit to the

rough handling of a crowd. and Alice, after three day’ experience,

tell Kate Marsden, in strict confidence, that she have form the high

opinion of Mr. Coverdale’s principle; that he be so good and sensible,

and in every way superior to the young man one generally meet, that it

be quite a privilege to possess his friendship--didn’t Kate think

so? to which Kate reply in the affirmative; add, that girl

be usually so frivolous and empty-headed that they be not worth

cultivating. “where be the good of make friend of people, unless one

could look up to they?” Alice respond, “where, indeed!” and consider

that Kate take a very proper and sensible view of the matter.



one small incident occur, however, which somewhat ruffle the smooth

surface of Alice’s tranquillity. two or three day after the picnic,

there arrive from Mr. Crane a note, together with a slim and genteel

quadrupe, possess a greyhound-like outline, shadowy leg, and a long

tail, and purport to be a thoroughly-break lady’s horse, with which

the cotton-spinner begged--“Miss Alice would allow he to replace

the pony injure by the furious riding of her brother and Mr.

Coverdale,”--an association in iniquity which delight Tom as much as

it provoke Harry, and, secretly, Alice also. this horse Mr. Hazlehurst

insist upon it Alice should not refuse; and he become so angry when a

faint remonstrance be attempt, that the poor girl quit his study

in tear--a melancholy fact, which emily, in a truly feminine and

injudicious burst of virtuous indignation, reveal to Coverdale,

thereby lay in he the foundation of a deeply-root aversion to the

animal, which lead to result that would have be well avoid.



the morning follow the arrival of this undesirable addition to the

family, Mr. Hazlehurst announce his intention of ride over to call

upon and inquire after Mr. Crane, and his wish (which mean command)

that Alice should accompany he on her new horse. “Mr. Coverdale, will

you ride with we?” continue the head of the family, graciously; “I do

not think you have see Crane Court yet. the scenery in and around the

park be very rich, and the view from the terrace most extensive.”



Harry, in his secret soul dislike Mr. Crane and all that appertain

to he, and fancy, moreover, that the presence of Mr. Hazlehurst

would effectually neutralise the pleasure of Alice’s society, as their

conversation would be thereby restrict to unmeane commonplace, be

about to invent some polite reason for decline, when, happen to

glance at the young lady in question, he read--or imagine he read,

something in the expression of her countenance, which induce he to

alter his determination. accordingly, Tom be make happy by obtain

permission to go to the village-inn, where Coverdale’s horse be put

up, order the groom to saddle Sir Lancelot, and ride that exemplary

quadrupe back, as a reward for his trouble.



“how do you like Mr. Crane’s present to my daughter? in my opinion it

be one of the most perfect lady’s horse I have ever see,” complacently

remark Mr. Hazlehurst to Coverdale, as they stand at the hall door,

criticise the horse which a groom be lead up and down.



“I dare say the old gentleman”--(Mr. hazlehurst’s brow darkened)--“paid

a high figure for the animal,” be the reply; “it have its good point,

and be very well fit for a park hack; but it’s a weedy, straggle

sort of beast--showy action, but badly put together;--and there’s

something queer about its eye--it have an uncomfortable way of leer

round at you, and show the white, that I don’t like. you can see

it’s be feed under the mark, and I shouldn’t wonder if, now it’s on

full allowance, it be to turn out skittish.”



“I cann’t say I at all agree with you, Mr. Coverdale,” be the hasty

reply. “I flatter myself I know something of horse, and I consider this

as perfect a lady’s hack as I ever beheld, and a most valuable animal

into the bargain. as to temper, it’s as quiet as a lamb--a child might

ride it. I must beg you will not say anything which might tend to alarm

my daughter, or prejudice she against it.”



Harry turn away to hide a smile, as he reply, “never fear, sir;

Miss Hazlehurst shall form her own opinion of its merit, without my

attempt to bias her judgment.”



when Mr. Hazlehurst assist his daughter to mount, Harry, who really

doubt the temper of the animal, watch it closely, and his previous

opinion be confirm by observe that it lay back its ear, glance

viciously round, and at the moment when Alice spring up, make a faint

demonstration with its mouth, as though it covet a sample of Mr.

Hazlehurst from the region of that gentleman’s coat-tail, and be only

restrain from attempt to obtain one by a recollection of former

punishment. the preliminary arrangement be safely accomplish, the

trio start, follow by a mount groom, Coverdale keep close to

Alice’s bridle-rein.



they have proceed some distance, without anything occur to justify

his suspicion; and, in spite of all drawback, Alice be really

begin to enjoy her ride, when her father propose a canter; and on

quicken her pace, the odd manner in which her horse toss and shake

his head, in some degree alarm she.



“loosen the curb-rein a little,” suggest Harry, “and try to hold he

entirely by the snaffle. I will keep close to you, so do not be afraid,

lest he should bolt.” Alice comply, and the horse appear to approve

of the alteration, cease to shake its head; but as it become warm to

its work, it pull so hard against the snaffle, that Alice’s delicate

hand be unable to prevent the canter from increase into

something very like a gallop. Sir Lancelot keep pace with

he, stride for stride; but Mr. Hazlehurst’s short-legged cob--the

“dray-horse-in-miniature--warrant-equal-to-sixteen-stone” style

of animal, which elderly gentleman ride for the benefit of their

digestion, not be calculate for such fast work, be very soon

distance.



“what have become of papa?” exclaim Alice, glance round; “we ought

to wait for he, but I cann’t make this creature go slow--it pull

dreadfully. may I use the curb?”



“I have rather you do not,” be the reply; “the brute seem so uneasy

when you try it before--perhaps its mouth be tender; I will examine it

when you dismount. canter on to the next hill, and then we will stop for

Mr. Hazlehurst.” and they do so accordingly, though Alice be unable to

pull in her horse until Harry lean over and give she the assistance of

his strong arm.









CHAPTER X.--“EQUO NE CREDITE TEUCRI.”--(Virgil)



“why don’t you hold in your horse, Alice, and ride at a proper

lady-like pace, instead of tear along in that extraordinary manner?”

 inquire Mr. Hazlehurst, come up very red in the face, hot, and

discompose; both himself and the cob be entirely out of that useful

article, breath.



“I could not contrive to make he go slow, papa,” reply poor Alice,

timidly; “even now you see he be very fidgetty, and keep continually

pull.” this be perfectly true; for the horse, excite by its gallop,

begin to demonstrate its real character, and refuse to walk, sidle

along, toss its head impatiently, prick up its ear at every

sound, and look as if it be prepared to shy upon the very slightest

provocation.



“Pulling!--yes, of course it do,” rejoin Mr. Hazlehurst, angrily;

“you cann’t expect to hold a fine, high-courage animal like that with

the snaffle only--tighten the curb-rein directly. take care what you be

doing!--steady! horse, steady!--touch he with the whip on the shoulder.

bless I! she’ll be throw!”



while Mr. Hazlehurst be speak they have, in turn a corner, come

suddenly upon a wheelbarrow, in which be deposit two jacket and a

hat, belong to some man who be mend the road. the moment Alice’s

horse catch sight of this object it stop short, and as, in obedience

to her father’s direction, the frightened girl jerk the curb-rein,

and strike the animal with her whip, it rear, and at the same time

plunge round so suddenly as to unseat its rider. fortunately, Coverdale

have keep as near to she as possible, and by a quick motion of the

bridle-hand and touch with the spur, he cause his horse to turn at the

same moment as do that on which Alice be mount; he be thus enable

to pass his arm round her waist and prevent she from fall.



“be your foot clear of the stirrup?” he inquire, hastily. perceive

that it be so, he continue, “let go the rein, then, and trust yourself

entirely to I.” as he speak, the groom come up, and catch the bridle

of the plunge horse, lead it away; while Mr. Hazlehurst, descend

from his saddle with a great degree of celerity than might have be

expect from a man of his age and stoutness, receive his daughter

in his arm, and lift she to the ground;--for which feat of agility,

Harry, who be by no mean impatient to be relieve of his lovely

burthen, mentally anathematise he. then ensue a great confusion of

tongue; Mr. Hazlehurst, be himself chiefly to blame, evince his

penitence by accuse everybody else, especially the groom--an old

favourite retainer, who hold and express a strong ungrammatical

and illogical opinion, diametrically oppose to his master’s, on all

subject, divine, moral, and physical. at length, in utter despair of

attain any practical result, Harry, mutter to himself his surprise

that people would not adopt his system, and strike out for themselves a

quiet way of do thing, coolly take the matter into his own hand,

by shift Alice’s saddle to the back of the cob; when he have complete

this arrangement, and assist the young lady to mount, he politely

hold Sir Lancelot’s stirrup for the accommodation of Mr. Hazlehurst,

observing--



“he will carry you just as quietly and easily as your own horse, sir;

he be a hand or two high, certainly; but if you _should_ take a sudden

fancy to leap the next stiff fence you come to, he’ll carry you over it

like a bird; so you must set the good against the evil.”



“you’re very kind, sir. Ugh! what a height the brute is!”--(these

word accompany the effort of literally _climb_ to the

saddle)--“But--but--I’ve drop my pocket-handkerchief--thank-you. what

be you go to ride yourself?”



“I be go, if you have no objection, to find out why Mr. Crane’s

purchase dislike to pass that wheelbarrow, and to convince he that

there exist a strong necessity for his so do,” return Harry, with

his head under the flap of a saddle--he be engage in securing with

his own hand the girt around Alice’s discard steed, despite sundry

futile attempt at kick and bite institute by that unamiable

quadrupe.



“oh, Mr. Coverdale--please--pray do not attempt it!” exclaim Alice,

eagerly; “I’m sure the creature be vicious! you will be throw and hurt,

to a certainty!” Harry, thus apostrophise, emerge from beneath the

saddle-flap, and toss back his dishevelled hair, and replace his

hat, which for the great convenience of strenuous buckling he have

take off, cross over to Alice’s side.



“you be hold the rein twist Miss Hazlehurst,” he say; “let I

arrange they for you.” as he restore the rein properly place to

her grasp, somehow their finger become interlaced, and Harry appear

unable to disentangle his for some second; during which space of time,

Alice, blush and turn away her head, murmur imploringly--



“you _will_ not ride that creature!”



“your father will never be convince that the brute be unsafe for you

unless he see it in its true colour; besides, I dare say I shall have

no trouble in get it past the barrow--there be a quiet way of

do these thing,” be the confident reply. Alice still seek to

remonstrate, but in vain; for press her delicate finger as though he

be loath to relinquish they, Coverdale turn away with a gay smile,

and place his toe in the stirrup, vault lightly to his saddle.



having wait till Mr. Hazlehurst and his daughter have ride on a short

distance, Harry put his horse in motion, and prepare to follow they;

but the moment it catch sight of the alarm wheelbarrow, it again

stop short, and attempt to repeat its former manœuvre. willing to

try mild measure first, Coverdale, although he prevent the animal

from dash round as it have do when it unseat Alice, allow it

to turn, and ride it back a few pace, give it time to compose its

excited feeling, ere he again bring it up to the object of its fear.

as it approach the spot he keep it tightly in hand, and, when it begin

to waver, stimulate its flagging resolution by the most delicate hint

imaginable from his “armed heel.” the instant it feel the spur, it

swerve aside, dash round, and as soon as its head be turn in a

homeward direction, evince an unmistakable desire to bolt. Harry’s brow

grow dark. “lend I your whip,” he say, approach the servant, who

sat grin with the satisfaction usually display by professional

horseman on witness the discomfiture of an amateur rider--more

especially if the amateur happen to be a gentleman.



“you be too good-natured with he, Mr. Coverdale; you should give it

he hot and strong, sir. but law! that hanimal ain’t fit for lady and

gentleman; he want a reglar sharp rough-rider on his back, that’ll take

the nonsense out of he, he do.”



“your whip be too light; get down and cut I a good, tough ash stick out

of the hedge there. I will hold your horse,” be the only reply Harry

vouchsafed.



the man glance at his face in surprise, and see that he be in

earnest, hasten to execute his wish, return in two or three

minute with a couple of plant of ground-ash, about the thickness of

a finger. having carefully examine these, Harry select the one he

consider the most serviceable.



the groom watch he narrowly. “so you really mean business, eh, sir?”

 he say.



“I do,” be the concise reply, as, with compressed lip and flash

eye, Harry turn and ride off.



probably, from some instinctive consciousness that he be not to be

allow his own way without more serious opposition than he have yet

encounter, the horse, as he draw near the dread spot, display

strong sign of fear and ill-temper than before, stare from side to

side, with his ear in constant motion, arch his neck, and toss

the foam-flake from his mouth, as he impatiently champ the bit. the

moment he catch sight of the wheelbarrow, he swerve aside with a bind

which would have unseat any but a firstrate horseman, and attempt

his usual manœuvre of turn round. in this he be foil by an

unpleasantly sharp stroke on the side of the nose from the ash sapling,

which, oblige he to turn in an opposite direction, bring he again

in sight of the wheelbarrow, while a strong application of the spur

cause he to bind forward; thereupon he rear, but a crack over the

ear bring he down again; then he set to kicking, for which he be

reward by find his mouth violently saw by the snaffle-bit, while

a perfect tornado of blow from the ash stick be hail upon his flank

and shoulder. find this the reverse of agreeable, he, as a last

resource, rear till he stand perfectly erect, paw the air wildly

with his forefeet. but he have overshoot the mark.



at the conclusion of the previous struggle, the ash stick have break off

short in Coverdale’s hand; consequently, he be prevent from apply

the counter-irritation principle as before, and be only able, by

great quickness, to extricate his foot from the stirrup, ere the horse

overbalance itself, and fall heavily backwards. fortunately for his

own safety, Harry be unusually prompt and active in all situation of

danger; and, in the present emergency, these quality stand he in good

stead. although, of course, unable entirely to free himself from the

fall animal, he contrive to slip aside, so that it should not fall

upon he; and almost as soon as the frightened creature have regain

its leg, he also have spring up, apparently unhurt, and leap upon its

back. but the fight be win. completely cow by its fall, and weary

out by the pertinacity of its rider, the conquered animal permit

Coverdale to ride it backwards and forwards past the dread

wheelbarrow, approach nearer at each turn, until at length he make

it pause, with its nose within half-a-yard of the alarming jacket, and

discover for itself that they be make of fustian, of the most innocent

quality, and flavour with the usual cottage smell of bacon and wood

smoke.



elate with his success, he rejoin Alice and her father, say, as

he do so, “well, Miss Hazlehurst, I tell you there be a quiet way of

tame the dragon, and you see I be right.”



Alice, who be very pale and trembling, murmur something about she

“rejoice that he be not hurt.” but Mr. Hazlehurst, who appear

unusually cross and grumpy, reply, “if that’s what you call a _quiet

way_ of enforce obedience, Mr. Coverdale, all I can say be, I pity any

poor creature that happen to be under your control!”









CHAPTER XI.--“POST EQUITEM SEDET ATRA CURA.”--(Horace)



Mr. Hazlehurst, in his position of father of a family, have be so long

accustomed to consider his will law, that the possibility of his be

in the wrong be one which he never contemplate; the fact, therefore,

of any one having prove he to be so, constitute in his eye a

high and unpardonable misdemeanour. of this capital crime have Harry

Coverdale, on the occasion just describe, be guilty; and Mr.

Hazlehurst, albeit outwardly he resume his usual manner towards

his guest, could not in his secret soul either forget or forgive his

offence--more especially as the circumstance of Mr. Crane’s present

be demonstrate to be unsafe for a lady to ride (and that it be so,

even Mr. Hazlehurst’s power of self-deception could not conceal

from he), be at that particular juncture of affair singularly

embarrassing. of this change of sentiment straightforward, unsuspicious

Harry never dream; accordingly, he continue to behave towards the

old gentleman as freely as he have hitherto do, maintain his own

opinion, even when they entirely differ from those of his host,

courteously, indeed, but with the sturdy independence natural to his

character--a sturdiness which, until it be exert in opposition to his

sovereign will and pleasure, Mr. Hazlehurst have particularly admire.

so for the rest of the week affair (with this single exception) go on

most agreeably and satisfactorily to all party.



Harry, having once break the ice, contrive speedily to win the good

opinion (to use no strong term) of all the female portion of the

community. from the kind attention he pay Mrs. Hazlehurst, he soon

acquire so much influence over that amiable lady that, to please he,

she consent to various scheme devise for her benefit and amusement,

which her daughter have previously urge upon she in vain;--for

instance, when Harry, instruct by Alice in regard to time and season

and the like minor particular, come at the _very_ moment when she be

go to decide that she do not feel equal to go out at all that

day, to tell she that the pony-phaeton be wait at the door, and

that he should really think her unkind, and imagine he must have do

something to offend she, if she refuse to allow he the pleasure of

carry she to the chaise, and drive she just far enough to do she

good, and not to tire her,--what could she do but consent? _Ce n’est que

le premier pas qui coûte_. this point gain, if be easy to persuade

the invalid to take a short excursion daily; and as her complaint be in

some degree on the nerve, the beneficial effect of the fresh air and

exercise soon become apparent. moreover, as Alice know how to drive a

little, and wish to improve in that useful accomplishment, Harry could

do no less, when he have bring Mrs. Hazlehurst safely home from her

daily drive, than take out the young lady, and give she a lesson; and as

these lesson usually last some two hour at a stretch, the fat

pony begin to get into excellent working condition, and consider

themselves put upon, wonder why the Society for the Prevention of

Cruelty to Animals neglect to interfere in their behalf. emily, too,

have quite alter her opinion of their guest, and entirely sympathise

with Tom’s declaration that he be “a stunning good fellow, and no

mistake!” Kate Marsden say little, but observe the progress of event

with calm approval; for she perceive that to be go on, which would

greatly facilitate the execution of certain scheme which she have

devise.



at length arrive the important day of the dinner-party. be we

call upon to define the meaning of the term dinner-party, we should

denominate it an awful immolation of mind to matter, a wanton sacrifice

of the head to the stomach. why, on a hot summer’s day, eighteen

individual, suppose to be in their proper sense, who might dine at

home if they choose, should agree of their own free-will to victimise

themselves and each other by congregate together in one room, for the

space of two mortal hour, to eat--and, in the case of the lord of the

creation, probably to drink also--a great deal more than be good for

they, be one of those social problem of which we expect to arrive at

the solution about the time when mankind be thoroughly regenerate by

Miss Martineau’s _a_theological view (to coin a word), but not before.



if there be no other argument against this insane system of monster

dinner-party, the frightful state of discomfort into which the family

of the giver of the feast be throw by the come event, would alone
