be sufficient to prove our case. unless the establishment be on a scale

proportionable to that of the individual who, on find the number of

his guest exceed the mean of conveyance provide for they, coolly

order round “more phaeton!” anarchy and confusion reign predominant

throughout the devoted mansion for at least four-and-twenty hour before

the affair come off. in the first place the servant, male and female,

all go mad; if you give an order, the recipient stare you vacantly in

the face, and do something else immediately; if you lay down a book,

or any similar article, in its proper place, somebody instantly remove

it and hide it in an improper one, where you be fortunate if you

stumble upon it by accident in the course of the follow six month.

the lunacy of the servant react upon their better--everybody be a

little out of temper, everybody be over-officious, and have a way of his

or her own for do everything diametrically oppose to the variously

diverge way of everybody else. from the early dawn the very

garret be redolent of “make soup,” which odour remain in possession

of the house till about the time at which luncheon should be, but of

course be not, forthcoming, when it be supersede, and retire _vice_

the venison put down to roast, which we would rather decree should be

“put down” as a nuisance--at least, as far as regard our olfactory

nerve. but it be an endless task to attempt to sum up all the

misery incidental to the preparation for celebrate one of those

“feast of _un_-reason,” nor do we expect very many of the gentle public

to sympathise in our view; for in every society which we have as yet

frequent, “_L’Amphitryon où l’on dine_,” though he be heavy as his own

dinner, be certain to be a popular man.



however this may be, one thing be certain, that Harry Coverdale, on the

morning precede the dinner-party at the Grange, experience in his

proper person many of the inconvenience allude to, and having make

several attempt to improve his position, by seek to induce somebody

to do something sensible or agreeable, all of which prove abortive, by

reason of the impossibility of extract even Alice from the vortex of

preparation--Harry Coverdale, thus victimise, _faute de mieux_, mount

his good steed, and set off to ride away from the blue devil; but the

remedy do not succeed--the devil follow he, and grow bluer and

bluer with every mile he pass over, and, at last, the bluest of they

all assume the likeness of Mr. Crane!



“confound Mr. Crane!”--thus run Harry’s thoughts--“confound the old

fellow! he’s come to marry Alice--my nice, warm-hearted little friend,

Alice! I don’t by any mean approve of it. he’s old enough to be

her father, or anybody else’s, for that matter: the thing be

ridiculous--quite absurd!--Besides, the dear little girl dislike

he--naturally she do: there’s nothing to like in he. why, she care

more about I than she do about he!” he pause in thought, remove

his hat, push back his thick, clustering hair, put his hat on again,

and continue: “I declare, if I’d not entirely make up my mind against

marrying, I’d enter for the stake myself, and see if one could not

jockey the old fellow and governor Hazlehurst too. Alice be a prize well

worth win; but it’s too late to change one’s mind! I ought to have

behave differently to she at first, if I’d want she to fall in love

with I--though I think I’ve get over all that pretty thoroughly, too.

ah! well, I’ve choose my line, and must stick to it; and as the shooting

season ben’t so very far off now, thank goodness, I shall contrive to

make it out somehow, I dare say. and, by Jove, there’s a whole pack of

bird sun themselves in that great field--five or six covey all get

together--and stunning good covey they must be, too. there’s a gap

in the hedge; I’ll leap over and see if I can get near enough to

count they. now, Lancelot--steady, sir!--you _must_ do it--over we go!

famously clear! I wouldn’t take five hundred guineas for you, you

beauty! that I wouldn’t. we’ll show some of ’em the way across country

when the hunting begin; willn’t we astonish their weak mind for they,

rather!” and so, pat and caress his horse, Harry make a wide

circuit, and avail himself of the shelter of a belt of tree,

contrive to get near enough to the partridge to count they; by which

process he arrive at the interesting discovery that there be exactly

thirty brace, with one bird over; which ornithological irregularity

rather distressed and provoke he, though why it should have do so

neither he nor, as we imagine, any one else, could possibly conceive.



but the partridge be count, back come the blue devil in great

force than ever: and his thought grow so troublesome, not to say

unbearable, that Harry begin to imagine he must be bewitch--a

supposition in which, perhaps, he be not so very far wrong after all.

as a last refuge against his persecutor, he resolve on a good gallop;

and so make his way across country, a distance of some eight mile, as

straight as the crow fly, leaping gate and crash through hedge in

a very reckless and steeple-chase kind of manner, which obtain for

he a more than sufficient amount of hard british swearing from sundry

irate member of the Agricultural Association, who, for once in their

life, have a real grievance to complain of. as he clear the last fence

lead into the park in which the Grange be situate, the village

clock strike six, and he could perceive a carriage, with the Crane

livery (green turn up with yellow), wind slowly through the

tree. three minute more find he in the stable-yard, and fling

the bridle of his reeking steed to his groom, while he utter the hasty

caution, “you see the state he’s in; take proper care of he,” he make

his way to his bedroom by a back staircase, overturn a water-can, and

run into the arm of a pretty housemaid (to whom he apologise by

mention that he be sorry he be in too great a hurry to give she a

kiss), in the course of his rapid career. and so, very hot, very

dusty, considerably tired, and with a most unromantic appetite, he set

vigorously to work to (as servant inelegantly, but graphically term it)

clean himself.



when, some twenty minute afterwards, Coverdale reach the

drawing-room, he find all the guest assemble. many of they, to whom

he be personally know, immediately claim acquaintance, recognise

he in spite of the improvement which his residence abroad have work

in his manner and appearance. some two or three of the young man be

old college chum, who be really overjoy to see he again, and who

immediately gather round he and besiege he with question. Glancing

round the circle, he perceive D’Almayne bend tenderly over Alice;

but the sight no long annoy he--he have get over _that_. Alice

see the exquisite in his true colour; Alice have laugh at he--poor

D’Almayne! but on her other hand sit the cotton-spinner, and he be

more formidable; for he do not (fortunately for himself) depend on

his personal attraction alone--there be twenty thousand solid good

reason per annum why he should not be refuse; reason which render

his alliance with Mr. Hazlehurst’s family so desirable, that all that

gentleman’s paternal authority be certain to be stretch to its

uttermost limit to enable Mr. Crane to carry his point. moreover, as

Harry enter the drawing-room, Tom have give he the follow note:--



“_dear Hal,--I have write to tell the governor that I shall be

detain in court so late that it will be impossible for I to get away

to-night (the truth, you heretic!). I shall start by the first train

to-morrow, and be with you to breakfast. keep a sharp look-out upon the

cotton-spinner; and if at any moment he appear as if he be prepare

to pop, throw a book at his head without hesitation! so will you

continue to deserve the good opinion of,



“Arthur H._”



at dinner, Coverdale be seat next a fast young lady, who rather make

love to he than otherwise; but she do not take much by her motion,

for Harry have a good deal of business on his hand. first, there be his

appetite to satisfy, and the monster be very insatiate after his gallop

across country; next, he feel it incumbent upon he to keep a strict

watch over Mr. Crane and Alice, who be seat nearly opposite to he;

and he seriously debate in his own mind whether a finger-glass might

not be consider a legitimate substitute for a book, on one or two

occasion, when the cotton-spinner appear to be attempt the

excessively tender. good eating require good drinking; thirst demand

Pale Ale, etiquette oblige Champagne, and the mixed duty of society

necessitate Port and Sherry; Hock be very refreshing in hot weather; it

be no use to hand round Curaçoa, if people willn’t drink it; Hermitage and

Lunel be so nice, that everybody take they; Claret be a necessity in

all properly order establishment; and if your host produce a bottle

of good old Burgundy, he must be a fool who refuse to taste it. but for

a man to do all this, and at the same time to think, feel, and express

himself as coolly and prudently as he would after a mutton-chop and

a glass of table-beer, would require he to possess a brain make of

cast-iron, and no heart at all, and such be by no mean the physical

conformation of our hero. Harry, however, possess a good strong head

of his own; and although, as dessert proceed, his eye grow bright,

and he involuntarily emulate D’Almayne by smile frequently, and

unconsciously display an even row of white tooth, these peculiarity

only serve to make he look especially handsome. but the wine do

something else; for, as the lady rise to leave the room, it inspire

he with a determination to jockey D’Almayne, who usually usurp

the privilege of open the door on such occasion--a “cut out”

 expedition which Harry conduct with equal skill and success. as Alice,

who come last, pass he, some spirit (whether of wine, or another

equally favourite theme for minstrel’s lay, we cannot tell) urge he to

bend his head, and whisper the inquiry, “have you be happy with your

delightful companion?”



a contemptuous smile, and a slight negative motion of the lip answer

the question; and, for a moment, their eye meet. Alice’s must have be

a singularly expressive glance, for Harry read therein that she be

anxious and dispirit, but feel a vague and general reliance on his

willingness and ability to afford her comfort and protection.



have Mr. Crane know the exact feeling with which Coverdale grasp

a finger-glass, and mentally calculate the amount of force it would

require to launch the missile against the chinchilla-crown head of

his opposite neighbour, that worthy man would scarcely have venture to

continue his mild and meaningless prosing so contentedly.









CHAPTER XII.--HARRY put his foot in it.



the moment Harry reseat himself at the dining-table, two of his old

college friend place themselves beside he, and plunge at once into

recollection of “auld-lang-syne,” completely monopolise he. the

sound of his own name eagerly pronounce, rouse he at length from an

interesting reminiscence of how gloriously drunk Jones of Magdalen have

be at Tippleton’s wine-party (when he _would_ sing a pathetic ballad,

begin, “there’s a wail on the mountain!” and be stop by a roar

of laughter, chorus the inquiry, “how the deuce it--the whale--get

there?”). the speaker be Mr. Hazlehurst. “excuse my interrupt your

conversation for a few minute, Mr. Coverdale,” he begin, “but we want

your opinion. you’ve travel and see the working of different tariff

regulation, and have opportunity of compare the prosperity of

other nation with that of our own, while at the same time you be a

sufficiently large land-proprietor to give you a stake in the country,

and to induce you to feel a strong interest in the general prospect

of the agricultural population. I be sure you must agree with I in

consider protection a most essential and salutary measure.”



“if I might be allow to make just one observation before Mr. Coverdale

favour we with his view on this important question,” insinuate

Mr. Crane, in the mild and most affectionate tone of voice

imaginable--wine always reduce this excellent man to a state of weak

and inappropriate philanthropy--“if I might observe that, with the

high respect for, and admiration of, the agricultural population

of this great country, I feel it incompatible with my feeling as a

protestant, and therefore, so to speak, in a general way as a brother,

not to say as a man also, and more particularly as a mill-owner, to

forget the thousand of operative who crowd our large city, and who,

when satisfied with cheap bread, add to the dignity and prosperity

of the nation; but, on the contrary, when deprive of this mean

of support, object to resign themselves to the dispensation of a

beneficent providence, and fly in the face of society as chartist,

leveller, red-republicans, and all that be dangerous and subversive of

morality and security of property. if I may so far presume as to call

Mr. Coverdale’s attention to the desirableness of provide food at

a rate which will enable the manufacturing class to exist without

constantly work themselves up into a state of illegal desperation, I

shall feel that I have, if I may be allow the expression, unburthene

my conscience.” thus say, Mr. Crane cast a timid and appealing look

from Harry to his host, and sip a glass of Burgundy with the air of a

man apologise for some misdeed.



“it be not a subject upon which I have ever expend any vast amount

of consideration,” begin Coverdale, wish in his secret soul that he

might have the feeding of Mr. Crane for the ensue six month

entrust to he, in which case he would have afford that gentleman

an opportunity of practically test the merit of _very_ cheap bread

indeed, and of nothing else--except, perhaps, cold spring water; “but

the common sense of the matter appear to lie in a nutshell: the two

great division of the poor class be the manufacture poor and

the agricultural poor, the manufacturer be the most numerous--to

sacrifice one to the other be unfair, but to offer up the great to the

less be ridiculous. free-trade have have a fair trial, and have be prove

to benefit the masse, though it lie heavily on the land-owner. well,

then, relieve land of its burthen, and make the income-tax permanent

to reimburse the Exchequer. that’s the line I should take if I be

Premier, which, thank heaven, I’m not.”



as Harry conclude, two or three man begin to speak at once, but Mr.

Hazlehurst, by a solemn wave of the hand, immediately silence they.

that excellent magistrate have drink more wine than be by any mean good

for he; his constitution be gouty, and he have not have a fit for some

time; before such attack he be usually as irritable as though his

brain be a hedgehog, and society at large a pack of wire-haire

terrier attempt to unroll it. Claret be the most unwholesome wine

he could take, and on the evening in question he have imbibe nearly

a bottle thereof; but of all this _dessous des carte_, Harry be

innocently unconscious.



“I beg your pardon, gentleman,” begin Mr. Hazlehurst, solemnly, “but the

right of reply at present rest with myself. moreover, if my ear do

not deceive I, Mr. Coverdale have make an observation which I must call

upon he either to explain or retract; but in the first place, let

I express my surprise and regret, sir,” here he address himself

pointedly to Harry, “that a young man in your position, a large

land-proprietor, a lover of field-sport, possess a practical

knowledge of land, and a personal acquaintance with the habit and

custom of the agricultural poor--the bone and sinew of our country,

should thus turn against and betray the interest of the class to

which he belong, and league himself with those who would, in their

shortsightedness, sap the vital of that free and independent character

which have make we the great nation that we be. with regard to the

observation to which I allude, I believe, that having stigmatise

the opinion I hold as a sacrifice of the great to the less, you

deliberately pronounce those opinion ridiculous. have I not repeat

your word correctly?”



“I certainly say that to sacrifice the great number to the less would

be ridiculous,” return Harry, completely take aback at this sudden

and unexpected accusation; “but I only meant--”



“you mean what you say, I presume?” interpose Mr. Hazlehurst, in the

magisterial tone of voice in which he be accustom to cross-examine

and be down upon equivocate poacher.



“of course I do,” return Harry, his eye flash as he observe a

sarcastic smile upon the face of Horace D’Almayne. “I always mean what I

say; but my remark relate solely to general principle, and have not the

small reference to you personally, sir.”



“which be equivalent to say, that I do not understand the common

meaning of word,” return Mr. Hazlehurst, in the same irritate tone

of voice. “really, Mr. Coverdale, your explanation do not tend to do

away with the unfavourable impression your observation force upon I.”



“it be equivalent to nothing of the kind, sir,” rejoin Harry, lose

his self-command as a second glance at D’Almayne reveal the fact that

he be hide a laugh behind an elaborately-work cambric handkerchief;

“but if you choose to put a wrong construction upon every word I

utter, it be useless for I to discuss the matter far with a man

so--a--so----”



at this critical moment, Tom Hazlehurst, who have be listen with a

countenance of blank dismay to the altercation between his father and

his friend, contrive, either by accident or design, to throw down

and break a valuable china plate. this incident create a diversion by

call forth an outburst of parental wrath, under cover whereof Harry

regain sufficient self-control to enable he to suppress the word

“wrong-headed,” with which he have be on the point of conclude his

sentence. at the same time, Mr. Crane, have a mortal antipathy to

anything like quarrelling, which, as he say, produce “an insalubrious

agitation of his nervous system,” or, in plain English, frighten he

out of his wit, suggest that they should join the lady--a proposal

which lead to a general move. five minute’ reflection, in an atmosphere

less oppressive than that of the heated dining-room, cause Harry to

perceive that, by having allow himself to be provoke by the obstinacy

of a pig-head and slightly tipsy old gentleman into even a momentary

forgetfulness of the respect due to Mr. Hazlehurst’s year and

position, he have act wrongly and foolishly. it moreover occur to

he, now that it be too late to be of the slight use, that owe to

this unfortunate disagreement he must have completely neutralise any

influence he might have possess with his host, and thus, in fact,

frustrate the whole purpose of his visit: by which mean Arthur would

be vex, and the possibility of Alice’s marriage with Mr. Crane rather

increase than otherwise. just as he be about to exchange the cool

air of the garden (whither on leave the dining-room he have betake

himself) for the less agreeable temperature of a crowded drawing-room, he

be pat on the shoulder by one of his college acquaintance.



“ah, Knighton! what be it man?” Observed Harry, wish his dear

friend at Jericho. “I take you for the stem of a tree, you stand so

motionless.”



“why the fact be, my dear fellow,” return Knighton, a well-dispose

goose, who, when Harry first commence his college career, have form

an enthusiastic attachment for he, in return for which he expect his

friend to advise he how to act and what to say upon every occasion,

trifle as well as important--a tax which even Harry’s good-nature

find somewhat oppressive, “the fact be, I consider it quite

providential, if I may say so, find you here to-night: you know I

always like to have your opinion before I make up my mind; there be

nobody with such good sense as you, at least, nobody that I’ve ever meet

with. my dear Coverdale, I’m go to take the most important step--that

be, if you see no reason against it, which I can scarcely feel a doubt

of; but I’ll tell you the whole affair, begin properly at the

beginning. when I be down in Hampshire three year ago----” but we will

not inflict Mr. Knighton’s amiable prolixity on the reader, suffice it

to say that, having link his arm within that of Coverdale, he parade

his victim up and down a gravel walk for the space of at least three

quarter of an hour, while he pour into his ear as dull a tale of

true love as ever run smooth: true love of the very mild quality,

which, from the beginning, be certain to end simply and naturally in

a stupid marriage, about the whole of which affair there could not by

possibility be two opinion. at length, when Harry have agree, with

everything and to everything at least twice over, and strongly advise

his tormentor to act as he feel certain he would have do if his advice

have be just the other way (for this young man, although he eagerly

seek counsel, by no mean consider himself bind to walk thereby),

it suddenly occur to Mr. Knighton that he be do an unkind thing

by his friend, and a rude one by his host, in not soon join the

lady; accordingly, at (literally) the eleventh hour, he exercise thus

much self-denial, viz. have nothing more to say, he say it.



when Coverdale enter the drawing-room, he cast round his eye to

discover what might have become of Alice and Mr. Crane, and fail to

perceive they, be about to find some excuse for make his way into the

boudoir beyond, when Emily pounce upon he to entreat he to sing for

the edification of some dear Mary Jane or other, who be die to hear

he; and the very identical Mary Jane herself second the request in

a mild, insinuating, blatant tone of voice, as of some bashful but

persuasive sheep, there remain nothing for he but to consent, which

he do with a very ill grace indeed. having dash through a tender and

sentimental italian love-ditty in a ferocious, not to say sanguinary,

style, he declare he be so hoarse that he could not sing another note,

and again make an attempt to enter the boudoir, which he succeed

in reach just in time to see Alice quit the room with a heighten

colour, and in a manner which betoken hurry and agitation, while Mr.

Crane remained gaze after she with a countenance indicative of the

deep and most helpless bewilderment. from these symptom Harry

rightly conjecture that while he have be off duty the cotton-spinner

have pop; but whether his offer have be accept or reject he be

utterly unable to divine. Mr. Crane look stupid and puzzle-pat--but

_that_ he be sure to do in any case. for the rest of the evening

Coverdale be in a fearful state of mind; people stay late, and it

seem to he as if everybody have enter into a league to worry and

torment he. first, the young lady who have sit next he at dinner get at

he again, and flirt at he so violently, that (his thought run

entirely on marry and give in marriage) he become possessed of a

nervous dread lest she should be go to make he an offer--this idea

gain confirmation from its suddenly occur to he that it be

Leap-year, he grow desperate, and pretend that Emily have make he

promise to sing again, astonish that damsel by cross over to inform

she that his hoarseness have entirely depart, and that he should have

the great pleasure in favour her friend with the song she have

wish to hear; for which piece of inconsistency Emily bestow upon he

a glance so penetrate and satirical, that he long to box her pretty

pert little ear for it. when the song be over, Knighton emerge from

behind a broad old lady, somebody’s mother-in-law, very far go in

Curaçoa, which she conceal behind a pious zeal for clothing the

female native of Barelyaragon (an unknown island, discover by Juan

de Chuzacruz in the sixteenth century, and forget ever since) in the

cast-off garment of the Bluecoat-school boy. the moment Knighton

get clear of this philanthropic elder he pounce upon Coverdale,

and carry he off to a recess, then and there relate to he an

additional episode in his amatory career, which be not of the slight

importance either to himself or to anybody else, but which take nearly

as long to communicate as the original history. during this infliction,

Harry’s attention be occupy by observe the behaviour of Mr. Crane.

almost as soon as Alice quit the boudoir, Kate Marsden have enter

it, and begin a long and apparently interesting conversation with Mr.

Crane, during which that gentleman, who at the commencement appear

rather low and desponding, gradually brighten up, and, under the

influence of his fair companion’s society, grow quite lively and

animate; in fact (if by any stretch of imagination the reader can

connect two such antagonistic and incongruous idea as Mr. Crane

and flirtation), an uninitiated spectator, behold the pair, might

legitimately have come to the conclusion that Kate Marsden and the

cotton-spinner be very decidedly and unmistakably flirt.



the long evening come to an end at last, and Coverdale having see

Knighton safely deposit in a dog-cart, with nobody to bore but

a sleepy groom, be make his way to the spot where the bedroom

candlestick be usually to be discover, when he suddenly encounter

Mr. Hazlehurst. stand aside to let he pass, Harry, in his most

polite and conciliatory manner, wish he good-night. the only reply

vouchsafed be the slight and stiff possible nod of the head, and

with a countenance as dark and lower as the most viciously dispose

thunder-cloud, the offend autocrat pass on.









CHAPTER XIII.--“DEEPER and DEEPER still.”



when Coverdale reach his own room, his first act be to lock the

door, his next to fle open the window; he then untie his neck-cloth,

pull off his coat and boot, and substitute for they a dressing-gown

and slipper, cast a long, linger glance at his cigar-case. shake

his head negatively, he mutter, “I daren’t risk it; old Hazlehurst have

a wonderful nose for tobacco--if it be but as good for partridge and

pheasant he’d make an invaluable retriever!”--he pause, sigh deeply,

partly for want of a cigar--partly because, though he be not at all

aware of it, one of the great reality of life be for the first time

dawn upon he; then draw a chair to the open window he seat

himself, and give way to thought.



“I’ve make a pretty mess of it this evening, and no mistake!”--thus

run his ideas--“gone and offend the governor, and render he as

cantankerous as an old rhinoceros, so that the more I want he to do

anything, the less likely he’ll be to do it. then in my confound

good-nature, I’ve allow that ass Knighton to detain I with his stupid

prosing, so that I lose sight of the cotton-spinner, and give he a

chance of make Alice an offer--a chance of which the old fellow be

inspire with wit enough to avail himself, I’m almost certain. Arthur

will be preciously savage! and enough to make he--the notion of

sacrifice Alice to such an old anatomy as that--a yellow-skin brute

like a resuscitated mummy, without more than two idea in his head, and

two such idea--cash and cotton! he think of nothing else, asleep or

awake. I wonder what answer Alice give he; but there ben’t much doubt

of that, the poor girl daren’t disobey her father--besides, woman don’t

refuse £20,000 a-year. well, I wish old Crane joy of his bargain. she’ll

soon get sick of he, and be miserable of course; then she’ll take to

flirt with every young fellow she meet, to get rid of her _ennui_;

choose out one to establish a platonic friendship with, perhaps!--I’ve

see all that sort of thing in France and Italy often enough. D’Almayne

very likely, he’s just the sort of puppy to lead a woman on--she

laugh at he now, but it may be different when she’s only old Crane to

contrast he with. by the way, I’ll give Arthur a hint on that score.”

 he rise, pace up and down the room several time, then continued--“I

wonder what the deuce be the matter with I! I feel most absurdly and

unpleasantly miserable.” he reseat himself by the window, toss back

his hair, and sit silently watch the moon, just then emerge from

behind a bank of cloud. it be a time and scene to elevate and refine

man’s nature; and Harry be not insensible to the influence. he think

of his boyhood, and his mother’s tender love; he recur to the

moonlight stroll in which he have confide these cherished memory to

Alice, and the warm and ready sympathy with which she listen to the

recital; then minute point in their subsequent intercourse force

themselves into his recollection--smile, word, and glance, trifle

in themselves, but when collect, suggestive of a definite idea; and

lastly, her look when she quit the dining-room that evening flash

across he, and with a sudden start he press his hand to his forehead

as he resumed--“Fool that I be, I see it all now--now when it be too

late I love she, and I might have win her love--it only require to tell

she of my own feeling, to change the affectionate interest she have

conceive for I into a warm sentiment; and now, perhaps pique by

my apparent indifference, she have accept this man, and seal her own

unhappiness--and mine too, for that matter; but I deserve it! why do

I let this chance of a bright future escape I! to fancy that the mere

physical excitement of hunting and shoot (pastime for a thoughtless

boy) could content a being endow with reason and feeling!--though

really I doubt whether I deserve such a title. I must have be

blind--stultify, not to see all this before!” bury his face in his

hand, he remain for some time in deep and self-upbraid thought;

rouse himself at length by an effort, he continued--“well! it’s no

good sitting here torment myself all night long--I’ll go to bed

(though, of course, I shall not sleep a wink), and in the morning I’ll

walk over to the station, meet Arthur--tell he how I’ve mismanage

everything he expect I to do, and find some excuse for leave this

place to-morrow. I should go mad if I be to stay here long! Heigho!

I wonder what will become of I--it will be no pleasure to look forward

to the shooting season now! I don’t believe I shall ever care to hit a

bird or mount a horse again. I’ll go to India, and join the army as

a volunteer, or start off to look for the north pole, or something.

I shall hang myself if I stay at home, and do nothing but think about

Alice and that detestable old crane!” by the time his meditation have

reach this point, Coverdale be unrobed, and, jump disconsolately

into bed, have not lay his head on his pillow for five minute ere he

fall sound asleep, and dream of a _battue_, in which he try to shoot

Mr. Crane (who, on that occasion only, appear ornithologically and

picturesquely attire in the tail and plumage of a cock-pheasant), and

could by no mean induce his gun to go off.



the sun shine in through the open window awake Harry, when he fancy

he might have be asleep about a quarter of an hour; on refer to

his watch, however, he find it be halfpast six, and as the train by

which Arthur Hazlehurst be expect would arrive at twenty minute past

seven, and it be a good half-hour’s walk to the station, he rise and

begin dress. as his thought recur to the event of the previous

evening, all his care and anxiety come back upon he with redouble

force, and he feel more thoroughly out of sort and unhappy than he ever

remember to have do since he have come to man’s estate. when the

operation of shaving oblige he to look in the glass, he be surprised,

and if the truth must be tell, rather alarmed also, as he catch sight

of the expression of his feature. “what a hang-dog, miserable brute

I look like!” he mutter to himself; “it strike I I drink more wine

than be good for one last night--that come of old Hazlehurst bring

out Burgundy after everybody have have enough. the old boy must have be

frightfully screw himself, or he would never have get so cantankerous

with I about nothing--I hate a man who grow quarrelsome over his

liquor! Heigho! I feel shockingly seedy and down in the mouth, what

the deuce be I to say to Arthur!--how on earth be I to set thing right

again with the old man! I wonder whether he will be stupid enough to

expect I to make an apology? I wouldn’t mind do it to an old codger

like that, but ’pon my word I should not know what to say--I’ve

nothing to apologise about that I can see. I hope Arthur willn’t be angry,

or bad still, unhappy about Alice--poor, dear Alice: if she come down

to breakfast look miserable, I shall never be able to stand it! I’d

well not look at she at all--that will be the only plan: I’ll be off

before luncheon. when I get home, all by myself, and have nothing to do

but sit and think, I shall have a pleasant life of it! well, I certainly

_have_ go and do it this time handsomely--rather!”



thus fret and worry himself he finish dress, and, make his

way quietly down stair, effect his exit unobserve. fancy he be

late he start at a brisk walk, and having cross the open part of

the park, reach a stile at the entrance of a grass-grow footpath

overshadow with tree. before enter this he look at his watch,

and find that instead of too late he be too early, by nearly half an

hour; accordingly, get leisurely over the stile, he stroll onward

in the direction of a rustic bench, which he remember to have see

some short distance far up the path, where, if the truth must be

tell, he propose to console himself with a cigar. as he come in sight

of this bench he perceive that it be occupy, and a second glance

be scarcely need to convince he that the occupant be Alice. for a

moment he be perplex as to what course to take, whether to join she,

or to retrace his step, and avoid a meeting which he feel, under the

circumstance, must necessarily be most embarrassing. perceive that

the young lady’s head be turn in the opposite direction, and that

she have therefore not yet see he, he draw back a pace or two, so as

to place the trunk of a tower elm between they. “what shall I do?”

 think Harry; “I have not an idea what to say to she that would be

likely to be of any use; in fact, there’s nothing to be say. she have

accept old Crane, and now she’s come here to meet Arthur, tell he

what she’s do, say she could not help it, and ask he to forgive she

and make the good of it. I shall be _de trop_ evidently, so the good

thing I can do be to jog back again; and yet--and yet I should like to

walk by her side, and look into her dear blue eye once more--heigho! I

almost wish my dream would come true, only reverse, and that I be

the pheasant and Crane go to shoot I, though I should not be in much

danger, for the old muff would be safe to miss I. well, I suppose

I’d well be off--be she there still? yes, but what be she

do--crying?--why by heaven she’s cry as if her heart would break!

oh, you know I cann’t stand this, so it’s no use think any more about

it; speak to she I must and will!” and, suit the action to the word,

he be about to spring forward and join she, when it occur to he

that it would only distress and annoy she if he be to obtrude his

presence upon she when, imagine herself alone, she be unrestrainedly

give way to her grief; so, with that tact spring from innate

delicacy of feeling which prevent Coverdale’s honest, straightforward

character from ever become rough or overbearing, he wait till poor

Alice have dry her tear, and with slow, listless footstep (sadly

different from her usual bounding and elastic gait) resume her walk in

the direction of the railway-station. as soon as she be fairly start

Harry emerge from his hiding-place, and follow she with vigorous

stride. when he have approach within hearing distance, he endeavour

by various mean, such as stamp with his foot, brush against

the underwood as he pass, and the like, to render she aware of his

presence, but for some minute without success. at length, however, a

violent onslaught he make against a blackthorn bush (by which mean he

acquire a practical knowledge of the penetrate property of thorn)

attract her attention, and with a start sufficiently violent to show

that her nervous system be unusually excited, she turn and beheld

he. re-assured by find that the alarm sound have be cause by

the approach of a friend, rather than by that of a wild beast or an ogre

(plague so common in the midland county of “England in ye nineteenth

century,” that of course her imagination have instantly suggest they),

Alice wait till he come up, and receive he with her customary

bright smile, although her heighten colour, and an unusual degree of

consciousness in her manner, prove that for some reason the meeting

rather embarrass she also.



“you walk betime, Miss Hazlehurst,” begin Harry, anxious to break the

ice, but not know in the slight degree how, when it should be

break, he be go to proceed; “you be really a pattern of early

rise; but I have a notion we be both bind on the same errand,

namely, to meet Arthur--be I wrong?”



“quite right,” be the reply; “I get up at a wonderfully early hour;

I suppose I be too much excited by such an unaccustomed event as a

dinner-party, to be able to sleep at all soundly.”



“you look fag and weary even now,” return Coverdale, regard she

anxiously, “and you will fatigue yourself still more by walk to the

station and back. be you prudent to undertake so long an expedition

before breakfast?”



“oh yes,” be the reply; “it will refresh I and do I good; besides, I

want particularly to see and talk to Arthur.”



“I will accompany you as far as the station, if you will allow I,”

 return Harry, “and, as soon as your brother arrive, leave you to talk

with he in peace; the few word I have to say to he will do equally

well after breakfast.”



Alice signify her consent, and the conversation continue for several

minute to turn on indifferent subject, though the burden of sustain

it fall chiefly upon Alice, Harry’s observation become short

and less coherent at each reply. at length, however, Alice’s stock of

small-talk fail she, and Harry, in despair, be about to hazard some

such original observation as, that the grass be look remarkably

green, when his companion suddenly address he.



“I be afraid that you will think that I be interfere very

unnecessarily and impertinently, Mr. Coverdale, but I must trust to your

kindness to make allowance for I.”



“she be actually go to confess the cotton-spinner to I, and tell

I I’m in the way, I do believe! cool hand woman be, and no mistake!”

 think Coverdale; he only say, however, “pray go on.”



“the fact be,” resume Alice, with a falter voice, “my brother Tom

inform I (you must not be angry with the poor boy, for he do it out

of regard for you) that you--that be that my father and you differ

about some political question after dinner yesterday, and that my father

be so carry away by the subject as to become injudiciously warm, and,

from Tom’s account, personal, and that his observation annoy you.

now, I be so very sorry this should have occur, for he have form

such a high opinion of you, and Arthur be so much pleased to see how

well you get on with he--a point on which he appear particularly

anxious.” (Coverdale bite his lip, and cut off a thistle’s head viciously

with his cane.) “but, if you could be so very good as to overlook

anything my father may have say, it would make I--I mean it would make

Arthur, and--and--all of we so much happy.”



“my dear Miss Hazlehurst,” begin Harry, vehemently, “how very kind of

you to trouble yourself about I! I can assure you I be most anxious

to say or do anything to regain Mr. Hazlehurst’s good opinion. I know

I make he rather an impertinent answer; but really I be so unprepared

for such an attack; and then, to make matter bad, that old idiot,

Mr. Crane--that be,” he continue, suddenly recollect to whom he

be speak, and turn crimson as he do so, “I beg your pardon

for speak so disrespectfully of he to _you_; I really forget--I be

certainly lose my sense!” with a blush as bright, though not quite

so deep colour as that of Coverdale, Alice, turn away her head,

reply:



“Mr. Crane’s only claim on my respect be, that he be my father’s friend;

if I must own the truth, I do not myself consider he very wise.”



“his _only_ claim do you say!” exclaim Harry, earnestly. “oh, Miss

Hazlehurst--Alice--pardon I if I ask you to deal openly with I; be I

indeed wrong in suppose that you be engage, or about to become so,

to Mr. Crane?”



“oh yes!” be the hurried reply; “such a fate would render I most

miserable.”



upon this hint Harry spake; the reality and strength of his feeling

impart an earnest dignity to his manner, and an unwonted eloquence to

his speech, which would have deeply affect his fair auditor, even have

her own heart not plead warmly in his favour. as it be, before they

arrive in sight of the railroad station, Harry have somehow come to the

conclusion, that the communication he should have to make to his friend

Arthur would be very much more satisfactory, though perhaps little less

embarrassing, than the one he have originally design. it certainly

be a considerable change in the tenour of his report to be force to

explain, that instead of consider himself the most miserable be

in the world, he feel convinced he be by far the happy; for that

Alice--resolve not to marry the cotton-spinner--have give her heart,

and promise her hand, to he.



and thus, short, sharp, and decisive, begin and end “Harry Coverdale’s

courtship:” all the result, good and evil, “that come of it,” may be

learn by any reader sufficiently persevere to peruse that which

remain to be tell of this veracious history.









CHAPTER XIV.--DECIDEDLY EMBARRASSING.



Alice and Harry be so deeply engross with each other and so

absorb in the interchange of those mysterious but delightful nothing,

which form the staple of lover’ communication, and which, deeply

interesting to the happy pair, appear to the unsusceptible public the

veri nonsense imaginable, that they be still some distance from

the station when the train rush up, sneeze out a few passenger, and,

snort and coughing, dash off like a well-dispose fiery dragon,

warrant quiet to ride and drive. walk on rapidly they soon

discover Arthur, embarrass by a carpet-bag and a mackintosh, make

the good of his way to meet they; the moment he come within speak

distance, he exclaimed--



“what do I behold! Harry Coverdale with a young lady on his arm! surely

the age of miracle be return! well I never do! do _you_ ever? and

Alice look so deliciously self-satisfied and unconscious, too! why

you stupid little owl (you’re very like one, with your hooked nose and

great eye), don’t you know you’re bore he to death? he care for

nothing but horse, dog, and gun; and above all perfectly abominate

woman.” Alice smile, and attempt to make a playful rejoinder, but in

vain,--her heart be too full; have she speak at that moment she must

have burst into tear. the speech affect Harry differently.



“I do nothing of the kind,” he say, angrily; “Arthur how can you be

so absurd!” pause for a moment, the ludicrous nature of the situation

occur to he, and, with difficulty restrain a laugh, he turn the

conversation by seize his friend’s carpet-bag, exclaim as he do

so, “come, give it up, of course I’m not go to let you carry it;

you’re look horridly thin and pale, as londoner always do: be

he not, Al--a--, Miss Hazlehurst? what! you refuse; give it up this

instant, or I declare I’ll carry you and it too.”



during the playful struggle which ensue for the possession of the

carpet-bag, in which contention Harry be soon victorious, Alice, glad

to obtain a few minute in which to compose herself, walk on. as the

young man hasten to rejoin she, hazlehurst, lay his hand on

Coverdale’s arm, inquire “how have it all go off? crane haven’t

venture to offer yet, of course?”



“yes, by Jove, he have though!” be the reply; “the old muff contrive to

pop last night--confound him!--when I be out of the room, and haven’t a

chance of throw anything at his head.”



“and Alice?” inquire the brother, eagerly; but his eagerness frustrate

its own purpose (no uncommon case by the way), for, pronounce the name

in a loud key than he be aware of, the fair owner thereof stop

short, and thus prevent the possibility of further explanation. as

they continue their homeward walk, Arthur, who be a quick observer,

soon detect a change in Harry’s manner towards his sister; for

which, at first, he feel excessively puzzled to account. a respectful

tenderness be apparent in his tone when he address she, and he

exhibit a degree of eager, almost affectionate, solicitude for her

ease and comfort, in all the minor incident of a country walk, such as

Hazlehurst, during the whole of their intimacy, have never before see

he evince towards a young lady.



“what have come to Harry now, I wonder?” thus run his reflection; “if

it be any one in the world but he, I should say he be flirt with

Alice; but Harry never flirt in his life, so that be impossible.” he

ponder for a moment, then an idea strike he. “I see it now; my father

have force the poor child to accept old crane; Harry know it, and

the pity his kind, warm-hearted nature lead he to feel towards she,

influence his manner. they be each come to tell I all that have

occur, and have meet by accident; yes, that must be it.” in order,

however, more fully to satisfy himself of the correctness of his theory,

he observe, in his usual light, jest manner, “I think Mr. Coverdale,

it behove I, as ‘a man and a brother,’ to inquire how you happen to

be march about the country, _tête-à-tête_ with my sister, at this

unconscionably early hour?”



Harry, who, between his desire to enlighten Arthur as to the new and

transcendently delightful, but especially embarrassing turn affair have

take, and the impossibility of do so before Alice--the overpowering

nature of his feeling towards that young lady, and his extreme

happiness at find they reciprocate--the great and imminent danger

_in re_ Crane, and the humiliating confession regard his lose

influence with Mr. Hazlehurst, together with the awkward position

in which he stand towards that outraged and obdurate elder--be in a

tremendous frame of mind, merely start and stare vacantly at his

interrogator.



but Alice, have by this time regain in some degree her

self-possession, reply quietly, “Mr. Coverdale and I be both

come to meet you, and encounter each other accidentally, walk on

together.”



as she speak, Arthur, strive to read her countenance, fix his eye

upon she. unable to meet his glance she turn away with an April look,

half tear half smile. “it must be as I think,” reflect Arthur;

“but if anything be to be do to save she, no time should be lose.

I’ll not waste the present opportunity. my dear Coverdale,” he continue

aloud, “I wish to have a few minute’ private conversation with my

sister; you and I be too old friend to stand upon ceremony, so you

will not be offend if I ask you to walk on, and wait for we at the

stile at the end of the path.”



this direct appeal bring Harry to his sense, but not feel sure

whether Alice would approve of have the whole burden of explanation

throw upon she, he glance inquiringly towards she ere he venture to

reply. now, Alice, fond as she be of her brother, be also (from their

difference in point of age, as well as from the fact that Arthur’s

nature be more firm and resolute than her own, and his manner quick

and abrupt) a little afraid of he. thus, be aware how very highly

he esteem Coverdale--an estimation which she be inclined to transcend

rather than to depreciate--a sudden fear seize her lest Arthur, deeming

she a mere silly child, should consider his friend have do a foolish

thing in choose she for a wife, when he might have select, at the

very least, some strong-minded peeress, and that he might be angry

with she for her presumption in having accept he. this feeling,

overpower for the moment every other, induce she to respond to

Harry’s look of inquiry by a slight shake of the head, and a glance

which would have keep he by her side if a whole regiment of brother,

arm with Minie rifle and Colt’s revolver, have attempt to separate

they. but Arthur, be totally unarmed, and having simply ask a civil

question, the answer which Harry, appropriately quote Walter Scott,

might have make to the hypothetical regiment, “come one come all, this

rock (not that there be a rock, but that be a trifle) will fly, from

its firm base as soon as I,” be unfit for the present emergency,

and no other equally good suggest itself. what he _do_ say be this,

“a--really--of course I’d do it in a minute, my dear fellow--but--a--I’m

not quite sure,”--here he glance at Alice--“that be, I’m positively

certain that--a--in fact, the thing’s impossible.”



“you’re certain that it’s impossible that you can walk on to the stile

before Alice and I! my dear Harry, what be you talk--or rather

(for the truth be you’re pre-occupied), what be you think about?”

 inquire Arthur, in amazement, see from the expression of his

friend’s countenance that he be really anxious and excited. Coverdale

be again hesitate how to reply, when Alice relieve he from his

difficulty by say hurriedly, “I will walk on, and leave you to talk

to Mr. Coverdale.”



as she speak, they reach the rustic bench before allude to, and

Arthur, completely mystify, seat himself, and make a sign to

Coverdale to follow his example.



“one moment, and I’ll be with you,” reply Coverdale, spring to

Alice’s side; “then I may tell he everything?” he continue.



“oh yes,” be the unhesitate answer.



“and you _will_ wait for we at the stile? I willn’t detain he five

minute.”



“if _you_ wish it.”



“_can_ you doubt it?” be the necessary lover-like rejoinder; and

Coverdale return to his friend, who look especially puzzled and

slightly provoke.



“now be silent!” exclaim Hazlehurst, as Harry be about, with the

great volubility, to plunge at once _in media re_. “you have live

amongst woman till you’ve learn to chatter like they, I think. I shall

never bring you to the point, unless you will let I cross-examine you.”



“fire away, then; only look sharp, for your sister must not be keep

wait,” be the reply.



“you’ve grow wonderfully polite and attentive all of a sudden,”

 return Arthur, sarcastically. “but now listen to I. have Crane make

Alice an offer?”



Harry reply in the affirmative.



“do she refuse he?”



“of course she do,” be the disdainful rejoinder.



“I don’t see any of course in it,” return hazlehurst, moodily.

“my father be resolve on the match: Alice have be bring up to obey

he implicitly, and the habit of obedience be very strong in such a

gentle, yield nature as her.”



“if she be gentle and yield I’m not!” exclaim Harry, vehemently;

“and with your support, and the knowledge that his daughter’s happiness

be at stake, Mr. Hazlehurst must listen to reason.”



“my dear boy,” return Arthur, earnestly, “what a warmhearte,

thorough-go friend you be! you really take as much interest in the

affair as if it be your own. I see you naturally reckon on the extent

of your influence with my father, and I have reason to believe you do

not overrate it. why, what be the matter now? have you take leave of

your sense?”



this inquiry refer to a sudden and alarming outbreak on the part of

Coverdale, who, when his influence with Mr. Hazlehurst be mention,

spring to his foot, utter what mild mamma, engage in the moral

instruction of their tender offspring, term a “naughty word.”



“you be enough to drive one mad!” he exclaim, angrily; “say, and

make I say, all sort of absurd thing at cross-purpose, because you

willn’t listen to the explanation I’m remain here on purpose to give

you; keep Alice wait, too!”



“well, let she wait,” return Arthur, testily, worry by Harry’s

constant reference to this point; “anybody would think you be Alice’s

lover instead of old crane!”



“and so I be,” be the unexpected rejoinder; “and what be more, old

fellow, her accept lover also! oh, Arthur,” he continue, seat

himself by his friend’s side, and lay his arm on his shoulder, “I’m

the happy, lucky dog in existence! to think that she should be

able to love such a rough, uncultivated--but you be not displeased, be

you--surprised, of course, you must be.”



“Surprised, indeed,” be the reply; “so much so, that even yet I

can scarcely believe it; it have almost take my breath away! but

displeased!--why my dear Harry, I’d rather she marry you than any man

breathing, be he prince, duke, or what not. it be the most charming,

glorious, wonderful thing that ever happen! but even now I cann’t

conceive how it have come about; and yet, when I begin to reflect, I

fancy that Alice be grow shy and conscious in regard to something

or somebody, before I go away. it’s natural enough that she should

fall in love with you; but that you should take a fancy to she, or

indeed to any girl, do, I own, surprise I. I have so thoroughly make

up my mind that you mean to be an old bachelor.”



“you could not have do so more completely than I have,” rejoin Harry;

“but the fact be, that from the first moment in which I see your sister

I fall in love with she, though I have not the most remote idea of it at

the time. I can trace it all now; hence my dislike of D’Almayne, and the

poor old cotton-spinner. I be afraid the fascination of the one might

win her heart, or the fortune of the other obtain her hand--in fact, I

be unconsciously jealous of they both. but now come on, we be really

keep Alice an unreasonable time. aye, you may laugh; I don’t care a

_sous_ now that you know all about it. why Arthur, old boy, you will

be my real _bonâ fide_ brother one of these days!--that be a contingent

advantage which have only just occur to I.”



seize his friend’s hand as he speak, he press it with such

good-will, that Hazlehurst be enable to give a shrewd guess at

the sensation produce by that interesting mediæval amenity, the

thumb-screw. and thus mutually pleased and excited, the young man

proceed, both talk volubly, and generally at the same moment, till

they reach the stile, where they find Alice await they, look

very timid, very conscious, but exceedingly pretty. she need not have

be uneasy, however, for Arthur have too much good taste and kind

feel to laugh at she at that moment; on the contrary, he hasten

to set her mind at rest by whispering, as he imprint a kiss on her

glow cheek--



“my darling child, you have make I almost as happy as you have render

_he_.”



the walk home be a very delightful one. Alice lean on Harry’s

stalwart arm, and feel the most perfect and irrational confidence in his

power to shield she from the effect of her father’s anger, Mr. Crane’s

despair, and all other uncomfortable consequence of the act of

filial disobedience which she meditate. Harry, already experience a

sensation of delicious proprietorship in regard to the sweet girl beside

he, feel himself exalt in the scale of humanity, and hold his head

a good inch high on the strength of it; from which moral and physical

elevation he look down upon all field-sport as soulless and ignoble

pastime, and despise they accordingly. Arthur, hope that his

sister’s attachment to a man in every way so worthy of she, would

inspire she with the firmness requisite to withstand successfully his

father’s possible opposition to the match, and that the matter would

eventually end by secure her happiness and that of his friend, “forget

his own grief,” to rejoice in their bright prospect. and so they

reach the pleasure-ground, where Alice, separate from the two

gentleman, run in to compose her excited feeling before appear at

breakfast.



“Arthur, wait one moment,” exclaim Coverdale, lay his hand on

his friend’s arm to detain he; “I have something important to say to

you;--isn’t she an angel, my dear boy?”



“why really, my good fellow, between friend, and see that you appear

to attach so much importance to the fact, I should say, take into

consideration the evidence in the case, and come to the point without

any unnecessary prolixity, that she be by no mean an angel, but simply

a very pleasant little female mortal, and--ahem! my poor sister, sir.”



“Psha! you stupid old humbug!” return Harry, give he a playful

push, which cause he involuntarily to leap over a flower-bed; “do just

listen to I for a minute, and give I a sensible answer if you can.

it’s all very pretty for my darling Alice, and you and I, to settle

this matter so sweetly and easily; but remember, there’s the governor to

bring round, and Crane and his confound £20,000 a-year to beat out of

the field; it strike I we’re in an awful fix, and about to become an

interesting young couple. what be to be the next move, eh?”



“oh, the affair lie in a nutshell,” return hazlehurst. “fortunately,

my father have always appreciate you properly, and now the unusual

degree of influence you have acquire over he will stand you in good

stead. he may be a little annoyed at first, when he find he must

relinquish his favourite design of purchase old Crane’s farm; but he

be very fond of Alice, and very proud of she.”



“he’d be a most unnatural old heathen if he ben’t,” mutter Harry,

_sotto voce_.



“consequently,” continue hazlehurst, not heed the interruption,

“when he perceive the immeasurable advantage to be obtain by

allow she to marry a man she love, and who be in every way deserve

of her affection, instead of an old scarecrow, who will be in his dotage

(I believe he be so already, more or less!) while Ally be still quite a

young woman, he cannot hesitate for a moment in give his consent. you

have well speak to he the instant breakfast be over; depend upon it

you’ll find he all amiability.”



“depend upon it I shall find he nothing of the kind,” return

Coverdale, snappishly; then, see the look of surprise that spread

over his friend’s countenance, he continue, dejectedly:--“Ah, my dear

boy, you little know the extent to which I’ve be put my foot in it

since you go away. Tom tell I I annoy your governor three or four

day ago, by take the nonsense out of that beast of a horse old crane

have the stupidity to give Alice; a brute which would have break she

sweet neck, if I haven’t luckily be at hand to catch she as she be

fall. then, to improve the matter, last night we all drank wine

enough, and the Head of the Family get a little too much into it to be

good for its proprietor; accordingly, he force I to give my opinion

about free-trade, and then pitch into I for so do, and declare

I’d insult he: upon which I lose my temper, and say something rude;

and, to come to the point, as you call it, he be now as savage as a bear

with I, and all the blessed influence you’ve be pay I such pretty

compliment about, if it ever exist, be scatter to the wind. I dare

not speak to he, it would be bad than useless; he’d be only too glad

to refuse I at once, lest he should lose such a good opportunity of

pay I off for last night. ah!” he continue, “you may well look

puzzled,--you would not like to have many client with such a talent

as I possess for unconsciously cut their own throat! what’s to

be done?--divide the wire of the electric telegraph at King’s Cross

Station, and then take Alice along the Great Northern to Gretna

Green--though Gretna Green have be do brown by some recent act, have

it not, and the harmonious and hymeneal blacksmith retire into private

life? come, advise, for I can hit upon nothing; only remember one

thing,--since Alice be good enough to say she will have I, _marry I

must and will be_, if all the father in England be to set themselves

against it!”









CHAPTER XV.--RELATES the unexpected benevolence of HORACE D’ALMAYNE.



Arthur Hazlehurst, with an aspect graver than his willnot, reply to

Harry’s appeal--“It certainly be very unfortunate that you should have

select last night, of all other, to displease my father; because,

owe to the Crane offer, time be of the great importance; but for

that I should not have care; you would only have have to wait for a

week or two, take pain to be especially polite and deferential in

the interval, and he would have totally forget his anger. as it be,

perhaps I have well speak to him,--he be sure to tell I about the

cotton-spinner, and I can avail myself of that opportunity to come to

the point; and now, if you have nothing well to propose, we’ll go in

to breakfast. love may possibly destroy the appetite, but a railroad

journey have a directly contrary effect.”



Harry _have_ nothing well to propose--(for a vague suggestion in regard

to punch old Crane’s head, if he (Crane) do not mind what he be

about, could scarcely be consider in the light of a serious, practical

amendment)--so they go in to breakfast accordingly.



this meal appear to be a most unsatisfactory one to “all who assemble

within those wall;” for, despite the presence of every delicacy of

the season, and a few over, each individual seem labour under some

secret sorrow, and a general wet blanket damp, and hang heavy on,

the spirit of the whole party; with the exception, perhaps, of Horace

D’Almayne, who be unusually animate, and watch the proceeding with

a look of quiet penetration.



when the lady quit the room, Mr. Crane call Mr. Hazlehurst aside,

and inform he that he wish for the honour of an interview; to which

request that gentleman accede in his most gracious manner, and they

adjourn together to the library.



Harry, with a significant glance to Arthur to remain on the look out and

watch proceeding, stroll off with Tom on some horse-or-dog-inspect

pretext, but really to keep himself out of harm’s way till he be

wanted,--so low an estimate have he now acquire of his own diplomatic

ability.



D’Almayne and Arthur be thus leave _tête-à-tête_, the former accost

the latter after the follow fashion:--



“Hazlehurst, _mon cher_, I shall die of _ennui_ if we have many such

_triste affaire_ as this meal of which we have just partake, now,

without be more inquisitive than my neighbour, you cannot suppose

I have remain entirely in the dark in regard to the little amusement

your friend and relation have devise to vary the monotony of life

withal.”



“and the result of these your observation?” inquire Arthur, coldly.



“be, that the various interest clash, and that delicate dilemma

innumerable must, ere long, present their horns;--now I, be an

easy-temper fellow, like to be happy myself, and to see every brother

man, and sister woman, happy also. I shall, therefore, have

much pleasure in do _mon petit possible_ to smooth away these

difficulty--an endeavour in which my influence with our good friend

Crane will greatly assist I; but to enable I to do this, you must of

course take I so far into your confidence, as to tell I whether I be

right in my preconceive ideas--_che dice, Signor_?”



Arthur reflect for a moment--he know D’Almayne to be quick-sight,

clear-head, and fertile in expedient, at the same time he believe he

be design and self-interested; in the present emergency, however, he

might, from his influence with Mr. Crane, be possibly of some use,

while he could scarcely, with the bad intention, render the aspect of

affair more complicated and unsatisfactory than it now appear.



accordingly, he replied,--“It cannot involve any alarming stretch of

confidence on my part, merely to tell you whether your ‘guess at

truth’ have hit the mark, or fly wide of it. so you have only to

propound your query, and I will answer they as clearly and concisely

as in I lie.”



“_C’est bon!_” be the reply. “a--to begin with--I be correct, be I not,

in suppose that last night my worthy friend Crane offer his hand and

£20,000 per annum (in which latter item his heart be of course wrap

up and include) to your amiable and accomplished sister?” hazlehurst

nod assent, and D’Almayne continued,--“The young lady, however, or I

be much mistaken, greatly prefer your excellent and energetic friend,

Mr. Coverdale (who, you must pardon I for say, remind I of a

well-intentione, enthusiastic bull in a chinashop), which preference

the gentleman return to such a degree, that I be inclined to believe

he _have_ tell, or in some other manner render the fair Alice aware of,

his love. her manner at breakfast this morning, be compound of such

an elaborate endeavour to conceal the conscious and confiding, behind

the most transparent _eidolon_ of indifference, that no one at all

acquaint with woman’s nature could doubt about the matter.”



“you be indeed a close observer!” exclaim Arthur, surprise out of

his caution. “Coverdale’s attachment be a thing I never even suspect

till--a--till this morning.”



“Mr. Crane tell I, your father be intensely anxious to purchase one of

his farm adjoin your estate, which he (crane) be unwilling to part

with,” resume D’Almayne; “thence, I imagine, proceed your respect

progenitor’s anxiety to bring about the match. to finish the catalogue

of my observation up to the present time, I conceive Mr. Crane to be

now in the act of urge his suit to Mr. Hazlehurst, and complain

that ‘Miss Alice’ as he call she (he always talk on such subject

like an underbred greengrocer, or second footman), rather kick,

than jump, at he when he offer she--ahem--his income and his

affection.”



“your surmise be so wonderfully correct,” rejoin Arthur (determine

to make a merit of necessity, and appear open with one who seem thus

well acquainted with all the family secret), “that in tell you that

as soon as Mr. Crane leave the study, I mean to appeal to my father in

my friend’s behalf, I shall, probably, only forestall you in express

another of your judicious anticipation.”



“I rather imagine that would be the next move,” be the easy,

self-satisfied reply.--“Mr. Coverdale, with all his surprising freshness

and _naïveté_ of character, could scarcely propose to urge his suit

in person, after having quarrel with your father over his wine last

night; for which reason, by the way, it require no very great tact to

divine that Mr. Crane’s proposal will find favour in Mr. Hazlehurst’s

eye, and Mr. Coverdale’s be reject.”



“and the remedy?” inquire Arthur, eagerly.



D’Almayne pause, then a meaning but disagreeable smile pass across

his handsome feature, as he replied,--“If I can induce Mr. Crane to

withdraw his suit of his own accord, yet continue his amicable relation

towards this family, and be willing to sell the farm to your father at

his own price, and by these mean lead Mr. Hazlehurst to regard your

friend’s offer favourably, shall I be act in accordance with your

wish.”



“Nay, my dear D’Almayne, if you can indeed persuade Mr. Crane to perform

so magnanimous a part, I shall consider you the good and clever

fellow in the world. as to my wish you to do so, I should as

soon have think of wish you to appoint I First Lord of the

Treasury--one only wish for such thing as one, in some degree,

expect to obtain. but surely you over-calculate your power of

persuasion,” return hazlehurst, scarcely know whether D’Almayne

might not be amuse himself at his expense.



“I will remain here and await the result of your interview with your

father, and if it terminate as I predict, I will attempt my little bit

of diplomacy;--the result will prove to you whether or not I overrate my

machiavelian talent,” be the confident reply--and so they part.



Mr. Hazlehurst, senior, be by no mean in an amiable frame of mind when

his son enter the library--the gout, considerably increase by the

wine-bibbing of the previous evening, pervade his entire system, mental

and bodily; and through the atrabilious medium of a disorder stomach,

he look back upon his disagreement with Coverdale, till it became

magnify into a serious quarrel. Mr. Crane have just inform he that,

on renew his offer to Alice on the previous evening, the young lady

mutter a few word, incoherent indeed, but, as he conceive, of a

negative tendency, and instantly convey herself away without

afford he an opportunity of obtain an explanation. Whereupon

Mr. Hazlehurst, wax wroth, declare she should accept he that very

morning; beg he to retire until he should have see his daughter,

and, as he be pleased to term it, bring she to her sense; and having

just dispatch a summon to the poor girl, be wait her arrival

to perpetrate an act of parental tyranny, when his son enter. the

consequence may readily be imagined:--Coverdale be angrily and

unceremoniously refuse; Alice anathematize, excommunicate, and

order magisterially to be imprison in her own room till far

notice, and Arthur severely reprimand for having introduce Coverdale

to the family (which, be it remember, he have do at his father’s

particular request), and caution against venture to countenance

Alice in her disobedience, or ever again to refer to the subject in his

(Mr. Hazlehurst’s) sovereign presence, on pain of be cut off with

the trilling patrimony of one shilling sterling. Arthur attempt a

mild remonstrance, whereby he obtain a particular request instantly to

leave the room, and a general order in regard to the entire alteration

of his conduct, and abnegation of his present opinion on all subject,

human and divine. return to the breakfast-room in the frame of mind

naturally consequent upon such a reception, he discover D’Almayne

comfortably lounge in an easy-chair, and peruse a handsomely bind

copy of the _Pleasures of Memory_.



glance up as Hazlehurst enter, he observe coolly--“I need not ask

you how it have go, _mon ami_, your face tell I.”



Hazlehurst stride impatiently up and down the apartment; then stop

short in front of his companion, he exclaim abruptly--“Try your

plan, whatever it may be; for common sense be throw away upon a man so

prejudiced and positive as my father have show himself to be; and common

patience cannot bear the irritate speech he make, when all the time

one feel that one be strive for the right, and that he be totally and

entirely wrong.”



“you be warm, _mon cher_,” be the calm reply. “papa have be

wrong-headed time out of mind, and will probably continue so till time

shall have pass away, together with all other sublunary weight and

measure; so why afflict yourself at the inevitable? but I will now

proceed without delay to try my eloquence upon the dear, reject Mr.

Crane--a--by the way, you must give I one promise. ‘on their own merit

modest man be dumb;’ now my modesty be so outrageously sensitive,

that I be not only dumb myself, but require my friend to be so

likewise; in plain English, if I do this thing to oblige you, you must

promise I to keep my share in the transaction a secret; the change

must appear to emanate from the united kind regard and amiable

self-sacrifice of your father and Mr. Crane.” see Arthur hesitate,

he continued--“Without this assurance, you must excuse my decline to

interfere.”



“be it as you will then,” begin Arthur.



as he speak the door fly open, and Alice, eager and tearful, hurry

in, exclaiming,--“You have see my father! can it be true that he be so

cruel as to refuse his consent. he have just write I such a dreadful

note, order I not to quit my room!”



here, catch sight of D’Almayne, she stop short in confusion and

alarm. that individual hasten to relieve she by walk to the door;

but as he pass Arthur he whisper, “you may make an exception in your

sister’s favour. I absolve you from your vow of secrecy as far as she be

concerned. I be a tender-hearted fellow, and beauty in tear be always

too many for I.” as he speak, he leave the apartment, and close the

door behind he.



Alice hear Arthur’s account of D’Almayne’s unexpected access of

benevolence with surprise; but not having witness the quiet confidence

with which he assert his power of influence Mr. Crane, she put but

little trust in his assurance, merely set they down as the vain

boasting of a conceited youth, who be actuate by a good-natured desire

to help they out of their difficulty. that she do he injustice may

be gather from the fact, that later in the day Mr. Crane seek a

second interview with Mr. Hazlehurst., after which the latter gentleman

summon Harry Coverdale to his august presence; and when that happy but

much confused young man enter the _sanctum sanctorum_ of the library,

send for his daughter Alice likewise, and having pronounce a strongly

acidulate, not to say, crab, benediction upon their youthful

head, dismiss they in time to write by that day’s post to his man of

business, to prepare the purchase-money for the Hazlecroft farm, then

the property of Jedediah Crane, Esq. the dinner-party that evening

pass off much more agreeably than the breakfast have do. Coverdale

sit by his lady-love, look the picture, or well still, the reality

of happiness; but Arthur Hazlehurst wear a gloomy brow when he perceive

that his cousin, Kate Marsden, have pair off with the cotton-spinner,

and that they appear mutually satisfied with the arrangement.









CHAPTER XVI.--TREATS of thing in GENERAL.



it must be confess that Harry Coverdale be of a somewhat impetuous

disposition. no soon have he obtain Mr. Hazlehurst’s consent to the

match, than he commence a system of alternate petting and persecution,

whereby he contrive to render the life of Alice and her mother

scarcely endurable, until he have induce they to fix an early day for

his “execution,” as Tom irreverently paraphrase the solemnisation of

the marriage ceremony. this object happily accomplish, a journey to

London be propose, whereat Mr. Hazlehurst look very black; but when

Alice seat herself on his knee, and, stroke his bald head, call

he a dear, good, kind, papa (on speculation, probably, for at that

moment he do not in the slight degree look the character), his heart

soften, and he consent to the plan. then somebody tell Arthur of a

wonderful doctor, who have find out a new system of cure everything,

and especially complaint analogous to that under which Mrs. Hazlehurst

laboured; accordingly, he determine his mother should form one of the

London party, and consult this fashionable fee-taker; and when Arthur

have determine on a thing, it generally come to pass. therefore, after

considerable pro-ing-and con-ing, and macadamizing of difficulty, the

matter be finally arrange by Mrs. Hazlehurst, her son, and her two

daughter, take up their abode at Cherry’s Hotel, in Jermyn Street,

while Coverdale establish himself in his old quarter at the

Tavistock, in Covent-garden.



then they begin to be overwhelm with business. first, the infallible

doctor be to be consult; so poor Mrs. Hazlehurst be drag out of

bed some three hour soon than usual, breakfast in a nervous tremor,

which render the ceremony a most unreal mockery, be transport from

her carriage to a stately dining-room, where some twenty fellow-victim

be already incarcerate, whence (having wait two hour, because, in

her ignorance of London rascality, she have omit to fee the noble

creature in plush and powder who have admit she) she be at length

(his nobleness not be able long to exclude she) usher into the

presence of the potentate of pill himself. this erudite individual be

a short, stiff man, with a short, stiff appearance,--the result of the

most severe application of starch and hair-brushes,--and a short, stiff

manner, assume, as be the stare and swagger of Van Amburg and other

tiger-tamer, for the purpose of browbeat and mentally subdue

refractory or sceptical patient. see at a glance, however, that

poor Mrs. Hazlehurst be already subdue, he obligingly let off a little

superfluous starch, slightly disarrange his hair, smile, to show a

fine set of false tooth, put in at trade-price by a friendly dentist,

and having thus bring himself somewhat near the limpness of average

humanity, add (as he would have probably express it) a couple of

drachm _syrupi saccarinis_ to his manner, ere he proceed to catechise

his patient as to her symptom, and the remedy that have be apply

to remove they. to each fact thus elicit, he reply by frown

portentously, screw round his mouth, and muttering, “I know it,” in a

gloomy and mysterious manner, as though he have acquire the knowledge

by some awful and supernatural course of study; and, indeed, as Mrs.

Hazlehurst’s confession involve she having have a dangerous fall from

her horse at a period when he, the doctor, must have be about five

year old, and that she have be lay up with a bilious fever exactly

two calendar month and four day before he be bear, he can scarcely be

suppose to have come by his information honestly and lawfully. in

fact, to a logical mind, the question resolve itself into the follow

hypothesis--that he must either be a true prophet, or a lie doctor.



having elicit all the fact he care to learn (which, if he know they

before, he might as well have save himself the trouble of do),

he draw himself up to his extreme altitude,--which be nothing very

tremendous after all,--got his starch up to high-pressure pitch,

judiciously temper its stiffness with soothe syrup, and deliver

himself of the following opinion:--“Madam, you have tell I nothing

that, the moment I behold you, I be not prepared to hear. I do not in

the slight degree impugn the judgment and skill of Mr. Smithers”

 (the Hazlehurst general practitioner), “but the instant I glance at

his first prescription I see he have take a wrong view of our case.

Superacetate of Euroclydon and bi-carbonate of Hydrocephalus would never

remove the pain and palpitation on our right side--”



“the _leave_ be the side on which I usually feel the pain,” begin Mrs.

Hazlehurst, mildly.



“eh! left--yes, of course; I say left, don’t I? I believe I observe

to you before, madam, that the moment I set eye on you I became

aware of--in fact, I _feel_ (if I may so express myself) that pain

and palpitation on our left side; and I say to myself, if that very

talented practitioner, Mr. Smithers, have administer Superacetate of

Euroclydon, and bi-carbonate of Hydrocephalus to that pain of our--with

the high respect for Smithers (he be walk St. Bartholomew’s when

I be dresser to the late celebrate and lament Flayflesh), I must

say he have mistake our case. now, I shall just--I make no secret of my

practice--I shall just throw in three grain of extr. Borealis Aurorae,

with equal proportion of Astri caninis, Geminorum siamesiæ, and

_sesqui_-carbonate (mind that) _sesqui_-carbonate of Pantapolion, and

our pain will lapse (as Byron so beautifully express it) into ‘a

happy memory of the past.’ you will take the mixture six time in the

twenty-four hour, and the pill immediately before dinner. with regard

to diet, everything you have be accustom to eat be wrong; your

appetite be weak, and you like delicacy, as they be call, well

than substantial joint, I dare say?”



Mrs. Hazlehurst acknowledge that his penetration have not fail he;

and he resume sharply--



“Madam, we musn’t touch they! they be poison in such a case as ours.

no; we must restrict ourselves to plain beef and mutton, _very_ much

underdone; stale bread, _no_ vegetable, _no_ fruit, _no_ nice thing,

_very_ bitter beer, with plenty of the camomile in it (that’s the

brewer’s secret, strychnine’s all a delusion), and stick to the
