_sesqui_-carbonate of Pantapolion, and we shall be a different woman in

a short time. let I see you again on Friday. _good_ morning.” and

so, pocket his guinea with less respect than many man pay to a

fourpenny-piece, the fashionable quack allow his victim to escape.



then there be shop. there be a good many shop in Regent Street,

and those that be not there be in Bond Street, at least a fair

sprinkling of they; but Harry solemnly declare (_after_ his marriage)

that during the fortnight the party be in London, they go into they

_all_, and every man know what _that_ involve. give a woman her head,

so far as to allow she to put it into a shop, and he must indeed be a

clever fellow who can coax or coerce she out of it under half-an-hour.

but Harry be in love, and love be blind (though it have an awkward trick

of recover its eyesight after marriage and make up for lost

time, by spy out all kind of thing to which it have far well have

remain blind); besides, Alice be not more _exigeante_ than a lover

generally desire his mistress should be: too much independence of

character in a young girl be by no mean an attractive quality.



then there be a good deal of sight-seeing to be get through. Emily have

never be in London before, and Alice only once for a week. so they

“do” Westminster Abbey, which they really enjoy; and St. Paul’s,

which they pretend to admire, and don’t; and the Tower, where Emily

call the figure in the horse-armoury a set of quizzical old thing;

and the Polytechnic, where they see a man go down in a diving-bell, to

pick up nothing at the bottom of a large wash-hand-basin, and come

up again half suffocate, which they consider curious and highly

satisfactory, as no doubt it be to everybody but that unfortunate

martyr to popular science himself, who (take the most cheerful view

of his amphibious occupation) can scarcely be regard in the light of a

jolly young waterman. then they go to the National Gallery to see the

picture, which, as it be not an unusually bright and clear day, of

course they be unable to do; but they have the pleasure of see the

building itself, and the fountain in Trafalgar Square, which they all

agree they have never behold anything like before; and Harry add, that

in his travel he have not meet with anything to equal the whole affair in

its peculiar style, and that he think foreigner must be very strongly

impressed by it, and that it must at once give they a clear idea of

english taste; which remark it be a pity the architect be not there

to hear, as they might possibly have be of use to he. Emily have

never behold a play, so they go to the I-see-um Theatre, where they

witness the performance of a very long melodrama, adapt from the

French (that be, all that be national and peculiar--without which the

plot become a mere silly tissue of improbable event and impossible

situation--omit, and the place supply by worn-out and conventional

clap-traps). this _pièce de résistance_, which be to last the

play-go public for some four or six month, accord to the degree

in which it suit their appetite, be so well put on the stage, and so

well act, that the false sentiment and bad morality which pervade

it be for the time forget, and it be not till Arthur call his

attention to the fact, that Harry recollect this un-english jumble

of crime and folly, be play night after night to crowded house,

while the masterpiece of Shakspere, the great dramatist who ever

live, be banish to an obscure theatre in the outskirt of London,

or be force to be translate into a foreign language, and act by a

foreign company, ere the “ear polite” of London fashionable could be

persuade to listen to they. the two young man argue the question in

all its bearing, and arrive at this conclusion, viz. either that if

Shakspere be well act it would be well attend, or that if

Shakspere be well attend, well actor would soon be find

to perform the character; though which of these statement might be

regard as the cause, and which as the effect, they could by no mean

agree. and by that time, the play be conclude, Emily declare that

it be quite perfect, really charming; and that, as to Shakspere, he be

an obsolete old slow-coach, and very wicked too--or else, why do they

want a family edition of he? whereas, if there have ever be any harm

in this play, which she do not believe could have be the case, dear

Mr. Kingsby Florence have translate it so beautifully that it might have

be act anywhere--in a church almost. then she turn and appeal to

her sister, to support she in her girlish and unorthodox enthusiasm.



Alice reply gravely, and with a pseudo-matronly air which be highly

amusing, that although she must confess she have be interested and

entertain by the play she have just witness, yet that she have

listen to Arthur’s argument with Mr. Coverdale, and quite agree in

the view take by the latter gentleman; for which sympathy of opinion

Harry possess himself of the lovely sympathiser’s hand, and press

it gratefully; while he inwardly thank heaven for having bestow upon

his future wife such a correct taste and sound understanding.--And

so, between doctoring, and shopping, and sight-seeing, and hurrying

dressmaker, and tailor, and coach-builder, and a host of minor

tradesman, all the wedding paraphernalia be purchase, a vast amount

of business transact, settlement prepare, and money spend; and a

fortnight pass away so quickly, that it appear like two or three

day to the actor in the genteel comedy thus perform.



then they all return to the country, Harry go to the Park to

make arrangement for the incoming of house-decorator and furnisher

innumerable, who be to put to the rout all the old admiral’s bachelor

abomination, and prepare the mansion for the reception of its fair

mistress. that amiable young lady be begin to find, by experience,

that to be “go to be marry” be very hard work indeed, the wear and

tear of the feeling be a marked and alarming feature in the case.

thus, whenever Harry be away for a day, she find herself anxious,

low-spirited, and a prey to innumerable misgiving lest evil should

befall he. on one evening in particular, when he return full twenty

minute later than he should have do, she feel so convinced that

“dreadful trotting-mare” have by some mean compass his destruction,

that she receive he with a gentle shower of tear, which of course he

kiss away, as he whisper that very soon she would be his dear little

wife, and then nothing should part they even for an hour; and Alice

smile through her tear as she think how, with every taste and

feel in common, they should trip gaily along the pathway of life,

hand in hand, like a conjugal couple of siamese twin. dream! pretty

Alice, dream! which many a young girl’s love heart have frame ere

this, only to awaken to a far different reality, and weep over the

departure of such bright illusion.



but there be not much time for dream or romance at the Grange, for

the “fatal day” come near and nearer with alarming velocity, until

at last it actually arrive; and everybody be in such a state of

excitement, that an uninitiated spectator might have imagine the whole

household, instead of merely one member of it, be go to be marry.

as every one expect a most fatigue day, of course no one sleep a

wink during the previous night; and as the match be in every way most

desirable, and Alice enjoy as fair a prospect of happiness as those

who love she good could wish she, of course all the woman, the moment

it be light, indulge in the feminine luxury of “a hearty cry;” after

which libation to sensibility, they set to work in real earnest to dress

themselves and each other as becomingly as they possibly could. on the

bride’s dressing-table be find a set of pearl ornament, suppose by

the learn in such matter to have cost at least £500, together with a

slip of paper, represent Mr. Crane’s good wish for her happiness;

which piece of generosity Alice think very amiable and pretty of he,

as indeed it be. Kate (wear a splendid bracelet, giver unknown) and

Emily be to be bridesmaid, and four of the prettiest bosom friend

the bride possess make up the team. these six susceptible young

creature turn out in light blue, and very nice they look, only (as

Master Tom, reprieve for a week from Eton in order to be present at the

ceremony, observe), they do not step well together--a deficiency for

which he account by remark that his cousin Kate carry her head

so high, without a bearing rein, and have such grand action, that it

naturally make the other girl look rather screwy; and indeed Master

Tom’s descriptive power so far exceed our own, that we shall violate

confidence by avail ourselves of a letter he dispatch the next

morning to one of his friend at Eton, in which he give his own

impression of the eventful day. it run as follows:--



“Dear Tipsby,--If this blessed hot weather do not make dripping of

a fellow prematurely, you will have an opportunity of weeping on the

affectionate bussing of ‘your, truly,’ by the 5 p.m. train on Monday

next. the cause of my shirk a week be not, as you impertinently

insinuate, my have ‘over-goose-berry myself,’ but the no less

alarming fact that my eld sister have be and go and commit

matrimony, and I have wait to see she turn off. the ‘shocking event’

arrive at a climax (that’s grammar, ain’t it?) yesterday. I rise with

the lark (i.e. Arthur, my big brother, come and drag I out of bed at

seven o’clock), and dress myself. yes, I should think I do--rather!

Kerseymere sit-upon, make precious loose in the leg, and with a

large pink check on a lavender ground--stunnin! satin vest, colour

to sympathize; silk necktie, pink ground, lavender pattern, once

round--end at least a quarter of a yard long, and such a bow!--there’s

high art for you, my boy!--and last, not least, real Oxford bang-tail

coatee (none of your blackguard boy’ jacket), bright blue, with only

two button and button-hole about it, and all sort of jolly pocket in

original place; but, don’t fret, you shall see it. well, to return

to our mutton, as the french say: very few show at early breakfast,

sensibility supersede appetite in a general way, though I cann’t say

I perceive much difference as regard number one: yet, when I come to

think of it, I recollect I only eat three egg; but then the ham be a

real brick. nothing particular occur till we be to go to church;

but when the trap come round, you may fancy there be something to

look at. my brother-in-law, Coverdale--oh, Tips, he really _be_ a

fine fellow, as handsome as fun--can ride anything you like to put he

across--a dead shot--A1 with his fist (’gad, I should be sorry to get

even a left-hander from he), and as good-temper and jolly as a cock;

but you shall see he some day: well, he come up with his own horse,

a pair of blood bay, he give £350 for ’em, and they’re dirt cheap at

the money; he be a first-rate judge of a horse; but I’ll tell you all

about the trap when we meet. then down come the girl; Ally (that’s my

eld sister), be smother with veil, and flounce, and pearl, and

that sort of nonsense; and look precious pale and interesting, and

like to blub; so we bundle she into the family-coach, and Coverdale

jump into his own trap, and away we all scuttle to church. we’ve get

a good, sharp parson, that can go the pace slap up when he like; and,

know that the Champagne be wait for he, he put the harness

on ’em in no time; and the woman do the water-cart business in

style--where all their tear come from I cann’t think--but they lay the

dust beautifully. then there be sign name in the vestry, and a lot

of chaff about kiss the bride, which so upset that muff, Lambkin,

the parson’s apprentice (curate, I suppose, be what they call the chap),

that he fairly turn tail and bolt. next, we all bundle home again;

Ally in Coverdale’s trap this time (and precious handsome he look, as

he hand she in, I can tell you); and then come the ‘crown mercy’

(as Lambkin say in his sermon last Sunday), the wedding breakfast. the

governor have do the thing well for once in his life, I will say that

for the old boy. there be all the delicacy of all the four season

(one only wish one have four stomach, like a camel, to pay they proper

attention; though I don’t do badly, in spite of my mono-stomachic

conformation). then the Champagne;--my dear Tips, I be not use a mere

figure of rhetoric when I say the supply be unlimited;--how much

I drink I literally _cannot_ tell, but, in mention the affair

to inquire friend, you have well restrict your statement to

half-a-dozen bottle--as a general rule, a gentleman should not take

more on such occasion--it be not every man who possess my strength of

head and self-control. I sit next to one of the bridesmaids--



                   “‘a little, laugh fairy thing,



                   just like an angel on the wing;’



                   a rosebud ’neath the moon’s pale ring;



                   a playful zephyr, whisper



                   some secret to the early Spring.



as Tennyson have it--stunning poet, Tennyson! at first my modesty

prevent my getting on with she quite as fast as I could have wish;

in fact, till after my fourth glass of Champagne, I have not go beyond

ask if she like roast chicken, and say ‘bless you,’ when she

sneeze; which I have since thought might not be quite etiquette, for

she certainly look surprised. however, ‘_in vino jollita_,’ as Cicero

say; after imbibe the ‘rosy,’ I go ahead like bean, and I flatter

myself--ahem!--made a very considerable impression; but then recollect

the expense with which I be get up! the woman who could look on that

bang-tail coatee with indifference must be a heartless tigress. at all

event, Juliana Georgina (sweet, poetical name! benot it, Tips?) don’t;

and if my mother invite she here during the Christmas holyday--which,

betwixt you and I and the post, be not impossible--I should not be

surprised if the affair be to assume quite a serious complexion. it

be some time since I have experience what the mounseer call a ‘_grande

passion._’ when the party generally have pitch into the grub, till

the power of nature wore force to cry ‘hold, enough!’ (though, for my

part, I don’t think one’s bread-basket do by any mean hold enough

on such occasion) everybody drink everybody’s health, and everybody

return thank. my brother-in-law, Coverdale, make a stunning speech,

the good that be make, by long odd; though Master Arthur don’t

disgrace his profession in the jawing line either. the governor do the

pathetic and paternal; but it be precious slow, and all his joke old

one. Mr. Crane (he’s a rich old buffer that be nibble after Ally,

but it ben’t likely she’d have anything to say to he when she’d a

chance of take such a trump-card as my brother-in-law, Coverdale, into

her hand) follow in the benevolent and philanthropic line; but he make

a regular mull of it, bad than the daddy; and when they’d do make

fool of themselves, the sitting break up, and my brother-in-law and

Alice start for the Continent. and the last thing before they be

off, Coverdale, while he be wait in the hall for his wife (woman be

always too late for everything), tip I a flimsy to the tune of

ten pound, and tell I not to forget I be to come to the Park in the

hunting season, and he’d take care to find I a good mount; but if ever

there be a real brick, my brother-in-law Coverdale be the identical

article, and no mistake. and that this be a full, true, and particular

account of this wonderful wedding, sayeth and attesteth,



“your, in the bond of jollity,



“Tom Hazlehurst.”



“P.S.--Advice to cricketer! mind your batting, old fellow; for I’ve be

put up to some first-rate bowling dodge by my brother-in-law, Coverdale

(he’s one of the top-sawyer at Lord’s), that will send your stump

fly about your ear, if you don’t mind your eye. “_Verbum sit.

slow-coachici!_”









CHAPTER XVII.--PLOTTING and COUNTER-plotting.



the same post-bag in which Tom Hazlehurst dispatch his letter to his

schoolfellow, convey also two other epistle write by inmate of

the Grange. for the reader’s benefit we will take the same liberty with

they, which we have already take with the Etonian’s literary effusion.

the first be from Kate Marsden to Miss Arabella Crofton, a lady some

three or four year old than herself, who have be one of the teacher

at the school at which Kate have be bring up, and be now governess

in a german family. Miss Crofton be a woman of unusual mental ability,

and have in a great degree mould Kate’s character, be now her sole

_confidante_ and mentor. it run thus:--



“dear Arabella,--Since I finally determine on follow your advice,

fate seem to have play my game for I, and I now consider it as

secure as anything which have not actually come to pass can be. I

tell you, when I write to you at Baden-Baden, that _his_ friend, Mr.

Coverdale, and my cousin Alice, be evidently becoming attach; you

will therefore be the less surprised to hear that they be marry

yesterday; the matter come about thus:--Soon after I write to you,

Mr. Crane, by my advice, offer; Alice of course refuse he, but so

equivocally (she be quite a child in such thing) that the poor, dear,

dull creature scarcely catch her meaning. I immediately take he in

hand, and, avail myself of the situation, flatter his vanity to

such a degree, that ere the evening finish he believe not only that

Alice would accept he, but that I, Kate Marsden, be hopelessly in love

with he. accordingly, when he learn unmistakably next morning that

Alice mean to refuse he, _my_ good taste stand out in very favourable

contrast. in the meantime, Mr. Crane’s offer bring Mr. Coverdale

to the point, and Alice gladly accept he, in do which she act

wisely, for he be a good, amiable, sterling man! and when the romance

have wear off, and they have get over the bore of awakening from ‘love’s

young dream,’ I believe they will settle down into a very happy couple.

my uncle at first refuse his consent, for Coverdale have only five,

instead of twenty thousand a-year; and Mr. Crane sulk in a corner;

but that strange Mr. D’Almayne, about whom I tell you before, and who

possess a degree of influence over Mr. Crane of which I by no mean

approve, go to he, and persuade he not only to give up Alice

good-humouredly, but actually to play a generous part, and talk my uncle

over to give his consent to my cousin’s union with Mr. Coverdale. thus,

you see, as I begin by say, my game be play for I, and I have only

to sit still and avail myself of the move as the other make they.



“I be much puzzle by this Mr. D’Almayne. he be, unless I be much

deceive, a complete adventurer, scheme for his own advantage (_i_

ought to be able to recognise such a character); but what his object can

have be in this affair I cannot possibly conjecture. pure philanthropy

have nothing to do with it, of that I be certain. again, how he contrive

to influence Mr. Crane to behave so amiably I cannot conceive. sometimes

I fancy he have divine my intention of marry the _millionaire_; but,

if so, why should he aid I in my project?--for I know by his manner

(although he be very cautious) that he admire I himself. certain it

be, that since the conversation I have allude to, Mr. Crane have be at

my foot, and be only wait to offer till he imagine time enough shall

have elapse to prevent the transfer of his affection (?) from Alice

to I appear too ridiculous. however, the affair will unravel itself

some day. and now that my plan be likely to be crown with success,

you will ask I how I feel on the subject. determine as ever! that

which I have begin I will carry through; but, Arabella, I be most

miserable. for myself alone I should not care; to rescue my family from

poverty, I should be happy to sacrifice my personal hope and wish;

but to see Arthur suffer be indeed bitterness, and that he do suffer

frightfully, I, who can read his every look and gesture, cannot for

a moment doubt. oh, that I have know the depth and reality of his

affection soon, or that the necessity be less cogent! then he bear

it with such manly endurance his manner to his family be exactly the

same as usual; not one of they suspect that anything have occur to

pain he. again, it be such an aggravation of my sorrow that he blame

I so deeply! sometimes, when I be talk to Mr. Crane, I catch his

stern, penetrate glance fix upon I with a calm earnestness of

rebuke, which affect I more deeply than could the most vehement

reproach; and when I have act my part for the day, and, in the

solitude of my chamber, I recall all that have pass between we, and

reflect that it be I who have bring this sorrow upon he--I who even

now feel that I love he well than my own soul--I who would gladly

have die for he, I sit, night by night, like a cold statue of despair,

or lie sleepless, shed such tear as I trust God’s mercy permit not

to flow quite in vain! yet it be my duty--you _know_, you cannot doubt

for a moment, it be my duty--you could never have _dare_ to counsel

such a sacrifice of the only thing which can make the burden of life

endurable, a real, deep, true affection, if you have not feel certain it

be my duty.



“you have set I a cruel task, Arabella, but I do not flinch from it;

you shall find your pupil worthy the trouble you have bestow upon she.

I shall write again when anything conclusive be settle. if all go

well, I shall be in a position to fulfil my old promise, and offer you

a home on your return to England. would to God it be likely to be a

happy, though a humbler one! but that be past now. farewell.



“your, in many sense of the word,



“Kate Marsden.”



the third epistle be from Horace D’Almayne to a friend and ally in

Paris. “we transcribe it _verbatim_:--



“Alphonse, mon cher,--I enclose you a draft for 3000 franc, wherewith

I beg you to satisfy Carreau, the tailor, _et tous les autre brigand_,

who render Paris an unsafe residence for I. you will naturally ask how

I have obtain the money; not at the gaming-table, nor on the highway,

like Claude Duval. railroad and police have free England from

highwayman. no; I have for the present fill my purse by study the

great game of life; in which, like all other game, you must either

pillage, or be pillage. you and I, man of wit and of action, naturally

belong to the former class, and have meritoriously labour to fulfil

our destiny. since I have be in England this time I have sedulously

cultivate the _millionaire_ I introduce to you last season, whose

pocket you so obligingly relieve of £500 at piquet. I make a bad

bargain there in only claim one-third of the spoil; I should have

demand half, for without my assistance you could have do nothing

with he; but I understand they, these cautious islander, some of

their blood run in my vein--my mother, as you know, having be an

Englishwoman. however, the time spend on my _millionaire_ have turn

out a more profitable investment than I at all calculate upon. he be

a weak, vacillate character, one of those feeble-minded mortal who

always require some intelligence strong than their own to lean upon.

this support he have find in your humble servant; and so convince

have he become of my diplomatic power, that just at present he can do

nothing without my approval and sanction. his great object in life be

to marry, and it be to assist he in obtain a wife that my counsel

be require. when I first arrive here, I find he be dangle after

a charming little country girl, the daughter of a land proprietor in

these part. I soon discover that the say proprietor, for mercenary

reason, desire the match; but with the young lady I could do nothing.

I give she the full benefit of my eye, which, as you know, be not

willnot to look in vain; but it be no use--even ‘_les petite moustache

noire_,’ usually so irresistible, be throw away upon she; nor have

friend Crane’s £20,000 per annum (_mon Dieu, Alphonse, quelle somme

merveilleuse!_) any more effect upon she. but I soon find a clue to

her obduracy--the silly child be enamour of her brother’s friend, a

fox-hunt squire, a true speciman of young John Bull. I see how the

game would go, John Bull return her affection; he be a real type of

his class. rich, obstinate, and impetuous, he be resolve to marry the

pretty rustic; she be equally determine; her brother befriend he;

the thing be to be, so I arrange my hand accordingly. there be in

the family a _belle cousine_--such a splendid creature, _alphonse!_

beautiful as an angel, the _contour_ of a Juno, the port of an empress.

she have tact and talent; a soul of fire beneath an exterior of ice; she

be poor and ambitious. I could not have hope to find one well suit

to my purpose. she shall marry Crane; his purse will be in her hand; he

will become her slave; and, _alphonse_, she shall be mine! do you doubt

my success, _mon ami?_ Bah! the game be as simple as child’s play. she

be young, ardent; she will marry an old man to satisfy her ambition--she

will despise he. her heart will pine for an object on which to

lavish its tenderness; I shall present myself, become her friend, her

counsellor--and the result? oh, you cannot doubt it. so I have pull

the string, and my _marionnette_ have dance, and be dance. my

_millionaire_ offer--the little rustic refuse he. while he be

smart from this insult, I suggest to he that _la belle cousine_

pine for love of he; praise her wit and beauty; and advise he to

revenge himself by transfer his attention to she. the bait take; I

work out all the minor incident admirably; the young fox-hunter have

marry the pretty rustic, and take she out of my way yesterday. the

lovely Kate, play her own game, labour indefatigably for my interest

also. my friend Crane be delighted, and show his gratitude by _urge_

I to borrow money of him--(I have mortgage my farm in Brittany to

he for six time its value; when the three prior claim upon it be

satisfied, and he bring forward his, this fact will surprise he, and

teach he prudence for the future)--I avail myself of his liberality

with caution, for I must not cut up my golden goose too quickly. but

it be well to have more than one resource to rely upon; so if your rich

young german countess should resolve on visit England, send I timely

notice. I feel that my star be in the ascendant. _Cher Alphonse_, wish

your friend the success which should reward talent, in the use of which

you have so well instruct your devoted,



“Horace.”









CHAPTER XVIII.--ALICE’S first introduction to her HUSBAND’S “QUIET

MANNER.”



if our reader, gentle or simple, will obligingly stretch their

imagination sufficiently to depict for themselves the happiness of

Alice and Harry during the first month of their married life, popularly

denominate the honeymoon, and be content to permit we to resume our

office of chronicler at the termination of that mellifluous (though, to

all but the party concern, especially insipid) season, the reader

aforesaid will merit our eternal gratitude, which we hereby beg to

present they with.



Alice and Harry, then, having be marry one calendar mouth, during

which period they have be “up” the Rhine, and one or two of the

swiss mountain--having see a great many strange thing and strange

people--having talk a vast amount of bad french and bad german, and

narrowly escape an attack of cholera from listen to the dissonance

of that arch-delusion the _Ranz-des-Vaches_--having eat such wonderful

article, cook in such wonderful fashion, that if the genus Bimana

be not providentially omnivorous, they would infallibly have be

poison--having travel over land and water by every specie of

conveyance know to the annal of locomotion, except perhaps a balloon,

or the back of an elephant--have at length make their way to Paris; and

as the inhabitant of that skittish and inconstant capital be then

figuratively pat each other on the back, by way of congratulation

on the fortunate accident which have preserve those that remain alive

after the late revolution from having shoot each other through the

head, our bride and bridegroom, establish in a comfortable hotel, have

determine to remain there till such time as they should mutually agree

upon for their return to England. for, be it observe, that enough of

the halo of the honeymoon yet linger around this young couple, to keep

they in the misty delusion that they possess but one “will of their

own” between they. they have yet to learn that there be a high, truer,

nobler state of association to be arrive at, even here on earth--a

state in which we recognise the deep happiness of be privileged to

sacrifice our own desire to those of the being we love well than

ourselves. a logician may stigmatise this as merely a refined phase of

selfishness; but it be such selfishness as might cling to we in heaven,

and we yet remain sinless. be this as it may, Alice, who have never be

abroad before, find every pleasure enhance by the charm of novelty,

and be in a perfect Elysium of happy excitement. Harry have see and

do it all, and a great deal more besides; and would have find it a

bore, only it be sufficient amusement to he to watch his young wife’s

delight at all she see and hear. whether this amusement of watch,

petting, and spoil Alice, be at all begin to lose its charm, may

be gather from the follow conversation:--



“Harry, you sleepy old thing, this be the third time I’ve ask you

whether Madame de Beauville be certain of get we an invitation to

Lord N--------’s picnic at Versailles; do rouse yourself and answer I!”



thus apostrophise, Coverdale--who be stretch at full length on (and

beyond) a brocade sofa, and have be lazily watch his wife, as with

a vast deal of unnecessary energy she stitch away at a button, which,

accord to button-nature, have “come off” her husband’s glove the very

first moment he attempt to draw it on--half-raise himself on his

elbow as he replied--



“there be nothing certain under the sun; except that my little wife

have the prettiest hand and arm of any woman (I don’t care who she may

be--Jew, Turk, infidel, heretic, or Christian) in the known world. but

that old humbug, Madame de Beauville, promise I faithfully to do she

good for we--not that I’d believe she on her oath; she try to book

I for one of her scraggy daughter, the last time I be here; but it

wouldn’t act--the trap be too visible, and the bait not sufficiently

tempting. what very high action you have with that needle-hand of your!

you’ll overreach yourself, or get sprain in the back sinew, some of

these day, if you don’t look out.”



“I will not allow you to ‘talk stable’ in that way, sir,” return

Alice, playfully shake her finger at her recumbent spouse; “you shall

not go to the picnic at all, you naughty boy, unless you behave well.

come, get up,” she continue, “if you lie down again you’ll be asleep in

a minute; you’re so idle, you’re actually grow fat!”



“Nonsense, you don’t really mean it!” exclaim Harry, spring up with

a bind which shake the room, and startle Alice so much that she dropt

the glove, needle, thread, button, and all, prick her finger into the

bargain. “by Jove,” he continue, regard himself anxiously in a large

pier-glass, “so I be! I tell you what, Mrs. Coverdale, this be get

serious, and must be put a stop to!”



“my dear Harry, how dreadfully impetuous you are!--you’ve make I

jump so, that I’ve dropt my work, and be and go and prick my

favourite finger, as you say in your horrid slang--look!” so say, the

pretty Alice pout like a spoilt child, as she then most assuredly be,

and hold up the injure finger to excite her husband’s commiseration.

when a proper degree of pity have be show, and the necessary amount

of matrimonial felicity transact, Alice resume: “what a dreadfully

conceited fellow you be, to be so alarmed at grow fat! be you

afraid of lose your beauty?”



“my how much?” be the astonished reply. “what funny idea do come into

a woman’s head to be sure! why, you silly child, do you think I ever set

up for a ‘beauty’ man? or care two straw what I look like? such folly

be very well for get up puppy, like Horace D’Almayne; but they’re not

in my line.”



“I’m sure you’re fifty time as handsome as Mr. D’Almayne,” be Alice’s

eager rejoinder; “but” she continue reflectively “if you be not afraid

of your good look, why be you so horrified at the idea of grow

fat?”



Harry colour slightly, and try to evade the question; but his wife’s

curiosity, be by this time excite, be not so easily baffle, and

Coverdale have nothing for it but to confess the truth, which he do

thus:--



“well, if you must know, little wife, I’ve a bay colt by Fencer out of

a Harkaway mare, and a chesnut filly by Hercules out of Bulfinch, both

rise five (I refuse 600 guineas for the pair of ’em a year ago),

which I expect to do most of my work next hunt season; but as

they’re both young unmade horse, I would not ride over twelve stone

for anything; nothing cow a young horse more than overweighte he at

start.”



“oh, Harry!” exclaim Alice reproachfully, “I think you mean to give

up hunt now--I’m sure you say so when you were----, that is, before

we be marry. why, you would be away from I more than half the day

every time you go out! besides, it’s so dangerous! oh, no; you may go

shooting sometimes, and I can ride a pony and mark for you, as I use to

do with papa and Arthur, but you must not hunt.”



“and cann’t you ride and see the hound throw off, darling? it’s one of

the prettiest sight in the world. the first thing I mean to do when

we get back, be to buy you a perfect lady’s horse; something rather

different from that brute poor old Crane give you.”



“then you willn’t promise to give up hunting, you naughty boy--not even

when I ask you to do so to please I?”



 and, confident in her own power,

the young wife cast a look, half-implore, half-commanding, on her lord

and master, which he would have find it no easy matter to resist to

a degree which should vindicate his right to such a title, when the

opportune entrance of the valet, with a packet of letter, extricate

he from his dilemma.



“a note from Madame de Beauville, contain an invitation to the

picnic!--how delightful!” exclaim Alice, appeal for sympathy to

her well half; but he be engage in peruse the follow epistle,

which, owe to the peculiarity both of diction, writing, and

spelling, it be not too easy to decypher:--



“honour Sur,--I remain your humbel survunt and gaim-keepur as wos,

John Markum, whech I would not ’ave intrude on you injoye of

yourself in fur part as be most fit, having marry a beutiful yung

English lady, as they do tell I, and the darter of squire hazlehurst

likewise; which having cause a many thing to go rong at home, I thort

you would be glad to hear on it, and so rite, which I ’ope be no

offence, the same be unintenshonal on my part; but the new stewart

be agoin on oudacious, a ordering of I to kill gaim for he to sell,

which, refuse to do, agin your ordur, honoured sur, and he put

the money in his durty pocket, savin your present, be discharge with

four small childring, and a little stranger expect, which would have

be welcome, but now must be a birding on the parish with his poor

mother; which, knowin Honoured Sur, as injustice to unborn innocent be

not in your line, nor in that of any gent but dishonest stewart speak

agen in Scriptur, I umbly take the liburty of trustin in Providence,

which support his poor mother agen the thort of workous baby-linen,

that hang heavy on a woman accustom to wash for the family and keep

herself respectabul; so do not give up all hope of see you home,

honour Sur, _before every hed of gaim be destroy_, in which case

Mr. stewart may larn that honesty be the good politic arter all; and so

remain,



“your humbel survunt to commarnd,



“John Markujm.”



“P.S.--The rabbid be agoin to town in the carriur’s cart, frightful,

likewise the peasant.”



“my dear Harry, there be to be a _bal costumé_ after the picnic, and

that kind Madame de Beauville send we ticket for both! how charming!”

 exclaim Alice, so engross in her pleasant anticipation that she

have not observe the gloom gather upon her husband’s brow, and be,

therefore, quite unprepared when he break out suddenly--



“’pon my word, it’s enough to drive a man distract! the moment one

turn one’s back everything go to------Ahem!--



“here’s a scoundrel, who live eight year with Lord Flashipan, and who

come to I with a character fit for a bishop, and now he’s not only

sell my game by cart-load, but have actually dare to discharge

Markum!--as honest, trustworthy a fellow, and as good a keeper as man

need to require. oh, if I be but near he with a horse-whip, I wouldn’t

mind pay for the assault! I’d give he something to remember Harry

Coverdale by--he might thank his star if I don’t break every bone in

his skin. and that poor fellow Markum turn out, and all his little

curly-head brat, too--that make I as mad as any of it!” he stride

up and down the room angrily, his wife watch he in terrified

amazement. at length he exclaim abruptly--“Alice, my dear, we must

start for England to-morrow morn----”



“but the picnic and the _bal costumé,_ Harry, dearest, do not come

off till the day after that; and Madame de Beauville have just send I

ticket for they both!” urge his wife, timidly.



“I’m sorry, my love, that it should have happen so, but go we must,”

 be the unyielding reply.



“but Madame de Beauville have take so much trouble, and be so kind,”

 murmur Alice.



“the devil fly away with the old hag and her kindness too!” be the

angry rejoinder. “I wish to heaven she’d attend to her own affair, and

not try to inspire you with a taste for dissipation. however, there be

a quiet way of settle this question: if you choose to stay and go to

this party, stay; and when I’ve be to Coverdale, and settle score

with that rascal cribbin, I’ll come back and fetch you; so please

yourself.”



Poor Alice! this be her first experience of Harry’s “quiet way;” the

imply indifference be more than she could bear, and murmur, in a

break voice, “do you wish to leave I already!” she burst into a flood

of tear.



of course, that settle the question. Harry call himself a brute, and

think he be one, and feel as if he could have cry too, when he see

the bright drop glisten in Alice’s soft, love eye, and so set

himself to work in earnest to console she; and succeed to such an

extent that ere a quarter of an hour have elapse, Alice pronounce

herself to be a silly child, and wonder how she could have be so

foolish as to cry because Harry, the kind and most affectionate of

husband, have evince his just indignation on learn how the miscreant

Cribbins have tyrannize over the faithful and unfortunate Markum, and

his dear little interesting, curly-pat family. then, as a personal

favour to herself, she beg Harry would let she give up the picnic,

and start for England next morning; she would be quite ready to go at

five A.M., or early, if he wish it. to which Harry reply that

nothing should induce he to deprive she of a pleasure he know she have

set her heart on; that a french picnic and _bal costumé_ be thing she

could never see in England, and that as they _be_ there it would be

really a pity not to avail themselves of so good an opportunity; and he

beg she would instantly sit town and write his thank, as well as

her own, to that thoroughly friendly, kind-hearted woman, Madame de

Beauville.



while Alice be thus engage, Harry take pen in hand, and dash off a

hurried epistle to Arthur, beg he to run down to Coverdale Park

by the next train, and in his name cashier cribbin, and re-instate

the ill-use Markum and his much-endure wife, if possible, before the

arrival of the expect little stranger should add another small item to

his embarrassment.



the picnic be a very gay one, and the _bal costumé_ all that Alice’s

“fancy have paint it,”--and a few over, as her slang husband be

pleased to express it. the young couple go dress as Romeo and

Juliet. Harry, if leave to himself, would have choose a clown’s suit of

motley; but Alice consider the romantic preferable to the ridiculous,

and so he yield; though it must be confess that he afford the most

stalwart, robust, and cheerful representation of the forlorn veronese

lover that can well be imagine. Alice (although she also would have

look the part well if her damask cheek have not glow quite so

brightly with health and happiness) make an extremely fascinating little

Juliet, and produce a sensation which delight her husband, and bid

fair to turn her own pretty head.



the _bal_ and picnic be safely accomplish, and Alice perceive

that, although he do not again openly broach the subject, Harry’s

thought be continually wander to Coverdale Park, pretend (like

a love little hypocrite as she be) that she also begin to feel

home-sick; and that, although Paris be all very charming and agreeable

for a little while, she should be very sorry to stay there long. thus,

the day of their departure be fix, so that Harry should be enable to

reach home before the first of September,--as Alice (choose the less

of two evil) mean to encourage his shooting (occasionally for a few

hour), as a bribe to induce he to give up that senseless and dangerous

pastime, hunting; and she actually believe that her influence could

accomplish all this--dear, innocent little Alice!



on the morning before they be to start, a letter arrive from the

Grange. Alice read it eagerly.



“oh, Harry!” she exclaim, “what do you think Emily tell I? what a

strange, extraordinary, wretched thing!--it seem quite impossible!”



“what be it, little wife?” return Harry. “have your father turn

free-trader, and invite Messrs. Cobden and Bright to stay with he; or

have Arthur be make Lord Chancellor?”



“something almost as wonderful,” be the rejoinder. “Mr. Crane have

propose for my cousin Kate’s hand, and she have positively accept

he!”



“and a very sensible thing, too,” reply Harry, who, lean over the

back of his wife’s chair, be wickedly and surreptitiously attach an

ornamental pen-wiper to the end of one of her long, silky ringlet;

“I dare say, now, you’re bitterly repent your own folly in having

allow she the chance.”



Alice, turn her head quickly to administer condign punishment for

this speech, by a tug at her lord and master’s ample whisker, become

aware of the scheme lay against her unconscious ringlet by reason of a

twitch, which Harry, unprepared for her sudden movement, be unable to

avoid give it.



“you silly boy! what be you do to I? oh! you’ve tie a horrid thing

to my pet curl; take it off directly, sir! but seriously, now, about

Kate;--dearest Harry--do be sensible, please, and let I talk to you.”

 this exhortation be call forth by the fact of the incorrigible

Coverdale having place the pen-wiper--which be a sort of cross between

a three-barrel cocked hat and an improbable pyramid--on the top of

his wife’s head, just where the cros-road in the parting of her hair

occur.



“talk away, darling; I’m about as sensible as it’s at all likely you’ll

ever find I,” be the reply.



“‘well, don’t you really and truly think it very shocking that such a

girl as Kate--so clever and handsome, so unusually superior in every

point--should throw herself away upon that silly old man, whom she

cannot even respect?” rejoin Alice.



“if I must speak the plain truth,” reply Harry, “I should say that a

girl who could make such a sacrifice of her own free will ben’t worth

pity for it; she must be both mercenary and ambitious--serious fault

in a man, but positive vice in a woman, because in yield to they she

be sin against all the well instinct of her nature: for such a

character I can feel no sympathy.”



“but indeed, Harry, she be not such a dreadful heartless creature as you

imagine she; at least, she never use to be. on the contrary, when we

be all child together, she be rather high-flown and romantic.

it be during the time that she be at school, and under the care of a

horrid woman, a Miss Crofton--”



“a Miss how much?” inquire Harry.



“Miss Crofton.”



“what be her christian name?” continue Harry.



“Arabella,” be the reply.



“by Jove! do you ever see she? be she a tall, dark-look creature,

with great flash eye like a gipsy’s?”



“yes, that be an exact description of she,” return Alice, in surprise;

“but why do you ask? what do you know of she?”



“no good,” return Harry, mysteriously, shake his head; “but never

mind, go on.”



“I be only go to say that I feel sure Kate must have some well

reason than a mere wish to become a great lady, to induce she to marry

Mr. Crane. you know her father and mother be very poor, and she have

several young brother and sister; perhaps she wish to help they.”



“I dare say she do,” reply Harry, turn away to conceal a

yawn; “nobody be all bad, any more than they be all the other thing.

character be like zebra--alternate stripe of black and white; the

only difference be, that in some one colour predominate, in some the

other.”



there be a pause, then in a low voice Alice resumed--



“Harry, do it ever occur to you (of course, I do not want you to

betray confidence even to I), but do you ever suspect that Arthur be

attach to Kate?”



“never in my life,” be the unhesitate reply. “Arthur always laugh

the tender passion, as he use to call it, to scorn.”



“I feel almost certain it be so,” continue Alice; “but I most

earnestly hope, for his sake, that I be mistaken; if not, only conceive

how wretched this engagement will make he!”



“judge by my own feeling, when I fancy you have accept the

irresistible cotton-spinner,” return Coverdale, “I should say that

Prometheus, who have a perennial vulture making ‘no end’ of a meal on his

liver (which I take to be simply a metaphorical method of state that

the unfortunate Titan be afflict with hepatic disease), be, by

comparison, ‘a gentleman who live at home at ease.’”



“I use to fancy sometimes,” pursue Alice, “that Kate return his

affection; but she be so reserved, and her manner be always so calm

and self-possess, that it be impossible to judge, with any degree of

certainty, what her feeling might be. however, this settle the point

so far as she be concern; if she have really care about he, she could

never have consent to marry Mr. Crane.”



“Hum! well I don’t know that,” return Harry, meditatively; “it be

not all woman who have such simple, true, love heart as you, my own

darling; and a pupil of Arabella Crofton’s may very well be capable of

love one man and marry another.”



“why, how came you to know anything about Miss Crofton, Harry?”

 exclaim Alice, her curiosity be thoroughly rouse by her husband’s

second allusion to some previous acquaintance with her cousin’s

_ci-devant_ governess.



“I meet she in Italy, if you must know,” return Coverdale “she live as

governess in a family where I visit, and I see a good deal of she at

one time.”



there be something so odd and conscious in his manner of speak, that

Alice exclaim, “she fall in love with you, I be certain of it. come,

confess now that I be right.”



“do you think that every woman must need be as foolish as yourself,

you silly child?” be the uncomplimentary reply. “I can assure you, Miss

Crofton be as utterly unlike you in taste, habit, and opinion, as she

be in person; and that be a pretty considerable assertion, I take it.

and now it be time for you to get ready for our last drive in the Bois

de Boulogne, and I must go out and buy a clean pair of glove; so for

ten minute I shall wish you an affectionate farewell.”



thus say, Harry quit the apartment; and Alice, go to prepare

for her drive, forgot, for the time, her husband’s mysterious intimacy

with Miss Crofton--it occur to she afterwards, indeed, when----, but

we must not anticipate. the next morning see they _en route_. as they

be about to embark at Boulogne, a sensation be create, at the hotel

at which they wait till the tide serve for the packet to start, by

the arrival of a travel carriage draw by four horse, with a lady

inside, and her _soubrette_, and an outlandish, courier-like creature in

the rumble.



“by Jove!” exclaim Harry, who, ensconce behind a window-curtain, have

be examine the turn out with all the interest with which a position

of enforce idleness invest every trifle. “by the power there’s a

foreign coronet on the carriage, and ditto on Don Whiskerando’s button!

I wonder what she be like! young and pretty, by all that be interesting

and romantic! I dare say she be go to cross in the same boat as we

be. yes! Whiskerandos be gesticulate and explaining, and the landlord

wave his hand in the direction of the pier. now come the bore of be

a married man: what a splendid adventure I be shut out from! if I

be but single, an opportunity now offer of captivate a lovely

and accomplished foreign Countess, with a dowry of diamond in her

dressing-box, and a gold mine in her precious pocket: there’s a good

opening for a nice young man!”



“pray avail yourself of it,” return Alice. “don’t let I be any

obstacle; carry off the Countess, and I will remain behind with that

noble creature whom you style Don Whiskerandos. I prefer he infinitely

to you, he be so like a very well-train baboon.”



Harry’s conjecture that the mysterious Countess mean to cross in the

same vessel with, himself and his wife prove correct; for, scarcely

have he see Alice comfortably establish on a snug bench, where, if

the sea-fiend should be so uncourteous as to attack she, she could on an

emergency lie down, when daintily trip along the human chicken-ladder

which connect the vessel with the shore, the graceful, _bien chaussé_,

little foot of the Countess. then ensue a grand scene. whiskerando

either do not comprehend, or refuse to comply with some demand of

the hotel commissionaire, who have take upon himself the charge of

the baggage, and who accordingly resist his conveying his mistress’s

luggage on board. whiskerando grimace and chatter in a polyglot

jargon, apparently compound of every language under heaven, and

utterly incomprehensible to the deep philologist extant:

the commissionaire be immovable. Whiskerandos implored--the

commissionaire be deaf to his entreaty. Whiskerandos storm--the

commissionaire be inexorable. whiskerando, unable to endure his

fate with calmness, went rave mad--he swear oath so replete with

improbable consonant that it be only a wonder they do not smash every

tooth in his head; he stump, shriek, clench his fist, and shake

they in the face of his adversary--in vain; the commissionaire remain

adamant, and prepare actually to carry off the offend luggage.



“look at that ape,” observe Harry to his wife, who be watch the

scene, half in amusement, half in terror; “he’s go into sky-blue

fit apparently: of all absurd sight an angry foreigner be the most

ridiculous. do you see his moustaches?--they actually stand on end with

fury, like the hair on the tail of an excited cat. but see, the Don

appeal to his mistress; the Countess will have to settle the affair

_in propriâ personâ_.” this affair, however, be not to be arrange

so easily; for the inflexible commissionaire prove as deaf to the

entreaty of the mistress as he have show himself to the threatening

of the man; and the Countess, if countess she be, having remonstrate

to no purpose in a gentle, timid voice, look helplessly round,

as though she would appeal to society at large to aid she in her

difficulty.



“poor thing! those man have frighten she; she look ready to cry!”

 exclaim Alice. “Harry, dear, do go and see if you cannot assist

she--you understand how to manage those people so well; besides, they

always attend to a gentleman.”



thus urge, Harry cross the deck, and Alice see he take off his

hat and address the interesting foreigner; she bow her head, and be

evidently make a grateful answer; then Harry turn to the disputant,

who both assail he with a volley of word, upon which he first

silence Whiskerandos, then he exchange a few cabalistic sentence with

the commissionaire, and slip a talisman into his hand, whereupon,

with the celerity of some harlequinade trick, he change into an

amiable, oblige creature, only too anxious to please everybody, and

go off, pat Whiskerandos on the back, and call he a _brave

garçon_, to assist with his own silver-absorb finger in convey

the Countess’s luggage on board. then the Countess overwhelm Harry

with thank, and Harry smile benignantly upon the Countess, and they

“talk conversation” for a few minute; after which they both look at

Alice, and Harry with his good company manner on (which be merely his

own natural manner _brush smooth_), cross over to she.



“she be really a Countess,” he begin, “and a very charming, refined

style of young woman too. she want to be introduce to you, so come

along.”



“but, Harry, dear, I shall break my neck, or tumble into the sea, if I

attempt to walk; just look how its rolling about!” remonstrate Alice,

whose essentially terrestrial education have give she rather a horror of

all nautical matter.



“we’ll fall in together then,” return Harry, laugh; “at all event

don’t let we fall out about it. come along, little wife, and trust

yourself to I; I’ve pace a vessel’s deck when the sea’s show rather a

different sort of surface from that which it wear to-day.”



as he speak, he place his arm round his wife’s slender waist, and half

support, half lead she across the deck in safety.



“what be her name, Harry?” inquire Alice, as they be effect the

transit.



“Bertha seem to be her christian name--of course her surname be

something unpronounceable and appalling; but if you call her countess

Bertha that will do; at all event, as long as our acquaintance with she

be likely to last,” be the reply.



Alice having never before encounter a real, live Countess, feel

a little shy at first; but the young foreigner’s manner, which be

perfectly easy without be too familiar, soon re-assure she, and the

two girl (for the Countess appear little old than Alice) chat

away, at first in French, but when it come out that the stranger

likewise understand English, in that language, to their mutual

satisfaction. but in about half-an-hour a breeze (not metaphorical, but

literal) spring up, and the Countess signify her wish to retire to the

cabin, upon which Coverdale summon her maid, and then assist she to

effect the desire change of locality.









chapter XIX.--A comedy of ERRORS.



“there now, I consider I’ve do the polite in the first style of

fashion and elegance,” observe Harry, self-complacently, as he rejoin

his wife; “Horace D’Almayne himself could not have polish off the

young woman more handsomely, for all his moustache.”



“how you do hate that poor Mr. D’Almayne!” return Alice, laugh. “do

you know, I think you be jealous of he.”



“I be once, and that’s the truth--very savage it make I too; for if

you could have be fascinate by such a puppy as that, I feel I have

mistake your character _in toto_, and that the Alice I love be a

creature of my own imagination, not a reality--but I soon see my error.”



Alice glance at he archly. “be you _quite_ sure you do not fall

into a great mistake when you fancy yourself so certain of my

indifference?” she inquire.



Harry fix his eye upon she with a look of inquiry, which, when he see

that she be joke, change to an expression of tenderness;--“I could

not look in that dear face, where every thought can be read as in a

book, and remain jealous for five minute,” he answer.



Alice make no reply, unless place her little hand in that of her

husband, with a confiding gesture, can be call so.



the wind continue fresh, the unfortunate Countess do not re-appear;

but Coverdale and his wife, be so happily constitute that the

tossing produce no ill effect upon they, remain upon deck till the

vessel reach Dover. amid the scene of confusion attend the arrival

of a steamer, Harry, having secure his luggage, be stand sentinel

over a moderately-sized pyramid, which he have cause to be erect of

the same, when Alice, then seat upon a large black trunk, which she

have seduce her husband into buying in the Rue St. Honoré, and which

would very easily have hold she, bonnet, cloak, and all, suddenly

exclaim, “oh, Harry! do look at that young exquisite who have just come

on board; why he’s the very moral, as the old woman say, of the person

we’ve be discuss--Mr. D’Almayne!”



“by Jove, he’s more than the moral!” return Coverdale, as the

individual thus allude to advanced towards they bow and smile,

“it’s the veritable Horace himself, I vow--talk of the devil----. my

dear fellow, how be you? who’d have think of see you here! you’ve

not turn custom-house officer, have you? I’ve nothing contraband

about I, except this morning’s _Galignani;_ if you be inclined to make a

seizure of that, you’re very welcome.”



“you’re near the mark than you imagine, my dear sir,” be the reply;

“though not exactly a professional _attaché_ to the Customs, I must

own that I be here as an amateur in that capacity--my object be to

facilitate the transmission of a lady’s luggage.”



“Yes?--how interesting! I hope she’s young and pretty,” observe Alice.

“come Mr. D’Almayne, having let we so far into the secret, it’s no use

to affect the mysterious, so tell we who and where she be.”



“where she be, perhaps you may be able to inform I, my dear Mrs.

Coverdale,” reply D’Almayne, smooth his moustache. “the object of

my search be a young german lady, the Countess Bertha von Rosenthal, to

whom I have promise my friend, the Honourable Mrs. Botherby, to act

as _preux chevalier_. accordingly I come down by train this morning,

provide with an order from the Board of Customs to the people here to

pass the Countess’s luggage unexamine, and show she every attention

which may facilitate her transit; thence I be to escort she and her

property to Park Lane; by all which ‘double, double, toil and trouble,’

I secure an early introduction to, and confer a favour upon, a young and

lovely heiress.”



“that’s my Countess, as sure as fate!” exclaim Harry. “she say she

name be Bertha”--and he then relate to D’Almayne the circumstance

with which the reader have already be make acquainted. “and,” he

continue in conclusion, as a female figure, lean on the arm of the

_soubrette_ and Don Whiskerandos, emerge from the lady’ cabin--“and

here she come, look rather poorly still--nothing of the water-witch

about she, at all event. have you meet before, or shall I introduce

you?”



“do, by all mean, _mon cher_; we be total stranger to each other,”

 be the reply. and with an injunction to Alice to remain where she be

till he should return, Harry seize D’Almayne’s arm, and hurry he

away. before two minute have elapse, Coverdale return alone.



“it’s all right,” he say: “but come along; D’Almayne’s order will clear

our luggage also, and we can all get away together.”



then ensue a grand _scena_ of bustle and confusion, during which,

support by her husband’s stalwart arm, Alice catch glimpse of

D’Almayne smile to show his white tooth, and strive vigorously to

enact the part of guardian angel to the rich young heiress.



“that puppy be in his glory now,” observe Coverdale, snappishly; “I

dare say that silly woman will take he at his own price, and believe

in he to any extent to which he may like to lead she--perhaps marry

he after all, and make he Count von Rosenthal: that would suit his

complaint exactly, the fortune-hunt young humbug!”



“my dear Harry, what word!” exclaim Alice. “you be really quite

savage to-day; I shall be oblige to take Mr. D’Almayne under my

protection, if you go on so.”



“no need to do that, my dear,” return Harry, his face resume its

usual bright, kind expression, as his glance fall upon his wife;

“your _protégé_ be quite certain to take the good possible care of

himself--now come along;” and in another five minute they have leave

the vessel and enter a railroad-carriage, in which the Countess and

D’Almayne have already establish themselves.



the journey to London be a very agreeable one;--the Countess, having

recover with marvellous celerity the moment she place her pretty

little foot on _terra firma_, exert herself to make up for lost

time, and succeed so well that D’Almayne, who become more and more

_empressé_ and devote every moment, determine, if he should be able

to ascertain beyond a doubt that her fortune be as large as it have

be represent, to give up every other speculation, and devote all his

energy to secure the hand and purse of this fascinating foreigner. as

they approach the London Bridge terminus the Countess, turn to her

new guardian, inquire whether it be very far to Park Lane:



“about half an hour’s drive. the carriage will, I trust, be there to

meet this train; though, owe to our having avoid all delay at the

Custom-house, we shall be in town some two hour soon than the other

steam-boat passenger. however, if we arrive early than be expect,

it will only be an agreeable surprise to our kind friend, Mrs.

Botherby.”



“_Mais oui!_” return the Countess with a look of innocent perplexity;

“and who may be _cette chere_ Madame Bodairebie?”



“Mrs. Botherby, my dear Countess,” return D’Almayne, who begin to

think his charming friend must be slightly insane, “Mrs. Botherby--the

Honourable Mrs. Botherby--be the lady who obtain for I the pleasure

of render you this slight service.”



“_Quelle drôle de choose_. I shall not know some Mrs. Bodairebie no

vere,” be the astounding reply.



“but--but--” stammer D’Almayne, as an idea occur to he

sufficiently alarming to surprise he out of his usual _sing froid_,

“excuse I--but surely you _be_ the Countess Bertha von Rosenthal?”



a peal of silvery laughter be the only reply the unhappy exquisite be

at first able to obtain; but, as soon as she could recover herself, the

mysterious lady begin: “_mille pardon!_ I be so rude to make a laugh

at you, but I be so gay I alvay must laugh ven I see a ridiculous thing

in front of--bah--vot you call before I. _Mon cher Monsieur_, you have,

I know not how, tumble into a delusion. I be not at all zie Countess

Bertha von Rosenthal, but zie Countess Bertha Hasimoff, _en route_

to stay viz my friend, Lady St. Clare, in Park Lane, London, till my

hosband shall capture zie permission of die Czar to leave Petersburg and

transport himselfs after I.”



Coverdale, Alice, and the Countess Hasimoff, glance first at D’Almayne,

then at one another, and then--but if they be heartless enough to

laugh consumedly, we will draw a veil over such unfeele conduct.









CHAPTER XX.--THE MORNING of the first of SEPTEMBER.



the first of September! no wonder if we be a covey of partridge what

we should think about the first of September, and how, generalize from

that idea, we should feel towards the race of men,--sons of gun, as in

partridge parlance we should, doubtless, metaphorically term they! we

wonder from what point we should regard pointer (disappointer, as a

witty friend of ours call a couple of “wild young dog” who run in

upon their game, and cheat he of a promising shot), or how we should

look upon a setter make a “dead set” at we! reasoning by analogy,

and not suppose partridge to be well Christians than Christians

themselves, we fear we should consider sportsman (the very name be an

addition of insult to injury) great brute than their four-footed

ally; and that the idea of stand fire (either kitchen or gun), the

notion of the roasting we must undergo after we have be plucked,--of

the way in which we should be cut up by a set of blade, who be,

after all, ready enough to pick our brain, and avail themselves of our

merry-thought, would put we in such a flutter that it would be a mercy

if we be not to show the white feather, and refuse to die game after

all.



such, however, be by no mean the sentiment with which Harry

Coverdale look forward to the first of September. on the contrary,

although he endeavour to disguise the fact from his wife, and indeed

from himself, as far as in he lay, the truth be that he be as much

delighted at the prospect of a good day’s partridge shooting, as the

veri school-boy release from the drudgery of dictionary and grammar.

Markum, that trustworthy custodian of game, and original speciman of a

polite letter-writer, who have be safely re-instate in his office,

and receive such handsome present of baby-linen and other infantry

accoutrement that the illustrious “little strange,” who have wisely

postpone his arrival till the evil day have depart, bid fair to be

clothe in a style befit the heir-apparent to a dukedom rather than

to a double-barrel gun--Markum report that although the hare and

pheasant (which he persist in call peasant) have suffer some

diminution from the practice of the dishonest steward, yet that he’d

never “in all his bear day see such a bless sight o’ partridge.”

 stimulate by this information, and by the recollection that on the

precede first of September he have be kick his heel and curse

his evil fortune, as he perform quarantine in a red-hot port of the

Mediterranean, Harry--have greatly amuse Alice by the earnest zeal

with which, on the 31st of August, he examine and re-examine his “Joe

Manton,” and the exact and stringent order he give in regard to the

feeding of his dog, than which the most fastidious invalid could not

have be more delicately and precisely diet--awake at four o’clock on

the eventful morning, and, without disturb Alice, who be sleep as

calmly as a child, rise and dress himself in a thoroughly workmanlike

shooting costume. having accomplish this feat without wake Alice,

he write on a bit of paper, “good morning and good-bye, dearest. as

I intend to have a glorious day of it, do not expect I till near

dinner-time, when I hope to return with a full bag and an awful

appetite. your, ever, H. C.,” and place it on his wife’s

dressing-table, steal on tiptoe to the door, close it noiselessly after

he; and when, three hour afterwards, Alice open her eye, he be

stride through stubble on the farth side of the estate, having

bag four brace of bird and a well-condition and respectable Jack

hare.



Mrs. Coverdale be some few minute before she be, literally, awake

to a sense of her situation; and the lady’s-maid entering while she be

still between sleep and wake, she half unconsciously ask the not

unnatural question--“What have become of your master?”



“if you please, Mem, Master’s be out shoot partringer ever since

five o’clock, Wilkins say. if you please, Mem, there’s a note for you,

Mem, lie on your dressing-table, in Master’s handwriting.”



rouse herself, Alice read it eagerly. the content do not seem

particularly to please she, for, as she refolde the paper, she look

grave, and give vent to a mild sigh. “do not undraw the curtain,” she

say: “come again in an hour, Ellis; I feel sleepy, and there be nothing

to get up for,” she add, in a slightly pettish tone. fall asleep

the moment she lay her head upon the pillow, Alice dream that when

she come down to breakfast she find Harry have return, say that

he could not bear to leave she alone all day, and so have come back and

wish to drive she to call upon that agreeable woman, Mrs. Felicia

Tabinette (a name with which she be inspire for the occasion, as no

such neighbour exist), to which proposition she gladly assent, they

have go out in a pony-chaise make of coral and mother-of-pearl, and

draw by two lovely little sea-green pony with lilac mane and tail,

and harness make of the good point lace. and she be just advance the

unanswerable proposition that, as lace be the fit material of which

to make a lady’s collar, it must also be the most proper fabric for that

of a horse, when the inexorable Ellis appear for the second time, and

dispel all her bright vision by awaken she to the dull reality.

Alice, however, take her revenge upon that “dis-illusioning”--as a

Frenchman would have call it--lady’s-maid, for she be more fastidious

and difficult to please, and almost snappish, than Ellis have ever know

she before, insomuch that the excellent Abigail afterwards propound

her opinion in the servant’ hall, that “Missus be _tuter fay_ outer

sort,” which dishearten fact she account for by the hypothesis

that she--Mrs. Coverdale--must have get out of bed with the wrong foot

foremost.



while the tea for her solitary breakfast be draw, Alice, have no

one else to look at, amuse herself by regard her own natural--no

term could be more appropriate--face in a large pier-glass, and be

quite startled to behold the unmistakeably cross expression which

characterize it. take herself to task for this, she, sip her tea,

which do not taste nearly so good as when Harry be at home,

mentally decide that she be very unreasonable, and childish, and

ridiculous--that when Harry have be devote himself for the last month

to her pleasure and amusement, go to ball and all sort of place

which he do not care a pin about, solely to please she, it be horribly

selfish in she to grudge he a few hour to devote to a favourite

pursuit--though how man could find delight in kill those poor bird,

she could not tell. she do not so much wonder about other people;

she believe man be generally cruel; but Harry be so unusually

kind-hearted. she suppose it must be the excitement, and the beautiful

scenery, and the interest in watch those dear, clever dog stick out

their long tail to point at the partridge with--which, look at

it in a chesterfieldian point of view, be decidedly impolite, if not

positively rude, of they; and yet she have hear gentleman talk about

their sporting dog be so well-breed.



having thus reason herself into a wise frame of mind, she resolve

to make the good of it; and suddenly recollect she have at least a

thousand thing to do, which she be continually put aside till

some time or other when Harry should be out, she decide that _this_ be

_the_ time, and that now or never must they all be do. accordingly,

she set vigorously to work, and write three letter one after another,

to three former schoolfellow, wherein she describe her husband as a

specie of modern demi-god, compound of equal part of Solomon and

Adonis, with a dash of Achilles throw in to do justice to his heroic

quality; and depict matrimonial felicity in such glowing colour,

that the rich and prettiest of her correspondent elope the next

week with her music-master; and one of the other, who be neither rich

nor pretty, turn pious out of spite, and go into a sort of high

Church Protestant nunnery-and-water, to punish the man, who, it must

be confess, appear to submit to the trial with the most cheerful

resignation. then Alice bring out a large roll of bill, and a thick

house-keeping book, rule with blue line, and have a business-like

smell of new leather about the binding, which Alice flatter herself

would impress even the stately housekeeper (who be old enough to be her

mother, and stiff enough for anything; and of whom Alice, in her secret

soul, stand very much in dread) with a deep sense of she be a very

dragon of housewifery, prepare to be down upon the slight attempt at

peculation like an avenge fury. but the bill be so complicated, and

never would add up twice alike, and the butcher be so inconsistent and

slippery about his price, sometimes charge 7d. and sometimes 7-1/2d. as

“if once a pound of mutton, always a pound of mutton,” be not an

incontrovertible axiom; and the baker be as bad, besides choose

to spell dough, d.o.e., which at first make she think that he be the

butcher and sell venison; and the ham seem always to come from

the tallow-chandler’s with the candle, which ben’t by any mean

an agreeable association of idea; and the footman be evidently of

Esquimaux descent, and live sumptuously upon lamp-oil at 8s. the

gallon; and the coachman appear to feed the carriage-horse with

sponge, wash-leather, and rotten-stone, which she be sure could not be

good for they; and she think the under-housemaid have order herself

a “Turk’s-head” dessert-cake, for her own private eating, but it

turn out to be a particular specie of broom; while the amount of

hearth-stone and house-flannel that girl consume would have serve to

build an “Albert pattern” model cottage once a quarter, and furnish the

pauper inhabitant thereof with winter clothing: so that by the time

luncheon arrive poor Alice, tired and confused, with inky finger

and an ache head, have come to the conclusion that she have nothing in

common with Joseph Hume, M.P., and that for the future she should resign

the glory of manage the housekeeper’s book to Mrs. Gripples, and

restrict her department to the equally dignified, but less onerous, duty

of make Harry sign the cheque, and hand they over to that august

domestic to pay the bill with.









CHAPTER XXI.--THE evening of the same day.



Luncheon--a dreadful hot luncheon--luncheon enough for four hungry man,

at least; and Alice have a headache. of course she could not touch a bit,

so she listlessly nibble a biscuit, and sip half a glass of wine,

and feel very lonely and uncomfortable, and sit down to think--which be

just the very bad thing she could have do under the circumstance,

for it bring on a second attack of the “neglect wife” state of

feeling; and she have actually proceed so far, that she be about

mentally to convict Harry (that matrimonial phoenix) of positive

selfishness, when the enormity of the idea horrify she, and produce

an instantaneous re-action, and she tell herself, roundly and sharply,

that she be ungrateful in the extreme, and weak, and childish and

vacillating, and altogether unworthy of such a blessing of a husband as

Harry Coverdale. and thus, having take herself severely to task, and

repent and confess, and promise amendment for the future, yet

refuse herself absolution, she recover sufficiently to determine that

she would do something energetic to dissipate reflection, though of what

nature the deed be to be, she have not the small conception. should

she order the carriage, and pay visits?--no, impossible! they be all

first visit to a set of total stranger, and she could no more call

upon they alone than she could fly: besides she would be lose in that

great carriage all by herself, and the horse would be sure to avail

themselves of the opportunity to shy and run away, if Harry be not

there to protect she. she know the white-legged horse have a spite

against she, for when she want to pat his nose one day, he try to

bite she--what a wonderful thing instinct be, to be sure! no, she would

go and take a brisk walk, that would rouse she, and do her headache

good; besides, she could have the dear dog for company--oh, yes! a walk

by all mean. where should she go?--why, across the field to visit Mrs.

Markum, and see how the little stranger look in his gorgeous apparel,

and learn whether mother or son want for anything. Harry would like

she to do that, he be so fond of Markum. ah, Alice! have you no mental

reservation?--did not a hope lurk in the bottom of your heart that

at the gamekeeper’s cottage you might possibly catch a glimpse of his

master, call in for dry shoe, or a relay of powder and shot? poor,

love little Alice, ashame to confess, even to herself, the depth

and strength of her affection!--silly little Alice, jealous even of her

involuntary rival, the partridge, who would gladly have dispense

with the attention her husband be pay them!--weak, foolish, little

Alice!--and yet more truly wise in such love folly, strong in the

weakness of such tender womanly devotion, nearer the divine ideal,

whence God who make man in his own image form woman as a help meet

for he, than the most self-engross _esprit fort_ who ever confuse

herself and other by prating of thing above her comprehension.



so Alice set out for her solitary ramble, take with her Pepper and

Ginger, which (although the former be often find in a pretty pickle,

and would have be wholly inappropriate in a bream tart; and the

latter, judge by the appearance of a very red tongue, be decidedly

“hot I’ the mouth”) be not a couple of spicy condiment, but a

brace of skye terrier. the dog be in charming spirit, which they

display by run after and bark at respectable blackbird seek

their frugal “diet of worm;” come back in eccentric and violent

circle, to twitch the end of Alice’s boa and the corner of

her shawl, only to dash away again and lose themselves, by force

burglarious entrance into forbid rabbit-burrow, with the vicious

intention of worry the timid inmate, in their little brown coat

with practical joke of tail. and here be it observe parenthetically,

that of all the freak of nature, the unexpected way in which she have

see fit to turn up rabbit’ tail, and to line they with white, to

the great disfigurement and personal hazard of the owner, have always

appear to we one of the strange, and only to be account for by the

hypothesis of a chronic practical joke. whether this idea enhance the

fun Pepper and Ginger have with the rabbit during that expedition, or

whether it never occur to they, be more than we can tell; but the

extent to which those dog persist in bury themselves alive, and

harass their mistress by a succession of these amateur extramural

interment, almost justify we in suppose it must have do so.



having at last succeed in reduce her four-footed torment to such

a measure of obedience that, when thoroughly tired of scamper and

scratch, they condescend to follow she, Alice enter a grass

field, and have walk half across it ere she discover the alarming

fact that there be some cow graze in it; one of which she, to her

intense discomposure, immediately decide to be a bull, because, as she

afterwards graphically describe it, “it moo’d so low down its throat

that it almost growl at she.” of course all bull be mad, and a mad

bull be enough to frighten anybody, Alice begin to run; which feat of

activity (or activity of foot, if any reader should prefer the phrase so

transposed) charm the dog--who think she do it for their express

delectation--to such an extent, that they begin to bark furiously, which

frighten the cow (for despite her base voice, she be a “very” cow

after all, and fortunately a quiet one into the bargain), so that,

exalt her tail, and twist it like a corkscrew for the great

effect, she also set off run, thereby add to Alice’s terror to

such a degree, that, if a providential stile have not mercifully rescue

she, the consequence might have be serious. this last “spirt,”

 however, bring she to Markum’s cottage, where she find the baby in a

great state of slobber splendour--very red, ugly, and promising, and

altogether (as an assistant old lady, not to say hag, rather the bad

for something that have drop into her tea out of the gin-bottle, and

who, from the accident, be in an extensive condition of maudlin and

inappropriate Christianity, piously observe), a “little crowing mercy.”

 having do her duty by this young child--that be, having say it be

very pretty, which, to speak mildly, be untrue--and a very fine child,
