which, as far as regard its dress, it certainly be--and exactly like

its father, which be an awful----well, never mind, pious fraud we’ll

call it,--Alice tip the inappropriate christian half-a-crown (in

exchange for which she receive a tipsy blessing), and take leave,

having obtain geographical instruction by which she might, on her

homeward route, avoid the proximity of the _basso profundo_ cow.



the walk back (with the trifling exception of an episode wherein Ginger

disturb the tenant of a wasps’-nest) prove singularly uneventful,

and Alice, in her secret soul, pronounce the whole expedition a

failure--which, as it have cure her headache, be very ungrateful of

she; but she be so engross by a little pain about the heart, which

nothing but her husband’s return could cure, that she have entirely

forget her headache.



the hall clock strike four as its mistress enter--four o’clock, two

long hour to dinner-time! the time when Harry would, that is, ought to,

return; for she daresay’d he would be late, and that they should not sit

down to table till half-past six, at the very early. what should she

do to fill up this unharmonious interval? why, as she have work so hard

all the morning, surely she have a right to amuse herself now. she would

read some entertaining book, which would make her laugh and raise she

spirit; for, despite her good endeavour, she be get decidedly

miserable. so to this end she open a parcel of book from the

library, and begin upon a new novel, by that very talented lady, Mrs.

Bluedeville, and read how a “fair and gentle girl,” bring up by a

select coterie of fiendish relation, and subject from infancy to

a series of torture, sufficient to have expend the stout negro,

develop, under these favourable circumstance, into a perfect Houri

of Paradise, with the “additional attraction” of possess the mind,

manner, erudition, and phraseology of an old Divine of the Church of

England. this interesting young martyr, release from her educational

Bastile, and turn out to grass for a brief space in a pleasant

meadow, wherein pasture a gallant, but very moral, officer of dragoon,

naturally fall in love with the same, who fortunately do not resent

the liberty. Angelica, take up from her month’s run and put to work

much too heavy for she, become well and well, until, as might have

be expect, she overdo the thing, and get too good to live,

have nothing leave for it but to die, which she accordingly do on the

arrival of the post which bring an account of the bold dragoon (in

whom, from a fancied resemblance to Harry, Alice have take the deep

interest) having fall a victim to his dauntless courage, which,

lead he to kill sixteen mount Sikhs in single combat, have

fail to preserve he from the vindictive fury of the seventeenth

evil-dispose survivor. strange to say this talented work, delightful as

it be, fail to render Alice much more cheerful; but it succeed in

occupy she till it be time to go and dress for dinner, and for this

she be grateful to the genius of Mrs. Bluedeville.



by six o’clock Alice, ready for dinner in more sense than one,

betook herself to the drawing-room, where she wait patiently for

half-an-hour, read up sundry part of Mrs. Bluedeville, which, in her

rapid flight through that lady’s instructive romance, she have fail to

peruse. at seven o’clock she ring the bell, and inquire of the butler

whether his master have come in, or whether, if not, anything definite

be know of his whereabouts. the reply be unsatisfactory in the

extreme.



Master have not return, he (Wilkins) could form no idea where he be

likely to be; but, as a general maxim, consider shoot to be a

highly dangerous amusement. would Mrs. Coverdale obligingly condescend

to ring the bell when she wish the dinner to be bring up?



shoot a dangerous amusement! yes, of course, so it be--gun

constantly go off of their own accord, and shoot those who be

carry they. how be it she have never think of this before? and she

have be blame Harry, when, perhaps----the idea be too horrible to

clothe in word, but it have occur to she, and for Alice now there be

no peace.



Mrs. Bluedeville be throw aside with no more ceremony than if she have

be a penny-a-liner; and with flush cheek and a beat heart the

anxious young wife begin to pace up and down the apartment. as the

minute creep by (_so_ slowly!) Alice’s fear increase, until, at

half-past seven, the suspense grow intolerable; and, ring the bell,

she be just give incoherent order for two mount groom to set off

in utterly useless direction, when bang! bang! go a double-barrel

gun in the stable-yard, and Wilkins (an amiable but timid London

servant) and his mistress nearly jump into each other’s arm.



still haunt by the conviction that something untoward must have

happen, Alice hasten to meet her husband as he enter the hall.

“oh, Harry dearest, how glad I be you be safe!” she exclaim; “but

tell I,” she continue, refer to the mysterious cause of his

prolonged absence, “tell I--what be it?”



“sixteen brace of bird, three hare, two couple of rabbit, a landrail,

and a woodpigeon; and a very fair bag I call it for one gun,” be the

unexpected reply.



relieve, yet slightly provoke, Alice resume: “but what have make you

so late? I have be dreadfully frighten about you--”



“Frightened! what at? oh, you silly child! but come, let we have

dinner; I shall be ready in less than ten minute. the idea of be

frightened!” and with a smile of compassionate derision, Harry march

off to dress, humming--



               “a southerly wind and a cloudy sky



               proclaim it a hunting morning.”



and this be Alice’s recompense for a lonely day spend in look

forward to, and long for, her husband’s return, end in

half-an-hour of breathless anxiety for his safety! she feel decidedly

cross, and we think she have a right to be so. during dinner she be

silent and dignified on principle--her husband should see that she feel

his neglect. but Harry don’t see it one bit, bless he! he be very

hungry, so for some time keep strictly to business, and he be very

happy, so when his appetite be appease, he rattle on about anything

and everything, and be so pleasant and cheerful that Alice feel dignity

would be quite out of place, have a little struggle with her feeling,

and then mentally forgave he.



to prove that she do so, she lay herself out to entertain and amuse

he, and with this view, when the servant have leave the room, she treat

he to a comic account of her day’s adventure, and having talk

herself into a great state of communicativeness and sociability, have

just reach the bass cow episode, when a slight sound, not very unlike

the voice of the cow itself, reach her ear--_Harry have fall fast

asleep!_









CHAPTER XXII.--KATE sow the WIND.



so Kate Marsden marry the cotton-spinner, and old Mr. Hazlehurst

repurchase his farm on very easy term. we wonder which of the two be

well pleased with the bargain! Kate turn very pale when she promise

to love, honour, and obey a man whom she dislike, despise, and

intend to rule; nor do we wonder at it, for, with all her fault,

Kate perceive the intrinsic beauty of truth, and love it, as she do

everything beautiful. but though she loathe herself for what she be

do, though her bitter enemy could not have take a harsh view of

her conduct than she herself take, she have go too far to retract, and

having swallow the camel of crush her own heart and that of Arthur

Hazlehurst, she could not stultify herself by strain at the gnat

of swear falsely in the service for the solemnization of matrimony.

Kate’s be one of that peculiar order of conscience which can commit a

sin knowingly, on an emergency, but dare not be guilty of a blunder. in

the one case, the end appear to justify the mean; while in the other,

the entire transaction be unworthy. Sophistry, Kate, sophistry! which,

while you think it, and act upon it, fail to satisfy even your warped

and distorted sense of right and wrong.



Kate Marsden marry Mr. Crane--there be a union! on the one side youth

and beauty; intellect, lofty enough to have aim at any achievement

which the mind of woman have accomplish; energy, sufficient to have

gain the object striven for; ambition, that when all be win would

have despise the trophy at her foot, and sigh for more world to

conquer; and a deep passionate nature, combine the fiery element of

a southern temperament with the steady perseverance and inflexible

resolution characteristic of a daughter of the sturdy north: on the

other side, advance age, mental weakness, timidity, and its natural

concomitant--suspicion, together with a general paucity of idea,

centre in a vulgar pride of wealth. all Kate’s friend congratulate

she, and many envy her good fortune; and Horace D’Almayne smile on

his future victim, as he surely reckon she; and Arthur Hazlehurst sit

alone in his dusky chamber, with bitter thought busy at his heart,

struggle, like a brave and good man, against the tempt fiend that

bid he rise up and curse she who have thus render desolate his young

existence; and the minister of religion stand before the altar, and

pronounce his blessing over this hollow mockery of marriage, which no

amount of blessing could hallow; and the happy pair drive off to some

fool’s paradise to enjoy the honeymoon.



poor Mr. Crane! if he have dream of the volcano of feeling that

smouldered at his side beneath that cold, calm exterior, he would

assuredly have fling open the carriage-door, spring out (albeit not

accustomed to such feat of activity), and never cease run until

he have reach Manchester. fortunately, however, his wife’s mind be a

seal book to he, and so he reach the end of his journey in peace

and safety.



having bear the honeymoon with resignation, Kate endure she

bad bargain _tête-à-tête_ at various watering-place, and amongst

innumerable lake and mountain of tourist notoriety, until she have

teach he the only accomplishment she care to inculcate, viz.,

obedience, which he learn very readily, see that it relieve he

from all trouble and responsibility. this point accomplish, she take

he to a fashionable hotel in St. James’s Street, where she write to her

friend, Arabella Crofton, to join she. however, before that excellent

young woman of the world have time to wind up the end of a few trifling

skein of policy, with which she have be construct net for small

bird at Baden-Baden, Horace D’Almayne find out the residence of the

happy couple, and proceed to call upon, dine with, and make himself

generally useful and agreeable to they. Kate do not like he, but

she have be for two month _tête-à-tête_ with Mr. Crane, and Horace

possess this advantage over that devoted husband, that he be _not_ a

fool, and Mr. Crane be. Horace be not a fool; on the contrary, he be

such a clever knave that it be really a pity that he be not something

well: he see the game he have to play, and he resolve to play it as

skilfully as his faculty and experience would enable he. he possess

considerable insight into character, and sufficient tact to accommodate

himself to the peculiarity, and avail himself of the weakness, he

might thus discover. accordingly, his first move be to endeavour to

lull Kate’s suspicion of he, which he see have be arouse; his next

to make himself by degree useful to she--necessary to she; then, let

he win her confidence on any subject (he would have be delighted if

she have tell he the day of the month, or that she have drop a pin, in

_confidence_, for it would have be a beginning), until by word, look,

or sign, she admit her indifference towards her husband, and then the

game would be his own.



with Mr. Crane D’Almayne’s course appear very simple. the

millionaire’s one clear idea be the omnipotence of wealth; he know

D’Almayne be poor, and that he have lend he money which he never

expect to be repay. he consider he in the light of a sort of

Master of the Ceremonies, who could guide he in the way of fashionable

life, whereof he feel his ignorance--a kind of upper upper-servant--the

Vizier to his caliphship, and he lend he money as a delicate way of

pay his wage. at present D’Almayne be in high favour with Mr.

Crane; his wife be look very handsome, quite a gem of a wife--equal

to his picture or his port wine; D’Almayne have negotiate his marriage

for he, and the speculation have be a successful one; he lend

D’Almayne £500 before he have be in town a week. Horace see it all, but

he be not proud; as he would have say, “it suit his book too well,”

 so he pocket his wage meekly.



“my dear Kate, can you amuse yourself for a couple of hour or so alone?

D’Almayne and I be go to look at a pair of carriage-horse--a--I

shall bring he home to luncheon, and--a--now I think of it, I ask he

to dine here and go to the concert at the Hanover Square Rooms with we

afterwards;” and having thus unfold his programme for the day, Mr.

Crane glance timidly towards his wife, to learn whether it would

receive her sanction and approval. there be a moment’s silence, and

then in a low, musical voice, Kate reply coldly--



“I have letter to write this morning, so the arrangement will suit I

perfectly. if the horse be fine one, I hope you will buy they.”



Mr. Crane stroke his chin (a habit in which he indulge when anything

please he) and smile. his wife be satisfied with he--happy man! but

he have stroke his chin rather prematurely, for, in the same cold tone,

Kate resumed--



“there be one point on which I be anxious clearly to understand you. be

it your wish that Mr. D’Almayne should virtually live with we? because,

that he will do so, unless some decide measure be take to discourage

he, be self-evident.”



this be a straightforward and uncompromise way of put the case

which slightly discompose poor Mr. Crane.



D’Almayne be, as we have say, eminently useful to his patron, so much

so, that at that precise epoch the good gentleman would have be sorely

puzzle how to get on without he; but the more he acknowledge this in

his secret soul, the less do he desire that any one, and especially his

young wife, should perceive it.



“well, my dear Kate,” he begin, “you see Mr. D’Almayne have turn his

attention to point which, engage as I have be for many year in

commerce, I have never find time or opportunity to render myself

acquaint with.”



“in fact, he have make himself necessary to you,” interpose Kate.



“no, my dear, no--by no mean necessary--not at all so; but that he be

useful, very useful to I, I confess. I be sorry to perceive that you

have take up a slightly unreasonable (if I may be permit to say so)

prejudice against this young man.”



“you be mistaken,” return Kate, calmly. “I be perfectly indifferent

to he. if it be your wish to make use of he, he will of course be

here constantly; but as you have so kindly yield to my desire that my

friend, Miss Crofton, should reside with we, his presence or his absence

will make little difference to I--only, if at any future time you

should hear comment on the intimacy, you will remember that I have

admit it solely to gratify you.”



Mr. Crane, propitiate by this concession, and by the ground on which

Kate have place it, be endeavour to stroke some form of thanksgive

out of his chin, when the door open, and the subject of their

conversation be show in. after a few desultory remark, Horace,

turn to Mr. Crane, observed--



“I call at the house-agent’s in my way here, and have obtain the

particular of two house which it will be quite worth your while to

look at; one be in Belgrave Square, the other in Park Lane.”



as he speak, Kate raise her head and fix her large eye upon his

face; but he appear unconscious of having deserve her scrutiny, and

be quietly examine some memoranda he have write on the back of a

card, regard the number of room and other particular respect the

house. so perfectly unconscious be his manner, that for once Kate’s

penetration be at fault. she remember have on one occasion, month

before, at the Grange, mention in his presence that if she go to

live in London she should prefer either Belgrave Square or Park Lane for

her residence; but whether _he_ also have recollect this, or whether

his selection be the result of accident, she could not decide.

moreover, it be not easy for she to determine how to act in the matter.

if he have make the selection intentionally, and she allow it to pass

unnoticed, it would be a sort of tacit admission that she be willing to

receive such secret attention from he, appreciate they as kindness

rather than resent they as impertinence; while, on the other hand,

if by any chance it be a mere coincidence, she be unwilling to afford

he even the minute triumph of perceive that she feel sufficient

interest in he to remember whether or not he have be present on an

occasion, since which several month have elapse, or that she care to

know if he have observe, or regard her wish. so she take a middle

course, and, avail herself of a pause in the conversation, inquire

carelessly--



“where do you say the house be situate, Mr. D’Almayne?” on

obtain the information she require, she add, “and how come you to

select those particular locality?”



as he turn to reply, their glance meet, but his face be perfectly

inscrutable.



“if, as your tone imply, they do not meet your approval, my dear

Mrs. Crane, we need take no farth trouble in regard to they,” be his

ambiguous reply. “I choose they because I fancy situation so generally

popular might not be displease to you.”



Kate be again foil, and D’Almayne, as he quietly observe it,

mutter inwardly, “won the first trick, at all event!”



Mr. Crane, leave the room to put on his great-coat, a precaution

without which he be most careful not to stir from home, D’Almayne

observed,--



“you would prefer bay carriage-horse to grey, or any more conspicuous

colour, would you not?”



surprise at his having thus discover her taste, Kate be so far

throw off her guard as to exclaim,--



“how in the world do you know that?”



Horace smile a quiet smile.



“I reason from analog,” he say; “your dress be always rich and

striking, but never showy; and the effect be produce by its consistency

as a whole.”



Kate involuntarily return his smile; tact and keen intelligence be

quality she highly appreciate.



“you be a close observer,” she say, “and shall be reward by learn

the interesting fact that I _do_ prefer bay horse to those of any other

colour.”



before the week be over, Mr. Crane have purchase a magnificent pair

of bay carriage-horse, and have take a lease of a noble mansion in Park

Lane. the only fault Kate could discover in either, be the conviction

force upon she that it be to the agency of Horace D’Almayne she be

indebted for they.









CHAPTER XXIII.--ADVICE GRATIS.



Harry could not give up shooting, Harry would not give up shooting, and

Harry do not give up shooting. on the contrary, he could, would, and

do shoot _every_ day, and all day long, except on Sundays, throughout

September and October; at least, there be so few exception that they

only prove the rule. Alice do not like it at all; at first she be

very miserable. one day Harry find she crying, and be considerably

surprised and greatly concerned at the unaccountable discovery, do

not rest until he have ascertain the cause, when he be particularly

shock, and blame himself so much, that he refrain from shoot

for two whole day, and really would have strive to reform his conduct,

only that, unfortunately, an invitation arrive to join a grand _battue_

at a certain Colonel Crossman’s. this, in his then frame of mind, he

would have refuse; but there be a Mrs. Crossman in the case, Alice

be include in the invitation, and they be beg to stay three or

four day; which, as the Popem Park preserve be the well stock

of any in the county, be an offer not lightly to be reject. thus,

unfortunately, they go--we say unfortunately, because Colonel Crossman

be, take as a whole, a jovial, hot-temper, selfish brute; and his

wife a quick-witte, worldly-minded, selfish fool. they do very well

together, because, as he usually live out of the house, and she in it,

and both do exactly as they like, when they like, their fault seldom

clash; if such a collision do take place, there be an awful tumult,

in which brutality have his way for the minute, and pay for it in minor

misery which folly indict upon he for the next fortnight. and yet

this amiable couple have a kind of theoretical and useless affection for

each other, which be engender partly by habit, and partly by a deep

and essentially vulgar reverence for appearance, which, together with

go to church once on Sunday, stand they in the stead of religion

and of morality. thus be they bad counsellor for our young married

couple. on the first morning of her visit, Alice be stand at the

drawing-room window, watch the figure of her husband and Colonel

Crossman stride through a turnip field about a quarter of a mile

distant, when Mrs. Crossman join she.



“ah! there they go,” she observe, in a vinegar-and-water voice; “we

shall see no more of they till seven o’clock, depend upon it.”



“do Colonel Crossman never return to luncheon?” inquire Alice

timidly, for she stand slightly in awe of the female soldier beside she.



“return to luncheon!” be the astonished reply, deliver in much such

a tone as might have be anticipate if Alice have inquire whether the

gallant colonel usually make his mid-day meal upon red-hot ploughshare;

“come home to luncheon! not he. he wouldn’t do such a thing to save my

life, I believe; certainly not if the scent be lie well. why, Mr.

Coverdale do not spoil you in that way to be sure, do he? the

colonel tell I he be a thorough sportsman.”



“so he be,” return Alice with a sigh, which escape she involuntarily.



“ah! no woman with a heart should ever marry a sportsman,” rejoin

Mrs. Crossman, with rather more vinegar and less water in her tone than

before. “out all day, from the first of September till the breeding

season come round again; then the moment they’ve finish dinner and

their bottle of port-wine, asleep they go, and only wake to stamp and

swear with the cramp, and drop off again, till they tumble upstairs to

bed, and be no comfort to anybody. you be a young wife yet, my dear,

and your husband’s hardly grow tired of you, perhaps, but wait another

month or two and you’ll see--man be all alike!”



there be just enough applicability to her own case in this tirade to

make Alice feel rather angry and thoroughly uncomfortable; but the

idea of compare Harry with Colonel Crossman be too bad, and anger

predominate as she reply, “Mr. Coverdale be not quite so selfish as

you imagine, my dear madam; certainly he leave I a good deal alone when

the shooting season first begin, but as soon as he be aware how dull

and lonely I feel, he give up shoot for, for--”



“half a day?” inquire Mrs. Crossman, sarcastically.



“he do not go out for two whole day; and since that he have generally

return to luncheon,” reply Alice, colour from vexation.



“wonderful!” exclaim Mrs. Crossman, with an affectation of extreme

surprise; “actually stay at home for two whole day, when he’s be

married as many month--what a model man! not that I believe Colonel

Crossman ever do so much as that even,” she continue, turn on the

vinegar. “I pick he up in India, you know--be actually weak enough

to fall in love with the creature! even go the length of refuse two

district judge and the resident at Bamboozel for his sake! and would

you believe it, we haven’t be marry above a week, when the man

be brute enough to go out hog-hunting, and leave I all by myself at

Boshbogie, on the border of the great Flurry-yun-ghal Jungle, with

nothing more conversable than tawney and tiger within thirty mile of

I; but, however, I be not long before I learn how to take care of

myself--and the soon you do the same, my dear, the well for your

happiness. man be easily enough manage if you do but set the right way

to work. if you choose to be always humble and meek to ’em, they’ll

let you lie down for they to wipe their boot on, but if you only show

they you’ve get a spirit of your own, and don’t care for ’em----



“but I don’t know that I have get what you call a spirit of my own,”

 interrupt Alice, smile at her companion’s vehemence, “and I

certainly do care about my husband.”



“ah, my dear, that’s all very well now; but wait a bit--wait till some

day when he want to go shoot, and you want he to do something else,

and then see of how much use your meekness and fondness will be to you.

he will think to himself, ‘oh! she will be just as well please a couple

of hour hence, as if I have lose my day’s sport for her silly nonsense.’

I know he will, man be all alike. no; soon or later you’ll find you

will have to pluck up a spirit, and treat your husband as he will treat

you. if he leave you by yourself all day, fill your house with company;

if he go out shoot and hunt with his friend, do you go out

riding or drive with your; if he have his season in the country, do

you have yours in London; opera and shopping be amusement you’ve just

as good a right to, as he have to go pop at the partridge and

pheasant; and if you care so much about keep he at home, hook some

young dandy (there will be plenty ready to nibble when such a bait as

your pretty face be hang out for they), and flirt with he steadily till

the desire effect be produce. that will bring your husband to his

sense, if anything will. I once settle the Colonel in three day by

go all respectable length with Adolphus Fitz-duckling. it lead to a

duel, though; but that be because both Duck and Crossman be army man,

and mix up with a fighting set. I take care never to go quite so far

again, except with a civilian; but then I haven’t get such a quiet,

demure manner as you have. a set of impudent young puppy in the Old

43rd use to call I ‘Flirting Fan.’ however, I can tell you I be able

to keep the Colonel in much well order, ‘flirt he down,’ as I use

to call it, than I’ve ever manage to do since I grow old--that be, less

young than I be at that time.” and so this good woman, or rather this

woman who, despite her fault, have some good in she, whereby she

vindicate her title to humanity, run on until Alice heartily wish she

back again amongst the tawney, or the tiger: we be afraid that at

that especial moment our little heroine would decidedly have prefer

the latter.



in the meantime, Harry and the Colonel be blaze away at the

long-tail most unmercifully: Harry, who be a crack shot, bring down

everything he point his gun at, while the Colonel, whose hand have

an awkward trick of shake, as if its proprietor be in the habit of

imbibe too much port-wine, miss much oftener than be agreeable to

he, on each of which several occasion he attribute his failure to,

and condemn in no measured term either the gun, or the bird, or

both. about two o’clock Harry pull out his watch, and glance at it

observed--“I don’t know what your arrangement may be, Colonel, but if

Mrs. Crossman be of as sociable a disposition as my little wife, she

will consider we great bear if we don’t return till dinner time.”



at this moment a splendid cock-pheasant rose, “whir” into the air at

some considerable distance from the sportsman, whereupon the Colonel,

consider it a difficult shot, call out, “your bird, Coverdale.”

 Harry, embarrass with his watch, which he still hold in his hand,

raise his gun, and catch his finger in the guard chain, pull the

trigger too soon, and miss with both barrel, while the Colonel,

see that the pheasant be now so far off that it could be no

discredit to miss it, pull at it, and by accident bring it down.



“Bravo! Colonel, that be the clever shot that have be make to-day by

long odd!” ejaculate Harry.



“ah! that’s a trifle to what I use to do when I be your age,” be the

slightly apocryphal reply; “nothing with feather or hair on it have

a chance, if I put my gun up at it, I can tell you. but what be you

say about go home? why I’m just get into shooting order;

you’re not knock up, to be sure, already.”



“no; nor six hour’ more hard walking would not do it,” return Harry,

laugh, as he mentally contrast his own power with those of the

Colonel, who, although he have carefully assign all the tough of

the work to his guest, be evidently begin “to want his corn,”

 as Coverdale metaphorically paraphrase the fact of his entertainer’s

require his luncheon. “I merely ask you whether Mrs. Crossman would

not disapprove of our remain out all day?”



“Mrs. Crossman may go and----hang herself in her own petticoat string!”

 be the uncourteous rejoinder. “ah! I see how it be,” continue the “old

soldier.”

“I see all about it: you’re a young hand yet, Coverdale, and I’m an old

one; take my advice. you’ve marry a nice gal, and a pretty gal--don’t

you go and spoil she; it’s the nature of woman to like to have their own

way; and one of their way--and a most aggravating and unaccountable one

it be--be always to have a fellow dangle about after they, and there

they’ll keep he drive ’em out, or ride with ’em, or dawdling

in shop, and pay their bill for ’em--they don’t forget that, mind

you--or read to ’em, or some such confound humbug. hang it, sir,

I’d soon be a galley-slave, or a black nigger at once! well, if you

begin by indulge a woman (they’re all alike in such point), she’ll

be your master ever after, and your life willn’t be worth a----” (as we

do not know the exact value of the coin to which the Colonel allude,

we abstain from a more particular mention of it). “no; if you’re to have

any peace or comfort in the married state, you must let your wife see

that you’re determined to show you’re the superior. the only way to do

it effectually be--come to heel, Countess, ah! would you then!” (and

whack, whack go the dog-whip against poor Countess’s sides)--“the only

way to break ’em in is--(whack)--to show ’em clearly whose will

be the strong, and whose must yield. I have trouble enough with Mrs.

Crossman, I can assure you. she be not an easy woman to break in, sir;

but she find she’d meet her match. if she scold, I storm; if she

rave, I swear; if she sulk, I whistle; if she cry, I light a cigar;

if she faint, I lay she on the hard board that I could pick out in

the floor, and smoke till she come round again. the only time she go

into hysteric I fling a pail of cold water over she--that cure she

at once and for ever. I dare say you think I an old brute, but the day

will come when you’ll recollect my advice, and be glad enough to act

upon it. woman be all alike, more or less.”



Harry _do_ think he an old brute, and thank his star that neither

in mind nor in person do Alice in the small degree resemble Mrs.

Crossman; he also think that he should never remember the Colonel’s

advice with any other feeling than disgust. ah! Harry--Harry!









CHAPTER XXIV.--A STORM BREWING.



“Harry! my dear Harry!--Wilkins, where _be_ your master? I _tell_

you I must speak to he before he go out, and now you’ve let he go

without----”



“wilkin! where the d------ oh! wilkin, what do you do with that bag

of snipe-shot I bring down from London?”



thus apostrophise by an agitated _soprano_ at the drawing-room door,

and an impatient _tenore robusto_ in the entrance-hall, Wilkins, the

amiable and timid London butler, who have play the character of Job’s

comforter to Alice’s _Didone abandonata_ on the memorable evening of

the first of September, make two or three step in the direction of the

drawing-room, then twist round with a sudden jerk, as though he have

be work by machinery with which somebody be play trick, rush

frantically into the hall, and hand his master a wrong bag of shot

exclaim, without any breath left--



“this--a--be they, sir; and my mistress--a--says----”



“swan-shot, you fool--that be, Wilkins, big enough to roll over a

bullock! it’s the snipe-shot I’m look for. no, not that. don’t you

know snipe-shot when you see it? when the scent’s get dull every

minute, too! I ought to have be out these two hour. that’s right, my

good fellow: don’t be a month about it--give it I. I shall be home to

dinner.”



“but my mistress particularly wish to speak----” falter poor

Wilkins. Harry, fling down with an angry gesture the shot-belt he have

just fill, and mutter that he have well give up go out at all,

stride off to the drawing-room, and put his head in through the

partially open door, as though he be afraid of be take prisoner

if he trust himself bodily in the apartment, exclaimed--



“how, then, little woman, what be it? quick, please, for I want to be

off.”



“there be an invitation just arrive from Allerton House for Tuesday

week. what be I to say?”



“oh, we must go, of course. I want you to get intimate with Lady

Allerton, she’s a charming woman, and Lord George be a good little

fellow in his way, though an awfully bad shot. Dinner, I suppose?”



“yes; but, Harry, wait one moment and listen to I!” exclaim Alice.

“you need not be in such a hurry; you will have plenty of time for that

horrid shoot before six o’clock.”



“horrid shooting, indeed! much you know about it,” mutter the

victimise sportsman, inwardly chafe at the delay; “it will be horrid

shoot in one sense, if I be hinder much long. the scent willn’t lie

when the dew be off, and I may as well go out with a walking-stick as

with a gun, for there will be nothing to shoot at.”



“well, I’ll let you go directly, you impatient, silly boy,” return

Alice, smile at the serious, business-like view her husband take of

his amusement. “the only thing I wish to say be, that if we accept this

invitation, we shall be almost certain to meet the Duke and Duchess of

Brentwood there; and you know I’ve be wait for you to go with I,

day after day, and I’ve never return their visit yet. you must take I

to call before Tuesday week; I’ve be quite rude already.”



“all right,” return Harry; “we’ll go in style, and call on the old

duchess. I’ll wear a red coat, and stick a peacock’s feather in my hat,

if that will please you. it’s a pity she’s so like a Chimpanzee! most

probably she be relate to the monkey tribe--suppose we ask she when we

call; it will be a new and original style of conversation, eh? well,

ta ta! it’s so late now that I’m afraid you willn’t have the felicity of

see I again till dinner-time;” and without allow his wife an

opportunity of remonstrate, Harry close the door, and be soon pay

off the long-bill in a way in which they scarcely approve of have

their “little account” settle. Alice watch he depart with a smile,

which fade into a sigh as she turn to write an acceptance to the

dinner invitation, and then employ and amuse herself as well she might,

during the weary hour which must elapse ere her husband would return.



Lord Allerton be the eld son of the Duke and Duchess of Brentwood,

who be the great people, _par excellence_, of the Coverdale Park

neighbourhood; and when the Duke and Duchess come to spend their

Christmas in the country, Alice, stimulate thereunto by the

conversation of the Mesdames Jones, Brown, and Robinson of those part,

feel slightly curious to know whether these ancient and venerable

limb of the aristocracy would deign to honour she by a call, and be

proportionably gratify and bored when, on a dreary morning, the dull

old Duchess come and pay she a singularly heavy and uninteresting

visit. to induce Harry to accompany she when she return this equally

flattering and alarming civility, have be for several day the sole

object of Alice’s existence,--an object in which, as the reader

may perceive by the forego conversation, she have hitherto be

unsuccessful.



the next morning Alice once again make an attempt to entice she well

half away from the pleasure of the plain; but the rabbit have begin

bark the young ash-tree in a favourite plantation, and be to be

“pull down” accordingly. this occupation last several day; at the

expiration of which period certain poacher, choose to join in the

amusement uninvite, have to be “pull up” for their iniquity--a

series of up and down which leave only two day vacant before the

important Tuesday dedicate to the dinner-party at Allerton would

arrive. the first of these day it rain cat and dog, and snow

fragment of polar bear so decidedly, that even Harry could not get out

till about half-past three, when, in desperation, he envelop himself

in a Macintosh, and gallop over to _the_ town, five mile off (as all

town be from all country house), to match some ribbon for Alice, and

look at the newspaper on his own account. the _County Press_ be

just out, and therein Harry perceive a lead article attack the

decision arrive at by himself and his brother magistrate in the

case of the “pull up” poacher. this be equally irritate and

interesting, he sit down in the reading-room of the library diligently

to peruse the same--phsa-ing, pish-ing, and “confound the fellow”

 at every second line. he have just get to a paragraph beginning,

“Mr. C--d--le may be well qualified to lead the way across a stiff line

of country after the hound, or roll over unoffending hare and rabbit

in a _battue_--but that be no proof that he possess an equal right

to ride rough-shod over the enactment of a British Parliament, or to

overturn the decree of abler lawyer than be to be find among the

bench of magistrate at H--------,” when a large hand be place over

his eye, and a loud, jovial voice exclaimed--



“never mind, Harry, my boy--little flipkin the editor’s get a wife with

the devil’s own temper, and she help he to write the leader; she take

a dislike to you when she be Miss Jamby, and keep the confectioner’s

shop, when you neglect she, and flirt with the girl behind the

counter, because she happen to be the prettiest, and now she’s pay

you off; you cann’t horsewhip a woman, you know, so you’d well take it

easy.”



before the speaker have arrive at the conclusion of his advice gratis,

Coverdale have remove the hand which impede his vision, and turn

round, exclaimed--



“why, it’s Tom Rattleworth, by all that’s extraordinary--I think you

be in Canada, with your regiment, man!”



“so I be till the gout carry off the governor, and leave I a

miserable orphan with £15,000 a-year in my pocket. when that lamentable

event occur I think I be, for the first time in my life, worth

take care of, so determined to cut the red cloth and pipe-clay

business, and come home and live virtuously ever after.”



“you seem to have recover your spirit pretty well, if one may judge

by present appearance,” return Coverdale, half-amuse, half-disgusted

at his quondam friend’s sentiments--“at all event you’ve not grow thin

upon it.”



“no! but that’s the very fact which prove how deeply I feel my forlorn

condition; it’s old Falstaff--be it not--observe how grief swell a

man? I don’t ride a pound under twelve stone,” be the rejoinder. “by

the way,” continue Rattleworth, “that remind I--it’s deucedly lucky

I meet you; you’re the very man that can tell I all about it--Broomfield

be anxious to give up the fox-hound; he be grow old and lazy, and he

want I to take ’em.”



“my dear fellow, I’m delighted to hear it,” exclaim Harry, eagerly;

“old Broomfield be completely past his work, and of all the man I know

you’re the fit to succeed he--you will do the thing as it ought

to be do. I should have undertake they myself, if I have not become a

Benedict: Broomfield try to persuade I.”



“well now look here,” resume Rattleworth, meditatively; “I’ve promise

to meet Broomfield to-morrow, and take his horse and everything at a

valuation. now there be not a man in the county whose opinion about a

horse I’d soon have than your; can you spare time to go with I? I

shall really consider it a personal favour if you will do so.”



“of course I will,” return Harry; for if he have a weak point on

which he be accessible to flattery, it be concern his knowledge of

horse-flesh; “there can be nothing I should like well, in fact--what

time do you go?”



“I be to lunch with he at one,” be the reply; “and we be to look at

his stud afterwards.”



“then I’ll meet you at the cross road by Hanger Wood, at half-past

twelve,” return Harry; and so, with a hearty shake of the hand, the

friend part.



Tom Rattleworth be the only son of a man who have begin life as a

land-agent and attorney in H----------; but have very early in his

career dabble in stock-jobbing till he make a considerable sum of

money, which his business connection enable he to lay out to great

advantage, he grow rich, purchase an estate, marry into one of the

county family, and bring his son up “as a gentleman”--that be, he

send he to Eton, where he learn nothing but how to get into and out

of scrape; and buy he a commission which he would have do well

without. nature having thus place a silver spoon in Tom’s mouth,

appear to consider his head sufficiently furnish without go to

any unusual expense in the article of brain; so she give he barely an

average quantity, and make up the deficiency by an actual passion for

horse-flesh. Thomas, thus endow, be the schoolfellow and holiday

associate of Harry Coverdale; and have one, and only one taste in

common, they have keep up their intimacy, until Harry start on his

grand tour, and Tom be send with his regiment to Canada, since which

period the interview we have just describe be their first meeting.



as Coverdale canter home through the mud, and rain, and sleet, it

suddenly flash across he that the next be the only day remain in

which to call on the Duke and Duchess of Brentwood before the dinner at

Allerton House; and his conscience smite he as he reflect that the

engagement he have form would prevent he from accompany Alice;

indeed, so annoyed do he feel at this unlucky coincidence, that for a

moment he be on the point of turn his horse’s head, and ride after

Tom Rattleworth to get off the engagement; but it be grow dusk, and

he reflect that Chase Hall, the residence of the renowned Thomas,

be so far out of his way that he should be unable to reach home by

dinner-time, and then Alice would get frightened about he, which would

annoy she more than be oblige to pay her visit alone; so with this

bit of sophistry he, for the moment, quiet his conscience. before he

arrive at his own house, he have mentally decide that, as it would only

worry his wife, he should say nothing about the Rattleworth engagement

to she that evening, and that in the morning he should mention it as an

equally unfortunate and unavoidable necessity, and persuade she to pay

the first visit without he. of course she would be a little

annoyed just at first, but she be so sweet-temper and amiable,

that--that--and here his reflection refuse to clothe themselves in

intelligible language;--had they do so honestly, the sentence would

have end thus--“that she would submit without make a scene.” and

so he canter home, where Alice, with her sunny smile and bright love

eye, be wait to receive he, and make a vast fuss with the poor

dear because he must be so wet, which, thank to Mr. Macintosh--his

admirable invention--he be not in the slight degree, though he

appreciate the affectionate fuss Alice make about he all the same.



Harry! you blind, stupid Harry!--as if her little finger, bless it, be

not worth all the horse-flesh that ever be foal, from Bucephalus,

down to the winner of the last Derby.



the next morning be a very fine one. Alice and Harry make their

appearance in the breakfast parlour about nine o’clock; each be a

little out of sort. Alice, not having be able to get any air or

exercise on the previous day, have wake with a headache, which Harry

continually forget, would leave the door of his dressing-room open,

and attire himself to the tune of “a hunting we will go.” then a new

morning gown, on which Miss Flippery, the dressmaker at H--------, have

stake her credit, do not fit, and in turn round to look at the

set of the back, Alice trod on the skirt, and tear it out of the

“gathers”--whatever they may be; and as woman seldom swear, and the evil

be scarcely serious enough to cry over, poor little Mrs. Coverdale be

unable to vent her annoyance, and bring it down to breakfast with she

accordingly. Harry, on the other hand, conscious that he be about

to commit an act of injustice, on which (although he repent of it

sufficiently to feel very uncomfortable) he be still determine, try

to keep up his courage by affect a degree of hilarity which cause

he to make bad joke about every subject mention, and to evince

such a total want of sympathy with his wife’s headache and consequent

depression of spirit, that Alice for the first time in her life

consider he tiresome and in the way, and feel inclined to say sharp

thing to he and snub he. after a longish pause, interrupt only

when, on two occasion, Harry be pull up for whistling, and a third

time for beat the devil’s tattoo on the chimney-piece, Alice

begin, “really Wilkins have take to burn the toast so black, it be

impossible to eat it. I wish you would speak to he about it, Harry.”



“certainly, my love,” be the cheerful reply; “what shall I say to he?

that although I approve of his black my boot, I disapprove of his

black my toast, and that I shall thank he to do it brown in future?”



“if you like to risk the chance, which be almost a certainty, that the

man will misunderstand you, for the sake of make a stupid slang pun, I

advise you to do so,” be the captious reply.



“Phew!” whistle Harry; “how solemn, and sensible, and serious we’ve

grow all of a sudden! I beg to inform you, Mrs. Coverdale that I expect

my wife to admire my pun, if nobody else do.”



“then you must contrive to make well one, and to time they rather

more appropriately,” rejoin Alice, so snappishly that her husband

look up in surprise. recall to herself by the unmistakeable

astonishment depict on the bright, good-natured countenance of she

well half, Alice continue in a milder tone, “you must not mind what I

say this morning, Harry, dear; my headache make I so dreadfully cross

and stupid.”



“poor little thing! you be shut up all yesterday, you know, and that

be enough to give anybody a headache,” return Harry, who consider

house be build only to dine and sleep in, and would have have Alice

spend her day _al fresco_, even as he delight to do. “you must go out

as much as possible to-day; luckily it be very fine.”



“yes; and I be to be honour with my husband’s company too, which be

a most unaccustomed pleasure,” rejoin Alice, brighten up at the

recollection. “it be certainly very good policy to make yourself so

scarce, though I wish you do not adhere quite so strictly to it; why

you have not drive out with I since we return from Popem Park! at

what time do you mean to order the carriage?”



“why it’s an hour’s drive at least; James have well be at the door by

two o’clock,” reply Harry. then turn towards the fire, and move

the ornament on the chimney-piece into wrong position, he continue,

with an elaborate attempt at _nonchalance_, which veil most

inefficiently his consciousness that he be about to perform an act

against which his moral sense rebel, he resume: “I’m afraid my love

that I must ask you to call upon the Duchess of Brentwood without I

this morning--a business engagement of--a--importance--that is, one that

I cannot avoid, will, I be afraid----”



and here he break off abruptly, for, glance at his wife, he perceive

an expression in her pretty face that he have never behold there before;

the bright eye be flash, the soft cheek burn, and the coral

lip pout with unmistakeable anger. Harry have at length go too far,

and his sweet-temper, love-hearte little wife be positively and

seriously angry with he. but so unusual a circumstance demand a fresh

chapter.









CHAPTER XXV.--THE STORM BURSTS.



Alice Coverdale, annoy and pain by what she consider she

husband’s injustice and unkindness, do not leave he long in doubt as

to her feeling upon the subject; for as soon as she could conquer a

choke sensation in the throat sufficiently to speak, she exclaimed:--



“really, Harry, I must say you be most unkind and inconsiderate; you

choose of your own accord to accept the invitation to Allerton House,

though I warn you at the time that it would necessitate your calling

on the Duke and Duchess first: you agree--in fact, you promise to do

so. there have not be a day since that I haven’t remind you of this

promise, so it be impossible you can have forget it;--there be a

time, and not so very long ago either, when you be ready enough to go

anywhere with I, and be only too glad to find I wish you to do so.

I little thought, poor foolish girl that I be, how soon thing would

alter; and now, when you know as well as I do that this be the last day

on which we can pay this visit, you’ve form some stupid engagement

(to go and shoot somewhere, I dare say; I wish gun have never be

invent--horrid dangerous thing--always go off unexpectedly and

kill people), and so make it impossible to return the Duchess’s call:

and tomorrow I shall be ashamed to look she in the face, or to speak to

she; though I dare say she willn’t give I a chance to do that, for she be

as proud as Lu------ as a woman can be.”



here, from sheer want of breath, Alice be force to pause, Harry

quietly remark: “woman can be as proud as man for that matter, _ecce

signum_, but now just listen to a little common sense for a minute. I

fully intend and wish to accompany you, but I happen yesterday, at

H--------, to meet with a very old friend of mine, who inform I that

he be go this morning to transact certain business matter which

would involve the expenditure of a considerable sum of money, in regard

to which affair he particularly require my advice and opinion.”



“he must be go to buy a gun or a horse then,” interrupt Alice;

“those be the only thing people imagine you understand; and I don’t

wonder at they either, when they see you waste half your life about this

horrid sporting. if you give up all intellectual pursuit in this way,

you’ll go on till you become fit for nothing but to hunt, shoot, eat,

drink, and sleep, like that dreadful old creature, Colonel Crossman.”



thoroughly provoke by this last speech (which touch on a sensitive

point in Harry’s disposition, and arouse a latent fear, by which he be

always more or less oppress, lest people should consider he, from his

fondness for field sport, a mere addlepate, fox-hunt squire), he

reply, with more asperity in his tone than he have ever before use, or

believe it possible he could use, towards Alice, “take care you don’t

become a peevish shrew, like Mrs. Crossman. you be angry, and forget

yourself; when you grow calm again, you will perceive how foolish

and unreasonable you have be to lose your temper about such a silly

trifle.”



“you think be rude to your friend and unkind to your wife a silly

trifle, do you?” inquire Alice.



Harry’s colour rise as he take a turn up and down the room to compose

his feeling ere he would trust himself to reply. “you want to make I

angry,” he say, “but I do not intend to afford you that satisfaction.

listen to I,” he continue, see that his wife be again about to

interrupt he, “listen to I, and when you have hear what I be about

to say, you can reply as you please. I make this engagement to oblige

my friend, without at the moment recollect that to-day be the time

appoint for call on the Duchess; but when I reflect that one be

business of importance, and the other a mere visit of ceremony, I

hope and believe you would be reasonable enough, when I should

have explain the matter to you, not even to wish I to give up my

engagement, and would exercise sufficient common sense and self-control,

to go and pay the visit alone.”



“then you think wrongly,” return Alice, with vehemence; “if you

require a wife who could go about by herself and visit a set of proud,

stiff people, who be stranger to she, and keep up your position in

the county, while you be out hunt and shoot all day, for your own

selfish amusement, you should have choose some fashionable woman of the

world, and not a poor simple country girl like myself, who rely on

your affection to protect and encourage she;” and here Alice show

strong symptom of a disposition to bring that “young wife’s last

resource” of a flood of tear to bear upon her disobedient and

refractory spouse.



Harry, see this, and having be throughout the interview haunt by

a latent consciousness that he be in the wrong, be strongly tempt to

yield, and, dispatch a messenger to Tom Rattleworth furnish

with some good and sufficient social white lie to account for his

non-appearance, to stay quietly at home till the time should have

arrive to accompany his wife to visit their aristocratic neighbour;

but, unhappily, Colonel Crossman’s caution, “you’ve marry a nice gal

and a pretty gal, take care you don’t go and spoil she,” flash across

he: “woman be all alike, more or less; it’s the nature of ’em to

choose to have their own way; if you indulge ’em at first, they will

be your master ever after; show your wife she have meet her match,” &c.

&c.--these, and such like precept, ring in Harry’s ear. Alice be

angry and unreasonable, strive for the upper hand, in fact; he must

not permit this: for her sake, as much as for his own, he be call

upon to assert himself, and vindicate his marital authority. yes,

painful as it be to his feeling to speak or act harshly to his young

wife, whom, even at that moment, he care for more than any other

create be, he would give she a lesson which should cure the evil at

once and for ever. so put on a very grave look he begin: “my dear

Alice, you be forget yourself, forget our relative position;

but there be a quiet way of settle such affair; verbose discussion

of this nature do not suit I--I be essentially a man of action. it be

the husband’s right to command, the wife’s duty to obey. I have hope

your own proper feeling would have save I the pain of be force

to remind you of this. I must now add, that I consider myself bind

to fulfil my engagement to my friend, and intend to do so: during my

absence, it be my wish and desire that you should drive and call on the

Duchess of Brentwood; if, which I can scarcely conceive possible, you

still refuse to do as I have point out, I shall, before I leave this

room, write a note to Lady Allerton, inform she that we be unable to

dine with she to-morrow, without assign any cause whatsoever for this

change of intention--which, as I cannot give the true reason, and will

not stoop to invent a false one, be the only course leave open to I.”



having deliver himself calmly and firmly of this despotic speech,

Harry fold his arm across his broad chest, and lean his autocratic

back against the chimney-piece, stand look as if he feel himself

completely “monarch of all he survey,” his wife include. meanwhile a

fearful struggle between good and evil be proceed in Alice’s mind;

a kind word or look would instantly have cause the good to triumph: but

her husband stand cold and inexorable as a statue of Fate. then the

same personage who tempt Eve to the sin which lose she Eden, suddenly

cause to flash across Alice’s recollection all Mrs. Crossman’s

argument, and she determine to follow her advice, to “pluck up a

spirit, and treat her husband as he treat she,” &c. Accordingly, by

a great effort restrain her tear, which during Harry’s harangue have

begin to flow, she look up with flash eye and crimson cheek, as

she reply:



“the obedience you require be not that of a wife but of a slave, and

I refuse to yield it. you have treat I unkindly and unjustly, and I

will not sacrifice myself to oblige you.” Harry make no reply, though

his lip move convulsively, as though he could scarcely command himself

to keep silence; then snatch pen and ink, he scrawl a hasty note,

seal and direct it, and rise, quit the room without utter

a single word. as the door close behind he, the tear which Alice have

hitherto with such difficulty repressed, burst forth unrestrained. she

be rouse from a paroxysm of weeping by the sound of horse’ foot, and

spring to the window, reach it in time to see Harry give a note to

a groom, who ride away at speed in the direction of Allerton House; then

mount his own horse, he also gallop off, ere Alice could muster

sufficient presence of mind to attempt to recall he.









CHAPTER XXVI.--THE ATMOSPHERE remain cloudy.



fall out with the wife of one’s bosom be a process that bear

a marked affinity to two other domestic operation which, from time

immemorial, have lapse into well-merit disrepute--viz., quarrel

with one’s bread and butter, and cut off one’s nose to spite

one’s face; the same moral but uncomfortable necessity of inherent

self-chastisement be common to all three. thus Harry Coverdale,

having vindicate his marital dignity, and gallop off the irritation

consequent upon so act, heartily wish the deed undo, and Alice

and himself friend again; for, little as he appear to prize it, she

affection have become necessary to he, and he could no more do without

it, than he could have dispense with sunshine in summer, or fire at

Christmas. accordingly it be in no very amiable frame of mind that

he join his fox-hunt ally; and it require all the allurement of

oyster, porter, devil bone, and unimpeachable port wine, to

enable he to “cast dull care away,” sufficiently to take a proper and

sportsman-like interest in all the minutiæ of the propose transfer

of stock, canine and equestrian. once fairly in for it, however, his

stable-minded propensity assert themselves, and he spend a deeply

interesting afternoon in feel back-sinew, detect incipient curb

and spavin, condemn an incurable sand-crack, and otherwise test

and pronounce judgment upon the quadrupedal inmate of Squire

Broomfield’s hunting stable. as the wane light herald the approach

of dinner-time (that important epoch in the day with all country

gentleman, and with most London one also), and the last horse have be

trot out and trot in again, and its petticoat (which groom call

‘body-clothing’) replace, Harry’s thought fall back into their former

gloomy train. anxious, therefore, to learn how Alice be progress

under the weight of his high displeasure, he be about to take leave,

when Tom Rattleworth draw he aside, observe in a confidential

whisper,--



“I say, Coverdale, old Broomfield be go to ask you to stay and

dine--I know he be, he look so pleased with himself. for mercy’s sake

don’t refuse, or else I shall have to endure a _tête-à-tête_ with

the old boy, and that will use I up all together--horse, foot, and

artillery; for, besides be bore to extinction, he will do I out of

every advantage you have obtain for I to-day. he’s an awful screw,

and I’m good for nothing at a bargain after the first bottle; so if you

leave I to his tender mercy, I’m safe to be butcher like a lamb,

and serve up in my own _mint sauce_ before we quit the mahogany.”



“I’m afraid I _must_ decline,” be the reply, “for my wife have be at

home by herself all day, and it be not fair to expect she to spend

the evening in solitude also. but you need not be victimise on that

account; come home and dine with we. you’ve never meet my wife; she

be in the school-room and a pinafore when you go abroad with your

regiment. say yes, and then you can tell old Broomfield that you be

engaged to I.”



“so be it then,” be the rejoinder, and thus be Mr. Broomfield cheat

of his guest, and Harry enable to avoid a _tête-à-tête_ dinner, and

possibly a scene, with his outraged spouse. in the meantime, Alice have

be endure all the mental torment consequent upon having be angry

with the person one love good in the world. first, the idea that she

have be most cruelly use, and extensively sin against, and put

upon, be the only one which present itself to her mind in anything

like a clear and definite shape; and she bewail her evil fortune in

a very thunderstorm of weeping. have by this mean condense, and

dispose of, a vast amount of superfluous steam, she grow calm and

more reasonable, when the uncomfortable possibility gradually dawn

upon she, that she also might have be to blame--that she have first

irritated, and then defy Harry, and utterly and completely fail in

her duty as a wife; and so penitent do she become on the strength of

this conviction, that if her husband have return at that moment, she

would have throw herself at his foot and humbly implore his pardon,

which act of unqualified submission must have disarm Harry so entirely

and totally, that he would instantly have forgive she, and frankly

confess himself to blame, and Alice would never again have experience

the effect of his “quiet manner.” but, unfortunately, Harry be at

that moment differently occupy, in impress upon Tom Rattleworth

the important fact, that Lucifer would be all the well for have a

red-hot iron pass lightly over his off fetlock at the first convenient

opportunity, and thus Alice’s extreme penitence evaporate as her anger

have do. the final conclusion at which she arrive be, that she

would confess her fault to Harry on his return, and then try calmly and

quietly to convince he of his injustice. if she should succeed in this,

of which she do not feel by any mean certain, they would exchange

forgiveness; and, warn by that which have occur, take heed to their

way, and live in harmony and affection ever after. all these

sentiment Alice propose to deliver when she and her husband should be

_tête-à-tête_ after dinner, at which time she have observe Harry to be

usually in an amiable and convincible frame of mind. it may easily be

imagine, therefore, that when she hear Tom Rattleworth declare

with much enthusiasm, and in a voice raise to the pitch in which its

possessor have be willnot to direct the gallant fraction of the british

army lately under his command to “Should--der ar-r-ums,” that he be

open to “be bless,” on the spot, if “the jolly old place do not look

stunning,” she be by no mean incline to afford he the benediction he

have invoke, and heartily wish he at the bottom of the Red Sea, which

we take to be the low geographical limit to which a lady’s anathema

can be permit to descend. she have not time to do more than condemn

her unknown visitor to the oceanic penal settlement aforesaid, ere a

sound as of a jib man impel forward by some powerful agency in

the rear, together with the follow expostulation, meet her ear:--“My

dear fellow, I’m not fit to be introduce; I’m all over mud, I be upon

my life!”



in another moment the drawing-room door fly open, and her husband and

a tall, large, bushy-whisker, bluff, young man, who look as if he

could only have be bring in door by way of a trick, like a pony, or

a wheelbarrow, stand before she.



“Alice, this be Tom Rattleworth, an old schoolfellow of mine, who be

very anxious to form your acquaintance, and have kindly consent to dine

with we,” observe Harry.



“Hey!--haw!” begin Tom Rattleworth, utter sound like a bashful ogre

in his intense consciousness of his muddy disqualification for female

society; “haw!--hey! the kindness all--haw!--the other way. I

hope--Mrs. Coverdale--my dear fellow--will excuse--I tell you I ben’t

fit to be see; but you seem to be--the road be--impetuous as ever--so

very muddy.” having deliver himself of this slightly incoherent

address, the embryo M.F.H. “make his reverence” to Alice, and then

perform the military evolution express in the mysterious term,

“to the right about! wheel!” he lay violent hand upon his host, and

force he out of the room as energetically as he have be himself

propel into it.



the dinner soon make its appearance, and be a “real blessing” to

all party, for it provide they something wherewith to occupy their

mouth, and thus obviate the painful necessity of manufacture

small-talk--a toil compare with which the labour of Hercules appear

child’s play, and the up-hill work of Sisyphus a mere game at ball.



the first sharp edge of his appetite take off, Tom Rattleworth begin to

converse fluently upon the only topic which never fail he, and which

invariably form the staple ingredient in his discourse, and, indeed,

in his thought generally--viz., himself and his own saying and doing.



Alice, bored and unhappy, utter monosyllabic reply, when she

perceive that she be expect to do so; and remain silent and

_distraite_ when such exertion be not require of she.



Harry, partly grieve at perceive the accustomed sunshine in his

wife’s pretty face overcast, partly irritated at what he imagine to be

the sulkiness of her manner; annoy at his friend’s egotistic chatter,

which he feel be disgusting Alice, and which he could not contrive

to check (see that the obtuseness of Tom Rattleworth’s faculty

render he totally impervious to a hint); and generally provoke by

the change from his usual state of careless, light-hearte happiness

to his present uncomfortable frame of mind--a change which he

rightly enough attribute in a great measure to his own hastiness and

mismanagement, almost lose his temper. this he display by rate the

lad who assist Wilkins, until he reduce that unhappy juvenile to

such a pitch of nervousness and general mental debility, that, having

inveigle his mistress into sugar instead of pepper a broiled

turkey’s leg, and replenish the Champagne glass from a bottle of

bitter ale, he be send out of the room in disgrace. but in this mortal

life (which would be quite unendurable if such be not the case)

all thing soon or later come to an end--and dull dinner be no

exception to the rule--thus, after the dessert have be place on the

table, Alice, having finish her half-glass of sherry and nibble a

fragment of some little vegetable absurdity preserve in candied sugar,

and look like a geological speciman rather than a sweetmeat, reckon

she have sufficiently fulfil her duty as hostess, and be watch for

an opportunity to escape and go and be wretche comfortably by herself,

when Tom Rattleworth, address she especially, began:--



 “’pon my word,

my dear Mrs. Coverdale, when I see you and my friend Harry here so happy

together.” (Harry seize a pear and begin denude it of its rind with a

kind of ferocious eagerness, suggestive to any one acquaint with the

_dessous des carte_ of his willingness to perform a similar operation

upon his _mal à-propos_ guest), “I declare it make a fellow feel quite

down in the mouth when he think of go home to enjoy his own single

blessedness, as they call it--though single t’other thing would be more

like the truth, I fancy--but then it ben’t everybody that’s as lucky as

Harry and you--not suit to each other so charmingly, you understand.”

 (Alice, avoid her husband’s eye, bend over her sweetmeat as though

she be anxious to count the number of spangle of candy sugar

it take to cover a square inch thereof.) “now there be a man in our

regiment--curious coincidence, _his_ name be Harry, too--but those

thing do happen so curiously--Harry Flusterton his name be--well,

ma’am, when we be quarter up at Montreal, there be a family there

to whom Harry and I take out introduction, and as we find ourselves

decidedly hard up for amusement, we use to visit there pretty much.

there be two or three daughter in the family, but the eld be

the one that take my fancy most, and Harry Flusterton be of the same

opinion. accordingly we both lay siege to she, but Harry soon begin

to shoot ahead, and I, find that it be no go, quietly take up with

number two, who, although she haven’t her sister’s point, figure, or

action, be by no mean a girl to be despise, especially in a

dull place like that; well, my dear fellow--haw!--my dear ma’am, I

mean--’pon my word, I’m not fit for lady’ society--but the long and

short of it be, Harry be marry--everybody think he be the lucky

dog breathing--I’m sure I do for one, and say as much to Eliza--that

be the young one, you understand, that I be oblige to put up with.

when I make that remark to she, she look at I queer like, and say

she, ‘I hope your friend be a very sweet temper, Mr. Rattleworth?’ ‘of

course he be,’ return I, for he be, up to the day he marry, as easy

temper a fellow as you’d wish to meet with. would you believe it, Mrs.

Coverdale, this charming creature that we have both fall so desperately

in love with (not but that I like Eliza just as well when I once get

use to she) turn, out a regular vixen--a perfect virago, ma’am; why

Harry himself tell I that they haven’t much more than get over the

honeymoon, when the first time he want she to do something she don’t

like, some nonsense about visit, or some such stuff, the way she

flare up be a caution to single men----”



“my dear Rattleworth, I’m sorry to interrupt you,” exclaim Coverdale,

who could bear it no long, “but I’m afraid my wife be a little

overcome by the heat of the room--those servant will make such

ridiculously large fire. my dear Alice, if you prefer the drawing-room,

I’m sure Rattleworth will excuse you; this place be like the black-hole

in Calcutta.” and while Rattleworth, talk all the time, spring to

open the door, Harry cover his wife’s retreat by institute a furious

onslaught upon the unoffending fire. it be well he come to the rescue

when he do, for in another minute Alice would have be in hysteric.

to get rid of his dear friend as soon as possible be Harry’s next

anxiety, but this be no such easy matter. Thomas Rattleworth,

Esq., M.F.H., be at that happy moment the victim of two strenuous

necessity--one to listen to the sound of his own voice, express

not so much his idea as his paucity thereof; and the other to imbibe

a bottle of port wine, in twelve dose of a wine-glass each; and

these necessity have the unfortunate property of re-act upon and

increase each other; for talk make he thirsty, and drinking

make he talkative, so that it be eleven o’clock before he have talk

himself out, by which time the terminus of a second bottle of port have

be arrive at. with a feeling of relief such as Sinbad the Sailor

might have experience when he feel the leg of the Old Man of the Sea

gradually relax their clasp around his wearied shoulder, do Harry

assist his friend to light a cigar, then watch its fiery tip gradually

disappear in the darkness, as Rattleworth’s cover hack canter off with

its master’s six foot one of good-natured goose-flesh.



leave to his own meditation, Harry start a cigar on his own account,

and, the night be a fine one, he pace up and down the gravel walk in

front of the house until he should have clear his brain from the fume

of the wine civility have force he to swallow. the calm star come out

one by one, and as he watch their bright effulgence, an idea of

his childhood, that they might be the eye of angel, recur to

his memory; and he could even fancy they appear to gaze upon he

reproachfully. no human be possess even the low order of

reflective power, or the faint vestige of imagination, can watch the

tranquil splendour of a starlight night--a scene which at once proclaim

God’s omnipotence, and appear a work fit to the majesty of the Great

being who create it for his own glory--without becoming imbue with the

idea of rest and peace, and desirous of realise these blessing in his

own life. “with God and infinity so near we, how we loathe the trifle

of existence! and, above all, how we despise and contemn the littleness

of our fallen nature! how we repent with bitter tear of shame and

contrition the evil they have work in ourselves, and through we

to other! and how, at such a moment, do the quality we inherit from

heaven--truth, and love, and mercy--expand within we, and fill our

soul, and raise we, for the time, above ourselves, and near to the

high estate from which we have fall--alas I that it should be only for

the time! Coverdale be not insensible to these elevate influence;

his love for Alice return in all its original strength and purity,

and he determine, before he sleep that night, to bring about a

reconciliation, even if his wife should refuse to confess that she have

act wrongly. yes! he would actually go the length of own that he

have be to blame and be sorry for it, and then Alice would forgive

he, and all would be as though this foolish disagreement have never

occur.



false reasoning, Harry! there be two thing a woman, however thoroughly

she may forgive they, never forget--neglect and unkindness; and when

once these have cast their shadow across the bright eager gladness with

which she yield up her whole soul as a thank-offering to he she love,

man, with his strong, sterner nature, can no more bring back the

delicacy and freshness of that young affection, than he can restore to

the peach the bloom which his careless finger have profane--the

love may still exist in its full reality, but the bright halo of early

romance which surround it have be dispel, never to return.









CHAPTER XXVII.--THE PLEASURES of keep up the game.



having look at the star, and profit by their quiet teaching, Harry

go in a sadder and a wise man, resolve, ere he sleep that night, to

confess his fault, and, if it might be so, obtain Alice’s forgiveness.

but Alice, tired and unhappy, have go to bed, and cry herself to

sleep like a weary child; and when Harry enter her room, he find she

lie with her head pillow on her arm, and the tear-drop scarcely

dry upon her long silken eyelash, as soundly asleep as though

care, and sin, and sorrow, be evil of which her philosophy have never

dream--so Coverdale could only invoke a silent blessing upon she, and

hasten to follow her example by go to bed and to sleep himself. thus

an opportunity be lose of regain the “high estate” in his wife’s

affection, from which he have fall by reason of his inconsiderate

selfishness, and hasty and impetuous temper; and it be a fact equally

true and try, that an opportunity once lose never return, even an

advertisement in the _Times_ would fail to regain it.



one of the strange and least comprehensive of psychological phenomenon

be the total change produce in our thought, feeling, opinion, hope,

fear, sympathy, antipathy, and all the other component part which

make up that wonderful spiritual steam-engine, the mind of man, by a

good night’s sleep. we go to bed desperately in love with some charming

girl we have flirt with half the evening, despise her cruel old male

parent, who _would_ come and disturb our _tête-à-tête_, and take she

away at least an hour soon than anybody not utterly callous to all the

fine feeling of human nature would have dream of do; and have

with unchristian malignity her tall cousin in the Blues, who, having

know she from her cradle upwards, dare to call her “Gussie” to our

very face--we sleep soundly, our mind lie fallow for some six hour,

and lo! a change have come o’er we; our goddess have step down from

her pedestal, and appear a very average speciman of white musline

femininity and flirtation, whom her father have improve into quite an

amiable model _paterfamilia_, at whose patient benignity in remain,

to please his daughter, at an evening party till half-past three a.m.

we actually marvel; and as to that fine young fellow her cousin, we be

really shock when we recall our unchristian feeling towards he, and,

as some slight compensation, mentally book he for an invite to that

dinner at Blackwall which we propose bestow upon a dozen of our very

particular friend, in the unlikely event of our exchequer hold out

till the white-bait season. thus, by the next morning, Coverdale have

sleep off the sharp edge of his penitence, and when Alice begin by

a great effort to refer to the event of the previous day, with the

intention of confess herself in the wrong, and ask forgiveness,

Harry, dread a scene with a degree of horror equally masculine and

English, check the flow of her eloquence by exclaim abruptly and

cheerfully, “yes, dear, certainly--but don’t say another word about it;

we be both very silly, and make each other very miserable, when we

might be as happy as the day be long; let bygone be bygone, we will

forgive and forget, and be wise for the future, eh?” as he speak, he

draw she to he, and seal his forgiveness on her lip with a kiss,

render all discussion impossible by leave the room.



this speech (kiss include) ought to have satisfy any reasonable wife,

but unfortunately at that moment Alice be not exactly in a reasonable

frame of mind; she have dwell so long on one idea, in accordance with

which she have arrange the whole programme of a dramatic reconciliation

scene, that she by no mean approve of Harry’s short cut to concord,

render null and void all her explanation of how, and why, and

wherefore she have come to behave ill, together with a spirited sketch in

monologue of her contrition for the past and vow of amendment for the

future; the whole to conclude with certain annotation and reflection,

which she trust would so affect her husband’s feeling, and convince

his understanding, that he would for the future restrict shoot to two

short morning a-week, and cast hunting “to the dog” entirely, and now

all the mysterious pleasure the gentle sex derive from talk a thing

_well_ over, be deny she.



ah! that “talk over,” what a wonderful female attribute it be! how

vast and important a part of “woman’s mission” do it constitute!

in fact, we have meet innumerable woman--the majority of our female

acquaintance, we should say--whose whole and entire mission appear to

consist of a “call” to “talk over,” first, their neighbour’ affair (a

duty to their neighbour in which they never fail), secondly, their own.

the french aphorism (seldom act upon, by its voluble originator),

_Cela va sans dire_, must seem unspeakably absurd to these advocate for

an indefinite extension of the “freedom of debate;” while the “silent

system” must appear a more “capital punishment” than death itself,

always suppose the excellence of a punishment to be test by its

severity: but we be slightly digress.



if anything be need to prove the absurdity of human

being--creature with immortal soul, place in this world to prepare

for eternity--darken the sunshine of each other’s life by bicker

about trifle, that evidence would be afford when we observe the

manner in which such mental _nebulæ_ vanish before the presence of

any of the stern reality of existence. thus when, breakfast be

conclude, Harry be call mysteriously out of the apartment to learn

that a mount groom have just arrive from Hazlehurst Grange, with the

intelligence that old Mr. Hazlehurst have be seize with a fit, from

which, when the servant come away, he be not expect to recover,

Coverdale’s only thought be how most tenderly and judiciously to break

the sad news to Alice. having execute his painful task with a degree

of tact and delicacy of feeling for which those who know only the rough

side of his character would scarcely have give he credit, and soothe,

to the good of his ability, the burst of grief with which Alice receive
