the intelligence, Harry continue, “and now, love, the moment you be

able to start, the phaeton will be ready; it be light than the close

carriage, and in an emergency like the present, every minute become of

consequence.”



“and you?” inquire Alice, glance at he timidly through her tear.



“I of course will drive you myself; you do not suppose I should let you

go alone.”



Alice could not reply, but as she press her husband’s hand

caressingly, the old love look come back into her eye, and Harry feel

that he be forgive. on reach the Grange the report of the sick

man be more favourable than Alice have dare to hope. an apoplectic fit

constitute one of the few exceptional case in which prompt medical

assistance do not necessarily increase the evil, and the Esculapius

of the neighbourhood have this time successfully interpose between death

and his victim; while Mr. Hazlehurst have receive a lesson sufficiently

severe to prevent he from object to the substitution of toast and

water and “bland” pudding for Port wine, bottle in the year 1830, and

the roast beef of Old England. Coverdale having remain at the Grange

for three day, during which time he have shake hand with, and lament

over Arthur (who, summon at the commencement of his father’s illness,

appear look so pale and thin, that it be decide _nem. con._ that

he be work himself to death--a view of the case which he rather

than otherwise encourage by the faintness of his denial), be force

to return to the Park to attend the next meeting of magistrate, and

finally to dispose of the offend poacher. accordingly, having

arrange with Alice to send the close carriage for she on the day but

one follow, he take leave of the Hazlehurst family, and drive to

H--------. here, after a long examination, the aforesaid poacher be

convict, and sentence, one to nine month’, another to a year’s

imprisonment--Markum’s evidence be so clear and convincing, that such

an issue become inevitable. as the gamekeeper leave the court, a tall,

gipsy-look fellow come up to he, and mutter in his car, “you’ll

live to repent this day’s work, Master Keeper; look to yourself one of

these dark night.”



“look to _yourself_ if I catch you on our ground,” be Markum’s

contemptuous rejoinder; “there’s enough oakum to pick in H-------- gaol

for Tom and you too.”



“who be that fellow,” inquire Coverdale, as the man, perceive that

the keeper’s reply be begin to attract attention, turn away with

a scowl.



“that be Jack Hargrave, Mr. Coverdale, sir,” return Markum; “brother

along o’ Tom, as we’ve give twelve month to; and sarve ’Im right, a

poachin’, thievin’ wagrant.”



“be this fellow a poacher also?” ask Harry.



“that be he then,” be the reply; “a reg’lur breed un, and as deep a hand

as ever set a snare, only he’s so ‘wide o’,’ that it’s not so easy

to nab the warmint; but I’ll be down upon ’Im yet, for all his

threatening. he’s bin hear to swear he’ll put a charge o’ shot under

my veskit some o’ these night; he’d well not, though, or he may find

there’s two can play at that game.”



“no violence, my good fellow, no violence; it’s not a light thing to

shed the blood of a fellow-creature--besides, there’s a quiet way of

manage these affair. I shall warn the police to keep an eye on that

man Hargrave; he look dangerous; and you may as well put on another

watcher, it willn’t do to be shorthande just now.” so say, Coverdale

turn away, and be soon deep in conversation with the inspector of

the mount rural police; after which, refuse to make one of a jovial

party who be about to dine with Tom Rattleworth, and be tolerably

certain to remain playing whist, and imbibe strong liquor till the

small hour should be again upon the increase, he drive home to his

solitary mansion.



it be the first time since his marriage that Coverdale have dine by

himself, and he feel proportionably lonely; everything tend to remind

he of Alice--her favourite dog, a little black-and-tan spaniel, with

large love eye, not unlike her own, leap on his knee after dinner,

and gaze wistfully at the empty chair opposite, utter a low whine,

as though it would inquire, “where’s my mistress?” the footstool,

whereon her dainty little foot be willnot to repose--the screen with

which she be accustom to shade her fair cheek from the too ardent

advance of the fire--each object, animate or inanimate, recall his

thought to Alice; and feel, even more strongly than he have ever

yet feel, how deeply and tenderly he love she, he for the first time

perceive that love in its true light, and, in acknowledge its full

reality, become conscious of the duty and responsibility such an

affection entail upon he. faintly and dimly at first the light break

in upon he; deeply do he feel the difficulty of the task, and his

own inability to perform it; and bitterly, most bitterly, do he regret

his own selfish carelessness, which have, as he be fain to confess,

tend already to estrange his young wife’s affection, and to convert

a gentle, yield girl, into a wilful and exact woman. and thus he

sit, ponder over and regret the past, and form wise and good

resolution for the future, while minute glide by unobserved grow

into hour, until the sudden restlessness of the little dog, which have

be sleep quietly upon his knee, rouse he, and look at his

watch, he perceive it be nearly midnight. as he do so the dog, whose

restlessness appear to increase, utter a short bark, while at the

same moment a distant sound be faintly audible, which Harry’s practise

ear instantly recognise as the report of a gun. to spring to the

window, open the shutter, and fle up the sash, be the work of

an instant; a like space of time suffice to resolve doubt into

certainty,--guns be be discharge in a favourite plantation about

half a mile from the house--a plantation in which the pheasant be as

well feed and tame as barn-door fowl; it be evident the poacher be

take their revenge, and that these sacred bird, the Lares and Penates

of Harry’s sporting mythology, be be ruthlessly slaughter on

their roost. Harry ring the bell furiously; then, before the alarm

Wilkins (who, having commence his career in the service of an

apoplectic alderman, labour under a chronic impression that somebody

be in a fit) have pass beyond the door of the servant’ hall, he

rush impetuously out of the dining-room, and meet that bewilder

domestic in full career, nearly frighten he into an attack of the

malady he so much dread for other, by exclaim, “here, quick! tell

Saunders, or some of they, to saddle the shooting cob, and bring he

round instantly; then find I a hat and pea-jacket. quick, I say!”



as the butler vanish on his mission, Coverdale take down from a peg in

the hall, a special constable’s staff which have be intrust to he on

behalf of her gracious Majesty, at a time when an extra dose of politic

and strong beer have prove too potent for the dense agricultural pate

of certain free and independent (_alias_ bribe and tipsy) elector of

the neighbouring county town. it be a stout piece of ash, about a foot

and a half long, thick than an ordinary broom-stick, and weight with

lead, for the benefit of any unusually opaque skull into which it might

be deem advisable to knock a respect for our glorious constitution.

Harry feel its weight, and, as he pass his wrist through the leather

thong attach to it, he think to himself they would be bold man who

could prevent he, with that in his hand, from go where he please.

the instant the cob appear he spring into the saddle. “do you and

Marshal get a couple of stout stick, and make the good of your way to

the ash plantation!” he exclaim hastily; “there be poacher out, and

from their venturing to come so near the house, I should fancy there

must be a strong gang of they, and Markum may want all the help we can

give he.”



so say, Coverdale gather up the rein, and without wait the

groom’s reply, ride off at a brisk canter. as he approach the wood, he

draw in and pause, uncertain whether Markum might yet have reach the

scene of action: as he listen, the sound of man crash through the

dry underwood become distinctly audible; then shout and a clamour of

angry voice, and finally, the unmistakeable noise of a conflict meet his

ear. pause no long, he put his horse into a gallop, and dash

on till he reach a hand-gate lead into the wood. this, to his

annoyance, he find lock; true, he have a master-key, which he have

fortunately bring with he, but he be force to dismount in order

to unfasten the padlock. while thus engage, the sound prove that the

affray be still rage fiercely, and, as he fling the gate open, a gun

be discharge, follow almost instantaneously by the report of

two other. fear mischief might occur before he could reach the

combatant, Coverdale remount hastily, and heedless alike of

obstacle and darkness, gallop down one of the grass ride through the

plantation, avoid collision with the trunk and branch of tree

by, as it appear, a succession of miracle. before, however, he

could arrive at the scene of action, the sound of blow, the shout

and imprecation, have cease, and nothing but a confused hum of voice,

together with a low moaning, as of some person ill or in pain, meet his

ear. force his horse through the tangled underwood, Coverdale come

suddenly upon a group of man, amongst whom he recognise several of his

own farm labourer, while two under-keeper be kneel beside the

prostrate figure of a man who, from the stiff, unnatural attitude in

which he lay, appear either dead or die. to leap to the ground, and

snatch a lantern from one of the bystander, be Harry’s first act;

then bend over the fallen man, he recognise in the ghastly feature,

distort and convulse with agony, the well-know countenance of

honest, sturdy Markum, while from a gun-shot wound in his right side the

dark life-blood be slowly flow.



“how have this happen?” be Coverdale’s hurried inquiry. “be it an

accident, or have any of those scoundrel dare to shoot he?”



there be a moment’s pause, and then one of the elder man reply,

“it wor no accident, Mr. Coverdale; but gile there can tell you well,

squire; he wor near to un when he drop.”



the under-keeper thus appeal to--a tall, strap young fellow, who

be vainly attempt to staunch the blood which still continue to

flow--turn to reply, while Coverdale, kneel beside the wound man,

endeavour to arrange a more effectual bandage.



“all as I know, sir,” he say, “be that I wor a watch nigh down by

the warren, when up cum poor Master Markum here, and ‘gile,’ say he,

‘ye’re want, lad; there’s they out as don’t oughter be.’ so he and

I, and the rest o’ our mate here, which master have appinte to meet at

eleven o’clock--for I expect he’d have some hint give he of what be to

be up, make for the ash spinney, and lay we down in a ditch. well, it

warn’t long afore we hear the blackguard at work among the pheasant,

a banging away like blaze. we wait till they get near we, and then we

up and at ’em like good un. there be more of ’em nor there be

o’ we, so they show light a bit. poor master there he jest wor

real savage; he hit out hard and straight, and roll ’em over like

nine-pin; they worn’t o’ no manner o’ use again he, not none on ’em.

well, they soon get enough of that sort of fun, and one arter another

cut away, till at last they all fairly turn tail and bolt--that be,

all but one, and he master collared, and say he, ‘stop a bit, Jack;

I’m agoin’ to send you to see your brother in H-------- gaol; I’m

afeard Tom should be dull for want o’ cumpany, poor chap!’ well, Jack

Hargrave, for he it wor, fit sharp for his liburty, but master wor too

good a man for he; and he’d a take he as safe as mutton, only Jack

hollard arter one of his mate as have a gun, and tell he to shoot the

-------- keeper, and not let he be take. the fellow stop and face

round--he wor a young chap as I know well--I’d cotche sight of his

face afore he cut away, a soft young feller, as anybody might bully into

anything; and when Jack rap out a volley of oath, and tell he to

let fly, and chance hittin’ he, shoot he do, and poor master let go

his hold o’ Jack’s collar, and roll over and over like I’ve see many

a hare and rabbid roll over afore his gun.”



“but there be more than one barrel discharge,” interpose Coverdale;

“I hear three shot in succession--how be that?”



“why, when I see poor Master Markum fall, I be jest agoin’ to kneel

down to raise he a bit, when I ketche sight o’ Jack Hargrave and his

pal a cutting away like lamplighter, and I feel mad like to think he

should get off scotfree arter what he’d be and do, and have my gun

in my hand, I give ’em the content of both barrel; it worn’t right,

I know, Mr. Coverdale, but if you’d be in my place, squire, I’m

bless if I don’t think you’d ha do the same, axe your pardon.”



feel a strong private conviction that “Giles” have only judge

he correctly, Harry look grave and shake his head, as if such

a possibility could not exist in the case of a magistrate, ere he

inquire, “do you think you hit either of they?”



“they’d get a farish start before I pull at ’em,” be the reply,

“and the light ain’t that good for a long shot, but I fancy Jack

Hargrave’s get something to take home with he, for he give a rare jump

as the charge reach he; but it warn’t enough to stop he, for I see

he a runnin’ like a greyhound arter-ward.”



while this conversation be proceed, Coverdale, by aid of sundry

neckcloth, and a strip that he cut from his own pea-jacket, have

contrive a bandage which in great measure stop the bleeding, and

Markum revive sufficiently to recognise those about he; as his eye

fall on Coverdale, a faint smile pass across his feature.



“be it you, squire?” he murmur in a low voice. “ah! you always have a

kind heart of your own; Jack Hargrave’s kep his word, you see. I expect

he and his mate ’as finish I atween ’em this time.”



“we’ll hope not, my poor fellow--but don’t speak. do you think you can

bear carry yet--yes? four of you take that hand-gate off its hinge,

and bring it here; we’ll lay he on that. we shall have a surgeon for

you directly, my poor fellow! I send one of the lad off on my horse to

fetch Mr. Gouger the moment I come up--now, steady with he. I’ll lift

his head--that’s it; now raise the gate steadily. gently there--well

do--be you all ready? step together mind--march.”



as he speak, Harry (who himself support one corner of the temporary

litter he have contrive) and three other, raise the wounded man on

their shoulder, and carry he to his own cottage, which fortunately

be near at hand. he bear the transit bravely, though the pain

occasion by such motion as be unavoidable, reduce he more than once

to the verge of fainting. shortly after he have reach his destination

the surgeon arrive. Coverdale wait until he have pronounce the wound

dangerous, though not necessarily mortal, then leave he to make

a more minute examination, he quit the house. he find a mount

policeman await he outside, who, make his round, have be

attract by the sound of gun at that unusual hour.



“ah, policeman, I be just go to send after you; my head keeper have

be shoot by these poach rascal, and be seriously hurt, I’m afraid!”

 exclaim Coverdale. “how be we to make sure of the fellow who do it?

it lie between a man call Jack Hargrave--”



“a reg’lur bad un,” observe the horse-patrol, parenthetically.



“you say you know the other man,” continue Harry, appeal to the

under-keeper; “be you acquainted with his name?”



“they do call he ‘winkey’ in a general way, from a trick he’s get with

his eyelid; but his right name be Jim Fags,” be the reply.



“I know he,” observe the policeman. “well, sir, as we’re acquaint

with the party, I should say we’re safe to be down upon ’em

somewhere to-morrow. I’ll ride over to H--------, and put all our man

on the scent.”



“Stay! that give I an idea,” say Coverdale; then turn to the

under-keeper, he continue in a low voice--“You be sure you hit

Hargrave--be you, gile?”



the young man nod in the affirmative, and his master resumed,--



“go and fetch Nero, poor Markum’s night-dog, muzzle he, and bring he

in one of the greyhound leash. we’ll contrive to take these rascal

before day dawn, policeman.”



while Coverdale be explain his plan to the patrol, Giles return

with the dog: it be a splendid animal, a cross between the English

mastiff and a spanish bloodhound. its size be unusual, and its strength

enormous. its eye glare red in the torchlight, like those of some

wild beast. when it see the policeman it utter a low growl, and the

bristle on its back stand up like a mane; but at a word from Coverdale

it relinquish its hostile attitude, and with a sagacious look, which

say almost as plainly as word could have express it--“I comprehend;

it’s not he they’ve send for I to worry”--thrust its huge head

caressingly into its master’s hand.



“now patrol,” resume Coverdale, “if you will ride along the skirt of

the wood, and lead my horse, I fancy I shall be able to put the dog on

the track of these fellow--and, if so, he will never leave it till the

game be run down. you have handcuff with you?”



“aye, and pistol too, for the matter of that,” be the reply.



“I don’t expect they will be require,” rejoin Coverdale; “the

scoundrel will scarcely want more fighting than they’ve have already;”

 then signal gile to follow with the dog he turn, and, re-enter

the plantation, soon reach the scene of the late conflict.



“now try and find, an nearly as possible, the spot where Hargrave be

when you fire at he,” begin Coverdale; “give I the dog to hold, and

take the lantern with you.”



gile obey; and having walk about fifty pace down a narrow pathway

through the wood, begin carefully to examine the ground on either side.

having pursue his investigation for some minute in silence, he pause,

examine the spot still more closely, and then make a sign to Coverdale

to join he.



on reach the place Harry observe, by the light of the lantern,

several dark spot, and a long mark on the soft ground, as though some

person have slip and nearly fall, then deep footstep lead towards

the outskirt of the wood. the moment the dog perceive the scent of

blood all the savage instinct of its nature awake, and, with a

bind, which test the strength of the leash, and nearly dislocate

Coverdale’s shoulder, it spring forward along the track of the

fugitive. five minute’ painful toil through bush and briar, bring

they to the outskirt of the plantation, where they find the policeman

wait with the horse. hastily spring to the saddle, Coverdale

make Giles attach a small cord he have bring with he to the end of

the leash, against which the bloodhound now strain impatiently; then

twist the other end round his own wrist, he be about to desire the

under-keeper to return, when the patrol interfere by observing,--



“well take Giles with we, sir!”



“why so, policeman?” rejoin Coverdale, sharply; “we’re two to two,

fresh man against tired one; besides, you’re arm, and they’re not.”



“Jack’s get a gun with he, and be likely enough to use it now his

steam’s up,” insinuate Giles, who by no mean approve of lose his

share in the expedition.



“and when we have nab ’em, I shall want help to convey ’em to

H-------- gaol,” plead the policeman. “I can take he up behind I.”



“as you will; only lose no more time,” be Coverdale’s reply; and

cheer on the dog, he ride forward at a brisk trot.



the track lead they through the Park, and then over hill and dale,

plough field, and rough stubble, till it bring they out upon a wide

bleak common, dot here and there with patch of furze and broom,

which show dark and shadowy in the moonbeam, like plume upon a

hearse. across the wild and most tangled portion of the heath the dog

lead they, still strain at the leash, and utter from time to time a

suppress whimper indicative of impatience. on the farth side of the

common rise a steep bank, in one portion of which a deep hollow have be

excavate for the purpose of obtain gravel. as the dog approach

this place, its eagerness become, if possible, strong than before,

until, at about thirty yard from the spot, it suddenly stop, and

again erect the bristle on its back, utter a deep growl. at the

same moment, Coverdale, whose sight be remarkably keen, perceive a

figure cautiously steal away under cover of the bush. point he

out to the policeman, whose horse be begin to evince symptom of

distress under its double burden, Coverdale observed,--



“I can only see one man, but let we make sure of he. get down gile,

and hold the dog. now patrol, while I ride round that bush and head the

fellow, do you go on and seize he; and if you want any assistance, I

shall be ready to afford it.”



so say, Coverdale ride forward to cut off the poacher’s retreat,

while the policeman, put spur to his horse, and draw his

cutlass, dash up to the fellow, and seize he by the collar.



overawe by the gleam weapon, and exhaust by his previous

exertion, the unfortunate Jim Fags (_alias_ winkey) attempt no

resistance; and the policeman avail himself of his pusillanimity to

produce the handcuff, and dextrously secure his prisoner. he be thus

engage when Coverdale, who be walk his horse quietly towards they,

suddenly catch sight of what, at the first glance, appear to he only

the stump of a tree, but on close inspection prove to be the figure

of a man, crouch under the shadow of the gravel-pit, while, at

the moment in which Coverdale first perceive he, he be take a

deliberate aim with a short gun at the unconscious patrol. for a moment

the policeman’s life hang upon a thread; but a slight movement of the

horse bring the unfortunate Winkey’s head into the line of fire, and

his accomplice lower his piece, and slightly alter his position,

while he take fresh aim. the opportunity be not to be lose--quick as

think Coverdale rise in his stirrup, and with the full force of his

muscular arm hurl the constable’s staff, which he have retain the

whole evening, at the head of the kneeling figure. fortunately for the

policeman, the missile take effect, and, stun by the force of the

blow, Jack Hargrave (for he it be) measure his length upon the turf,

discharge the gun harmlessly as he fall. before he could regain his

foot, Giles and the dog (who, but for his muzzle, would have tear the

poacher to piece) be upon he. in less than two hour from that time

both the culprit be safely lodge in H-------- gaol.









CHAPTER XXVIII.--ALICE succour the distressed.



Mr. Hazlehurst’s progress towards recovery be so satisfactory that

Alice, when the carriage arrive to fetch her home, feel not the

small scruple in leave he. as Harry consider the distance

between the Grange and Coverdale Park too great for his carriage-horse

to perform twice in one day, the equipage have be dispatch the

previous evening, and the servant be consequently unacquainted with

the event of the past night. having take leave of her mother--who,

rouse by the necessity of become a nurse instead of a patient,

appear rather benefit than otherwise by the unusual demand upon her

energy--and of Emily, now fast develop into a very pretty girl,

Alice start on her return home, and accomplish the great portion

of the transit without let or hindrance. when within about five mile

of the Park, however, one of the horse be discover to have cast a

shoe; and as it would have be worth more than his situation to have

take it far in so defenceless a condition, the coachman draw up at

a village blacksmith’s, where the evil might be remedie. under these

circumstance, Alice determine to walk on till the carriage should

overtake she, which, as the morning be fine, she consider the reverse

of a hardship. ponder many thing--for Alice be no long the

careless, light-hearted girl we once describe she--she trudge on, at

first briskly, then more leisurely, as the road begin to ascend, until

she might have proceed some two mile; and yet the carriage do not

make its appearance. toil up hill, attire as lady usually be

from November to April, with an amount of merino, velvet, and fur, which

might defy the severity of a siberian winter, and be clearly _de trop_

under the influence of a sunshiny morning in March, not unnaturally

render Alice hot and tired; and fancy, from her imperfect knowledge

of the locality, that she must be upon her husband’s territory, she

determine to make acquaintance with the inmate of a cottage which she

perceive by the roadside a short distance high up the hill, and, with

their permission, to rest herself until the carriage should arrive. with

this intention she approach the cottage, and find the door

close, rap at it with first her knuckle, then the handle of a

most frivolous and ephemeral little parasol; but neither of these

application produce the desire effect, she, like little Red

riding-hood, raise the latch and open the door. the sight which

meet her eye be one calculate alike to stimulate her curiosity and

interest her sympathy. in a cradle, on the opposite side of the room,

lie an unconscious and remarkably pretty and comfortable-look baby

fast asleep, while near it, with the light from the casement streaming

full upon her smooth dark hair, only partially conceal beneath her

neat white cap, sit the young mother, her face hide in her hand,

weep bitterly. start at the sound of the opening door, she remove

her hand, and disclose feature which, swollen and disfigure as they

be by grief, yet evince token of unusual beauty. she rise as Alice

enter, and hastily dry her tear, stand regard she with a wild

eager glance of inquiry.



“what have you come to tell I?” she say: “they have not relent--not

set he at liberty again?--or the other one--he be not bad--oh,

God!--not dead?”



surprised and embarrass by the strange eagerness of her manner, and

interested by her appearance and evident distress, Alice hasten to

assure she that she be not the bearer of any tiding, good or evil, and

having explain the object of her intrusion, continued--



“but you be anxious or unhappy about something; will you not tell I

why you be cry so bitterly when I come in--perhaps I may be able to

assist you?”



thus appeal to, the girl (for she appear scarcely above twenty)

fix her dark eye on Alice’s face, and read therein her kind and

love nature, which indeed be so legibly depict that the veri

dullard at decipher character could scarcely fail to discover it,

answer more gently than she have before spoken--



“I beg pardon, lady; but I’m amost crazy with grief this morning, and

my head’s so a-run on it, that I hardly know what I’m a-say or

a-doing on. Ye’re welcome to rest, lady, as long as you please;” and as

she speak she dust a chair with her apron, and place it for Alice,

who, seat herself, resumed--



“you say you be unhappy, but you do not tell I what about.”



the woman pause for a moment in thought, then continued--“I need make

no secret of it; the whole country round be ring with it by this

time. some poor fellow, lady, as have wife and child to feed, and no

money to buy bread to give to ’em, go to get a few of the bird and

thing that’s run wild in the wood of they that’s rich, and don’t

want ’em; and the keeper cum to stop ’em, and one of ’em get shoot

in the confusion; and the police have take my husband and my brother,

and swear the’re the man that do it; and the’re to be have up to-day

before they that’s sure to condemn ’em, innocent or guilty--gentleman

that chuse to keep the wild creature that God send for food for they

as want it, all for their own selfish amusement--beg your pardon,

lady--but it’s the truth; and when one’s heart ache like mine do, the

truth will out.”



“it be natural, perhaps, that you should think thus in your situation,”

 return Alice, gravely; “but depend upon it your husband and your

brother will not be punish unless they justly deserve it. the

gamekeeper be not kill I hope?”



“oh no, my lady! not hurt very serious neither I do hope; only they want

to make the most of it, to get a chance to punish my poor fellow, don’t

you see?” be the reply; “and if my husband be put in prison for long,

and lay out of work, what’s to become o’ I and the child?”



“you have more than this one, then?” inquire Alice.



for answer the woman rise, and pass into the inner room of the

cottage, in less than a minute return, bear in her arm a little

girl, apparently about two year old, whose bright, rosy cheek, and

eye evidently distress by the vivid sunlight, give unmistakeable

token of having be rouse out of a sound sleep. Alice possess a

thorough woman’s love of child, lead she to consider ugly one

pretty, and pretty one “little angel;” so she immediately take this

particular duodecimo angelic speciman on her knee, and win its celestial

affection by allow it to play with her watch, and a bunch of

miscellaneous rubbish attach thereunto, and denominate, on the _lucus

a non lucendo_ principle, a chatelaine. this reinforcement of infantry

having completely win the day (the “dear” sleep baby have be a

powerful, unconscious advocate of its parent’s cause), Alice begin to

consider how good she could assist the distressed mother. the first

point be to learn to whom to apply in favour of the culprit, and she

accordingly inquire on whose land they have be take, and in whose

service the wound gamekeeper reside? the answer be at the same

time embarrassing and satisfactory. of course, if the offence have be

commit upon her husband’s property, he could, if he would, decline to

prosecute the offender--if he would?--there lie the difficulty. Alice

be well aware of the serious light in which Harry regard the crime

of poach; and the attack on the gamekeeper even she be force

to reprobate; but if it should prove that the man be not seriously

injure, she trust to her newly-regain influence to enable she to

place the matter in such a light that Harry would agree with she in

overlook the culprit’s offence for the sake of his family; or, at all

event, if that be expect too much of his penitence, she have only

to ask it as a personal favour, and he surely could not refuse she.

so, carry away by her feeling of kindly sympathy, and act on the

impulse of the moment, she put forth all her power of consolation,

and end by disclose her name, and the relation in which she stand

towards that persecutor of poacher, Harry Coverdale, at the same time

promise to use her influence, which she represent as all-powerful,

to screen the culprit from the effect of their misdemeanor.



before her consolatory harangue be well conclude, the carriage

arrive, and Alice, having kiss the child (the unfortunate

baby be arouse expressly for the performance of the affectionate

ceremony, a violation of the right of the subject which it resent

by cry and slobber with a twenty-infant power over Alice’s velvet

mantle), leave five shilling in the hand of their mamma, by way of a

peace-offering, and depart, thoroughly satisfied with she _début_ in

the character of poor man’s friend and cottager’s comforter. all the way

she drive home she be build castle in the air for the benefit and

behoof of the ruin family, having mentally adopt the little girl as

lady’s-maid, and apprentice the baby, which be of the nobler sex, to a

serious and immaculate carpenter, before she reach the Park.



Coverdale be absent when his wife arrive, having ride over

to H--------, to assist at the committal of Jack Hargrave and his

accomplice; but she receive from Wilkins, who be, in more sense

than one, a confidential servant, an over-full, untrue, and

particularly-exaggerated account of the affray of the previous night,

from which she acquire two fact, which tend considerably to disquiet

she, viz.:--first, that the wounded man be markum, her husband’s

especial favourite; and secondly, that Harry have be personally

involve in the affair; both of which consideration increase the

difficulty of the negotiation for gaol-delivery to which she have

incautiously pledge herself. having take off her thing, she

proceed first to fraternise with her King Charles spaniel and the two

canary-bird (which latter plume biped celebrate her return in song

of shrill triumph, like a couple of inebriated penny whistle), then

to put all the ornament right, which the housemaid have dust into

uncomfortable and heterodox position. she have just discover a china

cup, which nobody have break, and which yet be divide in several

place, having probably split its own side laugh at the grotesque

figure with which its manufacturer have see fit to embellish it, and

she be hunt for a bottle of diamond cement wherewith to repair

the damage before her husband’s return, when the sound of horse’ foot

announce that event to have take place.



the first word that meet her ear be, “let one of the helper go down

to Markum’s cottage, wait till Mr. Gouger have see he again, and bring

I his report without a moment’s delay; if it should be unsatisfactory

I’ll send for Brodie by electric telegraph. be your mistress return?”



a warm embrace, an expression of his delight at have she back again,

a hurried enquiry after Mr. Hazlehurst, and then Harry rush into his

narrative of the poach affair, and in his eagerness to detail every

circumstance of a matter which interest he so deeply, do not notice

the tameness of Alice’s sympathy, or the lukewarm manner in which she

second his virtuous indignation against the miscreant who have all but

murder good, honest Markum: “and small thank to they that it be ‘all

but,’ for, if ever a scoundrel mean mischief, that scoundrel be Jack

Hargrave.”



Alice see this be no time to urge her suit, and so merely confine

herself to the general remark, that it be a dreadful affair for all

party, and that she pity the wife of the wretched man who have

commit the rash act, as much as anybody concern in the matter; to

which Harry replied:--



“that it serve they right for marry poacher, and that they might

think they be lucky not to be the victim themselves, for that a

fellow who would take to poach be capable of cut his wife’s

throat, or of any other enormity.”



Mr. Gouger’s report be, on the whole, satisfactory. Markum be go

on well, though he (Gouger) could not pronounce he out of danger;

the injury be very serious, and several day must elapse before the

ulterior consequence would be apparent; or, as the doctor himself

remark, the effect of extraneous particle of plumbago, or lead,

introduce into the vital system by the sudden expansion of saltpetre

and other explosive compound compress within the narrow limit of a

gun-barrel, and discharge thence by ignition, accord to the natural

law of projectile, be most subtle and deleterious, lead sometimes

to the total destruction of animal life, at other to a concussion of

the nervous system; or again, &c. &c.: from which sapient opinion Harry

collect that Brodie need not be send for immediately.



day glide by, the prisoner be remand till Markum’s chance of life

or death should be ascertain, and Alice have not find a fitting moment

in which to make her appeal. at length the surgeon, with grave look,

which might mean everything, anything, or nothing, advise, merely as

a matter of precaution, that the wounded man should make a deposition

before a magistrate, so that if anything _be_ to happen, the

jury might have the advantage of his statement of fact. Coverdale,

therefore, having persuade one of his brother magistrate to accompany

he, proceed to the cottage for the above purpose. shortly after

he have set off, Alice be inform that a poor woman be desirous

of speak to she; and on order she to be show in, she be less

surprised than embarrassed to recognise in the tearful applicant her

cottage hostess, the wife of the culprit, Jack Hargrave. the result of

the interview may be easily foresee. Alice descant on the greatness

of the crime commit, Mr. Coverdale’s virtuous indignation against

the offender, and the consequent difficulty of persuade he not to

prosecute they. Mrs. Jack bring forward, in reply, the baby and a

flood of tears,--arguments so unanswerable that Alice, having kiss the

one, and all but join in the other, dismiss the afflicted matron,

having renew her pledge of exert her whole influence in favour

of the prisoner. it be with a feeling akin to desperation that she

determine to plead she _protégées_’ cause the moment Harry should

return, certain that if she again allow her ardour to cool, she should

never have courage to enter upon the subject to he. accordingly, as

soon as he have finish give she an account of the clear and able

manner in which Markum have detail the proceeding of the eventful

night on which the affray have occur, she began:--



“I, too, have have rather a try interview; the wife of one of the

man who have be take up on suspicion have be here--a frail,

delicate-look, young creature, scarcely more than a girl, with the

dearest, sweetest, little baby imaginable. I do so wish you have see

it!”



Harry mutter a reply, which, though scarcely audible, convey the

impression that he be perfectly content without having have ocular

demonstration of its infantine perfection; and Alice continued--



“yes, I wish you have see both mother and child--its sweet, innocent

look, and the poor girl’s tear, would have plead her cause well

than any argument of mine can do, your kind heart could never have

resist they.”



“plead her cause,” repeat Coverdale; “that mean, because her husband

and his accomplice have be so obliging as to destroy my game, and

murder, or half murder, as the case may prove, my head keeper, she

consider it my duty to support herself and family, I suppose; she

have bring this irresistible baby as a safe dodge to work upon your

feminine susceptibility; and, with thorough woman’s logic, she have

persuade you to look upon she as a suffer innocent, and upon I as a

tyannical oppressor. now confess--be not this the truth?”



“no, really it be not,” reply Alice, eagerly. “I own I think you, from

your passion for field-sport, take rather an exaggerated view of the

crime of poach; but I quite feel as you do, that wound poor Markum

be a cruel and cowardly act; still, revenge it upon this family will

not benefit he nor ourselves.”



“I don’t wish the people to starve, of course,” return Harry, moodily,

“though I should imagine the young woman and her brat can scarcely have

get through all the game in her larder yet. I should not mind starve

on hashed hare and broiled pheasant’ leg myself for a week or two;

however, if the poor girl really be in want, I have no objection to your

relieve she, but do not be impose upon, darling, that be all that I

mean to say.”



the kindness of her husband’s manner, and the good-natured way in which

he appear willing to support the family of the man who have injure

he, serve alike to remove Alice’s fear, and to lead she to overrate

the extent of her influence with her husband; so, lean her arm on

his shoulder, while with her other hand she smooth back his cluster

hair, she continue, “what a good, kind boy it be, though it do growl

sometimes. but now, to show you that my _protégée_ be not seek to

impose on I for the sake of obtain money, I will tell you that her

petition be for quite a different object, and one creditable alike to

her feeling as a wife and a sister: she want you to act as only a

high and generous nature like your own would be capable of act--she

implore you to pardon her husband and her brother.”



“to do what!” exclaim Harry sharply, a dark shade come across his

feature.



“to let off two of the man who be engage in this unlucky

business--her husband and her brother--not to prosecute they, I mean,”

 return Alice, remove her hand from her husband’s shoulder and

prepare to “hold her own,” in the dispute she foresee impend.



“and their name:” inquire Coverdale.



Alice repeat they.



“as I expect,” resume Coverdale; “the man who fire the shot and his

accomplice, who, more guilty than himself, urge he to do it. now, ask

your own good sense, Alice, and reflect a moment before you answer. even

be I willing, can I in common justice let these fellow off?”



“oh, yes!” exclaim Alice, without a moment’s deliberation; “it be

so great--so noble to forgive an injury! revenge be but a mean, petty

feeling, after all.”



“an admirable reason for shake hand with an individual who have

knock you down,” return Coverdale, “but none whatsoever for

screen two malefactor from the just punishment of their ill-deed;”

 then, lapse into the magistrate, he continue, “you mistake the whole

scope and intention of our penal code, my dear Alice. we do not punish

offender as an act of revenge upon the individual, but in order to

benefit society by deter other from commit a like crime;

thus, lay aside personal feeling, I should be do an injury to the

community at large, by refuse to prosecute these fellow. you see this

clearly, do you not?”



Alice’s reasoning power do see it, and have see it all along, but

Alice have also see the poor wife and the meritorious and seductive

baby, and she care “fifty thousand time” (as she herself would have

express it) more for they than for the community at large; so find

that the argument be go against she, she, woman-like, adroitly

shift her ground. “accord to your reasoning, there would be no

room for such a quality as mercy,” she begin; “stern, inexorable justice

would condemn every criminal, no matter what extenuate circumstance

there might be; in each case punishment must follow sin, as effect

follow cause. I, for one, should be very sorry always to be judge by

such a cruel rule.”



“oh, if you’re go to put german metaphysical sophistry in the place

of english common-sense, I’ve no more to say about it,” return Harry,

gruffly; “only it seem to my simplicity that punishment always do

follow crime in this world, as soon as it’s find out. if a brat steal

the sugar, its mother slap it; if a schoolboy prig apple, the master

flog he; if an apprentice bolt with the till, the law transport he;

if Jack murder Tom, the hangman stretch his neck for him:--and serve

’em all right say I; it would be a precious deal bad world to live

in if it be not so, to my thinking.”



Alice pause to consider the justice of this remark--we will follow she

example!









CHAPTER XXIX.--HOW to make HOME HAPPY.



Mrs. Coverdale, resume the matrimonial discussion break off at the

end of the last chapter, thus pursue the argument by which she hope to

induce her husband to let off her poach _protégé._



“in the present case the innocent must suffer with the guilty. I see no

justice in ruin a poor family by imprison or transport the only

member who be able to work and support it.”



“the say member should have think of that himself,” return Harry;

“if he have be work and support his family, he would have be

safe from transportation, like any other honest man; but as he prefer

to steal my game and shoot my keeper, he thereby deprive his family of

the pleasure of his inestimable society; it be he, therefore, who have

bring this evil upon they, not I; and when I consent to your relieving

their necessity out of my pocket, I think I be do, to say the least

of it, as much as any reasonable woman ought to expect of I.”



despite her prejudice in favour of the seraphic baby and its

interesting mother, Alice feel the truth of her husband’s reasoning;

but she have boast of her power too confidently, and pledge herself

to exert it too deeply, to retreat; so, perceive that argument would

avail her nothing, she be oblige to fall back upon woman’s last

resource--personal influence, and strive to win from Harry’s affection

that which his reason have deny she. a dangerous experiment, pretty

Alice! and one in which, if your philosophy do but go deep enough to

enable you to discern it, you would perceive success to be a great

evil than failure, for it would argue culpable weakness in he on whom

you have to lean for support through life. but Alice be by no mean in

an ethical frame of mind at that moment, and care only for obtain

her point by any mean which occur to she; so, draw a stool close

to Harry, she meekly seat herself at his foot, and look up into his

face with her large implore eye, begin coaxingly, “Harry, dear, be

you quite, quite determined to say no?”



an affirmative bend of the head be the only reply.



“but if I make it a personal request,” she continue, lay her soft

cheek caressingly against his hand; “if I ask you to forgive these man

for my sake, and so afford I the exquisite pleasure of make this poor

woman happy? oh! you will not refuse I. if you do, I shall think you do

not love I. come, you will say yes.”



Poor Harry! he be sorely perplexed. have it be any personal

sacrifice--even a pledge to give up hunting or shooting--which she

require of he, he would gladly have yield, in the true and deep

tenderness towards his wife which his late self-examination have arouse.

but the serious thought which a review of his past error have call

forth, while they point out to he how he have fail in his duty to

she whom he have vow to love and protect, also prove to he that where

Alice be incline to act wrongly, or foolishly, he be bind to save

she even from herself; and his clear, good sense instantly tell he that

this be a request which she ought not to have urge, since to grant it

would necessitate a sacrifice of principle on his part. accordingly, he

replied--



“alice love, listen to I; this be not a mere matter of personal

feeling, or I would yield to you without a moment’s hesitation, but it

involve a question of right and wrong. I could not refuse to prosecute

these man without diffuse an amount of moral evil amongst the whole

of my poor tenantry, which year of the most careful supervision would

fail to eradicate. the utmost I can promise you be, that the culprit

shall have every opportunity afford they of clear themselves;

and if, as I be convince, that prove impossible, every palliate

circumstance shall be bring forward and allow its full weight. I

have already give you my free permission to assist the poor woman and

her child, and more than this you cannot expect I to say.”



“but I do, or rather I _do_, expect you to say more,” return Alice,

with flash eye and glow cheek; “I expect you to say what I

would have say to you, if you have appeal to I thus--that there be

nothing, even if it be life itself, that I would not give up for your

sake. but I see how it be, you do not really care for I, or, if you

do, man’s love be not like woman’s, it be merely the excitement of the

pursuit that interest you--the prize once attain become valueless

in your eye: in fact, love, which make the entire joy or sorrow of a

woman’s life, be to man but a superior kind of sporting--more engrossing

than a foxchase, or than hunt a poor stag to death, simply because

the game be of a high order.” she pause to give vent to a sob which

she be unable entirely to repress, then continue in a sarcastic tone

of voice: “however, mighty hunter as you be, I do not intend to give

you the satisfaction of be in at my death; I have too much of the old

Hazlehurst spirit about I to break my heart for a man who do not love

I. there be a _quiet way_, as you call it, of arrange these affair:

you have your own pursuit and amusement, henceforward I shall have

mine. you need not dread my again attempt to interfere either with

your pleasure, or your graver occupation. I have have too severe a

lesson on each point to forget it readily. but I expect you to exercise

the same forbearance towards I. from this day forth we each follow our

own line!” and, draw her shawl over her shoulder, with an imperious

gesture, as of an offended queen, Alice sweep out of the room, leave

Harry in a frame of mind which may be more easily imagine than

describe.



a complete change, which might have be date from the above

conversation, appear to have take place in Alice Coverdale. instead

of shrink, as she have hitherto do, from society, she rather court

it than otherwise--order the carriage, and visit the different

family in the neighbourhood, without consult Harry on the subject,

or seem to care in the slight degree whether he accompany she or

not. at first this conduct on his wife’s part occasion Coverdale the

great uneasiness; but, after a time, see that she be amuse and

interested by the new acquaintance she thus form, he begin to hope

that good might perhaps come out of evil, and that the intimacy then

commence might afford source of last pleasure when the feeling of

pique which have lead she to seek they should have long since die away.

and so the time glide on, work its usual change in man and thing

as it pass away.



Mr. Gouger having venture one day to commit himself to the rash

assertion that Markum be sink rapidly, and could not possibly

survive the week, from that hour the gamekeeper begin to amend, and have

sufficiently advanced in his progress towards recovery to be able

to appear and give evidence in person, when Jack Hargrave and his

accomplice take their trial at the next assize. so unmistakeably be

their guilt bring home to they, that they be each sentence to seven

year’ transportation, and would probably have have fourteen allot

to they, but for the thorough good faith with which Harry redeem his

promise to Alice that every extenuating circumstance should be clearly

place before the jury. indeed he labour so strenuously to impress

this point upon the counsel for the prisoner, that the learn brother,

entertain a proper degree of professional scepticism in regard to the

purity of human motive, immediately settle, to his own satisfaction,

that Jack Hargrave must be a natural son of the late Admiral Coverdale,

commend, with his die breath, to his nephew’s especial care and

protection. Alice receive the news of the verdict with great _sing

froid_, merely remark that she have feel certain all along that it

would be so; but when she have gain the privacy of her own chamber, she

indulge in a hearty flood of tear, occasion as much by what she be

pleased to consider her husband’s inhumanity, as by her compassion for

the poor woman and her transcendental baby.



as these latter individual exercise no further influence over the

destiny of our principal _dramatis personæ_, we may as well, ere

we finally take leave of they, add the information that Alice (having

support they much well than Jack Hargrave have do in his good

day), at the expiration of two year send they out at her own expense

to join that worthy, who, reform by seasickness and the amenity of

convict discipline, have obtain a ticket of leave, by reason of which

privilege he be enact the part of a penitent bullock-driver, to the

admiration of all right-minded settler in Australia.



the month of May have begin to temper with a dash of sunshine the fine

old English east wind of April, which annually sow their share of

the seed of consumption in the glorious british constitution--Harry

Coverdale have cease to oppress the brute creation, leave fox and

pheasant to increase and multiply by antagonistic progression--and all

London be flock to the Royal Academy Exhibition, to see a great many

very original portrait of gentleman, who scarcely look the character

after all--when one fine morning Alice receive a letter from the modern

Babylon, in Mrs. Crane’s handwriting. having eagerly peruse it, she

exclaimed,--



“Kate have write a most kind and press invitation to we to come and

stay with they; Mr. Crane wish it as much as she do.”



“or as much as she order he to do rather,” mutter Coverdale,

_sotto voce_.



“of course you can have no objection to my accept it,” continue

Alice; “for myself, at all event?”



“be not I invite?” inquire Harry, gravely.



“yes, certainly; only I do not know whether you could tear yourself

away from your dearly beloved dog and gun.”



“and you be willing to have go without I?”



“I do not wish to be any tie upon you,” be Alice’s reply, though she

colour slightly, and turn away her head as she speak. “you remember

our compact; I be a great advocate for free will.”



“between husband and wife such a question ought never to arise,”

 rejoin Harry, seriously but kindly; “there should be complete

unanimity. I hope you have forget all that folly.”



“I never forget unkindness,” be the cold reply; “but I see you be

go to favour I with a speciman of your ‘quiet manner,’ and as I be

not in the humour for a scene or a lecture, you really must excuse my

leave you;” and as she speak she rise to quit the apartment.



for a moment Harry’s eye flash, then a look of pain pass across his

feature, and, take his wife’s hand, he lead she back to the sofa on

which she have be seat, say gently, but reproachfully,--



“why will you misunderstand I thus? you wish to accept your cousin’s

invitation?”



Alice bow her head in token of assent.



“then write and tell she we shall be happy to do so; I shall be ready

and willing to accompany you at whatever time you and she like to

arrange together.”



“oh, that be very nice and kind of you!” return Alice, delight at

get her way so easily; “I think you be go to be cross and

disagreeable, as--as you sometimes be.”



“as usual, you be go to say,” rejoin Harry; “speak your thought

honestly, whatever injustice they may do I. but if, in future, instead

of condemn I unheard, you be to admit the possibility--nothing

more--of my be willing occasionally to sacrifice my wish to your,

it might save we both considerable pain and misconception; recollect

this, and reflect upon it quietly and calmly.” so say, he place his

wife’s writing-table before she, find she a footstool, and leave the

room.



as the sound of his retreating footstep die away in the distance,

Alice feel decidedly penitent, and wish she could unsay all the sharp

thing she have utter at the beginning of the conversation; but this

be a frame of mind too uncomfortable to last long, and so she console

herself by the reflection that if, on this particular occasion, she have

do her husband an injustice, it be his conduct at other time which

have lead she to do so. it be unfair to blame herself for the natural

effect his selfishness and unkindness have produce upon her mind; she

be sure there have be a period, before she be so rudely awaken from

her “love’s young dream,” when she have give he credit for possess

every noble, heroic, and tender quality under the sun: it be not

her fault that she could think so no long--people must take the

consequence of their own misdeed. and so, console herself with these

and many like argument, and magnify the mote in her husband’s eye,

and ignore the beam in her own, Alice talk herself into her

former frame of mind, and sit down to write her acceptance of Kate’s

invitation, convince that if her husband have say “yes” on this

occasion, he would say “no” on every other.









CHAPTER XXX.--INTRODUCES a lordly GALLANT.



that day week see Alice, Harry, and Celeste (a little pert _soubrette_,

whom Alice have bring back from Paris with she), on their way to the

railway-station at H--------; a groom and a couple of saddle-horse

(without which Harry could not support the burden of a London life)

having precede they by a slow train. as Harry have a great horror

of be too late, and have flurry and bustle Alice to such a degree

that, if she have not be the most good-natured little woman in the

world (except in matter connect with the feeling), she would

assuredly have lose her temper, of course they be at least a quarter

of an hour too soon, and be force to promenade up and down beneath a

brobdignagian glass roof, open at each end, and enjoy the large supply

of draught afford by this ingenious compromise between indoor and

out of door. having pace up and down the platform for some ten minute

or so--lose Celeste and the trunk, and find they again--and narrowly

escape violent death from wild luggage-barrow, urge by reckless and

excited porter, neatly bind in green corduroy, and number like

the lot in a saleroom,--the train by which they be to fly to London

crawl up ignominiously at the tail of a strong-minded cart-horse,

which a heroic but unclean supernumary conduct in the way he should

go. just as Alice have take her seat, and be implore Harry to join

she before a dreadful green dragon of a locomotive engine (which have

be get up its steam, and take in its fuel, and wet its

whistle, and otherwise perform its awful toilet in a neighbouring

cavern, whence it issue look as vicious, and dangerous, and eager to

burst in a tunnel, as a furious steam-devil could do) should get at

he and do he a mischief, a tall, elegant-look young man, who be

seek for an unoccupied place, suddenly exclaimed--



“I beg pardon, but surely I have the pleasure of see Harry--a--that

be--Mr. Coverdale?”



“a true bill, sir,” reply Harry; “but just at present you’ve all

the pleasure to yourself, for I must honestly confess that I do

not recollect you; and yet--no--yes--why, it cann’t be little Alfred

Courtland?”



“as for the ‘little,’ I must leave you to judge for yourself; the

copy-book tell we that ‘all weed grow apace,’ and I’m afraid I’m

a shocking example; but Alfred Courtland I most certainly be, and

delight to meet an old acquaintance--if an urchin in the under-school

dare pretend to have be on such a footing with one of the sixth form.”



“Little Alfred Courtland, six foot high, and cultivate whisker!

wonder will never cease,” resume Harry, meditatively: “but be you

go by this train? jump in here, man, and I’ll introduce you to my

wife. Alice, this be Alfred--I beg his pardon, but I cann’t remember

he’s not a little boy still--_Lord_ Alfred Courtland. you remember Arthur

Hazlehurst, my _fidus Achates_, don’t you, Courtland? my wife be his

sister. ticket! well, here they be. what a suspicious generation these

railway official be! anybody would suppose they have be accustom

to deal with thief and pickpocket all their life, instead of honest

englishman. but I hate the railroad, root and branch, that’s a fact;

they’ve ruin the breed of horse in this country.”



while Harry run on in this style, Alice have time to observe her new

acquaintance more attentively. he appear very young, scarcely above

nineteen or twenty. his figure, though tall and graceful, be slight

and boyish; his head be small and well set on, and his pale, delicate

feature be shade by a profusion of fair curl hair; while his

bearing and appearance be singularly refined and aristocratic; or, as

Harry afterwards observe, “he look thorough breed, every inch of

he.” his expression be good and amiable; but a want of firmness and

resolution about the line of his mouth belie the promise of intellect

afford by his high, smooth brow, and bright, speak eye.



“and what be you do with yourself?” inquire Coverdale, after sundry

mutual acquaintance have be talk over, and the reminiscence

usual between old schoolfellow run through; “be you at either of the

university?”



“yes, I’m a Cantab,” be the reply; “but scarcely more than nominally

so, for during my first term I get a tumble into the cam, boat--dine

at Ely in my wet clothe, and be reward for my carelessness by an

aguish low fever, which I be only now recover from; so I be order

to be perfectly idle and amuse myself--a prescription which I be afraid

agree but too well with my taste and habit.”



“and find country ingredient too mild, you be go to town to try

and get a strong dose there, I suppose?” inquire Harry.



“you must be a wizard,” be the reply. “the fact be, my people have

winter abroad, and chiselborough become so dull the moment the hunting

be over, that I find _ennui_ be bring my ague back again; so

hold solemn conclave with the apothecary and my valet, we yesterday

decide, _nem. con_., upon a couple of month’ sojourn in the modern

Babylon.”



to this piece of intelligence Harry vouchsafe no further answer than

a shrug of the shoulder, by which significant gesture he intend to

telegraph to his wife his opinion as to the wisdom of trust the young

gentleman to his own sapient guidance amidst the shoal and quicksand

of a London season. at this period the dragon, which have be draw

the train very quietly and peacefully, suddenly give a prolonged scream

(by courtesy term a whistle), pant violently, hiss a good deal,

and have by these manœuvre “blow off” its superfluous steam, it

kindly postpone burst for a short time, and condescend obligingly

to stop at the Tearem and Smashingly Junction, without demand any

immediate sacrifice of human life. Coverdale and Lord Alfred instantly

jump out (although perfectly aware that they should be oblige to jump

in again at the expiration of three minute and a quarter), and,

after the fashion of impatient male humanity, which, as Harry somewhat

paradoxically observe, “cannot stand sit,” begin stamp up and

down the platform as though a legion of black-beetle, or some such

entymological freebooter, have creep up their trouser’ leg, and they

be strive to dislodge they. some operation, however, which be go

on under one of those queer kind of shed peculiar to railway-station,

which give one an idea of a child’s toy magnify, attract their

attention, and cause they to discontinue their amusement. after gaze

earnestly for a few second, Harry exclaimed,--



“they’ll never do it so, never! there, do you see, he’s stand right

before he, drag at his head, and yet expect the poor animal to

go on; the man must be an idiot! yes, of course, hit the poor thing for

your own fault, and frighten he, so that you’ll be able to do nothing

with he. ah! I think so; they’ll have an accident directly, the

fool! as if there ben’t a quiet manner of do these thing. hold

my great coat, Alfred; I shall be back in two second.” and suit the

action to the word, he toss his coat to his companion and run off.



“where _have_ he go to?” inquire Alice, disconsolately, from the

window of the railway-carriage.



“to assist a stupid groom to put a very fine horse into one of the

horse-box,” be the reply. “he say he should be back in a minute.”



“now, gentleman, take your place; the train’s go to start--take your

place,” vociferate an individual, who look like a very oddly-dress

soldier, but who be the railway-guard.



“oh! where can he be? we shall start without he!” exclaim Alice in

dismay.



“I’ll go and look for he,” rejoin Lord Alfred, good-naturedly.



“if you would be so very kind,” return Alice, her lovely eye

sparkle with gratitude.



“well not, sir; only lose your own place, without find the

gent--train’s agoin’ to start. I must shut the door,” grumble a cynical

porter.



“pray keep it open till the last moment!” exclaim Alice, draw out

her purse, while Lord Alfred, disregard the porter’s advice, dash

off on his mission.



“be I allow to give you anything?” continue Alice, timidly, as a

vague suspicion of the illegality of bribe railway porter flash

across she.



the man look up and down the platform, and perceive no informer

near, do not commit himself by word, but partially close the door,

so as to conceal the action, hold out his hand, with the palm turn

suggestively upwards. as his finger close over the half-crown which

Alice, with a strong idea that she be commit an indictable offence,

place within his grasp, an angry and imperative voice call out, “now

then, shut that door there!” and in spite of Alice’s remonstrance, the

porter be about to obey, when, breathless with run, Lord Alfred

spring into the carriage, the door be slam to, a bell ring

furiously, the dragon give a short, pert scream of delight at get

its head, and the train start. unheede, in fact scarcely hear

Lord Alfred’s mild remonstrance that he believe it be reckon

dangerous to put one’s head out of the window of a railway carriage,

Alice immediately commit that folly, and be reward for her

imprudence by see, just as the train be get to its full speed,

Harry rush distractedly on to the platform, shake his fist at the

retreating carriage, and then, watch in hand, stride up to the

station-master, and evidently afford he a speciman of his quiet

manner. with a feeling half way between an inclination to laugh and

a disposition to cry, Alice resume her seat, and, under pretence

of arrange her veil, take a glance round the carriage. her only

companion, besides Lord Alfred Courtland, be a species of prize old

gentleman, who having spend his life hitherto in grow as fat as the

nature of the case admit, be evidently resolve to guard against the

possibility of his shadow become less, by devote the remainder of

his existence to the duty of eating, drinking, and sleeping, which

latter accomplishment he be then display to the admiration of all

lover of that science of which honest Sancho Panza so fervently bless

the inventor. having mentally sum he up in the definition “wretche

old thing,” Alice next take a survey of her new friend, and decide that

he have such a good, innocent, child-like expression of countenance,

that young and handsome as he be, she would not have mind even if the

“wretched old thing” have not be present to play _chaperone_ in dumb

show.



“how very provoking for Mr. Coverdale to lose the train, and all through

his good-nature, too,” begin Lord Alfred; “I see the affair as well as

he do, but it would never have occur to I to interfere.”



“nor to any one else except Mr. Coverdale,” return Alice, scornfully;

“his devotion to horse and dog be quite exemplary.”



“as a pattern or as a warning?” inquire Lord Alfred, favour she with

a look of intelligence for which she be scarcely prepare.



“you be laugh at I,” she say; “but I will honestly confess that

it be rather try to see Mr. Coverdale place himself and I in a

ridiculous, if not actually an embarrassing situation, merely for the

sake of a horse.”



“it be a very _fine_ horse,” observe Lord Alfred, meditatively.



“and therefore the worthy animal of the two--thank you for the

compliment, my lord,” be the slightly pique reply, which of course

produce a carefully veil but teasing rejoinder; and with such-like

light _badinage_ do they beguile the time, until having rush for

some distance over acre of turnip, stubble, grass-land, and other such

agricultural territory, change as by some pantomimic agency to the

roof of house, with elegant _parterre_ of chimney-pot, they be

surprised to find they have reach the London terminus.



the cessation of movement having rouse the prize elder from his

meritorious slumber, Alice wait until, with many snort and grunt he

have arouse his leg (which be evidently each enjoy a separate and

independent nap of its own) and toddle off upon they, ere she inquire

in rather a forlorn tone, “and now I wonder what be to become of I?

would you kindly ascertain when the next train will be in?”



Lord Alfred make the inquiry, and obtain the cheer intelligence

that the next train which stop at the Tearem and Smashingly Junction

would arrive in exactly two hour fifteen minute and a quarter, at

which time, as nearly as Alice could calculate, the Crane butler would

be remove the fish and soup.



“it be impossible that you can wait here all that time, my dear Mrs.

Coverdale!” exclaim Lord Alfred. “what will you like I to do for

you? you must tell I exactly what you wish.”



“you be very kind,” return Alice, feel much incline to get into a

fuss at the oddness of the situation which thus force she to rely on

a handsome young man, with whom she have be acquaint some two

hour. then submit to her fate with a feeling of desperation,

she continue, “first give I your arm, and conduct I to the lady’

waiting-room; and then if you would be so kind as to look for Celeste,

my maid, and--really I be ashamed to trouble you, my lord, but there

be some trunk she ought to find, and she cann’t speak a dozen word

of English intelligibly; and--how you’re to recognize she I cann’t tell;

really how Mr. Coverdale could----”



but before she could finish her accusatory sentence, Lord Alfred,

anxious to distinguish himself in his new capacity of squire of dame,

have disappear. in less time than Alice have deem possible, he

return with Celeste and a bundle of shawl and wrapper on one arm,

and carry a carpet-bag with the other.



“my mission have be accomplish with the most signal success, I

flatter myself: and now I hope your difficulty be end, my dear Mrs.

Coverdale; Celeste and I have find _all_ the trunk. fortunately, my

brougham be here, and I need scarcely add, entirely at your service.”

 see she hesitate, he continue, “don’t be alarmed about the

propriety, I have be too well drill in such matter by my sister

to intrude where I be not want.”



“really your lordship be most kind,” exclaim Alice, all her scruple

vanish before his good-nature and consideration. and there be

nothing for it but to take his arm (relinquish somewhat hastily by

Celeste when she discover that it be a _Milor Anglais_ with whom

she have make so free) and allow he to put she into the well-appoint

brougham, Alice do so with an interesting succession of smile and

blush which make she look most dangerously pretty. thereupon the two

hundred guinea horse, which be so thoroughly stuff with oat that it

might almost as well have be a corn-bin, and which, be an animal of

the high breeding, have evince such an amount of disgust and terror

at the hiss, snort, whistling, and other low habit of the steam

dragon, that nothing but the strong sense of propriety and a very

severe curb-bit could have keep it from run away, stand on its hind

leg like a Christian, vindicate its transcendentalism by salaam

like a Turk ere it resume its quadrupedal attitude, and finally set

off, at about the rate of fifteen mile an hour, with its head and tail

as erect as if some invisible giant be attempt to lift it up by

they.









CHAPTER XXXI.--SPIDERS and fly.



“my dear Kate, I think your cousin, Mrs. Coverdale, have just drive

up; and yet I don’t know. be it likely, or, as I may say, probable, that

she should arrive in a brougham?”



“with a high-step horse, and a coronet on the panels?--scarcely, I

should imagine.”



the speaker be Mr. Crane, who have grow rather less like a

scaffold-pole since we last be favour with his society, and Horace

D’Almayne, who appear quite himself and quite at home. attract by

their remark, Kate join her husband at the window.



“it cann’t be they,” she say, “there be no luggage;” but, as if to

contradict her remark, at the moment she cease to speak a cab

dash into Park Lane with a _fair_ amount of imperial, cap-case,

portmanteau, carpet-bag, and other female travel _miscellania_,

and draw up behind the brougham. as it stop, a tall, handsome young

man spring out, and open the door of the brougham, offer his arm to

Alice, and conduct she up the step most carefully.



“why, that surely cannot be Mr. Coverdale; or, at least, if I may

be permit to say so, he have become singularly thin and--and

youthful-look, if it be,” bleat Mr. Crane.



“no, that be not Harry Coverdale,” return Kate, wonderingly, “nor do I

see anything of he either!”



“if Mrs. Coverdale have lose her husband, really she have find a most

attractive substitute--a--it almost seem one of the case in which such

a loss might be consider a gain,” lisp D’Almayne, in so low a

tone that Mr. Crane, who be nearly as slow of hear as he be of

understanding, do not catch the remark. “really quite a touching

farewell,” he continue, as Alice, ere she enter the house, shake

hand most cordially with her young cavalier; “and the gallant, gay

Lothario jump into the brougham (which, coronet, high-step horse,

and all, evidently call he master) and be lose to our admire gaze.”



at this juncture a fat and rosy butler (who look as if he have be

bring up by hand upon port wine, and have remain faithful to it ever

since) fling open the door, and announce Mrs. Coverdale.



throw off, for once in her life, all coldness and reserve, Kate

embrace her cousin warmly, and, hold she by both hand, lead she to

the sofa.



“my dear child,” she exclaim, “how delightful it be to see you once

again!”



“but if I may be permit,” begin Mr. Crane, “if I may be allow to

inquire, what have you do with--or perhaps I should rather say--what

have become of our good friend, Mr. Coverdale?”



“and how come you in a brougham with a coronet upon it? and who be

that handsome and distinguish-looking young exquisite whom you have

inveigle into play courier--eh, Mistress Alice?” inquire Kate,

archly. “I expect to find you a pattern wife, and to have your example

hold up for my imitation twenty time a day; but I have alarm myself

very unnecessarily, it seem.”



“don’t tease, dear,” be the reply; “it be all the fault of that silly

husband of mine: he get out at one of the station, and seduce by the

attraction of a restive horse, contrive to be out of the way when the

train start, and so I be force to do the good I could for myself.”



“which theory you reduce to practice by select the handsome young

man you could find as a _cavalier servente_,” return Kate, laugh.

“but who be your friend? I hope he be come to call upon you!”



“oh, yes, he mean to call--to-morrow I think he say. I’m glad you

consider he handsome: it’s always satisfactory to have one’s taste

approve of by one’s friend; and I honestly confess I admire he

particularly.”



Mr. Crane’s countenance, during this speech of Alice’s, be wonderful to

behold; the intense surprise with which he listen to the beginning of

it gradually change to the deep disgust as she continue, afford

such a clear index to his thought that Horace D’Almayne turn away

to hide an irrepressible smile, which Kate perceiving, observe with a

slight shade of annoyance.--



“and now, having mystify we thoroughly, be kind enough to tell we who

the gentleman really be, and how he come to offer you his brougham and

his service.”



thus appeal to, Alice be oblige to confess that, in point of fact,

there be nothing wrong or romantic in the adventure from begin

to end--that Lord Alfred Courtland be an old schoolfellow of her

husband’s, who have travel in the same carriage with they, and who have

naturally do all he could to save she from be inconvenience by the

effect of Harry’s stupidity, on which she dwelt rather more at length

than Kate approve of,--that young lady have a very keen perception of

right and wrong, although she by no mean always act up to the light

thus afford she.



some few hour later Harry arrive, very anxious about his wife, and

decidedly crest-fall and penitent, and bear all the quizzing which

fall to his share with most exemplary patience; although any attempt

to excite his jealousy in regard to Lord Alfred Courtland prove a dead

failure, his reply be that “he be always a very good little boy, and

that he do not see much difference in he except in height.”



when the Coverdales go up to dress for dinner the follow dialogue

ensued:--



“how well your cousin Kate be look,” observe Harry; “the pomp and

vanity of this wicked world appear to agree with she; now she have

grow a little stouter, she really be a splendid woman.”



“yes, she appear in well health,” return Alice, slowly, “but--”



“but what?” inquire Harry. “a woman’s ‘but’ be like the postscript

to her letter; it unsay all she have say before. come, out with this

_arrière pensée_, as that puppy D’Almayne would call it. by-the-way, he

seems regularly domesticate here. I wonder old Crane like it; I should

not, in his position, I know.”



“I wonder Kate like it,” return Alice; “however, my ‘but’ have nothing

to do with the fascinating horace. I be go to say that although Kate

look well, yet she have a listless, weary expression of countenance,

which give I the idea that, with all her rich and splendour, she be

far from happy.”



“the same be a result rather to be expect than otherwise, when

a lovely and talented young female see fit to espouse an elderly and

feeble-minded old scarecrow,” rejoin Harry, make frantic dive

into his portmanteau, and fish up patent bootjack, miraculous

razor-strop--everything but the dress-neck-tie he be in search of.



“I don’t believe they see anything of Arthur,” continue Alice,

reflectively; “I ask Kate, and she seem to know nothing about

he--such friend as they use to be at one time--it’s very odd!”



“I don’t see the oddness, myself,” return Harry, speak through

his dressing-room door, which stand ajar; “there be a great difference

between feel spooney about a pretty cousin, when you’re live in the

house with she, and have nothing well to do, and dangle after she

to the neglect of your business, when she live at one end of London and

you at the other--when, moreover, she’s married to a dreadful old muff,

antiquate enough to be her father, and slow enough to be the father of

every fool in the kingdom. I think it’s easily account for by

prose mean, without adopt the poetical hypothesis of a romantic

attachment--two fond young heart blight, and all that ‘keepsake’

style of business; besides, Arthur’s a great deal too good a lawyer

to fall in love; it’s only idle fellow like myself who commit such

folly.”



“you must go and call on Arthur to-morrow, and you will soon perceive

by his manner whether he be averse to come here; but mind you be very

careful not to let he see that you suspect anything; I be quite sure he

would be most sensitive on such a point,” observed Alice, in a tone in

which you would caution a schoolboy against play with gunpowder.



“keep your advice for you own benefit, most sententious Alice, see

that you be the suspecting party, and that such an idea would never

have occur to my unassisted reason,” be Harry’s rejoinder; and the

dinner-bell at that moment ringing, the conversation cease.



the next day, however, Arthur put an end to the controversy by make

his appearance in Park Lane soon after luncheon. although no one allude

to the circumstance, it be the first time he have set his foot in Mr.

Crane’s house, or indeed see Kate since her marriage. he look pale

and over-work, and there be a restless excitement in his manner,

which Alice’s quick eye at once discover. beyond this, however, there

be nothing which tend in the slight degree to confirm she in her

suspicion. he apologise quietly and naturally to Kate for not having

call oftener, adduce business as a good and sufficient reason for

his remissness; then, turn to Alice, he inform she that she could

not have choose a more unfortunate time for her visit to London, at

least, as far as he be concern, as he be oblige to start the next

morning for Naples, be send out by the Foreign-Office on an affair

of some importance, which, if he could bring the matter to a successful

issue, might tend to his ultimate advancement. Kate, on the contrary,

appear nervous and ill at ease, and probably feel that for once

she could not rely on her self-command, take an early opportunity of

quit the room, leave the brother and sister _tête-à-tête_.
