“Alice, you be change,” exclaim Arthur, as the door close on she

whom he have once so deeply love, towards whom he now feel as we can

only feel towards those whom we have admit into the inmost recess

of the heart, and who have avail themselves of the privilege to

profane and make desolate the sanctuary, “you be a girl, you have

become a woman; have matrimony produce the alteration?”



“yes, I suppose so,” be the rejoinder. “you know one cann’t remain a

child always; the reality of life be sure to find one out soon or

later, and I be a mere baby in the way of the world when I marry.”



there be a spice of regret in the tone of this remark, which do not

escape Arthur’s quick ear and keen intelligence, and he hasten to

reply--



“you mean more than you say; why, surely, Alice, with such a husband you

must be perfectly happy; it be impossible that it can be otherwise.”



as he speak, he fix his dark eye questioningly upon she. unable

fairly to meet his gaze, Alice turn away her head, as she reply,

with an effort at careless gaiety--



“don’t alarm yourself, most romantic of barrister; there be no

Bluebeard’s closet at Coverdale, nor do Harry turn into a skeleton,

or anything else but his bed, at twelve o’clock at night. he be just the

thoroughly good fellow (that be the term you man delight in) he always

be, and devote to----



“his wife!” interrupt Arthur.



“well, I be go to say dog, gun, and horse,” return

Alice; “and I’m afraid I must adhere to my text, unless you prefer

fiction to fact.”



she speak jestingly; but the line which care, and think, and

intellectual exertion have already trace on Arthur’s brow deepen, as,

after a pause, he murmur, half in reply to Alice, half in soliloquy--



“I be disappointed, deeply disappointed; it ought to be so different!

I--I wish I be not go abroad to-morrow; and yet I _could_ not be a

frequent visitor in this house!”



the last word be inaudible, though, by one of those intuition which

often compensate for the inefficiency of our physical power, Alice

divine his train of reasoning, and with subtle generalship divert the

attack, by carry the war into the enemy’s country, as she replied--



“do not puzzle your brain about I and Harry; we jog on very serenely

together, now we have find out each other’s peculiarity.”



“but you never _have_ any peculiarity, either of you,” interrupt

Arthur, positively; “except that Harry be the fine, noblest, manly

fellow going, and you be a good, simple-hearte, sweet-temper little

girl. what do you mean by peculiarity?”



“never mind we,” continue Alice, not heed his interruption; “I want

to know something about you. you say I have change from a child into

a woman, but you have turn from a young man into a middle-aged one

during these last six month; you be either ill or unhappy, or work

yourself to death--all three, perhaps.”



“oh, you be fanciful, and not use to the pale face of we Londoners,”

 return Arthur.



“you cannot put I off in that manner,” continue Alice, pertinaciously;

“people do not look ill and careworn without some cause for do so.

how be it, pray, that you never come here? so fond as you use to be of

Kate, too! I expect to find you regularly instal as _l’enfant de

famille_. do you know I begin to have my suspicions----”



“hush!” interrupt Arthur, in a low, stem voice; “whatever you may

suspect, never refer to this subject again, there be some sorrow in

life for which there be _no_ remedy; these must be endure and struggle

with in silence, for so only _can_ they be bear. if you would not give

I pain, forget that this idea ever occur to you.”



as he speak his pale face flushed, and his lip quiver with the emotion

he strove, but be unable entirely to conceal.



“forgive I, dear Arthur!” exclaim Alice, whilst tear of ready

sympathy glisten in her eye; “I speak carelessly--foolishly: indeed,

indeed, I do not mean to give you pain! but you be not angry with I?”



as she speak she lay her hand caressingly on his shoulder and glance

up in his face with a beseech look, which would have melt the most

flinty-hearted stoic. Arthur draw she to he, and kiss her smooth

brow, in token of forgiveness, ere he replied--



“before we quit this subject, never to resume it, let I say this much

to you: in this matter I have nothing to reproach myself with; as far as

I have be able to see what be right, I have act up to it. this be

my only comfort. that I have suffer much, I will not attempt to deny;

but I be thankful to say the blow, though severe, have not paralyse I.

the sunshine of my life may be destroy for year, perhaps for ever,

but my vigour and energy be leave I, and I will yet make myself a name,

and win myself a position that the mere possession of wealth can never

bestow. now, forget that this conversation ever take place.”



as he speak the door fly open, and Harry and Lord Alfred Courtland,

having encounter each other at the club, make their appearance

arm-in-arm, like a pair of well-grow siamese twin, and Alice be

dispatch all in a hurry to put on her “thing,” to be take to a

private view of the annual exhibition of the Society of Amalgamated

amateur in Water-colour, whom Harry irreverently paraphrase as the

“Amalgamated Muffs;” a definition the truth of which a close inspection

of the effort of those mild and amiable caricaturist do not tend to

disprove. as they stroll up and down the room, wait for Kate and

Mr. Crane, who have promise to join they, Lord Alfred,--on whose arm

Alice be lean, and who have be rattle on with great volubility,

and in the high possible spirits,--suddenly observed--



“I do find myself such a complete country cousin in London, that really

it’s quite ridiculous! I meet all sort of celebrity, and don’t know

one of they by sight. now, for instance, do you see that pair of young

exquisite lounge elegantly along, like a couple of self-enamour

sleep-walker, and dress like beatific vision of dandy, rather

than mere sublunary fop? I’m sure I’ve meet the young of they

somewhere--he with the _petite moustache noire_, which be so

irresistible that I should certainly cultivate a pair myself, if I do

not feel morally certain that my prejudiced progenitor would cut they,

and I, off with the same shilling.”



“in fact, cut off his _h_eir because you would not cut off your,”

 pun Coverdale. “but in regard to your beatific swell, I fancy Alice

can enlighten you as to the patronymic of one of they, if she choose;

he be a very particular friend, to say nothing more, of her. she only

marry I because she fail in captivate he.”



Alice reply to Lord Alfred’s expressive look, which ask as plainly

as word could have do, “be this _all_ jest, or be there a small

foundation of fact for it to rest upon?”--“If that have be my only

reason for accept my romancing husband, I should have remain Miss

Hazlehurst still; however, I plead guilty to know Mr. D’Almayne,

as he happen to be an intimate friend of Mr. Crane, the gentleman who

marry my cousin Kate, and in whose house we be now stay.”



while they thus chat, the follow conversation be be carry

on in French between the subject of their remark and his companion, a

showily-dress man, some half-dozen year old than Horace D’Almayne,

with handsome feature, but a worn, dissipate look, which involuntarily

prejudice one against he. he speak with a thoroughly foreign accent,

and the animate gesture with which he seek to elucidate his meaning

also tend to prove he be not a native of this country.



“the plan have be work out,” he continue, refer to some subject

with which D’Almayne appear acquaint, “and with _his_ name as

director, and £1000 ready money to pay clerk, and establish the concern

on a respectable foundation, the affair will go charmingly; John Bull

shall buy our share and hand we his money, and in six month’ time,

with that and”--here he sink his voice--“the club in J-------- Street,

we may set fortune at defiance.”



“mind you be careful about keep our connection with the club

secret,” return D’Almayne, almost in a whisper; “we be not in Paris,

remember; and the slight suspicion that we play, would be fatal to

your hope of induce man of capital to join the other affair.”



“do not fear, _mon cher_; I know my game,” be the reply. as he speak,

his eye fall upon the Coverdale party, and hastily indicate Lord

Alfred Courtland to his companion, he continue, “do you see that

striple? he be point out to I last night as a pigeon worth

plucking, and easily handle; he be a young _milor_, very soft, and what

you call ‘green.’ you must get introduce, and bring he to ‘the club.’”



“the boy be not of age yet,” return D’Almayne, “and english father

never pay gambling debt; so you must not hope for large gain from

he.”



“he can sign bill and post-obit I presume,” rejoin his companion,

with a sneer laugh; “but the people he be with be regard you as

if they be of your acquaintance--be it so?”



“decidedly,” be the reply. “I will effect the introduction you desire

at once, but as soon as it be over you must find an opportunity of

withdraw; I will join the party, feel my way cautiously, and you

shall see Milor Courtland’s childish face in J-------- Street before a

fortnight have pass. _Allons, mon cher._”



having offer two finger to Coverdale, and three to his wife,

D’Almayne glance towards Lord Alfred with a supercilious look, which

seem to express, “I perceive you, but on account of your extreme youth

and inexperience, be wholly indifferent to the fact of your existence;”

 at least so his lordship interpret it, and be immediately seize with

an eager desire to know the man who could thus afford to look down on

he.



“introduce I to your friend, will you, Coverdale?” he say; “I must

get he to give I a few lesson in dress and deportment; he really be a

second Brummell.”



“he really be a conceited, empty-head puppy,” return Coverdale,

_sotto voce_, “and it’s little good you’ll learn of a jackanape like

that; but I suppose if I don’t introduce you, somebody else would--so

come along.” then place his hand on his shoulder, and urge he

forward, he continued--“D’Almayne, here’s my friend, Lord Alfred

Courtland, wish to be introduce to you: he think it his duty to know

every well-dress man in London, and you’re so _facile princep_

in that line--so transcendently get up--that he’s die to ask your

tailor’s address, and the length of tick he allow.”



“you’re so oblige as to laugh at I, Mr. Coverdale, because I cannot

reconcile myself to your English Schneiders, and still patronise Blin

_et Fils_, in that paradise of tailor, Paris; but--ar--really you be

uncivilised in this particular, and require reform in your coat more

than in your constitution, which, glorious as you consider it, you be

always alter. do not Lord Alfred Courtland agree with I?” and as

he make this appeal, Horace D’Almayne simper, to show his white tooth,

stroke his moustache, and await a reply.



Ere Lord Alfred have find word to imply his admiration of Horace’s

taste, without pay he an actual broad and un-mistakeable compliment,

Harry put his idea to flight, by exclaiming--



“listen to a word of common sense, Alfred, my boy. man make coat--if

you can properly call a tailor a man--but coat can never make man. you

may dress an ass up in the grand lion-skin going, but you can make

nothing of he but an ass, nevertheless. in fact, I never believe a

man’s a man till I’ve see he with his coat off; then if he can use his

fist as a man should, I believe in he.”



“aha! I comprehend; ce monsieur refer to your english science of the

box. very clever science be the box; I be acquire he of a professeur,

who keep a restaurant, what you call a public-house, in Smissfiel.”



as D’Almayne’s companion thus speak, Horace seize the opportunity of

introduce he, which he do as follows:--



“allow I to make you acquainted with my friend, Monsieur Adolphe

Guillemard, a gentleman connect with the financial interest in

Paris, and with that of Europe generally.” then, in a stage whisper, he

added--“He be educate in Rothschild’s house.”



so Harry bow, and Lord Alfred bow, and Alice incline her head

in rather a stately manner, because she do not approve of Monsieur

Guillemard’s rove eye; and Monsieur Guillemard bow and scrape, and

lay his hand on his waistcoat, where his heart ought to have be, and

abase his unappreciated optic, and appear profoundly touch, and

anxious to weep on the bosom of society at large; and Mr. Crane, who

at that moment come up in his wife’s custody, not make allowance for

foreign manner, think he be in a fit. then Monsieur Guillemard draw

out his watch, and find he have an engagement at the Bourse, as he

be pleased to call the Stock Exchange; and so take leave of his new

acquaintance, squeeze both the yellow kid hand of his _cher_ Horace,

and with short, jaunty footstep, as of a male ballet-dancer, quit

the spacious gallery, sacred to the noble effort of the Amalgamated

amateur. and when he have depart, of course his friend begin to talk

he over. D’Almayne draw Mr. Crane aside, and relate to he wonderful

anecdote of his (Guillemard’s) skill in foresee political event

and their consequence, and the splendid hit he have thus make in

stock-jobbing for himself, and other who have wisely avail themselves

of his talent, and what Baron Rothschild have say and thought of he;

until Mr. Crane begin to imagine he an incarnation of Mammon, and

yearn to fall down and adore he on the spot. for, be it observe,

parenthetically, that Mr. Crane, albeit nominally a member of the

Established Church, be verily and indeed a worshipper of a certain

golden calf, to whose likeness he have for year strive earnestly,

and not unsuccessfully, to assimilate himself. and Harry remark

confidentially to Alice, Kate, and Lord Alfred, that he be prepared to

bet a pony that Guillemard be neither more nor less than a “leg,” and

that whoever have many dealing with he would be safe to put his or her

foot in it--which sentence sound like nonsense, but be only slang.

and Lord Alfred laugh, and reply that Harry say so because he be

jealous of the superior cut of Monsieur Guillemard’s garment. Alice

agree perfectly with her husband, which, Kate remark, be the most

original feature of the whole affair--an observation intend for a mild

and playful jest, but at which Alice blush, and Harry suddenly became

engross by a spirited sketch, in very water-colour, of Ophelia as she

appear when drown, which, accord to the talented representation

of Miss Appela Brown, H.S.A.A., be remarkably jolly, and slightly

inebriate--next to which hang a portrait of Miss Brown herself, seat

at her easel, her pre-raphaelite countenance beam with mingled talent

and astonishment on the picture grow beneath her gifted brush--a

compound expression, at which, as the subject be some demi-god, or

other mythical celebrity, in heroic muscular proportion strongly

develop, and nothing else, we can scarcely feel surprise. then the

whole party devote their serious attention to the performance of

the amalgamate one, and be reward by behold many fearful and

wonderful thing. there be “young gentleman take from life,” and

transport by amalgamate magic into the region of romance--an

_un_likeness of Snook’s ruddy face be affix to Hamlet’s velvet

body, or Mary Ann Jones’s very _retroussé_ profile head Joan of Arc’s

steel bodice, and a select squadron of twelve french soldier in green

hunting-coat and fancy hat and feather, prepare to “_mourir pour la

patrie_” to any extent which the say Mary Ann might require of they.

then there be landscape with gamboge foreground, pasture comical

cow of shape and colour unknown to zoology; and middle distance,

gloomy with indigo tree, and cast-iron rivulet purl rigidly over

wild rock, suggest by bald place, show the naked paper through

a severe application of sepia and neutral tint. ferocious battle be

there also, design by gentle girl, who have never witness so much

as a street row, wherein gallant Henri Quatre-like party, with slim

waist, feminine complexion, and white wave plume, slaughter

strong man in funny dress, and prance over their dead body with the

most heroic magnanimity and indifference. then there be Mount Vesuvius

during an eruption, which, to judge by the colouring, must have be the

eruption attendant on scarlet fever; and Mont Blanc well iced, show

the _mer de glace_ (the most difficult mare to mount on record, as “we

know who” would say), and the last batch of proselyte from the Egyptian

Hall slide serenely down on their haunch, as wolf be _report_

to do, only the proselyte appear to have get the “advantage” of the

wolf, by reason of their coat-tail. scripture piece, too, have some

of these rash amateur perpetrate, wherein “daughter of Babylon”

 appear like the _corps de ballet_, and king, prophet, and

patriarch have evidently find their prototype in Mario, Lablache,

and Tamburini--a fact which afford Horace D’Almayne an opportunity of

observe that it be charming to perceive in England the amiability of

the Muses; as Apollo, the divinity of painting, instead of be drive

to rugged nature for material, or, bad still, compel to fall back

upon his own power of invention, be obligingly supply with they by

Melpomene and Thalia; which same he and Mr. Crane think a very smart

say--the former because he have make it himself, the latter because he

do not understand it.



as they stroll on through the gallery, Kate take an opportunity, when

Mr. Crane have relinquish her arm, in order to adjust his great-coat

more to his satisfaction, to lag behind a few pace, glance at

D’Almayne as she do so, who immediately join she.



“I have make the inquiry you wish,” he say in a low tone, “and I

be truly glad to be able to assure you your sympathy have fall on a

deserving object; the poor woman be as she represent herself--a widow,

with a family of young child depend upon she for support, and her

poverty be extreme.”



“many thank for take so much trouble,” return Kate, in a tone of

voice more cordial than she generally use towards her companion; “and

now tell I how good I can assist they.”



“I have a plan, but can scarcely give you the detail here; when would

it be agreeable to you to”--(here his eye rest for a moment on Mr.

Crane, contend with a button-hole)--“to resume the subject, and give

I your opinion on my scheme?”



Kate reflect a moment, during which she struggle with an instinctive

feeling, and deem it reasonless, conquer it, then replied--



“if you should be disengage at eleven o’clock to-morrow, and would look

in, I should be very much oblige to you.”



while this conversation pass between Kate and D’Almayne, they have

be themselves the subject of observation to a party of stranger,

who, come probably from the country, have not yet attune their voice

to the requirement of London sightseeing. accordingly, the follow

remark be distinctly audible to those for whom, of all other, they

be not intend.



“what a lovely young woman!” observe Mater Familias; “I suppose the

mustachioed gentleman be she _futur_.”



“she don’t look over love at he, if he be,” grumble Pater F.



“perhaps that be because her father (regard Mr. Crane) be so close,

and do not approve of the match,” suggest Sarah Jane, the eld

daughter, to Louisa Anne, her sub----



“_Au contraire_,” remark the intelligent London cousin, a clerk in

the Ignorance and Delay Office, who be popularly suppose to

know everything and everybody; “the old boy be a rich Manchester

cotton-spinner, and the young lady his wife; she marry he for his

tin, and half London be rave about her beauty.”



“poor thing!” mutter Mater Familias, who, for fifty-two, be

unusually romantic--“poor thing, how I pity she!”



while listen to these agreeable remark, D’Almayne have keep his eye

steadily fix upon an amalgamate catalogue, desirous not to add

to Kate’s embarrassment; but at length, surprised at her silence and

immobility, he venture to glance towards she, and be alarmed to

perceive that she have turn pale to her very lip, while she grasp

the brass rail, which be place to protect the picture, convulsively,

in order to save herself from fall. any one with less tact than

D’Almayne would, in officious eagerness to assist she, have make a fuss,

and cause she to become the subject of general attention; but Horace

know well how to turn the situation to account; hand she a chair,

he say quietly--



“the heat have make you feel faint; sit down for a moment, and perhaps

the feeling may pass off.”



as Kate hasten to follow his suggestion, she glance towards he,

to read in his feature whether he also have overhear the conversation

which have affect she. whether his subtle intellect have lead he to

divine her intention, and he be enact the character he consider

most likely to tell with Kate, or whether he be merely obey a

natural impulse, we do not attempt to decide; suffice it to state that,

when she look at he, he be scowl after the amiable family, whose

conversation have cause the embarrassment, with so angry an expression

of countenance, that a fear seize his companion lest he should be about

to do something indignant and foolish, which might attract attention

to she, and produce the scene she dread. a moment’s reflection on

his cautious, prudent character, would have prove to she the

unreasonableness of such a fear; but she speak without allow herself

this--



“what be you go to do?” she say, in a hurried whisper: “you _can_

take no notice of--of----;” and unable to find word to express she

meaning, she pause in confusion. D’Almayne finish her sentence for

her:--



“----Of those people’s ignorance of the usage of society? no, I be

not so inconsiderate; pardon I that I allow you to see my just

indignation, but for the moment I be completely carry away by

feeling. now,” he continue, “if you can make the effort, let we join

the other; no one have, as yet, observe your indisposition.”



by way of reply, Kate rise and take his proffered arm.



“get they away from this place,” she say, hurriedly; “I shall suffocate

if I remain here long.”



Horace bow assent, and after exchange a few indifferent remark with

Alice and Lord Alfred Courtland, turn to Mr. Crane, observing--



“will you forgive I for plead the cause of one of your new

carriage-horse? the coachman tell I it have a slight cough; and it

will scarcely tend to get rid of the ailment to wait too long in this

pierce east wind.”



“no, indeed,” cheruppe Mr. Crane; “and a horse that cost a hundred and

thirty pun (he mean pound!) must not be injure, even, if I may be

allow to say so, to please the lady.” and having speak, straightway

he fall into a fidget; so that, in less than two minute, the noble

production of the Amalgamated Amateurs become as a dream of the past to

our _dramatis personæ_.



on reach the street, with his wife hang on his arm, Mr. Crane, ere

he place she in the carriage, thus address his domestic--



“why, coachman, you never tell I one of the horse have a cough.”



as he speak, Kate, perfectly understand that the horse’s cough be an

invention of D’Almayne’s, to enable they to get away from the gallery in

accordance with her wish, involuntarily glance towards he. but

where manœuvring and _finesse_ be require, Horace be quite in his

element. catch the attention of the servant (whom he have himself

recommend) by a fictitious attack of the malady under which the

quadrupe be suppose to labour, he, by an almost imperceptible

contraction of the eyelid, telegraph his wish, ensure their

fulfilment by suggestively tap the silver head of his cane, to

express that in that metal should his compliance be reward; so Mr.

Crane be glibly inform that his horse have suffer under a bronchial

affection for about the space of four day, more or less; but that

he, the coachman, having apply an invaluable specific, know only to

himself, have not consider the matter sufficiently serious to trouble

his master withal;--for which reticence he bear meekly Mr. Crane’s

peevish rebuke, console by the expectation of five shilling the next

morning from Horace D’Almayne.



the polished boot of that good young man trod upon rose rather than

granite, as he amble down Pall Mall; for, by mean of those trifle

which make the sum of human thing, he have achieve a great and

almost unhope-for success--he have succeed in establish a private

understanding with the young and beautiful wife of the millionaire!









chapter XXXII.--A glimpse at the GREEN-EYED MONSTER.



having console himself by a canter in Rotten row, for the minor

martyrdom he have undergo in his pursuit of the fine-art, as

misrepresent by the Amalgamated Amateurs, Harry make the good of his

way to Park Lane. as he enter, a note be hand to he by the pompous

butler, who take the opportunity to inform he, in a voice husky with

the bee’s-wing, from which his throat be never entirely free, that

“dinner would be serve in a quarter of an hour.”--“Then I’ve no time to

lose,” be the reply, and without look at the note, Harry dash

up stair, three step at a time. on reach his room, however, and

find that Alice’s toilet be by no mean in an alarming state of

forwardness, he recover his composure, and open the note; it run as

follows:--



“on my arrival here two hour ago, I be surprised and embarrass by

hear that you and _your bride_ be stay in the house. have I be

aware of this fact, I need scarcely tell you I would have delay make

my appearance until your visit should have end. but, although I know

you have marry a connection of Mrs. Crane, such a probability never

occur to I. however, it be not likely that, mix in the same

grade of society, we should pass through life without ever again

encounter each other; and I be still weak enough to dread our first

meeting, and to wish it over. I know your generous nature, and feel

the utmost confidence that the past will remain a secret between we. it

will, perhaps, be well--easy for we both, not to pretend to meet

as stranger. an accidental travel acquaintance will sufficiently

account for our know the same place, people, &c. for your own sake,

as well as mine.



“I implore you to be careful--I have never forget your advice, and

have strive to act upon it--but mine be a rebellious nature. destroy

this note as soon as you have read it.



“Arabella.”



with stern compress lip and knitted brow, Harry peruse this

mysterious epistle, and when he have finish it, crush it in his hand

and throw it on the fire with a gesture of impatience.



“your letter do not seem to please you,” observe Alice; “do it come

from a dun, or be there a screw loose (don’t I get on with my slang!) in

the stable or the kennel?”



absorb in thought, Harry make she no reply, until, surprised and

slightly annoyed at his silence, she resumed--



“have the mysterious epistle stricken you dumb, or have we become so

thoroughly matrimonial, that you don’t consider it worth while to

answer your wife when she ask you a question?”



“eh! what? I beg your pardon, dear, the letter? no it be not from a

dun. I never be prey upon by those vampire, thank Heaven; ‘out of

debt, out of danger,’ have always be my motto,” reply Coverdale,

rouse from his reverie.



“if it be not from a dun, whom be it from then?” continue Alice,

pertinaciously.



“you be singularly curious all of a sudden,” rejoin Harry; “all

I shall tell you about the matter be that the note refer to a

disagreeable affair which happen three or four year ago, and which I

have hope be entirely pass and forget.”



“and having raise my curiosity thus, do you actually mean to say that

you will not gratify it far?” inquire Alice.



“as you can have no good reason for ask, and as I have a very good

and sufficient one for keep my own counsel, I be afraid I must leave

you in ignorance,” be Harry’s tantalizing reply.



Alice glance at his face, and read there that he be in earnest,

and mean to act on what he have say, pout like a spoilt child who have

be refuse some coveted plaything, while Coverdale betake himself to

his dressing-room in a “who-the-deuce-would-have-think-of-she-turn-up!”

frame of mind, from which he have by no mean recover when, with

his wife, still mildly vindictive, hang on his arm, he descend to

the drawing-room.



there they find Mr. and Mrs. Crane, and a lady whom Kate introduce as

her old and particular friend, Miss Crofton. having bow to Alice, Miss

Crofton turn towards Harry, observe to Kate, as she do so--



“I have never have the pleasure of meeting _Mrs_. coverdale before; but

Mr. Coverdale and I be old acquaintance; when I be travel in

Italy with the Muirs. Mr. Coverdale be also indulge his taste for

the fine-art, and we encounter each other at several point of the

route.”



as she speak she hold out her hand to Coverdale, who, after a moment’s

hesitation, and with a slight accession of colour, just touch and

immediately relinquish it, say, in a cold but polite tone of

voice--



“do you know whether the Muirs be in England now, Miss Crofton?”



as the person address remark his look and tone, she press her lip

together so forcibly that every trace of red vanish from they; but

repress all other sign of emotion, she reply to his question. then

take a seat next Alice, she begin cultivate her good grace with

a degree of tact and talent which evince her power of shine in

society, and deserve more success than it appear to meet with.



Arabella Crofton be a handsome woman of thirty, look young than

her age. she be tall, and her figure be fully develop without be

actually _embonpoint_. her hand and foot, although proportion to she

height, be beautifully model, and the former unusually white and

soft. in feature she resemble Kate, so much so that she have more

than once be mistaken for her former pupil’s eld sister; but the

_expression_ of the two face be totally dissimilar. in Kate Crane

a fiery passionate nature be keep under control by an equally strong

degree of pride, and an amount of self-respect which serve she in place

of a high principle; in Arabella Crofton lie conceal even a great

depth of passion, but its sole antagonist be an intellect keen, strong,

and acute, though not of the high order, and a determination of

will and fixity of purpose which, while it lead she straight towards the

object she seek, render she somewhat unscrupulous as to the mean by

which it be to be attain; and as the mind usually write itself more

or less legibly on the countenance, so do the expression differ in Kate

and her late governess. still Miss Crofton’s be a face to attract and

rivet attention, a face which exercise a species of fascination over

those who behold it, so peculiar that it be not easy to define it.

as you gaze upon it, you feel that you be in the presence of an

intelligence of no common order, but of whose nature, hope, fear,

wish, and design, you be entirely ignorant--nay, in regard to which

you could not decide whether the good or evil principle predominate. in

this sense of power with which she impress other, together with the

uncertainty how it might be direct, lie the secret of much of Arabella

Crofton’e influence. Alice, not be metaphysical, do not attempt to

define the sensation with which her new acquaintance inspire she; have

she do so, it might have appear that she have form much the

same estimate of her manner and appearance as that with which we have

furnish the reader. but if Alice do not moralize, she arrive at

strong and definite conclusion without that process, for before she have

be half an hour in Miss Crofton’s company, she feel morally

convinced that she should hate she, and that it would turn out that the

_ci-devant_ governess either have do, or be about to do, something

which would completely account for and justify this sudden animosity.



during dinner, a note arrive from Lord Alfred Courtland, offer

Alice and Harry seat in his opera-box, which offer, after a few polite

speech to and from Mr. Crane, in his (in?) capacity as master of

the house, be accept. as they drive to the theatre, the follow

conversation pass between the husband and wife, the lady of course

begin it.



“what a detestable woman that Miss Crofton be! I’m sure I shall never

be able to endure she. I see now where Kate’s fault come from. Miss

Crofton have teach she to be worldly-minded, and ambitious, and all

sort of horrid thing which she never use to be; and the creature be

an old acquaintance of your, too! do you know she well--intimately?”



“eh? yes! I see a good deal of she at one time. how slow this fellow

drive, we shall lose the overture!” be Harry’s reply, which, if he

intend thereby to change the subject of the conversation, prove a

dead failure, for Alice continued:--



“oh! then you be not mere acquaintance, as she try to make out! I

think she ben’t speak the truth. well, and do you like her?--I

dare say you do, for I feel sure she be in love with you; indeed I

think she be still, by the way she cast down those great rolling eye

of hers whenever you say a word to she. I declare I feel quite jealous.”



Coverdale pause for a moment, ere he reply: “my dear Alice, you speak

thoughtlessly, but you do not know how such remark annoy I--fault I

have, and more serious, one than until lately I be at all aware of;

but to suppose that since I first see you, I have ever devote one

minute’s thought to any other woman breathing, would be to do I a foul

injustice.” Alice perceive, from his manner of speak, that her vague

suspicion have really pain he, and have no other ground for

they but an instinct which she confess to herself to be utterly

unsanctione by reason, she determine to confess her sin and obtain

absolution. this be in many case a tedious and difficult operation, but

when individual be on those easy and agreeable term which sometimes

last so long as a year after marriage, the process become greatly

facilitate. thus, by a little graceful and appropriate pantomime, Alice

cause it to be understand that she feel deeply penitent, and in a state

of mental self-accusation only to be allay by a remedy consist (as

some light-minded jester have phrase it), like a sermon, of “two head

and an application.” when this specific for female grief have be duly

administer by Harry, peace be for the time restore, and the evening

pass away most harmoniously in every sense of the word.









CHAPTER XXXIII.--TELEMACHUS and MENTOR.



the Opera-house be very full and proportionably hot on the evening

when Coverdale and his wife visit it (it be the _début_ of the

since famous Signora Bettimartini), Alice, unused to London gaiety,

and uneasy from the suspicion she could not contrive to banish,

acquire a headache, which, when she go to bed, prevent she from

fall asleep. thus, be anxious to court without loss of time

nature’s sweet restorer, of course she choose the most vexatious and

exciting topic she could select as a subject of thought, and begin to

speculate on all the evidence she could call to mind in regard to her

husband’s relation, past and present, towards Arabella Crofton, who,

as the reader must have perceive, be just at that especial epoch poor

little Mrs. Coverdale’s _bête noire_. the first circumstance she could

recollect to form the initial link in her chain of evidence, be Harry’s

inquiry about she when Alice casually mention her name during the

halcyon day of their honeymoon. in this conversation, Harry have

confess to a previous acquaintance with Miss Crofton, and when press

far, add that he know no good of she, or word to that effect. his

manner, Alice remember, be so peculiar that her curiosity have be

at once excited, or as she mentally put it, that “naturally she feel she

husband ought immediately to have tell she everything about it--_she_

have no concealment from he, she be sure.” follow up this train

of thought, another instance of this unkind and unflattering want of

confidence occur to she--the mysterious epistle which he have receive

that very afternoon, which have annoy he so much, and about which he

have refuse to afford she any explanation; and here a new idea flash

like an infernal inspiration across her brain--could that note be in

any way connect with Miss Crofton’s arrival? “yes! it must be so.” she

remember when they enter the drawing-room, and she have feel surprise

at find a stranger there, Harry seem to take it as a matter of

course: good reason why, he know it previously--this hateful woman, this

detestable creature, Arabella Crofton, have write to he privately,

inform he of her arrival! oh! she see it all; and how she would try

to wean his affection away from his poor wife--his poor, neglect,

betray wife! and succeed most likely--man be such fickle, wicked

thing; and then it would break her heart, _that_ there could be no

question of and she should die in the course of a year--in six

month, very likely, for she ben’t at all strong though she have a

colour--consumptive people always have brilliant complexion--think of

her poor aunt Kitty! and Harry would be sorry when it be too late,

perhaps. and so, draw a vivid picture of her repentant husband

grieve over her untimely decease, she cry herself to sleep, bedew

with her tear the “fickle, wicked thing,” calmly slumber at her

side; who straightway dream that, be out hunting, and ride

a young thorough-breed, he have charge a brook, and that his horse,

refuse it, have pitch he head foremost into its rapid water.



a month soon elapse--the London season be at its height. everybody

have be everywhere, and be go again; Grisi and Mario have arrive,

recover from sea-sickness and british catarrh, and “surpass

themselves” in their favourite character. a mob of costly equipage

jostle each other round Hyde Park every afternoon; carriage-horse,

deprive of their sleep o’nights, begin to grieve coachman’s heart

by reveal the position of their rib; young lady from the Country

dance away their rose and their _embonpoint_; man whose book for the

Derby be at all “shy” tremble in their patent-leather boot; the glory

of the lilac in the square have depart; water-cart make unpleasant

canal of the principal thoroughfare; the Honourable Mrs. Windsor Soape

have present her young daughter at the last drawing-room, and try

without success to stuff she down the throat of several eligible

eld son; Lady Close Shaver have inveigle an hundred and seventy

unfortunate into her hot drawing-room, bore they with Signor

Violini’s scientific rendering of Beethoven’s sonata in a b C minor,

poison they with bad ice and bad Champagne, and turn they out

to grass upon lobster salad, of which the principal feature be the

unaccountable absence of lobster: these, and many other misery,

attendant on the “joy of our dancing day,” have be gladly suffer

by the fanatical votary of the Juggernaut of Fashion, and still the

coverdale linger within the precinct of the modern Babylon. Lord

Alfred Courtland having receive a summon to join his family at

Leghorn, have refuse to obey it on the plea of ill health, back by

a physician’s opinion, which cost one guinea, and be worth----! well,

really, in this case it _be_ worth something, for it save Lord Alfred

a lecture, and he dislike be lecture, even for his good--silly

young man! so he stay in town, do as other folk do, and hope

thereby to become a man of fashion; but, as he only act like other

people, and do nothing very clever, or very foolish, or very wrong, he

by no mean succeed in obtain the reputation he covet. with this

consciousness of failure before his eye, he one night lounge dismally

out of his stall at the Opera, and be proceed with deject step

along the lobby, when he suddenly encounter Horace D’Almayne, well

dressed and well pleased with himself than ever.



“well meet, my lord; I be just wish for an agreeable companion,” be

his complimentary salutation. “I be naturally a sociable animal; if you

have no well employment, will you take pity on I for an hour or so?”



deeply impressed with such unexpected condescension, and overcome by

the transcendant cut of D’Almayne’s waistcoat, nothing remain for Lord

Alfred but gratefully to consent; which he accordingly do. link his

arm in that of his companion, D’Almayne continued:--



“you be look _triste, ennuyé_; have Grisi develop a cold, or

Cerito a corn? be it opera or ballet which have thus bore you?”



“neither one nor the other,” be the reply; “though even opera cease to

excite after one have grow accustomed to they.”



“yes! that be true; except to an educated musician” (and D’Almayne

look as if he humbly trust that he be equal to Mendelssohn, at the

very least), “I can conceive they grow tedious; but,” he continue, “you

should seek some more exciting amusement: mix in clever, witty society;

do thing--see thing; in fact, enjoy life as a young man with such

advantage of person and of station should do.”



“it may seem easy to you, who have achieve a reputation in the _beau

monde_, and can command any society you please, to accomplish this; but

it be the reverse of easy for a young man in these day, even if he have

a handle to his name, to persuade people that he have anything in he; in

fact I think a title stand rather in a young fellow’s way on enter

London life; people have somehow take to connect the idea of a lord

and a fool, until I believe they begin to think the term synonymous!”



“what a frightfully democratic opinion for one of your order to

promulgate!” return D’Almayne, smile at the disconsolate tone in

which Lord Alfred speak; “really you ought to have be bear on the

other side of the Channel; but I think I perceive your difficulty: you

do not care to be admit into society merely for your rank, but wish

to achieve a distinctive social reputation for yourself; be it not so?”



“yes! you have express my idea exactly; a great deal well than I

could have do myself,” be the reply. “and now tell I in what way be

this desirable consummation to be effect.”



“nothing be more easy. in the first place you require self-confidence;

let people see that you think yourself a fine fellow, and they will

begin to think so too. in the next place, take a decide line of some

kind, and adhere to it steadily; but, in order to be able to do so, be

careful, ere you select it, that it be in accordance with your natural

disposition and taste.”



“good general maxim,” return Lord Alfred; “and now to apply they to

the particular instance.”



D’Almayne pause for a moment ere he replied--



“if you really wish I to constitute myself your mentor, you must allow

I more opportunity of enjoy your society than I have hitherto

possess, and then, from time to time, I dare say I may be able to give

you a few hint which you may find practically beneficial; and as there

be nothing like make use of the present occasion, what say you to

allow I to introduce you to a kind of private club, where I and a

few of my particular set sometimes meet after the Opera, and while away

an hour or two with a hand at whist or écarté, or exchange our idea

on the topic of the day over a game of billiard; the stake be,

of course, suit to the measure of our purse, my own be an

uncomfortably shallow one. we be close to the entrance, shall we turn

in?”



after a moment’s hesitation, the result of an indefinite notion that he

be about to do something wrong, Lord Alfred consent; and D’Almayne

knock at the door of what look like a good private house. the portal

unclosed, and immediately shut again by some mysterious agency, for,

when they enter, no domestic be visible; and they proceed along

a passage to a second door cover with red baize, with a glass eye,

place Cyclop-like in the middle of its forehead, through which a human

face observe they for a moment, then disappear, and the red baize

door open and admit they of its own accord, as the outer one have

set it the example. follow his companion up a flight of stone stair,

at the top of which yet another baize door with a cyclopian optic

present itself, Lord Alfred Courtland hear the sound of laugh

and conversation, and in another moment find himself in a large,

well-light apartment, round which be disperse sundry small table,

at which be seat, in group of three or four, from a dozen

to fifteen man, all of whom be recruit exhausted nature with

Champagne, pine-apple ice, or more substantial viand, if their taste

incline they thereunto. place himself at an unoccupied table,

D’Almayne inquire in his most insinuating tone--“Champagne, Claret,

Johannisberg--what be your pet vanity, my lord?--_c’est affreux_, the

inefficient ventilation of that Opera-house. I be positively famish

with thirst, and must drown my enemy before Horace be himself again.”



“having obtain the privilege of consider you my mentor, I cannot

do well than avail myself of your valuable taste and experience in

the selection of a beverage,” return Lord Alfred, fall into his

companion’s humour with that dangerous facility which be at once his

bane and his great charm. so Champagne and ice, and biscuit, all

first-rate of their kind, be bring and discuss; and during the

demolition thereof, one or two intimate of D’Almayne, faultless in mien

and manner, lounge up, and be introduce to his lordship, and drank

wine dreamily, and talk smart nothing with a sleepy wittiness as of

inspire dormice; and otherwise exhibit symptom of that life-weary,

all-to-piece condition which very young man believe in as the _ne plus

ultra_ of modern dandyism; and Lord Alfred’s heart leap within he

as he think that now he have at last really begin “life,” and be in

a fair way to become a man-about-town. such wonderful being be we,

_ætatis_ nineteen!



when a man be thirsty nothing be easy than to drink a bottle of

Champagne without know it, perhaps even till the next morning;

I never hear of the delusion last long. whether Lord Alfred

Courtland drink more or less than a bottle on the occasion in question,

history relateth not, but certainly, when he rise and stroll into the

billiard-room, he felt considerably exhilarate, and eager to

achieve something “fast,” which might tend to impress his incipient

“about-townishness” in the mind of his fashionable acquaintance. thus,

hear the rattle of dice in a further apartment, he, to D’Almayne’s

surprise and amusement, declare billiard a bore, and whist “slow,” and

“vote” for something with a little more fun in it. so, “Dante-like,”

enter the infernal region, they very soon “know a bank whereon”

 much “wild _time_” have be waste, and an immense crop of wild oat

sown;--and off which certain proprietor have reap many golden sheaf,

while the sower themselves have gain only experience, teach they

how to take care of their money, about the time when their money be

all go, which must have be more improving than consolatory to the

“clean-out one.” then first upon Lord Alfred’s youthful ear fall

the command, diabolical in its persuasive eloquence, “_Faites le jeu,

monsieur!_” then timidly, and with feeling akin to those of mediæval

youth who, in the good old feudal time, sign uncomfortable compact

with the evil One, which never turn out satisfactorily for they even

in this world, do Lord Alfred stake his first guinea, and unfortunately

lose it. we say unfortunately, for have he win, and so come, see, and

conquer, he might have listen to the appeal of conscience which

just then be strive to make a coward of this neophyte man about

town; but, as matter stand, he feel a stern necessity to vindicate the

_sing froid_ with which he could support a run of ill luck; and play

again--win, double his stake--win; then, against D’Almayne’s advice,

stake his winning on “_le rouge_,” and that colour prove successful;

and then the gambler-spirit come upon he, and he play with a fierce

eagerness, and drink more Champagne, and play again, until two hour

later D’Almayne almost force he away from the table, and take he

home, flushed and excited, a winner of one hundred pound! poor boy! as

he leave that haunt of sordid vice and idle folly, he believe that he

have do something clever, and spirit, and manly, and long for the

next evening, when he might again distinguish himself; but could he have

foresee half the consequence of this, his first step in evil, or the

sorrow he be thereby bring upon true heart that love he, he would

have shrink from again cross the threshold, as though it be indeed

that of the hell which in their unseemly jest man term it.



rise late the next morning, he be inform that a gentleman be

wait to see he, and on enter the sitting-room, find Horace

D’Almayne in an easy-chair and an elegant attitude.



“I be anxious about you, _mon cher_” (they have grow wonder fully

familiar over their Champagne), “you appear so much excited last

night,” he begin, uncross his graceful leg, clothe in a seraphic pair

of _blin et Fils chef-d’œuvres_.



                   “sure such a pair be never see!”



“you seem so carry away by your enthusiasm that I think you would

not sleep, and thus venture to call at this unreasonable hour to see

how you be get on.”



“very kind and friendly of you, I’m sure,” return Lord Alfred, quite

overcome by such unhoped-for condescension on the part of his model

Mentor. “I suppose I do get rather excited, but I’m all right again

this morning,--at least I shall be,” he continue, as a dizzy swimming

in the head oblige he to grasp a chair-back for support, “as soon as I

have have a cup of coffee.”



“or if I might suggest, a bottle of Seltzer-water with a suspicion of

Cognac in it, be a much more efficient substitute: allow I to brew for

you;--may I ring the bell?”



receive the permission he seek, Horace act accordingly, and

when the servant appear, desire he (on a glance from Lord Alfred,

delegate all authority to he) to bring a bottle of Seltzer-water,

brandy, and a lemon. possess of these _desiderata_, he commence

shred off two or three delicate little spiral circle of lemon-peel,

like yellow watch-spring, then drop these into a brobdignagian

tumbler, warrant not to run over under any severity of effervescence,

he add thereunto a liqueur glass full of the pure (and strong)

Cognac. unwire the Seltzer-water, he allow it to draw its own cork

(for thus, under his skilful control, do the operation appear to be

perform), and, force it to explode into the tumbler, he present

the beverage, foam wildly, to Lord Alfred, who, at the risk of

immediate suffocation, drink it off in that rabid condition, and

providentially survive, declare himself greatly benefit by the

treatment. having thus re-invigorate his patient’s exhausted frame,

D’Almayne proceed to perform the same friendly office by his mind, and

very good counsel do he bestow upon he--only that his advice have

this peculiarity, viz., that whilst in word he recommend Lord

Alfred Courtland to bend his step in a northerly direction, that young

nobleman feel an unaccountable conviction that by proceed due south,

he should raise himself in the estimation of his Mentor and of all other

man of spirit. thus he hear, with a complacent smile, that D’Almayne

be surprised at the manner in which he have carry all before he at

the gaming-table on the previous evening; that every one imagine he

to be an old hand at such matter; and one individual, who be generally

suppose to make a very decent living by gambling, have declare his

conviction that Lord Alfred play on a system, and a deucedly clever

system too!--At all of which D’Almayne appear alarmed and uneasy, and

assure his friend that it be a very dangerous talent for a young man,

and that it would be a great relief to his mind if Lord Alfred would

promise never to go there again; to which his lordship reply by

light a cigar, hand the box to his mentor, and ask he whether

he consider he such an irreclaimable muff as not to be able to win

or lose a matter of a hundred pound without make a ninny of himself.

declare himself innocent of any such disrespectful innuendo, D’Almayne

also light a cigar (it be impossible in these piping time to

do anything without plenty of puffing), and these new ally grow

loquacious and confidential; but with this difference, that Lord Alfred

give his confidence, and Horace obligingly receive the sacred deposit.

thus, after a fair amount of the horticultural cruelty, clepe “beat

about the bush,” have be commit, that good young man be make

acquaint with the “secret sorrow,” which, as the reader be aware, be

with much success perform the part of the “worm I’ the bud” to Lord

Alfred’s “damask cheek.” as soon as Mentor thoroughly understand

the state of the case, which he do in an incredibly short space of

time--tact be so strongly develop in he that it almost amount to

intuition--he follow the advice of Polly in the “Beggar’s opera,” by

“ponder well” before he venture to prescribe for the complaint

of his Telemachus. having sit with bent brow until his cigar be

exhausted, he fling the end into the grate, smooth his beloved

moustache, and then speak oracularly:--



“you see, _mon cher_,” he begin, “you be take to the _rôle_ of a

_flâneur_, what you call a man-about-town, full early for an Englishman;

thus, the chief thing you want be self-confidence, without which a man

can neither do proper justice to himself nor to his position. now it

seem to I the good thing for you would be to get some pretty woman

of good station to take you in hand; you must try and establish a

flirtation with somebody.”



“_Cui bono?_” inquire Telemachus; “the governor would never stand I

marry for--oh! not for the next five year!”



“marry before you’re one-and-twenty! my dear fellow, what can have

put such a frightful idea into your head!” exclaim Mentor, aghast at

the supposition. “no, no; marriage be the last thing I should dream of

recommend, except quite as a _dernier ressort_. for which reason,

I be about to add, that the good practice to set you at ease with

yourself, and therefore with other people, will be to devote your

attention to some pretty and fashionable married woman;--there! don’t

look so awfully scandalize; of course I only mean a sentimental

and platonic affair--just enough to excite and interest you into

self-oblivion. when you once forget your _ipsissimus ego_--when, as that

pun friend of your, Mr. Coverdale, would say, you cease to mind

your _I_--all your anxiety in regard to popular opinion will vanish,

and you will soon find that with your face, figure, address, and

position, Lord Alfred Courtland will become the admired of all admirer.

and that remind I that Mrs. Coverdale would be just the person for

that purpose;--she be very pretty, move in good society, and, _entre

nous_, be smite with you already!”



“but really--of course I don’t set up to be any well than my

neighbour,” stammer the poor boy, colour at the possibility

of be suspect of such slow attribute as good feeling and right

principle, and yet unable entirely to silence the prompting of his

well nature;--“of course I don’t set up for a saint; but Harry

Coverdale be an old friend and schoolfellow, and one of the good

creature in the world; I should not like--that is, I really

couldn’t--but, I beg your pardon, I don’t think I exactly understand

your meaning.”



“I don’t think you do,” return D’Almayne, his sarcastic tone

express such unmistakable contempt that Lord Alfred actually wince

as if in pain.



“I don’t think you have the faint glimmer of my meaning. you don’t

suppose I intend you to order a chaise and four, and run off with pretty

Mrs. Coverdale to the continent, do you? my idea be much less

alarming, I can assure you! _par exemple_--your friend Harry be a

physical force man; he be a mighty hunter, a dead shot; he love only

his dog and his horse; but require a Joe Manton to ensure he good

sport, and a pretty wife to sit at the head of his table: Mrs.

Coverdale, on the other hand, have a soul--read Tennyson, feel she

husband’s neglect, and pine for some one who will appreciate she and

sympathize with she; you, in the kindness of your heart, pity she, and

know you can afford she the consolation of congeniality, obligingly

make up for her good man’s deficiency; therefore, you read poetry with

she, explain the obscure passage which neither she, you, nor any one

else can understand; her mind repose on your superior intelligence; she

trust you, and confide to you important secrets,--the exact age of her

dear female friend, whom she suspect of design upon your heart, the

dress she be go to wear at the next fancy ball,--and eventually, with

heighten colour and avert eye, the history of that ring with the

turquoise forget-I-not, together with a biographical sketch of the

noble giver--show how he live pathetically, and die in the odour of

heroism, fight at the head of his regiment in the Punjaub, the centre

of a select circle of slaughter foeman; which latter confidence may be

consider as the latchkey to the fair lady’s heart, ensure you

admittance at all time and season.”



“and having attain this agreeable position, how long do you expect so

pleasant a state of thing to last, and what be to be the end of it?”

 inquire Telemachus.



“oh! until she have got rid of her romance, and you of your diffidence;

by which time you will have grow mutually tired of each other, and the

London season will have come to an end,” be mentor’s oracular reply.

Telemachus muse, light a fresh cigar, and muse again. he like the idea,

have a faint suspicion it might be wrong, but be quite sure it would be

very pleasant. mentor, think this a promising frame of mind in which

to leave his pupil, would not weaken the force of his argument by vain

repetition, so make an engagement to meet again in the evening, and

depart. and while _les petite moustache noire_ wound female

heart as he pass down courtly St. James’s Street, the spirit of the

good young man, their wearer, glow within he, and--



                        “as he walk by himself,



                        he talk to himself,



                        and thus to himself say he!”



“ha! ha! Milord Courtland, you be mine--your purse, your credit, your

influence--all be mine! but what a child it be! what a baby! _sacré!_

at his age I be win twenty pound a day at billiard in New

Orleans!--And you, Harry Coverdale, _mon ami_, I will teach you to watch

I with black look when I be converse with _la belle millionaire_;

you have well attend to your own wife now--young, pretty, and

neglect! _Le petit_ Alfred have a fair game before he, if he have but

wit to play it--yes! all go as it should! fortune fill the sail!

there be a cool head and a steady hand at the helm: _vogue la galère!_”









CHAPTER XXXIV.--CIRCE.



in this “tight little island,”--of which as a whole we be all so

proud, although it afford ample occupation for its public in grumble

at its institution, _viâ_ its _Times_ newspaper--the only season of the

year when fog be not, and every day do not resemble a “washing-day”

 on a large scale, the only period in fact when the country be endurable,

be the early summer. thus the educate class, whose well-balance and

carefully develop mind enable they to arrive at sound conclusion,

and whose well-store pocket render they free to come and go

untrammelle by pecuniary consideration, have bind themselves by the

law of the tyrant Fashion to spend June and July in London, where they

simmer in hot room, when they should be in bed and asleep, until all

the goodness be boil out of they--which new “theory of evil” beg to

offer to the notice of Miss Martineau, and all other speculative

mind anxious to elevate humanity by substitute earthly nonsense for

heavenly revelation. but however you may brick she up and smoke-dry she,

nature will assert herself, and, turn with disgust from oats at 40s.

the quarter in a mahogany manger, pine for green meat and a canter over

the spring turf. so a compromise have be effect between town and

country amusement, and horticultural _fête_ have be devise to

afford parboil fashionable breathe time between their round of

dissipation, together with a gentle reminder of the “pleasure of the

plain,” which they be sacrifice to their craving for unnatural

excitement. horticultural _fête_ be bring about in this wise: early

in the inclemency of a british spring, while London be shiver over

its fondly cherish fire, that noun of multitude perceive in the first

column of its _Times_ a notice that member of the Horticultural Society

may obtain ticket at privileged price until some specify day;

thereupon All-London write to its particular friend the M. H. S. for

an “order,” and the member vouch by implication for All-London’s

standing and respectability--into which he have probably go no deep

than its coat--All-London besiege the office of that floral autocrat,

Dr. Lindley, and clamour for ticket, cry “Give, give,” and

insatiable as the daughter of the horse-leech. have at length obtain

its desire, All-London button up its great-coat and wait timidly but

eagerly for the first Horticultural. but the London season be an outrage

upon, and an insult to nature, and nature take her change out of the

first Horticultural; it be a pour wet day, Chiswick become Keswick,

and the Duke of Devonshire’s ground, yield to hydraulic pressure,

cease to be dry ground any long. Dr. Lindley... we have not the

pleasure of that gentleman’s personal acquaintance, but we can imagine

Dr. Lindley feel disappointed and... express it. then All-London

exchange its greatcoat for a paletot, and look forward with a timid

anxiety to the second Horticultural, which be in June enjoy the

advantage of April weather, and be only showery, so the bold

quarter of London go, from the Herbert Fitz-tip-top, careless of the

bronchial tube of their serve-man and carriage-horse, down to the

Robinson Joneses, safe in the immunity of a hack brougham, driver, and

horse--a long-suffer trio, so accustomed to wait in the rain, that

use have become a second nature to these amphibious hireling. our

enterprise pleasure-seeker come back ere dewy eve, and say that,

consider the fact that flower willn’t blow out of door in cold

weather, and that the gravel be a swamp, and the turf a morass, the

tent very hot, and the east wind very cold, and that there be

nobody there except a few dreadful people, who really ought not to be

anywhere--(Mrs. Robinson Jones be actually push up against Mr. Cutlet

and his rib, her own butcher, who make a clear £2000 a-year, while

genteel Robinson Jones scarcely average £1500 at the Bar; but what

do that signify?)--and that the female Quarter-of-London have get the

ridiculous sol of its little french shoe wet through in five minute,

and have feel a tightness at its chest ever since; allow for these

and several other slight drawback, it really be not such a complete

failure after all! but even english weather have its bright side;

and, content with take the shine out of the first two, on the third

Horticultural _fête_ the sun seems resolve to come out strong, and,

set parasol at defiance, imprint his burn kiss on the pale

feature of all the pretty woman in town, like an ardent old luminary as

he be. and All-London, find that it really be a beautiful day,

put on its good bib and tucker, and take its wife and daughter to

Chiswick. where the road be water they be very muddy, where they

be not water they be dusty; and as the dust stick to the carriage,

and the dust stick to the mud, and the horse get first very hot

go there, then very cold wait there, and the pole of every

other carriage invariably run through the back panel of the vehicle

immediately precede it, coachman be not, as a general rule, fond of

the third Horticultural; but nothing _can_ please everybody, and

these Flower-show “please the lady” (to quote Mr. Crane’s favourite

phrase), and that be the great point after all. it be probably with

a view to “please the lady” that Mr. Crane have think proper to

invest capital in half a dozen horticultural ticket--see that his

own horticultural taste be confine to drink Sherry-cobbler in

an arbour, whenever such a privilege be vouchsafe to he, and his

knowledge limit to the capability of discriminate between a cabbage

and a cauliflower. the weather having be such as we have describe it

during the first and second _fêtes_--on both which occasion Mr. Crane

bewail the useless expense into which his gallantry have seduce he,

with a truly touching degree of pathos--these ticket remain unused

until the third and last flower-show, when “the face of all nature

look gay,” and “bright Phoebus” obligingly condescend to “adorn

the hill,” the ex-cotton-spinner and his spouse, Harry Coverdale and

Alice, together with Arabella Crofton, avail themselves of five of

they--Horace D’Almayne quietly pocket the sixth in a fit of mental

(and physical) abstraction. they be to start at a quarter before two,

as Mr. Crane always prefer be early on all occasion; but at a

quarter before two, when the carriage draw up to the door, Alice be

not ready, and moreover it be Alice’s own fault that she be not ready;

and thus it fall out. Lord Alfred Courtland play the flute well for so

young a man, and an amateur; since he have be in town, a talented

professor instruct he in this art, who be an exile patriot--that be

to say, he and several other ardent young man have attempt one fine

morning to take their “Fatherland” away from the gentleman in

possession, and give it to the secret-blood-and-bone-united-brother

band--the same be a pet name by which they see fit to call

themselves. what they would have do with their fatherland, if they have

get it, neither do they, nor do any one else appear to have the least

idea; but this difficulty of dispose of their country be fortunately

spare they, as their enterprise consist simply of a stroll along the

principal street of their native city, in company with a drum and a

little red flag, bear the cheerful device of a skull and cross-bone,

with the motto, “death to Tyrants!” which stroll continue until they

accidentally encounter a company of soldier, who convey they--drum,

flag, and all--to the state prison, where they be detain, until it

be discover that they be eat their head off, the authority

exile they, to save their keep. Herr Hildebrand Tootletoot-zakoffski,

one of this devoted band, have bring his polish sorrow and his german

flute to England, and between they both manage to make a much more

comfortable income than tyranny have hitherto allow he to enjoy under

the mildewed institution of his own blighted country. for the rest he

be a mild little man, addict to converse on music and patriotism

with a sort of washy sentimentality, which enable he to pass as an

individual of refined taste and cultivate mind with those who do not

look beyond the surface; personally he rejoice in a complexion as of

bad putty, and an amount of heroic beard and moustache which would have

stuff a chair-cushion very comfortably. and be such as we have

describe he, Herr Hildebrand--an acquaintance of and introduce by

Horace D’Almayne, who, in his multifarious occupation, may have be a

band-brother, for aught we know to the contrary--have suggest to Lord

Alfred Courtland the great advantage it would be to he in his, the

professor’s, talented absence, if he, Lord Alfred, could find any

amiable pianiste of his acquaintance, able and willing to play duet

with he, to “improve his time;” and as he say this in the presence of

and immediately after a _tête-à-tête_ with Horace D’Almayne, it really

be scarcely necessary for that judicious mentor to suggest to his

lordship pretty little Mrs. Coverdale, although to guard against

mistake he do so. thus Alfred Courtland and Alice have play a good

many duet in Park Lane; and on the morning in question, luncheon be

announce in the middle of one of these interesting performance half an

hour soon than usual, to guard against the possibility of anybody’s

be too late, Alice, feel by this time quite at home in her

cousin’s house, coolly tell Lord Alfred to come down and partake of the

mid-day meal, as she be resolve to finish the duet after it be over,

before she go to dress, and if they make haste she be sure there be

plenty of time. but time unfortunately be one of those stubborn fact

with which it be impossible to take a liberty without suffer for

one’s rashness; and although the latter part of the duet be rattle

through with a costa-like rapidity, which elicit from his breathless

lordship an acknowledgment that “it be the pace that kill,” yet when

all the rest of the party be assemble, Alice be only half dress.

then, as be his willnot on such occasion, Mr. Crane fall into a fretful

fuss, and trot up and down the room, and make everybody fidgety and

uncomfortable, especially Harry, who be provoke with Mr. Crane for

be annoy with Alice, and with Alice for having give he cause for

annoyance.



“there be a quiet way of arrange the matter, my dear sir,” he say;

“let those who be ready start in the barouche, and I will wait and

drive Alice in the mail-phaeton.”



“yes, and then we shall never meet at the garden, and never all come

away at the same time, and my arrangement will be completely subvert,

and everything will go wrong,” whine Mr. Crane. on this Harry run up to

hasten Alice, and Alice, who be attire herself at express speed, be

cross, and snub he out of the room, and he rejoin the company in

the drawing-room with compress lip and an angry flush on each cheek;

and Arabella Crofton favour he with a glance of intelligent pity,

which, if it be intend to soothe his wounded spirit, fail in its

effect most signally. after the lapse of an awful ten minute, by the

expiration of which period Mr. Crane be on the verge of tear,

the culprit Alice make her appearance, look very pretty, but not

altogether as penitent as might have be desire; but as she say in a

cheerful tone that she “really be quite distressed at having keep they

all wait,” we will hope she feel more than she allow to appear.

then arise a debate and confusion of tongue and opinion as to how

the party be to divide. Harry offer to drive the phaeton, Mr. Crane

having privately hint that such an arrangement would meet with his

approval,--who be to accompany he? Harry suggest his own wife,

mean to treat she to a gentle reproof on the road for her want of

consideration in having keep a whole party wait merely to finish a

silly duet with that boy Alfred Courtland. but Kate disapprove of this

arrangement--perhaps because she have begin to suspect that the Coverdale

couple do not always in “their little nest agree,” and have read in

Harry’s flash eye warn of a perturb spirit. whether Alice’s

conscience lead she to the same result we do not pretend to decide, but

for some reason she second her cousin until she discover that by

do so Arabella Crofton would be her substitute, by which time the

affair be settle beyond her power of altering. her annoyance would

have be sensibly diminish, however, if she could have know that

the arrangement be if possible more distasteful to her husband than to

herself, but unfortunately there be no _clairvoyant_ at hand to afford

she this desirable intelligence. having hand up his companion, and

do all that his chivalrous nature teach he be due from a gentleman

to _any_ woman entrust to his care, and nothing far, Harry

gather up his rein, place himself by Miss Crofton’s side in the

phaeton, and sit bolt upright, drive off with an unapproachable

expression of face, which indicate, as plainly as word could have

do, his resolve not to advance beyond monosyllable until they reach

Chiswick. but Harry be in such matter no match for the astute woman of

the world who sit beside he. apparently fall in with his humour she

lean back in the carriage, and the only sign she give of her

presence be an occasional sigh, which escape she, as it appear,

involuntarily. before they have proceed far, however, they encounter

the peripatetic theatre of that inconvenient humourist, dear old

Punch, with his private band pop-go-the-weasel like an harmonious

steam-engine; whereof the horse (the identical pair which have run away

with Harry and Alice in the early spring-time of their courtship, and

which Mr. Crane still retain, although he carefully avoid

drive they himself)--preferring probably a more classical style of

music--begin to express their disapprobation by plunge violently,

nearly dash the phaeton against a coal waggon, a catastrophe which

nothing but the most consummate skill on the part of their driver could

have avert. as Coverdale succeed in reduce the rebellious steed

to order, he could not help involuntarily glance at his companion to

ascertain how the incident have affect she. she be lean forward,

her attitude and the expression of her feature indicate excitement and

interest rather than terror, while her fine eye, dilate and sparkle

with a more than ordinary lustre, be fix upon his countenance with

look of unmistakable admiration. courage, or as he would have term it

“pluck,” especially in a woman, where he consider it as an “additional

attraction,” while in a man it be simply a _sine quâ non_, always

delight Harry Coverdale; and, be as innocent and natural as a

child, he could no more help express his sentiment, than he could

exist without inhale vital air.



“well, I never do see such nerve in a woman!” he exclaim; “why you

look pleased rather than frightened! not that there be any danger,

except of damage Mr. Crane’s near hind wheel. they don’t bit these

horse properly, and that white-nosed animal haven’t the tender mouth

at the good of time.” and as he speak he administer a smartish

cut across the ear as a practical comment on the delinquent’s oral

insensibility.



“you be such a good whip,” be the reply, “and it always interest I

to see brute force control by skill, energy, and strength of will.

you guide these fiery horse with such a calm sense of power, that I

could never feel afraid when you be drive I.”



Miss Crofton be decidedly a clever woman; if there be one thing on

which in his secret soul Harry pride himself, it be on his driving;

and this practical compliment, stand as it unfortunately do in

somewhat marked contrast to his wife’s feminine dislike of certain

contention with “queer temper” horse, which have at odd time come

in for a speciman of Coverdale’s “quiet manner,” appeal to his

weak point--he be mortal, and it touch he, and at the touch his

taciturnity vanish, and straightway he begin to confide to his

dangerous companion all his most secret thought and feeling in regard

to----bitting hard-mouthed horse. it seem an unlikely topic for

Arabella to make much of, and yet she allow he to run on, listen

with a smile of pleased attention; for though his talk be solely

equestrian, yet it serve as well as any other subject to melt away the

icy barrier behind which Harry have hitherto entrench himself, and thus

effectually defend himself against all attempt at a renewal of the

former intimacy which appear to have exist between they. having

explain completely to his own satisfaction the advantage which in the

instance under consideration would be gain by drive “brown muzzle”

 up at the “cheek,” and the white-nosed horse in the “low-bar,”

 together with copious note, descriptive and explanatory, and

voluminous annotation and reflection on this momentous question, Harry

metaphorically resume his seat amid continue cheering, and Arabella

Crofton rise in reply. of course she start on horse, to which she

soon attach carriage, by mean of which she in an incredibly short

time contrive to ride back to Italy, and find Harry stand it well

than she expect, she continue in a voice indicative of deep but

repress feeling--



“ah! that be a strange, strange summer we pass there! and yet, now I

can calmly look back upon it, there be many happy hour, bright, sunny

little bit, to set against the deep shadow of such a life as mine,

time when I enjoy the privilege of your friendship, before”--and here

her voice faltered--“before I forfeit that and everything, even my

self-respect, by my own mad folly!”



she pause in emotion, and her companion reply in a kind, frank

manner,--



“why distress yourself by revive a disagreeable reminiscence?” (as

he use the word a slight shudder seem to convulse she, and a look

of pain, but not the pain of contrition, flit across her handsome

features)--“an affair which I have, as I promise you, practically

forget, which I should never again have enter upon with you, and in

regard to which my lip be seal to every other live creature.”



“you be kind and generous-hearted, as you ever be,” be the

rejoinder, “but I cannot forget so readily”--here she pause, sigh

deeply, then continued--“I be so glad to have have this--this

conversation with you; your manner have be so cold and stern, I be

afraid you have repent of your promise that if we ever meet again it

should be as friend.”



“well, you see,” return Harry, in an embarrassed tone, “you see

circumstance have change with I since the time to which you refer;

and I think--in fact, you yourself say in that note it would be

well--I assure you I mean nothing unkind, why should I? as long as

you----” and here, having be on the point of “put his foot in it,”

 as he mentally paraphrase his colloquial _étourderie_, Harry pause

in confusion, actually blush in his generous fear of wound his

companion’s feeling. having relieve his embarrassment by give that

unfortunate scapegoat, the white-nosed horse, one more for himself, he

resumed--“And now let I ask you whether you approve of the wife I have

choose?”



Harry make this inquiry, not because he feel particularly anxious

to learn Arabella’s opinion of Alice, but because he want to say

something, and this be the first idea which occur to he, thus the

moment he have speak he wish the speech unsay. Miss Crofton hesitate

for a moment ere she reply, in a slightly constrain tone of voice--



“your choice do your taste credit; for, in her style, Mrs. Coverdale

be singularly pretty, and I can imagine her very attractive--when she

please.”



“you speak as if she have not please, in your case,” rejoin Harry,

smile at the unmistakable emphasis with which the concluding word have

be speak. Miss Crofton smile also; then with a melancholy expression

she replied--



“in my anomalous position in life, I be too well accustomed to slight

to feel a moment’s annoyance at such trifle.”



“but it annoy I though,” return Coverdale, fire up with the

indignation all generous nature feel at the idea of indignity be

offer to any one in a dependent situation. “I be surprise at such

want of right feeling, or even common courtesy, in Alice! she cannot be

aware of the impression her manner have make on you. I shall speak to she

about it.”



“do not think of such a thing!” exclaim Arabella, hastily; “it be

folly in I to mention it:”--she fix her eye on his face, and read

there that his resolution be unchanged, she lay her hand gently on his
