arm, and continue. “listen, and I will tell you the whole truth: womanly

instinct, I suppose, make your wife dislike I from the first moment she

be introduce to I. I have try in vain to conquer her dislike,

and we now, by a sort of tacit consent, avoid each other; be you to

interfere in my behalf, it would be of no avail; on the contrary,

it would increase the evil, and, pardon my saying, might lead to a

disagreement between you; for, I may be mistake, but I have fancy

Mrs. Coverdale appear a little impatient of control sometimes--I hope I

be mistaken.”



she wait for a reply; but Harry, not be able to deny the charge,

and not choose to assent to it, remain silent, and she, rightly

interpret his reserve, continued:--



“in that case, I implore you, do not dream of advocate my cause. be

I to be the occasion of any difference between you, it would render I

most unhappy.”



after a moment’s silence, she added--



“I be so much interested when I hear you be go to be marry, and

hope, nay pray, that you might be as happy as I would--would always

have you. I be grieve to think that Mrs. Coverdale should not fully

appreciate the prize she have draw in that most uncertain of all

lottery, marriage; but I feel sure she will learn to understand you

well, and all will come right: you be evidently much attach to she,

and that be the case, she _must_ love you.” then in a low tone she

added--“_You_ be not one likely to love in vain.”



what reply, if any, Harry would have make to this speech, will never be

know, as at that minute they enter the line of carriage set down

at the gate of the Chiswick Gardens, and Coverdale have enough to occupy

he in prevent his excitable horse from commit a breach of the

peace. whether or no the phaeton groom be an observant man we cannot

say, but if he feel the degree of amiable interest usually display by

domestic servant in the affair of their superior, he must have be

strike when mentally contrast Mr. Coverdale’s manner of hand Miss

Crofton into and out of that open carriage by an immense accession of

cordiality, for which he be probably more puzzled to account than we

trust the reader find himself.









CHAPTER XXXV.--FLOWERS and THORNS.



“we have somehow contrive to lose sight of the barouche,” exclaim

Coverdale, after look up and down the line of carriage in vain; “I

expect they must have escape we when that white-nosed horse shy at

Punch; I fancy I know which way they have turn, but I must have go

down a wrong street--poor old Crane will be in fit--I wonder what we

have well do?”



“what I should suggest be to walk slowly backwards and forward inside

the gate, and watch for their arrival,” return Arabella, wish in

her secret soul that one of the barouche-horse might have fall dead

lame, or that any other catastrophe, not involve injury to life or

limb, might have befall the rest of the party.



after parade up and down with most laudable perseverance for nearly

half an hour, during which time the crowd grow thick and thick,

and everybody arrive except the party they be in search of, Harry

suddenly exclaimed,--



“you’ll be tired to death with all this pushing and squeeze; they must

have come some short way, and get here before we; let we go on to the

conservatory, we shall meet they there, I dare say.”



when they reach the conservatory, however, they find the crowd so

dense that to attempt to discover their miss friend would have

involve a difficulty, beside which that popular definition of a forlorn

hope, “look for a needle in a bottle of hay,” would have sink into

comparative insignificance. there be a couple of chair near the exit

from the conservatory, from which a lady and gentleman rise as they

approach.



“suppose we take possession of those seat,” suggest Arabella, “and

watch the people as they come out; I must honestly confess I be both hot

and tired.”



“I sympathise in the first adjective,” return Harry, take off his

hat to allow the air to cool his heated brow; “I’ve walk up hill

through heather on the moor for six hour at a stretch, and not be

so warm as this; but then I must own I be in well condition; one eat

too many dinner in London, don’t you see, and cann’t get exercise enough

to keep a fellow in work order.”



having make a suitable reply to this and sundry other thoroughly Harry

Coverdale-ish remark, Miss Crofton turn the conversation by asking--



“pray, be that Mr. D’Almayne a particular favourite of your?”



“not a bit of it,” be the unhesitate reply; “rather the other thing,

in fact. I consider he a confounded puppy; and have what you lady

call a presentiment that some of these day I shall be oblige to give

he a lesson which he will not forget in a hurry.”



“then you also have observed--” begin Arabella.



“I have observe nothing in particular,” interrupt Harry, quickly;

“but I know this, if I be old Crane I would not have an insufferable,

ridiculous, young fop dangle about my house every day, and all day

long.”



“I think it be silly and imprudent in Kate to allow it,” return

Arabella, “and I venture to tell she so, but she do not take the hint

kindly, and I have not attempt to recur to the subject. I be afraid

her marriage have not improve she; I really believe since I speak to she

she have be kind to Mr. D’Almayne than before; he and his insinuate

young friend, Lord Alfred Courtland, have almost live in Park Lane this

last week.”



“_his_ friend!” exclaim Harry, “little Alfred be my friend--he and I

be at school together--that is, he be at the bottom when I be at the

top; I introduce he to D’Almayne myself, and now I wish I have leave it

alone; oh, there’s no harm in little Alfred--besides, I never hear he

speak a dozen word to Kate Crane.” a meaning smile pass across his

companion’s handsome feature, but she only said,--



“I be sorry he be your friend; I be afraid Mr. D’Almayne be a dangerous

acquaintance for so vain and weak a young man.”



“Alfred be no fool, though perhaps firmness be not his strong point,”

 return Coverdale; “vain perhaps he be--all handsome boy be, I

suppose. but why do you say you be sorry he be my friend?”



Miss Crofton be silent for a minute, then in a timid and hesitate

voice replied,--



“you will be angry with I if I tell you my reason for dislike Lord

Alfred’s constant visit; you will doubt what I say, and impute to I

all kind of false and evil motive for say it.”



“go on,” return Harry, in a low, stern voice, “you have say too much

for I to rest satisfied not to hear more--tell I _all_ you know or

suspect; but take care--if, as you say, you value my good opinion--that

you speak only the simple truth.”



thus urge, Miss Crofton proceed cautiously to relate, that much as it

grieve she to say anything which might cause he pain or annoyance,

she would not disguise from he that she feel convinced Lord Alfred

Courtland be deeply smite with Alice, and that his frequent visit

to Park Lane be the result of his admiration--that, moreover, Horace

D’Almayne be evidently do his good to nurse what have be a mere

boyish fancy into a warm and strong feeling; of his motive she

be unable to judge, but of the fact she be certain; she believe,

moreover, that he possess a strong and daily increase influence over

the young man.



“and Alice?” inquire Coverdale, with flash eye, “what of Alice?

beware how you tell I that she encourage this misguided, foolish boy!

for by heaven, if you do, and it should appear that you have misjudge

she, I should be tempt to inform she and all the world the reason

which have induce you to invent such malicious calumny!”



“you wrong I by your unkind suspicion,” be Arabella’s calm reply, “as

much as you wrong yourself by an ungenerous threat which you would be

incapable of executing; it be not for I to judge Mrs. Coverdale one way

or the other. I have satisfy my conscience in warn you; I leave

you now to examine and observe for yourself, and test the truth of my

statement--but of one thing I be certain, Horace D’Almayne have some deep

scheme _in petto_, and that he be an unscrupulous adventurer, clever

enough to render he a most dangerous associate for any one--a person to

beware of, in short.”



“if I become convinced he be put young Alfred up to any such

rascality as you imagine, I’ll break the scoundrel’s neck for he!”

 growl Coverdale, in a tone like the rumbling of distant thunder.



as he speak some one touch he on the shoulder, and look round, he

be more surprised than pleased to see the object of his kind intention

stand behind the chair on which he be seat. how long he might have

be there, or how much of their conversation he might have hear, it

be impossible to tell; but so convinced be Coverdale that D’Almayne

have be play the eavesdropper, that he be on the point of inquire

what amount of information he have thus acquire, and especially whether

he have clearly understand the fate that await he, if he be really

incite “little Alfred” to make love to his wife, when D’Almayne, who

possess a womanly predilection for always have the first and last

word, began--



“pardon I if I interrupt what appear a most interesting conversation,

but I have be hunt all over the garden for the last half-hour

to find you. Mr. Crane imago you have elope with his phaeton and

horse, and Mrs. Coverdale be so completely _au désespoir_ at the

loss of her husband, that even Lord Alfred Courtland’s attention be

powerless to console her;--really, Miss Crofton, it be too cruel of you

to seduce Benedick from his allegiance to his Beatrice--you might be

content with enslave we poor bachelor!”



this speech be not particularly palatable to Arabella, and she would

probably have pass it over in contemptuous silence have she not glance

at Coverdale; but, perceive by his flash eye and quivering lip that

he be so angry that he literally dared not trust himself to reply,

she hasten to prevent anything unpleasant occur between they, by

observe in her usual calm, slightly sarcastic manner--



“it be like Mr. D’Almayne’s policy to screen himself by throw the

blame on the injure party. we have be roam up and down like

restless ghost, hunt for Mrs. Crane and Mrs. Coverdale for the last

half-hour--ever since we arrive, in fact, until I grow so tired, that

out of compassion Mr. Coverdale allow I to sit down and rest.”



“one word, Mr. D’Almayne,” interrupt Harry, regardless of an imploring

look and gentle pressure of the arm from Arabella Crofton, “you make a

joke (for I suppose you do not wish I to consider you speak seriously)

about my wife a minute ago; now I’m a quick-temper fellow--touchy you

may call it, upon some point, and this happen to be one of they; so

to prevent anything disagreeable, I tell you frankly _I don’t like such

jokes_--you understand?”



Horace _do_ understand; he glance at Harry’s face. the handsome mouth

be sternly compress--the small, well-cut nostril quiver, and the

large dark eye flash with the anger he could scarcely restrain, his

tall form be draw up to its full height--his broad chest dilate,

and the muscle stand out on his stalwart arm until their shape become

visible beneath the “Zephyr Paletot;” altogether, Coverdale do not look

just then the kind of man with whom it would be pleasant to quarrel: so

D’Almayne, deeming “discretion the well part of valour,” smile, and

say something which might mean anything, and convey a clear idea of

nothing, in his most fascinating manner, and then pilot his companion

to the spot where he have agree on a rendezvous at a certain time with

the Crane party. they have not yet make their appearance, however, and

D’Almayne (who, since Harry give he the “caution” convey in his last

speech, have evince a marked desire to keep on good term with, and

out of arm reach of, so dangerous an acquaintance), guess their

whereabouts, volunteer to go and fetch they.



“pray do not quarrel with that man,” urge Arabella, as D’Almayne

quit they; “you be as little his equal in scheme and manœuvring,

as he be yours in strength and courage, and for this reason he be more

to be dread than if he be a very Hercules; do not lose your temper

with he, for by so do you will put yourself in the wrong and play

his game; come, be guide by I in this matter; believe I, my only

object be to secure your happiness.”



as she speak, she look up in his face with such an expression of

interest, not to say affection, that Coverdale, whose anger at the bad

be always a very evanescent affair, feel an impulse of pity for she,

which appear in the softened tone of his voice, as he replied:--



“don’t be afraid; I’m not go to give he his desert at present, and

I’m very sorry I speak harshly to you just now; but I know Alice to

be so good, and true, and pure--innocent and spotless as a child (by

heaven, the slight blow to my faith in she would drive I mad!), and

the mere mention of that foolish boy suppose she to be a fit recipient

for his romantic sentimental nonsense, make I lose my temper: but you

need not fear my do anything hasty. I shall, as you advise, observe

Alfred Courtland, and if, as I feel certain, his attention annoy Alice,

I shall speak to he seriously and kindly (I know the boy have a good

heart, and that it be D’Almayne who have set he on this business, if he

_be_ set on it); then, find I be aware of it, his fancy will die a

natural death; but I have little expectation that my preaching will be

require. Alice’s indifference will work the good cure.”



as he speak, the Crane party come in sight, Kate and her husband lead

the van, closely attend by Horace D’Almayne; while, at some little

distance behind they, linger Alice on the arm of Lord Alfred

Courtland. as they come up, he be address she in an earnest,

plead manner. Alice appear thoughtful and _distraite_, but the

moment her eye fall upon Harry and Miss Crofton she start, colour

up, and turn to her companion, say in a hurried, eager tone--



“such constancy and perseverance, my lord, deserve rewarding,” and as she

speak she give he a rosebud she carry in her hand, which he fasten

in his button-hole with an expression of eager delight.



Alice’s word and action be neither of they lose upon her husband or

his companion.









CHAPTER XXXVI.--ARCADIA in the NINETEENTH CENTURY.



it be popularly assert and believe that everything have two side

to it. even a plum-pudding have an inside and an out; and that romantic

malady, clepe “love unrequited,” although at first sight it appear an

entirely one-sided affair, often demonstrate its bilateral capability

by prove a much less heart-rend business than be imagine, when

the lapse of time enable one to discern the bright side of the picture.

the Crane expedition to the Horticultural Fête form no exception

to this law of nature:--thus, at the moment when Harry, like Hamlet’s

unfortunate papa, be have poison pour into his ear, and be

gradually work himself up to the bolster-scene-in-Othello pitch,

Alice, that pleasant little Desdemona, unconsciously amuse herself with

Cassio, Lord Courtland, emulate Dr. Watts’s “busy bee,” by flit

from flower to flower, laugh at very small joke, and altogether

conduct herself with great levity, and in a singularly undignified

manner--at least, so Mr. Crane think; and as he be say to be make of

gold, his opinion ought to have partake of the value of that precious

metal. but Mr. Crane have never quite forgive Alice for not appreciate

his many excellence, and be disposed to judge she harshly. after a

time, however, when the novelty of the scene begin to wear off--when

Alice have review the content of Howell and James’s, Swan and Edgar’s,

Redmayne’s, and other ruination shop, on the fair form of the

lady of the land--when she have “oh-how-beautiful-ed” and

“isn’t-it-lovely-ed” the flower to her heart’s content--when she have

hear, and long to dance to, the Guard’s band, suddenly a dark

vision rise to her mind’s eye--her husband _tête-à-tête_ with that evil

mystery, Arabella Crofton, obscure the sunshine of her spirit; the

rose-colour spectacle through which she have behold Vanity Fair fall

off; the serpent have enter in; and, for Alice Coverdale, Chiswick be

Paradise no long. thereupon she decide that Lord Alfred be a silly,

tiresome boy, and worry she with his childish nonsense; that Mr. Crane

be a fractious old idiot, who ought to be shut up in an appropriate

asylum; that Kate look bored and tired, which she do not wonder

at; that Horace D’Almayne be fitter for the Zoological than the

Horticultural Gardens, and deserve to be cage with the chimpanzee

without loss of time; and, finally (forget their separation have

result from a caprice of her own), that Harry be very unkind to stay

away from she in that way, with that hateful creature, Arabella Crofton,

whom she be sure he like after all, though he do pretend to treat she

so coldly.



then people begin to push and crowd, and dress become tumble; and

D’Almayne having leave the party to look for Harry and Miss Crofton, Mr.

Crane mislead they, and they fall into difficulty, and be very hot

and uncomfortable; and Alice quite pine to meet her husband, whose

sturdy arm would have support she, and whose tall figure and broad

shoulder would have force a way for she through the crowd. next, Lord

Alfred begin to tease she to give he a flower from her bouquet, and

get snub for his pain; until Horace D’Almayne, return, make

his report, viz., that, after much toil and trouble, he have at length

discover Miss Crofton and Mr. Coverdale, seat together in a

shady corner, apparently absorb in some deeply interesting topic

of conversation. this information, tally so exactly with her bad

fear, and find poor little Mrs. Coverdale both vex and tired,

very nearly produce a burst of tear, to avoid which pathetic

display she do that which the unfortunate first Mrs. Dombey fail

to effect--viz., she “make an effort,” and become, not exactly herself

again, but Alice Coverdale as she appear when enact the heartless

coquette. and this she do, poor child! not from a want, but from a

superfluity of heart. so, seek to read her truant husband a practical

moral lesson on the iniquity of charioteere dangerous damsel, in

common with whom he possess mysterious antecedent, she afford Lord

Alfred a “material guarantee” of her favour, in the shape of the flower

he have covet; and having thus firmly rivet his chain, ostensibly

pet and make much of her captive. this conduct on his wife’s part be

by no mean calculate to soothe Harry Coverdale, pain, ruffle,

and excite by his conversation with Arabella Crofton; and, without

reflect on the prudence or politeness of such a proceeding, he leave

his late companion to take care of herself, and stalk with stately

step, as of an offended lion, up to Lord Alfred Courtland, observe, in

a tone of dignified irony--



“I be much oblige to your Lordship for take such _extreme_ care of

Mrs. Coverdale, but will now relieve you from any further trouble on her

account: take my arm, Alice.”



Lord Alfred, strong in the possession of his rosebud, feel inclined to

resist, and murmur something about its be a pleasure rather than

a trouble; while Alice be just determine to support she swain, when

luckily she happen to read in Harry’s flash eye symptom of the

approach of an attack of his “quiet manner,” so hastily disengage she

arm, she place it within that of her husband, say, as she do so--



“I be not go to let this truant escape, now that I have catch he.

he deserve punishment--so I shall inflict my society upon he for the

rest of the afternoon, unless,” she add, with a glance which bewitch

Lord Alfred more completely than before, “I should find any stringent

necessity to exercise my feminine prerogative of change my mind.”



“your friend Mr. Coverdale’s method of relieve you of your fair charge

be more vigorous than polite, _mon cher_,” remark D’Almayne to Lord

Alfred, who, feel he be _de trop_, have leave the wed pair to their

own device. “however, I think I have obtain a clue, which I have only

to follow up, to arrive at a discovery which will help you on with

your pretty little lady-patroness, by render she more the _femme

incomprise_, and neglect wife than ever.”



“indeed!” be the reply; “what a clever fellow you be! I certainly owe

Coverdale one, for his manner to I just now be anything but nice. tell

I, what have you discover?”



“well, it seem nothing very remarkable at first; but many a large and

goodly oak have grow from as small an acorn. listen--the immaculate

Harry Coverdale have a private understanding with that dark-eyed gipsy,

Arabella Crofton; they be a great deal more intimate and confidential

in a _tête-à-tête_, than they allow themselves to appear in general

society. I must try and learn what pass between they in Italy, and

I think I can do so with very little trouble. I see a man in town

yesterday, Archie Campbell, who marry one of the Muir girl, with whom

the fair--or rather the dark--Arabella live as governess, when they

try to exchange their Scotch brogue for the _lingua Toscana_. she

go to Italy with they, and there meet Harry Coverdale--that I know as a

fact; for additional particular, I shall apply to the said Archie.”



“then do you think--do you conceive--do you mean to imply, in fact, that

Mr. Coverdale be attach to this Miss Crofton?” stammer Lord Alfred,

colour, as though he, and not Alice’s husband, be the suppose

delinquent.



“you always put thing into such plain word, _mon cher_; it be a

foolish habit, and the soon you can divest yourself of it the well,”

 be D’Almayne’s reply; “probably the mighty Nimrod, in flirt with

Miss Crofton, mean no more harm than you do by your platonic attachment

for his pretty wife. nevertheless, if such should prove the fact, and

you gently insinuate the same to _la belle Alice_, the chance be that

she will be kind than ever, to evince her gratitude for your having

render her jealous of her husband--not that you seem to require any

help--I see where that rosebud come from, _coquin_; but now you may, if

you will, render I a service; find your way to the entrance-gate, and

wait till my friend, Monsieur Guillemard, make his appearance--probably

you will find he wait there already--and having discover he,

bring he here.”



as the obedient lordling stroll away on his mission, the indefatigable

Horace gather a rose; then approach Kate Crane, he lisp in his

most dreamy and affected style--



“I’ve be search everywhere to find a rose of that peculiar tint

which might harmonise and yet contrast well with your dress; at length,

I be charm to say my effort have be successful. Mr. Crane, will you

favour I by present this rise to Madame? come through your hand,

I feel sure it will be accept.”



“no, positively; that be, really it will be much more fitting--if I may

be allow to say so--that, as you have be so obliging as to find it,

you should yourself present it. Mrs. Crane will, I feel convinced, be

happy to acknowledge your politeness, by accept a flower offer--if

I may be permit to say so--with such propriety and respect.”



D’Almayne appear about to avail himself of the permission which Mr.

Crane thus graciously accord he; when suddenly draw back, he

exclaim, “excuse I one minute; the thorn be so very sharp, I be

afraid to hand it to you without some protection against them;”--then,

take a slip of paper from his waistcoat pocket, he wind it round the

stem of the flower, and fix his eye with a meaning look on those

of Kate, he give she the rose. having do so, he begin talk to Mr.

Crane; and soon contrive, by a judicious selection of topic, chiefly

connect with the Stock Exchange, to engross that zealous Mammonite’s

attention. as soon as his wife perceive this to be the case, she

unroll the paper from the stem of the rose, and, glance at it

hastily, perceive the follow word write in Horace D’Almayne’s

neat hand: “give I five minute’ conversation--I will make the

opportunity, if you will avail yourself of it.” instantly crush it

in her hand, she rush into conversation with Arabella Crofton, on the

merit and demerit of certain new annual; which subject, skilfully

manage, last she until Lord Alfred Courtland return, arm in arm

with Monsieur Guillemard, well get up, more jaunty, and in yellower

kid glove than ever. this vivacious foreigner be instantly capture

by Horace, and desire to explain, “as he alone could do,” the peculiar

advantage of that famous investment in Terra Cotta preference bond,

as Mr. Crane have an odd £10,000 lie comparatively fallow--only at

three-and-a-half per cent--which he would be glad to put out well. so,

foolish avarice and clever roguery amble off together. then D’Almayne

contrive to dispatch Coverdale and his wife to look at a wonderful

speciman of the _Hypothetica Screamans_, and to saddle Lord Alfred with

Arabella Crofton, although that smite young aristocrat would have

prefer to have trot mildly about after Alice, like a pet lamb.

have dispose of these supernumerary, he as a matter of course

offer his arm to Kate, who have quietly acquiesce in his arrangement,

and follow at such a judicious distance that, although they still

belong to the party, in effect they enjoy all the advantage of a

_tête-à-tête_.



D’Almayne be the first to break silence. “this be most kind,” he say,

“and lead I to hope that you be at length beginning to understand

I--to perceive that my only wish be to act the part of a true friend

towards you. I have a conviction that I owe a duty to you, for I

often reflect with pain how large a share I have in bring about your

marriage.”



at these word Kate give a slight start, and her colour deepen: not

appear to observe these sign of agitation, her companion resume:



“you may not be aware that it be by my advice that Mr. Crane

transfer his attention from your cousin (whose affection for Mr.

Coverdale I perceive would oppose an effectual barrier to his wish)

to yourself:--my object in do so be twofold. Mr. Crane have show

I much kindness and attention; he be anxious to marry some one

whose presence would invest his home with an air of distinction and

attractiveness which his wealth could never bestow. the moment I behold

Miss Marsden, I feel that no one could do so more efficiently. thus,

from an impulse of gratitude towards Mr. Crane, I persuade he that it

would be in every way a most suitable and desirable match, and induce

he to make such an offer to Mr. Hazlehurst as should neutralize any

objection that gentleman might have have to your occupy the position

he have destine for his daughter. again mistake, in great measure,

both your character and that of Mr. Crane, I believe you would have

suit each other far well than I fear be the case: I fancy you

ambitious, and that the power which wealth would bestow would render you

not only content, but happy; while I trust marriage would develop in

Mr. Crane trait of generosity and tenderness of which I now be force

to confess his nature be incapable. have I guess this soon, I need

scarcely add, the respect and admiration I have always experience for

one so gifted as you be, would have prevent my advocate the match.

all that now remain for I be to compensate, as far as it be in my

power to do so, for any little failure in tact (believe I they be

nothing more) of which my excellent friend, Mr. Crane, may be guilty;

and I speak thus honestly and openly, in order that, appreciate my

motive, you may place full confidence in I, and thus enable me,”--and

here he sink his voice almost to a whisper--“to assist you in bear

the burden which I have unconsciously help to place upon you.”



“I must believe you mean kindly by I,” be Kate’s reply; “but you be

aware that, with I, deed tell well than word. have the application

be make?”



“yes.”



“and with what result? but I fear I need scarcely ask.”



“not a favourable one, I regret to say. Mr. Crane see Mrs. Leonard,

hope, I fancy, that she might have learn some tiding of her

husband; but when he become aware of the object of her visit, he not

only refuse to assist she, or to do anything for her child, but grow

irritated, reproach she with what he term her husband’s infamous

conduct, declare he have lose thousand of pound by his negligence, and

wound up by threaten that, if she ever set foot in his house again,

he would give she in charge to the police. when I visit she, I find

she in tear, and utterly heart-break by this failure of her last

hope.”



“you must go to she again,” exclaim Kate, eagerly; “tell she you

have mention her necessity to a lady of your acquaintance, who be

willing, and, thank God, able to assist she; give her money; find out

what she most require; devise some plan by which she may be enable to

support herself and educate her child. oh! if I can save this poor

family from ruin, it will be some little----” she check herself

abruptly, then continue: “Mr. Crane be most liberal to I, and allow

I more than I have the least occasion or desire to spend on myself--so

do not let they want for anything. and oh! be most careful--you say she

be a lady, poor thing!--be most careful not to wound her feeling. you

do not know how shrinkingly sensitive poverty make nature that be at

all refine.”



“I fear Mr. Crane’s word, speak, I dare say, under a very just feeling

of annoyance, both pain and irritated she,” return D’Almayne. “she

naturally draw a strong line between the fact that her husband have

be imprudent and unfortunate, and the insinuation that he have be

criminal. Mr. Crane, I grieve to say, appear to doubt the truth of

her statement, that Mr. Leonard be ignorant of his partner’s intend

flight and defalcation.”



“ungenerous! cruel!” murmur Kate, carry away by her excitement, and

forgetting, or perhaps at the moment scarcely heed, the fact that

D’Almayne’s quick ear be eagerly drink in these acknowledgment of

the estimation in which she hold her husband.



“I be most anxious to save you all trouble in this matter,” resume

D’Almayne; “but it would be a great satisfaction to I, and relieve I

of a responsibility for which I be scarcely fit, if you would not

object to visit Mrs. Leonard yourself: she be already most anxious to

see and thank the kind benefactress to whom I have inform she she be

indebted. be you once to talk to she, you would perceive the gentle

yet strong nature we have to deal with; you would learn her hope,

fear, and prospect, from her own lip, rather than through such

an unworthy interpreter as myself; you would see the interesting

children;--may I hope that you will consent?”



Kate pause--consider; but her answer demand a fresh chapter.









CHAPTER XXXVII.--A CONCESSION, and a “PARTIE QUARRÉE.”





the question we leave Kate Crane consider in the last chapter she

decide thus:--



“I should like to visit Mrs. Leonard,” she say slowly. “I feel the

truth of all you urge--but there be difficulty in the way; Mr. Crane

would greatly disapprove of such a proceeding on my part.”



“he need never know it,” suggest D’Almayne, in a voice little above a

whisper.



“he need not,” return Kate, calmly, “but I have since my marriage make

it a point of conscience never to do anything which I should object to

Mr. Crane’s hearing of; I still consider the rule a good one, and be

disinclined to break through it.”



“do not your sensitive conscience,” rejoin D’Almayne, “lead you to

refine rather too much, until, adhere to the form of goodness, you in

a great degree lose the substance, and thus, by a chivalrous scruple of

never disobey your husband, miss an opportunity of do real

good, by which you would neutralise the injury which Mr. Crane’s

_peculiarity_ may otherwise inflict upon this unfortunate family? I

think, if you reflect on this for a minute, your excellent sense will

convince you that your amiable but romantic scruple be fallacious.”



Kate do reflect, and apparently her conviction assume the shape

D’Almayne have predict, for she reply in a less assured voice than

that in which she have formerly address him--



“Mr. D’Almayne, you have speak more honestly and openly to-day than you

have ever do before, and I will treat you with equal frankness. you

be acquaint with Mr. Crane before I have ever hear his name; you

appear to know he well; you have allude generally to his good point,

and have point out his weak one with equal talent and perspicuity. I

neither admit nor deny your statement--but, in the individual instance

before we, I believe that you be right. you have be very kind in this

matter; you first introduce this poor Mrs. Leonard to my notice; you

have since take much disintereste trouble on her account; you possess

great tact, and have divine the happiness it afford I to assist

those who, from misfortune and poverty, have fall from the rank of

gentlewomen;--therefore, in this matter, I feel you have a claim to work

with I; for the first time, therefore, I will repose confidence in you.

I wish to visit this poor lady--how be I to accomplish it without my

husband’s knowledge?”



Horace D’Almayne have win his point, Horace D’Almayne be happy! yet he

do not clap his hand, neither do he hurrah wildly, nor dance a lively

measure around Kate Crane, whom he believe he have circumvent in a

different manner; but he force his imperturbable countenance into an

expression of philanthropic benevolence and gratitude, and arrange with

Mrs. Crane a plan by which, during her husband’s daily worship in the

temple of Mammon his god--an edifice more familiarly know in the good

city of London as the Stock Exchange--she should visit unfortunate Mrs.

Leonard, and witness with her own eye how justly the prince of this

world (who be identical with the monarch of a low kingdom still)

distribute his subject’ property.



about this time all the member of this disunited party assemble, and

jointly and severally end their day’s enjoyment (?) by return home

tired, deject, and suffer more or less from that ailment which

defy those guinea-pig, “the faculty”--an ailment as rife in St.

James’s as be cholera and smallpox within the precinct of St.

Giles’s--an ailment which, thank to those bitter curse, the form,

ceremony, requirement, and prejudice of society, afflict and hang

heavily on many an honest man and love woman--an ailment indigenous

even in our glorious constitution, and which have as many _aliase_ as

shape, the spleen, _ennui_, but true name of all, the _Heart-ache!_



“_Ogni Medaglia ha il suo reverso_,” there be no rule without its

exception! Horace D’Almayne be the exception to this particular

rule--he be not trouble with heart-ache, because, in the metaphysical

sense of the word, he do not possess a heart; but nature have make it up

to he by give he a very clear head, and thus it reasoned:--



“yes, my pretty Kate, _tout va bien_; you have grow civil, almost

kind--not yet affectionate, but that be to come. yet she be clever;

doubt, suspect me!--what child woman be, even clever woman; once

appeal to their feeling, their impulse--bah! their reason lie captive

before you--they be puppet in your hand. _ah! c’est bien drôle cette

petite existence ici bas!_ for the rest, all go well; the beautiful

Kate shall compromise herself--the millionaire shall open wider his

purse string--the bank win for I--the little Alfred play my game--_courage,

Horace!_ thy star be in the ascendant, you will die a rich man

yet!”



*****



the morning after the Horticultural Fête, Coverdale suggest to his

wife that they have, in his opinion, spend sufficient time and money in

the gay metropolis, and that agricultural and manorial duty call he

to the country forthwith; but Alice plead so earnestly for only one

week more of dissipation, with Lady Tattersall Trottemout’s _soirée

dansante_ at the end of it, that Harry could not find it in his heart

to refuse she. Scarcely have he yield the point, when a letter arrive

from Tom Rattleworth, Magistrate, and Master of Fox-hound, to inform

he that, owe to the baneful influence of a certain grand seigneur in

the neighbourhood, it be propose to enclose a common and turn a

road, which would destroy a favourite fox cover, and give Coverdale

half-a-mile far to drive to the near railway-station--that the

matter be to be decide at the next meeting of Magistrates--that he

(Thomas) have strive tooth and nail to get up an opposition, in which he

have be tolerably successful, and that he consider it only require

Coverdale’s presence to prevent the evil altogether. thus urge, Harry

have but one course to pursue, viz., commend his wife to Mrs. Crane’s

safe custody, and start for Coverdale Park forthwith, promise to

return in time for “Lady Tat. Trott.’s benefit,” as he be please

to term it. Alice at first oppose his going, but when she find the

question resolve itself into one of these alternative, either that

she must let he go alone, or give up her ball and accompany he, she

opposition cease. so Harry pack his carpet-bag and depart--and

the hour roll by on their patent noiseless wheel, until the time

appoint for that notable solemnity, Lady Tattersall Trottemout’s

_soirée dansante_, arrive.



on that day Lord Alfred Courtland invite to a quiet dinner, at his

comfortable bachelor lodging, Horace D’Almayne, Monsieur Guillemard,

and a youth who, because he be in every particular Lord Alfred’s exact

opposite, be an especial crony of his.



Jack Beaupeep, _ætatis_ twenty-five, be a clerk in a public office

with a salary of £150 per annum, on which, by mean of his talent,

he contrive to live at the rate of----anything under a thousand. as,

however, we shall not have very much to do with he in the course of

this history, we will spare the reader further detail by sum up

his character in the two expressive word, “fast” and “funny.” everybody

know a fast, funny man; and his be a bad case of the complaint. at

a quarter to eight, P.M., on the day in question, this excellent young

buffoon of private life betook himself to Lord Alfred’s lodging, and

find himself first in the field, look around with a practised eye

for the good mean of turn the situation to comic effect. first he

perceive a valuable statuette of Venus, as she appear before the

discovery of the art of dress-making, for which his innate sense of

propriety lead he to improvise a petticoat, by mean of a doyley and

a small portion of the red tape of old England, purloin from her

Britannic Majesty’s store that morning, and secrete by the delinquent

for any possible exigency of practical jesting. having attire this

young lady to his satisfaction, he obligingly bestow on she a real

Havannah cigar, which, thrust through an opening leave by the sculptor

in she clench hand, with the end rest against her ambrosial lip,

resemble a speaking-trumpet, and give she that “ship-ahoy!” kind of

appearance with which early engraver be pleased to endow Fame. he

then write and water on the pedestal of the statuette thus embellish

a label, bear the inscription, “_Eugénie_, Empress of the French,”

 murmur to himself, “delicate little compliment to the illustrious

foreigner who be come.” next he avail himself of a pair of

boxing-glove; “unearthing,” as he term it, the roll insert in

two of the dinner napkin, and substitute for they these elementary

instructor in the noble art of self-defence; and, lastly, espy the

cruet-stand, he have just time to reverse the content of the pepper and

sugar caster, and confuse _all_ the sauce, when to he enter Lord

Alfred Courtland.



this young nobleman’s appearance have considerably change since first

we have the pleasure of describe he. by abstruse study, and unflagge

attention to the saying and doing of man-about-town, he have acquire

many noble attribute--he could lounge and dawdle, and walk with a

pert yet lazy roll in his gait, as of a tipsy dancing-master, or of a

cock-sparrow afflict with sciatica; he could lisp as though his very

tongue be too about-town-ish to speak plain, unadulterate English;

he could make play with his eye half shut, like a timid girl, or stare

with they offensively wide open, like an insolent coxcomb, though he be

not quite perfect in this last manœuvre as yet. also, his clothe be

large and loose enough for himself and half another man-about-town

besides; and he have a bunch of baby’s toy, model in gold, dangle

from his watch-chain--lilliputian house furniture, and a gun, and a

sword, and a pistol to match, and a little man in armour with impossible

feature, accompany by a horrid little skull of the same after his

decease, with two of his little golden marrow-bone cross under it, as

if they be say their prayer; there be likewise a ridiculous fish,

which wag its tail, and a fox’s mask, as it be “know” to term the

physiognomy of that astute quadrupedal martyr; the whole to conclude

with a limp and jointed Punchinello, or Tomfool, as a _pendant _(in

every sense of the word) to the fool of large growth who wear these

childish absurdity. thus attire and adorn, Lord Alfred Courtland

withdraw one white hand from a pocket of his liberal trouser, and,

lay it on Beaupeep’s shoulder, with a want of energy, general

lassitude, and fish-out-of-water-ishness of manner, which do he

infinite credit, drawl forth--



“ah! my dear fellar! this be very good of you, to come at such short

notice!”



“not at all, not at all,” be the brisk reply, for Beaupeep do not go

in for, or revere, the all-to-piece style, but rather make it a theme

for playful jesting; “when I get your invite, I just scribble off

a line to Palmerston to say I’d dine with he to-morrow instead of

to-day.”



Lord Alfred quietly raise his eyebrow, while, nothing abash,

Beaupeep continued--



“it’s very jolly to be on those term with a man like ‘Pam.,’ and I

consider it quite sufficient recompense for my unwearye devotion to my

public duty.”



“it really willn’t do with I, my dear Jack,” interrupt Lord Alfred, in

a tone of affectionate remonstrance; “reflect how long we’ve know each

other!”



“by the way,” recommence Jack, ignore the interruptional rebuke,

“talk of ‘Pam.’ put I in mind of the Foreign Office, which, not

unnaturally, lead to the inquiry of who may be the illustrious ‘Mossoo’

who be to make our fourth to-day?”



“Monsieur Guillemard! oh, he be a very gentlemanly and intelligent

Frenchman, and a particular friend of Horace D’Almayne’s.”



“but _what_ be he?” continue Beaupeep, pertinaciously; “be he a noble

political exile, or a _perruquier_ from the _Palais Royal_, who cann’t

meet his liability? do he gain a frugal living by impart a

knowledge of his native tongue in six lesson, at half-a-crown each?

or----”



“Hush! here he be,” interrupt Lord Alfred, as a smart rat-tat-tat at

the house-door announce an arrival; “he have something to do with the

fund, and the financial interest, and the Rothschilds, and all that

mysterious pound, shilling, and pence business, in regard to which I

have, I be afraid, no clearly define idea.”



“except to spend ’em first, and make your governor shell-out

afterwards, you lucky beggar you!” be the plainly audible aside, as the

servant announce Monsieur Guillemard and Mr. D’Almayne.



after the ceremony of introduce the volatile Jack to the new comer

have be perform, that individual immediately attach himself, and

devote his conversation to Monsieur Guillemard, whom he persist in

address as “Mossoo le Comte,” and whom he seem to imagine just

catch in some very foreign country indeed, and ignorant of the simple

english manner and custom; a delusion to which that gentleman’s

limit acquaintance with Bindley Murray’s, or, indeed, any other

british grammar, lend some slight colouring.



“I think I observe, Mossoo le Comte, that you come in a Hansom cab?”

 remark Jack.



“yer, we promenade in a ver handsome carb, a handsome hor also; you

shall drive some much more handsome hor in your street than with we,”

 be the reply.



“the native british cab be a great and noble product of the liberal

institution of this free and happy land,” return Jack, oratorically;

“if an Englishman choose to walk, an enlightened legislature not only

allow he to do so, but provide he with a granite pavement to walk

upon; if he choose to ride, the legislature have a cab await his

slight wink--a mere contraction of the eyelid, Mossoo le Comte,

obtain for the weary Englishman a luxurious vehicle, a swift and

steady horse, and a skilful driver, prepare to convey he one mile

in any conceivable direction, for the trifling outlay of sixpence

sterling.”



“with the advantage of study the _patois_ of Billingsgate in for the

money, when the cabman return thank for his fare,” add D’Almayne.



Jack Beaupeep favour he with a glance of inquiry which, if it have

be frame in word, would have run thus--“Are you a knave or a fool?”

 apparently decide in favour of the former hypothesis, he resumed--



“the additional attraction to which you so perspicuously allude, my dear

sir, involve yet another striking peculiarity--viz., this driver,

who so carefully conduct you through the crowded thoroughfare of our

colossal metropolis, be no servile hireling, no parasitical serf to

crouch at your foot, but a man, sir--a freeborn Briton--with as much

vest right in ‘Rule Britannia’ as yourself. Sir! when a dissatisfied

cabman allude to my eye and limb, I open widely those asperse

optic, proudly draw up those vituperate limb, and rejoice that he and

I be fellow-countryman!”



“my dear Jack, we’re not upon the husting; we have none of we the

slight intention of come in for anywhere; and dinner have be

serve for the last five minute,” suggest his host, mildly.



favour he with a melodramatic scowl, which, at “Sadler’s Wells” or

the “Victoria,” would, in theatrical parlance, have “bring down the

house,” Jack exclaimed--



“be it thus a haughty aristocracy strive to trample on the honest poor

man! it be not well in ye, my lord, and before an illustrious foreigner,

too; alas, my country!”--then perceive that Guillemard be regard

he with a glance which evince extreme doubt as to his sanity, that

D’Almayne be look supercilious, and Lord Alfred annoy at his

absurdity, Jack experience the proud conviction that he have attain

his object--viz., to astonish, confuse, and discomfit everybody.

having do so, he drop the heroic, and condescend to make himself

agreeable after the fashion of ordinary mortal, which, as he be really

clever and well-inform, he succeed in do to a degree that, in

great measure, efface his previous misconduct from the recollection of

his associate. he preface his reformation, however, by contrive to

seat Guillemard by one of the boxing-glove napkin, a manœuvre which

elicit from that perplex foreigner the exclamation, “_Mais que

diable!_ vot shall zie be?” and a reprove “Jack, you idiot, how can

you!” from Lord Alfred, who be equally amuse and scandalise at his

friend’s absurdity. but a Frenchman’s tact be seldom long at fault;

and by the time Guillemard have extricate the boxing-glove from its

envelope, he continued--



“_ah, je comprend_, I apprehend! Monsieur Jacques Pipbo! _il est

gai, il est farceur_, he vos play vot you call von practicable joke,

_n’est-ce-pas, Milor?--bien comique!_ ver fonney, ha! ha!”



so, harmony be establish, they eat, drank, and be merry;

Champagne, Moselle, Rhine wine, french wine, wine with name we know

but cannot pronounce, wine with name we do not know and could not

spell if we do, be produce, and do justice to, during dinner and

dessert; and then they quietly settle down to Claret at 80s. the dozen,

which taste well, as they agree, out of tumbler; Fribourg’s fine

cigar also make their appearance and be not neglect; and for some

time these four lord of the creation enjoy life undisturbed. but

frenchman seldom sit long over their wine. D’Almayne have too many

scheme, which require a cool head to carry they out, to venture to

inflame his brain with the juice of the grape; and by ten o’clock Lord

Alfred propose a hand at piquet, to while away an hour or so, until

it should be time to adjourn to Lady Tattersall Trottemout’s ball,

to which Mentor and his pupil be invite; so Guillemard and his

host begin to play, Jack Beaupeep and his companion watch they,

and bet half-crown on the vary chance of the game. at first,

fortune seem inclined to befriend Lord Alfred, for he win three time

consecutively; and Jack, who, as he observe, be resolve “to back

the thorough-breed colt,” realise capital to the amount of

seven-and-sixpence.



“_ah! bah!_ Horace, _mon cher!_ you shall bet wis I _contre moi-même!_

I cannot play for a so little stake, he do not agree wis I!”

 exclaim Monsieur Guillemard, toss down the card pettishly.



“let we double they, Monsieur,” begin Lord Alfred, eagerly; “I be just

go to propose it when you speak; nothing be more _ennuyant_ than

play for inadequate stake.”



“_Mais oui!_ you have reason, my Lord. Horace, _mon ami_, mix I _de

Veau sucrée_ wis a Ouinam Laque ice in he; I have thirst; he make hot

this evening.”



“not a bad idea, only I’ve a well one,” rejoin Lord Alfred; “brew

some Sherry-cobbler, Jack; ring the bell, and order the material: it’s

your deal, Monsieur Guillemard.”



Sherry-cobbler be _not_ a safe thing to play piquet upon, especially

when your opponent confine himself to _eau sucrée_. Lord Alfred lose,

grow excited, double the stake again and lose, treble they and win,

then play on recklessly against a run of ill-luck, until D’Almayne

interfere.



“it be twelve o’clock, Alfred, _mon cher_; we shall be late for Lady

Tatt.’s.”



“----Lady Tatt.!” be the uncomplimentary reply; “I shall not go.”



D’Almayne lean over he, and observe in a whisper, “you forget _la

belle Alice_ be expect you,” draw the card from his reluctant hand.



rise sulkily, Lord Alfred walk with a slightly unsteady step to a

writing-table, take pen and ink, and hastily trace a few word, hand

the paper to Monsieur Guillemard--it be a cheque for £500!



“ring for the brougham, D’Almayne,” he continue; “Monsieur Guillemard,

you must give I my revenge at an early opportunity; good night,

Jack;” then turn away with a laugh, as he perceive that youthful

legislator, who have “go in” for Sherry-cobbler rather too zealously,

fast asleep on the sofa, he retire to his dressing-room to remove, as

far as he be able, the outward effect of wine and excitement.



as he quit the apartment, D’Almayne, after a hasty glance at the

“use up” Jack, draw Guillemard aside, and speak French, say in an

eager whisper, “you be much too precipitate, and will ruin everything;

what could persuade you to win so large a sum from he at one sit?”



“you conceive it that I be too impressed! _Regarde!_ one give to I this

_billet_ at the dinner-table,” be the reply.



hastily snatch it, D’Almayne read as follows:--



“---- Street, Eleven, p.m.



“Prince Ratrapski, the russian nobleman, have be play deeply; have

have a run of unparalleled luck, and _break the bank_; unless you can

come by £500 immediately, there will be an unpleasant exposure, and

D’Almayne and yourself will be, before morning, the tenant of a

debtor’s prison, with



“your devoted,



“Le Roux.”









CHAPTER XXXVIII.--SOME of the joys of our dancing day.



Lady Tattersall Trottemout live in the Brompton and Kensington region,

and know everybody. her deceased papa have walk into Manchester

some fifty year since, with a good head on his shoulder, and

fourpence-halfpenny in his breech-pocket. be tired with his walk,

he sit down in Manchester, and rest there for the space of forty

year, during which time, by a process peculiar to that city, his

fourpence-halfpenny grow into an hundred and forty thousand pound. unto

he be bear, in lawful wedlock, one only daughter, the subject of the

present brief memoir, who, on his retirement to “t’ Oud Churchyard” (as,

in his Lancashire dialect, he be accustom to denominate his final

resting-place in the burial-ground of the Collegiate Church), inherit

the fourpence-halfpenny and its compound interest; with which, when

her mourning for her father be end, she purchase Sir Tattersall

Trottemout. this noble baronet, who be by no mean worth the price

she give for he, have be essentially a fast man, and have run through

everything he could lay his “blood-red hand” upon--his own fortune and

the fortune of several of his relation include--and when they be

all go and spend, he run through his reputation; which last “rapid

act” do not take he long, as that “bubble” be not as “wide as a

church-door, nor deep as a draw-well,” when he begin upon it. thus,

find himself under a cloud and in difficulty--the only thing he

have yet encounter which he could not run through (the good old day

of “pink” one’s tailor instead of pay he be unfortunately

past)--Sir T. T. feel that his time be come, and that he must prepare

his mind for another--that is, a married--life. so, _ætatis_ forty-five,

he go into dock, dye his hair and whisker, purchase a new set

of tooth, lay in a stock of patent leather boot, and run down to

Manchester to captivate an heiress. the respectable owner of the

enlarge and embellish fourpence-halfpenny have, at that epoch, be

about one year _under_ the turf which his future son-in-law have be

_on_ for above twenty; and his orphan daughter, of sweet nineteen,

be immediately smite and wound by the aristocratic appearance and

distinguish manner of the break-down title blackleg who seek

her... fortune. she, be then a simple-minded, honest girl, absurd

as it may appear, love the creature; and, despite the advice of several

kind-hearted, strong-head, fearfully vulgar old man, who be her

trustee, guardian, legal adviser, &c. &c. (policeman, so to

speak, appoint by the lamented deceased to prevent his develop

fourpence-halfpenny being prematurely reduce to its pristine element),

this young lady vow she would marry Sir Tattersall Trottemout--and do

so. but, as the baronet’s talent for run through any amount of

cash be rumour even at Manchester, the ancient policeman tie up the

fourpence-halfpenny so tightly that nobody could manufacture duck

and drake with it--not even Sir Tat. Trott.: so, after a few abortive

attempt, that ornament to his order give up his evil course, and

settle down quietly on cigar, brandy and water, and whist with

half-crown point--a notable example of the reformatory power of

matrimony. his lady-wife go through the usual agreeable process of

awake from “love’s young dream,” and discover that, after the

manner of Caliban, she have, in her simplicity,--



                   “make a wonder of a poor drunkard,”



she, like a sensible woman, resolve to put up with her bad bargain,

keep her husband in respectable order, and create or discover some fresh

interest in life for herself. in accordance with this determination, she

restrict the marital cigar and brandy and water to certain definite

limit; test several phase of London society; and then take her line,

and choose her associate accordingly. be an intellectual woman, and

have literary taste up to a certain point, she affect the society

of artist of all class, and in every department of art. thus, at she

_soiree_, you might meet literary man of various specie: historian,

novelist, journalist, critic, _et hoc genus omne_; painter,

sculptor, musician; the lead actor of the day, male and

female,--in fact, all the celebrity whom the London season delighteth

to honour. but, know that talent require an intelligent audience,

Lady Tattersall Trottemout associate a certain proportion of the

_profanum vulgus_ to worship her collect divinity. her party,

therefore, soon became note as the most agreeable of their kind; and to

one of these meeting, in which dancing be to be the chief feature of

the evening, be our friend in Park Lane invite. Harry have promise

Alice that, if it be possible, he would return to escort she to

this notable gathering; however, on the appoint evening, ten o’clock

arrive, but no Coverdale. Alice be rather frightened and considerably

annoyed, but Kate persuade she that there be no just cause for alarm;

and so, leave a note for Harry, beg he to join they, if he should

arrive in time to make it worth while to do so, they proceed to the

“spacious mansion” of Lady Tattersall Trottemout.



for some time, little Mrs. Coverdale be sufficiently amuse by

observe the appearance, manner, and custom of the various

notability, as they be point out to she by no less a personage

than her hostess, who, attract by the simple beauty of her new

acquaintance, and the evident pleasure and interest she take in all

that be go on around she, actually devoted to her ten minute of

the valuable time in which, on such occasion, a clever mistress of the

house be expect, and actually contrive, to say and do something

civil to an hundred and fifty human being, all prepare to magnify

any accidental neglect into an intended slight, and to resent it

accordingly. but, ere ten minute have well elapse, an illustrious

stranger arrive, who be so intensely foreign that he could not be

prevail upon to speak or understand any language of which the deep

philologist present be able to make head or tail, and who, in his

consequent bewilderment, have seat himself on the music-stool, with

his back towards the key-board of the pianoforte--thereby establish a

complete blockade of that harmonious and indispensable instrument, which

no representation in French, german, or italian, could induce he to

relinquish: so a breathless female aide-de-camp, in flaxen ringlet

and white muslin, hurry up to report this frightful dilemma to

the commandress-in-chief, who, with the great presence of mind,

dispatch she to summon Count Cacklewitz, the young hungarian patriot,

who, it be generally believe, could speak everything, even his

own language, and then hasten in person to raise the siege of the

piano-forte. Alice, thus deserted, fall into the hand of a tall, gaunt,

blue woman, rejoice in a red nose and a long fluent tongue, who begin

to talk high art to she, and confuse she about transcendentalism and

Carlyle,--_the_ Oxford Graduate (viz., Turner’s single and singular

disciple, wonderful Mr. Buskin), and pre-Raphaelism,--the meaning of

Tennyson, when he condescend to be obscure (for he _can_ write real

poetry, which “he who run may read” and feel),--and of the dark

Brownings, and Macaulay and the romance of history, and many other

hackneye pseudo-literary topic of the day, until our unlucky little

heroine lapse into that state of mental incapacity usually describe as

not know whether one be stand on one’s head or one’s heel. then

begin vocal music, which mercifully silence Alice’s strong-minded

persecutor; and a rather raffish baritone gentleman, who want shearing

dreadfully, and be all voice, eye, and feather, like a lean bird,

accost a singularly hard-feature, middle-aged german lady, as “oh!

thou belove one!” to which she make an appropriately tender _soprano_

reply; and the company listen with much forbearance, for quite ten

minute, to the united affection of this interesting couple, detail

to an accompaniment now rapturous, now pathetic, at the end of which

period they both suddenly exalt their voice, bellow their love at

each other in one final outburst of sympathetic insanity, and subside

into a refreshing silence. then a young lady in a pink sash inform

the company that her brain be on fire, her heart consume, and her

digestive organ generally in a state of spontaneous combustion, because

her fatherland writhe in the grasp of tyrants--“tra la, tra lira

la!”--which unpleasant state of affair be much applaud by hairy

exile, with microscopic washing bill, which they never pay, and a

monomania in regard to freedom, which they never obtain, but which have

keep they in hot water (the only water they patronize) from their youth

upwards. lastly, a very mild young gentleman of England excite himself

about some “Rivar! rivar! shine rivar!” into which pellucid stream he

keep put his foot “deep and deep still,” until every one be so

sorry for he, that the whole party appear on the verge of hysteric,

and be force to conceal their emotion behind fan, flounce

pocket-handkerchief, and white-gloved hand. then the votary of

Terpsichore stand at ease upon their light fantastic toe (except in

the case of tightly-shod martyr), and polking be the order of the

night--at which period Alice look about and wonder what have become

of Lord Alfred Courtland, who have say a great deal on the subject of

the delight he expect in dance with she, and have engage her hand

for the first polka.



now, whether any strictly moral reader, with that bad opinion of poor

human nature which very strict morality usually induce, have decide

that “every woman be at heart a rake,” and believe our little

heroine about to prove herself a “dreadful creature,” and transfer she

affection from her lawful husband to her unlawful admirer, we do

not know; but if any reader have set his (or her) heart on such a

consummation, we be sorry to be oblige to inform he that he be

mistaken. Alice consider Lord Alfred a good-natured agreeable boy,

whose conversation serve to amuse she, and to whose society she have

become accustom; she would a thousand time rather have talk to

Harry at any time, but Harry be not always attainable--indeed, the

chance be generally against she see anything of he from breakfast

till dinner-time, and then Lord Alfred become a very good and safe

substitute.



but the first polka be over, and a _valse à deux temp_ follow

it, neither of which Alice dance, and still no Harry, no Lord Alfred

appear; and in despair she be oblige to say yes to a heavy cornet in

the Life-Guards, who be big enough to eat she, and polke like a

polite young elephant. glad to escape without be squeeze to death

or trample under foot by this ponderous young warrior, Alice have just

find a seat, when D’Almayne and Lord Alfred lounge in; the latter

immediately join she, and claim her promise to dance with he; but

Alice be tired and bored, and feel that it be in some degree owe

to he that she have become so, and that he ought to have be there

soon, she reply coldly--



“I promise to reserve the first dance for you, my lord, but the first

dance have be over some time, and several other have follow; I do

not feel disposed to dance at present.”



of course, Lord Alfred endeavour to excuse himself, and when Alice

decline dancing, say, “very well, then he should sit still too--all

the night, if she please, for he certainly should not dance with any

one else.” so, after she have tease he until he very nearly lose the

little good temper which the event of the early part of the evening

have leave he, she take compassion on he, and dance with he twice

consecutively; but when he urge she to do so a third time, she refuse;

and on his press she, tell he plainly, that as her husband be away

she feel bind to be more than usually particular, and that it be not

_étiquette_ to dance the whole evening with one gentleman; at which

rebuff his lordship be pleased to take offence, and lead she to a

seat, he bow and leave she. desert by his lady-love, and swindle

out of his money by his pseudo-friend, this victimise young nobleman

look about for his protector and adviser--at once patron and

parasite--Horace D’Almayne, but for some time without success; when at

length he do discover he, he be engage in such an earnest private

conversation with some gentleman unknown, that Lord Alfred feel it

would be ill-breed to interrupt they; accordingly, he lounge through

the room, resist several introduction to “great heiress” and

“lovely girl in London,” all declare to be die to dance with

he, wander listlessly into the refreshment-room, drink a tumbler of

Champagne and sodawater, and be think seriously of turn sulky and

go home to bed, when D’Almayne seize he by the arm, exclaiming--



“Alfred, _mon cher_, where have you hide yourself? I’ve be hunt

for you for the last half hour. why have you leave _la belle_ Coverdale?”



“oh, yes! that be good! look for I, indeed, when I pass you twice

close enough almost to brush against your elbow, and you never even see

I, so engrossed be you plot treason with some party unknown,” be

the captious reply.



“Ungrateful! when it be for your interest I be exert myself,”

 return D’Almayne, reproachfully; “but you do not explain why you have

quit _la belle_ Alice; you really be not sufficiently attentive; no

pretty woman like to be neglect.”



“she’s a little fickle, heartless coquette, and I’ll let she see that

I’m not so completely her slave as she appear to imagine,” answer

Lord Alfred, snappishly, at the same time fill his glass with

Champagne; “she refuse to dance with I more than twice because it

be not _étiquette_, and she wish to be extra particular because

her husband be not here. I don’t think he’d overwhelm she with his

attention if he be, unless he mean to alter very much. no: the fact

be, she be out of humour, and choose to vent it on I; it would just

serve she right if I be to go home, and leave she to her own device.”



“do nothing of the kind, _mon cher_; but listen to I, and--excuse I,

but don’t drink any more Champagne, or you’ll do something absurd; your

comic friend brew that Sherry-cobbler too strong. go quietly back to

the Coverdale; try and persuade she to dance, but if she refuse,

show no annoyance, and get she to allude again to her husband: then

carelessly and incidentally, as if you have no design in what you be

say, suggest that she would scarcely be so particular, if she

know what a naughty boy he have be in Italy, and having excite she

curiosity, tell she the following little anecdote.”



as a bevy of man enter the refreshment-room at that moment, D’Almayne,

link his arm with that of Lord Alfred, lead he aside, and make to he

a communication, the nature of which will appear in the due course of

this history. Lord Alfred seem surprised, and, to his credit be it

speak, even pain, by the information thus afford he; and when

D’Almayne have conclude, his auditor remain a minute or so bury in

thought, then he ask abruptly--



“you be _sure_ there be still some clandestine understanding between

they--you be quite certain?”



“I be as certain as a man can be of any clandestine proceeding to which

he be not a party,” be the reply; “you be aware of what I observe

on the occasion of the Horticultural Fête. I now relate to you the

antecedent; you be no long a child, but sufficiently a man of the

world to draw your own deduction.” the adroit flattery on the weak

point tell: faith in truth and honour would argue a want of knowledge of

life; so with a slight laugh, assumptive of an omniscience in evil, he

reply, “I be willing to give he the benefit of a doubt, if it be

possible; but, as you say, the thing be clear enough; and now, how be

this to advantage I?”



“do you ask?” be the surprised rejoinder; “I think you tell I just

now that the cruel fair one have snub you, by throw her duty to

her husband at your head; so it occur to my simplicity that this

information, properly apply, would prevent a recurrence of such

rebuff.”



“but surely you would never have I tell _she_, and her own husband the

hero of the adventure!” expostulate Lord Alfred.



“listen, _mon cher_, one moment,” be D’Almayne’s reply, speak in a

low, impressive voice; “I do not wish you to follow _any_ particular

line of conduct; I have no interest to serve, no desire to gratify, by

your doing or abstain from anything; but when you tell I you desire

to gain such and such a social position, and ask my advice as to the

good way of attain your wish, I, as your friend, point out the

mean to you--it be for you to judge whether they be such as you choose

to employ. you must now excuse I: I see some old acquaintance of mine,

to whose memory I be anxious to recall myself.”



“then you really advise I to tell she!” exclaim Lord Alfred, seize

D’Almayne’s arm in his eagerness and indecision.



“I really advise nothing of the kind, _mon cher_,” be the reply; “I

have already caution you against that abrupt plain-speaking of your;

you _should_ divest yourself of that rustic habit. you could scarcely

sin more deeply against good taste and good breeding than to go to _la

belle_ Coverdale, and bring a rail accusation against her husband,

nor could you divine a plan more certain to frustrate your hope

and wish; but if, grieve over her misplaced confidence, you

philanthropically incline to hint to she that he be scarcely the

immaculate ascetic her imagination depict, _c’est tout autre chose!_

and now you must excuse I;” and as he speak, he gently free his

coat-sleeve from Lord Alfred’s grasp, and regard he with a

half-sarcastic, half-compassionate, but wholly irritate smile, he

turn and quit the spot.



thus leave to his own reflection, which be none of the most agreeable,

Lord Alfred pause for a few moment in indecision; then, with a hand

tremulous from excitement, again replenish his glass, toss down the

Champagne, and return to the dancing-room.



during her admirer’s absence, Alice have, for want of some more

interesting occupation, be converse with Arabella Crofton, use

all her skill to try to elicit some particular of her acquaintance with

Harry in Italy, in which endeavour she have be most adroitly foil

by the quiet self-possession of the _ci-devant_ governess, who tell she

most readily all she do _not_ care to learn, and nothing that she do.

as Lord Alfred approach, an individual be introduce to Miss Crofton,

who desire the honour of her hand for the next polka, which desire

that young lady obligingly gratify, thus afford his lordship an

opportunity of seat himself by Alice, of which he instantly avail

himself.



“it be never right to believe in a fair lady’s nay,” he begin, “so I

have return to afford you an opportunity of confess your change of

mind with a good grace; come, they be just go to begin a new polka,

let we take our place.”



“if lady do always change their mind, I be go to be the

interesting exception which prove the rule,” be Alice’s reply.



“how provokingly and unnaturally obstinate you be to-night, Mrs.

Coverdale! you pretend to be fond of dancing, and yet, because I ask

you, you resolve to sit still!”



“I have already tell you my reason,” rejoin Alice; “in Mr. Coverdale’s

absence I do not choose to dance the whole evening with any one

gentleman.”



“what a pattern wife you be!” be the reply; “you give up your own

amusement, and destroy all my pleasure, out of regard for the ghost of

a scruple, which I dare say have never enter Mr. Coverdale’s brain;

really, the patient Griselda be nothing compare to you.”



Alice be annoy by his pertinacity, and, consider this speech

impertinent, be about to repeat her refusal in term which would have

enlighten his lordship very considerably on these point, when it

flash across she that he might have take rather too much Champagne;

and the idea having occur to she, his flushed face and excited manner

confirm it. have sufficient like for he to wish to prevent he

from make himself ridiculous, she good-naturedly resolve to engross

his conversation herself, and, aware of what she conceive to be the

true state of the case, not to take offence at anything he might say,

intend to read he a lecture on the following day. in accordance with

this resolution, she replied--



“I consider it a great compliment to be compare to the patient Grisel,

more particularly as I be not of opinion that she and I have very many

quality in common. by the way,” she continue, seek to change the

subject, and take the first idea that occur to she, “what do you

think of the lady whose chair you be occupy? I have never ask your

opinion of Miss Arabella Crofton.”



the question be a most unfortunate one. Alice’s continue refusal to

dance with he have annoy Lord Alfred, and wound his vanity; the

reason of her refusal be her absurd devotion (as he consider it) to

her husband; and now she, as it be, hold the cup of revenge to his

lip by the question she have ask he. up to this point his well

nature have struggle with the temptation successfully, but now it have

acquire an additional strength, and overcome he.



“I wonder you should care to know _my_ idea on the subject,” he say;

and as he proceed to work out Horace D’Almayne’s suggestion, his

tone and manner unconsciously assume a resemblance to that excellent

young man’s sarcastic and suggestive delivery: “Miss Crofton be merely

a recent and very slight acquaintance of mine; you should apply to Mr.

Coverdale--_he_ could tell you many much more interesting particular of

her history than I be able to communicate, if he be willing to do so.”



all temptation to do thing foolish or wrong be orthodoxly suppose

to come from the Prince of Darkness; if it be so, the fact speak very

highly for the intellectual capacity of that sable potentate, as the

say temptation invariably adapt themselves in a most wonderful manner

to the various weakness and inconsistency of our nature. thus,

as Alice’s speech have, unintentionally on her part, appeal to Lord

Alfred’s lead foible--vanity, so, in turn, do his reply re-act upon

Alice’s vulnerable point--jealousy of Arabella Crofton, and consequent

curiosity as to her former relation with Harry Coverdale. accordingly,

forget time, place, propriety, even her doubt in regard to the

perfect sobriety of the person she be address, in the overpowering

interest of the question, she ask, hurriedly--



“why do you say that? to what do you refer? have Mr. Coverdale ever tell

you anything on the subject?”



Lord Alfred smile at the effect which his hint have produce; though,

when he mark his victim’s eager eye and trembling lip, his good

feeling make one last appeal, and he half resolve to leave D’Almayne’s

communication untold. have he be completely himself, the good

resolution would have be form and adhere to; but he have “put an

enemy into his mouth to steal away his brain,” and be no long able

to control his impulse; so, by an effort, he silence the voice of

conscience, and replied--“I shall break no confidence by tell you why

I suppose Mr. Coverdale well ‘up’ in Miss Crofton’s previous history

than I be, for he never mention her name in my presence; indeed, now I

come to think of it, it be a subject he always studiously avoid; but my

information relate to certain romantic passage say to have occur

in Italy.”



“in Italy!” exclaim Alice, aghast at this apparent realisation of all

her vague fear and suspicion. “go on,” she continue, impatiently;

“I can listen to no hint asperse my husband’s character; if you have

anything to say against he, do not insinuate it, but speak out plainly

and honestly.”



“really, you mistake I,” be the reply; “I have no accusation to bring

against Mr. Coverdale: but your question recall to my mind an anecdote

which I hear lately, and I be amuse at your require information

from I which your own husband be so much well able to afford.”



“and what be this remarkable anecdote? pray let I have the benefit of

hear it, my lord,” rejoin Alice, in vain try to look and speak

in an unconcerned manner.



“really I think I have well not tell you; you lady be apt to be a

little jealous sometimes without reasonable cause. ‘where ignorance be

bliss,’ you know----” he pause with a tantalise smile, then see

from Alice’s manner that she be not in a humour to be trifle with, he

continued--“Well, I see you mean to hear it, so I may as well tell you

at once--not that there be anything very wonderful to tell. you must

know that, some three or four year ago, Miss Crofton, be then

young and handsomer than she be now (she be not _my_ style, but many

people consider she vastly attractive still), be live as governess

with a family of the name of Muir, and in that capacity accompany they

to Florence. John Muir, the eld son, be an old college friend of Mr.

Coverdale’s, and meet by chance in Switzerland, they join force,

and spend two or three month at Florence, make occasional excursion

into the adjoining country. everything progress with cheerfulness and

serenity in this italian Arcadia, until one fine day the eld Miss

Muir elope with an individual who represent himself as a Neapolitan

count, and prove to be merely either valet or courier to the same. this

break up the party, and Mr. Coverdale take his leave; but scarcely have

he be go twelve hour, when, lo and behold, Miss Crofton, who have

be much blame for not having look after the elope-with young lady

more closely (I suppose she be look after somebody else), suddenly

disappear. after hunt about Florence in vain, Pater Familias Muir

somehow obtain a clue to the lady’s whereabouts, follow which he

reach a village some thirty mile distant, where he discover Miss

Crofton, and, if my informant do not err, Mr. Coverdale also. whether

it have be his intention to place she in that position now so much

more worthily fill, or whether he propose an arrangement of a less

permanent character, history telleth not; suffice it to add, as the

book say, that the eloquent representation of Pater Muir induce the

lady to return with he to Florence, whence he instantly dispatch

she to England under some safe escort, while Mr. Coverdale pursue his

onward course to Turkey and the East.” he pause, but as Alice make

no reply, merely conceal her countenance behind a voluminous fan,

somewhat small than a peacock’s expand tail, he continued--“Such

be the _historiette_ relate to I; but scandal-monger be so give

to exaggerate, that I dare say it be not half true, so do not _worry_

yourself about it, my dear Mrs. Coverdale.”



this consolatory codicil be add because his lordship hear, or

fancy he hear, a sound analogous to a repressed sob proceed from

behind the fan, and this pseudo-profligate young nobleman carry a very

tender heart under his embroider waistcoat.



on receive this confirmation of she bad, nay, more than her bad,

fear, Alice’s first impulse be to give way to a flood of tear--an

impulse so strong that, unable entirely to check it, the sob which Lord

Alfred have partially overhear be the result. the story chime in with

her jealous suspicion so exactly, that it never for a moment occur

to she to question the truth of it; on the contrary, it would have

require the clear evidence of its falsehood to make she disbelieve

it. have by a great effort repress her tear, her next impulse be

to prevent any one, especially Lord Alfred, from perceive how deeply

his intelligence have affect she. accordingly she turn to he, and

reply in as careless a tone as she could summon--



“a very pretty bit of scandal, truly; and, as you say, worth as much, or

as little rather, as scandal usually be; however, the tale have serve to

amuse I and put I in a good humour; so, as you seem to have set your

heart upon another dance, I suppose I must exercise my woman’s privilege

in your favour, and change my mind. they be go to waltz--shall we

begin?”



Surprised and delight at the success of his experiment, and almost
