inclined to attribute supernatural wisdom to Horace D’Almayne, Lord

Alfred hastily offer his arm to his enslaver, and in another minute

they be whirl round the room in all the giddy excitement of a rapid

waltz. while the dance be still proceed, a tall, strike-look

man enter the room, and shade his eye from the unaccustomed

brilliancy of the light, carefully scrutinise the dancer, until his

glance fall upon the figure of Alice and Lord Alfred, when a shade come

over his handsome feature, and lean his shoulder against the side of

a doorway, he remain with his eye track the evolution of two of

the figure glance before he. after he have remain motionless for

some minute, absorb in his own thought, which be, apparently, of

no over-pleasant nature, a gentle touch on the arm arouse he, and,

look round, he perceive Arabella Crofton. she be about to address

he, but by a warning gesture he silence she, and she remain stand

silently beside he until, in a low, stem voice, he ask abruptly--



“how often have she be dance with he?”



“three time, I believe; but I assure you--”



“hush!” continue Coverdale in the same stem, impressive voice, which

be just above a whisper; “I want fact, not comment. have she dance

with any one else since he have be here?”



“not that I be aware of,” be the reply. “she dance with a young

guardsman before he come.”



“and since?”



“they have be either dance or talk together, except for about ten

minute, during the last two hour.”



Coverdale make no reply, but his lip become more sternly compress,

and the shade on his brow grow deep, until the dance conclude, then

muttering--



“this must not go on: I shall make she come away”--he strode across the

room to where (her late partner bend gracefully over she, and talk

about nothing with the deep _empressement_) his wife be seat.









CHAPTER XXXIX.--ARABELLA.



on perceive her husband, Alice start, and, between surprise

and anger, her cheek assume a hue more resemble that violent and

unsentimental flower the peony, than the blush-rise, to the use of which

our minor poet be so strongly addicted. this blush which, with all

his trust in and affection for his wife, Harry could scarcely fail to

misinterpret, do not tend to impart any great degree of cordiality to

his manner, as he thus accost her:--



“I scarcely expect to find you still here, so late as it be; but I

only reach Park Lane within the last half-hour. there have be an

accident on the line, and our train be delay between two and three

hour. you look flushed and tired. you’ve be tempt she to dance too

much, I’m afraid, Courtland. I see the carriage wait as I come in. I

should think you must have have enough of this nonsense, Alice! what say

you to come away? I’ve lot of news to tell you from home.”



“I’m afraid your budget must wait a little long. I’m engage to Lord

Alfred for the next dance, and intend to fulfil my engagement; so you

have well submit to your fate quietly, and provide yourself with a

partner,” be Alice’s cool reply.



“Courtland will excuse you, I be sure,” urge Harry; “come away, if for

no well reason than that I wish it.”



“an all-sufficient one in your autocratic eye, I dare say,” be the

flippant rejoinder; “but the barrel-organ remind we too constantly

that ‘briton never shall be slave,’ for I to think of sacrifice my

freedom to all your imperious fancy. come, my lord, they be go to

wind up with Sir Roger de Coverley; let we take our place.” so say,

Alice accept the proffered arm of her _cavalier servente_, and walk

off with he, leave her husband to struggle against his rise anger

(which in she then frame of mind she see and disregard) as good he

might. a severe struggle it be, and one in which nothing but his deep

love for she, and fear of compromise she by word or deed, could have

render he successful. by a powerful exercise of self-control, he

contrive to avoid any outward manifestation of his feeling; and after

watch Alice and her partner for some minute, with flash eye and

an aching heart, as they hurry through the boisterous evolution

of that romp dance, he wander listlessly through the room, now

partially desert, seek some spot where he might be alone with

his troubled thought, and avoid the necessity of reply to the

commonplace of society, to which, at that moment, he feel himself

completely unfit. having pass through the music-room, he find

himself in an elegantly-furnish boudoir, which at first sight he

believe to be untenante, and, fling himself into an easy-chair,

lean his head on his hand, and give way to painful reflection. after

remain in this attitude for several minute, a sound resemble a

sigh catch his ear, and, hastily look up, he perceive Arabella

Crofton.



“be you here when I enter?” he inquire.



“yes; I be stand in the recess of the window, and the curtain

conceal I. I should have speak to you, but as I perceive you be

preoccupy, I be afraid to disturb you, and do not intend to move

until you have leave the boudoir, but your ear be so quick that you

detect I. I wish,” she continue, in a timid, falter voice, “your

brow do not wear so deep a shade, or that I be in any degree able to

remove it.” as she speak; she draw near to he, and lean her arm on

the back of the chair on which he be sit.



kindness and affection be never so much prize as when we have suffer

injustice at the hand of one we love. word cannot console at such a

moment: but sympathy--the conviction that another heart feel for and

with we, be able in some degree to do so. whatever fault arabella

Crofton might possess,--and that they be neither few nor light no one

be well aware than Harry Coverdale,--the truth and strength of her

regard for he he do not doubt. deeply, fondly, earnestly as he

love his wife, he must have be more than mortal have he not perforce

contrast the levity (to use the mild term) and unkindness of she

on whom he thus lavish his whole treasure of affection, with the ready

sympathy, the watchful tenderness of one who, if she have be all evil,

nay, if she have not possess in some degree unusual generosity of

character, might have hate he with a strength proportion to the

regard she now appear to feel towards he. man be constitutionally

deny the relief which the gentle sex derive from tear; but if, when

a woman would weep, a man of deep, strong feeling can be sufficiently

soften to give vent to his sorrow in word, the effect be somewhat

analogous. Harry’s heart be full to overflow, and Arabella’s

well-time sympathy cause the torrent of his grief to burst forth.



“why do she try I thus!” he say; “it be, it _must_ be, mere want of

thought; she _be_ wilful, I see it, as clearly as I see and know that

it be my culpable neglect which first make she so; but this be a hard

punishment for even so gross a fault! if she know how her cold look and

hard word pain I--how it grieve, destroy I to be force to deny

her anything--to feel it my duty, as I perceive it to be now, to oppose

her slight wish! and then to see she do thing which may give

those who do not know her truth and purity as I do, occasion to slander

she--Arabella, it madden I!” he press his hand to his forehead to

still its throbbing; but when his companion appear about to attempt

to console he, he resume, abruptly--“Don’t speak; you cannot defend

she--her conduct admit of no defence, and I will not hear she blame!

neither can you advise I; as far as action go, my course be clear--I

shall take she out of town tomorrow; and as I cannot have it out with

that scoundrel D’Almayne, or the weak, ungrateful boy he be ruin,

without compromise she, I must postpone the day of reckon with

they--it will come soon or later, that be all clear enough; but that

be not the point”--here word fail he, and cover his eye with his

hand, he relapse into his former gloomy silence.



Arabella Crofton be a woman of strong passion, and naturally of strong

impulse also, but these she have learn in great measure to control;

thus her manner be usually quiet and collect, and she both speak and

act accord to a rule lay down by herself for her own guidance, and

tend towards some definite end. but when, as in the present instance,

she be actuate by any overpowering feeling, she be for the moment

completely carry away by it, and would act for good or evil, as the

impulse which control she be a right or wrong one, even in direct

opposition to her own plan and intention. she dislike Alice

most heartily, and she have many--we cannot say “good,” but

sufficient--reason for do so; yet she sympathise so strongly with

Harry’s grief at the idea that his wife be encourage the attention

of Lord Alfred Courtland, that--believe, as she do honestly, Alice

to be merely amusing herself, possibly for the sake of annoy her

husband, but evidently not from any deep feeling for her admirer--she

could not help try to comfort he.



“do not afflict yourself so deeply,” she say; “I cannot bear to see you

suffer thus! believe I, you think too seriously of this matter; Mrs.

Coverdale be only amuse herself with this foolish, infatuated young

man. I be as certain as if I be in her confidence that she do

not really care for he; the very openness with which she accept his

attention prove that it be so; as soon as she have leave the gaiety

and frivolity of town, she will forget his very existence.”



“she may forget _he_,” be the bitter reply; “but will she ever forget

the cause which have drive she to encourage he--which have force she

to seek amusement in all these heartless gaiety and folly? will she

ever forget the time when, pursue my own selfish pleasure, I leave

she, day after day, alone--she who have always be accustom to live

in a cheerful family, will she ever forget my neglect, and restore to

I that love without which life have no long a charm for I--that love

which I once possess, and which, God help I! I fear I have alienate

for ever!”



“yes, she will,” be the eager reply; “if she ever love you, she love

you still; real, true love never die: it would be well for some of we

if time could efface feeling!”



the evident emotion with which she utter these last word touch

Harry’s kind heart, and, regard she with a look of pity interest,

he rejoined--



“Poor Arabella! you too have have much sorrow to contend with; and no one

can lament more deeply than I do the share I have have in increase

it. mine be a strange fate!--love that I cannot return be lavish and

waste on I, and the only affection I pine for, I have alienate by my

own rash and inconsiderate conduct!”



she stand by he as he speak, in the excitement of his feeling he have

take her hand and clasp it in his own. at this moment two figure,

which have be pause at the door of the boudoir, pass hastily on--by

the rustling of the dress, one of they be evidently a woman.



“but now hear I once more,” he continue, raise himself, and

regard she kindly but steadily; “I be sorry, very sorry, to find that

you have not yet overcome--however, we will not allude to that--if at

any time you want a _friend’s_ advice or assistance, apply to I: my

purse, I need scarcely say, be always at your command; in fact, as I be

well-off, and you unfortunately be not, I think it be an over-refine

though generous scruple, which prevent you from allow I to assist

you as I might and wish to do. why do not you remember and strive to

follow my advice? you be still in a dependent situation quite unworthy

of you; while you have talent and power which, if you would employ

they in some straightforward, honest avocation--instead of form

plan and seek object of, to say the least, questionable

advisability--would secure you a respectable and comfortable position.

think of all this, dear Arabella, and then apply to I, as to an old

friend, to advance you fund to carry out my idea in any way which

seem to you most advisable.”



for a moment she remain silent; then bend over he, so that her

ringlet mingle with his dark curl hair, she murmured--



“you be good, and kind, and generous, as you ever be; and--yes, I

_will_ strive to make myself worthy of your friendship; if I fail, you

know my impulsive, passionate nature, and you will pardon, not condemn

I; for my _great sorrow_, you now know how to pity I! you say you

intend to leave London to-morrow, and I think it will be wise in you to

do so--perhaps we may never meet again, and so, my dear, dear friend,

farewell!”



he have retain her hand, and she return his cordial, warm pressure;

then, by a sudden impulse, she stoop, press her pale lip upon his

high, smooth brow, and--be go.



Harry follow she with his glance as she leave the room.



“poor thing!” he murmur, “she have many high quality; and such a

life as she lead must be a complete purgatory to her proud, impetuous

disposition; I hope she will fall into good hand, and--and keep out of

my way. Alice evidently dislike and suspect she, and nothing I can say

be likely to lessen the feeling. now for take my poor, dear, naughty,

foolish, little wife home, and lecture she. she seem angry with

I; because I do not arrive in time to accompany she to the ball, I

suppose--as if I could prevent railway-train from break down!--ah,

it’s wretched, miserable work all of it!”



having arrive at this cheerful conclusion, Harry rise and proceed in

search of his wife.



in the meantime, the country-dance being end, Lord Alfred have offer

his arm to his partner, and propose a stroll through the room--a

proposition to which Alice, who, in her present state of feeling, be

anxious to do anything rather than hasten the inevitable _tête à tête_

with her husband, consent. as they pass a group who be gather

round a clever copy from one of the great work of some old master,

D’Almayne approach Lord Alfred, and, make some light remark to

screen his real object, find an opportunity to whisper to his pupil--



“take she to the door of the boudoir, and detain she there to look at

the picture in the anteroom for a minute; there be a _tableau vivant_

inside the apartment which will interest she deeply!” partially guess

his meaning, Lord Alfred execute the task with so much tact and skill,

that all this by-play be completely unnoticed by Alice, and when

they reach the door of the boudoir, which stand ajar, she stop

to examine a picture, in perfect unconsciousness of any plot or

contrivance; as she do so, the follow sentence, speak in tone of

deep emotion, fall upon her ear:--



“_love that I cannot return be lavish and waste on I, and the only

affection I pine for, I have alienate by my own rash and inconsiderate

conduct!_”



the sound of the voice be all that Alice require to enable she to

decide that the speaker be her husband: and a hurried glance prove to

she that his speech have be address to Arabella Crofton, her rival,

as she have long suspect she to be--a fact in regard to which she now

receive the assurance of her own sense.



Harry’s speech could bear but one interpretation: the “love waste

on he which he could never return,” be her own--his wife’s! the

“affection he pine for, and have alienate by his rash and inconsiderate

conduct,” be that of Arabella Crofton, the “rash conduct” he be so

bitterly repent--his marriage. yes, she see it all, and feel that for

she there be no long such a thing as happiness in this life. now that

she know, that she have hear from his own lip, that he no long love

her,--nay, that he have transfer his affection to another,--she feel

how all important, how essential it have be to she--existence without

Harry’s love to brighten it, would be like the universe without

sunlight--cold, dark, desolate.



poor little Alice! she have act very wrongly; she have be self-willed,

petulant, unjust, and disobedient to her husband; but if suffer could

atone for sin, the bitterness of that moment might have expiate graver

offence than those of which she have be guilty. her first idea be to

get away from the spot: lose as he be to she, Harry should never say

she be a spy upon his action. she turn to communicate her wish

to her companion, and see his eye fix on her face with a peculiar

intelligence which she have never observe before, and in an instant the

thought flash across she that she have be bring there by design;

and, without allow time for reflection as to the advisability of

make such an accusation, she exclaimed--



“you know they be there, and bring I on purpose to see they, and so

to destroy the happiness of my future life! what have I ever do to you

to deserve this at your hand!”



utterly take aback by this direct and unexpected attack, Lord Alfred

colour up, stammer something unintelligible, and at last attempt

to screen himself behind the equivocation that he do not know Mr.

Coverdale be in the boudoir.



“if you do not know it, you suspect it,” be the reply; “your

feature be more honest than your word, my lord, and betray you.”



greatly confound at this most unexpected result of his scheme, Lord

Alfred vow, and protest, and attempt to clear and defend himself,

but in vain. the shock Alice have receive have couch her mental vision,

and, turn a deaf ear to his excuse, she sternly desire he to

take she back to Mrs. Crane immediately; and then preserve an offended

silence, so that his lordship be glad to take she at her word, and lead

she back to the drawing-room, in which the Crane party have ensconce

themselves.



“Kate, let we get home--I be weary to death; somebody say the

carriage be wait.”



the word be commonplace enough, but something in the tone in which

they be utter cause Mrs. Crane to regard her cousin attentively,

and her quick eye soon discern that there be something amiss. “Alice,

be anything wrong, dear? you be not ill?”



“yes! no! my head ache--only let we get away!” be the reply.



“but some one tell I that Mr. Coverdale have arrive; where be he?--you

will wait for he?” return Kate, alarmed and surprised at Alice’s

unwonted agitation.



“he will come when he like; he--have find some friend of his, I

believe,” murmur Alice. “_only_ let we get away!” she add, in so

implore a tone that Kate, convince some _contre-temp_ have occur,

dispatch Mr. Crane in search of Miss Crofton, and, take leave of

Lady Tattersall Trottemout (who think they have resolve to spend the

night there, naturally deplore their “run away so early”), repair

to the cloakroom. here the other, include Harry Coverdale, join

they, and in another quarter of an hour they be safely house in Park

Lane.



thus end Lady Tattersall Trottemout’s _soirée dansante_; but its

consequence continue to influence the life of those whoso fortune we

be trace, for many a long year.



nothing pass between Coverdale and Alice in reference to the scene we

have just describe until the next morning, when, before they go down

to breakfast, Harry observe abruptly, “Alice, it be my particular wish

that you should go down to the Park to-day: can you be ready to start by

the four o’clock train?”



“yes,” be the unexpectedly acquiescent reply; then, after a moment’s

pause, “what reason be I to give Kate for leave she so suddenly?”



astonish at such a ready consent where he have expect strong

opposition, if not an actual refusal to comply with his desire, Harry

look steadfastly at his wife, but her face be turn away, so that he

could not read its expression. “my true reason I will explain to you at

some time when we can talk the matter over coolly and quietly,” be

the reply; “the reason I wish you to give your cousin--which be a good,

true, and sufficient reason in itself, although not the only one

by which I be actuate--be, that your sister Emily have receive an

invitation to stay with a friend of her, which Mrs. Hazlehurst be

anxious she should accept, think she require change; but emily

very properly refuse to leave her mother. I dine there the day before

yesterday, and hearing of the dilemma, propose that you should take

Emily’s place for a fortnight or three week--I be not wrong in make

such an offer, be I?”



“no; I shall be very glad to see and be of use to dear mamma,” be the

reply.



“I should have tell you all this last night,” continue Coverdale, “but

for reason I will not enter upon at present.”



he wait for some comment on his speech, but he wait in vain; Alice

continue to add the finish touch to her toilet, until, be

completely equip, she quietly observe, “it be time to go down, I

think; the breakfast bell will ring directly;” and, suit the action

to the word, she depart, leave her husband to follow when he

please. Kate be surprised to hear of their sudden determination to

leave town, and sorry to part with they; but their reason for so do

be such a plausible one, that she could urge nothing against it. she

see that there be something more--that neither Harry nor his wife be

at their ease; but Alice keep her own counsel so closely that all Kate’s

endeavour to win her confidence be futile, and she be oblige to

content herself by suppose that it be a mere matrimonial breeze which

would blow over, as such affair usually do, without any very serious

consequence result from it.



Coverdale Park be reach without adventure, and appear as cool,

and calm, and happy as the country usually do to the eye of

fashion-weary Londoners; and Harry, unaffectedly delight to escape

from the uncongenial atmosphere of a crowded city to his home,--which he

love with his whole heart,--forgot, in the pleasure he experience, the

amount of Alice’s misdemeanours, and be only anxious to be reconcile

with she, and to assure she of his perfect and entire forgiveness. but

since the previous evening a change--for which he could not account, and

which begin to render he very uneasy--have come over Alice, she be

no long irritable and petulant at one moment, yet amuse and

light-hearted at the next, but a settle gloom hang o’er her brow, which

indicate sorrow rather than anger; and although she have never allow

he to surprise she in tear, her eye bear unmistakeable trace of

weeping. their _tête-à-tête_ dinner pass off heavily enough: as they

sit moodily over their dessert, Harry observe, “the evening be most

lovely--come out and take a stroll.” he speak kindly, almost tenderly,

and as Alice look up to reply to he, her eye fill with tear;

hastily check they before they could be observe, she agree. her

husband carefully place a shawl over her shoulder, bring from the

hall her garden bonnet, and, draw her arm within his own, they walk

on for some distance in silence. at length Harry observe, “Alice, dear,

you seem downcast and unhappy--why be this? surely you cannot regret

that hot, miserable, artificial London? you must be glad to get back to

our own dear, quiet home again?”



“I do not in the least regret London,” be the reply; “on the contrary,

I be glad to be once more in the country again.”



“then why this gloomy manner?” urge Coverdale; “I may have be a

little annoyed with you at time lately, but I be quite prepared to

believe it be mere thoughtlessness on your part; in fact, I never

consider it anything else. I feel sure when you come to reflect

seriously on the matter, you will yourself see that your conduct be

a little injudicious; and, in that case, believe I the affair be from

this moment forget and forgive.” Harry pause for a reply, but

for several moment none be forthcoming; at last, his patience be

exhaust, he inquire in a tone of surprise, “Alice, do you hear what

I be say?”



“I beg your pardon,” rejoin Alice, start, “I be _not_ attend

properly at that moment; you be blame I for something, be you

not? I be very sorry--what be it?”



as she speak, Harry glance towards she to discover whether she have be

really too much pre-engross to attend to he, or whether she merely

affect to have be so for the amiable purpose of provoke he;

decide in favour of the first hypothesis, he resume: “I be say,

my dear Alice, that although your flirtation with that foolish boy,

Alfred Courtland, have cause I some uneasiness--because people dare

to remark on it, unluckily not in a way that I could take up--yet that

I be convince it be merely thoughtlessness on your part, and be

anxious to forgive and forget it.”



if he have expressly try to rouse Alice from the state of gloomy

depression into which she have fall, Harry could not have devised

mean more effectual than the speech he have just address to she. with

flash eye she hear he to the end, then inquire: “and pray who

have dared--(you may well use the word!)--who have dare to accuse I of

flirt? but I need not ask,” she continue, bitterly; “no one but

Miss Crofton would have venture to asperse your wife’s character before

you--from no one else would you have listen to such a falsehood--no

one else could have induce you to believe it!”



astonish, and, if the truth must be tell, somewhat confound at

have the table thus turn upon he, Harry exclaim, “Alice, what

do you mean? what be you talk about? have you take leave of your

sense all of a sudden?”



“if I have I should scarcely be surprised,” be the rejoinder; “but I

know only too well what I be say, and the cause I have to say and

believe it; however, I do not want to reproach you, that would do no

good; but--but--know what I know--” an hysterical sob choke she

voice--“it be too hard that _you_ should accuse _I_ of flirting”--and

here, utterly overcome by her feeling, she burst into a paroxysm of

weeping. wholly confound at this unexpected result of his very

mild remonstrance, which have be intend more as a judicious way of

forgiving Alice’s misdemeanours than as a reprimand, Harry lead she to

a seat, and then use his good endeavour to console and bring she to

reason; but in vain, nor be it until she be fain to stop through sheer

physical exhaustion that her tear cease; by which time, what between

bodily fatigue (she have not be in bed until between three and four

on the previous night, or rather morning, could not sleep then, and have

accomplish a railroad journey since) and mental agitation, she be

so completely wear out that even Harry, who be not usually too

clear-sighted on such point, perceive this be not a fitting

opportunity to continue the discussion.









chapter XL.--DEEPER and DEEPER still.



on the afternoon of the day after that on which she return home,

Alice be to go to the Grange, and take her sister’s place as companion

to Mrs. Hazlehurst. during the morning, Harry be occupy with

his bailiff and the farming account, but he make his appearance at

luncheon. when that meal be conclude, and the servant have quit

the room, he begin gravely, but kindly--“Alice, dear, I do not wish to

distress or annoy you, but, before you leave home, I must once again

refer to the conversation of last night. I know not who have couple

my name with that of your cousin Kate’s friend, Miss Crofton, nor what

falsehood they may have coin to blacken my character in your eye;

but, since I have know you, I have never attempt to deceive you on

any point; and I tell you now, on my honour as a gentleman, that nothing

ever have pass, or be in the small degree likely to pass, between

myself and that young lady, calculate to cause you the slight pain

or even uneasiness. do this satisfy you, or, if not, can I say or do

anything that will?”



“yes!” exclaim Alice, her face flush with eagerness as the idea

strike she; “promise to tell I exactly all that pass between you and

she in Italy!--promise I this; show I that you be willing to confide

in I; trust to my affection to forgive you, should you tell I anything

you think may displease I, and I will, on my part, try to forget my own

conviction that--that--in fact, that you do not love I as I believe

you once do. tell I all frankly, and there may yet be happiness in

store for we both.”



she pause, breathless with emotion, and fix her large eye on her

husband’s countenance, as though she fain would read his very thought,

await a reply; but for a minute none appear likely to come.

Coverdale, push back his hair, rub his forehead, and evince

unmistakable sign of annoyance and perplexity, at length rouse

himself by an effort, and, in a constrain, embarrassed tone of voice,

replied--



“ask I anything but that: I be under a solemn promise never to mention

the fact you desire to learn; I cannot break my word even to regain

your affection.”



“I will ask nothing more of you,” return Alice, in a tone of

deeply-wound feeling; “it be foolish to ask that--I might have know

you would refuse to answer I; and it be bad than folly to fancy you

care to retain my affection! and now let I go home to mamma; thank

God, I may yet be of some use and comfort to she, and, at all event,

I know that _she_ love I--oh! that I have never leave she!” and,

disregard Harry’s exclamation, “Alice, hear I! indeed you mistake--”

 she hurry out of the room.



her husband remain motionless until her retreat footstep become

inaudible, then, spring from his chair, he begin pace up and down

with hasty stride, while his idea arrange themselves somewhat after

the follow fashion:--



“well, I’ve make a pretty mess of it now, and no mistake! of all thing

in the world for she to have fix upon--to want to know about Arabella;

and poor Arabella have behave so nicely and kindly too in this affair!

I _cann’t_ tell she! besides, there’s my promise--come what may I’ll keep

my promise; but I be an unlucky dog as ever live! ah! I never ought to

have marry, that’s the whole truth. woman don’t seem to understand I,

and I’m sure I don’t understand they; whether I’m stern or whether I’m

kind it all turn out alike, and all wrong. poor, dear, little Alice!

she be make herself just as miserable as she have make I; and, for the

life of I, I don’t know how to say or do anything to mend matter! I

must leave it to time, I suppose. perhaps her mother may talk she into

a happy frame of mind. I be glad she be go back to the Grange; I

think I’ll leave she there for a short time--home influence may soften

she, and induce she to judge I more charitably. I’m certain it’s all my

own fault, somehow! she be as sweet-temper as an angel when I marry

she.” he continue to pace the room, and after some moment a new notion

seem to strike he. “I wonder whose be put these idea about

Arabella into her head,” he resume; “somebody have be tell she

about the Florence business, that’s clear--lie most likely, and in

order to set she against I. that man D’Almayne, I mistrust he--he’s

play a deep game of some kind; and his manner to Kate Crane I

disapprove of strongly. if he have be meddle--if he have dare to say

or insinuate anything against I to Alice, by heaven, I’ll--I’ll--no, I

could not trust myself to horsewhip he, at least not just yet, I should

kill the scoundrel. I’ve a great mind to run up to London, when I’ve

take Alice to the Grange, and try and find out something about it;

but I willn’t be hasty--I _must_ not! the interest at stake be too

important--Alice’s happiness for life, to say nothing of my own,

which be bind up in her, depend upon how I behave for the next few

month--no; I willn’t act rashly or hastily, nothing shall induce I to do

so!”



of all the high and solemn mystery that enshroud the spirit-life

none be more inscrutable, yet invest with a deep and more vital

interest, than those apparently irreconcilable paradox--predestination

and free will. our possession of this latter attribute be a tenet hold,

and carelessly acquiesce in, by Christians of every denomination; yet

how little do we realise or estimate its practical importance! it be

impossible to reflect, even for a moment, on so vast a field of thought

without elicit idea at once salutary and impressive. nor can we

fully recognise our obligation as responsible being until, in trace

the fortune of some follow-creature, of whose path through life our

limited power enable we to perceive only the dim and shadowy outline,

we see how what appear trifle--make a right use of, as they should

be, or abuse, as they too often be--influence a lifetime here, and,

fearful thought, determine an eternity hereafter! in thing spiritual,

as well as in thing material, cause govern effect; and the law which

regulate consequence be equally stringent and immutable in both case,

although in the former they be not so easily traceable. still, to

the earnest, careful, and patient observer of the mysterious way of

Providence, suggestive glimpse be afford, aid by which he may

reason from thing see to thing unseen. thus, remark how some

strange train of event result from a single act which we may long have

feebly propose to perform, but the execution of which we have delay

from day to day, until some unexpected excitement have quicken our

resolve into action, we may legitimately argue that these event have

be, as it be, wait for the touch which be to set the train in

motion; that if that motive power have be apply soon, the same

result would have be proportionably hasten; and that if it have

never be apply at all, the history of event would have bear a

different record. we be so fearfully and wonderfully constitute, and

the dealing of the Creator with his creature be so complicated and

inscrutable, that we know not what great event may hinge upon our

slight action. the avalanche lie in all its dread sublimity,

apparently as immovable as the mountain-side it rest on; the careless

foot of some chamois hunter dislodge a stone--the spell which enchain

the destroyer be break--with the velocity of the whirlwind the mass

descend, crush and overwhelming all before it--and heart-rend

memory be all that remain to bear witness of some once prosperous

village and its inhabitant.



one, who see all clearly where we but blindly and feebly catch a ray of

light, pray for his executioner in these remarkable words--“Father,

forgive they, _they know not what they do!_” idea such as the foregoing

be calculate to inspire feeling of awe; but, if they be true,

they should not be put aside because they give a solemn view of our

responsibility; when, moreover, rightly consider, they teach an

important practical lesson--namely, never to neglect what appear to be

little duty, or carelessly to fall into little sin. it seem but a

little duty to extinguish a fallen spark; yet that spark may kindle a

fire which may consume a city, which, save for that accident, might have

endure for century. it seem but a little sin to utter a playful jest

on some serious subject; but that jest may inspire a doubt which may

injure a waver faith, and endanger a soul’s salvation. some may deem

these remark misplace in a work of fiction; but if it be a novelist’s

endeavour to depict truly the various phase of human life, nought that

truly affect the spring of human action can be foreign to his subject.



the evening of Lady Tattersall Trottemout’s party be not the first

occasion on which Harry Coverdale have bestow good and sound advice on

Arabella Crawford, but never before have it produce the desire effect.

now, however, a new impulse spring up within she--she would conquer she

hopeless, selfish, sinful love for he, and strive to render herself

worthy of his friendship, and win at least his esteem; but how should

she begin practically to work out his advice--how attempt to render

herself independent--what duty lie most directly in her path? her

intention be honest and sincere, and that morning’s post bring

an answer to her question. a female relation whom she have hitherto

neglect, be take seriously ill, and write wishing, but scarcely

expect, she to come to she immediately. this lady be old,

uninteresting, and in straiten circumstance; to go to she be an act

of unmitigated self-sacrifice, and in Arabella’s then frame of mind

this be its great attraction. Kate Crane be sorry to part with

she, although the short time they have pass together have suffice to

convince she of the disagreeable fact that her dear friend no long

suit she as she have do in her schoolgirl day. there be a very

simple reason for this, although Kate do not at once perceive it:

Arabella Crofton be at an age when the mind and body having reach

maturity, if they do not remain stationary, yet alter so gradually, that

the change be almost imperceptible; she be, therefore, much what she

have be four year previously. Kate, on the contrary, have advance from

a girl into a woman; and her intellectual power have not only

develop until they be now in every respect superior to those of she

_ci-devant_ governess, but her taste have be form on a well and

purer model, and her natural instinct be of a high and more refined

character. thus, Arabella be constantly jar against and annoying

Kate’s sensitiveness by thought, word, and deed; and she feel that a

gulf have grow up between they, which would effectually prevent her

perceive the effect this feeling have produce upon Kate’s manner,

although she be ignorant of the cause. thus, the parting between the

friend--for, from old association, friend they still be--be by no

mean so painful as under other circumstance they might have consider

it.



leave to her own device, Kate bethink she of the expedition to visit

Mrs. Leonard, which Horace D’Almayne have propose to she on the occasion

of the horticultural _fête_, but which she have never yet find an

opportunity to accomplish. Mrs. Leonard’s history be a distressing one.

her husband have be partner in a north country bank, at which Mr. Crane

usually keep a considerable account. on one occasion, when his balance

there exceed even its usual limit, a junior partner suddenly

abscond to America, take with he so considerable a sum that the

bank be oblige to stop payment, and Mr. Leonard find himself a ruin

man. in his adversity, his mind became engross by one fix idea,

which almost assume the character of a monomania--viz., that it be his

mission to trace out his late partner, and recover the money with which

he have make away; this notion prey upon he until one morning he, too,

suddenly disappear, leave a letter to inform his wife that he have

set out in search of the delinquent, and that she would hear nothing

more of he until he have succeed in his object. on inquiry it appear

that he have take a berth in an american packet, which have just sail,

and, beyond that, all trace of he be lose. consequently, his family

have fall into actual poverty, which, day by day, assume a sterner and

more hopeless character. a gentleman well verse in the detail of Mr.

Crane’s early acquaintance with Mr. Leonard (who, before Mr. Crane have

amass the fortune he now possess, have several time advance he

money, and in a measure, therefore, contribute to his success in life),

advise Mrs. Leonard to apply to he for assistance; and, be

aware how much the _millionaire_ be guide by the opinion of Horace

D’Almayne, suggest that she should make her first application through

he: in which appeal the fertile brain of that good young man perceive

matter which might be make profitable to the furtherance of his design,

and re-arrange his hand, so as to take in the new card thus place

within his reach.



the plan which D’Almayne have settle with Kate be this:--she be

sit for her portrait to an artist friend of Horace’s, to whose

painting-room she go twice a-week; D’Almayne propose to send away the

carriage and servant, when he would have a hire brougham in readiness

whence her own carriage could again fetch she and convey her home. Kate

dislike all this clandestine contrivance; but, consider the end of

less objectionable scheme, and so reluctantly consent. she reach she

lie between Islington and Pentonville, and contain a description of

house too good, or, more truly speak, too expensive, for the very

low order to reside in, and yet so confined and comfortless that

Leonard be a singularly gentle, lady-like person, evidently wear down

consist of two boy and three girl, the eld son and daughter be

completely win all their affection, from the poor mother, who begin

to see light break in upon her darkness, to the olive-branch of

four--whose vision of unlimited sugar-plums bade fair to be realise.

ah! it be easy to buy golden opinion of the poor and needy in this

world: generosity, _i.e._, judiciously dispose of superfluous cash, be

a virtue strangely overrate. the widow’s mite be an offering for which

one can feel respect, even with a well-fill stomach; but that shrine

for an Englishman’s heart must be indeed empty, ere he can thank dive

for his crumb. but, when Kate smile brightly, and speak kindly and

tenderly as she open her purse-string, what wonder that the inmate

of that house of mourning be ready almost to worship her beauty and

munificence? nay, in the excess of her gratitude, poor Mrs. Leonard so

laud Horace D’Almayne for the sunshine he have cause to fall upon the

“frost of her despair,” that this excellent young man really begin to

believe himself to have be actuate by pure philanthropy, and wish

he have not, from disuse, entirely lose the power of blushing. so _he_

talk, and _she_ talk, and _they_ talk, and be all very much

pleased with themselves and with each other; and Kate Crane turn to

depart, with her purse and her heart equally lighten by this most

satisfactory visit. D’Almayne, enrapture alike with the success of his

scheme, and with himself for having so cleverly devise and execute the

same, lead Kate to her brougham with nearly as conspicuous a display

of gallantry to the lady, and admiration of himself, as that which

distinguish Lord Bateman’s proud young porter on the memorable

occasion of his playing gentleman usher to the fair Sophia. having

place she in the brougham, hand her parasol (why do lady take

parasol about in carriage, where there be not the most remote chance

of their be require?), and a shawl, and a carriage-bag full of

elegant rubbish, and smirk to show his white tooth three time--once

for each article--he receive as a reward a kindly smile (for Kate

really feel oblige to he for the opportunity of do good which he

have afford she), which he receive with a look of deferential ecstacy,

and the brougham, with its fair occupant, drive off.



on a sordid pallet, in the garret of the house opposite to that in which

Mrs. Leonard reside, lie a man who, having live wickedly, be then

die miserably: stricken with remorseful terror at the near approach

of death--inevitable, fearful, retributive death--gate to the stern,

inexorable Future, when he would be weigh in the balance and find

wanting--he have wish, poor wretch! to undo some of the evil he have

commit, and so send to a rise young barrister, then get up

evidence in a dispute peerage case, to confess to he the forgery of a

name in a parish-register and other iniquity, the knowledge of which

would materially strengthen the cause of the young lawyer’s client. the

interview, a most painful one to any man of feeling, be conclude;

and, having take copious note of the die forger’s confession in the

presence of a competent witness, soothe the miserable be with

such comfort as human sympathy could suggest, and promise to send

the clergyman who his patient and gentle persuasion have induce he

to receive, the young barrister leave the house at the moment D’Almayne

hand Kate Crane to the brougham. why do the strange turn first red

then pale? why do he clench his fist till the nail dig deep into

the flesh? why do he make a hasty stride forward, then, with an

exclamation, half curse half sob, as hastily draw back, and screen

himself in the shadow of the doorway until the carriage have drive off?

he start because he have see the woman he once love well than

his own life--the woman he have strive to forgive and forget, and have

succeed in accomplish neither the one nor the other--leave a shabby

house in a disreputable suburb, whither she have be in the society of

a notorious libertine! he clench his fist and stride forward from an

impulse of rightful indignation, which make he burn to annihilate

the scoundrel who stood triumph in his villainy before he: but he

check himself as the bitter remembrance flash across he that

_he_ have no claim on she which could give he a right to interfere,

although--and this, even at that moment, be the most painful thought

of all--another had!--who be evidently incompetent to fulfil the

sacred trust which he have undertake. so, with old wound thus cruelly

re-open, Arthur Hazlehurst, heart-sick and weary, return to his

chamber, ponder many thing, both of this life and of the life to

come.









CHAPTER XLI.--ADVICE GRATIS.



it be a dreary thing when much of life seem still before we, and a

dark, unfathomable future lie between we and the grave; it be a bitter

thing to sit alone and ponder on the day to come, and discover

no bright spot in the darkness--discern no kind hand to beckon we

forward--hear no friendly voice to council and encourage we in the

battle of life; it be an uphill task to struggle through existence

without an object on this side the tomb--a hard and cruel lot to hope

for nothing until death shall have change hope into fruition! to live

in order to fit oneself to die be the duty of every Christian, but to

live for that alone require a far high degree of spirituality than to

lay down one’s life for the faith: the stake and the axe of persecution

be tender mercy compare with the chronic martyrdom of such a

life-long sacrifice.



some such gloomy thought as these pass through the overwrought brain

of Arthur Hazlehurst as, late in the night after Kate’s visit to Mrs.

Leonard, he fold up the last document of which he have make himself

master relative to the dispute peerage case in which he be retain.

the evidence of which he have that day become possessed would, he feel

certain, ensure his client’s success, in which event his own career

would in all probability be a prosperous one, and fame and fortune

become his; but how worthless do these appear, now they could no long

be share with she he love! until the incident of that morning have

so powerfully affect he, he hope that he have in great measure

eradicate this affection, which his good sense enable he to perceive

could only be a source of grief to he: but the pain he have then

experience effectually dispel the illusion, and he be fain to

acknowledge that, strongly as he condemn her conduct in sacrifice

his deep and true regard to (as he deem it) a desire for wealth and

the pomp and vanity of fashionable life, he yet, despite his reason,

love she as he feel he never could love any other woman; and the

think that through her husband’s neglect and incompetency she

be expose to the insidious advance of such a character as Horace

D’Almayne weigh upon he, and grieve and irritate he until he could

endure it no long. “come what may of it, I will see she and warn

she; she shall not be lead on by that scoundrel without know his true

character!” he exclaim, rise and hastily pace the room. “for what

purpose could she have accompany he to such a neighbourhood as that?”

 he continue, musing; “he may possibly have get up some plausible lie

to induce she to do so, merely to compromise she in the eye of her

husband--such a scheme be not unlikely to have occur to his subtle

brain. yes, come what may, I will see she to-morrow; and, unless she be

indeed lose to all well feeling, I will rouse she to a sense of duty,

and thwart that scoundrel’s design. if her husband should learn my

interference, I care not; because, in his incapacity, he neglect the

sacred trust he have undertake, that be no reason why I should stand

tamely by and see she sacrifice; no--I will save she in spite of

herself! this shall be my revenge for the happiness which she have

blight. God grant my interference may not prove too late!”



his mind occupy with such thought as these, Arthur Hazlehurst pass

a sleepless night, and the first moment he could tear himself away from

business on the follow day, he betake himself to Park Lane. Kate

be from home when he arrive; but having notify to the servant his

intention of await her return, he be show into the drawing-room,

where he find a tall, fashionably-dress young man stand in a

disconsolate attitude by the fire-place, to whom he make a slight

inclination of the head, heartily wish he at Jericho, or any other

locality equally remote from Park Lane; then, take up a book, he leave

he to his own device. thing remain in this thoroughly english and

unsociable state for about ten minute, towards the end of which period

the fashionable young man, having stare hard at Hazlehurst, grow first

interested, then excited, and finally the spirit move he, and he

spake:--



“I beg pardon--a--really I don’t think I can be mistake--a--very

absurd, I’m sure, if I be--but I be at school with one Arthur

Hazlehurst--and--”



“and I be he,” be the reply; “but you have the advantage of I; for I

be at school with some four hundred boy, and, to tell you the honest

truth, it do not at this moment occur to I which of they you may have

be.”



“yet Alfred Courtland have to thank you for such slight skill as he may

possess in the noble art of boot-cleaning, brush clothe, and

fry sausage; besides early lesson in the demolition of oyster and

porter--enforce by example rather than precept,” be the rejoinder;

and, the unsocial ice of Old England be thus break, the _ci-devant_

school-fellow talk on until they grow quite intimate. at length, Lord

Alfred look at his watch, be silent and _distrait_ for a minute or

two, then begin in a timid, hesitate voice, “I be wait here to see

Mrs. Crane; but, I don’t know--that is, I feel as if I could tell you

all about it quite as well; you can do what I wish well than she

could; and I don’t think you’ll be angry with I when I’ve make you

understand the affair.”



“suppose you come to the point, and try to do so at once,” reply

Arthur, anxious to get he away, if possible, before Kate’s return.



“well, you see, my dear Hazlehurst, I wish you haven’t be abroad, and

then you would have understand it all so much well; but since you go

away--though, by Jove, now I come to think of it, I see you here one day

when Coverdale and your sister first come to town--deuce odd I don’t

make you out then; but if I recollect, you go away just as I come

in--” and thus ramble on, he give a true, though by no mean a full

and particular account of his intimacy with the Coverdales, continue:

“your sister be very kind to I, and take so much trouble about our

duet. she piano, and I do a little in a mild way on the flute, you

know, and we be great friend, and get on very serenely until the

other night, when I be fool enough to do, or rather to say, something

which make she angry--a good right she have to be so; but the fact be,

I’d have some man dine with I, and we drink a lot of wine, and then

sit down to card, and I lose my money and my temper, and in this frame

of mind I meet Mrs. Coverdale at Lady Tattersall Trottemout’s ‘let off,’

and she snub I--I dare say I deserve it, but I don’t like it; and,

as my evil genius would have it, a man I know relate to I a tale in

regard to her husband’s flirtation with a pretty governess in Italy,

and to tease she I, like a fool, must need go and repeat it to she; and

she take it more seriously to heart than I have expect, and be angry

with I, and--but I see you be get impatient--”



“not at all, not at all,” return Arthur, who, preoccupy with his own

care and anxiety, and nervous in regard to the approach interview

with his cousin, scarcely hear or understand half Lord Alfred be

say, and be only desirous to get rid of he before Kate should

arrive; “no; it’s merely a legal habit I’ve fall into of try to

bring people to the point with as little delay as possible. yes; I

quite understand--Alice tell her husband of your flirt with a pretty

governess, and he say something which offend you.”



“no; it be _i_ who tell the story,” interrupt Lord Alfred, aghast

at the state of confusion his auditor appear to have fall into, and

from which he immediately endeavour to extricate he by commence a

long explanation.



oblige in self-defence to attend, Arthur soon find out that Lord

Alfred’s object in his ill-timed confidence be to ask he to convey his

apology to his sister, whenever he might be write to she; whereupon,

consider the whole affair a mere silly, boyish punctilio, he

replied--



“if you’ll take my advice, my Lord, I should say, get a sheet of

rose-scented paper and a diamond-point pen”--(a sheet of foolscap

and a goose-quill would be more appropriate, be his mental

commentary),--“and sit down and write your penitence to the fair lady

yourself. Alice must be greatly alter for the bad if she do not

grant you a ready pardon.”



“but do you really think--” begin Lord Alfred, in remonstrance.



Arthur cut he short--“I don’t _think_ about it, my dear Courtland; I

feel as certain of the result as if I have already see her answer. do

you suppose I don’t know my own sister, man? but, to come to the point,

here’s her address;” he draw a card from his pocket, hastily scribble

a few word, then hand it to Lord Alfred, continue, “and the soon

you go to your club and write the letter, the soon will your mind be

at ease.”



puzzle, confused, half-alarm and half-pleased with the new idea thus

force upon he, one thing alone seem clear to the bewildered

young nobleman, viz., that for some reason unexplaine his old new

acquaintance be desirous of getting rid of he; and, not have yet

sufficiently acquire the habit and feeling of a man-about-town to

be utterly regardless of the wish of other, he shake Arthur’s hand,

promise to act upon his advice, and depart.



he have scarcely be go five minute when a thunder knock at the

house-door announce that its mistress have return, and ere Arthur have

time to do more than spring to his foot, Kate, attire in the rich

and most become out-of-door costume, enter. as she perceive who

be her guest, she start, and her colour go and come rapidly; but

recover herself by a powerful effort, she advance towards he, and,

extend her hand, observed--



“you be such an unaccustomed visitor, that I could scarcely believe my

eye. when do you return from the continent? I be afraid you expect

to find Alice here, but she and Mr. Coverdale leave I some day since.”



“I return the day before yesterday,” be the reply, “and find a note

from Coverdale, inform I they have leave town; my visit here to-day be

to yourself.”



as he utter the last word, his voice unconsciously assume a sterner

tone, and a shade come across his care-wear feature. an idea suddenly

flash into Kate’s mind, and in a voice which sufficiently attest she

alarm, she exclaimed--



“something be the matter! I be sure of it the moment I see you. _you_

would not come _here_”--(she unconsciously emphasize the word in

italics)--“unless such be the case. what be it? I be strong, I can

bear it--be my father worse?--dying?”



as she speak she sink into a chair, and, fix her eye upon his face,

await his reply.



“you alarm yourself unnecessarily,” he say calmly, almost coldly; “I be

the bearer of no ill tiding: that I have an object in visit you I do

not deny; whether you will consider it a justifiable one I know not;

I regard it in the light of a duty, and therefore, even at the risk of

pain and offend you, it must be perform.” he pause for a reply,

but as Kate remain silent, he continue: “your brother be mere boy,

your father a confirm invalid; circumstance lead I to doubt whether

your--whether Mr. Crane be aware of the character of a person who be,

I be grieve to find, a constant visitor at this house; and I therefore

conceive I have a duty to discharge to one whom I have know from

childhood--one in whose welfare an irrevocable past, which cannot be

forget while memory remain, force I to interest myself. Kate, I

be here to warn you against the insidious advance of that heartless

profligate, Horace D’Almayne!”



as he speak, he fix his eye upon she with a searching glance. Kate

colour, draw herself up haughtily, and appear about to make an angry

reply; check the impulse almost as it arise, she answered--



“I be bind, and indeed most willing to believe, you mean kindly by I;

I will therefore explain to you that which I would not have condescend

to explain to any other man live--that I merely admit Mr. D’Almayne’s

intimacy to oblige my husband, who have become so accustomed to his

society and service, as to consider they indispensable. Mr. D’Almayne

may or may not deserve the harsh epithet you apply to he; but if you

be aware of any circumstance seriously affect his character, it be

to Mr. Crane you should mention they, not to I.”



for a moment Arthur remain silent, then press his hand to his

forehead, he murmur inaudibly, “she can actually stoop to deceit!--is

such a change possible!”



surprise and hurt at his silence, Kate resume: “why do you not speak?

you look at I as if you doubt my assertion!” unheede her question,

Arthur still continue to regard she with an expression in which grief,

surprise, and disapproval, contend for the mastery. at length he say,

in a low deep voice, which cause a shudder to pass through the frame of

his auditor--



“I have suffer much on your account, but such pain as this I never

think to experience!--Kate, you once say you have never attempt to

deceive I--can you say so now?”



“I be at a loss to understand you,” be the reply; and as she grow angry

at what she deem unmerited insult, her self-possession return, and

she speak in her usual cold, hard tone of voice. “I can only repeat what

I before state, that I allow Mr. D’Almayne’s intimacy merely to oblige

my husband. from your manner you still appear to doubt the fact--may I

ask why?” Arthur pause for a moment, then, with an eager and excited

voice, he exclaimed--



“Kate, hear I! I have not take this step lightly, or without due

consideration. I seek not to refer to the past, though that past

be never absent from my memory; but you may imagine it cost I some

resolution to come here to-day, when I tell you that I have rather have

see you lie dead before my eye, feel towards you as I feel

one short year ago, than behold you surround by the luxury of

wealth--know as I do that you have obtain they by the sacrifice

of all that be lovable in woman, by sin against all your good and

noble impulse, by forfeit all that render life aught but one

weary, endless round of care and duty! to look on you as you be

now--to read, as I can read, in every feature of your countenance,

which, though a seal book to other, I have study too long not to

decipher at a glance, trace of that desolation of heart which you have

prepare for yourself--to see you thus, and to know that I be powerless

to help you, and that you must sustain the burden of such an existence

unaided, be to I bitter pain, and I have avoid this house as though

it be plague-stricken. but as I sit through the long hour last night,

strive to weigh dispassionately the past and the present, I arrive at

the conclusion that even yet I owe you a duty, and I come here to-day

actuate only by a desire to warn you, and to save you from a fate, to

contemplate the mere possibility of which inspire I with horror. I

come, regardless of my own feeling, forgetful of my wrong, to do you

a benefit; and now you close your soul against I, and receive I

with hard word and cold look! Kate, I have not deserve this at your

hand!”



“but, indeed--believe I you be mistaken,” reply Kate, eagerly; “I

appreciate and thank you for the interest you still take in one who, as

you truly say, have forfeit every claim on your regard; but your fear

and suspicion be groundless--the intimate foot Mr. D’Almayne have

attain in this house be merely a natural consequence of the trust Mr.

Crane repose in he. why will you not believe the truth of what I tell

you?”



“because it be impossible for I to do so without doubt the evidence

of my own sense,” be the stern reply. “if you require any further

reason for my scepticism it be this: I be in -------- Street,

Pentonville, at two o’clock yesterday!”



“and if you be,” rejoin Kate, with flash eye, “you see nothing

to justify you in entertain such a cruel and unjust suspicion of

one whom _you_ should have be the last to believe likely to sacrifice

_anything_ for love; and whom you might have know well than to

deem an easy prey for the first self-confident libertine who should

condescend to display his butterfly attraction in her presence. I

consider that you have insult I deeply--so deeply as to relieve I

from part of the weight of self-reproach with which I have hitherto

deplore the injury that by my choice of a career I have inflict on

you. you say it pain you to enter this house; I now therefore beg

you to leave it, and will esteem it a favour--the only one I desire of

you--not to enter it again until--yes! until I send for you!”



as she speak she rise hastily, and ring the bell. astonish at the

effect of his speech, and for the moment overpower by her vehemence,

Arthur stand speechlessly regard she. then rouse himself by an

effort, he say in a low, deep voice, that, tremble with suppress

emotion--



“remember the word you have speak! I shall need no second bidding; I

will not enter this house, nor will I see your face again, _until you

send for I!_ and since you thus drive your good friend from you, and

encourage your bitter enemy, may God protect you! and when you see

and repent of your error, may he bless you also!”



as he utter the last word, he seize his hat, hurry from the room,

and ere Kate could sufficiently recover herself to attempt to stop he,

she hear the house-door close behind he: and then the proud woman’s

haughty spirit fail she, and murmuring--“I shall never see he

again--never, never!” she bury her face in her hand, and weep

bitterly.









CHAPTER XLII.--L’EMBARRAS DES RICHESSES.



the reader, if that noble myth who rule the destiny of we poor writer

be possess of an average amount of memory, will recollect that on the

evening when Lord Alfred Courtland entertain Jack Beaupeep and friend

at his comfortable bachelor lodging, a gentleman then first mention,

bear the euphonious patronymic of Le Roux, convey to Monsieur

Guillemard the startling intelligence that the Russian Count Ratrapski

have break the bank in J-------- Street. how, although immediately after

receive this news, Horace D’Almayne have proceed to Lady Trottemout’s

_soirée_, and, accord to his willnot, make himself universally

agreeable, and transact a more than usual amount of mischief, by

bring about the most serious disagreement which have yet occur

between Harry Coverdale and Alice his wife, it must not be suppose that

the intelligence do not interest he. on the contrary, it appeal to

he in his weak point--the pocket; for in that gambling establishment

(of which D’Almayne be part proprietor) have he invest his little

all, and the loss incur by the good fortune of Count Ratrapski

swallow up every farthing he have in the world, leave he nothing but

his debt and his talent to live upon. this position, however, by no

mean possess the charm of novelty for our excellent young friend; on

the contrary, as it be a favourite theory of his--which he never lose

any opportunity of reduce to practice--that it be the duty of those

who have money to support those who have not, he rather prefer be

insolvent; and, paradoxical as it may appear, consider himself good

off when he be bad off--for then he be oblige to exert all his

energy to ensure that some purse well fill than his own should

relax its string to provide for his necessity.



thus, on the very day on which Arthur Hazlehurst have his unsatisfactory

interview with Kate Crane, the husband of that proud beauty meet by

appointment, at an office not far from the Royal Exchange, Monsieur

Guillemard,--Mr. Vondenthaler, a belgian capitalist,--Mr. Bonus Nugget,

a man well know upon ’Change,--the Hon. Captain O’Brien,--and last,

though not least, Horace D’Almayne. Mr. Crane having seat himself,

after undergo the ceremony of introduction to Mr. Vondenthaler, who

be the only member of the party unknown to he, D’Almayne open the

proceeding by observing--



“well, gentleman, I be glad to tell you that everything be progress

as we could wish, and that my previous calculation, which I have the

honour of lay before you at our last meeting, appear likely not

only to be verify, but exceed. Mr. Vondenthaler inform I that

the application for share from the principal foreign merchant be

incessant; and Mr. Nugget and Captain O’Brien will tell you the same in

regard to their own connection. be it not so, Captain?”



“indeed, and it be, thin,” reply the gentleman thus accost, who

possibly, from his having mix so much with the aristocracy of Europe

generally, speak with a strong irish accent. “Bedad, sir, the way they

come tumble in be perfectly astonishing; ’tis, upon I conscience!”



“the only thing that remain then, before we proceed to issue the share

and receive deposit, be to decide how many we shall allot to each

director _ex officio_, and how many you gentleman may desire to retain

for--your friend,” observe D’Almayne, glance expressively towards

Mr. Crane as he speak.



“in regard to the share to be hold by director, I would suggest five

hundred,” begin Mr. Crane.



“_Das ist gut_; dat shall be he,” mutter Mr. Vondenthaler.



“I’ll not object to that same,” exclaim the Captain, “if you leave

a thunder wide margin for the share we may retain for our friend;

for, to be plain with ye, gentleman, my good friend in the world,

and that’s terence O’Brien, mean to go in for this business in real

earnest; and if I cann’t invest capital that will take five figure to

write, bedad I’d rather be out of it altogether.”



“ten thousand, which I presume be the sum you hint at, Captain O’Brien,

could not I think be object to,” observe Mr. Bonus Nugget, as if

£10,000 be a mere cab-fare.



“_Mais oui_, we will all demand so much as he, he be so small;

_n’est-ce pas, mon cher?_” interpose Monsieur Guillemard, favour

Horace D’Almayne with a grimace indicative of the tender affection.



“if I might be allow--if I might venture to suggest,” begin Mr. Crane,

timidly, “I would propose that, at so early a stage in the affair, no

limit should be place to the number of share the director may hold.

I be, ahem! a--myself I be a man who have be tolerably fortunate in my

commercial speculation, and might be dispose--in fact, I may say I

be dispose--to embark an amount of capital considerably above the sum

lately mention by Captain O’Brien.”



“Sir! your sentiment do you honour! Sir, I’m proud of your

acquaintance; you’re not one to do thing by half, I see. I like plain

speak--the speculation’s a davlish good speculation, or you would

not find such man as Mr. Vondenthaler and my friend Bonus Nugget in

it. we’re go to give our valuable time and trouble to work the thing

ship-shape; and bedad, sir, if we’re not to profit by it, I’d jist like

to know who should!”



“yes; that be all very well for you, O’Brien,” observe Mr. Nugget,

speak with an air of authority; “but I happen to know a thing or two.

Mr. Crane, gentleman, be--I say it to his face--able to go down to his

banker, and draw a cheque, which they will honour, for more money than

any two of we could raise between we. very well; now it’s no news to any

of we to be tell that ‘money be power.’ but if Mr. Crane think, because he

can embark his £50,000,--or I believe I might raise the figure as

high again without overstate the matter,--that he be go to ride

rough-shod over the practical man who have start this scheme, and to

take the lion’s share of the enormous profit that he be sharp enough to

foresee must accrue, I for one beg to tell he I willn’t stand it.”



“Ya! ya! _das ist gut!_ Ve have not start to be shod rough by Cranes!

Herr Bonus he know a thing! _das ist recht und gut!_ Ve vill not

be rough by crane!” mutter Mr. Vondenthaler through the thick

hay-colour moustachio invariably wear by belgian capitalist.



“_Mais oui_, you have reason, Monsieur Vondenthaler, _mon ami_: but

if you yourself have mistake, _n’est-ce pas?_” interpose Monsieur

Guillemard, eagerly. “I be assure Monsieur Crane be not _un homme

comme ça_; he shall not _se promener a cheval_--vot you call ride on a

horseback ovaire we _du tout; au contraire_, zie grate skim whom we

be zie undairetaker for, shall advance herself on his capital for zie

good of we all. _Voyez vous, cher Monsieur Bonous!_”



“’pon I conscience, now ye’re the first set of man I ever yet clap

eye on that make a fuss about take money when it be offer to

’em!” exclaim the Hon. captain O’Brien, surprise into a strong

brogue than he have yet allow to appear. “sure, now, by the time

we’ve tunnel under the whole of Arabia Pethreea, and fling our

Britannia-metal tubular bridge across the Persian Gulf, we’ll find money

grow pretty tight with we.”



“as there seem some difference of opinion on the point,” return Mr.

Bonus Nugget, “I would suggest that we summon a general meeting of all

the director, and appoint a manage committee to decide such matter

for the future.”



this proposition be agree to _nem. con._, and a day having be fix

for their next meeting, D’Almayne began:--



“in my capacity as secretary, I have to call your attention to one point

before this meeting break up. I have, in accordance with a resolution

pass at the last board, go into the current outlay, and find that to

pay the engineer now survey the portion of the line already decide

on, and other expense which I will not detain you by enumerate, the

account at our banker be overdrawn. I would propose, therefore, that

two of the director should sign a cheque for £3000, to be place to the

company’s credit.”



“well say five,” interpose Nugget; “it don’t do to be overdraw our

account; I’ve know a trifle like that ruin a speculation as promise

even as the present one. don’t let this occur again, D’Almayne; I can

let you have money at any moment, as you be well aware.”



“_ya! ya!_ or I, vin you please; you must not starve he for no

account,” chime in the belgian capitalist.



“certainly, £5000 should be pay in at once,” observe Mr. Crane,

produce a cheque-book. “I shall have much pleasure in advance the

sum, if you gentleman will sanction my so do.”



this both Nugget and the Belgian protest against, each urge

their claim as originator of the scheme; but O’Brien silence their

opposition, and settle the matter by exclaim in his off-hand

manner--



“let Mr. Crane have his way, sir!--he’s a fine fellow entirely--a

liberal and enlightened man he be--one of the merchant prince of this

great counthry; and though I’d the misfortune to be bear an aristocrat

myself, I’ve no class bigotry about I. I admire a true Briton when I

meet with one; and whoever wish to bully and browbeat that Briton in

my presence, must do it some time when Terence O’Brien ben’t there to

stand up for he. shake hand, Mr. Crane--I’m proud to know you. take

this pen and write, sir! Browbeat a man like that, indeed!--’pon my

conscience, what next I wonder!”



and so, under cover of the Captain’s blustering, Mr. Crane sign a

cheque for £5000, for which D’Almayne give he a receipt in the name of

the company; then bow to his co-director, and exchange a word or

two aside with D’Almayne, he depart. as the sound of his retreating

footstep die away in the distance, D’Almayne, quietly pocket the

cheque, observed--



“if we can but get the share to sell for--say twenty thousand, the

speculation will not pay badly. you see, Guillemard, these crafty

islander--these denizen of ‘_perfide Albion_’--their pocket be not

impregnable when you assault they judiciously. five thousand pound from

one man be not such a bad morning’s work!”



“thrue for you, I boy!” exclaim the Irishman; “by the power, a few

more such morning’ work will make man of we, if it please providence

to keep we out of jail so long; but it’s a dangerous game your playing.

sure now there’s jist five of we here present--why wouldn’t we take a

thousand a-piece, and make ourselves scarce without any more ado? I’m

content for one, bedad.”



“you’ll do nothing of the sort, Terence,” be the reply: “for two very

good reason: one be, that if you remain quiet and follow my lead, I

will enable you to bolt--if it come to bolt--with £10,000 instead of

one; and the other, that Mr. Crane’s cheque be very safely button up

in my pocket, to be apply as I think well; and any man who attempt

to take it from I will become practically acquainted with the merit

of this ingenious little instrument,” and as he speak he draw from his

breastpocket a small, beautifully-finish revolve pistol, whereupon

the individual term Nugget interpose by observing--



“Nonsense, D’Almayne, put that thing away: we’re not in New Orleans,

man; and the report of that would blow our scheme to the devil long

before the bullet have penetrate O’Brien’s thick skull. but really

there be nothing to disagree about that I can see: it’s quite clear,

gentleman, that D’Almayne know perfectly well what he’s do, and that

our interest could not be in well hand. we meet again on Friday.

D’Almayne, you’ll see I to-night in J-------- Street; and now that

we’re in fund again, Ratrapski will be as good as a fortune to we:

a man do not break the bank twice.” then, nod familiarly to the

other, Mr. Bonus Nugget resume his usual “City” look (worth five

hundred a-year to he at the most moderate computation), and depart.



“Terence, never look sulky, man; I mean no harm; what I say be as

much for your good as my own,” begin D’Almayne, in a conciliatory

tone. “come, I want you and Guillemard to dine at Blackwall, to meet an

unfledged lordling, to whom I’ll allow you to sell a horse, if you like;

and you may do a little bit of ‘turf’ business too, if he’ll bite; only

it must be do in a quiet, gentlemanly way mind, because I’ve ulterior

view in regard to my young friend: he have a taste for the club in

J-------- Street--you understand?”



“I believe ye, I boy! an it’s a fine child ye be intirely; and the

way ye’ve cut yer wisdom tooth be a credit to yer bless mother--always

suppose ye ever possess such a respectible relative,” be the

Hibernian’s reply.



“by the way, if you’re really go in for the horse business,” resume

D’Almayne, meditatively, “you may as well do the thing properly. get

a flash trap, you know, and drive we down; and--who’s that

sporting-look young fellow you have pack you at Epsom--dark curly

hair, and grey hawk’s eye?”



“oh, Phil Tirrett, the great Yorkshire breeder’s son; he be his father’s

London agent, and a very promising young--”



“Scoundrel,” interpose D’Almayne, “I read it in his face. however,

you’ll want somebody to back up your lie, and he’ll pass with such

green boy as we shall have to-day; so bring he. let I see--it’s now

two o’clock--call for I at the Pandemonium at five; and, excuse I, but

drop the irish blackguard, and assume the foreign _militaire_ as much as

you conveniently can. remember, you’re captain in the austrian service,

and I be in your regiment, your sub., for a year.”



“Bedad! it’s as well you remind I of that same, for it have slip my

memory some way,” be the affable reply, as, arrange his auburn, not

to say red, hair under his hat, the gallant Captain prepare to take

himself off. Ere he do so, however, he chance to cast his eye on the

belgian capitalist, who be amuse his leisure moment by perform

some intricate manœuvre with a pack of card, an occupation which he

interrupt by slap Vondenthaler on the back with such force that a

covey of card fly out of the pack about the room.



“what devil’s dodge be you plan there, you old sinner!” he

exclaim; “let’s look at ye!” he continue, seize he by the chin,

and turn his head so that the light fall upon his countenance;

“bedad! they moustachio alter you surprising! nobody that have not know

ye as I’ve do, since I could handle a dice-box, and that be before

I be into I teen, would recognise in Mr. Vondenthaler, the belgian

merchant, Le Roux the old _croupier!_”



“leave he alone,” observe D’Almayne; “Le Roux’s a steady, sensible

man, and one I have a great respect for; he know his work, and do it

well and quietly; and I’d back his long head against your noisy talent

(for the ‘gift of the gab,’ as you term it, be a noisy talent and

a dangerous one) any day, Captain.” then, turn to Le Roux, he

said--“The bank will re-open to-night, and we shall be there in force.

mind the Champagne’s well than the last batch. let everything be

in first-rate style, and spare no expense. Guillemard, you hear the

rendezvous? five o’clock, monsieur, _au revoir_.”



so say, D’Almayne bow with as much scrupulous politeness to the

worshipful fraternity of ------ man of science he be quit, as if he

have be leave the council-chamber of a prince. call a Hansom cab,

this industrious and zealous young man drive to his west-end lodging,

and exchange his suit of quiet black, in which he have dress the

man-of-business character he have be pleased to enact, for more

butterfly garment, go down to a certain fashionable club, where he

feel sure of meet Lord Alfred Courtland, and find he accordingly,

but by no mean in the amiable, docile frame of mind in which he usually

rejoice. the hour precede that at which D’Almayne enter the

club have be spend by Lord Alfred in concoct, pursuant to Arthur

Hazlehurst’s advice, a penitent letter to Alice Coverdale--a composition

which have cost he much trouble and anxiety, and wherein he have

endeavour in some measure to justify himself, by shift as much

of the blame as he truthfully could on to the shoulder of Horace

D’Almayne; and he have just close and dispatch this accusatory epistle

when, as though to overwhelm he with shame at such a betrayal of one

who profess himself, and whom in great measure he still believe to

be, his friend, his aspersed mentor seat himself opposite to he, and

address he by his usual endear epithet of “_mon cher_,” invite

he to dine with he that day, and meet a few choice spirit at

Blackwall.



“you’re very kind, but you really must excuse I,” be Lord Alfred’s

reply. “I’ve be knock about a good deal lately, and begin to want a

little quiet.”



“yes, I know,” be D’Almayne’s rejoinder; “such be always one’s morning

theory--but one never put it in practice; when eight o’clock come, _il

faut diner!_ seriously, however, I cann’t let you off. I have ask

two or three man to meet you, who be most anxious to make your

acquaintance”--(this be _strictly_ true),--“and who will be awfully

savage if you don’t come.”



“Come--of course he’ll come, and so will I too, if anybody will ask I,

and there’s a lark in hand--what do Milton say?--



                   ‘a bird in hand be well far,



                   than two that in the bush be.’



Fine poem, _Paradise Lost_. by the way, do you ever hear my riddle on

that head? ‘why be the fact of the content of a backgammon-board having

be throw out of the window like Milton’s _chef-d’œuvre?_’ do you give
