it up? because it’s a _pair o’ dice lose_.’ none so dusty that--eh?

for a commoner like I? we poor devil that have to grind all day to

procure our modest chop and our unassuming pint of London porter, cann’t

be expect to say such brilliant thing as you noble swell, who have

have nothing to do but cultivate your understanding ever since you come

into the world with gold spoon in your mouth. but you have not tell I

what’s up yet.”



here the speaker, who be none other than the facetious Jack Beaupeep,

pause for want of breath, and D’Almayne interpose with a reply to his

question--



“the particular event exalt at the moment you join we be a bachelor

dinner at Blackwall to-day, for which I be try to beat up a few

recruit; let I hope you will enlist under my banner, and, with such a

reinforcement, I be sure Lord Alfred will surrender at discretion.”



“all serene!” rejoin the voluble Jack; “I be ‘to let unfurnished’

(with a dinner)--and let I tell you a Blackwall feed be a special mercy

that’s not to be sneeze at. Come, Alfred, my boy, merge the haughty

noble in the jolly-good-fellow till further notice, and say ‘I will.’”



“have it your own way. since you’re both determined on my capture, it’s

hopeless to resist,” say Lord Alfred, his feeble attempt at reformation

completely defeat; “but I certainly have make up my mind to spend a

quiet evening.”



“so have I,” return Jack; “but then I do not expect such luck as to

come in for a noisy one. what time, and where do we meet?”



“at the Pandemonium, at five o’clock,” be D’Almayne’s reply; “and mind

you be both punctual.”









CHAPTER XLIII.--EATING WHITEBAIT.



Nero fiddle while Rome blaze! we possess the record of the main

fact, but all detail connect with that memorable performance have

perish in the lapse of age. we can imagine, however, that the novelty

and horrid grandeur of the situation by no mean interfere with the

skill and execution of the imperial amateur; but rather add a force

and brilliancy to his playing, for which it may not have be usually

remarkable. if he have at all a turn for improvisation, an opportunity

then offer for his make a great hit; the roaring of the flame, the

crash of fall building, the coarse laughter of a brutal soldiery,

mingle with the shriek of woman and child, and with the shout

change to the half-curse, half-prayer, of the death agony of brave,

true-hearted man, strive to rescue the helpless one, and perish in

the exercise of their noble daring, all must have afford a suggestive

theme for the _crescendo and diminuendo_ of the tyrant’s catgut, which

may have be hand down to posterity, until the tradition may have

furnish the thesis of that classic and artistic composition, the

“Battle of Prague.”



everybody consider Nero a hateful tyrant, and everybody may be in

the main right; although good Dr. Goldsmith, in his interesting roman

history (which have be perpetually “abridge for the use of school”

 ever since it be write, and be not half short enough yet), have

probably apply too deep a coating of lamp-black even to Nero. but,

though as manner and custom change, the outward seeming of thing

vary with they, human nature, too bad ever to be all good, and too

good to be all bad, remain much the same, despite the preaching of

Paul, and the watering-pot of Apollos.



thus, while in the heart of mighty London vice fill model prison

with the recklessly deprave, or, far bad, the recklessly

hypocritical--while hospital-ward teem with those comparatively

fortunate victim of disease and improvidence whom some good Samaritan

have thus far rescue, when a frightful majority be die untended in

reek alley and other hotbed of pestilence--while covetousness and

hatred be scarcely restrain from break forth into rapine and

murder by the strong arm of the law--my Lord Alfred Courtland, and the

leech who seek to prey upon his youth and inexperience, drive

down to Blackwall to nibble a small fry of ridiculous little

fish, envelop in batter, call whitebait, and esteem, for some

undiscoverable reason, a delicacy.



exactly as the clock strike five, a dark, well-appoint drag, with

three bay and a chestnut--all thorough-breed, or thereabout--draw up at

the entrance to the Pandemonium. Captain O’Brien, hand the rein to a

dark-whisker, good-look young fellow, who be his companion on the

box, descend, and enter the club, be introduce by D’Almayne to

Lord Alfred Courtland and Jack Beaupeep; the first mention individual

acknowledge his salutation by the slight possible removal of the

hat, together with an all but invisible motion of the head, the latter

by a profound salaam, together with the diffident remark--



“Sir, you do I proud.”



“not at all, sir, not at all; on the contrary, it’s proud I be to make

your acquaintance, and you a mimber of the government, too. do ye know

Smith O’Brien, now?” not wait a reply, he continued--“Oh, he’s a

great legislathur entirely; and sure they that don’t die first will live

to see he prime-minister of this country, one of these fine morning;

and a _prime_ minister he’ll make, sure! ‘justice to Ireland’ will be

find engrave in copper-plate on his heart, by any gentleman who may

have the pleasure of attend the _post-mortem_ examination of his

remain, and long life to ’em!”



“be we wait for any one?” inquire Horace, fearful lest his

hibernian associate should disgust Lord Alfred by his offensive

familiarity at first start. “Guillemard have, I see, already take his

seat. have you any objection to pull up at the Guards’ Club, O’Brien?

there be three or four army man who have promise to come, and your

drag will carry they easily.”



the Captain agree to this--as indeed he appear willing to agree

to any and everything suggest by D’Almayne--they take their place;

O’Brien insist on Lord Alfred succeed to the box-seat, vacate for

that purpose by the dark-whisker, hawkeyed youth, who be none other

than Phil Tirrett, the horse-breeder’s son, whom Horace D’Almayne have

designate as a very promising young scoundrel--a style of character

which he be so well able to recognise, and so thoroughly competent

to form an opinion upon, that we feel convinced he only do the young

gentleman’s merit justice.



by no mean captivate by O’Brien’s manner or address, Lord Alfred be

at first haughty and monosyllabic; but perceive that D’Almayne be we

scrupulously polite to this son of Erin as to the most polished member

of the fashionable world, it occur to he that in his character

of man-about-town the correct thing be to assume a general

languid citizen-of-the-worldship; and, as a duty to his presume

imperturbability, to appear, not all thing to all man, but the same

thing to every man. thus, rouse himself, he pay a die-away and

meaningless compliment to the workmanlike manner in which Captain

O’Brien--“Ar--put his team along, and--ar--the correct style of the

whole affair.”



this lead to an equestrian and sport rhapsody on the part of the Hon.

Terence, intersperse with anecdote--strange, if true--of the dam and

the sire, and the own brother and sister, of the individual member

of the team, and especially of the chestnut, which have been--“The

sweetest thing, sir, across a stiff country that ever man ride; no day

be too long and no burst too fast for he, bedad! and the bitter

moment ever I, Terence O’Brien, know (bar the loss of I maternal

grandmother, by spontaneous combustion, from fortuitously sit down

upon a lighted cinder, which have providentially pop out of the fire

for that purpose), be when I stake he above the near hock at Melton,

last season; and he’s never be fit to gallop since, or it ben’t in

harness ye’d see he now--and he cost I a cool £400, and worth all

the money now, if he be but sound,” &c. &c.



the witty author of _Tristram Shandy_, in introduce to the reader that

most lovable of humorist, my Uncle Toby, have discourse eloquently on

the various hobby-horse which take possession of, and enslave, the mind

of man. fortification, which be my Uncle Toby’s mania, engross

his thought, and influence his conversation, until nothing but his

simplicity and kindness of heart save he from degenerate into a

complete bore; but when a man’s hobby-horse be the equine animal itself,

you can no more unhorse he than if he be--as assuredly he ought to

have be, if mind and body have bear a proper affinity to each other--a

centaur. O’Brien _be_ a centaur, and having once mount his hobby,

he ride he all the way to Blackwall, to Lord Alfred’s extinction, or

thereabout; but consider that a certain amount of “turf” adhere to

the character of a man-about-town, he bear the infliction like a--well,

suppose, though we have foresworn slang as low, we for this once say--a

brick.



three guardsman, and a young heavy dragoon, who live to consume beer

and cigar, and produce moustachio and stupidity be duly add to the

party; and by the time they reach Blackwall everybody grow hungry, and

prepare to do ample justice to the whitebait. of course, everybody

have at some period of their earthly career eat a Blackwall dinner, and

such feed be all exactly alike. First appear a course of fish,

enough to constitute a dinner in itself: sea-fish, river-fish,

pond-fish--fish boil, fry, stew, and bedevil in various way,

which it would require the knowledge of the suppose inventor of cook

himself to detail; then come the wonderful whitebait themselves, their

stupid little body envelop in skeleton dress of batter; and then

fish be ignore, and develop, accord to the “Vestiges of Creation”

 theory, into the high form of animal, into which the high form of

all--man--pitch cannibal-like, until the culinary cosmos be resolve

into its pristine chaotic element. and around this hecatomb of

slaughter zoology and feast humanity skip nimble waiter, furnish

with bottle of every shape and hue; for, since Noah first discover

the seductive beverage, wine-bibbing have be a levelling principle,

by mean of which the lord of the creation have be accustom

to assimilate themselves to their subject the brute, despite the

hydraulic pressure of Father Matthew, and all other who have pledge

themselves to cold-water such degrading custom. and, indeed, we fear

that of the two party whose respective motto might be “_in vino

verita_” and “truth lie at the bottom of a well,” the latter will

continue to constitute the minority until the end of the chapter; or, as

Jack Beaupeep express the same sentiment, when D’Almayne propound

to he a somewhat similar theory, be “safe to kick the bucket, if they

don’t put their foot in it in any other way:” but that misguide young

man not only make, but rejoice in shock bad pun.



the dinner have be do ample justice to--the wine (and their name

be legion) have not be at all neglect--Lord Alfred have become quite

intimate with the guardsman, who, as the wine unlock their tongue,

begin, in a quiet, gentlemanly way, to quiz everything and everybody,

especially the heavy dragoon, who rejoice in the patronymic of

Gambier--a name on which the other military gentleman be pleased to

exercise their wit whenever they address he. as, for example, 1st

guardsman, _loquitur_:----



“I say, Beaupeep, have you hear Fred’s (2nd guardsman’s) last?”



“I haven’t even hear his first,” be the rejoinder.



“no; I should think not,” continue no. 1; “he make that when he be

quite a _baby in arm_.”



“Ye may as well say before he could speak, while ye be about it,”

 suggest O’Brien.



“Bravo, Captain! you willn’t well that,” say the narrator.



“however, Fred’s last and bad be this--‘Why be the gallant cornet

opposite, an addition to any mess-table?’ do you give it up? ‘because

he’s half game and half beer!’”



“I dare say it’s very funny,” mutter the heavy subject of the jest,

“but I don’t see the point myself.”



“it’s a _pint_ of half-and-half,” observe Jack Beaupeep, explanatorily.



“or ‘heavy’ wet, if he be out in the rain,” add guardsman no. 2.



“talk of heavy wet, put _I_ in mind of come down with the dust.

when be you go to perform that operation in regard to the Windsor

Steeple-chase?” inquire the cornet, surlily; who, not have anything

witty to reply to his assailant, substitute instead the most unpleasant

topic he could select.



“that be soon answer,” be the rejoinder; “whenever you’ll make a

fresh match between the horse, and give Rattletrap a chance of show

Teacaddy the way home, when he’s not be prick in shoeing by a

confound blacksmith.”



“oh! if that’s all, you may hand over the cash to-morrow morning,”

 return the dragoon; “the mare’s in first-rate order, and I’m game to

back she for a match, hurdle-race, steeple-chase, or what you will,” be

the confident reply.



“ah! be it a steeple-chase now, ye’re talk of?” interrupt O’Brien,

fill himself a tumbler of Claret; “sure an’ I’ve get a horse I’d be

proud to enter, if it ben’t jist put I hand in your pocket and

take the money out of ’em; for if he’s in the race, I’d name the winner

before they start.”



“he must be a wonderful animal, Captain,” observe the first guardsman;

“high-pressure, express train style of quadrupe, eh?”



“furnish with a _screw_-propeller, more likely,” add his companion,

ironically.



“Faith, an’ ye’re wrong there entirely: it’s little of the screw ye’ll

find about Broth-of-a-boy. talk about railroad, indeed, I never know

what flying be till the day I first gallop he in the Phoenix Park. I

only wish I’d have he in Spain, when I serve with the legion of Sir

De Lacy Evans; it ben’t overtaken and kilt entirely by their blackguard

dragoon I’d have be then--though it’s little but hard blow and hard

swearing they get out of I, as it be, the Lord be praise!”



“hear, hear! a story, a story!”



“military reminiscence of Captain O’Brien! order, order!”



“silence for the noble anecdote!”



“out with it, Captain!” &c. &c., be some of the exclamation with

which the Hibernian’s last speech be hail by various member of the

party, upon whom the whitebait (?) be begin to tell.



thus urge, that worthy, clear his throat by a sip at the Claret,

which half empty the tumbl, began:--



“well, boy” (here he catch a look from Horace D’Almayne, which cause

he, nothing abash, to add parenthetically), “if in the congeniality

of good fellowship you will permit I to call ye so, the story’s nothing

so very wonderful, after all--it be just a bit of a spree, do ye see,

nothing more; but such as it be ye’re welcome to it”--(polite aside

from Jack Beaupeep for Lord Alfred’s benefit--“You’re _too_ liberal,

really!”) “I be with Sir De Lacy Evans in Spain, captain in a

regiment of lancer; a rare set of rattle dog they be, too--up

to everything, from rob a henroost to burn town and sack

monasteries”--(Beaupeep aside--“A decidedly _sac-religious_ act that

last!”)



“on one occasion, we be station at a place distant about four mile

from a village occupy by a strong body of Carlists; well, sir, for

several night run, our sentinel on the side towards the village

be assassinate--stab through the heart they be! we have ’em

double, two man to each post; bedad, the only improvement that effect

be, we get two man murder instead of one; and yet the scamp that do

it always contrive to get away clear and clean--we never so much as

clap eye on he! well, I bother and puzzle the matter over, and

thought of this thing and that thing, and at last I get hold of a notion

I fancy might work well; so I cut off to our Colonel, and ‘colonel,’

say I, ‘with your kind permission, I think I can stop these

assassination.’ ‘what be it, O’Brien?’ say he, ‘you’re a clever,

rise young officer, and a man that bid fair to be an ornament to his

profession;’ but I willn’t trouble ye with the illegant eulogy he be so

polite as to pronounce upon I that day”--(“Hear, hear!” from Beaupeep

and the guardsmen). “so I jist obtain his permission to select two

well-mount trooper out of my own company, and leave to do what

I please with they and myself during the night, and that be all I

want. I happen at that time to have a particularly fast mare--a

sweet thing she be, bay, with black point, nearly thorough-breed, a

head like an antelope, and as to pace, ’gad there ben’t a horse in

the regiment could come near she. before nightfall I pick out my two

trooper--sharp, plucky young fellow, that I know I could depend upon

if it come to hard fighting, each of they well mount; and I take care

to see that their horse and the mare be properly feed and water, so

as to be fit for a stiff burst; then I amuse myself with sharpen the

point of my lance till it be as keen as a razor. about a stone’s

throw from the post where the sentry they use to assassinate be

stationed”--(“Of course, the same man every night till further notice,”

 murmur Jack Beaupeep, continue his run commentary)--“there be

a thicket of olive bush and other shrub; behind this, as soon as it

grow dusk, I post my man with the horse, while I avail myself of a

rise in the ground to advance nearer, and lie down, hide from sight

by a stunt bush or two. well, I wait and wait, and watch and

watch, so that a mouse could not have stir without my noticing it;

but nothing do I see, except the shadowy figure of the sentinel pacing

up and down in the moonlight, as though he be the discontent ghost

of one of his murder comrades”--(“Very pretty--quite poetical, I

declare!” from Beaupeep).



“well, at last, just about a quarter of an hour before daybreak, which

be the dark period of the night in those latitude, whether I have

doze off for a minute I don’t know, but I be startle by a noise

differ from the monotonous tread of the sentinel, and which sound

to my ear like the cracking of a dry twig; in another moment I perceive

a dark, round object move upon the ground, which I soon make out to

be the head of a man draw himself along, snake-fashion, upon his

stomach--while so close have he get to the unconscious soldier that I

perceive, if I would save the poor lad’s life, not an instant be to be

lose. I therefore give the signal to my trooper to come up, and draw

my sword, rush forward to secure the assassin. as I do so, a light

active figure spring up from the ground, and brandish a long keen

dagger, make a furious stab at the sentry; but, fortunately, my approach

confuse the scoundrel, so that he miss his stroke, and, instead

of kill the man, merely inflict a slight flesh wound of no

consequence. notwithstanding his surprise,--for, as the soldier

afterwards declare to I, his antagonist seem to have rise out of

the earth,--the sentry attempt to seize he; but he contrive to slip

out of his hand like an eel, and before I could reach the spot, have

disappear in the darkness. in another moment the dull sound of a

horse’s foot gallop over the turf prove to I that he be away; but

my own horse be bring up, I spring into the saddle, snatch my

lance from the trooper who hold it, and order the man to follow I,

start in pursuit. ’pon I conscience, gentleman, I niver reflect

on I feeling at that critical moment but it make I--ah, well! I’ll

trouble your Lordship for the Claret.”









CHAPTER XLIV.--LORD ALFRED COURTLAND sow a few WILD OATS.



Captain O’Brien, having finish his glass of Claret, and turn up the

point of his carroty moustache, thus resume his story:--



“at first it be as much as I be able to do to track the fellow by the

sound of his horse’s hoof upon the soft turf, but I trust a good deal

to the mare’s instinct to follow the horse before she; fortunately we

have not very far to go before we get upon the hard village road, and

then there be nothing to do but ride he down, for the grey light that

precede the dawn enable I to see his figure distinctly. but that same

ride he down be easy to talk about than to do, for the scoundrel

have obtain a long start of we, and though I be well mount, I soon

perceive that he be equally so. away we rattle at a slashing

pace, and for about a mile the two trooper manage to keep up pretty

tolerably; but by the time we have ride rather more than twice that

distance, I find my friend be gradually draw ahead, and that if I

wait for my man, I should soon have see my last of he; so give

the mare her head, and a trifling reminder with the spur beside, I leave

they, and they gradually tail off in the distance, until a turn of

the road hide they altogether. in my time, I’ve ride steeple-chase,

hurdle-race, and every specie of race that the divil ever invent,

but a fast thing than that morning’s ride I never see nor hear of.

the horse be well match as to speed, mine be rather the fresh,

but then the Carlist be the light weight; the thing could not have

be fair. however, after a couple of mile or so more, I be glad to

perceive that I be gradually creep up to he; and I suppose he begin

to suspect it too, for, as the light increase, I see he every now and

then look round suspiciously, and urge his horse still fast at each

successive glance. about a mile from the village, I have gain upon he

so decidedly that it be evident I must overtake he before he could

reach its friendly shelter. apparently he be of the same opinion, for,

before I be aware of his intention, he unsling a carbine he carry,

pull up suddenly, and turn in his saddle, level it, and take a

deliberate aim at I. everybody that know Terence O’Brien, know he’s

no coward, but ’pon my conscience, at that moment, I wouldn’t have

be sorry to have turn my horse’s head, and cry quit with he;

however, a bullet be a style of article that don’t allow a man much

time for deliberation, so see it be a case of hit or miss, I only

ram in the spur hard, bend down my head, couch my lance, and

gallop on. Bang go the carbine; and almost before the report reach

I, a bullet whistle through the air; I hear a sort of ‘thud,’ as

when an arrow strike a straw target, and feel my throat-strap suddenly

tightened,--the messenger of death have pass through my cap, sever

a lock of hair and just raise the skin, without do I the slight

injury; but it be a close shave in every sense of the word. well, as

soon as the scoundrel perceive that his shot have fail, he feel that

his only chance be to exert every nerve to reach the village before I

overtake he; so, fling away his discharged carbine, he dash on,

urge his fail steed with voice and spur, and even, as I gain

upon he, with the point of his dagger. another minute bring we in

sight of the village, where a sleepy sentinel be pace up and down the

road in front of a sort of toll-house. astonish at the sight of two

man ride like lunatic, he first attempt to close the bar fix

there to defend the entrance to the village, then, recognise my

companion, he pause, and before he have come to any decision, we have

dash past he--my friend obligingly desire he to ‘shoot the dog of

a Christino,’ as we fly by; an order which, fortunately for I, he be

too much confused to execute, discharge his firelock harmlessly into

the air. as we pass the toll-house, I be not above two horse-length

from my antagonist, and gain upon he at every stride. any

feeling of compunction I might have have at the thought of slay a

fellow-creature, have be effectually put to flight by the shot he have

so deliberately fire at I; thus when I find myself at length come

up with he, I grasp my lance more firmly, set my tooth, drive

the spur into the mare, and dash at he. in another moment I have

overtake he, the point of my lance enter his back between the

shoulder-blade, and by the mere impetus of my onward career I drive

it through he. as the weapon transfix he, the poor wretch utter a

yell of agony, and fall forward on his horse’s neck a corpse. if you’ll

believe I, gentleman, it ben’t till I’d thus square account with the

rascal for our sentry that he’d murder in cold blood, that the idea

ever strike I how I be to get back again, with the carlist village

between I and our camp. the first thing I try, be to pull my lance

out of the dead assassin, as he lie on his face in the middle of the

road; but the more I pull, the more it wouldn’t come--I’d drive it

in with such force; and, at last, with a wrench I give it, I snap the

staff in two. see there be no time to lose, I be about to turn my

mare’s head in a homeward direction, when it occur to I that they’d

never believe in the regiment that I’d kill the fellow;”--(“Not an

improbable thing,” soliloquised Beaupeep)--“so I jump down, secure

the scoundrel’s sash and dagger, remount, and ride off. as I expect,

the sentinel’s shot have rouse the village, and just as I get back, a

company of soldier be turn out, half-awake and in great confusion,

and the lieutenant contrive to draw a file across the road to stop I.

there be nothing for it but impudence; so, draw my sabre, I wave it

in the air, then look round, as if I’d get a regiment at my back, I

sing out, ‘come on, boys!--trot, gallop, charge!’ and dash at ’em,

cut down the lieutenant, and what between their fright and their

confusion, break their line, ride slap through ’em, escape by good

luck half-a-dozen bullet that be send after I, and should have get

clear away but for a patrol of dragoon that come up on hear the

firing, and who, learn how the matter stand, give chase. as their

horse be fresh, while the race she’d win have pump every puff of

wind out of my mare, they soon overtake I; and after two or three

minute’ hard fighting, a cut in the sword-arm disable I, and I be

force to give in. well, they carry I back to the village, settle

that I be a spy, besides having kill Don Pedrillo Velasquez de

Hatadoro, or some such jargon; for which double crime I be to be hang

at noon. owe to the fortunate arrival of my lancer and a regiment

of rifle, however, that event be indefinitely postpone, but I’ll

mercifully spare you the recital of the scrimmage, which end in our

take the village; and, as talk be dry work, I’ll just thank you for

the Claret, D’Almayne, I boy!”



much cheering and acclamation follow the conclusion of the Captain’s

story, under cover whereof Jack Beaupeep insinuate to Lord Alfred his

opinion that the history in question be well suit to the capacity

of the marines than to that of able-bodied seaman, to which his

Lordship, quote Horace, reply, that “Judaeus Apella” might believe

it, but that he do not; which, as he say it in the original language

of the roman poet, elicit from his companion the remark that it

sound very pretty, and he wish that _he_ understand Dutch.



“but about this say race; what be it to be, and when be it to come

off?” inquire the heavy cornet, who possess every requisite except

brain to become a first-rate blackleg.



“do you really mean that you’ve a horse you’d like to enter for, say a

hurdle-race, captain O’Brien?” observe the first guardsman, think

the gallant Hibernian have be rhapsodise, and desirous of expose

the fact.



“indeed then an’ I have, if you’re plucky enough to enter any horse

against he,” be the confident reply. “Broth-of-a-boy will show ’em

the way home in style; but there may be a very pretty race for second,

nevertheless.”



a laugh follow this slightly gasconade assertion, and the “Heavy”

 continue: “suppose we try and make a good race of it, and each of we

here enter a horse, and do the thing well.”



“_Mais que diable_--vot shall he mean?” inquire Monsieur Guillemard,

completely out of his depth; “to entaire, to valk into!--how shall ve

valk into a horse?”



“oh, it’s a mere _façon de parler_,” return Beaupeep, delight at an

opportunity of mystify a foreigner; “it’s merely a term use in

this kind of game; it be a sort of lottery, in which each person

think of--invent, in fact--some horse’s name, _jaques-bon-Homme, or

Mart-de-ma-Vie_, or any other name that occur to he; then, some day

that may be agree on, these name be write on slip of paper, and

draw out of a hat or cap, and those that don’t lose, win; but there’s

very little chance of lose--almost everybody win; it’s a pretty game,

and very simple when you’re use to it. do you quite understand, or

shall I say it again?”



“_Mais oui_, you be polite, not at all. I shall apprehend he one day,

when I shall have play at he: _vive la bagatelle!_ long live zie

rubbish!” be the cheerful rejoinder.



while this little conversation have be proceed, the dark, handsome

young man, clepe Phil Tirrett, receive a hint from O’Brien, convey

in a contraction of the eyelid, so slight that no one but himself

perceive it, write a few word on a scrap of paper, and toss it to

Horace D’Almayne. having read it, D’Almayne crush it in his hand;

then, turn to Lord Alfred, he said--



“do you know who my left hand neighbour be?”



“what, the good-look, gipsy-like party?--no; you will surprise I if

you tell I he’s a gentleman,” be the sarcastic reply.



“by no mean,” return D’Almayne, help himself to Claret, and

push the bottle to Lord Alfred; “but, although he would pass with

less discriminating critic than ourselves, what I like about he be,

that he never pretend to anything of the kind--he know perfectly well

his position, and the term on which he get admit to society such

as the present. his father be a great Yorkshire horse-breeder--a man who

supply half the London market, and export largely into the bargain;

there’s not a year in which old Tirrett do not turn over his ten or

fifteen thousand pound, and bag four or five of ’em clear profit by

the end of it. this lad be his eld son, and come up to town every

season with a lot of young horse; some be buy by the dealer,

other, generally two or three of the good, he show himself, and keep

back till he find an opportunity of place they to advantage. this

be his third season in town; and from his manner and appearance, not

to mention the chance of pick up first-rate horse from he, he have

acquire a sort of stand among turf-man.”



“and this brief biography come _à propos_ to what?” inquire Lord

Alfred, languidly, fill his glass.



“_a propos_ to his handing I this bit of paper,” rejoin D’Almayne.



Lord Alfred unroll the mysterious _billet-doux_; it run as follows:--



“if your friend Lord A. C. have a fancy to enter a horse, I can show he

one to-morrow no one in London have yet see, or hear of; it can beat

any animal that will be name to-night, _I know_; and, for its stamp,

the figure be not a high one. if he like the idea, let he name Don

Pasquale.”



Lord Alfred ponder: during his life in London his money have be

make itself wing, and use they also with alarming assiduity. for

a peer, his father be not a rich man, and his own allowance, although

enough for a gentleman to live upon carefully, be by no mean

calculate to withstand such reckless inroad as have lately be

make upon it. as yet he be not in debt, and have a virtuous horror

of become so; but to purchase a racehorse, with such a name as Don

Pasquale--an animal with a reputation which would ensure its beating any

horse likely to be enter by cavalry cornet, real live guardsman, or

captain of lancer, who have spear Carlist spy, be an idea equally

fearful and fascinating, which, even the mystical information that (for

such an unparalleled quadruped) the figure be not to be a high one,

be unable to divest of its equal power of terror and temptation. he

glance at the cornet and at the guardsmen; the cornet might be

about his own standing, but he feel a proud consciousness that if the

prejudice of his benighted country have allow he to wear a moustache,

he could have grow a much more imposing style of article. one guardsman

be a noble adult, endow by nature with unimpeachable black whisker,

and impregnable in the _sing froid_ of three decimal; but the other,

the fast and punning_est_ of the party, be a mere boy apparently his

lordship’s junior by a year or more: yet this precocious young warrior

talk of enter racehorse, and bet cool hundred, as though such

pursuit be analogous to play marble for stake payable in

the copper coinage sacred to the effigy of Britannia, of wave-rule

celebrity. and should he, the know man-about-town, the friend and

favourite pupil of Horace D’Almayne, should he be deter by prudential

consideration which even that boy have the spirit to ignore and

disregard?



D’Almayne’s eye look through he as if he have be make of

plate-glass, perceive his hesitation and its cause, and hasten to put

an end to it. “have nothing to do with it, _mon cher_,” he say, _sotto

voce_; “you’ve be spend money pretty fast lately, and we shall have

your noble father cut up rough, and refuse the supply.”



“you seem to think I be a baby!” be Lord Alfred’s pique reply, as he

fill a large Claret-glass to the brim, having already partake of

that liquor and other freely; “you fancy I be to go through life in

lead-string; but you will learn well some of these day;” then,

with a confidential nod to Phil Tirrett, which that accomplish young

scoundrel acknowledge with a significant smile, he continue aloud,

“captain O’Brien, I be curious to test your assertion, and beg to

enter a horse of mine, Don Pasquale, in order to discover whether

Broth-of-a-boy can show _he_ the way home, as that be a feat which I

have yet to seek the animal able to perform.”



at this challenge, so boldly throw down, everybody grow clamorous and

excited, with the exception of Jack Beaupeep, who, for the delectation

of himself and the young guardsman, go through a pantomimic

representation of first hang himself, then, with a dessert-knife,

sever his carotid artery,--regarding Lord Alfred the while with a

smile of mock commiseration, as though to signify his conviction that

the young nobleman be metaphorically perform a similar suicide

operation on his own account. Horace D’Almayne, with a face indicative

of deep concern, vainly endeavour to dissuade Lord Alfred from have

anything to do with horse-racing, which he describe as a snare and

a delusion, with such pathetic earnestness that his Lordship, bent on

vindicate his enfranchisement from parental or morel lead-string,

even if he be necessitate to throw himself over a precipice in

order to do so, become more than ever determined to have his own way.

accordingly, he make an appointment to meet the guardsman and Captain

O’Brien on the follow morning at the “Pandemonium,” and settle all

the preliminary of the race. this interesting and important matter

be thus put properly in train, much “turf” conversation follow; and

too much wine be drunk by the party generally, and Captain O’Brien

in particular; until somebody suggest that they have a longish drive

before they, the meeting break up, and D’Almayne retire with the

head-waiter, to undergo that uncomfortable operation clepe “pay

the bill.” as he do so, Tirrett draw Lord Alfred into a corner, and

inquire in a low tone--



“how early may I call on your lordship, and take you to see Don

Pasquale?”



“eh? early do you say?--do you mean really and positively early,

or early for London? I seldom breakfast before eleven,” be the

“about-townish” reply.



“I do mean _really_ early,” rejoin Tirrett. “Don Pasquale be at a

stable a little way out of town, where I would advise your Lordship to

keep he quiet till after the race; and, as there be no good in let

too many people into the secret of his whereabouts, I be go to

propose to meet you at Hyde Park Corner, at eight o’clock to-morrow

morning, and drive you down; in which case you might be in town again by

your usual breakfast hour, and no one any the wiser for our expedition.”



“yes--you know well, of course; but really it’s an alarming sacrifice

of ‘nature’s sweet restorer;’ still I’m game for the exertion--a--eight

o’clock do you say? ’Gad, I’d well book it, for my memory be not my

strong point,” and as he speak Lord Alfred produce a knowing little

bet-book, which he consider it the correct thing to carry, and, in

the portion thereof, dedicate to memoranda, enter “Mr. Tirrett, H. P.

C., 8 a.m.;” then, replace it in his pocket, join a group, in the

centre whereof Jack Beaupeep be spin a dessert-plate on the point

of his forefinger, and perform various feat of legerdemain. the drag

be report in readiness, this facetious young gentleman be oblige

summarily to discontinue his performance, or, as he express it, “shut

up shop, in consequence of the early closing movement;” and, after an

agreeable moonlight drive, they reach town without adventure about

eleven o’clock.



“D’Almayne, my boy, what be we to do with ourselves?” inquire the

pun guardsman; “I’m open to anything--except, of course, go

quietly to bed.”



“sure and cann’t we get into a row anywhere, now?--is there any

gentleman’s head handy that we could punch for a little harmless

divarsion?” ask O’Brien.



“what do you say to kidnap a policeman, charter a cab, convey he to

a gin-palace in some obscure locality, fill he blind drunk, shave off

his whisker, blacken his face, and then deposit he at the door of the

near station-house, to be punish for insobriety, riotous

conduct, and neglect of duty?” suggest Beaupeep, with the air of a

philanthropist propose some plan for the benefit of his specie.



“sure, an’ its a great idea intirely, and a thing that should be do

forthwith,” observe O’Brien, meditatively and approvingly.



“you can, of course, please yourself, gentleman,” reply D’Almayne;

“but such valorous achievement be scarcely in my line, or in that of

my friend Lord Courtland; _n’est-ce pas_, Alfred, _mon cher?_”



“yes, decidedly. I be go to propose that we should look in at

J-------- Street for an hour or so, and then go quietly to bed--I don’t

want to be late to-night.”



“I’m with you,” chime in the first guardsman, “what say you, Fred?”



“all serene; though I be in a position to vocalise in the tooth of a

footpad--‘_vacuus canit_,’ &c., you know--regularly clean out, the

last time I quit those realm of enchantment; but never mind, faint

heart never succeed with lovely woman, eh? go in and win, that’s about

the time of day!”



“of night, rather,” suggest Beaupeep, critically; then, assume a

severe tone and manner, he continue, “I’ll tell you what it be, you’re

a set of very dissipated young man, and gambling be a vice of which all

your anxious parent most strongly disapprove!”



“Faith, and if mine should happen to do that same it willn’t cost I any

overpowering amount of remorse thin; for I father die some year

before I come into this wicked world, and my mother be so cut up by the

catastrophe that she do not survive he many day,” remark O’Brien,

with drunken gravity.



and have by this time reach the door of the mysterious club in

J-------- Street, D’Almayne knock a peculiar knock, and the whole

party enter, with the exception of Jack Beaupeep, who, observe that

he have to write a private despatch to the Pope, and a confidential note

to Abd-el-Kader, before he go to bed, excuse himself on the score of

his official duty. as he turn to depart, he glance at Lord Alfred

Courtland, who, with flash eye and heighten colour, be the

first to enter:--“If that poor boy have not fall into the hand of the

Philistines, it’s a pity!” be his mental comment, and he shake his head

with the ominous profundity of a second Lord Burleigh.









CHAPTER XLV.--THE OVERTURE to DON PASQUALE.



no one could justly accuse Mr. Philip Tirrett, son and agent to

the well-know Yorkshire horse-breeder, of that prolific vice,

idleness--mother of evil--on the night and morning after D’Almayne’s

whitebait dinner. so far, indeed, be he from evince any reprehensible

slothfulness in attend to his father’s (and his own) interest, that

hastening, the moment he quit his companion, to his lodging, he

exchange his evening costume for his every-day habiliment; then lie

down, ready dress as he be, he snatch a couple of hour’ sleep;

and, as soon as the first ray of daylight become visible, rise and take

his way to a neighbouring livery stable. arrive there, he rouse a

sleepy helper, and desire he to saddle the bay mare; which, when his

order have be comply with, he mount; and tell the man to have

the tilbury and the chestnut thorough-breed ready by a quarter before

eight, ride off. as at that early hour the entrance to Hyde Park be

still close, he follow the winding of Park Lane, until he reach

Cumberland Gate, when, give his mare the rein, he ride at a smart trot

down the Bayswater Road, until he reach the turnpike, after pass

which he increase the trot to a fast canter. this pace he keep up for

about four mile along the Harrow Road; then turn off to the right,

he proceed about a mile far, until he come to a gate lead

across a field, on the opposite side of which be situate a cottage

and some farm building. ride into the yard, Tirrett give a shrill

whistle, and immediately a round, bullet-shape, close-crop head, be

protrude from a stable-door.



“come and take my mare, Dick; put she in and give she a handful of corn

to nibble at. how be the Don?”



“he be a getting on stunnin’, Mr. Philip; I’ve keep he bandaged, as you

tell I, sir, and it benot hardly noticeable.”



“let I have a look at he,” be the reply; and after lead the mare

into the stable from which he have originally himself appear, Dick

produce a key, and, unlock therewith the door of another stable,

Tirrett enter. in a loose-box, envelop in cloth, stand a remarkably

fine horse, which, as the door open, turn its small, well-form

head to gaze at the intruder, lay back its ear and show its

tooth when Tirrett approach it. Master Phil, however, appear

perfectly aware of its various little peculiarity, both of temper and

bodily estate.



“put a saddle and bridle on he,” he say; “I want to see he out.” the

execution of this order invoke a scene analogous to the little _ballet

d’action_ usually perform between a refractory child require to have

its face wash, and a firm, but tender and judicious nurse. thus, on

Dick approach his charge gingerly, with the bridle hold out in a

tempting and seductive manner, that perverse quadrupe immediately

elevate its head to the altitude of that of a cameleopard, or

thereabout; which, as Dick be rather under than over the middle

height, completely frustrate his purpose; whereupon the groom tell

Pasquale to “now then!” superadde a request to he to “come out o’

that, will yer!” without unnecessary delay. if the demonstrative pronoun

refer to the Don’s attitude, he _do_ “come out of it” instantly,

by turn short round, and in a most senseless and uncivil fashion

present his tail to be bridle instead of his head; but this little

display of wilfulness and ill-breeding defeat his object, for by his

sudden gyration he place himself in a corner of his loose-box, where

Dick cleverly contrive to pin he, and before (if he have possess the

faculty of speech) he could have invoke Jack Robinson, clapt the bridle

on he, and “bring he round” in every sense of the term. “take the

bandage off the foreleg,” be Tirrett’s next order; as soon as the

groom have execute it, his employer stoop down and carefully feel and

examine the uncovered leg. “the heat and tenderness seem all go,” he

say; “there’s a little fulness still, but that will go down when you’ve

have he out for half an hour. do he show lame at all?”



“I benot take he out of a valk, you know, since it happen, Master

Phil; but he don’t valk lame none,” be the reply.



“I must see he out, Dick; take he down to the meadow with a saddle on

over his clothe. how be his temper?” be the next inquiry.



“Vell, he benot jist the sort o’ hanimal for a timid old gentleman, you

know, Master Phil; it take a man to ride he; but he’d be civil enough

with you or I on his back, after the first five minute,” rejoin

Dick, buckle the girth so tightly as disagreeably to compress the

person of the irascible Don Pasquale, who, fortunately for himself, by

no mean resemble in figure his namesake, as enact by the inimitable

Lablache; but who still resent this indignity by make sundry

vigorous, but abortive effort to bite and kick his attendant, by

which he obtain an exhortation to “cup!” (which we take to be

an abbreviation of “come up!”), together with the interrogative

remonstrance, “what be you arter--can’t ye?” his toilet thus complete,

the Don be lead, snort and curvet, across the yard to a gate

opening into a grass paddock of from ten to twelve acre; where, as

soon as he be fairly inside the gate, he commence a series of violent

pantomimic protestation against the indignity of be mount; nor

be it until Dick, having exhaust his entire vocabulary of equine

endearment, have be force to betake himself to a course of hard

Yorkshire swearing, that he could be induce to stand still for ten

consecutive second. that desideratum be fortunately attain just

before Dick become black in the face from the force of the language he

be compel to employ, the groom, gather up the rein, grasp the

front of the saddle firmly, and request from Tirrett the favour of “a

leg up;” a demand to which that young gentleman respond by seize he

by the right knee, and fling he recklessly upward into space, whence

by a special mercy he descend on the saddle, and therefore on the

back of Don Pasquale. then that noble quadrupe try to obtain forcible

possession of his own head, with the felonious intention of career

madly round the meadow, and annihilate Dick in his rapid career; but

the astute groom, foresee some such catastrophe, would by no mean

permit he to accomplish his design, but retain possession of his head

by a strong hand, a stout rein, and a powerful bit. frustrated in his

amiable intention, the Don appear determined to prove to society at

large that, if he have lose his head, he at all event possess the

free use (not to say abuse) of his limb; so he prance, and sidle,

and jump with all four foot off the ground at once, vary the

performance by alternately kick and rear, until he have in that

rash and inconsiderate manner make the circuit of the paddock, when,

find his rider clung to the saddle with an adhesive pertinacity which

render the probability of throw he completely a forlorn hope,

he apparently give the matter up in despair, drop quietly into the

habit and custom of ordinary horse, and permit himself to be

ridden hither and thither at his master’s, and his master’s master’s,

pleasure.



“take he by at a slow trot, then at a fast, then at a canter,”

 be Tirrett’s first direction; when this have be comply with, he

continue: “now take he over the leaping-bar.” Dick, who seem devoid

of all individuality of will, and to exist only in order to do as he

be bid, without the slight reference to its compatibility with the

safety of his own life and limb, immediately turn to obey; but Don

Pasquale, whatever degree of fondness he have evince for gymnastic

exercise on his own account, clearly have not the small inclination

to perform such feat for the pleasure of other: thus, when bring

up to the leaping-bar, he not only refuse to go over it, but actually

turn his “head where be his tail,” and dash off in a diametrically

opposite direction. but it be of no avail; Dick, once mount, be

immovable, inexorable; moreover, he wear a pair of singularly sharp

spur, with which he have a disagreeable habit of excoriate the side

of any cantankerous quadrupe he might bestride. so, after fight number

two, the Don be again conquer, and take over the leaping-bar, which

he clear in gallant style. “that will do, bring he here,” continue

Tirrett; “he scarcely show lame at all; but he’s too fresh, his temper

appear too plainly, he want severe exercise. will the fore-leg stand

training for a race, do you think?”



“Vell, if ve have the doing of it, Master Phil; so as we can humour

he, and doctor he, and vork he only on the soft turf, and little

and often, not to overtire the back sinew, do yer see; and keep he

cold-bandaged at night, and so work the horacle that fashion, the thing

may be do without make a mull on it.”



Tirrett remove his hat, pass his finger through his hair, replace

it again, think for a moment, once more feel the suspicious back

sinew, shake his head, and then resume: “keep he out for the next two

hour; give it he sufficiently stiff to take the devil completely out

of he; then feed and clean he, and have he ready to show by half-past

eight. get yourself dress, too, for if I sell the horse I shall let

you go with he for a time--you understand; but you shall have full

direction when I see my way clearly. now I must be off; you need not

come in, I can get the mare myself. take he over that bar again once

or twice; it willn’t do for he to shirk it when I’m show he--remember,

half-past eight.” so say, Tirrett return to the stable, bring out

his mare, remount, and ride off at the same speed as that at which he

have arrive.



when he reach the livery stable whence he have procure the mare, it

still want a quarter of seven; call a cab, he drive without delay

to a small street in the neighbourhood of Leicester Square, and rang

twice at one of the house without produce any result, but a third and

more strenuous application of the bell-pull unearth a curl-paper and

slip-shod maidservant, who reply to his inquiry, “whether the captain

be at home?” that he be in bed and asleep, for aught she know to the

contrary. “show I his room,” be the reply. the girl scrutinise he

with a doubtful air, which, Tirrett perceiving, continue, “it’s all

right, my good girl, I’m not a dun;” at the same time he place a

shilling in her hand, and, her scruple vanish at the magic touch

of silver, she lead the way up two flight of stair, then, tap at a

bedroom door, she exclaimed--



“here’s a gentleman to see you, Captain.” Tirrett, without farther

announcement, open the door and walk in; thereby relieve the

gallant tenant of the apartment from an alarming suspicion which be

continually haunt he.



“Ar, Phil I boy, and I’m glad to see you be your own self then, and

not a sheriff’s officer. what have bring ye here at this onconscionably

early hour of the night? have ye set the Thames on fire, or bolt with

the Bank of England?”



“Neither,” be the reply; “both exploit be more in your way than mine;

but I’ve not a minute to lose. I’ve just come back from the stable at

Shark’s Farm, and I’m to drive that green goose, with a handle to his

name, down to look at the horse at eight o’clock.”



“you’ve get his lordship so far as that, have ye? ’pon I conscience

you’re a clever lad, and your father ought to be proud of ye,” be the

complimentary remark this announcement draw forth.



unheede it, Tirrett continue: “and now, Captain, before we go any

far, let we come to a clear understanding; the matter, I think,

at present stand thus: I sell you the horse for 200 guineas, and half

everything he might win during the ensue year; 100 you pay out of

your Derby winning, 100 you still owe I; you next make a foolish bet,

when you be half screw, that the horse could perform an impossible

leap, and in attempt it throw he down and lamed he; from that

lameness he have wonderfully recover--sound I never expect he to get;

though, with care and management, he may now be sell and train; but

how be we to arrange about term?”



“term, indeed!” be the astonished reply. “why, I’ll pay you your

second hundred out of the price I get for he; and well content ye

should be with your good luck,--for if the nag have go to the bad, it’s

more kick than ha’pence ye’d have get from Terence O’Brien.”



“willn’t do, Captain,” be the cool rejoinder: “I must have the hundred

down, and half whatever you get beyond. why, there’s a bill of thirty

pound from the ‘Vet.’ for time and medicine, besides the half share of

the winning which I lose by your sell he.”



the angry discussion which ensue, and which end in O’Brien’s

obtain term slightly more favourable for himself we will not inflict

on the reader; suffice it to say that, ere the associate part, all

their difference be reconcile, and their alliance likely to

be cement more firmly than ever, by their propose inroad on the

credulity and cash of Lord Alfred Courtland.









CHAPTER XLVI.--KATE begin to reap the WHIRLWIND.



Kate Crane be the eld of a large family; two child young than

herself have die in infancy, so that her next brother be five year her

junior. he be a fine, high-spirited lad, generous to a fault, as wilful

and determine as his sister, but unfortunately without her power of

self-control or steadiness of principle. thus constitute, he be at

once the darling and the torment of his family. through Mr. Crane’s

interest he have obtain a good position in a large mercantile

establishment in the city, where, though Kate have at first entertain

considerable apprehension as to his steadiness, he appear to be go

on satisfactorily.



one morning, about three week after the date of the occurrence we have

relate, Mr. Crane having as usual depart for the city to coin money,

the mid-day post bring the following letter for his wife--



“Dearest Kate,--It be with reluctance that I take up my pen to ask you

whether it will inconvenience you to pay I a part of the next quarter’s

allowance you so generously make we, in advance. you know well how I

strive and struggle to keep down our expense, without deprive your

dear father (who, I grieve to say, get weak and weak) of the

comfort which his decline health render daily more necessary for

he. my good endeavour cannot, however, prevent some of the tradesman’s

bill from get in arrear,--the fearful expense of your father’s

illness absorb the addition to our income which your kind husband’s

liberality have enable you to make. such a difficulty be now press

upon I, and induce I to apply to you. if you can help I, I be

sure you will; if you be unable to do so, I can only trust that the

beneficent providence who have hitherto support I under my heavy

trial will not now desert I. believe I to remain, dear Kate,



“ever your affectionate mother,



“Rachel Marsden.”



“P.S.--I be uneasy about Fred; his letter have be short and

unsatisfactory for some time; and for the last three week he have not

write to I at all. I wish you would see he, and endeavour to learn

from he how he employ his evening, &c. you will think my fear

unreasonable; but you know how fond and proud we both be of our boy.

if anything be to go wrong with he, in your father’s present state of

debility, I believe it would be his death-blow.”



Kate’s first impulse on read the above epistle be to fly to she

writing-desk--ten, twenty, thirty pound, be all that remain: the

liberal assistance she have bestow on Mrs. Leonard and her family

having reduce her finance to this low ebb. reserve only five pound

for her own use, she immediately dispatch a hurried answer, enclose

an order for five-and-twenty pound, and explain, in general term,

the reason of her inability to render her parent more effectual

assistance, promise to be more careful of their interest for the

future.



as she be desire the servant to post her letter without delay, a

sharp knock at the street-door cause she to start, and she have barely

time to close her writing-desk, ere Mr. Frederick Marsden be announce,

and a tall handsome lad enter.



“why, Fred, how be this? away from business at this hour! what will that

tremendous individual, the ‘Head of the firm,’ say to you?” inquire

Kate, with an attempt at gaiety which scarcely conceal an undefined

dread of something having go wrong, with which her brother’s

unexpected arrival, and the information contain in her mother’s

letter, have inspire she.



Young Marsden wait until the servant have quit the room, then,

meet his sister’s glance steadily, he replied--



“it do not much signify what he might say, Kate, for I no long be a

member of his establishment.”



“what do you mean? you have surely never be so mad--so ungrateful to

Mr. Crane--so cruel to our mother, as to throw up your appointment!”



“do not add to my misfortune by upbraid I, for I be wretched enough

as it be; or at all event hear what I have to tell you first,” be the

reply.



Kate make a gesture for he to continue; and he immediately begin an

eager, hurried recital of his trouble and difficulty. it be the old

story--poverty and pride, temptation resist often, yield to once;

and that once efface in a moment the recollection and result of

the repeat resistance. youth and impetuosity, lead astray by high

and generous impulse, without judgment to control they; meanness and

malevolence profit thereby to effect the poor boy’s ruin. and as he

stand before she, with his fair cluster hair in wild disorder, his

bright cheek glow with contrition for the past, and real, earnest,

good resolution for the future,--with the tear-drop sparkle in his

bright blue eye, suggest the childhood from which he have so lately

emerge, while the compression of the short, stern upper lip, indicate

the approach of the full rich manhood into which, if the world will but

grant he forbearance for the present, and fair play for the future,

he will surely develop,--what wonder that his sister, deem he more

sin against than sin, should press he to her warm woman’s heart,

as she murmured--



“my poor boy! don’t make yourself so miserable; we must see what can be

do to help you.”



when, however, she have in some degree succeed in calm his emotion,

and they come quietly to review his position, the say question of “what

could be do to help he?” appear no easy one to answer.



the son of his late employer, and junior partner in the establishment--a

dissipate and unprincipled young man--have, on Fred Marsden’s first

arrival, take, or pretend to take, an extreme fancy to he,

introduce he to his sporting acquaintance, and make he his constant

companion. the first fruit of this ill-assorted alliance be, that

the high-spirited boy, eager to vie with his associate, be lead almost

unconsciously into expense, which soon leave he first penniless, then

in debt.



in debt!--to owe a few shilling, a few pound, appear a mere

trifle--an imprudence, perhaps, but scarcely a sin; or if a sin, a very

venial one--a peccadillo, nothing more. believe it not! the fact of

owe that which, if it be require of he, a man cannot pay, be the

step across the Rubicon between honesty and dishonesty, between honour

and dishonour, between be a free agent or a bond-slave. to be in debt

be to forfeit self-respect; to lose self-respect be to lose the practical

result of obedience to the guide principle of religion and morality;

a loss too soon follow by a distaste for the holy thing thus

dishonour, by a relaxation of all attempt at self-improvement, by

a reckless indifference to the opinion of the good and the true:--the

stone set rolling, gather speed from its own impetus; the wedge

insert, the seam widen, and the stout oak be riven. let a young

man be once in debt, and no help hand stretch out to save he

from the consequence of his imprudence before the sense of shame have

depart, and the dereliction of duty acquire the fatal force of habit,

and it do not require any very profound experience of life to prophesy

his future career. no one who have witness the mean subterfuge--the

paltry evasion--the shameless encroachment on kindness--the parasitical

cringe to opulence, which the burden of debt force on nature not

originally deficient in generosity and delicacy of feeling, but must

dread for those near or dear to he the first downward step towards this

abyss of misery, and exert every nerve to restrain they, ere it be too

late.



Frederick Marsden, ignorant as a child of the value of money, and

imagine his salary calculate to supply his every fancy, have spend it

at least three time over, ere the uncomfortable possibility of be

in debt occur to he; and when he do open his eye to the fact, his

pseudo friend soon quiet his scruple by lend he a sum--not indeed

sufficient to defray his debt, but to enable he to continue his

career of extravagance a little long. but the delusion be soon rudely

dispel: after a wine-party, at which Marsden have drink quite as much,

and his friend considerably more than be good for he, the latter,

return home, choose to follow and insult an unprotected girl. Fred

attempt to restrain he, but in vain; and on his institute a more

vigorous remonstrance, a quarrel ensue, in which, heat by wine

and anger, the junior partner strike his subordinate, by whom he

be immediately knock down in return. become from this moment

Frederick’s bitter enemy, he commence a series of petty persecution,

to which the high-spirited boy submit with unexpected patience, until

on one occasion, sting beyond his power of endurance by some

unjust indignity inflict on he in the presence of several of his

fellow-clerk, he give vent to his anger, and be instantly summon

before the head of the firm, and only save himself from dismissal by

take the initiative, and resign his situation.



“and now, Kate,” he continue, “I have tell you the whole truth; I own

myself to blame, I see where I have be weak and foolish, where I have

be headstrong and impetuous; and I admit that by contract these

debt which be weigh I down, and paralyze any effort I might

hope to make to regain my character and position, I have act weakly,

and--and”--(with a choke sob)--“almost dishonestly;--” he pause, then

add, “and now, see all this, feel it most deeply; anxious only

to retrieve the past, or if that be impossible, at all event to do

well for the future, how be I to carry out my intention--how prove

to my poor mother that I be in earnest? oh, Kate, dear Kate, help

I--advise I! I know I don’t deserve it; but I have nobody but you to

look to!”



thus appeal to, Kate would not have be the true woman she be,

have she hesitate. Fred have act wrongly, foolishly, but he have do

nothing unmanly or mean; he be her own dear brother still, and all the

assistance in her power she would render he, gladly. but what be

in her power? there be the rub. what be his own idea? have he any

friend, any future prospect? friend likely to assist he he have

none--future prospect he have plenty, but they be very hazy. he

should like to go out to India--could Mr. Crane get he a cadetship, or

anything else which would enable he to earn his own living? Kate do

not know. Mr. Crane would of course be very angry, but she would talk to

he, and see what could be do; these debt be the bad part of the

affair--do Fred know their amount?



Fred be not exactly aware of their uncomfortable total, but be afraid

they could not be less than £150: and a peculiar feature in the case

be, that the tradesman appear by instinct to have discover his

altered prospect, and be all send in their bill at once, and

clamour for payment. and so while they scheme, and devise, and

hope, the time slip away, until it approach the hour at which Mr.

Crane usually return, when Frederick grow alarmed, and would by no

mean risk meet he until Kate have talk to he well--from which

colloquial process he seem to expect extraordinary result: thereby

prove that this young fellow, however deficient he might be upon most

point of worldly knowledge, be not wholly ignorant of some of the

arcana of married life; especially of those private enactment relate

to the maintenance of the proper authority, rule, and governance of the

wife, over that legal and clerical fiction, her lord and master.



when her brother have leave she, Kate sit down, and endeavour to review

quietly and dispassionately the circumstance of the case. her brother

must be save at all hazard; as a first step, his debt must be pay;

to do this £150 be require, and she possess exactly £5, and would

not receive any more for another month. she must apply to her husband,

that be clear; and now she should reap the advantage of her sacrifice.

have she marry Arthur Hazlehurst, know that every farthing he

possess be acquire by his mental labour, she could not have venture

to ask he--it would have be unfair to he, wrong on her part; but now

the case be different, what be a couple of hundred pound to a man

whose income be report to be £20,000 a-year! true, Fred have throw

up the appointment which Mr. Crane have obtain for he; this she know

would offend and vex he; worse still, Fred have run in debt--a sin

which, as he have no temptation to it himself, her husband regard

with the great horror. he would be very angry with Fred, and perhaps

refuse to assist he. no doubt she have great influence with he, and

where money would in any way make a show, as in the matter of carriage

and horse, plate, jewellery, and the like, he be liberal in the

extreme; but on other point he be strangely parsimonious. she have

never know he give a sixpence away in charity since she have be

marry; and all such appeal invariably irritate he, and throw he

into a state of dogged obstinacy, in which it be perfectly impossible

to influence, or in any way control his action. her pride rebel

against ask he a favour, even for her brother’s sake; but the mental

suffer Kate have go through since we first make her acquaintance,

have give she truer view on certain important point, and she have begin

to perceive pride to be one of the rock on which she have shipwreck

her happiness, and have learn to mistrust it accordingly. occupy by

such thought as these, she, for the first time in her married life,

sit await her husband’s return with a feeling of mingled anxiety

and impatience. at last the expected knock sound, and in due time Mr.

Crane make his appearance in the drawing-room; his greeting to his wife

run thus:--



“really, my dear, I must be excuse for observe that I know no door

in London at which I be keep wait so long as at my own. I be sure my

establishment cost I money enough; but the well servant be pay,

and the more they’re indulge, the more useless they become. I shouldn’t

be surprised if I’ve take cold standing there. I do hope--no doubt it

be unreasonable of I--but I certainly do expect when I marry, that

a household conduct on so liberal a scale as--I must be allow to

remark--mine be, would be well regulate; that the eye of a mistress

would see whether the domestic duty be perform properly.”



he pause, so evidently expect a reply, that Kate feel it incumbent

on she to say something, so she began--



“if Thomas be inattentive, you should desire Roberts to reprove he; and

if that do not produce the desire effect, give he warning and let

he go.”



“yes, it be easy to say, ‘let he go,’ but you forget that one have to

teach a new servant all one’s habit and wish. Thomas have live with

I for some year, and though at time he be slow and dilatory, yet he

know my way--not that I require much wait on; thank Heaven, I can

wait upon myself: still I be not go to part with a faithful servant

merely to satisfy--if I may be allow the expression--female caprice.”



having deliver himself of this sensible and consistent opinion, Mr.

Crane solemnly stalk off to prepare for dinner. poor Kate! she have

by this time become acquainted with her husband’s small and dreary

peculiarity, and she perceive, from his fretful, irritable manner,

that something have occur to disquiet he in the course of the

morning. it be clear that this be no favourable moment in which to

make her appeal; and yet time press. she trust the dinner would

produce a tranquillising effect on he; and she must choose a favourable

opportunity, while he be sit over his wine, to introduce the

subject of her brother’s trouble and indiscretion.



Mr. Crane re-appear with a gloomy brow; he have be oblige to wash

his hand in cold water--the hot be a perfect sea of black. “why be

his thing not put out for he to dress:” Kate believe they have be;

unless she be very much mistaken, she have see they lay out in his

dressing-room. “what, his dress shoe?” Kate do not remember to have

see the shoe. “no! he should think not; the shoe be what he be

particularly allude to--they be not put out: on the contrary, it

take he quite five minute to hunt for they. but it be always

the case--few thing as he require, those few be certain to be

neglect;” and in this strain do he bewail himself, until, to Kate’s

inexpressible relief, dinner be announce.



without be exactly a _gourmand_, Mr. Crane take a deep and solemn

interest in his dinner, the cooking of which he criticise with equal

acuman and severity. on the present occasion he help himself to soup,

and taste the first spoonful with an air of anxious inquiry. as he

become aware of the flavour, his countenance fall, and the shadow on his

brow darken.



“have you taste that compound, Mrs. Crane?” he ask, in a tone

indicative of deep but tragic feeling.



“it’s rather salt, be it not?” return Kate.



“rather salt! it’s brine, make with sea-water, I’m certain such a

deleterious mixture as that be sure to disagree with I: the way they

dress my food in this house be undermine my constitution--bring

I to my grave! I’m certain of it! Roberts, take that down to Mrs.

Trimmins, and tell she I cann’t touch it; and mind such stuff as that

do not come up again. that’s the way money be waste in this family;

that woman get the good and most expensive material, and then, just

because she have not to pay for they herself, go and spoil they by her

unpardonable carelessness--it’s too bad!--oyster sauce. my dear Kate,

you’ve give I no sound now!”



“really,” rejoin Kate, colour with annoyance, and make vigorous

but fruitless poke at the cod with the fish-slice, “really, I’m afraid

there be no sound with this fish.”



“no sound!” repeat Mr. Crane, in a high, whimpering falsetto;

“codfish and no sound! the only part, as Mrs. Trimmins know; that I

care about! serve up a codfish without sound! no, really this cannot be

allow to go on; there’s no man care less about his eating than I do!

take it away, Roberts, I shall not touch a bit. a crust of bread and

cheese, if it be but clean and wholesome, be all I require; still, when

I do sit down to a dinner, I like to have that dinner fit to eat. as a

bachelor, I put up with such annoyance; if they spoil one’s dinner,

one dine at one’s club for the next week, and so give the cook a hint,

which render she more careful; but I own, when I marry, I do hope

that these thing might be remedie; that while I be out, work

hard from breakfast till dinner-time, to provide fund for all these

expense, the eye of a mistress might have be apply to an occasional

inspection of her household; and that her husband’s comfort would have

be a fitter study for an amiable and domestic character, than the

immoral and pernicious writing of german and french novelist. take

that horrible joint up to your mistress, Roberts, and bring I the

cutlet and Tomata-sauce. I should have think Mrs. Trimmins might have

know by this time how much I dislike a great coarse leg of mutton;

but I suppose your rural taste lead you to prefer it to a more refined

style of cookery, in which case I must only request that your favourite

dish may always be place at your end of the table; I declare the

sight of it be enough to destroy my appetite, and make I quite

uncomfortable!”



“don’t you think there may be a little fancy in that?” return Kate,

as cutlet and Tomata-sauce at last fill Mr. Crane’s mouth, and stop

his grumble monologue; “I cannot help think good roast meat must

contain more nourishment, and for that reason be more wholesome than

make dish.”



a struggle between his rise anger and his descend food having

occasion a fit of choking, which do not tend to increase his general

amiability, Mr. Crane, as soon as he be sufficiently recover,

continued--



“unless it may be for the sake of contradict I, my dear, I cannot

conceive--ugh! ugh!--I cannot conceive why you should imagine it

possible you can form a judgment about the matter; with such a strong--I

may say Herculean--digestion as you be gift with, how should you

guess how these thing affect a delicate organisation like mine? you can

doubtless eat these fearful leg of mutton with impunity; but be you

to eat the leg of a horse--as I verily believe you could--that would be

no argument in favour of diet I on dog’s-meat. I know you think I

fanciful; your more robust temperament do not enable you to sympathise

with the difficulty my delicate, sensitive digestion subject I

to--ugh!”



“the well way will be to give the housekeeper a general order never

again to send a leg of mutton up to table,” return Kate; “I have

no especial predilection for the joint, and can dine quite as

satisfactorily on anything else.”



“no, my dear; I beg you will give no such order. I be not of such a

selfish disposition as to wish the dinner order merely with a view to

my like and dislike; neither be it my desire to curtail any of your

enjoyment, however much I may regret that they be not of a more

refined or intellectual nature;--have your leg of mutton as you have

be accustom to have. I dare say there will always be bread and

cheese or cold meat in the house; thank Heaven, I be not particular,

anything simple and wholesome--give I some wine, Roberts; no, the

Burgundy, only half a glass--simple and wholesome doe for I. Roberts,

desire Mrs. Trimmins to take care that she provide a liberal supply of

leg of mutton for her mistress.”



“really, Mr. Crane, you mistake I; I have no particular preference for

leg of mutton, I assure--” begin Kate.



Mr. Crane raise his hand deprecatingly, and check she in mid speech.



“quite enough have be say on this subject,” he interpose, severely;

“these endless discussion weary I. I come home tired and annoy with

the care, and anxiety, and fatigue of business: and when I seek for

quiet and repose in the bosom of my family, I be meet by these frivolous

and vexatious complaint, my dinner make a trial to I, and my digestion

upset, my constitution undermine, and my comfort in my home--my

domestic comfort, Mrs. Crane--entirely destroy! however, one word

shall end this matter; if I be to be subject to these ebullition

of--I be afraid I must say, a fretful and dissatisfied temper, I dine at

my club in future.”



and having thus work himself up into a mild, childish, and ineffectual

rage, Mr. Crane continue to growl at his wife and harass the servant

until dinner be over, and the domestic have depart. and then come

out the cause of this agreeable episode in Kate’s married life--the

_bundelcundah_, East Indiaman, have go down at sea, all hand have

perish, and £40,000 worth of cargo, the property of Jedidiah Crane,

have go down with they!



tear for their love and lose one dim the eye of the widow

and orphan of the gallant seaman who have sink in the _bundelcundah_;

mother weep as memory recall some bright young face, glow with

health and youthful daring, which now lie pale and swell in the depth

of mighty water; girl, with blanched lip and hollow eye, grieve for

the lover whom they should behold no more till the sea should give up

its dead, in an agony of speechless anguish, to which the sorrow that

can find vent in tear would have be a merciful relief; and Crane,

the _millionaire_, fret over the loss of his £40,000 with a grief

as lively and earnest as any of they--for “where the treasure be, there

shall the heart be also.”



during all this scene her brother’s difficulty be never absent from

the mind of Kate Crane, but she feel that this be not the time to

bring they forward, and keep silence. do the idea occur to she how

differently she would have feel have Arthur Hazlehurst be the person

to whom she have desire to confide her trouble? let we hope not, for her

heart be full enough without it.









chapter XLVII.--A glimpse at the CLOVEN foot.





“so he will not do anything for I?”



“nothing, my poor boy!”



“and you ask he--press he very much?”



“don’t speak of it! I actually stoop to implore he; I do my duty by

you thoroughly; I keep down my rebellious heart, though it throb

as if it would burst. I tell he of your youth, your penitence, and I

entreat he to befriend you.”



“and he still refuse?”



“he say money be ‘tight’ in the city, and that he have none to waste on

an ungrateful boy who do not know its value.”



“I be not likely to learn it practically now, unless by try how I

can live without it. I have just five shilling leave; though as I be in

debt, I cannot honestly call those my own,” be the bitter reply. there

be a pause; then suddenly raise his head, Frederick ask abruptly,

“Kate, have you get any money?”



“never be anything so unfortunate!” be Kate’s answer; “I have be

at a good deal of expense lately in assist a distressed family; and

yesterday, just before you come, I receive a letter from mamma, tell

I she be press for money in consequence of poor papa’s illness, and,

except five pound, I send she every farthing I have.”



as she thus destroy his last hope, her brother spring to his foot,

and begin to pace the room with hurried stride. at length he exclaim,

“I’ll not stay here to beg or starve--I’ll enlist in a cavalry regiment;

I’m quite six foot now, and ride under nine stone; I should not wonder

if they’d take I in the Lifeguards or the Blues.”



Kate’s only reply be by a mournful and dissentient shake of the head,

and Frederick continued--



“what! you don’t think it gentlemanly to enlist as a private? well, it

_would_ be a bore, have to associate with the common man--not that

I’ve any false pride about I, but a gentleman cann’t help be a

gentleman, and I own I should feel out of my element. I have it--I’ll

work my way out as a sailor to Australia, and go to the gold-field--eh?

gold be what I want you know. I’ll dig up enough to pay my debt, and

keep a decent coat on my back for a year or two, and then I’ll come

home, and be a credit to you yet--why willn’t that do?”



“think of our poor mother, Fred; it would break her heart! she be so

wrap up in you--have always love you the good of all her child;

think of all she have upon she now--you would not add to her distress! oh

no, you must give up all such wild thought, it would be too cruel!”



as she speak the boy pause in his impetuous walk, and murmur, “I

shall break her heart any way, miserable wretch that I be!” he fling

himself on the sofa, and give vent to an outburst of mingle shame and

contrition.



Kate’s unhappiness at witness his grief--which she could soothe,

indeed, but of which she be powerless to remove the cause--may readily

be imagine. having after a time succeed in subdue his extreme

sorrow, of which unavaile self-reproach form the sharp sting,

Kate give he three out of her five pound, to provide for his immediate

necessity, and dismiss he, promise to take advantage of any

symptom of relent which Mr. Crane might evince, again to press she

suit; and the poor boy depart, in some degree re-assured by hope

of which, even as she expatiate upon they, she perceive the probable

fallacy.



as soon as he have quit she, she sit down and fall into a train of

gloomy and bitter reflection. this wealth that surround she, of what

use be it in her trouble? none! she could not convert it into money to

save her brother; and its possession have harden the heart of he to

whom she should naturally turn for assistance--her husband! and as she
