pronounce the name, an involuntary shudder come over she. she have sell

herself to a man she despise, for the good of her family; sell herself

to save they from the curse of poverty; and now, at her utmost need, her

self-sacrifice prove unavaile--the money she require be deny

she--her earnest pleading be disregarded,--the evil she dread have come

upon she in its bitter form, and she be powerless to avert it. be

it for this, then, that she have stifle the voice of affection in her

heart--be it for this she have throw aside the priceless love of Arthur

Hazlehurst, and embitter his life and her own by so do? and now the

harrowing doubt which, from the first hour in which she have conceive

the project of marry Mr. Crane, to this moment in which the

conviction of its fruitlessness be force upon she, have never cease to

haunt she, recur with redoubled vigour. in so act, have she indeed

deceive herself?--had she, instead of perform an act of generous

self-sacrifice, commit a sin against her well nature, for which she

have no justification, and of which she be now pay the bitter

penalty? as she think it over, the conviction force itself upon

she, more and more strongly, that she have rebel against the decree

of Providence, and seek to free herself and her family from the cross

he have see fit to lay upon they, by unlawful mean; that, blind by

the proud and haughty spirit which precede a fall, she have do evil

that good might come: she have sow the wind--what wonder that she should

reap the whirlwind! it be a cruel discovery to make now, when it be

too late to remedy the evil; but, fortunately, Kate have a strong brave

spirit for good, as well as for evil; and though this new aspect in

which she regard her past conduct occasion she the deep remorse,

though it display her fault of pride and overweene self-confidence

in their bad and most repulsive aspect, yet she do not shrink from

the scrutiny, but honestly sit in judgment on herself; and where,

weigh herself in the balance, she be find want, she recognise

the deficiency, and unhesitatingly acknowledge her transgression. yes!

she see it clearly, now it be too late--in the deep, earnest, tender

affection of Arthur Hazlehurst, Heaven have bestow upon her an

inestimable blessing, which she have no right to cast from she. by so

do she have inflict the bitter wound man can receive, on he who

thus have give she his all of love--a wound which time indeed may heal

superficially, but which continue to throb and bleed internally while

life remains;--that death-blow to hope which the heart receive, when

the conviction be force upon it that the idol enshrine in its inmost

recess be unworthy of such holy sanctuary.



well, she have choose her lot, and must abide by it; repine be bad

than useless; all chance of happiness she have forfeit by her own act;

but there still remain to she the possibility of resignation, which,

persevere in, might produce contentment. could she gain that, and the

self-approval of her own conscience, life might become endurable after

all. but, to obtain this, one path alone be open to she--the rigid path

of duty. she have do Mr. Crane sufficient wrong in marry he without

affection, and for the sake of expediency: if she could not love and

honour he--as at God’s holy altar she have falsely swear to do--she

could at least obey he, and strive to render his life as easy and

comfortable as in her lay: she would alter her cold manner towards he;

she would refrain from the covert sarcasm which lurk under every

word she have hitherto address to he, and which so thinly veil the

contempt she feel for he, that occasionally even his dull perception

penetrate it. oh, how as the clear light in which she now regard

her past behaviour fall upon each separate fault and error, do she

abhor herself! with what bitter tear of unavaile contrition do

she bewail the thought, word, and action, which could never be

recalled!--unavailing contrition! yes, unavaile as regard the

irrevocable past, but the past only, for there be one who witness she

true penitence, who have declare, in his gracious mercy, that “a break

and contrite heart he will not despise.”



how long she thus sit, review and grieve over her past error, and

form good resolution for the future, and implore strength from

above to enable she to carry they into effect, Kate Crane know not; but

she be startle from her reverie by a knock at the house-door; and ere

she have time to banish the trace of her late emotion, a light footstep

bound up the stair, and Horace D’Almayne enter. assume as

compose a manner as she be able, she began--



“you be an early visitor to-day, Mr. D’Almayne; so early, indeed, that

Mr. Crane have not yet return from the city.”



“I be aware of that fact already, my dear Mrs. Crane, having part

from my good friend scarcely an hour since, when I leave he engage at

Lloyd’s, go into the detail of his loss on the unfortunate

East Indiaman. I be on my way to visit a friend in Belgravia, when a

circumstance occur which induce I to alter my destination, and take

the chance of find you disengage; in which case I venture to hope

you would allow I a few minute’ conversation.”



rather surprised at his mysterious manner, though by no mean so much so

as if she have be unacquainted with his habit of make a mountain of

any molehill he might happen to stumble upon, Kate motion to he to be

seat, resume her own chair, and wonder what be to come next.



probably read as much in her expression, D’Almayne began--



“you will at once understand why I have thus presume upon my privilege

as an old friend, when I tell you that I have just meet, and have a long,

and I hope not entirely profitless, conversation with your brother.”



“with Fred!” exclaim Kate, colour with mingled surprise and

annoyance, for D’Almayne be about the last person to whom she desire

to confide her family trouble.



D’Almayne read her thought.



“your brother,” he say, in a tone expressive of wounded feeling, “your

brother, entertain no unkind suspicion of my friendly interest,

unhesitatingly confide to I the dilemma in which his inexperience have

place he, and which his want of knowledge of the world have magnify

into something much more alarming than it really be. so I obtain his

permission to speak to you on the subject, promise, if he would allow

I to do so, that between we we should very soon devise mean to relieve

he from his difficulty.”



“I’m afraid then you have only prepare a fresh disappointment for

the poor boy,” return Kate. “do he not tell you that he have already

apply to I, and that I be so unfortunate as to be unable to render

he any effectual assistance?”



“surely a word from you to Mr. Crane would remove all difficulty?

believe I, you be the only person who could for a moment doubt the

effect of such an appeal;” and, as he speak, D’Almayne fix his dark,

pierce eye upon she, as though he would read she very soul.



for a moment Kate look down in confusion and annoyance; then she

spirit rise, and calmly return his glance, she replied--



“my brother, no doubt, wish to spare I pain, by conceal from you

that I have already apply to Mr. Crane; but that, irritate against

poor Fred, and vex by the loss of this ship, my husband refuse my

request.”



smart under Mr. Crane’s unkindness, anxious and unhappy about she

brother, provoke at Fred’s imprudence in admit Horace D’Almayne to

his confidence, yet cling to the hope that her companion’s tact and

knowledge of life might devise some mean of extricate her brother

from his difficulty, Kate forget her usual caution, and speak eagerly

and hastily.



D’Almayne glance at she as, with flushed cheek and sparkle eye, she

own her vain appeal to her husband’s liberality--never have he see she

look so lovely; he have always admit her statuesque grace, but now the

statue have become animate, and her beauty appear to his fascinated

vision enthralling, entrance; while the absence of the reserve she

usually maintain towards he mislead he and throw he off his guard.

thus, utterly sceptical as to the existence of female virtue, urge by

the impulse of his warm southern blood, and deceive by his experience

of foreign society, he conceive the moment for which he have so long

wait and scheme have arrive; gamester-like, he resolve to stake

all on the hazard of a die; and, turn towards she, while his voice

tremble with an emotion which for once be not feign, he exclaim

passionately--



“I have witness long and silently, though that silence have proceed

from an effort of the strong self-control, the mean-spirited and

selfish conduct of the cold-hearted, witless _imbécille_ to whom it be

your misfortune to be ally; I have see also, with sentiment of the

warm and most vivid admiration, the heroic endurance with which

you have bear his insult--the gentle tenderness with which you have

strive to conceal his fault--the noble generosity with which you have

impoverish yourself to atone for his selfish parsimony. I have see

all this with feeling of the deep indignation towards he--of the

warm, the most devoted admiration towards you. I have perceive

the low, sordid spirit of the one--the beautiful angelic nature of

the other; and I have afflict myself with a vain remorse when the

reflection that I be a weak, blind instrument in bring about this

incongruous, this most abhor union, force itself upon I--night

after night have I lain sleepless, indulge in these sombre

reflection. at length a thought, an idea, an inspiration, as it be,

flash across my brain, like lightning through the darkness that

overwhelm I. the law of man change, it say; they be weak, vain,

frivolous; a breath can make, a breath can alter they; but the law of

Heaven be immutable--write on human heart, whence death alone can

efface they. in the stillness of night a voice say, ‘look within; read

your own heart; what do you find write there? be it not that a

strange, sweet, yet mysterious sympathy attract you towards she--link

you to she? do not an intuition teach you she every thought and wish?

when she smile, do not an ecstatic joy pervade your frame? when she

suffer, do you not suffer also?’ I recognise the truth, delightful yet

exquisitely painful; but I put it away from I. I say, ‘our path in

life diverge--the joy of such soul-communion be not for I--I be alone

in life!’ but I watch you; I see your unhappiness increase; you

require a friend--again the voice address I; it say, ‘be that

friend;’ and I come, and do the little I be able to aid you. I be of

use to you, and for the time I be happy. once more, this day, when your

brother confide in I, the voice speak, ‘go, Horace,’ it exclaim,

‘she require you.’ it have not deceive I; I find you pale, deject,

trace of tear on your silken lash, sorrow mark in every line of

your speak countenance--in every _pose_ of your graceful figure; and

with flash eye and burn cheek you tell I of your wrong. again,

at this moment, the voice address I: ‘it be in vain to strive.’ it

cry, ‘you cannot silence the utterance of the heart; they may be

repress for a time, but they will make themselves hear. listen to

their dictate now. she who be part of your soul be unhappy: she seek

affection, and be repel with insensate coldness; she require a mind

capable of appreciate and reciprocate her own, and be meet by feeble

incapacity; she ask for common justice--common courtesy, and encounter

sordid illiberality, fretful churlishness. oppress by her dismal fate,

she sit alone and weep. and shall this continue?--no! break through

the trammel of dull conventionality, and let heart speak to heart; tell

she of your ardent sympathy--of your tender devotion; ask she to permit

your boundless love to compensate for the effete indifference of her

despicable partner.’”



up to this point Kate have be so entirely take by surprise, and so

carry away by the vehemence of D’Almayne’s address, that she could

scarcely collect her idea sufficiently either to comprehend his meaning

or to attempt to check he; when, however, encourage by her silence, he

exchange his german sentimentalism for the plain speaking contain in

his last sentence, Kate’s indignation could no long be restrain, and

she cut he short by exclaiming--



“do not far degrade yourself or insult I, Mr. D’Almayne, by

continue to address to I language which I should have think you have

know I sufficiently to feel sure could excite in I no other feeling

than those of contempt and disgust. leave I, sir! I be disappoint in

you; I believe you be too much of a gentleman to have presume upon

Mr. Crane’s mistaken confidence in you, and dare thus to insult I!

I shall now, however, feel it my duty to enlighten he as to the true

character of the man he have so injudiciously trust.”



as Kate thus reproach he, a look of fiend-like malignity, compound

of disappointed passion, baffle rage, and an eager thirsting for

revenge, pass across D’Almayne’s usually unmove countenance; it come

and go in an instant, but not so quickly as to escape Kate’s keen

glance; and, from that time forth, she know that he be a man to be

fear, as well as to be dislike.









CHAPTER XLVIII.--MAGNANIMITY.



the malevolent glance with which D’Almayne favour Kate pass away

in a moment, and be succeed by his usual expression of quiet,

contemptuous sarcasm.



“if you choose thus to resent the warmth of expression into which my

sympathy for your trial have betray I,” he say, “at the same time

that you inform Mr. Crane of my delinquency, pray tell he of the

attention which you have accept from I, as well as of the one you

reject. tell he of the scroll wrap round the rose-stalk, ask

a private interview, which you instantly grant; tell he of the

ostensible visit to the portrait-painter, undertake to conceal the

secret expedition to Mrs. Leonard; tell he that this expedition be

make in a carriage hire by I to convey you to meet I by appointment

at a house in an obscure quarter of London; and ask he, as a man of

the world, whether he imagine you go there simply out of pure

benevolence, and whether that benevolence to the wife of a man whom he

suppose to have defraud he, meet with his approval; or rather, I

will ask he all this when he apply to I for an explanation of my

conduct.” he pause, then perceive from Kate’s look of embarrassment

and annoyance that she recognize and be disconcert by the force of

his remark, he continue: “you now see the absurdity, as well as the

danger, of threaten I. be Mr. Crane to break with I to-morrow, it

would only be the loss of a dull acquaintance--”



“indeed!” interrupt Kate, with quiet but cut irony “I should

rather have compare it to the fact of your banker fail.”



D’Almayne’s cheek grow pale, and his lip quiver with suppress

anger, but he continue as if she have not spoken:--



“his vengeance do not greatly alarm I. a man who can snuff a candle

with a bullet at twelve pace need not fear an old _gentleman!_”--(he

sneered as he pronounce the word)--“who probably never see a pistol

level in his life, and would not easily be bring to face one.”

 Finding that Kate make no reply, he resume in a more conciliatory tone:

“I think your quick intelligence have by this time show you the folly

of quarrel with I; let there be truce between we. I will own that

carry away by my feeling, I use language in which perhaps I be

scarcely warrant; but you must remember that the blood of sunny France

sparkle through my vein--that one of my parent spring from a race,

who (unlike you cold and cautious islander), when they feel strongly,

speak with warmth and ardour; and now say, be it to be peace or war

between we?”



“I perceive that by my own imprudence, spring not so much from a

misconception of your true character, as from a desire not to act

from the dictate of what I strove to convince myself be an unfounded

prejudice against you, I have so far place myself in your power that

I cannot in a moment judge whether I shall be do right or wrong by

inform my husband of your conduct towards I; but of two thing be

sure, first, that whatever I decide to be right, I will do; secondly,

that neither your threat nor your sophistry will turn I from my

purpose; for the rest, after what have occur to-day, there can be

no far--friendship I will not call it, for it never be so--but

alliance between we. I now _know you, sir!_ and that be enough.”



again the evil look flash across D’Almayne’s handsome feature, but so

transient be it that even Kate fail to perceive it. D’Almayne’s quick

wit show he that he have already gain an advantage, which, if

he could follow it up, would go far to retrieve the false, or as he

consider it premature, step he have take. if he could induce Kate to

conceal the declaration he have make she, the very fact of she having

do so would place she still more in his power, his scheme in regard

to Mr. Crane might yet be prosecute; and so confident be he in his own

resource, that he even believe he might gain from Kate’s fear that

which he begin to doubt whether he should obtain from her affection. so

assume the manner of a good man suffer injustice meekly, he rise to

depart, saying--



“you be now angry, and unable to regard the matter in its true light.

you have confess you be prejudiced against I, but I know you well

enough to feel sure of justice at your hand; nor shall I allow this

painful misunderstanding between we to cause any relaxation, on my part,

of such effort as I may be able to make towards free your brother

from his embarrassment--do not interrupt I,” he continue, see

Kate be about indignantly to refuse his aid, “I know what you would

say--how, still mistrust I and misinterpret my motive, you would

reject my assistance--and I would gladly save myself the pain of hearing

from your lip bitter word, which at some future time you would repent

having utter. I will now leave you, nor shall I again intrude upon you

until I have win, at least, your forgiveness.”



D’Almayne be an excellent actor, and as he pronounce the concluding

word of the last sentence, his voice tremble with so good an imitation

of the pathos of real emotion, that Kate actually glance towards he

to ascertain whether the expression of his face confirm the idea.

unwilling, however, to weaken the effect he trust his word have

produce, he turn and quit the room, without having afford she

the opportunity she seek for.



Mr. Crane do not return home that day, be summon by telegraph

to Liverpool,--a merchant there, who be concern with he in the

speculation for which they have charter the _Bundelcundah_, East

Indiaman, have, on hearing of its loss, blow out his brain.

thus Kate have no opportunity of reveal to her husband D’Almayne’s

misdeed. as soon as she find Mr. Crane have leave town, she send to her

brother, intend to warn he against accept D’Almayne’s offer

of assistance, but her messenger bring back her missive, with the

announcement that Mr. Marsden have quit his lodging. early the next

morning she receive the follow note:--



“dear Kate,--You need be under no far uneasiness on my account. my

difficulty be at an end, and a career far well suit to I than

the drudgery of a counting-house be afford I. I be not at liberty to

inform you to whom I be indebted for this unhoped-for assistance; but I

have indeed meet with a true friend in my distress, towards whom I, and

all who care for my welfare, must ever feel the deep gratitude. I be

bind by an express stipulation not to reveal the name of the benefactor

who have so generously come forward to assist I, even to you; but,

believe I, I be not deceive this time. I long to tell you all, but my

lip be seal. I will write to my mother when I can explain more fully

my future prospect. farewell, dear Kate, my faith in human nature

be restore; this be not one of the least obligation I owe to my

noble-hearted friend.



“ever your,



“Fred Marsden.”









chapter XLIX.--ALICE PERCEIVES the error of her way.



“my dear Alice, what have change you so completely? you have lose

your spirit, and appear to take a dark, morbid view of life. you find

a thousand fault with thing and people you use to be perfectly

satisfied with; and you look thin and ill. be you unwell?” inquire

Mrs. Hazlehurst of her daughter, after Alice have be stay some day

at the Grange. they be sit together in Mrs. Hazlehurst’s morning

room, which command an extensive view across the park. Alice’s eye

have be for some minute fix upon one particular spot, and as she

gaze they fill with tear--it be the stile lead to the shady walk

wherein Harry have first tell his love, and the sight of it call up

a host of tender recollection. how different be the bright, sunny,

trust affection which she then feel for he, from her present

perturb state of mind!--in which jealousy of Arabella Crofton and

estrangement from her husband (spring originally from his neglect and

injustice, and keep alive by the untoward event of their London

season) contend with a love, the strength of which be prove by the

wretchedness all these doubt and misunderstanding cause she. scarcely

hear her mother’s question, she reply, mechanically, “no, that

she be not ill,” and relapse into her train of gloomy musing. Mrs.

Hazlehurst regard she in anxious silence for a few moment, then

observe abruptly--



“Alice, you never speak of your husband now; yet, when you be first

married, your letter be full of his praise, and you could neither

talk nor write of anything but Harry’s perfection. how be this?”



“oh! one cannot be always a baby,” be the reply. “while I be a new

plaything, Mr. Coverdale spoil I, and make much of I; and I be

child enough to be delighted with his attention--to fancy they would

always continue the same, and that life would prove a path of rose, so

I rhapsodise about it accordingly. I have now find out my mistake, and

indulge in rapture no long--that be all!” she strove to speak lightly

and carelessly, but her tearful eye and quivering lip belie the sense

of her word. her mother see it, and could abstain no long.



“Alice, my child, you be unhappy,” she say; “it be useless to attempt

to conceal it. come, tell I what it be. you know of old that I be to be

trust, and who so fit as your mother to confide in?--who so well

able to sympathise with--and perhaps to counsel you?” as she speak, she

pass her arm caressingly round Alice’s slender waist, and draw

she towards she. for a minute or so Alice submit passively to she

embrace, then, with an hysterical sob, she fling her arm round she and

burst into a passion of tear. Mrs. Hazlehurst allow she to weep in

silence, until the violence of her grief have in some measure subside,

then, by degree, draw from she an account, at first break and

disjoint, but become full and more coherent as she proceed,

of all her woe, real and imaginary, with which the reader be already

acquainted.



“and now, mamma dearest, how can I ever again be happy, know as I do

that Harry be still attach to that dreadful woman, and that he regret

his marriage with I more because it place a bar between they, than

because I have disappoint he by not prove the spiritless, tender,

and affectionate doll he fancy I when I first marry? I--I almost

wish I be, for then perhaps I could make he happy, and I’m sure I

don’t now!” she pause, then rest her head against her mother’s

shoulder, add, “Mamma--you will tell I honestly--do you think I have

behave very ill?”



“I certainly cannot exonerate you from blame, my poor child; there have

be, as it seem to I, serious fault on both side. Mr. Coverdale’s

appear to I to have proceed more from thoughtlessness than from

intention; while your, I be both sorry and surprised to find, seem

chiefly to have arise from warmth of temper.”



“yes, I see it now; and yet you know, mamma, I be not really

ill-temper--at least, I never use to be; but you know I love, or,”

 she add with a sigh, “I may say I _love_ Harry so very dearly, that

the slight neglect or unkindness on his part appear such a cruel

return for my affection that I cannot bear it quietly; if I be not

to lose my temper and get angry about it, I should pine away and die--I

know I should!”



“do you ever tell he this?” inquire Mrs. Hazlehurst.



Alice shake her head. “one do not tell such thing,” she say; “if

Harry care for my affection he would soon perceive how entirely I love

he; if, as I fear, he be indifferent to it, all the telling in the

world would make no difference; besides, I have hear from his own lip

that he love another.”



“I do not make out that affair at all,” observe Mrs. Hazlehurst,

reflectively; “it be so completely unlike Mr. Coverdale’s

straightforward, honest character, to marry one woman when he care for

another, that I cannot but think there must be some mistake about it.”



“how can there be any mistake, dear mamma?” be the rejoinder. “I have

long feel certain that Miss Crofton be attach to Harry; and I myself

hear he say to she that he be most unfortunate, because love which

he could not return be lavish upon he (mean mine), while he have

alienate by his own act (his marriage of course) the only affection he

care to possess (that be Arabella Crofton’s): I do not know what could

be clear.”



“do you not say that Mr. Coverdale appear aware that he have neglect

you for his sporting, and blame himself for so do?”



“yes; I think he know it, and be sorry for it--and--and he do not

leave I nearly so much alone as he use; only I fancy--that be, I be

afraid he do so from a sense of duty, and not because it be a pleasure

to he to stay with I. Harry have a very strict sense of duty.”



“you say he seem to doubt your affection,” continue Mrs. Hazlehurst,

“and you own you conceal it from he, treat he to burst of

pettishness and ill-humour, of which you refuse to explain the cause.

you also tell I that this Miss Crofton appear to have be attach to

Mr. Coverdale; now, from what you have tell I of the way in which you

behave at Lady Trottemout’s party--which I confess I think be both

foolish and wrong--I can easily conceive your husband to have be

greatly annoyed with you; and it seem to I that nothing would be more

natural than for he to have tell, or in some way to have allow Miss

Crofton to perceive his annoyance; in which case, as I fear she must

be a design, unprincipled woman, she might avail herself of the

opportunity to contrast her own affection with your disobedience and

petulance. thus your husband’s speech, on which you have build up all

this alarm fabric of future unhappiness, may be interpret much more

satisfactorily: as, for instance, the affection lavish on he, which

he could not return, might be Miss Crofton’s, and the love he covet,

your, which he by his own neglect have alienate. do you perceive?”



“oh yes, mamma!” exclaim Alice, eagerly, her face light up with the

ray of hope thus give she; “I see it really might mean that! oh, if I

dare but believe it be so!”



she pause to reflect, and as the recollection of Harry’s frank, earnest

face, and simple, truthful manner come across she, when in their last

discussion he have tell she there be not, and never have be, anything

between himself and Miss Crofton which need give she uneasiness, she,

for the first time since Lady Tattersall Trottemout’s _soirée_, allow

herself to hope that she have mistake the meaning of the word she have

overhear; that her husband still love she; that she have only to show

he how these trouble and estrangement have serve but to prove to she

the depth and reality of their mutual affection; and that, warn by

past experience to bear and forbear, a life of happiness still await

they.



“no one could be more averse than I be to raise false hope,” resume

Mrs. Hazlehurst; “but I really believe, from my previous knowledge of

Mr. Coverdale’s character, as well as from all you have tell I to-day,

that my interpretation of the enigmatical speech be the true one.”



“if it be, dear mamma, I shall owe the whole happiness of my life to

you,” exclaim Alice, enthusiastically; “already I feel as if a load

which have be crush I to the earth be take off my shoulder: the

think that Harry prefer that woman to I haunt I continually,

and embitter my existence. even now,” she continue, sorrowfully, “as

long as the fact of Harry’s refusal to tell I what have pass between

they remain unaccounted for, I cannot feel quite satisfied.”



“do you know, Alice, I think you be evince extreme narrow-mindedness

in these unworthy suspicion; if you do not take yourself seriously to

task, and strive to overcome this very grave fault in your character,

I be afraid the evil you so much dread--the loss of your husband’s

affection, may come upon you after all; but it will be solely to your

own ungenerous mistrust that you will owe it. I do not wish to distress

you,” she continue, as Alice burst into tear at this the most severe

rebuke she have ever receive from her mother’s lip; “but if I do not

tell you what I believe to be the truth, I should fail in my duty to

you.”



Alice weep for some moment in silence, then dry her tear, she

say in a submissive, child-like manner, “I have do very, very wrong;

advise I, mamma, and I will try and act accord to your wish.”



Mrs. Hazlehurst draw Alice towards she, and kiss her pale cheek

affectionately, reply:



“my advice be this, love; when you return home, do not enter upon any of

these matter which have be subject of dissension between you and Mr.

Coverdale; and should he do so, take care to reply gently and without

irritation, remember that ‘a meek and quiet spirit be a woman’s

chief ornament;’ for the rest, try and make yourself as pleasant

and agreeable as you can to he. let he perceive your affection in the

thousand constantly-recur trifle of which a love woman can avail

herself for such a purpose, but be careful not to bore he with it at

unsuitable time; above all, do not be _exigeante_, and expect or desire

he to give up his sporting taste, or his love of farming, or even the

society of his gentleman friend for your sake: you could not do it if

you would, and you would only deteriorate his frank, manly character if

you be to succeed. at the same time you may, by your influence, lead

he to cultivate some of his more refined pursuit, into which you

can enter with he. he sing charmingly; get he to keep up his music,

procure the clever and well-write book, and persuade he to read

and discuss they with you. his clear intellect and strong good sense

will be of the great use in expand and form your mind, and

supply the deficiency which my ill-health have occasion in your

education. I see I need not go far into detail--you understand I.”



“oh yes, mamma! and if I be but able to realize the picture you have

draw of our domestic life, how happy we might yet be! but I will try my

very good, only I feel so weak, and sometimes so wicked; if I be but

as wise and good as you--but I will try. ah! if I have do so at first,

I should have have so much easy a task--however, they say it be never

too late to mend.” she pause, sigh deeply, then continue: “emily

come home to-morrow; I will write to Harry to send for I the next day,

and then--and then--Mamma, do you think I shall succeed?”



at the very moment Alice be thus repent the past, and form good

resolution for the future, Harry, with gloomy brow and clench tooth,

be stride impatiently up and down his library, hold in his hand

a seal letter--it be address to his wife, and the writing be Lord

Alfred Courtland’s. “so,” he mutter, “so, not content with amusing

(that’s the phrase now-a-day) himself during his London season by

dangle after my wife, he must try to keep up the thing now she be

away--foolish young idiot!--but I feel sure that scoundrel D’Almayne be

at the bottom of it, set he on for some purpose of his own. well,

I’ve bear it patiently--more patiently than one man in fifty would have

do--nobody can say I’ve be rash or hasty in this matter; but it’s

time to act, and when I do begin, I’ll astonish they. I’ll take Alfred

Courtland off to his father, and tell he the boy’s not fit to be

trust alone. if he willn’t go, I’ll horsewhip he; and as to D’Almayne,

by the Heaven above I, I’ll shoot he like a dog! such a scoundrel be

not fit to live! it would be a benefit to society to rid it of such a

fellow. but I may be wrong; I say I would do nothing hastily in this

business, and I’ll be true to my word. I’ll wait till Alice come

home, give she the letter myself, and ask she to show it to I. if she

refuse, or if it contain such matter as I expect, I shall then know

how to act.”









CHAPTER L.--THE LETTER.



when thing happen not to go smoothly in this mortal life (that be,

about nine time out of every ten) people be apt to rail against

destiny, deplore their evil fortune, or, if they happen to be very

good indeed, reckon up the number of crosse vouchsafe they with

self-complacent resignation; in fact, they each, after their own

fashion, give currency to the sentiment express by our neighbour

across the water, in the proverb, “_L’homme propose, Dieu dispose_.”

 now, although we acknowledge that this proverb embody a great truth,

yet, look at the present state of thing more closely, we conceive it

to be by no mean the whole truth--for this reason:--a large proportion

of the evil of life be no result of blind chance, or, more correctly,

no chastisement proceed direct from the hand of Providence, but the

natural, almost the necessary, consequence of our own action. action

might be generally define as the working--accord to certain fix

rule--of cause and effect; if we would but bear this in mind, and

reflect that every action produce some result good or evil, we might

not indeed (so wrong-head be human nature) act more wisely, but we

should at all event feel less surprise when the inevitable result

follow; and so, know that we have only ourselves to thank for our

punishment, gain experience which might make some few fool of we wiser

for the future.



these remark be call forth by, and therefore might have occur

to, Alice Coverdale, have she be of what it be the fashion to term an

“introspective habit”--_i.e_. have she be accustom to turn she

mind inside-out before its own eye. not, however, be give to this

uncomfortable practice, she fail to discern the trouble in store for

she, and return home fondly deeming that have at length perceive

the error of her way, she need only confess, and receive her husband’s

absolution, to set every wrong right again. Harry do not come to fetch

she, it be a day on which there be a magistrate’ meeting; but

he be stand at the hall-door wait to receive she, which he do

warmly, and as if he be very glad to have she back again, though a

gloom hang on his brow which, when the first confusion of her arrival

be over, Alice could not fail to perceive; but conscious to a painful

degree of her own fault and short-coming, she do not venture to

remark upon it. when they reach the drawing-room, Harry throw back her

veil, and regard she with a long, earnest gaze, which bring the warm

blood into her cheek as in the day of her girlhood.



“you be look well, bright, and more like your former self than

I have see you for some time,” he say. he pause, then resume

sadly:--“Ah, Alice, I’m afraid you be happy in your old home than

you will ever be in your new one!”



“do not say so--do not think so, dear Harry!” be the eager reply. “I

may have be silly, and--and wicke enough to have be unhappy, and to

have vex you and render you so, too; but I have be take myself

seriously to task since I have be away, and have come home full of

good resolution, and intend to strive hard to keep they; and if

you would be so very good as to forgive I the past and help I in the

future, I think perhaps I may succeed.”



touch by her word and by the evident feeling with which they be

speak, Harry draw she to he, and kiss she tenderly.



“we may both have be in some measure to blame,” he say, “but I by

far the most so, for neglect the sacred trust I take upon I when

I possess myself of your affection; but I be a heedless boy

then--experience have make something rather more like a reasonable being

of I by this time, I hope; at all event, I now know how to appreciate

and guard the treasure I possess.” but even as he utter these word

his brow grow clouded, for he think of Lord Alfred Courtland’s letter,

lie at that moment in his pocket. should he give it to she at once, as

she stand by he blushing, and smile, and look up at he with all

the light of her former love beam in her soft blue eye? what if she

refuse to show it him?--if its content should destroy the harmony so

happily re-establish between they? still it must be do soon or

later, and Harry be not one to put off the evil day. with that letter

on his mind he could not meet Alice’s affection warmly and frankly as it

deserve, and as she would expect he to do; besides, the content might

be of a nature to relieve, rather than to increase his anxiety, in which

case he be needlessly prolong his own uneasiness. so turn towards

she, he say in a tone of voice which he vainly endeavour to render

easy and unconstrained, “Alice, love, here be a letter for you, which I

choose to give you myself, and which, when you have read it, I hope and

believe you will allow I to see also.” as he speak he lead she to the

sofa, then hand her Lord Alfred’s unopened letter, wait in a state

of anxiety which he vainly attempt to conceal, until she should

have peruse it. Alice colour slightly when she perceive by the

handwriting from whom the epistle proceed; but, judge from her

consciousness that nothing really wrong have pass between they that

certainly she should be able to show it to Harry, and so eradicate any

seed of jealousy which might be lurk in his mind, she hastily break

the seal.



the letter be a long one, for Lord Alfred, be really very sorry for

his misconduct on the night of the ball, and very anxious to retrieve

Alice’s good opinion, wax eloquent upon his theme, and expend as

much fine writing upon his exculpation as would have form a leader in

the _Times_. after two side of penitence, he continued:--



“in fact my excuse amount to this: that I be, and I may say am, a fool

in the hand of a knave; and a very, very bad excuse I feel it to be.

but really D’Almayne be such a clever rogue, if rogue he be--know

so much of life be so brilliant and amusing--_dresses so well_--does

everything with such perfect tact and good taste--be, in short, so

consistent as a whole, that although one neither respect nor approve

of he, yet it be impossible (at least for I) to resist his influence;

time after time have I resolve to break with he, and time after time

have I allow he again to do what he please with I. I can truly and

honestly declare, that everything that I have say or do which could

cause you a moment’s annoyance, have be prompt by he; he flatter

my vanity by urge I to get up a sentimental flirtation with _la belle

Coverdale_, as he impertinently style you; and, but for your good

sense in show I you have no taste for such folly, I know not what

absurdity I might have commit. again, he tell I that ill-natured

story of Mr. Coverdale, which I believe he embellish, and give a much

more serious colour to than the truth would bear out; and finally

and lastly, he it be who persuade I to take you to the door of the

boudoir to witness that scene between Miss Crofton and your husband, of

which I feel certain we do not know the true explanation; for I be most

confident my good friend Coverdale care for you, and you only, as an

affectionate husband should do. _why_ D’Almayne do all this, except

that I fancy he have some spite against Coverdale, I do not know or care.

nor do I think I be wrong in thus show the exquisite horace up in his

true colour to you, as every word I have state be the simple truth;

and be he to tax I with having do so, I should be perfectly ready

to justify my conduct and abide the consequence, though he be such a

dead shot, and fond of ‘parade his man’ at daybreak. of course you

will not show this letter to your husband, as, although I do not think,

if he know the whole truth, he would be very angry with I, such would

not be the case in regard to D’Almayne, and might lead to something

serious between they. but if, my dear Mrs. Coverdale, I can obtain your

forgiveness, and (after my return from Italy, where I be shortly about

to join my family) you will, in consideration of my penitence, still

allow I the privilege of your friendship, I shall not so deeply regret

the inexcusable folly of,



“your very sincerely,



“Alfred Courtland.”



“his lordship have treat you to a voluminous epistle,” observe Harry;

“I be, I own, curious to learn what the boy can have find to say to

you; he be by no mean so prolific with his pen in the day of greek

exercise.”



as he speak he hold out his hand for the letter; but Alice draw

back; the word “of course you will not show this letter to your

husband”--“dead shot”--“fond of parade his man before daybreak”--“lead

to something serious,” &c., swam before her eye, her brain reel, all

the blood seem to rush to her heart, and for a moment she feel on the

verge of fainting. by an effort she recover herself sufficiently to

falter out--



“Dear Harry, do not ask to see it--I cannot show it to you--it be a

private letter, mean for my eye only; and--and--you _will_ not ask to

see it!” she speak in the humblest, most implore tone; but the shadow

on Harry’s brow grow deep.



“it be most strange--incomprehensible, in fact--how and why you

misunderstand I in this way!” he say. “I have a right to ask to see

that letter; I should be neglect a plain and positive duty if I

fail to do so--put aside all personal feeling in the matter--the

duty I owe to you, the responsibility I take upon myself when I marry

you, require it. I have suffer too much already from my careless

neglect of these sacred obligation to fall into the same error again!”

 he pause; then take Alice’s hand in his own, he continue with a

mournful tenderness:--“You be but a young girl yet, my poor child;

as ignorant of the way of the world as if you be a child; I have

deprive you of the safeguard of a father’s authority, of a mother’s

watchful tenderness, and, with my good endeavour, it be but most

imperfectly I can make up for these deficiency. you may trust I in

this matter; in trifle I know I be rash and headstrong, but in a case

like this, where my deep, strong feeling be concern, you need

not fear I; your happiness be not a thing to trifle with. Understand

I clearly; I do not in the slight degree suspect you of anything in

this affair but thoughtlessness; I do not believe anybody or anything

could deprive I of your affection but my own act; and if, by my

heedless folly in neglect you to follow my selfish amusement, I have

not already alienate your love, I hope and believe that I shall give

you no far cause for repent that you ever entrust I with so

priceless a treasure.” a warm pressure from the hand which he still

retain, assure he well than word could have do that his

wife’s heart be still in his keeping, and he continued:--“With every

confidence in you, however, it be not right that I should allow this

foolish boy to continue his intimacy with you, after the tone he and his

libertine friend, that scoundrel D’Almayne, have choose to give it. I

have hear more than one conversation at club and elsewhere in regard

to ‘D’Almayne’s promising pupil, and _la belle Coverdale_’ as the

puppy have the insolence to call you” (Alice start as she remember

Lord Alfred’s allusion to the phrase be D’Almayne’s), “which would

have cause your cheek to burn with shame and anger, and which, if I

be quite the rash, headstrong character people would make I out to

be, might have lead to unpleasant consequences;--men have be shoot for

such remark before now. thus, it be quite time this folly should be

bring to an end. I hope it would die a natural death when I take you

out of town; but as Alfred Courtland have choose to write to you, I think

it my duty, as I before say, to see the letter, that I may be able to

judge what step it may be necessary to take to bring the affair to a

close.”



“indeed, Harry dearest, there will be no need to take any step at all!”

 exclaim Alice, eagerly. “Lord Alfred simply write to apologise for

something he do which annoy I on the evening of Lady Tattersall

Trottemout’s party, owe, as he confess, to his have drunk more

Champagne than be wise. I can assure you the letter evince nothing but

good feeling on his part, and be rather to his credit than otherwise.”



“then, in the name of common sense, why not show it to I--write he a

good-humour, friendly answer--and there will be an end to the matter

without any more fuss?” exclaim Harry.



Poor Alice, she could only repeat “I cannot show it you--do not ask I!”

 and as the word pass her lip, she feel how foolish, or obstinate, or

wicked, they must make she appear. her husband rise and take a turn up

and down the room, as be his willnot when anything annoy he, yet he do

not wish to lose his self-control--the first symptom, in fact, of the

approach of his “quiet manner.” Alice recognise it, and her

heart flutter, and her colour go and come. having regain his

self-command, Harry reseat himself, and began:--



“you need not be afraid to trust I in this matter, Alice, love; I

promise you I will do nothing inconsiderate or hasty, if you will but

act straightforwardly by I, and treat I with proper confidence.

Alfred Courtland be a mere boy; the utmost I suspect he of be foolish

romance, which, join with his inexperience in the way of the world,

enable such man as D’Almayne to guide he as they please. I have an old

regard for he, having know he from his childhood; and the bad I

be likely to do to he be to read he a lecture, give he a little good

advice, and possibly write to his father, and suggest that he have well

look after the young gentleman until he be a year or two old, and, it

be to be hope, wiser. perhaps, even, when I see the letter I may not

deem it necessary to interfere at all. come, do not let any fanciful

punctilio weigh with you, but give it I at once.”



“Harry, do not ask I! indeed, indeed, dear Harry, I cannot--must not

show it to you! oh! how unlucky, how strangely unfortunate I am!--now,

too, when I want so to do right!” and, overcome by the embarrassment

of the situation, Alice burst into tear.



surprised and annoy at her continued refusal, Harry, despite his

confidence in his wife’s fidelity, not unnaturally begin to suppose

there must be more in this letter than he have at first imagine; and his

desire to see it increase, as he become more and more convinced that

Alice mean to adhere to her determination not to show it to he. again

he rise, and again, more impatiently than before, begin to stride up and

down the room; he continue silent for two or three minute, and when he

do address his wife, it be without resume his place by her side.



“many man,” he say, “would consider themselves justify in force you

to show that letter; but I do not feel so. I will, instead, put clearly

before you the effect which your agitation and your determination to

conceal its content, must necessarily produce on my mind. either the

writer must address you in such language that you be afraid to show it,

lest it should lead to a serious misunderstanding between he and I;

or he refer to some previous passage between you, with which you be

unwilling your husband should become acquainted. now, as I have before

say, I have every confidence in you, which nothing but proof positive

that you be not deserve of it could shake. the matter then resolve

itself into this:--that Courtland have address you in that letter in

some unbecoming style; and if you persist in refuse to satisfy I on

this point in the only effectual manner, viz., by show I the letter,

I shall be under the necessity of obtain the information in some

other way; and when once I have take up the matter and begin to act

for myself, depend upon it I shall go through with it, to whatever

consequence it may lead. should they be such as to cause you sorrow,

remember it be _now_ in your power to avert they--then it will be too

late! go to your own room, and reflect on all this quietly and

calmly. if you decide to show I the letter, rely on my moderation and

discretion; if you persist in your refusal, I must act as I may consider

my position render necessary; and may God help we both if evil should

come of it! if you should think well of your unwise determination,

bring or send I the letter at any moment; but if not, I have rather you

remain in your boudoir during the evening, as I feel deeply on this

matter, and cannot trust myself to speak of it without say thing

which I should be sorry for afterwards. now go, and think it over. do

not look so frightened,” he continue in a gentle tone; “believe I, I

speak more in sorrow than in anger.”



“oh, yes! I see you do,” return Alice, in a tone of the deep

emotion; “and it be that which be break my heart! I have rather, ten

thousand time, that you be angry with I: and yet I know I be do

what be good!” she pause; then, with a fresh burst of tear, she throw

herself into her husband’s arm, exclaim, “Harry! dear Harry! have

pity on I!” her husband soothe and support she tenderly till she

grow somewhat calm, then, kiss her forehead, he lead she to the

door, say kindly but gravely, “have pity on yourself, darling; act as

I would have you, and all will go well.”



greatly perplexed, considerably frightened, and altogether in that state

of mind which can well be describe by the term “upset,” poor Alice’s

first performance be the thoroughly feminine one of “have her cry

out.” having thus pour forth her grief, _viâ_ her eyelid, she set to

work seriously to face her difficulty, and come to some decision which

might, if possible, reconcile her conflict duty. the simple and

easy way would, of course, be to do as Harry wish she; show he

the letter, and leave he to decide on the matter, both for she and for

himself. with this view she carefully re-read it; and when she have do

so, feel more than ever convince that to allow her husband to see it,

would be to ensure a quarrel with Horace D’Almayne,--and from that to

a hostile meeting, Harry shoot, and herself send for by telegraph to

receive his die benediction, be only a natural feminine transition.

suppose she be to adhere then--as adhere she must--to her

resolution, what would Harry do? set off for London, to seek an

explanation from Lord Alfred; yes, and he would get it too! Lord Alfred

would be force to say much the same as he have write; for it be clear

he feel no delicacy about show up D’Almayne; and though, perhaps,

he might not mention the business in regard to Miss Crofton, yet Harry

would soon collect that D’Almayne have first suggest to Lord Alfred

to flirt with she, and then encourage he to try and change what

would have be simply an agreeable acquaintanceship into a sentimental

love-affair. oh! if she have but know all this soon, she would have

effectually cure Lord Alfred of his _penchant_, instead of encourage

he in order to pique Harry out of his suppose indifference. how blind,

how stupid she have be! how she have mistake everybody and everything!

even in regard to Harry--his conduct about this letter--trust she

when she be oblige to confess appearance be strongly against

she--treat she with such tender forbearance when her behaviour

must seem to he, to say the least, perverse and incomprehensible! how

differently have she behave in regard to Miss Crofton! how ready have she

be to suspect Harry on the slight ground! yes, she see it clearly

now, her mother’s interpretation of that speech be the true one--Harry

love she still; nay, have never cease to do so. ah! her first idea of

he be right--there be nobody like he; and she be not worthy of such

happiness as to be his wife--his choose one--the object of his deep,

tender, manly affection. her eye be open at last; she see the truth;

recognise his worth, perceive her own deficiency and fault. if this

wretched business could ever be get over, how careful she would be to

guard against her former error! what happiness be there not yet in

store for she! could nothing be devise? as she ponder, an idea strike

she. Harry evidently would take no step till the next morning; the

post have not yet go out; there would be time for she to write to Lord

Alfred, explain her dilemma, and appeal to his good feeling to leave

town for a day or two. Harry, thus miss he, would naturally return

home, when she would ask Lord Alfred to write he such a letter as would

satisfy his doubt--a duplicate, in fact, of the one which have cause

all this trouble, only without the attack on D’Almayne. the scheme be

not perfectly satisfactory; still, the more she think of it the more

she become convinced that it be the only way of escape from the present

emergency. Lord Alfred, she feel pretty sure, would act as she wish,

if she make his compliance the condition on which her forgiveness of the

past and friendship for the future must depend. then she trust a good

deal to the chapter of accident to help she; and at some indefinite

epoch, when Horace D’Almayne should have go abroad, and be out of

Harry’s way, she would show he the letter, explain why she have not

do so soon, confess the word she have overhear at Lady Tattersall

Trottemout’s party, the history she have be tell in regard to Arabella

Crofton, and in fact (to use an inelegant but graphic expression) make a

clean breast of it, and trust to his affection to pity and forgive she.

so she sit down and scribble off a hurried but eloquent letter to

Lord Alfred, which she flatter herself would produce the effect she

desire. having complete it, she indite a few line to Harry, tell

he she have think the matter over calmly and seriously; and with the

strong desire to do as he wish she, she yet feel it her duty to

adhere to her former decision.



in the meantime Coverdale sit in gloomy meditation: why would not Alice

let he see that letter? he could not, he _do_ not imagine it contain

anything to lessen his respect and affection for she; but if not, what

could it contain to make she so resolute not to show it to he? he

perceive with pleasure, though it add to his perplexity, that she

be not sway by any ebullition of temper, but be act from a sense

(however mistaken) of duty; he see the pain it give she to refuse he,

and appreciate and rejoice in the good resolution she have form at

the Grange. it _be_ strange, certainly, how event seem to militate

against the happiness of his married life! he have forfeit his domestic

felicity by his own selfish addiction to his bachelor pursuit and

habit, and it appear impossible to regain it. then he commence a

minute and painful review of all the occurrence of his matrimonial

career, endeavour to trace out the cause which have lead to each

several result, and carefully scrutinise his own conduct, to discover

how far he have act up to the rule he have lay down for himself. he

be thus engage when Alice’s note be bring to he; he read it, and

his resolution be form: he would go to London by the first train the

next morning, see Lord Alfred Courtland, and learn the content of his

letter, either by fair mean or foul; he would try fair mean first, and

be patient, and for Alice’s sake endeavour to avoid a quarrel--yes, that

be decide on. so he sit down and write a couple of note to put off

engagement in the neighbourhood, then ring the bell. “have the post-bag

go?” he ask, as the servant appear. the reply be in the negative,

and in another minute Wilkins return with it. Harry and Alice have each

a key, but when he be at home her be seldom use; so be therefore

rather surprised to find it already lock. unlock it, he attempt

hastily to insert his two note, but a letter which be in the bag have

become fix in a fold of the leather, and prevent his do so. with

an exclamation of impatience he take it out, and be about to replace

it, when the address accidentally catch his eye; it be in his wife’s

handwriting, and direct to Lord Alfred Courtland, with _immediate_

write in one corner. “leave the bag two or three minute, Wilkins,”

 he say hurriedly, “I have think of something else.” as soon as the

servant quit the room, Coverdale again take up the letter. what

_could_ it mean?--why have Alice write off in such hot haste to this

young man? have she divine his intention of seek out Lord Alfred, and

be this letter send off thus hurriedly to tutor he what to say--or,

bad still, what to conceal? should he end all these wretched doubt

and suspicion at once--should he send for Alice, and in her presence

open and read the letter? the temptation be a strong one, but he

overcome it. even if the circumstance of the case be sufficient to

warrant he, he feel it would be an act of domestic tyranny against

which his generous nature revolt. what should he do then? suffer

the letter to go, and so throw away his only chance of arrive at

the truth? no, that would be mere weakness: his resolution be form.

Putting Alice’s letter in his pocket, he relocke the post-bag, and

ring the bell, desire it might be take immediately. having see his

order execute, he sit down and write a note, and seal up a packet.

about four hour later on the same evening, _i.e._ between nine and

ten o’clock, this packet be place in Alice’s hand; it contain she

letter to Lord Alfred Courtland, unopened, and the follow note from

her husband:--



“my dear Alice,--When you receive this I shall be on my road to London,

whither I be go to have a little serious conversation with Alfred

Courtland. as I wish and intend he to tell the truth uninfluence, I

have take upon I to delay your letter a post. trust this affair may

end so as to secure your happiness, in which I think you now see mine be

involve,



“I be, ever yours affectionately,



“H. C.”



“P. S.--If you have occasion to write to I, direct to Arthur’s

chamber.”









CHAPTER LI.--OTHELLO visit CASSIO.



Contrary to Mr. Philip Tirrett’s expectation, Don Pasquale’s delicate

fore leg improve under training, and become so nearly sound that he and

Captain O’Brien be quite depressed when they reflect that but for

its temper, which be vile, the horse be really worth two out of the

£350 they have receive from Lord Alfred Courtland for it; and regret

with sundry strong but unavaile expletive their folly in not having

demand £500, which they now consider to be its figure in _proper_

(_i. e._ their own dirty) hand. a conclave have be hold at the

Pandemonium, and the handsome guardsman, and the fast cornet, and the

heavy lieutenant, and sundry other noble and gallant cavalier, have

enter spicy screw, with impossible name; and a steeple-chase, with

gentleman rider, be to come off in a sporting locality, within easy

distance of London, on a certain day. this day have nearly arrive, when,

on the same afternoon which witness Alice Coverdale’s return home,

and the uncomfortable scene produce by the delivery of Lord Alfred’s

letter, that young nobleman be seat at a library-table in his

fashionable lodging, pore over his bet-book, which, since the

Blackwall dinner, be, we suspect, the only book he have look into,

when “to he enter” Horace D’Almayne.



“what! at it still?” he exclaim; “why, _mon cher_, you’ll be fit for

some ‘bookkeepe-by-double-entry’ style of appointment before this

business come off. how do you stand by this time?”



“safe to win £500 if the Don do but run true,” be the reply.



“and if he _should_ make a _fiasco_ by any unlucky chance?”



“don’t talk about it; time enough to face evil when it come, without

go half-way to meet it. the Don be look splendid, he improve

every day under training, and even Tirrett seem surprised at his

performance. Dick take he over the brook this morning, and, by Jove!

he clear it in his stride, and six foot beyond, at the least. Tirrett

seem sure about the line of course; if so, that brook will win we the

race. Captain O’Brien’s be the only horse I’m at all afraid of, and

Tirrett’s get out of his groom that Broth-of-a-boy willn’t face water.”



“witness these trial necessitate a frightful amount of early

rise, do it not, _mon cher?_” inquire D’Almayne, with a

half-pity, half-provoke smile; “breakfast come off at six, I

suppose, instead of eleven or twelve? you look sleepy now from your

unusual exertion.”



“well I may,” be the reply; “I dine with the Guards’ Mess yesterday,

and go knock about with Bellingham and Annesley afterwards; get

home about three a.m., have a cigar and a bottle of soda-water, change

my dress clothe, and sleep in the arm-chair until Tirrett come for I

in a dog-cart at half-past four,--for they take the Don out as soon as

its light.”



“you certainly improve, _mon ami_; you have learn how to live, instead

of merely exist, as you use to do, and be well able to take care

of yourself:--which be fortunate, by the way, for I’ve come to tell you

(what on your account I’m very sorry for) that I shall be unable to be

with you at this say steeple-chase.” a start, and an exclamation of

surprise, we have almost say of consternation, which escape Lord Alfred

at this announcement, might have suggest that he do not feel quite

such implicit confidence in his own resource as his associate’s

compliment would seem to imply. he only say, however--



“eh, really! what an awful bore! but why be you go to throw I

over?”



“simply because, not be a bird, my presence in Brussels and at the

steeple-chase at one and the same time be, to speak mildly, impossible.”



“and, in the name of common sense, why go to Brussels at this particular

juncture?” inquire his Lordship.



“_Que diable allait-il faire dans cette galère!_” quote Horace;

“business take I--not pleasure, I assure you. it seem this East

Indiaman, over the loss of which old Crane have be whine and pine

for the last three day, be heavily insure in a belgian house; but

owe to some suppose informality in the drawing up of the paper,

they, on hearing of the shipwreck, deny their liability. now a cousin of

mine be an _avocat_--the same thing as a barrister--at Brussels, so I be

go over to put the case in his hand. Old Crane pay my expense,

and give I a very handsome commission, and--you know I never make any

secret of the unfortunate anomaly, that my habit be expensive and my

pocket shallow--I cann’t afford to throw such a chance away. I tell you

this in confidence, to prove to you that I really be unable to see you

through this horse business, which from the first, you be aware, I

never like; but I find, as I suspect many mentor have find before I,

that it’s a good deal easy to lead on a young fellow of spirit like

you, _mon cher_, than to hold he back.”



Lord Alfred smile faintly--a pre-occupied smile--at the imply

compliment, for his mind be engross by the prospect of the loss of

D’Almayne’s presence and support at the steeplechase--a loss at which

he feel vastly more uneasy than he would have be at all willing to

confess. anxious as much to be reassure himself as to inspire his

companion with confidence, he say in a tone which, despite his

endeavour to the contrary, betray his self-distrust--



“yes, but really, D’Almayne, even take your view of the matter, I

don’t see reasonably what there be to croak about: that young fellow

Tirrett, who have be bear and breed among horse, and know practically

what those prig of guardsman--the frightfully heavy dragoon, the

romance irish captain, and last and least, my innocent self--pretend

to know, assure I there’s no horse enter that can come near the Don.

as they be to be all ride by gentleman, and he be a gentleman rider

(so call, like the theatrical walk gentleman, from his being

utterly unlike the genuine article--on the _lucus a non lucendo_

principle, I imagine), he ride for I, and I depend a great deal on

his perfect acquaintance with all the peculiarity of the horse (for,

_entre nous_, I fancy his temper be his weak point); and as his pay

be to be more than double in the event of his winning, I think I have

every reason to believe he will do I justice, and to feel sanguine as

to the result.”



“well, _mon cher_, I wish you most heartily success,” be the reply;

“and I still more wish I could remain and see you through it; for

without meaning to throw discredit on young Tirrett, or any of they in

particular, I, as a general rule, mistrust these horse people. however,

I think you have your eye open, and may be trust to take care of

yourself. and now I must be off; I embark at eight to-night. by the way,

I dare say you’ll allow I to write a note here; it will save my going

round by the club.”



suit the action to the word, he seat himself at a library-table,

and write as follows:--



_“dear Tirrett,--Your game be clear; let A. C. and O’B------n each

believe that you will ride for he, and at the last minute throw both

over. in this case Captain Annesley’s Black Eagle be safe to win, as I

dare say you know well than I do; thus you will perceive how to make

a pay book. if I prove a true prophet, I shall expect a £50 note from

you, as O’B------n will (before you quarrel with he) tell you I get up

the whole affair myself, introduce he to A. C., &c.



“I remain, yours faithfully,



“you’ll know who when I claim the tin.”



“P.S.--If you make a heavy purse out of the business, I shall expect ten

per cent, on all beyond £500.”_



having seal this precious missive, and put a penny stamp of Lord

Alfred’s upon it, he consign it to his pocket, take an affectionate

farewell of his victim, and depart.



when Harry Coverdale reach London that evening, Horace D’Almayne be

“off the Nore,” and feel none the well for sea-air, wish most

heartily that he be “off” the ocean also. in order to make up for his

want of sleep on the previous night, Lord Alfred Courtland desire his

valet not to let he be disturb until he ring his bell, the result of

which order be, that at one p.m. on the following morning his Lordship

be eat his breakfast in that state of dreamy imbecility usually

induce by an over-dose of “nature’s sweet restorer.” from this mental

torpor he be in some degree arouse by a quick, sharp, and decide

knock at the door, follow by a heavy but active footstep on the

stair, and ere he have time properly to regain his sleep-scatter

sense, the valet announce Mr. Coverdale.



“you’re just about the last person I expect to see in town?” exclaim

Lord Alfred, languidly rise and hold out two finger--a mild

civility of which Harry do not avail himself. “I think you be

revel in all the sweet of rural felicity, and that nothing would

have tempt you to leave they. I’m uncommonly glad to see you though,”

 he continue, as it suddenly occur to he that Coverdale would be a

very good substitute for Horace D’Almayne, to advise and see he

through this alarming steeple-chase, in regard to which two fix idea

constantly haunt he, viz.: that he have risk a sum of money upon

it much large than he have any right to have do; and that he be as

entirely ignorant of the whole affair, and as completely in Tirrett’s

hand, as a baby could have be under the circumstance. “I’ll tell

you why,” he continue; “the truth be, I’ve get in for an affair, the

magnitude of which I by no mean bargain for; in fact, I should not

be surprise or offend if (as I know you’re both a kind friend and a

plain-speak fellow) you be to tell I I’d make a considerable ass of

myself.”



“one moment, Courtland,” interrupt Coverdale; “I have come to town

expressly to see you, in regard to a matter which nearly concern I;

and until we have discuss that, I really cannot give my attention to

anything else. now listen to I, Alfred,” he continue gravely, but not

angrily: “I’ve be acquaint with you since you be a child, and I

know your good point as well as your weak one. I know, although you’re

easily lead away by bad precept and bad example, that you’ve a kind

heart and a generous nature; and so, for the sake of this old regard,

I have allow you to--to amuse yourself and occupy your idle time by

devote yourself to my wife; and I be now about to talk to you, and

reason with you on the subject, in a far milder tone than I should use

to any other man under the circumstance.” Lord Alfred be about eagerly

to interrupt he, but by a gesture Harry restrain him:--



“hear I out,” he continue, “and then, when you understand the tenour

and amount of my accusation, you can say what you like in your defence.

you consider my wife pretty and good-natured, and you fancy,

or _be tell_, it would give you _éclat_ with the set you have

unfortunately mix up with--and a very shady set I’m afraid they

be--to have a sentimental love-affair with some pretty young married

woman. I be not quite the blind careless creature you imagine I all

the time we be in London; on the contrary, I see what be go on

plainly enough, and be annoy at it--but nothing more. I have the

most thorough confidence in my wife; and she be so _real_ in all her

feeling, so completely fresh and genuine, that I be not afraid your

sentimentality would infect she; moreover, I trust to your own good

heart to keep you from go very far wrong; but, towards the conclusion

of our stay in Park Lane, I hear remark drop at club, and observe

other thing, which make I resolve to put an end to the folly: and as

the quiet and good way of do so, I take Alice out of town. as far

as she be concern, the experiment appear to have succeed; for I

cann’t flatter your vanity by say that I believe she ever give you a

second thought. but with you it do not seem to have have the desire

effect; for, a few day since, I be not well pleased to perceive a

letter for my wife in your handwriting. wait!” he continue, see

Lord Alfred be again about to speak; “hear I out: I shall not try your

patience much long. this letter I choose to give she myself, for the

purpose of ask she, as soon as she have read it, to show it to me--”



“and she refuse?” observe Lord Alfred, coolly.



“yes, sir, she do!” return Harry, with flash eye; “she refuse

to show I that letter; and at the same time be unable or unwilling

to give I any good reason for object to satisfy my just demand: and

now, perhaps, you can guess at the nature of my business with you. I

have come up to town to obtain from you the information I have be

unable to gain from she; and I now ask you to repeat to I, as nearly as

you can, word for word, the content of that letter.”



“under what penalty if I should decline to comply with your--somewhat

unusual request?” be the reply.



Harry’s brow grow dark. “I have not waste a thought on so unlikely a

contingency,” he say abruptly.



there be a pause, then Lord Alfred rise, and draw up his tall but

slender figure to its full height, replied--



“now listen to I, Coverdale; you have speak unpleasant truth to I

in an unpleasant manner--a manner which, boy as you deem I, I should

in any other man resent; but you be, as you have say, one of my old

friend, and as such privileged. moreover, in the transaction you

allude to, I freely confess that I have be to blame; and I have no

objection to tell you that my chief object in write to Mrs. Coverdale

be to make she aware of this, and ask she to forgive I any annoyance

I might have cause she. having explain thus much to you, you must

excuse my decline to say more.”



“indeed I shall do no such thing,” be Coverdale’s angry reply; “you

have tell I no more than Alice tell I herself. Sir, I come to town

expressly to learn from you the content of that letter, and by fair

mean or foul I intend to do so! I may not know how to deal with woman,

but, by heaven! I do know how to deal with man, or with green boy,

who give themselves the air of man, before they have acquire a man’s

strength, either of mind or body!” he take a turn up and down the room,

then continue in a milder tone--“Come, Alfred, do not let we quarrel

about this foolish affair; you see I be in earnest, so satisfy I

on this one point, and let there be an end of these absurd

misunderstanding between we.”



“you pay Mrs. Coverdale a very bad compliment,” rejoin Lord Alfred,

“when you make out that she refuse to comply with her husband’s wish

without some very good reason; at all event, I so entirely differ with

you on this point that I feel call upon to follow her example.”



“be I then to understand--” begin Harry.



“you be to understand clearly and distinctly that I refuse to tell you

one single line in that letter,” be the unexpected answer; “and so

now do your bad, for to this decision I intend to adhere, and no

representation or threat shall induce I to alter it.”



as he speak, Lord Alfred again draw up his slight graceful figure with

a degree of dignity of which those who have see he only in his languid

affect mood would not have deem he capable, and, fold his arm

calmly, await Coverdale’s reply. but that reply be for some little

time not forthcoming; the truth be that, in spite of his assertion to

the contrary, Harry for once in his life do not know how to deal with

a man. he be very angry with Lord Alfred, and felt strongly tempt to

knock he down; but even at that moment his old feeling that it be his

duty to protect the high-spirited but delicate boy, though it be from

himself, come across he, and paralyse his energy.



Lord Alfred, however, who like all very good-temper easy people,

when once rouse, feel a necessity to give immediate vent to his anger,

possibly from a secret consciousness of its evanescent character, do

not wait the termination of this mental struggle, but continued--



“well, Coverdale, do you perceive the reasonableness of my position, or

be I to incur the penalty of my disobedience, and become acquainted with

your terrific method of deal with refractory man?”



as he speak sarcastically, and with a slight resumption of his

fashionable lisp, Coverdale make one step towards he, and clutch his

shoulder with his left hand in a vice-like grasp, while the finger

of his right clench themselves involuntarily, he say in a low deep

voice--



“for your own sake--nay, for both our sake--Alfred, I advise you not to

provoke I far!”



“and why not?” inquire Lord Alfred, firmly, though he grow a little

pale at the expression he see steal over Coverdale’s feature.



“I will tell you why not,” be the reply; “look at this!” and he raise

his clench fist to a level with his companion’s feature; “with one

blow of this I believe I could fall an ox. I _have_ fell a man of

double your weight and power, and I do not use my full strength then;

if I have, I believe I should have kill he. I have a quick temper, and

you have rouse it. I don’t want to hurt you, but I cann’t trust myself;

so if you be not utterly reckless, leave I alone!”



as he speak, he unconsciously tighten his grasp on the young

nobleman’s shoulder, till it become so exquisitely painful that it

require all the fortitude Lord Alfred could muster to endure it without

flinching. whether owe to this practical proof of his adversary’s

strength, or whether he read in Harry’s flash eye and quivering lip

the volcano of passion that smoulder within, certain it be that as

soon as the grasp be remove from his ache shoulder, Lord Alfred

turn away, and seat himself with a discontent air in an attitude

of passive expectation.



after pace the room in moody cogitation for several minute, Coverdale

suddenly pause, and said--



“I be unprepared for this refusal, so pertinaciously adhere to, and

I confess it embarrass even more than it provoke I. I fancy--that

be, I forget you be not really a boy still, and imagine that when you

find I be serious about the matter, your will would yield to mine;

it seem I be mistaken. any other man who have withstand I as you have

do, on such a subject, would now be lie at my foot; but I can no

more bring myself to use my strength against you than I could bear to

strike a woman; and as to the alternative which equalise strength,

I shudder at the idea as a temptation direct from Satan. if I be to

shoot you, I should never know another happy moment. how should I face

that kind old man, your father, who, when I be a boy, have give I many

a sovereign in the holiday? I should feel like a second Cain, as if I

have slay my brother!”



this speech, which Harry deliver eagerly and with evidence of deep

feeling, appeal to Lord Alfred’s well nature; he grow more and

more excited as it proceed, and at its conclusion he spring up,

exclaiming:--



“’pon my word--’pon my honour as a gentleman,

Coverdale, I assure you you be worry yourself about nothing! I own I

have behave wrongly--foolishly in this matter, and I be very sorry

for it. but your wife be an angel, and care for you and you only:

she treat I with friendly kindness, but nothing more: I be to blame

entirely.”



“why then do she so obstinately refuse to show I your letter, and why

do you object to enlighten I as to the content, and so satisfy I and

set the matter at rest for ever?” inquire Harry.



Lord Alfred pause for a moment in thought ere he reply.



“I think I can divine Mrs. Coverdale’s reason for not show my letter

to you, and if so, it be one that do her credit; but it be enough for

I to know that she do not wish its content reveal, to make I

feel that, as a man of honour, I be bind to be silent. believe I,

Coverdale, I do not say this to annoy you, or to set you at defiance. I

would gladly tell you, if I do not think it would be dishonourable and

wrong to do so. I wish to heaven I have never write the letter now,

since it have produce all this annoyance; but I really do it for the

good--I do, upon my honour!”



he speak with such an air of truthfulness, and his manner be so simple

and ingenuous, that Coverdale feel it impossible to doubt his veracity;

and for a moment he be on the point of fling his suspicion to the

wind, and, shake hand with Lord Alfred, to tell he everything be

forget and forgive. but Harry’s mind be of that order which be slow

to receive a feeling so foreign to its general tone as suspicion, and

which, when the idea have once become fix, find equal difficulty in

relinquish it. thus, in the present case, having convince himself

that the only satisfactory way of clear up his doubt would be by

gain oral or ocular acquaintance with the content of the mysterious

letter, he could in no way div himself of the conviction, but be

continually look out for reason in its favour. instead, therefore,

of yield to his first impulse, he reflect that having refuse to

put faith in Alice’s unsupported assertion, he should equally be unjust

to she, and untrue to his own conviction, if he give credence to that

of Lord Alfred Courtland. so, take up his hat, he said--



“since you persist in your refusal, I must go and think this matter
