over coolly and quietly; you shall see or hear from I before this time

to-morrow.” he turn to depart, but Lord Alfred hold out his hand:--



“we part as friend?” he say, inquiringly.



“neither as friend nor foe,” be the reply. “you shall learn my

decision to-morrow.” and reject his proffered hand, Coverdale quit

the apartment.









chapter LII.--A GLEAM of LIGHT.



no alarming amount of imagination will be require to enable the reader

to conceive that Harry return to his hotel considerably provoke and

dissatisfied at the result of his interview with Lord Alfred Courtland.

he have encounter opposition where he have expect an easy victory;

where he have feel certain of success, he have fail most signally;

and by no mean the least embarrassing part of the matter be, that

he really do not know whether to be most angry, or please, with Lord

Alfred, for his unexpected firmness. but, if the past be perplexing,

the future appear much more so. on quit Lord Alfred, he have call

at Horace D’Almayne’s lodging, where he acquire the information that

their usual occupant have start for the continent on the previous

evening. baffle in every attempt to obtain information concern the

mysterious letter, which haunt his imagination with the pertinacity

of some intrusive spectre callous to the restringent influence of bell,

book, and candle, Coverdale, after lie awake the great part of

the night, bend his step, the first thing the next morning, in the

direction of his brother-in-law’s chamber, wish to consult he,

but at the same time feel so unwilling to blame Alice, even by

imputation, that the chance be against his take such a step. on

reach his destination, however, the difficulty solve itself, for,

early as be the hour, Arthur be from home, but Coverdale find a

letter await he in Alice’s hand writing. hastily tear it open, an

enclosure drop from it, and on stoop to pick it up he perceive,

to his extreme surprise, that it be the identical epistle which have

already cause he a journey to London and a sleepless night; and

which, but for his forbearance and kindliness of disposition, might have

involve he in a serious quarrel--if nothing bad--with his former

friend and school-fellow. Alice’s letter, which bear every mark of

having be write under feeling of the great excitement, run as

follows:--



“Dearest Harry,--Your hasty departure have overturn all my plan and

arrangement, which, believe I, be make with a view only to try

and avert the catastrophe which, I shudder to think, may be even

now impend. justice to Lord Alfred, who may have incur your

indignation, as well as my anxiety to clear myself in your eye, by

make you acquaint with the whole truth, induce I to send you

the interesting letter which have give rise to all this sad

misunderstanding; and, as I imagine you have ere this see and come to

some sort of explanation with Lord Alfred, my reason for withholding

it exist no long. when you read it, you will perceive _why_ I be

so unwilling to show it to you. I feel convinced that the passage

refer to Mr. D’Almayne, which completely confirm the unfavourable

opinion you have always entertain of he, would irritate you greatly

against he; and, when Lord Alfred proceed to write of he as a noted

duellist, a dead shot, &c., you may smile at my womanly weakness, but

can you wonder that I hesitate to show you the letter, that I choose

rather to allow you to think untrue thing of I, than to clear myself

at the risk of imperille your safety? and now, dearest Harry, if you

love I as you say, and as I hope and believe you do, if you would ever

have I know another moment’s peace, and not be weigh down by endless

self-reproach, return home, I implore you, without take any far

step in this matter. I be not afraid, when you have see his penitent

letter, that you will be angry with Lord Alfred, but entreat of you to

avoid that hateful Mr. D’Almayne. even suppose that he have be

the cause of all this unhappiness; that be now pass, and he will be

powerless to influence our future life.



“I be quite willing, if it will be any satisfaction to you, to agree

never to spend another spring in London; I have see enough of its

heartless dissipation and frivolity, and for the future hope to find my

happiness in our own dear home, which, if you do but return to it

safe and sound, I would not exchange for a queen’s palace. pray, pray,

dear Harry, come back without delay. I have worry and fret

myself quite ill already, and shall be wretched till I see you again.

ever your penitent, but love,



“Alice.”



having peruse his wife’s letter with mingle feeling of satisfaction

and regret,--satisfaction to find how completely she be able to clear

herself, and regret at the pain and annoyance which he be sure this

affair must have occasion her,--Coverdale unfold and read carefully

Lord Alfred’s epistle, which have occasion result the writer little

contemplate. at his Lordship’s ingenuous confession of his folly and

absurdity, Harry smile, mutter, “poor boy! I wish I have not be

so sharp with he yesterday;” but as he go on his brow contract,

and when he come to the account of Horace D’Almayne, and the report

he have circulate in regard to Coverdale and Miss Crofton, he could

restrain his rage no long, and spring up, he exclaim, “Scoundrel!

mean, pitiful, lie scoundrel! but he shall answer to I for this. a

bold rogue, who would execute his own villainy, be a prince to a rascal

like this, who lay a plot to deprive I of my wife’s affection, and

then make a cat’s-paw of that poor foolish boy to carry it out. I see

it all now. the behaviour which appear so strange and unaccountable

in my darling Alice, proceed from a very natural feeling of jealousy,

excite by all these abominable report; and, the bad of it be, that

even now I cann’t be entirely open with she, because of my promise

to Arabella. I wish to heaven I have never be fool enough to bind

myself!--and yet how could I avoid it? for she have a good heart, and a

generous disposition--though, partly from a bad education, partly from

natural temperament, her idea be sadly warp. I be sure she really

love I; of course, in a conventional point of view, it be not right

in she to do so; but--well, it’s no use humbug--I don’t believe the

mam ever breathe, who honestly, and from his heart, could blame a woman

for love he; principle and reason may accuse she, but feel defend

she so eloquently, that the cause be gain at the first hearing. I

think I act rightly by she. if I have it to do over again, I don’t see

how else I could honourably behave; perhaps it be weak to make she a

promise of concealment, but she be _so_ unhappy, her proud spirit be

so utterly crush and break down, that I would have do anything, not

actually wrong, to console she.”



he pause, reseat himself, then resume more quietly, “perhaps it

be as well that scoundrel D’Almayne be not within reach: if I be to

horsewhip he, as I most assuredly should and would, I suppose I should

be force to meet he, blackguard as he be, if he be to challenge

I; and he would do so, I dare say, though I know he to be a coward at

heart, for his social position be his livelihood, and he must maintain

that, or starve. I utterly abhor duel--it’s so very like deliberate

murder; it be different in the old day, when man wear sword

habitually; then, a couple of fellow quarrel and tilt at

each other across the dining-table, while their blood be up, and a

flesh-wound or two generally let off their superfluous energy, and cure

their complaint--it be little more than knock a man down who have

insult you. there be none of that waiting, and then coolly, calmly,

take the life of a fellow-creature in cold blood, which be the

disgusting part of the modern duel. and now about little Alfred. poor

boy, he have be sadly lead away by that scoundrel, but his heart be in

the right place still; that be a very nice letter of his to my wife,

and I’m glad he write it, though it have cause I some trouble and

annoyance. well, I’ll call on he, and tell he I do he injustice,

and then go down to the Park by the next train, to comfort my darling

Alice. by Jove, I feel quite a different man since I read that

letter--Harry’s himself again.” and in proof of his assertion, he begin,

for the first time for many week, to whistle his favourite air--



                   “a southerly wind, and a cloudy sky,



                   proclaim it a hunting morning.”



another ten minute, and a Hansom cab suffice to take he to Lord

Alfred’s lodging.









CHAPTER LIII.--AFTER the MANNER of “BELL’S LIFE.”



“I dare say the lazy young dog ben’t up yet,” be Coverdale’s mental

comment, as he knock at the door of Lord Alfred Courtland’s lodging.

although, as a general rule, the idea might not be a mistaken one, yet

this particular occasion be evidently an exception, for, on enter

Lord Alfred’s sitting-room, Coverdale find that young gentleman most

elaborately get up in an unimpeachable sporting costume, but sit

with an open letter and his bet-book before he, look the picture

of despair. as Coverdale enter, he glance upward with a slight

start; then, without wait to be speak to, he exclaim, in a strange

reckless tone, as different from his usual manner as a tempest from a

zephyr, “well! which be it to be? peace or war? either will suit I,

though I should rather prefer the latter; about the good thing that can

happen to I would be for you to put a bullet through my head; at all

event, it would save I the trouble of blow my own brain out, for I

expect that be what it will come to before long.”



“nonsense!” be the reply. “what do you mean by talk such childish

rubbish? what be the matter with you, man?”



“first answer my question, and let I know whether I be speak to a

friend or a foe,” rejoin Lord Alfred.



“a friend, as I always have be, and always will be, to you, as long as

you deserve an honest man’s friendship,” return Coverdale, heartily.

“Alice have send I your letter, and it do you great credit; but I

always know you have a good heart; so, for any trouble or annoyance you

have cause I, I freely forgive you, and I’ll answer for it Alice do

the same; and I don’t know that you may not have teach she a lesson

which may be very useful to she in after life. she be young and giddy,

and please with admiration and gaiety; and this have show she the

danger and folly of such frivolous pursuit as these taste lead to.”



as he speak, he hold out his hand; Lord Alfred seize and shake it

warmly.



“my dear Coverdale,” he say, “you have make I happy, or I might more

truly say, less miserable, than five minute ago I would have believe

it possible for anything to do; it be not your anger, or its

consequence, I dread; but the truth be, I always have the great

regard and respect for you--I be proud of your friendship--and the idea

that, by my fault, I have forfeit it, lower I in my own estimation,

and be a source of continue uneasiness and regret to I. you think I

be talk exaggerated nonsense just now, but I assure you when you

come into this room five minute ago, I be thoroughly reckless; just in

the frame of mind in which man commit suicide, or any other act of

wicked folly.”



Coverdale, though he by no mean comprehend the “situation” (as it

be now the fashion to term all possible combination of event), yet

perceive that his companion be thoroughly in earnest, and require

sympathy and assistance; so he evince the first by get up and

lay his hand encouragingly on Lord Alfred’s shoulder, while he

offer the latter in the following word: “what be it, my boy? anything

that I can help you in?”



“if anybody can, you be the very man,” reply Lord Alfred, as he

eagerly grasp his friend’s hand; “but really,” he continue, while

the tear that sparkle in his clear blue eye prove his sincerity,

“really, I don’t know how to thank you for all your kindness, when I

have deserve so differently at your hand too; but you always be the

most generous, well-hearted----”



“there! that will do, you foolish boy,” interrupt Coverdale, who, like

all simple truthful character, feel uncomfortable at hear his own

praise; “we’ll take it for grant that I’m no end of a fine fellow,

and proceed to learn what particular scrape your wisdom have fail to

keep you out of.”



“Scrape, you may call it,” be the reply; “partly through my own folly,

partly through the rascality of other, I be almost certain to lose a

couple of thousand pound on a steeple-chase, for which I’ve be idiot

enough to enter a horse, and where to lay my hand on as many hundred

be more than I know. I shall not be able to meet my engagement, and

shall be stigmatize as a blackleg and a swindler, at the very time when

it be through the villainy of blackleg and swindler that I shall be

place in such a position!”



“cann’t your father?” begin Coverdale.



“if you don’t wish to render I frantic, don’t mention my father,” be

the unexpected rejoinder; he pause, then resumed--“Coverdale, I will

not trust you by half, I know you will hold my confidence sacred.

my father be most kind and liberal to I, more liberal almost than he

should be, for he be not a rich man, and have many call upon he, and

this year I know he have meet with severe loss. I have an allowance

on which I could have live well, and as become my rank; but Horace

D’Almayne, under pretence of show I life, take I to a gaming-house,

I acquire a taste for play, or rather I play, because I think it

the ‘correct thing’ and I be now not only without money, but actually in

debt. then come this horse business,”--here Lord Alfred give Coverdale

a succinct account of the various particular of the affair with

which the reader have be already make acquainted. “I feel, up to this

morning,” he resume, “tolerably confident of success, rely chiefly

on Tirrett’s riding, which be say to be first-rate; imagine, then,

my rage and disgust when half an hour ago _this_ be give me!”--As he

speak, he hand Coverdale the follow note:--



_“I be sorry to inform your lordship that circumstance, over which I

have no control, oblige I to decline the honour of ride Don Pasquale

for you to-day.



“I be,



“your Lordship’s obedient servant,



“Philip Tirrett.”_



“pleasant and encouraging, certainly,” observe Coverdale, when he have

finish read the note.



“that fellow Tirrett be the great scoundrel unhung!” exclaim Lord

Alfred, crush the paper in his hand with an action suggestive of

his willingness to perform a similar process of annihilation upon its

writer.



“by no mean,” return Harry, coolly; “he be simply a very average

speciman of his class, half-jockey, half-dealer, and whole blackleg of

a low stamp--there be hundred such on the turf; however, he seem to

have get you into an awful fix this time--we must try and find out

what can be do. I’ll stay and see you through it at all event; it’s

fortunate to-day be the day, for I could not have remain beyond; I

dare say I shall be back in time to catch the eight o’clock train, and I

shall then be at home by eleven. what time do you start, and how do you

get down?”



“I go down on a drag which leave the Pandemonium at twelve. I’ll take

care to keep a seat for you, if you really be kind enough to go with

I. I be really quite ashamed to avail myself of your kindness, when

I know how anxious you must be to get back, and calm Mrs. Coverdale’s

fear; but I feel your presence and your knowledge of the right way in

which to deal with these people will be so invaluable to I, that I have

not sufficient self-denial to deprive myself of they.”



“all serene! don’t make fine speech about it,” rejoin Harry. “I’ve

one or two place to call at, and I’ll meet you at the Frying Pan, as

they call that diabolically name club of your, five minute before

twelve; and, above all, don’t look so woe-begone, or you’ll have the

odd against Don Pasquale increase to a frightful degree; put on a cool

nonchalant air, like your precious friend and adviser, D’Almayne, who

may thank his star that the german Ocean lie between he and I just

now, for I’d have horsewhip he, as sure as I stand here, so that he

should have spend the next fortnight in his bed at all event, and it

would have be a mercy if I haven’t break some of his bone for he;

but I’m glad he’s away, for, after all, I suppose one have no right to

take the law into one’s own hand. well, I must be off, but depend upon

my meeting you, and in the meantime look alive, and don’t sit poring

over that stupid betting-book; you’re in a mess, that I don’t deny, but

that be no reason why you should lose heart: on the contrary, you’ll

have need of all your pluck to get you through it. never despond, man!

when thing come to the bad, they’re sure to mend. look at I: since

I receive that letter from my little wife, and read your notable

composition, I’m a different creature.” so say, Coverdale resume his

hat, and be about to quit the room, when glance at his companion’s

countenance, he suddenly stop.



“Alfred, my poor boy,” he say kindly, “I cann’t leave you with such a

face as that! listen to I, I’ll do all I can for you, to get you out of

this scrape to-day, and very likely thing may turn out well than we

expect; but if the bad come to the bad, you have only to promise I

two thing, viz., to give up your intimacy with Horace D’Almayne, and

not to enter a gambling-house again for the next ten year; and whatever

money you require, shall be place in your banker’s hand before

settle-day.”



as he speak, Lord Alfred grasp his hand, endeavour to falter forth a

few word of gratitude, but, utterly break down in the attempt, burst

into tear.



Harry, nearly as much affect at the sight of his friend’s emotion,

mutter, “Pshaw! there’s nothing to make a fuss about,” wring his hand

cordially, and hastily quit the room.



at ten minute to twelve a well-appoint drag, with four slapping

grey, excite the admiration of street boy in the vicinity of the

Pandemonium, by draw up at the door of that fast of club, and

five minute later, Harry Coverdale, habit in a loose dust-colour

wrapper, make his appearance, and toss a small carpet-bag to one of

the groom, desire he to put it in the boot. Lord Alfred be eagerly

wait to receive he, and introduce he to sundry noble sportsman, or

man desire so to be consider, who be to compose the live freight

of the drag; one or two of they be old acquaintance of Coverdale’s,

amongst they be the facetious Jack Beaupeep, who appear in his

usual charming spirit, and take an early opportunity of inform

Coverdale, in the strict confidence, that a certain young man, with

pale and swollen feature, who, he declare, live only to play on the

cornopean, might be expect to produce new and startling effect upon

his next performance, he (Jack Beaupeep) having already contrive to

insinuate percussion cracker into all three valve of his victim’s

instrument. one minute before twelve a tall, good-look man, attire

in a white hat, and a wonderful driving cape, whose christian name be

William, and his patronymic Barrington, but who, from his passion for

driving, be more commonly know by the _sobriquet_ Billy Whipcord,

descend the step of the Pandemonium, and, arrange the rein

scientifically between his finger, mount the box and assume his

seat, at the same time not take, but bestow, the oath for the

benefit of an obtuse helper, who have “presume to buckle the off

leader’s billet in the check, instead of the low bar, when he know the

mare pull like----” well, suppose we say, “like a steam-engine!” as

the first stroke of twelve peal from the high church steeple of

St. Homonovus, which, as everybody know, stand exactly opposite the

Pandemonium, the aforesaid Billy Whipcord obligingly make his team a

present of their respective head, the attendant helper seize

the corner of the horsecloth which have hitherto guard their

thorough-breed loin from whatever may be the equine equivalent for

lumbago, and jerk they off with a degree of energy which threaten

to take hide and all together, with a bound and a plunge the denude

quadruped spring forward, the boy cheer, the club servant perform

pantomimic action, indicative of admiration and respect, and the drag

start.



Monsieur de Saulcy, Mr. Kinglake, and other traveller, French, English,

and American, who take pleasure in go to the East to make mistake

about the site of Sodom and Gomorrah, hazard a futile hypothesis

in regard to the Holy Sepulchre, or, in some similar fashion, exert

themselves to prove that other than wise man come from the West in

these latter day, inform we, that when a camel die, vulture and other

strange fowl suddenly congregate around the body, though in what way

the intelligence (for those bird can have no _Bell’s life_) reach

they, be a point on which no _savant_ have yet be find wise enough to

enlighten we--wherefore, in general term, the fact be state to result

from instinct. by a like instinct do strange creature mysteriously

appear on the face of the earth, when a steeple-chase, or other sporting

event, be arrange to come off in any give locality: human vulture,

hawk, carrion-crow, bat, and owl, all (singular as an ornithologist

may deem it) with very black leg, attract by the fascination of

horse-flesh, assemble from the four quarter of--heaven, we be go

to say, but, on second thought, we cannot so conclude the paragraph.

still, from whatever locality they come, come they do in flock, and

gather at certain point, whence they may witness the start, or, “the

jump into the lane,” or, “cross the brook,” or the “awkward place,”

 over which the horse that leap, tumble, or scramble first, be safe to

win, as their various taste may lead they.



there be one feature in these affair, for which we have never be able

to account, viz., the mysterious presence of a certain average amount of

baby; they invariably arrive in tax cart, and entirely engross the

mental and bodily faculty of one mother and one female and sympathetic

friend each, so that every ten baby necessitate the presence of twenty

woman, who, from the moment they set out, to the time at which they

return, never appear conscious of the race-course, the company, the

jockey, the horse, or, indeed, of anything save their infant tyrant.

that these woman can have bring the baby for their own pleasure, be

an hypothesis so absurd, that no one who have see the going on of these

young pickle towards their parent and guardian, can for a moment

entertain it; a more, perhaps the most, probable one be, that the

infant come to please themselves, for, although we have never observe

that they pay much attention to the strict business of the race, yet,

in their own way, they appear to enjoy themselves very thoroughly. their

manner and custom be mark by an easy conviviality, and absence from

the restraint which usually fetter society, which we can conceive

must render their babyhood one epicurean scene of gay delight. thus,

monopolize the good place in the cart, shade by the family umbrella,

and dress in the late fashion from Lilliput, these young sybarite

recline languidly on the maternal bosom, or sit erect, “mooing,”

 crowing, and “wa wa-ing” in the face of the company generally, roar

at the sight of family friend whose acquaintance they do not desire

to cultivate, or clawing at the eye and hair of the select few whose

homage they be willing graciously to receive. then, wildly reckless of

appearance, and consult only their own ungoverned appetite, they

not only resolve to dine in public, at the maternal expense, but when

their desire have be gratify by their self-sacrifice parent,

betray a thankless indifference to the safe custody of the good thing

afford they, which render their vicinity dangerous to all decently

attired Christians (those only excepted, who consider a “milky way” the

way in which they should go), during the remainder of the festivity.

thus (we say it boldly, though we know we be provoke the enmity of

all our female reader, who consider a _darle_ baby can never be _de

trop_), we hereby declare our opinion, that by the law of the Jockey

Club, all dog and infant found unmuzzle on any race-course, should be

seize by the police, and instantly----------we leave the mind’

eye of the anxious mother of England to supply the blank. but we be

slightly digress.



as they reach the field whence the start be to take place, in which a

booth or two and a very mild speciman of a grand stand have be erect,

Harry find an opportunity to whisper to Lord Alfred----



“now, remember what I tell you; appear as cool as if you haven’t sixpence

depend on this race; if long odd be offer against the horse, take

’em; I’ll stand the risk up to a fifty-pounder; if it have transpire

that Tirrett willn’t ride for you, say quietly that you be provide with

an efficient substitute--as soon as I see clearly how the land lie,

I’ll tell you more.”



Lord Alfred look--as he be--singularly puzzle, but of the hundred

who be flock to that race-course, Coverdale be the only man on

whom he feel he could rely, and he most willingly place himself in his

hand.



having insinuate the drag into the most favourable position for

behold from its roof the line of the course, the Hon. Billy Whipcord,

having acquit himself so as to call forth an encomium even from Harry

Coverdale, who be a severe critic in such matter, descend from

his seat, and, with most of the other, repair to an extempore

bet-ring, compose of all the knowing one present.



Lord Alfred be about to accompany they, when Harry lay his finger on

his arm to detain he.



“what time do you order the Don to be on the ground?”



Lord Alfred refer to his watch.



“he willn’t be here for the next half-hour,” be the reply. “it be

consider advisable to spare his excitable nerve as much of the noise

and bustle as possible.”



“he be at a farm somewhere near, be he not?” continue Coverdale. “I see

your saddle-horse on the ground; let we canter down and have a look at

he.”



Lord Alfred agreeing, at a signal from his master the packgroom ride

up, and resign his horse to Coverdale, the friend mount, and be

about to ride off in the direction of the farm-house, when the Honourable

Billy Whipcord intercept they with a face express the deep

concern.



“my dear Courtland,” he begin, “a report have somehow get abroad that

Tirrett willn’t ride for you, and that irish blackguard, Captain O’Brien,

do not scruple openly to declare that he be to ride Broth-of-a-boy for

he instead; the rumour gain ground every minute, and the Don be go

down accordingly; all his good friend be hedge wherever they can get

a bet take. I hope there’s no truth in it.”



Coverdale glance for a moment towards Lord Alfred, who reply

carelessly, “don’t alarm yourself, my dear fellow, I can hardly suppose

even Phil Tirrett would have the face to throw I over and ride for

O’Brien; but, if he should indulge in such a caprice, I know my man,

and be prepare with a substitute so efficient, that I rather hope

your tiding may be true.” see that the Honourable William look

incredulous, he continue, “if you’re inclined to follow the hedging

dodge yourself, I’m as willing as ever to back the Don against the

field: how do the odd stand?”



reassure by this practical proof of his Lordship’s sincerity, the

Honourable William (who, in spite of his innate honourableness, be

rather a “leg” than otherwise), hastily mutter “that he’d a very safe

book as it stand, and that if the Don be all serene, he have no wish to

alter it,” and return to reap some advantage from the information he

have acquire.



“how do I do that?” ask Lord Alfred, as they canter off.



“splendidly!” be the reply; “when all other trade fail you, you’ll

be able, with a little of my able tuition, to turn horse-chaunter and

blackleg.”



Lord Alfred shake his head, add, “only let I get out of this affair

safely, and if you find I do anything in the horse line again, write

I down the veri idiot that ever run his head, open-eye, against a

brick wall.”



five minute’ brisk riding bring they to the gate at which Tirrett have

enter on the morning after the Blackwall dinnerparty. as they do so,

a horseman leave the yard by a hand-gate at the opposite corner. Lord

Alfred gaze after he eagerly.



“who be your mysterious friend?” inquire Harry.



“I cann’t be certain,” be the reply, “but the figure, and the way in

which he sit his horse, be very like that young scoundrel, Tirrett;

I’ve a great mind to gallop after he, and either make he ride for I,

or horsewhip he;” and Lord Alfred look quite fierce and determine,

as if he mean to do as he say, and be able; but Coverdale, smile at

his energy, restrain him--



“gently there--take it coolly! why, you’re become quite a fire-eater,”

 he say, laugh; “but, seriously, if you could make he ride for you

against his will, he would only contrive to lose you the race. and, as

to horsewhipping, if you be to horsewhip every blackleg who

break down with you in turf affair, you’d require a portable

thrashing-machine, for mortal arm could never stand it.”



as he speak, they reach the stable, dismount, and, tie their

horse up to a couple of ring in the wall, Lord Alfred draw a key from

his pocket, and, apply it to the lock, admit Harry and himself.

so quietly do they enter, and so engross be the groom with his

occupation, that they have full time to observe he before he be aware

of their presence. fully equip (with the exception of his coat)

for appear on the race-course, he be stoop over a pail of water

bathe his nose, from which the blood be still rapidly drop.

Coverdale glance expressively at Lord Alfred, then whisper, “speak to

he--I want to see his face.”



“why, Dick, what be it? have you hurt yourself, my lad?” he inquire,

good-naturedly.



raise himself, with a start, the man look round. “no, my Lord, it

be nothin’ to sinnify; honly, have I wo a reche hup to get the Don’s

saddle, hit slip, hand fall right hon my blessed nose, hand set hit a

bleed howdacious!”



“do you obtain that genius, with the horse, from Tirrett?” inquire

Harry, _sotto voce_; receive a reply in the affirmative, he continue,

“then let I have a word or two with he in private--I think he may be

make useful, but one never can get anything out of these fellow, except

in a_ tête-à-tête_.”



Lord Alfred nod assent, and, feign some plausible excuse, leave the

stable.



as soon as they be alone, Harry address the groom with an

intelligent half-nod, half-wink, which, however ineffectual it might

have prove in the case of a blind horse, produce a decide impression

on the sharp-sighted Dick.



“Hark ye, my friend,” he begin, “it strike I you and I be old

acquaintance.”



“cann’t say as I ever remember set heye on your honour afore,”

 be the reply, though something in the expression of the man’s face

contradict his assertion.



“do you never live with Count Cavalho, a spanish nobleman?”



the man pause, then answer in a surly tone, “and suppose I do, what

then?”



“merely, that while I be in Paris, a groom in his employ be detect

sell the corn and hay; the moment the charge be bring against he

the fellow decamp, but the evidence of his dishonesty be so clear,

that the Count offer a reward of fifty pound for his apprehension;

the man be not find, but I should know he by sight if I be to meet

he,” and again Coverdale fix his pierce glance upon his companion’s

feature.



having pause for a minute, during which time the groom stand eye he

furtively, and shift uneasily from leg to leg--at the expiration of

that period, Harry ask abruptly, “why do young Tirrett strike you in

that brutal manner, before he leave the stable just now?”



he speak at a venture, but the arrow hit the bull’s-eye. throw

completely off his guard, the man exclaim, with an oath, “you know

everything! who in the world be you?”



“my name’s coverdale,” be the reply. “I’m no wizard, but I’ve be on

the turf long enough to keep my eye and ear open; and now listen to

I; you know all I’ve say be true, you perceive that I could expose you

if I be so inclined; you _have_ no cause to entertain any very strong

affection for Mr. Philip Tirrett; therefore I see many reason why you

should do as I wish you--none why you should not.”



he pause for a reply, and, after a moment’s hesitation, the groom

begin, “I see it ain’t o’ no use try to gammon you, Mr. Coverdale,

you’re right about Tirrett, he cum here a wantin I to lame that horse,

and so git myself into trouble, may be; when, as I tell he, there ain’t

no need for it, for he ain’t agoe to ride it, and barrin myself and

he, there ain’t nobody else as can ride it to win, I’ll take my davy

o’ that, so he’d no call to cut up rough, and knock a feller about like

that--but I owe he one for it, and I’ll pay it some of these day. as

to that hay and corn business of the Count’s, I don’t do the correct

thing altogether by he, I know, but though I have to cut, and it be all

lay on to I, there be other more to blame nor I, I do assure you,

I be but a boy like at the time, and I wor lead on, don’t ye see? still,

it’s true enough; I don’t want the thing bring up again. my lord here,

he’s a nice young feller--precious green, tho’! I _never_ did--” he add

parenthetically, with a sympathy-demand wink at Coverdale, “and he’s

treat I very kind and liberal, and so the long and the short of it

be, if I can oblige you, sir, why I’m agreeable.”



“well, you can oblige I, and it shall be worth your while to do so,”

 be the reply; “and as I see you’ve get an honest side to your nature,

I’ll be frank with you. Lord Alfred have trust Tirrett to win this race

for he, and have bet very largely on the faith of his riding for he;

Tirrett, be a scoundrel, have throw he over, and we’re in a fix--the

only way I see of get out of it be to ride the horse myself.”



here the groom interrupt, by audibly ejaculating, “the Lord have mercy

on your poor neck!”



“to ride the horse myself,” continue Coverdale, coolly; “and I want you

to tell I honestly, first, whether if the brute be properly ride, he

have a fair chance to win, and secondly, if you be go to ride, and

try all you know to come in first, how you would set about it.”



for a minute, the man remain mute with surprise, then mutter,

“well, I’ve see you ride, and you’ve a well seat, and nearly as good

a bridle-hand as Phil Tirrett himself; but, lor, to think of ride a

steeple-chase on that beast the first time you’re on his back! however,

if you _will_ do it, listen to I,” and, draw Harry aside, he

whisper innumerable hint and direction in his ear, in as low a tone

as if he fear the very wind of Heaven would reveal the matter.









CHAPTER LIV.--SETTLING PRELIMINARIES.



“to keep a light but steady hand on he; to be careful not to pull at

he or check he with the curb; but to see his mouth with the snaffle,

if he cann’t be hold without; never to hit he, upon any consideration,

by reason that he’ll stand the spur, but not the whip; to be prepare

for his knock my brain out when he throw up his head, or break

my back by a way he’s get with his hind-quarter when he fling up his

heel; to look out for his pleasant little trick of jump off the

ground all four foot at once in a slantindicular direction, when

anything surprise he; to let he take his leap in his own fashion, or

he’ll either rush at they or refuse they altogether; to jump on his back

before he bite or kick I, if I can possibly do so; and, above all,

to show he, from first to last, that I’m not in the slight degree

afraid of he--I think these be the chief point to which I be advise

to direct my attention in ride the fascinating quadrupe on whom you

have invest your capital,” observe Coverdale to Lord Alfred, as they

canter back to the race-ground.



“you shall not do it--you must not think of it!” rejoin Lord Alfred,

hastily; “you’ll be throw and kill, and Mrs. Coverdale will say it’s

my doing. I could not bear it--it would drive I mad. come, promise

you’ll give it up!”



“silly boy!” return Coverdale, with a good-natured smile “tell I,

would _you_ give it up in my position?”



“well, yes--no, perhaps I should not; but then you know the case would

be a very different one.”



“certainly it would,” return Coverdale; “I be not the heir to an

ancient peerage--the noble constitution of England would not suffer

injury in one of its three notable estate, if my neck be break;

but I don’t see the necessity for pre-suppose any such sombre

contingency--this be not the first time, by many, that I’ve gallop a

queer horse across country. why, man, from the day I be fourteen

I’ve break all my own hunter, and let I tell you, a hot-temper

four-year-old thorough-breed be rather an awkward customer to deal with.

a timid old gentleman would find himself decidedly misplace astride

such a quadruped. but here we be. now recollect, keep up a bold

exterior, as the melodramatic gent paraphrase ‘never say die.’ back

the Don as freely as if Tirrett be go to ride for you, and

mention I as the illustrious gentleman-jockey you have secure as his

substitute.”



Lord Alfred nod assent, and they rejoin the group around the

bet-ring, in the centre of which stand the gallant Milesian, Captain

O’Brien, vociferate loudly in what he would himself have term a

thunder rage. the cause be soon discover: Mr. Philip Tirrett

have, five minute before, make his appearance on the course, and coolly

inform the captain not only that he be mistaken in suppose he

intend to ride for he, but that he be go to perform the service

for Captain Annesley, of her Majesty’s Life Guards, upon his famous

steeple-chaser Black Eagle, which, in his poor opinion, look very

like a winner. as Lord Alfred and Harry come up, the Honourable

Billy Whipcord, who, so to speak, live upon horseflesh, and have a

tolerably heavy book on the race, be in a great state of agitation and

excitement, exclaim, “here, Lord Alfred, what do you say to all this?

there’s a squabble as to who Mr. Tirrett be to ride for. I think you’d

settle with he, long ago, to ride Don Pasquale?”



“such be, no doubt, the understanding between we,” return Lord

Alfred, firmly; “nor have I reason to suspect that he mean not to fulfil

his engagement, until I receive a note some two hour ago, tell I

that circumstance prevent he from ride for I. these circumstance

I now, for the first time, conjecture to resolve themselves into the

fact that he have be bribe by some one to ride for Captain Annesley.”



“pray, my Lord, do you intend that remark to apply to I?” inquire

Captain Annesley, who be a tall, handsome, fashionable-look man, with

black whisker and moustache.



“I intend the remark to apply to Mr. Tirrett,” be Lord Alfred’s

reply; “he have engage to ride for I; I believe he have be bribe to

break that engagement, because I can imagine no other reason so likely

to influence a person of his character; but it’s a matter of perfect

indifference to I who may have bribe he, and as I be fortunate enough

to have secure the service of a gentleman on whose honour I can rely,

as well as upon his horsemanship, I care very little about the whole

matter, and must leave you, gentleman, to settle your difference

without my interference.”



as he speak he be turn to leave the spot, when Tirrett step

before he and prevent he.



“not so fast, my Lord,” he say, insolently; “I consider that you’ve

insult I by the term in which you have just speak, and I desire you

to recall your word.”



an indignant refusal from Lord Alfred apparently exasperate the young

blackleg beyond endurance, and raise his horsewhip threateningly, he

advance a step towards his opponent. as he do so, a heavy hand be

press against his chest, effectually bar his farth progress,

while a deep voice say sternly, “stand back, sir! I should have think

you have be on the turf long enough to recognize a gentleman when you

see he, and to know that such person be not to be bully, though

they may be swindle. let I give you a word of advice: you will have

quite enough on your hand to get out of this morning’s work without

some unpleasant _exposé_. your associate, Captain O’Brien, seem

inclined to be disagreeably communicative--don’t get yourself

horsewhip into the bargain!”



when Coverdale make the reference to O’Brien, Phil Tirrett turn pale,

and gnaw his under lip in fruitless anger; but, as he conclude,

he get up the steam sufficiently to inquire, with an insolent laugh,

“horsewhip, eh?--who’s likely to do it, I should like to know?”



“_i_ be,” be Coverdale’s quiet answer. their eye meet--but Tirrett

could not endure Harry’s steadfast gaze; so, favour he with a most

melodramatic scowl of hatred, he slunk away through the crowd. after

much angry altercation, Captain O’Brien’s horse be withdraw--other

preliminary of the race settle--and the time appoint for start

draw nigh, when Captain Annesley lounge up to Lord Alfred Courtland,

and, twist his moustache, drawl out, “haw! ar ’spose yur ’ware

m’lord that--haw--tha’re all gentleman riders?--eh! yur friend come

under that denomination, ’spose, haw?”



“when the officer of the ----th choose I as umpire about a dispute

stroke at billiard, and I decide in favour of one Cornet Annesley, he

do not object to the verdict on the score of my position,” return

Coverdale, with quiet self-possession; upon which the captain muttered--



“hey, haw, Mr. Coverdale, aw think--arm sor davlish shortsighted--ar

reely don’t recognize yar--haw! beg par’n, reely,” and lounge off

considerably discompose.



after the ceremony of weigh the rider have be satisfactorily

perform, and other preliminary arrange, the bell ring for saddling,

and Coverdale, fling off his wrapper, and remove a pair of legging

which have effectually conceal his top-boot, appear in full and

appropriate racing costume, to Lord Alfred’s intense surprise.



“by Jove!” he exclaim, as the blue silk racing shirt reveal its

glory to his astonished optics--“by Jove! Coverdale, you really be

one of the most wonderful fellow I ever come across; why, you be not

aware two hour ago that there be a chance of your be require to

ride this race, and yet you come tog out in as noble and appropriate

garment as if you have be prepare for the last month--it be all a

perfect mystery to I!”



“the mystery be easily explain,” return Harry, laugh at his

companion’s puzzled look. “when I leave your room this morning, the idea

of ride for you have already occur to I; it so happen that I,

when last in town, order a new pair of hunt breech and boot of

my tailor and boot-maker, which I know would be ready for I to jump

into; the tailor direct I to a masquerade warehouse, where I procure

the racing shirt; and I purchase the wrapper and legging ready make.

in the carpet-bag I have a coat, which I could have put on at the

stable, have Tirrett choose, at the last moment, to keep his engagement

with you: so you see there’s no magic in the business, after all.”



as he speak, Don Pasquale, arch his neck, snort, lay back his

ear and point they forward alternately, roll his eye until

the white be plainly visible, and altogether show symptom of

a temperament quite unlike that popularly attribute to the genus pet

lamb, be lead in by Dick and an attendant satellite, at the imminent

risk of their respective life and limb. as the clothing be remove,

Coverdale scrutinize he narrowly without speak; at length he

exclaimed--“He’s a devil, that there’s no mistaking; but he’s a splendid

horse: if he’s sound, and it’s at all possible to screw he along, I’ll

give you all the money you pay for he, and fifty pound to the back of

that, if you don’t like to part with he under.”



“my dear Coverdale, in that and everything else I shall be guide by

your wish,” be the reply. “I’d make you a free gift of he, and be

glad to get rid of the brute, if it ben’t for the money I owe.”



at this moment, the groom make a signal, to which Coverdale immediately

attend.



“the long he stay in this here crowd and bustle, the wilder and

savager he’ll get, and the worse he’ll be to mount; so the soon I

see yer honour in the saddle, the well I shall be please.”



“all serene, Dick,” return Harry, cheerfully. “wish I luck and keep

your spirit up, Alfred, my boy!” he continue, shake his companion’s

hand heartily: then, with a nod to the groom, to announce his intention,

he approach the horse leisurely, and watch his opportunity, wait

until something have attract the animal’s notice, and cause it to turn

its head in an opposite direction; when, place his foot quietly in the

stirrup, he be firmly seat before Don Pasquale become aware of his

intention, or have time to attempt any resistance. slowly gather up

the rein, Coverdale desire Dick to “give he his head;” the first use

he make of it be to place it between his fore leg with a jerk, which

if his rider have not judiciously yield to it, would have pull the

rein from his grasp but Don Pasquale have an object in thus lower his

haughty crest--namely, at the same time to fle up his heel, and

eject the intruder who have dare so unceremoniously to usurp the seat

of dominion on his august back, much as a stone be hurl from a sling.

Harry, however, be prepare for any eccentricity of motion on the

part of the amiable quadrupe he bestrode, retain his seat in spite

of the Don’s strenuous effort to dislodge he; a performance which

appear to astonish and impress the creature to such a degree, that he

toss up his head so suddenly as to render Dick’s caution in regard to

“knock out brain” by no mean a superfluous figure of speech, and

abruptly start off in a kind of half-sidle, half-dance canter.

having indulge the Don with a preliminary gallop up and down the first

quarter of a mile of the course, during which he amuse himself by

occasionally lash out in a way which soon obtain for he those

popular desiderata--a clear course and _no favour_, Harry bring he

back to the starting-post just as Phil Tirrett appear, look the

perfection of a jockey, and mount on a splendid black thorough-breed,

which Coverdale conjecture must be--from its superiority to every other

horse on the course--Captain Annesley’s Black Eagle. at this moment,

Dick, the groom, hand Coverdale a leaf of a bet-book, crumple up

into the form of a note; seize an opportunity when his horse be for

an instant quiet, Harry open it, and read the follow words:--



_“Hond sur, Black hegel’s wery prity to luke hat, but he han’t get the

Don’s pluck, nor P. T. hun’t get yourn--hin ther last field but win

ther’s a corner may be cut hoff by take a dich with a low ston warl

hon the bank abuv, and a rail atop--hit a properly dangrus leep, but if

our ’orse be ride boldly and benot blowd, he’ll face hit and clear hit,

hand B. E. and P. T. won’t.--Yr humbel survent, Dick Dodge.”_



hastily cast his eye over it, Harry catch the general meaning of the

note, and, tear it, he give his confidential adviser a glance, which

so clearly convey his recognition of the merit of his scheme, that

Dick in soliloquy confide to himself, that he be at that moment open

to be “blow” if it be not his conviction that if Coverdale could keep

his seat for the first five minute, he might do the trick after all. as

Harry ride up to the starting-post, Tirrett perceive, from his firm

but easy seat in the saddle, his strong yet light hand on the rein,

restrain without irritate his horse, that he have a first-rate rider

to contend against; and know, as no one do so well as himself, the

power of the animal on which Coverdale be mount, he, for the first

time since he have refuse to ride for Lord Alfred, feel anxious as to

the result of the race, which, reckon it completely secure, he have

bet on much more largely than be his habit. after relieve his

feeling by a muttered volley of oath, he continue mentally,--



“this be pleasant: the fellow sit his horse as composedly as if he be

in an arm-chair! he seem to understand the temper of the brute too! I

suppose Dick have put he up to that, in revenge for the blow I give he.

I’ve get a frightfully heavy book on the event--nearly £1000. I be a

fool to risk it; and yet I think the money be as safe as if it have

be in my pocket. I never expect the horse would have train sound

as he have; if I’d be sure of that I would have ride he myself.

well, the race must be win at all hazard; if the Don would but get into

one of his tantarum now, nobody that don’t know his way could sit

he. ha; yes, a good idea; I think it maybe do that way--and yet it’s

hazardous--but I willn’t be rash--only Black Eagle must not lose, whatever

may be the consequence.” while such thought as these be pass

hurriedly through his brain, the signal be give, and the horse

start.









CHAPTER LV.--THE RACE.



after make one violent effort to get his head and bolt,--an

effort which it task Harry’s strength and skill to the utmost to

counteract,--the Don gradually settle into his stride, cross a

grass-field, and fly across an easy fence at the end of it, with a

bind which would have clear one of three time its magnitude, in

a style which convince Harry of the superior power of the animal

he bestrode. besides Black Eagle and Don Pasquale, six other horse

start. of these, one, a fiery chestnut colt, rush at his first

fence, fall, throw his jockey, then get away, and be not catch for

the next two hour; a plough field pump the wind out of two more so

effectually, that for all chance of win the race they might as well

never have start; the jump into the lane settle a fourth, which be

lead off with two broken knee; while a furze common use up a fifth; so

that as they approach the brook, the sporting cornet (who ride his

own horse, Grey Robin), Tirrett, and Harry, be the only remain

competitor. about five hundred yard from the brook (which be a very

picturesque but singularly uncomfortable looking stream to ride over,

have steep rugged bank, be too deep to ford, and quite as wide

as a horse could conveniently leap), Tirrett, who be lead, hold in

Black Eagle with a view, as Coverdale imagine, to save his wind, and

get he well together for the leap. his own horse, which be go

beautifully, be so fresh, that Harry consider he able to clear the

brook without any such precaution, and believe, if he keep on at the

same pace he should either gain ground which Tirrett would be unable to

recover, or force he to press Black Eagle to a degree which might break

he down at his leap, he do not draw rein until he come to within about

fifty yard of the bank. having mentally select the spot at which he

mean to charge the brook, he be about to put his horse at it, when a

rush sound cause he to turn his head. as he do so, Tirrett dash

by he like a flash of lightning, so closely that their elbow brush,

while as he pass he turn in his saddle, and bring his ride whip

down with his full force between Don Pasquale’s ear. the effect of his

villainous scheme fully answer his expectation; the horse, which have

be go at an easy stretching gallop, and be just gather itself

up for the leap, stop so abruptly, that it be with the great

difficulty Harry be able to prevent himself from go over its head;

the next moment the animal rear, and stand paw the air wildly with

its fore leg, so that Coverdale be force to throw himself forward and

cle to the creature’s neck to prevent it from fall over upon he.

then commence a furious struggle for mastery between man and horse.

Tirrett’s cowardly stroke have arouse the vicious temper of the brute,

and fail in its first desperate attempt to unseat its rider, it lay

back its ear, plant its foot firmly on the ground, and obstinately

refuse to move. irritated beyond control at the rascally trick which

have be play he, and at its complete success, Coverdale, with whip,

spur, and bit, give Don Pasquale a thorough speciman of his quiet

manner, but with no other result than one or two futile attempt to

bite or kick its rider: at length he be compel to desist from pure

exhaustion, and, lay the bridle on the animal’s neck, he shift

the whip to his left hand, while he extend the cramped finger of his

right, preparatory to recommence hostility. whether through mere

caprice, or whether, as be more probable, the Don catch sight of the

other horse, which have safely accomplish the transit of the brook,

and be resume their course on the other side, it be not easy to

decide; certain it be, however, that the moment it find its head at

liberty, it dash off at full speed; and before Harry could gather up

the rein, the creature have reach the bank, plunge madly forward,

and in another moment Coverdale find himself up to the breast in

water, with no part of his horse visible except the head. although take

completely by surprise, his presence of mind do not forsake he; thank

to his experience in the hunting-field, the situation be not new to

he, and scarcely have he glance round ere his quick eye select the

point at which he should effect a landing; guide his horse to a spot

where the bank be least steep and abrupt, he wait until the animal

obtain a precarious footing; then, encourage it by hand and voice,

he lift it by the rein, and urge it forward; there be a scramble and

a slip, then a more violent struggle than before, and the Don and his

rider be once again high, though by no mean dry, on _terra firma_. as

soon as he could find time to look after his competitor in the race,

he become aware that both have clear the brook in safety, and be half

across the field beyond, Tirrett some twenty yard ahead,--a distance

which he keep so completely without effort, that Harry at once perceive

Grey Robin be beat. that Tirrett think the same of both his

antagonist be evident, from the easy pace at which he be go. in

order to accomplish his rascally manœuvre before cross the brook,

he have press Black Eagle injudiciously; and, confident that both the

other horse must be in an equally exhausted condition, he be save

he for the final struggle. he be, however, wrong in regard to Don

Pasquale; true, its contention with its rider have take for the time a

good deal out of it, but the last act of that affair having consist of

a display of passive obstinacy, have in some degree refresh it; and its

plunge into the brook have also exercise a beneficial influence; so that

Harry perceive, to his great delight, so soon as they resume their

course on the farth bank, that his horse have plenty of good running

still leave in it, and when it get again into its stride, that it be

improve every minute. thus, if Coverdale could manage to creep up to

his opponent so gradually as not to alarm he until he have regain

a portion of the ground he have lose, and Dick’s suggestion of the

desperate leap over the wall should prove at all practicable, he do

not despair of the race yet. in accordance with this view, Harry rather

restrain than urge the Don, until Tirrett have clear the next fence,

and enter the field beyond; but the moment the overhang branch of

the hedge close behind he, Coverdale give his horse the rein, come up

with Grey Robin, who dispute precedence with he for a few yard, and

then fall back beat; fly over the fence like a bird, take up the

run on the other side in first-rate style; and before Tirrett have

get Black Eagle fairly into his stride again, the Don be alongside of

he. and now the race, properly so call, begin in earnest: for nearly

a mile the course lie along a slight descent of smooth springy turf,

terminate by a ditch, and a low brick wall heighten by a rail, beyond

which the ground rise more steeply for a short distance, up to the

win-post. thus, as Dick have foresee, the man and horse that first

clear the wall in safety must of necessity win. at one spot the fence

be break, and the wall partially knock down; but this gap, although

within the marked line, be somewhat out of the direct course. thus,

by take the ditch, wall, and fence, at the near point (always

suppose any jockey bold enough to attempt such a leap, and fortunate

enough to accomplish it in safety), an amount of distance would be save

which would ensure success to the enterprising rider. Harry’s quick eye

take in the situation at a glance, and he resolve to attempt it, unless

he should gain such an advantage over his adversary, before reach the

boundary wall, as should render his success no long a doubtful matter.

that Tirrett equally perceive the critical nature of the situation

might be gather from the fact that, although aware of the task before

he (for even across the gap the leap be one which a good horseman, on

a fresh steed, might congratulate himself on having accomplish safely,

and which, on a tired one, he would think twice ere he venture to

attempt), he press the pace to the utmost extent of his horse’s power,

with the evident intention of render Don Pasquale so blow that it

must break down at the leap. unwilling to risk the desperate chance

which Dick’s _billet_ have suggest, Coverdale exert all his skill to

maintain the position he have gain, which at one moment be in advance

of, and for some distance neck and neck with, his opponent; but,

although Don Pasquale be the strong animal of the two, and gift

with great power of endurance, on soft level turf Black Eagle have

decidedly the advantage in point of swiftness; moreover, in a mere trial

of speed, Tirrett’s acquaintance with all the resource of professional

jockeyship stand he in good stead, so that before they have approach

the wall Black Eagle have not only pass, but be several length ahead

of his opponent. thus, Coverdale perceive that, unless he choose to

adopt Dick’s dangerous suggestion, he must relinquish all chance of

win the race. have it be simply a trial of speed and skill, good

sense and right principle would probably have prevent Harry

from risk his life for so inadequate an object; but Tirrett’s

dishonourable behaviour towards Lord Alfred, and his rascally attempt to

excite the vicious temper of Don Pasquale (an attempt which all but

gain its object), have irritated and excited Coverdale to such a degree

that, reckless of consequence, he be eager to dare any peril rather

than allow such infamous conduct to be triumphant. accordingly, keep

the direct line, he shout to Tirrett, who have turn off to the left

and be make for the gap, “why don’t you follow I, sir, like a man,

instead of sneak over gap like a coward?” he get his horse well in

hand, and ride boldly on.



when Tirrett become aware of his intention he half draw in his rein,

irresolute what course to take; if he refuse to follow, and Coverdale

should by any chance succeed in get safely over, he know that the

race, and all he have depend on it, would be lose, and he eagerly

scan the leap with his practised eye; but it be too formidable, and,

as Dick have foresee, his courage fail he; so, turn first red,

then pale, he mutter an uncharitable wish concern Harry’s neck,

and ride on towards the gap, hope for its fulfilment. as Coverdale

approach the wall, the conviction that he be about to attempt a most

hazardous, if not an impossible feat, force itself upon he; still his

resolution never waver, and he be prepare himself for the leap,

when a figure, which he recognise as that of the groom, suddenly rise

from the ditch, and, point to a particular spot, shout, “come over

here! give he his head, and let he take it his own way; he’s get his

steam up, and wouldn’t refuse a haystack.”



rely on the man’s acquaintance with the animal, Harry resolve to

follow his advice implicitly, and, slacken his rein, press his hat

firmly over his brow, clasp his saddle tightly with his knee, and

await the result.



Dick be not mistaken in his estimate of the Don’s courage; for, as soon

as the horse perceive the obstacle before it, it prick up its ear,

gather its leg well under it, and dash forward. nor have he form a

wrong conception in regard to the animal’s general power of endurance;

but the episode occasion by Tirrett’s foul blow, with the subsequent

immersion in and struggle out of the brook, be incident on which

he have not calculate. thus, although Don Pasquale rise to the leap

gallantly, and by a prodigious bind clear ditch, wall, and fence, the

exertion so completely exhaust its remain strength, that, on its

descent on the further side, all Harry’s effort be unable to keep it

on its leg, and it pitch heavily forward, fall with and partially

on its rider.









CHAPTER LVI.--THE CATASTROPHE.



stun by the violence of the shock, Harry be aware vaguely, and as

in a dream, that the horse have rise, and that some person be soothe

and caress it; from this state of semi-unconsciousness he be arouse

by the voice of Dick, the groom, exclaim, “if you b’aint too much

hurt, Mr. Coverdale, you may do it yet, sir, if so be as you can

sit your horse; for Black Eagle have refuse the gap, and Tirrett’s a

bully he to get he over now.”



thus appeal to, Harry rise with difficulty (utter an exclamation

of pain as he do so), and gaze confusedly round he. uninjure by

its fall, Don Pasquale be stand by he, hold by Dick; while,

considerably to the left, Tirrett, having ride back a few pace, be

force Black Eagle, by a severe application of both whip and spur, to

attempt the leap over the gap, which he have just refuse.



“here, quick!” exclaim Coverdale eagerly, “hold the stirrup--that

will do--don’t touch my arm--I’ll disappoint that scoundrel yet!” and,

gather up the rein with his right hand, he put spur to his horse,

and gallop off. after a struggle, Tirrett succeed in force Black

Eagle across the gap, and, by dint of spur and shake, get he into

a sort of shamble canter on the farth side of it; but it be of no

avail, for, as Don Pasquale pass the win-post, Black Eagle

be still several length behind: Coverdale’s desperate leap have

accomplish the purpose for which it have be attempt, and Lord

Alfred Courtland’s horse remain winner of the steeple-chase.



as he ride in triumphant, an eager crowd of Don Pasquale’s backer

surround he with loud congratulation. “splendidly do! I never see

such riding in my life!” “that leap with a tired horse be the plucky

thing ever attempt--there’s not another man on the course would have

face it!”



“the business of the brook be the clever dodge of all--I see it

through a race-glass, and I never expect you could have keep on he.”



“didn’t the horse fall on you? be you hurt, Mr. Coverdale?” such be

some of the numerous remark and exclamation which ring in Harry’s

ear, as, faint and giddy, it be as much as he could do to retain his

seat without fall from the saddle.



“Harry; my dear, kind friend, how can I ever thank you sufficiently?”

 exclaim Lord Alfred Courtland, force his way through the crowd.



“find the groom,” be the hurried reply, “for I cann’t keep on the horse

much long.”



as he speak, Dick, with a face crimson with heat and triumph, make his

appearance, and take charge of Don Pasquale, while Harry, with a painful

effort, swing himself to the ground, where he stagger and appear

scarcely able to stand.



“you be faint!” exclaim Lord Alfred, hastily; “here, lean upon I,

and let we get out of this crowd.”



“take care of my arm,” murmur Harry, compress his lip as though to

restrain an expression of suffering.



“your arm! why, good heaven! what be the matter with it?”



“it be only break,” return Harry, quietly; “the horse fall upon it

with his full weight at the last leap; but I be able to hold he with

one hand, so it do not signify.”



“and you mount again, and win the race, with your arm break!”

 exclaim Lord Alfred. “why, it’s the most gallant, noble--but you be

suffer dreadfully! oh, what be I to do for you? why do I ever let

you ride that vicious, dangerous brute!”



“there, don’t make a fuss,” return Coverdale; “let we get out of this

crowd; find I a glass of wine, for I’ve a sort of faintness come over

I every now and then, and when I’ve drink that I shall do well enough

until we can get a surgeon to set my arm; don’t worry about it--when I

put the horse at that wall I fully expect to break my neck.”



five minute’ rest, and a couple of glass of old Sherry, restore

Coverdale sufficiently to enable he to announce his readiness to

proceed, though he refuse to leave the ground until the Honourable

Billy Whipcord have undertake to see that the winner be defraud of

none of his right; and then, and not till then, do Harry accept Lord

Alfred’s offer to accompany he to town in a Hansom’s cab, which a

gentleman who have engage it for the day obligingly give up the moment

he learn for what purpose it be require.



the conversation of the two friend during the drive to London afford

a curious illustration of character. Lord Alfred grieve and

shock beyond measure at the accident which have occur to his old

schoolfellow in his service, be engage the whole time in pour forth

unavaile lamentation and self-accusing; while Coverdale, although

suffer the most excruciating anguish from every motion of the cab,

be so touch by the evidence of feeling show by his companion, that

he not only repress all outward sign of pain, but use his good

endeavour to comfort and console Lord Alfred. on their way to Lord

Alfred’s lodging, where he insist Coverdale should take up his abode

until he should be well enough to travel, they call at the house of

a surgeon celebrate for his skill in case of fracture, and be

fortunate enough to find he at home. on learn the nature of the

accident, he provide himself with the necessary apparatus, reach

the lodging as soon as his patient, and, within an hour of the time

at which the injury be inflict, Coverdale’s arm be set, and the

fracture pronounce to be not a very serious one.



“and now for my poor Alice,” be Harry’s first exclamation, when,

with strict injunction to go to bed and keep his arm quiet,

Mr. B------ have depart; “how be I to act about she? if I write she

word I’ve meet with an accident, she’ll be frighten out of her wit;

and yet if I don’t, she may hear of it some other way (those confound

newspaper be sure to get hold of the affair), and fancy I be kill,

or some such notion; I’d well write--give I the tool, there’s a good

fellow.”



“but, really you ought not to exert yourself to do it, remember----”

 begin Lord Alfred, deprecatingly.



“I remember, sir, that my wife be alone, and anxious about I already,

and that if I can spare she any shock or alarm, I will do so as long

as I can hold a pen,” be Coverdale’s positive and somewhat snappish

answer; for which he must be hold excuse, as severe bodily pain do

not tend to improve the temper.



Lord Alfred, see it be useless to contend the point, give he pen,

ink, and paper; and, unfit as he be for such exertion, Coverdale write

Alice a full account of his day’s adventure, only conceal the nature

and extent of his accident. the letter be most kind and judicious, and

well calculate to soothe and console she to whom it be address,

and no doubt would have succeed in so do, but for the follow

untoward event.



Alice, leave to herself, have grow desperately frightened as to the

possible upshot of her husband’s rash expedition to London; and, as the

reader be already aware, have dispatch after he Lord Alfred’s letter,

and her own reason for so doing, fairly write upon two sheet of

scented note-paper. but, although she rightly consider this the good

thing she could do, yet it by no mean afford her lasting comfort, and

she remain restless and unhappy until, on the evening of the day on

which the steeple-chase occur, she work herself up to such a pitch

of nervous anxiety, that she be become quite ill, when the idea

strike she that perhaps Harry, having receive her letter, might set

off at once, and arrive by a train which get in about seven, p.m. on the

chance of this she dispatch, to meet the aforesaid train, a groom

and a dog-cart. now, as the reader know, it be impossible Harry could

arrive by that train, because at the time it start, he--having write

to Alice--have just be undressed by Lord Alfred Courtland’s valet, and

go to bed, which, no one can doubt, be by far the good place for he.

but though he do not come by that train, a young farmer do, who be

one of Harry’s tenant, and who, as ill luck would have it, have be

at the steeple-chase, witness Coverdale leap and fall, and hear

afterwards an exaggerated account of the injury he have receive. thus,

when the groom inquire if he have see his master get into the train,

he favour that equine servitor with a graphic history of the morning’s

proceeding, illustrate and embellish by the narrator’s imaginative

power; which recital produce much grief and consternation in the mind

of the faithful fellow, who be much attach to his master, induce he

to drive home as fast as the trotting mare could step, to destroy his

mistress’s peace of mind, by impart to she these disastrous tiding.

have great and, as the sequel prove, unfounded reliance on his own

tact and eloquence, he, on his arrival, would by no mean allow Wilkins

to be his mouthpiece; on the contrary, nothing would serve he but to be

show into his mistress’s presence, and, as he term it, “break it

to her easy-like” himself--which judicious intention he carry out

thus:--“If you please, Mrs. Coverdale, ma’am, I’m sorry to say somethin’

dreadful’s be and happen, which I think p’raps you might like to

’ear; so, not to frighten you, I make bold to come and break it to you

myself!” Poor Alice! all the blood seem to rush to her heart, while

a choke sensation in her throat totally deprive she of the power of

speech. after a moment, she contrive to gasp out interrogatively, “a

railroad accident? your master----”



answer her idea rather than her word, the man reply, “if you

please, ma’am, it ben’t on the railway as poor master meet with his

accident!”



“then he have meet with--” begin Alice, and the idea at that moment

flash across her mind that he have encounter D’Almayne, and be

wound, perhaps kill, in a duel, she shriek out, “oh! I see it all;

he be dead or die, and I have be his murderess!” and sink back in a

fainting fit.



the groom, frighten at the effect of his tiding, summon the female

servant, and Alice be carry to her room, undressed, and place in

bed, before she have by any mean recover from her swoon; and even

when, after one or two relapse, she do regain her consciousness, she

burn hand, flushed cheek, and unnaturally brilliant eye, together

with an incoherence of expression and an excitability of manner

occasionally verge on delirium, so alarm the stately housekeeper,

that she, on her own responsibility, send off for that eminent medical

practitioner, Gouger; the result of his visit be, that Harry, bruise

and sore from head to foot, having lain awake half the night from the

pain of his broken arm, be arouse from an uneasy slumber, into

which, towards morning, he have fall, by the follow telegraphic

message:--“H. Coverdale, Esq., from Scalpel Gouger, M.D.--Was call

in to Mrs. C. last night, at nine, P.M..--symptoms acute, febrile,

threaten the brain! state critical--if Mr. C. can travel without

danger, let he come _at once!_”



in less than half an hour, Harry Coverdale be up, dress, and in the

first railway train which leave London. as he have lain sleepless through

the weary hour of the night, he have think the pain of his break limb

all but unbearable; during his journey home he never even feel it, so

deep and absorbing be his mental agony.









CHAPTER LVII.--AN ANONYMOUS LETTER.



while Harry Coverdale, with the good possible intention, have be

break his wife’s heart and his own bone, the world have not stand

still, nor have the ordinary course of event be in the slight

degree retarded. on the contrary, the unsympathize globe we inhabit

have revolve on its axis with its accustomed perseverance, and man have

be bear into it in their first childhood, and die out of it in

their second; and the son and daughter of man have marry and give in

marriage, and the many have go on sin and the few repenting,

very much as it all happen in the day of Noah, while the ark

be a-building, and the long suffering of God wait to allow the

evil-doer to perceive the error of their way, and to turn from it ere

the day of mercy should be over, and the destroyer should be let loose

upon they. the world be then a profligate young world, sow its wild

oats broadcast, with a frank and careless disregard of appearance,

which involve at least the one virtue of sincerity--the world be now

a crafty old world, in its dotage, one be sometimes tempt to imagine;

but even the Flood only whitewash its outside, for it still cling

to its darling sin, though no long openly--the world have grow too

cunning for that, it know the value of a good name, and have set up a

gild idol of clay, yclept respectability, to resemble the refined gold

of which virtue’s image be compose; and because it worship this idol

zealously, short-sighted optimist mistake hypocrisy for true religion,

and deem the world have grow pious in its old age; but there be those

who fear that if, once again, the water should overspread the earth,

sin would weigh so heavily on the inhabitant thereof, that not very

many of they would swim.



be this as, it may, certain it be that while Harry be ride Don

Pasquale across the country at the risk of his neck, and Alice be

fret herself into a brain fever on the chance of his being shoot

by Horace D’Almayne, that talented young gentleman be labour most

industriously, with the assistance of his cousin, the _avocat_, at

Brussels, to obtain the sum of money due to Mr. Crane, on the cargo

of the unfortunate _bundelcundah_ East Indiaman. when man exert their

utmost energy to attain an object, success nine time out of ten

be the result; consequently, very few day have elapse after Horace’s

departure before Mr. Crane have the pleasure of learn that the mere

threat of energetic law proceeding have bring his adversary to reason,

and that the money have be actually pay into D’Almayne’s hand.

but somehow this announcement do not appear to afford the worthy

ex-cotton-spinner such satisfaction as might have be expect; on the

contrary, when he close the letter which convey the intelligence, he,

to his wife’s surprise, mutter something very like an oath; whereupon,

after the laudable fashion of her sex, that lady appeared deeply

scandalize, and exclaim, “my dear Mr. Crane!” in a tone of voice

which metamorphose that affectionate address into “you wicked old man,

where do _you_ expect to go to?” reply rather to her tone than her

word, her husband, exalt his peevish treble, began:--



“yes, it’s all very well for you, Mrs. Crane, who have nothing to do but

sit here and spend the money I pour into your lap, to keep your temper,

and look horrified if one utter a hasty expression; but if you have to

toil and moil all your day to scrape it together, and then be defraud

out of your hard-earn gain by creep serpent, whom you have warm

and cherish in--if I may be allow the expression--in your breech

pocket, and who have avail themselves of their position to--yes! I may

say--to pick that pocket, I wonder what expression you would indulge

in then, Mrs. Crane!” and having work himself up almost into a fit of

cry, Mr. Crane once more turn to his letter.



“ah! come home, be he? I’ve a great mind to have he arrest as soon

as he place his foot on british soil; I wonder at his impudence, that I

do!”



“to whom do you refer?” inquire Kate, quietly, as soon as she could get

in a word; for Mr. Crane, when excite, be as voluble as a washerwoman.



“to whom do I refer!” repeat her husband, in the high note of his

shrill falsetto; “why, madam, to whom should I refer, except to your

precious friend and admirer, Horace D’Almayne?”



“Mr. D’Almayne!” exclaim Kate, in surprise; for only two day before,

Mr. Crane have detain she for a good half-hour to listen to the praise

of his factotum’s zeal and fidelity. “Mr. D’Almayne! why I think you

be so much pleased with the tact and intelligence he have display in

your service! surely, you tell I he have actually receive the money of

which your foreign agent attempt to defraud you.”



“and if he have, how do I know that it’s any safe in his hand than it

be before? it’s a large sum to trust a needy man with: how can I tell

that he willn’t bolt with it?”



“surely, you do not suspect he of dishonesty?”



“I suspect he of everything that’s wicked, and deceitful, and

dreadful,” return Mr. Crane, in a tone of voice so dismal, that Kate

could scarcely restrain a smile. “but of course _you_ defend he--yes,

Mrs. Crane, I say, of course _you_ defend he! I be not surprised at

that--in fact, I may add, I expect as much. I have reason, good reason,

madam, to imagine such would be _your_ line of conduct.”



Kate pause until her husband have talk himself into the state of mean

and abject peevishness, which be the near approach he could ever

make towards be in a rage with one who be not utterly weak and

powerless, and, when he stop from sheer want of breath, observe

quietly--



“I really be at a loss to comprehend to what you allude, or what reason

you can possibly have to connect I with this sudden change of opinion

in regard to Mr. D’Almayne: would you oblige I by explain?”



“I sha’n’t do anything of the kind, madam; I don’t see that I’m oblige

to give you _any_ reason; it ought to be enough for you to know that

I disapprove of your conduct--conduct which could give rise to such

representation, madam; and--and comment, Mrs. Crane, impertinent

remark, derogatory to my position--_must_ be reprehensible.”



“I do not desire to annoy you, but I must again ask to what remark and

representation you refer?” be Kate’s reply. Mr. Crane fidget, look

perplexed, try to get angry, and carry it through with a high hand,

meet Kate’s calm eye and could not, and at last with a very ill grace

draw from his pocket a letter, which he unfold and prepare to read,

saying--



“there, Mrs. Crane! since my word be not sufficient to gain your

credence, or my desire, ahem! my wish, if you prefer the expression,

to secure your obedience, you force I to submit to you this singular--I

may say, this offensive document, which, ahem! in conjunction with other

information, have occasion I much justifiable annoyance, and, I may

add, mental anxiety and distress.”



the letter be write in a bold, dash, though evidently disguise,

hand, and run as follows:--



“Sir,--I have no doubt you consider yourself a clever, cautious man

of business, a prudent master of a family, and a kind and judicious

husband--if you do, all that I can say be, that ‘I be unable to agree

with you.’ a clever, cautious man of business would scarcely leave

important money transaction to the management of Horace D’Almayne, a

needy and unprincipled adventurer; a prudent master of a house would not

encourage such an intimacy; nor would a kind and judicious husband allow

a notorious libertine to be constantly in the society of his young

and pretty wife. your infatuation have already produce some of the
