unpleasant result naturally to be expect from it; you have advance

above £5000 on a bubble company, not one farthing of which you will ever

see again, whilst you have incur liability, to learn the extent of

which you have well consult your man of business, and I wish you joy

of the revelation I expect you will obtain from he. in regard to your

young wife, I have no positive information to afford you; but that

D’Almayne have design upon she, I know,--and he be not a man to fail in

an adventure of that description, even without take into consideration

the circumstance of a beautiful young woman be married to a man of

your year. you may wonder why I trouble myself to write thus to you; so

I will tell you: I owe D’Almayne a grudge, and it suit I to take this

opportunity of discharge the debt. but though this be my object, all I

have tell you be only the plain truth; I suspect it come too late to be

of much use to you; but that be your look-out, not mine.”



the letter be without signature.



Kate listen attentively while Mr. Crane read aloud, with much

hesitation and stammering, such portion of the alarming epistle

as concern his property and his wife, carefully suppress every

sentence which relate to his own weakness and gullibility. when he have

conclude, she remark, “the letter be a singular one, and appear to

I to bear a certain impress of truth; if I be you, I would attend to

the hint in regard to your pecuniary investment.”



“and as to those which affect my wife, what would you advise in regard

to they, madam?” inquire Mr. Crane, screw up his face into an

expression of feeble sarcasm, which give he very much the appearance

of an ancient monkey. Kate pause: here be an opportunity which might

never occur again of enlighten her husband as to her experience of

Horace D’Almayne’s true character. she have every reason to do so; his

threat of reveal the clandestine visit she be prepared to forestall,

if necessary, by an honest confession of the entire affair, prefer

to bear with her husband’s fretful displeasure (of which, if the truth

must be tell, she do not stand very greatly in awe), rather than to

excite his suspicion by a concealment which would lend countenance to

the insinuation of this anonymous correspondent--yes! she have every

reason to tell all she know concern he, even to his late avowal of

affection, and yet she feel she could not do it. in the first place she

shrink, as any pure-minded woman would shrink, from confess that

such an avowal have be make to she; but especially do she shrink from

confess it to such a nature as that of Mr. Crane: he would never see

the matter in its true light--never believe that she have not, in some

measure, encourage such advance--never comprehend the disgust

and loathing with which they have inspire she. but another and more

stringent reason withhold she--her brother Frederick! she still believe

that D’Almayne have befriend he, and save he from, at all event,

the immediate consequence of the dilemma into which his youth and

inexperience have plunge he: true, she mistrust his object in

perform this act of benevolence--or, rather, she feel convinced that

he have do it merely to establish a claim on her gratitude;--still

the fact remain the same--in her difficulty, when all other human aid

appear to have forsake she, he have come to her assistance, and by

do so have save her brother: believe this, could she expose his

baseness? the question be a difficult one.









CHAPTER LVIII.--DIAMOND CUT DIAMOND.



those who be skilled to read that strange, yet easily to be penetrate

mystery, a woman’s heart, will have at once decide how Kate Crane

determine to act in regard to D’Almayne--he have save her brother, and

though he have offer she an unpardonable insult, she would not betray

he, so she reply calmly--“I should on that point advise you as I do

on the former one: reflect whether the accusation be likely to be

true; whether you have observe any encouragement give by I to Mr.

D’Almayne; whether, from what you know of my character, you imagine

it likely that I should be so devoid of principle, so want in

self-respect, as to accept Mr. D’Almayne’s or any other man’s attention.

recollect a speech I once make you, which really appear as if I have

have a presentiment of this accusation--a speech in which I beg you

to bear in mind that, if at any time comment should be make on the

intimate footing on which Mr. D’Almayne visit at this house, it be

accord to your express wish and desire that he do so, and on that

account only do I tolerate it. if, when you have thus consider the

matter, you still feel dissatisfied, I advise you to use every endeavour

to arrive at the truth. my own opinion be, that the letter be

write by (as the writer honestly enough confesse) an enemy of Mr.

D’Almayne’s, he have rake up every accusation which scandal may have

invent to blacken that gentleman’s character; still, as, if there

be any truth in the charge, the knowledge of it would prove of great

importance to you, it behove you quietly and carefully to inquire into

they, and I would recommend you to do so without delay.”



Kate’s perfect self-possession and coolness always produce great effect

on Mr. Crane, and in the present instance they so thoroughly convince

he that his anonymous correspondent have accuse his wife falsely, that

without more ado he start for the city to investigate the truth of

the other charge, leave his well-half to strive against the

uncomfortable conviction that unintentionally she have play the part of

a hypocrite.



one of the element of Horace D’Almayne’s success in life be his

punctuality in all matter of business: if he say he would do a thing,

he do it; if he promise to be at any place by a fix time, at the

appoint day and hour there be Horace to be find: this consistency

even in apparent trifle cause other to place great reliance on he,

and contribute to establish a certain degree of _prestige_ and weight

of character which often stand he in good stead. no one be well

aware of this fact than Horace himself; who, perceive the value of the

practice, have adopt it as one of his guide principle, to which

he invariably act up with a consistency worthy of a well code.

accordingly, having transact Mr. Crane’s business to his own

satisfaction, he appoint a day on which to return to England, and

when the time arrive, embark; but, unable finally to conclude the

transaction without proceed to Liverpool, he select a vessel bind

for that port. on his arrival, after a favourable passage, he take up

his abode at a small, quiet hotel, much frequent by foreigner. having

engage a private room, he be look over the paper which he have

bring with he, when his quick ear catch the sound of a voice with

the tone of which he fancy himself familiar--listen attentively,

he overhear the follow colloquy:--



“can I have a private sitting-room here?”



“well, sir, we’re very full; should you require a bedroom also?”



“no; I be go by the New York packet, which leave at eight o’clock

this evening.”



“if you’ll wait one moment, sir, I’ll see; but I’m a’most afraid we’re

full.”



anxious to obtain a view of the speaker, D’Almayne cross the room

with noiseless tread, and look out through the half-open door; the

figure near to he be that of the waiter at the hotel; the person

with whom he have be converse be, or appear to be, a seafaring

man of the more respectable class, and at the first glance D’Almayne

believe he to be an entire stranger--still, the voice, so peculiar

and so well know, he surely could not be mistake in that! and again

he scrutinise the stranger’s appearance. he be a tall thin man, well

advanced in life, with sharp acute feature, and keen grey eye; his

hair be cut short, and of an unnaturally raven blackness; and his face

be closely shaven, without the slight trace of whisker or moustache.

for a moment, Horace D’Almayne pause in doubt, during which interval

the stranger’s evil genius oblige he to cough, a dry husky cough

which, once hear, be not easily mistake--it be enough. in go

to seek the master of the hotel, the waiter have to pass the door of

D’Almayne’s room; a sign from that individual’s finger cause he to

enter it.



“show that gentleman into this room, as if it be the untenanted

apartment he have inquire for--leave the key in the lock inside, and if

I ring the bell twice fetch a policeman instantly; but as I hope such an

extreme measure may not be necessary, do not say a word about the affair

to any one.” as he speak, he slip a sovereign into the man’s hand,

add, “manage this cleverly and quietly, and a second await you.”



the waiter bow, and with a nod of intelligence quit the room. the

door of the apartment be so place that when open it shut in an angle

of the wall, in which stand a screen quite large enough to conceal the

figure of a man; in this corner do D’Almayne ensconce himself; scarcely

have he do so ere the waiter return, usher in the stranger for

whose benefit these arrangement have be make. perfectly unsuspicious

of any stratagem, the new comer signify his approval of the

accommodation provide for he, place a leathern valise which he

carry in his hand on the table, and then seat himself by the window

with his back towards the door, which the waiter immediately close,

at the same time leave the room, when with noiseless step D’Almayne

glide from his place of concealment, and double-lock the door place

the key in his pocket. the slight sound make by the bolt shooting

into its socket attract the stranger’s attention, and turn round

quickly, he give a most perceptible start as his eye fall upon his

companion; recover himself instantly, he rise, and bow to

D’Almayne, said--



“the waiter must have make some mistake! I ask for an unoccupied room.

I must apologise for thus intrude on you, sir; but the mistake be not

on my part.” as he speak, he take up his valise preparatory to leave

the room, but D’Almayne motion he to a chair, as he replied--



“there be no mistake in the case, my friend, unless it be your fancy

that, because you have shave off your whisker and dye your hair, I

should not recognise you--that be a complete mistake.”



the person thus address turn pale and bite his lip; but, make an

effort to recover himself, replied--



“I do not understand you, sir; you be labour under some delusion;

allow I to pass directly, or I shall ring and summon the waiter.”



“you’d well not,” return D’Almayne, drily, “for that be the signal

agree on--for he instantly to fetch a policeman.”



the stranger glance towards the door, on which D’Almayne quietly

produce the key, and, when it have catch his eye replace it in his

pocket; he then stretch his hand, with a hesitating and uncertain

action, towards a stout stick on which he carry his valise; but

D’Almayne draw from the breast pocket of his surtout the beautifully

finish little revolving pistol which he always carry, and, having

somewhat ostentatiously display it before the eye of the individual

he be thus brow-beating, return it to its place of concealment, as

the other with a sullen dog look replace his stick, and murmured--



“well, Mr. D’Almayne, suppose you do happen to recognize I indulge

in a little freak--supposing I have disguise myself the well to carry

out a little intrigue of my own, why should that so greatly surprise

you? I do not think you have ever find I absent from my post when

business require I; you must be aware I have the interest of the

establishment as much at heart as any of the party connect with it;

when they begin to play to-night in J-------- Street, my frolic will be

over, and I shall be in my proper place.”



“I think it’s highly probable you will, always suppose that place to

be a cell in Pentonville prison, or, as you lodge in Westminster, the

Penitentiary, perhaps; but it strike I, that if I have not fortunately

meet you, you would at that hour have be toss about in St. George’s

Channel--as I happen to know you have take your passage in a New York

packet, which be to sail at eight this evening.” as D’Almayne speak, he

fix his pierce eye on the individual he address, who, unable to

bear his scrutinize glance, turn away mutter with an oath,

“-------- he, I think he be safe in Holland.” after a moment’s

reflection, he appear to decide on the course good for he to

follow--under what be evidently a contingency equally unforeseen and

unsatisfactory.



“assuredly there never be any one like you, Mr. D’Almayne, for

shrewdness and penetration,” he say, in a tone of apparent frankness;

“here be I (suppose by all who take an interest in my whereabouts to be

in London), in a disguise in which my own mother (the poor soul have be

dead these twenty year) would not have recognize I; at the first

glance you penetrate it, and by intuition appear to have discover my

intention! how you have track I, or whether you have meet I by

accident, I be unable to divine; but, as you _have_ discover I, I

think it be good to be frank with you, and to throw myself on your

generosity--confident that you will deal leniently with your old

associate, if I may venture to use the term, though, perhaps, your

faithful follower would be more true; for I be well aware how such

talent as yours raise you above we plod poor fellow. but I will

make a clean breast to you, sir. the fact be, I be no long young;

scarcely still middle-aged; and the life I have be for so many year

engage in be a hazardous and exhausting one. I have be a frugal and

careful man, and I do not scruple to tell you, sir, that I have

contrive to save a few hundred pound. well, sir, I have for some time

wish to leave England, and settle in America, where I be unknown, and

might begin the world afresh--in some quieter and more respectable line

of life; so I think I would avoid all the difficulty and all the

trouble which, none be well aware than you, sir, would attend my

quit London just at this time, by take french leave, and set

off in disguise and under a feigned name, hope that in Mr. Maxwell,

the traveller for a Manchester cotton firm, no one would recognize Le

Roux, the croupier; and now, sir, having tell you all, I throw myself on

your generosity not to attempt (though I see no pretext on which you

could legally do it) to detain I.”



while Le Roux have be make this statement, which he do with the air

of a man convince against his will that the only course leave open to

he be to declare the whole truth, come what may of it, D’Almayne have

take a pencil from his pocket, with which he have be write certain

calculation on the back of a card. as soon as the other have conclude,

he observe quietly--“I have be make a rough estimate of all the

available cash on which you could lay your hand, and it appear to I,

that, owe to my folly in rest content with the belief that it be

your interest to be honest, you have at least £15,000 in that leathern

case of your--a sum quite sufficient to tempt you to bolt, especially

at a time when you fancy I be safely out of your way. I make it out

thus the establishment in J-------- Street have never less than £5000

ready to pay all demand; to that, of course, you have unlimited access,

and have avail yourself of it. then come the Overland Route Railroad

speculation; Guillemard write I word that the share be go off

tolerably fast, and that something like £10,000 in hard cash have be

pay into our banker; a cheque sign by two of the director would

enable you to draw out the whole amount at any moment--your own

signature as Herr Vondenthaler, the belgian capitalist, provide for

one, and the other would offer little difficulty to a man of your talent

and experience. I have so strong a conviction that, in consequence of my

absence, you will have do I the honour to select my name, that it be

upon a charge of forgery I intend to have you apprehend, and to take

you up to London in my company and that of a policeman.”



during this speech the vary expression on Le Roux’s face would have

form an interesting study to the physiognomist or the artist--at

first, assume indifference, change to surprise, anxiety, and

ill-conceal alarm--then astonishment and fear, merge in a state of

bewildered terror which again give place to an astute subtle look, as

an idea occur to he which might yet interpose to save he from the

utter ruin to which the supernatural discovery, as it appear to he,

of his intended and partially execute villainy expose he. as soon

as D’Almayne have end, Le Roux turn to he, and say in a low calm

tone--



“you be, without any exception, Mr. D’Almayne, the clever man, for

your year, that I have ever meet with in our profession. I don’t say

it to flatter you, sir; but I say it because it be my deliberate

conviction. one of your strong point be your clear good sense, and it

be to that I be now about to appeal. you have, how I cannot divine, get

I completely in your power, and, know or suspect all you say you

do, it be useless for I to attempt to deceive you; it be clear you

can ruin I if you choose; but how will it advantage you to do so? or,

rather, how can you expose I without excite a host of unpleasant

inquiry about yourself? I presume you scarcely wish your connection

with the gaming-house in J-------- Street publish to the world at

large, nor would you like too much reveal concern the private

history of the director and general management of the railway company,

and yet I don’t see how you could place I in the hand of justice

without my enlighten the public on some of these point. as I be sure

you be aware of the force of these remark, I need say no more; but I

put it to you, as a sensible man of the world, will it not be well

for I to pay you that £1000, which, I dare say, you can remember, I be

indebted to you, for ‘value receive,’ we’ll say, and for you to forget

that you happen to meet I here to-day?” as he speak, he fix his

sharp cunning glance upon D’Almayne, as though he would fain read his

inmost thought; but even to such an old hand as Le Roux the gambler,

Horace’s expression be a seal book. but he be not long in doubt

as to the effect of his appeal; for in his usual tone of calm sarcasm,

Horace replied--



“cleverly put, Monsieur Le Roux; but there be two important flaw in

your argument. in the first place, your offer prove the truth of my

suspicion, only that, as you be not usually famous for the liberality

of your disposition, its amount satisfy I that I have rather under

than overrate the sum of which you have contrive to gain possession.

as to any accusation you can bring against I, I care little or nothing

for they; they may be true, but you have damage your own character so

deeply that no one will believe you. you may _assert_ that I be part

proprietor of the gambling-house, and you may call Guillemard to prove

it; I shall deny the fact, and he will back my denial. you will assert,

also, that I have get up this nefarious railroad speculation in order to

levant with the capital as soon as I could obtain a sufficient amount to

gratify my cupidity; I shall reply that you have do what you accuse

I of intend to do, and that I have be the mean of bring you

to justice. you will adduce, in proof of your assertion, the fact that

I introduce you as a director under the feigned name of Vondenthaler; I

shall rebut this accusation by declare that I have always know you

as Vondenthaler, which I believe to be your true name; and that your

identity with Le Roux, the croupier, be never even suspect by I.

of course, in these instance, I shall be swear falsely; you, truly;

nevertheless, I shall come off with fly colour, and you will be

transport. _Telle est la vie!_ would you oblige I by ring that

bell twice, for the policeman?”



the transition, from the assurance of successful cunning, to

self-distrust, anxiety, rage, despair, which flit across the sharp

but expressive face of Le Roux, show how strongly D’Almayne’s word

have agitate he. for a moment, he stand tremble in every limb,

clench his hand until the nail dig into the flesh; then, carry

away, by the impulse of his overpower terror, he fling himself at

Horace D’Almayne’s foot, exclaiming--



“for God’s sake, Mr. D’Almayne, have pity on I! I be an old man, sir;

old than I seem. I be sixty-five next month; I be, indeed; and I have

lead such a wretched, miserable life! I have always be somebody’s tool,

somebody’s slave. Sir, I have be for year the victim of a monomania:

as a very young man, I lose every halfpenny I possess (and that be

enough to have secure I a competence in some respectable line of life)

at the gaming-table; and since that time I have be haunt by the idea

that, by intensely study, and constantly calculate the chance,

I should discover some infallible system by which I could not only

retrieve my loss, but realize a large fortune. over and over again

have I try, and over again have I fail; until, at last, experience

have bring some little wisdom, even to such a miserable fool as I have

prove myself, and I have give up all attempt at discover a system;

but, sir, when this last hope fail I, the little honesty I have leave

desert I, and you have divine the result. Mr. D’Almayne, I have a

wife and three little innocent child at Brussels; they be to join

I in America if this attempt (which they only know of as a mercantile

speculation) have prove successful. if I be send out of this country as

a convict felon, it will break my wife’s heart; and my little child

will be leave to starve. Mr. D’Almayne, for the love of Heaven, have

pity, if not on I, on they!”



during this appeal, Horace remain in an easy and fashionable attitude,

with his back against the closed door which detain his captive, and

the point of his white and taper finger insert in his trouser

pocket; at its conclusion, he say, in his usual cool and indifferent

manner, “I think, my good friend, you begin this harangue with a

complimentary appeal to my common sense; not wish to discredit your

flattering opinion, let I ask you, be it likely, that, having toil

and scheme for the last twelve month to bring these two project of

the gambling-house and the railroad company into work (and pay)

order, I should allow you to go quietly to America, carry with you

the fruit of my labour, forethought, and sagacity, merely because, when

your last subterfuge have fail you, you whine out a beggar’s petition

about the love of Heaven, and a wife and three child? Bah! it be

childish, it be really too absurd! still, for old acquaintance sake,

I do not want to be hard on you; and if you will do exactly as I shall

propose, perhaps there may still remain some middle course, by which

such an uncomfortable result as transportation for life may be spare

you. what say you?” poor wretch! his crime discover, its fearful

penalty await he, and the “tender mercy of the wicked” his only

hope and refuge--with remorse for the past, and despair for the future,

rend his very heart asunder--what remain for he but to give

himself up, soul and body, as the dupe, tool, and agent of Horace

D’Almayne?



 long and earnest be their conference: the valise be open;

money and paper produce and examine; account go into; arrangement

for the present, and scheme for the future, discuss and agree upon.

the result may be sum up in a few word: when the New York packet

sail, at eight o’clock that evening, Le Roux have take possession of

his berth, with his valise considerably lightened; and Horace D’Almayne,

having see his associate safely out of the country, depart by the

last train which leave for London, some ten thousand pound rich than

he have be on his arrival that morning in the good city of Liverpool!









CHAPTER LIX.--HORACE weather the STORM.



Mr. Crane obtain nothing by his visit to the city, except a bad cold,

catch in a draughty omnibus, in which he ride because he be too stingy

to indulge himself with a cab; all the man he wish to see be out of

town, or attend some special appointment, and no information could he

obtain in regard to the security of his property invest in the “Direct

Overland Route to India Railway” share, so he return home in a bad

temper than any in which Kate have yet see he, and lead she such a life

of misery, during the evening, by mean of a process term, in the

_patois_ of back kitchen and washhouse, “nag” at she, that when

she retire to her own room, at ten o’clock, she be so utterly wear

out, that she sit down and cry, from sheer nervous depression. if

Arthur Hazlehurst could have see she then, he would scarcely have

recognize in that shrinking, trembling, spirit-break woman, the proud,

cold, haughty, beautiful Kate, who have win his heart but to trample

on it in her career of worldly ambition;--if he have hear she break,

falter prayer that death might soon relieve she from the daily,

hourly martyrdom of strive to render respect and obedience to a

man whom she do not hate, only because hate involve some degree of

equality, and Mr. Crane she too utterly despised,--if Arthur could have

witness her total prostration, mental and bodily, he would scarcely

have retain his hard thought of she, although the gentle one which

might have replace they, would, in their way, have be exquisitely

painful to he.



the next morning, Mr. Crane’s cold be bad, and Kate recommend he

to dispatch a note to his man of business, ask he to come to Park

Lane; which advice, be good and sensible, be, of course, reject,

and Kate be ask whether, not content with impoverish he by her

extravagance, and by the burden of support her pauper relative, she

wish to ruin he quite, by induce he to neglect the management

of his property. having deliver himself of this kind and judicious

remark, so well calculate to call forth and rivet the affection of

the wife of his bosom, this noble speciman of “Man, the great master of

all,” take ’bus for the city, to clip the wing which, he fear, his

rich be about to make for themselves. his man of business be again

“in court,” and uncome-at-able; but when he reach the office of the

“Overland Route to India Railway Company,” he find there Mr. Bonus

Nugget, in as near an approach to a rage as be at all compatible with

his high standing and intense respectability; a frame of mind in which

Mr. Crane speedily sympathise, when the disastrous intelligence be

communicate to he that a sum of nearly £18,000 have be draw out of

their banker’ hand, in the joint name of Horace D’Almayne and Herr

Vondenthaler, the former be abroad, and no trace to be discover of

the latter. poor Mr. Crane! he love his money dearly, he could not

bear to part with it even to pay a bill; and, as to give it in

charity (“fool it away” be the term he apply to such senseless

squandering), that be an unbusiness-like weakness of which he have never

be guilty; and now to have his idol thus rudely tear from he, oh! it

be too cruel. if Nugget have not be present, he would have sit down

and cry, for his sympathy with, and pity for himself be unbounded;

but, as he be not alone, he swear instead, for the sake of appearance;

but he do not swear well; for to anathematize, _con brio_, demand more

energy than Mr. Crane possess. having swear, however, to the good

of his ability, he and Mr. Nugget go into the affair of the company

together, and really, accord to the latter gentleman’s showing, the

speculation appear to be progress so well, that these minister of

Mammon agree the defalcation must be make good, and the public be keep

in the dark as to aught be “rotten in the state of Denmark.” so,

strange and mysterious proceeding be enter upon; bill for large

sum of money, draw by Mr. Nugget and endorse by Mr. Crane, and

cheque bear that gentleman’s signature, be deposit with the

company’s banker, to replace the £18,000 with which Herr Vondenthaler

have elope; also astute detective be place on that gentleman’s

track, and desire to look out for Horace D’Almayne, should he venture

to set his foot on english soil,--an imprudence which Mr. Crane

declare, confidentially, he be sure he never would be fool enough to

commit. for once, however, that worthy man’s sagacity be at fault, as

he be inform, on his return home, that a gentleman be wait to

see he in his library; and greatly be he astonish, and, if the truth

must be tell, considerably alarmed also, when the stranger prove to be

none other than the unblushe Horace himself. their interview be

long, but it end much more agreeably than it begin; for Horace, first

clear himself from the imputation of having have any hand in the

railway company defalcation, by prove that, at the time the cheque be

draw and present, he be at Ostend, gradually elicit from Mr.

Crane the fact of the anonymous letter, which, when it be with

much reluctance submit to he, he at once recognise to be in the

handwriting of the perfidious Vondenthaler. having produce satisfactory

evidence of this fact also, he produce something still more

satisfactory, viz., certain bill, promise to pay on demand, at an

early date, the cash which he have proceed to Holland to obtain.



this palpable proof of his factotum’s integrity quiet all Mr. Crane’s

suspicion, and D’Almayne be from that moment reinstate in his

patron’s good opinion. but now, accord to his own showing, this

excellent young man be himself the victim of circumstance. his name

having be the name select by the forger Vondenthaler, he feel

that he ought to withdraw from the railway company altogether; if he

remain, he should always be an object of suspicion. he know the nature

of city capitalist well; they have not all such enlightened view, such

generous soul, as his excellent friend Mr. Crane; besides, he could

not reconcile it with his honour to remain a director without pay, in

ready money, his share of the loss they have sustain by the rascality

of Vondenthaler--a man who, he blush to reflect, he have introduce. he

would most gladly pay his share that minute, but he honestly confess

he have not the money ready.



he know what he would do; he would sell his estate in Normandy--England

be the country of his adoption; if he could not live there, life would

become a burden to he. no! he would go to France, sell his estate, and,

with the proceed, return to redeem his honour. but it would be at a

sacrifice; he must part with his share in the Overland Railway, share

that be certain to become so fine an investment: do Mr. Crane know

any one who would like to purchase they? Mr. Crane pause, consider,

and then, in what he consider to be an off-hand, indifferent manner,

though eager rapacity twinkle in his cunning eye, and quiver on his

trembling lip, he reply, “if it will be any accommodation to you,

D’Almayne, I don’t know that I should object to take your share myself;

and, in regard to your Normandy estate, it seem a pity you should

be force to sell it, at a time, perhaps, when you may not obtain its

proper value. you have the title-deed in England; suppose we look

through they together. I have lend you money on they already, and might

perhaps be willing to advance you more on the same term--six per cent.,

I think? this would afford you time to look about you, and to sell

your estate, if you must part with it, to well advantage.” Horace

D’Almayne’s gratitude be quite touching to witness; so be his manner

at dinner, which Mr. Crane insist upon his stopping to partake

of. Kate be greatly astonish, and not well please, to find he

reinstate in his former high position in her husband’s favour; but he

treat she with such respectful deference, and his conversation be so

clever and interesting, that it be impossible for she not to contrast

his social advantage with those of Mr. Crane, which do not gain by

the comparison. Kate be nervous and unhappy, a state of mind in which

kindness, or its reverse, be feel with a morbid degree of acuteness; and

just as much as Mr. Crane’s peevish irritability oppress and annoy

she, do Horace D’Almayne’s soft voice, polished manner and considerate

tact, calm and soothe she, and reinvigorate her droop spirit.

if Kate Crane have a heart to win, now be the time to gain it. Horace

D’Almayne be by no mean a tyro in such case; he perceive the

situation at a glance, and avail himself of it to the utmost. when

he rise to take leave, Kate, know to what his departure would expose

she, and be, as we have before explain, overwrought and ill,

forget her self-control so far as to observe, “it be very early; be you

oblige to go so soon?” the moment she have speak the word she

would have give world to have recall they. her husband’s fretful

observation, “really, my dear, it’s past ten o’clock,”--and D’Almayne’s

look of triumph, ill-conceal under the guise of polite, conventional

regret at be oblige to leave such kind friend, show she

the indiscretion of which she have be guilty. but, ere she could

sufficiently collect her idea to attempt to redeem the false step

she have make, Horace have bow himself out. then Mr. Crane take up

his parable, and draw a feeble picture of a vicious young wife, who,

possess a sapient, tender, and judicious husband, in the prime of

life, lay herself out to attract the attention of, if he might be

allow the expression, mere boy, who, fortunately for she, have too

strongly innate idea of--yes, of propriety and morality, to avail

themselves of her very reprehensible levity, &c. &c. Poor, proud Kate!

she bear it all silently--her will be now as strong for good as it have

once be for evil, and duty seal her lip, though she suffer none

the less for her silence. Saint Bartholomew be flay alive, yet we

nowhere read that the good man be garrulous under the operation. when

D’Almayne quit Park Lane he return to his former lodging, and,

take pen, ink, and paper, write the follow note to the waiter at

Liverpool:--



_“a well-wisher of yours have much pleasure in enclose for your

acceptance a £10 note; should any impertinent inquiry be make in

regard to the gentleman who have visit your hotel lately, he

feel sure you know your duty too well, as a faithful servant of the

establishment, to reply to they in any way which might injure the

interest of your employer or _your own!_ in which case you shall hear

again from--



“MORE where this come from.”_



having dispatch this machiavellian document, Horace the indefatigable

seek and obtain interview with Guillemard, Bonus Nugget, and

Captain O’Brien, from all of whom he obtain useful information; then

proceed to the gaminghouse in J-------- Street, where he find the

Russian Prince Ratrapski, unprofitably sober, and play for sovereign

only. to he therefore he devote himself with so much success, that

between five and six on the following morning the Russian be take home

in a cab, considerably disguise in liquor, having lose above £20,000 to

the bank. it be a laudable practice of some pastor, to exhort the

member of their flock to chew the cud of reflection before they retire

to rest, and so to strike a balance of the good and evil deed which, in

the course of that day’s transaction, they may have perform. now,

although Horace D’Almayne have either no conscience at all, or one of

such an elastic material that its expansive limit be still

undiscovered; although, moreover, if he belong to a flock, it must

have be compose of the very black sheep know to zoology, he

nevertheless conform to this good habit of self-examination; and on

the night, or rather morning in question, his meditation assume some

such shape as the following:--



“_Voyons, Horace, mon ami!_ you have not be slothful, what have you

accomplish? the affair of Le Roux safely get over, without the fact of

our having encounter each other be suspect; good so far: but the

interview might transpire at any moment; I dare not remain here very

many day, scarcely hour, longer.--Crane, ha! ha! there be no pleasure

in dupe he, he be so dense a fool; but if there be no pleasure there

be profit, which suit my book equally well--what between the share and

the Normandy mortgage, I shall draw £5000 of he; to-morrow morning I

must obtain the money.--Then the Russian; I do that neatly; my

share will be £7000; though I shall claim more, for it be all my

management--yes, when I turn my back upon this _triste_ and mercenary

country, I shall be able to take at least £30,000 with I.” he pause,

reflect for some minute, then continue: “with such a capital as that

to start with, in America a man with a head on his shoulder may do

and become almost anything, president perhaps, who know? _she_ be

ambitious, I can read it in her haughty glance, her queenly step; such a

career might tempt she!” again he muse, but the working of his feature

show how deeply his feeling be excited. rouse himself with a

start, he exclaim, passionately, “I shall fail with she, I know; I

feel it! --she do not love I, nor, except at time when I make she

feel my power, do she even hate I; I wish she do, for then I should

have more hope--why should she be so indifferent to I? I have play my

game well and carefully; if I have it to play over again, I do not see

how I could mend my hand. that declaration, perhaps, be premature; yet

with any other woman, though it fail at the time, it would have tell

afterwards. I wonder whether she have any attachment before she marry

Crane? that cousin Arthur Hazlehurst, perhaps; if so, she love he

still; in that case, I need not seek far for revenge, even if she again

disdain my passion. married to Crane and love her cousin, she must

bear about a live hell in her own bosom. strange the power she have

over I; I really and honestly believe I be as completely in love with

she as if I be a green boy of eighteen! if I have know she five year

soon, before I become so thoroughly and hopelessly involve, I might

have be very different, who can say? that old man Le Roux be right,

the life of an adventurer be an unsatisfactory affair, either to look

back upon, or bad still, to look forward to; but so it be with every

phase of life, when you come to know it well and examine it

closely;--for what be we place here? nay, what be we ourselves? have

we live before? shall we live again? can spirit exist without matter?

who know? the religionist? bah! a set either of feeble-minded

enthusiast, bigote to childish superstition, or canting hypocrite,

who assume piety as a cloak beneath which to conceal their vice, as the

devil be say to lurk behind the cross. who then? philosopher,

metaphysician, your man of science? solemn pedant, dreamy mystic,

vain fool, who, because they have invent a rushlight, fancy they can

illuminate the universe--ah! charlatan all of they; an adventurer’s

career be preferable to a life devote to such dreary mummery. I may

succeed with the fascinating Kate yet; she be singularly amiable last

night; and if so, _Horace, mon ami_, the line you have select will not

prove such an unprofitable one, after all.”









CHAPTER LX.--ANXIETY.



Harry Coverdale be bless with an iron constitution, or as he would

himself have express it, the good keep and training he have come in for

ever since he be a colt, have put real hard flesh and muscle on he, so

that take he when you would, he be always in work order. thus,

although the hurried journey he have perform with a broken arm and a

series of bruise from head to foot, would have stretch most man on a

bed of sickness, and although Scalpel Gouger, M.D., elongate his

already sufficiently lengthen visage on behold his condition, and

prophesy result of which lock-jaw be by no mean one of the most

terrible, Harry yet experience no ill effect from his imprudence. his

stiffness wear off after a day or two, the bruise disappear one by

one, and the broken bone begin to re-unite as quickly as in the nature

of thing be possible. but although his bodily ailment give he little

cause for uneasiness, his mind remain a prey to anxiety, grief, and

remorse; for Alice, his young wife--the depth and strength of his love

for whom he become painfully aware of, now that, as it appear, he be

about to lose she--lie at the point of death. the demon of fever have

fix his burn finger upon she, and hold she in an iron grasp which

no mortal power seem able to unclasp. when Harry arrive, Alice do

not recognize he, her state alternating between attack of delirium, in

which she talk with the wild incoherence, and interval of stupor,

during each of which she lie perfectly unconscious and prostrate by the

violence of the paroxysm which have precede it. Poor Harry lose not an

instant in make his way to her room, disregard the housekeeper’s

entreaty to wait for Dr. Gouger’s return. when he enter, Alice be

sit up in bed, with flushed cheek and eye brilliant with the

unnatural lustre of feverish excitement, and talk with the utmost

volubility; at first he fancy she recognize he, for regard he

earnestly, she exclaimed--



“so you have come at last, have you?--and now tell I quickly, what news

do you bring I?” without wait a reply, she continue: “why don’t you

speak? no news, do you say?--it be false, you be try to deceive I.

I can read it in your face.--What! have they meet already? then Harry be

kill. ah! I know it, I know it! D’Almayne be a dead shot--Alfred

Courtland tell I so in that letter.--What do you mutter?--an

accident,--it be no accident.--D’Almayne have shoot he, kill he in a

duel; but it be my fault, I make he angry,--I drive he to go up to

London,--it be I who have murder he. oh, Harry, my own love husband,

if I could but have die for you!--shall I never see he again?” she

continue wildly: “ah, yes, I must, I will! let I go to he, I say;”

 and as she speak she attempt to get out of bed. throw his uninjured

arm round she, Harry prevent she from accomplish her purpose,

though she struggle so violently that he be oblige to obtain the

assistance of the hire nurse who have be recommend by the medical

man.



“Alice, love, look at I,” he say, tenderly. “I be safe--I be here by

your side--I will not leave you. do you not know I?” gaze at he

wildly, she tear herself from his embrace, exclaim in a tone of

horror--



“know you? yes, I know you, fiend! demon! you be Horace D’Almayne! do

you come here with my husband’s blood fresh upon your hand, and dare to

insult I by your detestable caresses?--are not you afraid that the

ground will open and swallow you? leave I, leave I instantly, or, weak

woman as I be, I will take my vengeance into my own hand, and stab you

to the heart!”



this idea that Harry be D’Almayne recur to Alice’s mind whenever she

behold her husband, and be the source of so much pain and distress to

he, that for both their sake Mr. Gouger forbid he to enter her room

for two or three day, by which time he trust the delusion might have

wear itself out. the prohibition be a judicious one, as it enable Harry

to obtain the rest he so much require; and when, after an interval of

nearly a week, he again return to his wife’s apartment, although she

be still unable to recognize he, she no long evince any repugnance

on his approach. her fit of delirium become less violent and frequent,

but she appear to be gradually sink into a state of prostration,

mental and bodily, which to the eye of the medical man be even more

alarming. her next fancy be, that Harry be her brother Arthur; she

talk to he of old scene and recollection, of their childhood, and

half break poor Harry’s heart by deplore in the most pathetic term

the loss of her husband’s affection, which she declare Arabella Crofton

have steal from she.



“ah, Arthur,” she would exclaim, “it be _cruel_ of she, because, you

know, I love he so _very, very_ much! until I see he I mean never

to marry; I fancy I could not bear to leave dear mamma, and emily,

and Tom, and all of you. but it be of no use: he be so good and kind,

and brave, and handsome; and though he be a little rough at first, I

soon see what a noble, gentle heart his rough manner conceal, and when

I find he love I (for he do love I once, Arthur), how could I, how

could any girl, help love he with her whole soul.”



Poor Harry, as she thus wildly talk, would lean over and kiss she

pale, wear cheek, and tell she he be her own love husband, and dote

on she, and her only,--that he never care, and never would care, for

any other woman, and she would smile faintly, and reply--



“No, Arthur, Harry would not say that; he love _she_ before he know I,

over in Italy; Alfred Courtland tell I all about it,--how they run away

together, and all.”



as she utter these word Coverdale start, and a shade pass across

his brow; not heed it, Alice continued--



“oh! she be a dreadful woman, and so clever! all the foolish thing I

do to pique Harry, in order to regain his affection, she show they up

to he in a false light, and make he believe I as wicked as herself,

and so she steal his love away from poor, poor Alice;” then she would

turn her face from he, and wail feebly like an unhappy child. at other

time she would burst into the most violent self-reproach.



“yes, I deserve it all,” she would exclaim; “I deserve to lose his

affection; what right have I to expect he to give up all his manly

sport, which have make he so brave and strong, to sit at home with a

poor foolish girl like I, who have not even wit enough to amuse he;

I who should have be too proud even of his slight notice, and to

thwart he and try to make he do foolish and wrong thing, and to lose

my temper, and grieve and wrong him,--oh! how wrong and wicked of I!

--I must have be mad to do it; and now he have leave I, go with

Arabella Crofton to Italy, and I shall never see he again, never,

never!” and then she would break off and resume her weeping.



and so the weary day pass on; Emily, who have come over as soon as she

have hear of her sister’s illness, be an indefatigable nurse, and she

and Harry sit up with the patient on alternate night, Coverdale have

on one occasion discover the hire nurse fast asleep when she ought to

have be wide awake and give Alice her medicine. as soon as his arm

cease to cause he such violent pain, Harry’s attendance by his wife’s

bedside become unremitting, and night after night he send Emily to bed,

and remain watch Alice’s break slumber, or to the good of his

power soothe she, during her fit of delirious excitement. could those

who have know Coverdale as the rough and eager sportsman, or the just,

but stern and inflexible, magistrate, have see he then, as (heedless of

the pain of his injure arm) he tend with all a woman’s devotion, and

more than woman’s strength and judgment, the sick couch of his (as at

time he fear) die wife, they would have be unable to recognize

the same individual whose nature they, in their hasty judgment, have so

wholly mistaken. his die wife! ah! how the idea haunt he. Alice,

his love one, would die; she would be take from he while they be

both so young, and he would have to live on during long, dreary year

alone!--alone! yes, but how bitterly do he feel the hope-crush

significance of that cruel word! true his married life have be a

somewhat stormy one, still it have teach he the charm of that spiritual

companionship with a beloved and love woman, without which a man’s

good nature remain incompletely developed. to feel a deep, true, and

unselfish affection for an object worthy of so precious a boon, raise

a man’s whole moral nature, and (if he be good for anything) make he

wise and well; to be love in return, render he happy despite the

toil and trial of life.



of these great truth, the event which we have in the course of this

history endeavour to pourtray, have cause Harry to acquire a painful

consciousness; he have become aware also of the cause which have hitherto

militate against the full amount of the happiness to be enjoy in such

a position. he have learn from poor Alice’s delirious confession, both

the depth of her attachment to he, and the fact that experience have in

her case also produce its bitter but salutary fruit. thus, should she

indeed be restore to he, what a bright, enviable future lie extend

before they! even as the thought occur to he, his eye fall upon

her thin, pale feature, her parch lip, sunken cheek, and the dark,

ominous hollow beneath her closed eye; nay, as she lie motionless,

wrap in a heavy, oppressive slumber, the horrible idea flash across

he that she might be dead already; and with a shudder he place his

hand upon her wrist, to feel the beating of her feeble yet rapid pulse,

ere he could satisfy himself that his frightful suspicion be but the

offspring of a morbid fancy. still, the idea have occur to he, and he

could not divest himself of it--what if she should never wake again,

or if she should die without any return of reason--die, ignorant of the

depth of love tenderness towards she which fill his breast! oh! if

he could but purchase her life at any sacrifice; there be nothing

he would not gladly give up--wealth, position, even his cherished

field-sport, everything!--how powerless he be, and how utterly

wretche! Accustomed, as he have hitherto be, to rely entirely on

his own strength, both of mind and body, to accomplish his wish,

the situation be equally new and painful to he. but Coverdale have a

powerful and singularly healthy mind, and even while he smart under

this severe chastening, he recognize the Hand which inflict it, and

the purpose for which it be send; and, mindful of the lesson of his

childhood, the strong man sink upon his knee by the side of his wife’s

sick couch, and pray to his Father in Heaven to spare, in his mercy,

the one little ewe-lamb, without which he must wear out the rest of his

earthly pilgrimage desolate and lonely-hearted.



the crisis of Alice’s complaint be now rapidly approach, and Harry

send for one of the lead London physician, who, after a careful

examination of the patient, and a long and solemn consultation with Dr.

Gouger, be pleased to say the latter gentleman have pursue exactly the

orthodox method of treatment; that he fear Mrs. Coverdale’s state be

a very precarious one, but that she could not be in safe hand than

those of Scalpel Gouger, M.D.



after Sir J. C-------- have take his departure and his fee of fifty

guineas, Coverdale, who have send Emily from Alice’s bedside, with strict

order to take a long stroll and refresh herself, be somewhat surprised

to see her return in less than half an hour considerably excited, and

with a heighten colour, which make she look remarkably pretty. she

beckon Coverdale out of the sick room, and then began--



“oh! Harry, dear, I want to speak to you, please; and you must be good

and kind, and not fierce, you know!”



in spite of his heavy heart, Coverdale could not help smile at his

little sister-in-law’s address.



“what be it, my dear child,” he say, kindly; “I’ll promise to behave

prettily; my fierceness, as you call it, be tolerably well take out of

I by this time.”



“well, I be walk in the Park, you know,” resume Emily, “and just

as I get to Markum’s cottage, I perceive a tall, aristocratic-look

young man talk to Mrs. Markum; as soon as she catch sight of I,

she exclaim, ‘here be Miss Hazlehurst, sir; she have just come from the

house, and can tell you the last account of poor mistress.’ Whereupon,

the gentleman approach I, and take off his hat, say, ‘I believe

I have the pleasure of address a sister of Mrs. Coverdale?’ I bow

assent, and he continue, ‘my name be Alfred Courtland. I do not know

whether Coverdale have tell you--(here he stammer and blush, so like

a frightened girl, that I begin to feel quite brave)--that be, whether

you be aware, that it be in my service he meet with his accident, and

that--that, in fact, I cannot but feel that your sister’s illness have

be, in great measure, bring on by my folly; the consequence be, that

ever since I hear of her attack, I have be miserable. Coverdale say

he would write I word how she be go on, but I suppose in his sorrow

and anxiety his promise have escape his memory. I bear the suspense as

long as I be able, until yesterday, hear by accident that Sir J.

C-------- have be send for, I could stand it no long;’ so I put

myself into a train the first thing this morning, and come down to learn

the truth; may I venture to hope that, as you be able to leave your

sister, her danger have be exaggerated?’ then I tell he that dear

Ally be still very ill, but that you be head nurse, and have force I

to come out to get a little air; and I say I be sure you would like

to see he. he be dreadfully afraid of intruding, and for some time

refuse to come, but at last he change his mind, and walk home with

I; he’s in the library, and you _will_ go and see he, there’s a dear

boy, for he be very unhappy, and I’m sure he’s a nice fellow.”



at any other time Coverdale would have be amuse at the extreme zeal

with which Emily have take up and advocate Lord Alfred’s cause, and

have tease she about her undisguised admiration of the handsome young

peer, but his heart be too heavy for jesting, so he merely replied--



“in the library, do you say? it’s very good of the boy to take such

interest about poor Alice, but he always be kind-hearted. go to she at

once, Emily, dear; she be asleep when you send for I, but she might

wake at any minute, you know--go to she, I willn’t be away long.”



on reach the library, Coverdale find Lord Alfred await his

arrival in an extreme state of nervous trepidation; grasp his hand,

Harry shake it warmly, saying--



“this be very kind of you, Alfred, my dear boy; you see you find we

still anxious; I hope there be no serious cause for alarm, but you know

it’s a case in which a man cann’t help feel very, _very_ anxious.”



as Coverdale thus speak word of encouragement, which his look and

manner, his quivering lip, brim eye, and the forced cheerfulness of

his voice, alike belie, Lord Alfred, more deeply affected than he could

have be by the most vehement reproach, lose all self-control, and,

burst into tear, exclaimed:--



“do not speak so kindly to I; it kill I. I’d rather by half you would

horsewhip I until I could not stand, for that be what I deserve. oh!

what misery my wicked folly have bring about! but for I, you would

never have meet with this accident, and Mrs. Coverdale would have escape

the anxiety and the shock which have bring on this illness; if I could

but do anything to help you or she, I should hate myself less.”



Harry approach he, and lay his hand on his shoulder.



“listen to I, my dear boy,” he say, kindly, but impressively, “these

thing cannot happen to a man without oblige he to reflect seriously,

and, as I hope, to some good purpose; you should not judge of your own

conduct, or of any one’s else, simply by result; we be instrument in

God’s hand to work out his design; and all that we can do be to make

ourselves acquaint with the rule he have lay down for our guidance,

and strive to act accord to they, but the result be in his hand,

and there we must be content to leave they. you have act foolishly,

but you be aware of it, and sorry for it; and in such a case, to look

back be bad than useless; the only good in ever recall the past be,

that the recollection may guard you against fall again into a similar

temptation should such a outcome in your way. so much for sermonize;

and now, you say you want to make yourself of use, and I can see you

mean it. my poor Alice’s mother be a great invalid, and the shock of

hearing of this affair have make she more ill than usual; she be most

anxious about her daughter. emily--you meet emily?”



“yes, a most interesting, charming young lady; I know she directly from

her likeness to poor Mrs. Coverdale,” be the reply.



 “well, Emily or

I write every day, but the letter take twelve hour to get there by

post; now, Sir J. C-------- be come down this afternoon to see poor

Alice again, and Gouger fancy some change be about to take place in

she; he suppose the crisis of the complaint be at hand--in fact--”

 Harry pause, for as he speak of the approach of the moment in which

Alice’s sentence for life or death be to declare itself, a choke

sensation in his throat deprive he of the power of utterance; try

to conceal his emotion under a feigned cough, he resume, “now, if you

wish to perform a really kind and good-natured action, will you remain

here until the physician have give his opinion, and then take my

dog-cart and mare, and drive over to the Grange, and detail his report

to Mrs. Hazlehurst? they will give you a kind welcome and a bed, and you

can either go to town from thence, or come back and dine and sleep here;

you’ll not be a bit in the way, and will help to amuse Emily, and tempt

she out of the sick room; for the good little girl be so zealous in her

attendance on her sister that I live in constant dread of her knock

up, and then I should have two of they on my hand at once--what do you

say?”



“say! if you think that by go to the world’s end I can be of the

small use or comfort to you, you have only to speak the word, and

I’m off,” be the eager reply; then in a plaintive tone, Lord Alfred

continue: “Coverdale, be you quite sure you don’t hate I for all this

misery I’ve bring upon you?”



“go into the dining-room and eat some luncheon, you young muff,” be

the unsentimental reply; “why, you have not a well friend in the world

than I be, or at all event a more sincere one, you stupid boy; but,

come along, I’ll send Emily to play hostess, and mind you make her eat

well. I know that girl will knock up if she refuse her corn.”



the luncheon pass off pleasantly enough--Emily not be overburthene

with shyness, and possess a flow of animal spirit, which even

her anxiety for her sister could not wholly overcome, chat away so

pleasantly, that Lord Alfred catch the infection, and take his share

in the conversation with spirit, so that when the meal be over, they

part mutually pleased.



Sir J. C-------- arrive true to his appoint time, examine his

patient, look grave, consult with Dr. Gouger, and then the two

medico summon Coverdale. as he enter, the physician, who be a tall

gaunt man, with a large, sharp nose, raise himself on tiptoe, as if he

be try to fly, then give it up as hopeless, subside on his heel

again, clear his throat, stroke his chin, look at Coverdale as if

he wish to feel his pulse or give he a pill, and begin in a bland and

insinuate tone of voice--



“you be anxious, my dear sir--naturally anxious as to the state in

which we (here by a little condescending but patronize pantomimic

action he indicate Gouger) have find Mrs. Coverdale?”



Poor Harry, boil with anxiety and impatience, shoot a “yes, of

course,” at he as if he have be a partridge. in no way disturb,

however, the autocrat of all the pill-box continued--



“the duration of your justifiable anxiety, my dear sir, will not be much

far prolong; in less than twelve hour the complaint will have

reach its crisis, and the result will not be long in reveal itself

to educated eye.”



“and you think----you feel reason to believe that------the result will

be favourable,” stammer Harry, his stalwart frame tremble from head

to foot with the emotion he be unable to conceal--“You do not think

your patient bad than when you last see she?”



the physician pause: then reply, gravely--



“it would be mistaken kindness to disguise from you the truth, sir. Mrs.

Coverdale be in a most precarious state--her life hang on a thread; I

do not say that she must die, but it be my duty to tell you that it

be more than probable that she may do so; the next twelve hour will

probably decide the question. she be now apparently sink into a heavy

slumber--from this she may never awake, or it may be succeed by fit

of delirium, from which she would be unable to rally.”



Harry shudder, then asked--



“and what would be a favourable symptom?”



“if Mrs. Coverdale should wake free from delirium, so as to be able to

recognize those about she, you may reckon that the fever have wear itself

out; and the only thing then to dread will be her extreme weakness; in

that case every effort must be make to keep she up; give her port wine,

or even brandy, a teaspoonful every five minute if she appear faint;

but my friend, Mr. Gouger, be quite aware of the proper measure to be

take--she cannot be in well hand.”









CHAPTER LXI.--ALICE appoints she SUCCESSOR.



that suppose great arbiter of life and death, the London physician,

have depart, leave at least one ache heart behind he; for

Coverdale could not disguise from himself that, although Sir J.

C-------- have not actually pronounce Alice’s sentence in plain word,

his intention have be to prepare he for the bad. in pity to Emily’s

youth and warm affection for her sister, he do not acquaint she with

the immediate proximity of the crisis on which depend their loved

one’s fate and his happiness; nor, not place any great reliance on

Lord Alfred Courtland’s power of keep a secret, do he enlighten he

either; but he make some excuse for detain he and offer he a

bed, so that he might be unable to start on his mission to Hazlehurst

Grange until the next morning.



as the evening advance, Alice, who have be alternately doze and

wake up to bewail herself in wild, incoherent sentence, fall into a

deep, heavy sleep.



Dr. Gouger, having yield to Harry’s earnest request that he would

return and sleep at Coverdale Park that night, set out to pay two or

three indispensable visit, promise to be back in good time.



about eleven o’clock, Emily use every argument she could think of to

try and induce Harry, who have sit up during the last three night,

to allow she to take his place, but in vain; and read in his pale,

anxious countenance that his mind be make up, she content herself

with obtain his promise that if any change take place, she should

be summon immediately, go to bed, and dream that Lord Alfred

Courtland be a persian prince, disguise as a physician, who have

bring a talisman to cure Alice, for which he be to be liberally and

appropriately reward with she (the dreamer’s) own fair hand and the

Archbishopric of Canterbury.



Emily have scarcely retire when Dr. Gouger return. Alice be still

rapt in a heavy sleep, from which he give strict order she should not

be arouse.



“who sit up with she?” he inquire.



“the nurse, of course,” return Harry: “that be, if snore in an

arm-chair deserve to be call so; and, until she be out of danger, or,

if it should be so, until God may see fit to take she from I, I will

never leave she!”



“well, then, if she wake of herself before morning, be very careful

not to startle or alarm she. watch her eye closely, and see if she

recognize you; if she do so, that will be a favourable symptom; if

she speak to you, control your feeling, and answer she quietly and

calmly; then instantly send for I. I think you perfectly understand?

well, then, as I’ve ride a good many mile to-day, and have even

a long round to take to-morrow, I’ll go and lie down. I shall not

undress, so I can be with our patient the moment you send for I.”



thus say, the doctor, who be a short, plump, florid little man, with

a plain face preserve from insignificance by a pair of bright, keen

eye, and a magnificent forehead, yawn twice, and betook himself to

the spare room allot to he.



Twelve o’clock! Alice still asleep! the nurse having arrange

a formidable line of medicine bottle ready for use, produce a

well-thumb volume from her pocket, and adjust her spectacle,

sit down to read by the night-lamp. one o’clock! the nurse, after many

fruitless attempt to keep up appearance, and delude Harry into the

belief that she be wide awake, begin to nod over her book, occasionally

vary the performance by try to swallow a suppress snore, and

choke in the attempt. two o’clock! no change in the patient; but the

nurse, who during the last half-hour have settle down into a deep and

undisguisable sleep, begin to snore so loudly that Coverdale, afraid of

her disturb Alice, take she by the shoulder, and lead she quietly,

but unresistingly, into the dressing-room, and seat she on a sofa; to

which discipline, the nurse, who have once or twice before experience

the force of Harry’s quiet manner, submit with a lamb-like meekness

and docility, of which those who have see she tyrannize in the sick

chamber of her poor client, would scarcely have deem she

capable. three o’clock! how long the hour seem, and how dreary! the

stillness--break only by the measured breathing of the patient and the

distant snoring of the banish nurse--the deep, solemn stillness of a

country house at night, become painfully oppressive to the overwrought

sense of the watcher. will the crisis never arrive? Alice move

slightly, and moan in her sleep. Harry tremble from head to foot. be

she about to wake? will she recognize he? No!--she sink again into

a deep, heavy slumber, and Harry breathe a sigh of relief and of

thankfulness that the fearful moment be again postpone. four o’clock!

the dim grey light of dawn begin to peep in through the opening in the

shutter, cause the lamp to shed lurid, flicker ray around the

sick room, and thus add to, rather than diminish, the darkness.

how cold it have become! and how every nerve and fibre in Harry’s injure

arm ache and throb! what an eternity of anguish appear capable of

be condense into a few minute of severe bodily pain!



Hark! what be that low, wail sound outside the window? he start, and

turn pale! why do those foolish, hateful legend of Banshees, throng

and crowd into his brain? why do he remember with shiver dread that

old wife’s tale of a white lady who weep and wring her hand before

the death of any member of the Coverdale family? he laugh at it as a

boy, and dress himself in white to frighten the maid. he cannot laugh

at it now! again it come, loud and more prolonged! but he know

this time that it be the howling of a dog--the King Charles’s spaniel,

Alice’s pet, which he have be oblige to have tie up, lest it might

disturb she; but hitherto it have bear its confinement quietly. why

should it howl so dismally to-night? do any strange instinct warn it of

its mistress’s danger? ah! that word--danger!--yes, a danger from which

all his deep fervent love, and his unequalled, manly strength, be

alike powerless to shield she. how crush, and helpless, and miserable,

well-nigh despairing, he feel! and yet be they not both in the

hand of a merciful Father? God’s will be do! but as the word of

resignation pass his lip, the big tear roll down his cheek as the

recollection of all that he might be resign wrung his love breast.

cover his eye with his hand, he strive to shut out all thought, all

feel! how long he remain in this position he never know; but as

soon as he remove his hand, it strike he that Alice have change

her attitude. shade his eye from the glare of the lamp, he gaze

earnestly at she. yes, she have move, and surely she be awake. while

he yet look, unable to trust the evidence of his sense, a soft, faint

voice, scarcely above a whisper, pronounce his name: so low be the

sound, that, fancy it might be a delusion of his own overwrought

sense, Harry bend down his head, as he ask, in a quiet, gentle tone

of voice--



“Alice, darling, be you awake? do you call I?”



for a moment there be no reply, and then the same gentle voice

whispered--



“Harry, dear, you have be away a long, long time.”



as she speak, she try to raise her arm to draw his face nearer; but

the waste muscle refuse to do their duty, and the poor thin, almost

transparent hand, drop powerless beside she.



“I be very weak, Harry, love,” she say; then, with an effort at

recollection, she add: “where be I?--here, at home? have I be ill

long?”



“you have be very ill, my own darling; but you will soon get well now.

don’t try to talk, or think about it yet. I will fetch you a soothe

draught, and then you must endeavour to go to sleep again.”



fearful of over-excite she, he rise to call the nurse. as he turn to

leave she for this purpose, Alice again stretch out her hand to detain

he.



“Harry, love, do not go away, please. I will do everything you tell I,

but I shall die if I lose you again.”



Harry stoop, and kiss her pale, thin cheek.



“I be only go to call the nurse,” he say. “I will never leave you

any more, dear!”



Alice faintly endeavour to return his caress, and sink back exhausted

on her pillow.



Harry rouse the still sleep nurse, and dispatch she to summon Dr.

Gouger. then return to his wife’s bedside, he take her thin hand in

his; and as his affectionate pressure be feebly return, the hope

that Alice might be restore to he a hope which that night of anxious

watching have nearly destroy--begin once more to reanimate he.



Dr. Gouger, accustom to be call up at all hour of the night, make

his appearance in an incredibly short space of time. as he approach

the bed, Alice perceive he, and smile faintly in token of

recognition--a favourable symptom, at which the doctor nod approval.

having make a careful examination of the patient, he prepare a draught,

which he give she. then say, “now try and go to sleep, my dear madam,

and I trust to find you much refresh to-morrow morning,” he turn to

leave the room.



Harry follow he to the door.



“well?” he say, in a tone of the deep anxiety.



“the disease have wear itself out. Mrs. Coverdale be free from fever, and

the only thing we have now to fear be weakness,” be the doctor’s reply.

“she must be keep perfectly quiet both in mind and body for some day.

when she wake in the morning, throw a cape or something over that arm

of your; it might give she a shock if she be to perceive it suddenly.

it be a very favourable symptom she having recover consciousness

so completely,--in fact, the case be go on as well as, under the

circumstance, I conceive to be possible.”



“thank God!” be all the reply Harry could make; but as Alice, with

her hand in his, fall into a sound, refreshing slumber, his whole soul

pour itself out in silent but heartfelt thanksgiving to the Father of

all mercy, who have accept his penitence, and again entrust to his

care the tender flower which, in his inconsiderate carelessness, he have

once neglect.



when Emily come down to breakfast on the follow morning, she quite

start with pleased surprise to perceive the bright, happy expression,

of her brother-in-law’s countenance.



“I need not ask whether Alice be well,” she begin; “I can read it in

your face. but have any great change take place since yesterday?”



in reply to her question, Harry tell she all--tell she even more than he

have ever confess to himself--how, day by day, his hope have diminish

and his fear increase, until, after the physician’s caution on

the previous morning, he have make up his mind that the medical man

consider Alice die; how he have conceal from she that the crisis of

the complaint be at hand, and how he have pass the night in an agony

of trembling expectation, long for and yet dread the moment in

which she should awake; together with his delight when he hear she

pronounce his name.



Lord Alfred Courtland set off in high glee for Hazlehurst Grange,

certain of a hearty welcome, as bearer of such good tiding, and

happy, as he declare, than he have feel for the last six month.



a week pass away. for two or three day, Alice appear to progress

favourably--as favourably as even her husband’s anxiety could desire.

she know every one, and converse reasonably upon all subject; but with

the return of consciousness, a settle melancholy appear to have

take possession of she. this, together with her extreme weakness, give

uneasiness alike to her indefatigable nurse, Harry and Emily, and to

Dr. Gouger. take Harry aside one morning, he began--



“there be symptom about Mrs. Coverdale which I cannot understand,

and which appear to I more mental than bodily. they be retard her

recovery; and if you could ascertain the cause, and be able to remove

it, I do not hesitate to tell you that you would prove a more effectual

physician than I, or any one else, can be to she; but you must bear

in mind her state of extreme debility; she be not fit to discuss any

exciting topic at present.”



“then how would you recommend I to proceed?” inquire Harry, the

doctor’s warning having impress he with two diametrically opposite

ideas:--first, that it behove he to ascertain whether anything, and

(if anything) _what_, be prey upon his wife’s mind; and, secondly,

that by so do, he should probably lead she to talk on some exciting

subject, which, in her present weak state, be the thing of all other

to be avoid. how be these difficulty to be reconcile?



Dr. Gouger’s answer do not tend greatly to elucidate matter.



“really, my dear sir, _that_ be a point on which I can give you no

advice. in the treatment of all bodily ailment, I, with all due

deference to my professional brother, consider myself as competent as

any man; but be I so far to overstep my proper province as to attempt

to ‘minister to a mind disease,’ as our great poet have it, I should be

guilty of unpardonable presumption. no, my dear sir, I have give you

the suggestion, and must leave it to your sound judgment how far, or in

what way, it may be desirable to act upon it.”



Poor Harry! just the very point upon which he feel most incompetent to

form an opinion, be those on which he be call upon to decide and

act; but Harry have one adviser which never fail he--his own simple,

straightforward common sense; and to that, and the so-call chapter of

accident, he resolve to trust.



during the remainder of that day, however, the aforesaid chapter do

not afford he the opportunity he seek for. Alice appear weak and

depressed, and more inclined to sleep than to converse. on the following

morning, she seem a degree strong and less disinclined to exertion.

she inquire into the particular of the steeple-chase, and especially

interested herself in all the detail relate to the leap at which he

meet with his accident, and his “pluck” in remount and win the

race with a broken arm.



after Harry have give a full, true, and particular account of the affair

from begin to end, and his wife have evince all proper interest and

sympathy, a pause ensue in the conversation, which be break by Alice.



“Emily have be tell I how you _would_ sit up with I, night after

night, when you ought to have be lie in bed yourself with your poor

arm,” she say; “how kind and good it be of you! I hope you do not

suffer very much pain now?”



“oh, no! it be troublesome at time, but in general it be pretty easy,”

 be the reply.



after another pause, Alice ask, in a low, tremble voice--



“do you think I should die, Harry?”



“I be naturally very anxious and unhappy about you,” return

Coverdale, “and--well, since you be get on so nicely, I will

confess that I be terribly frightened about you at one time,--that

night on which the crisis take place especially; I never wish to pass

such another six hour, I assure you!”



“Harry, love, I hope it would not make you very unhappy to lose I. just

a little sorry I should wish you to feel; I should like you, when you

be recollect I, to think, ‘she be a poor, foolish little thing,

very obstinate and perverse at time, but still she love I as well as

such a silly little thing could.’”



“Alice, my own darling, why indulge in such gloomy fancy?” reply she

husband, tenderly; “you know, you must be sure, it would break my heart

to lose you. ask Emily whether I be not a different creature since the

doctor have pronounce you out of danger?”



“Harry, my own dearest husband, I love to hear you say that, and I know

it be true; but, dear Harry, you must not be very unhappy if such a

thing be to occur, for--for--I think I shall die yet; I think I grow

weak and weak every day; I shall never have strength enough to get

well again.”



Coverdale be about to interrupt she, but she place her finger on his

lip to imply her wish that he should remain silent as she continued--



“yes, dearest, I believe I be gradually sink into my grave; it make

I very, very unhappy at first; for life be pleasant, and I be young

to die! besides, I know, love, what a bad, tiresome wife I have be to

you, and I do so want to try if I could not do well; I know what a

proud rebellious, wilful temper I have show towards you, but indeed I

don’t think I have altogether a bad heart, and I do hope if I try,

very hard, perhaps I could make you happy; but lately I have begin to

think it may be well for you as it be.”



“my own darling, what strange, silly fancy be these? Gouger say you

be go on as well as possible; you make I wretched to hear you talk

so, and what _do_ you mean by it be well for I as it be? if I be

to lose you, I should never know another happy hour.”



“you think so now, dear,” be the reply, “and very kind it be of you to

be so fond of your naughty, tiresome little wife; and I know you will

be very unhappy at first when I die; but you must go abroad or take a

shooting tour somewhere, to keep you from think and fret about

I; and--you must not be angry at what I be go to say, dear--in a

year or so you must come back, and then you can marry some one who will

make you a well wife than poor, silly little Alice--some one who have

be attach to you a long time, and whom there will be no reason why

you should not love in return when I be out of the way; she be more

clever and courageous than I be, and will be able to enter into your

pursuit, and help you with your magistrate’s business, and--and--oh! I

be sure you will be very happy with she, dear!”









CHAPTER LXII.--MRS. COVERDALE think better of it.



Harry listen with all the patience he could muster while Alice be

thus comfortably arrange her own decease and his second marriage,

then speak gravely, though still in the most affectionate manner, he

replied--



“I cannot even _feel_ annoy with you now you be so ill and weak, my

poor child, but the matter to which you allude be most repugnant and

distasteful to I; it be a subject, in fact, on which I would not

allow any human being but yourself to address I. I will not pretend to

misunderstand your allusion; but I do most solemnly assure you that you

be mistaken, and that be it, indeed, God’s will that you should be

take from I, no new tie should come between my soul and the memory of

the only woman, except my poor mother, whom I have ever really love. I

see that you do not believe I! it be unjust, almost unkind of you!”



Harry speak with deep feeling; and Alice, with tear in her eye, place

her poor, thin hand within that of her husband as she replied--



“I do most fully believe that you love I as you say, and that at this

moment you do not imagine you could be happy with anybody else, but it

be a comfort to I to think that when I be part from you there will

still be some one to care for you. I assure you I feel quite differently

towards Miss Crofton _now_; I be jealous of she, dreadfully jealous--I

confess it! but I now be grateful to she for love you, and sorry I

ever entertain such uncharitable feeling towards she. I mean to leave
