she all my jewel, except one or two little thing I should like to give

poor Emily.”



Alice pause, partly through weakness, partly because she want she

husband to signify his approval of her sentiment, which she consider

be the least he could do, in return for what be, in fact, to she, an

act of almost superhuman charity and self-denial. but Coverdale be in

no humour to comply with her desire; on the contrary, so distasteful be

the whole matter, and poor Alice’s idea of the situation so far from

the truth, that he be drive to his wit’ end with perplexity and

annoyance, which nothing but a sense of his wife’s unfitness to sustain

so energetic a mode of address prevent from break forth in a burst

of his “quiet manner.” as he continue silent, Alice resumed:--



“you must not be angry with I for know about it, Harry dear, for the

knowledge be force upon I, nor be I aware what Lord Alfred Courtland

be about to tell until I have hear so much that my womanly dignity

would not allow I to stop he; I do not choose to let he think I

could believe it possible you have do anything I should be afraid to

hear, and so he tell I all.”



“and pray what might _all_ be?” inquire Harry, as calmly as he be

able.



“oh! about she be in love with you, and your run away together,

and old Mr. Somebody (I cann’t remember name) take she away again, and

prevent you from marry she; yes, he tell I all about it.”



“he tell you a pack of lie, so mix up with a little truth, that

unless I be able to give you a detailed account of the affair I could

not separate they, and I be under a solemn promise not to say anything

about it; but I know what I will do. in the meantime believe this--I

love you with my whole heart and soul, and you only, and if you have any

regard for I you will strive to banish all these silly fancy, which

only delay your recovery, and get well as fast as you can for my sake.

and now you have talk more than be good for you, so I shall send emily

to you to read you to sleep.”



as soon as he have put this resolution into practice he betake himself to

the library, and write as follows:--



“dear Arabella,--The promise I make you at the inn, at Fiumalba, I have

up to this time keep faithfully; I now ask you to release I from it. my

wife’s happiness (in which my own be bind up), perhaps her life even,

depend upon your do so; she have just pass the crisis of a brain

fever, her bodily weakness be lamentable to witness, and the mental

depression naturally arise from it lead she to take a morbid and

desponding view of her own chance of recovery: in such a position,

anything that will conduce to raise her spirit and tranquillise she

mind will effect more than twenty doctor. some mischief-maker have

cause she to obtain a garbled account of a certain occurrence, to which

I will not far refer; nothing but _the whole truth_ will suffice to

set her mind at rest. arabella! I deeply regret this necessity, but it

cannot be avoid, and I trust to you to act towards I as I would act

by you if the situation be reverse.



“I remain always,



“your true and sincere friend,



“Harry Coverdale.”



for two or three day after that on which the forego conversation

between Coverdale and his wife take place, Alice continue much in the

same condition, the idea that she should die, and that after her death

Harry would espouse Arabella Crofton, and be much happy than she have

be able to make he, appear never absent from her mind; her appetite

decrease, her sleep become broken and fitful, and Mr. Gouger’s face

grow long, and his head shake more and more like that of Lord Burleigh

in the _Critic_, every time he visit she.



one morning, on Coverdale’s return from the neighbouring town, whither

he have ride to procure some delicacy wherewith to try and tempt

Alice’s capricious appetite; he be equally surprised and pleased on

enter her room to perceive a brightness in her eye and a colour in

her cheek, such as he have fear never to see there again.



“why, Alice darling, this fine morning have inspire you--you be look

more like yourself than I have see you this many a long day!” he

exclaim, as he seat himself by the easy-chair which Alice have gain

sufficient strength to use as a substitute for her couch.



regard he with a smile and blush, which tinge she late, cheek with

the most delicate rose-colour, she replied--



“you have grow very clever in read people’s face of late, Harry

dear; but you be quite right in fancy something have inspire I--at

least, if feel very happy be what you mean by inspiration. but oh!

how foolish I have be! how wrong, how unjust I be ever to doubt you!

Harry dearest, can you forgive I for not feel certain that you have

always act as nobly and generously before I know you as you have do

since? if you could tell how I hate and despise myself for my silly,

illiberal suspicion! but you must wonder all this time what have set I

rave in this strange way. what do you think of my having have a

letter from--yes! actually from Miss Crofton, tell I--here, read it

yourself, I be certain every word of it be true; and oh! how I pity she

for be oblige to write it, and, indeed, for the whole affair, poor

thing!”



as Alice speak she draw a letter from the pocket of her dress, and give

it to her husband; it run as follows:--



“I have receive a note from Mr. Coverdale, urge I to release he

from a promise he most kindly make I, at a time when, bow down by

shame and contrition, his do so save I, as I verily believe, from

madness or suicide. he tell I your health and his happiness depend

upon my complying with his request; it become then a duty in I to

do so; and, however painful it may be, I will not flinch from it. it

appear to I that the most effectual way to remove any misapprehension

from your mind, in regard to the nature and extent of my acquaintance

with Mr. Coverdale before his marriage, will be to give you a concise

account of the occurrence which take place during the summer I spend

in Italy, whither I have accompany a family of the name of Muir, in the

capacity of governess. the Muirs be well-mean, commonplace people,

not possess the slight tact or refinement of feeling. I be at

that time young, and morbidly sensitive; and the slight they put

upon I, without, as I can now perceive, intend any unkindness, or,

indeed, be aware of the effect their thoughtlessness be produce

upon I, be a daily martyrdom to my proud spirit. we spend three

month at Florence; and shortly after we have settle there, John Muir,

the eld son, who have be make a tour among the swiss mountain,

rejoin his family, accompany by Mr. Coverdale, who have know he

at the university. slightly attract, I fancy, by the good look of

my eld pupil, who be an unusually pretty nonentity, Mr. Coverdale,

always talk of the necessity of continue his journey to the East,

still linger at Florence. the great kindness of heart and delicacy of

feeling which lie hide under a roughness of manner that can only mislead

a very superficial observer, soon lead he to perceive and pity my

isolate position; and from the moment in which he become aware how

keenly the sense of dependence prey upon I, he treat I with a

degree of deference and attention which could not but contrast most

favourably with the neglect I experience from other. under the cold

manner which circumstance have force I to assume, I have conceal a

naturally ardent and impetuous disposition, and as deeply as I have be

affect by the ungenerous conduct of the Muirs do I now appreciate Mr.

Coverdale’s sympathy and kindness--in a word, for I have resolve to

conceal nothing from you, I love he with all the force of my

passionate nature. but the very strength of my feeling lead I

studiously to conceal they; nor, until the elopement of my eld pupil

with a scheming italian adventurer break up the party, do I give Mr.

Coverdale the slight opportunity of suspect the warm interest he

have excite in I; but when about to bid he farewell as I imagine for

ever, my self-control give way, and I burst into a passionate flood of

tear. equally grieve and surprised, he soothe I with his accustom

kind and considerate delicacy, beg I always to look upon he as a

friend, and apply to he in any emergency, as to a brother; and as soon

as I become somewhat more composed, leave I. the next tiding I hear of

he be that he have quit Florence. Scarcely have I retire to my

room, to endeavour to calm my excitement, and to struggle to subdue my

hopeless attachment in tear and solitude, when Mrs. Muir send for I,

and reproach I with equal virulence and unkindness for her daughter’s

elopement, which she declare to have be the consequence of my

neglect. ‘have you,’ she continue, ‘be less engross by seek to

ensnare the affection of Mr. Coverdale, you would have be well able

to perform the duty of your situation, and this misfortune might never

have come upon we.’ sting by the mixture of truth and falsehood in this

cruel reproach, I reply--I know not what--proudly, and I can now well

believe impertinently; and the next thing that I become aware of be,

that a sum of money sufficient to defray my expense to England be

place before I, and that I be dismiss. throw thus on my own

resource in a foreign land, without a single friend near to help or

advise I, what wonder that I instinctively turn to the only quarter

from which I have for year (for mine have be a desolate youth) meet with

kindness, consideration, and sympathy; and that from the chaos of

conflict emotion one idea alone stand out clear and define--to seek

Harry Coverdale, throw myself on his generosity, tell my tale of sorrow

and of love, and leave the result to he and destiny. that such a course

be unwomanly, almost unpardonable in I, none can be more bitterly

aware than I be; but I pray God that those of my own sex who be

inclined to condemn I may never be tempt as I be tempt--may never

fall as, but for the superhuman goodness of heart, and the tender,

simple, yet chivalrous nature of your husband, I should have fall.

with I, to resolve and to act be simultaneous. I lose not a moment in

ascertain the route Mr. Coverdale have take, and ere the Muir family

be aware of my departure I have follow he to Fiumalba, a small town

within a few hour’ journey of Florence. without allow myself an

instant’s time for reflection, I seek the hotel at which Mr. Coverdale

be stop, and in my distraction fling myself at his foot, and tell

he everything--how I love he well than any other create

being--well even than my own womanly pride and good name--how I felt

convince that such love as mine must in time win return--how that if he

would make I his wife, I would devote every thought; every action of my

future existence, to secure his happiness--how, if he refuse I, I

would lie down at his foot and die, but never leave he. then do he

indeed redeem his promise of act by I as a brother--then do he save

I from my bad enemy--myself. having soothe and quiet my agony of

spirit, by his calm good sense and judicious kindness, he appeal to my

reason--set before I how, by yield to my request, and make I the

partner of his future life, while unable to feel for I that degree of

affection without which such a tie must become unbearable, he would be

do I an injury rather than confer a benefit; nor do he leave I

until he have obtain my consent to allow he to return to Florence,

explain the whole matter to Mr. Muir, expostulate with he as to the

cruelty and injustice of thus dismiss I with an undeserved slur on

my character as a governess, and endeavour to arrange that I should

remain with his wife and daughter, and accompany they on their return to

England. in this negotiation he be successful. Mr. Muir,--an easy,

self-indulgent character, yet one who could, on occasion such as that

to which I refer, act kindly and honourably,--accompanied Mr. Coverdale

back to Fiumalba, where he inform I that he have prevail on Mrs.

Muir to agree to the above proposal, add that he and Mr. Coverdale

be the only person aware of the imprudent step I have take, and that

they be both willing to make I a solemn promise never (unless by my

desire) to reveal the transaction to any one. utterly break-spirited

and miserable, I consent, and, take leave of my preserver, return

with Mr. Muir to Florence. from that day, until our accidental meeting

in Park Lane, I see Mr. Coverdale no more. what it have cost I to write

this I will not attempt to describe, but that every word of it be the

simple truth, I call Heaven to witness; that the knowledge of it may for

ever reconcile all difference between you and your noble, generous

hearted husband, and that you may be restore to make he as happy as I

be certain it be in your power to do be the wish and prayer of one who,

if she have err deeply, have suffer equally, as she hope not without

some good result.



“Arabella Crofton.”



when Harry have finish read the letter, he return it to his wife,

observe, “that be, as she say, a faithful account of all that ever

occur between we. you now see why I be unable to explain to you the

apparent mystery. I hold a promise to be so sacred a thing, that

nothing--not even the loss of your affection--could induce I to break

one. and now, my poor child, I hope you be satisfied that I indeed love

you with my whole heart, and that the affection of a thousand Arabella

Croftons would never compensate I for the loss of one bright smile or

fond look from my own darling wife.”



Alice attempt to reply, but her heart be too full for word: burst

into a flood of tear of mingled joy and contrition, she fling her arm

around her husband’s neck, and in that prolong embrace end once and

for ever all Harry Coverdale’s matrimonial dispute and discomfort.









CHAPTER LXIII.--LORD ALFRED SEVERS his leading string.





Lord Alfred Courtland and Horace D’Almayne be both member of the

Pandemonium, at which notable club the latter, when he have no rich

victim on whom to quarter himself, chiefly spend his day. the visit

which Lord Alfred have pay to Coverdale Park, and his subsequent mission

to Hazlehurst Grange, have impress he deeply, and bring out all

his good quality. on his return to town, he take himself to task more

seriously than he have yet do, for the careless and extravagant life he

have be lead; and, warn by experience how futile such repentance

might prove, unless follow by some practical effort at self-reform,

he set to work with his accustomed impetuosity, to remedy the

evil result from his injudicious attempt to become a fast

“man-about-town.” the Honourable Billy Whipcord relieve he of one

difficulty, by purchase Don Pasquale for the same amount which

Lord Alfred have give Tirrett for the animal, and with the money thus

obtain, together with his winning on the steeple-chase, he, like an

honest fellow, pay all his creditor. feel much happy for this

step in the right direction, he determine to follow it up by another,

and accordingly write to his father, say that, his health be now

re-establish, it be his wish to return to Cambridge, and endeavour to

make up for lost time. having dispatch this letter, and ride for a

couple of hour in the Park, the necessity of dining occur to he,

and he turn his horse’s head towards the Pandemonium. as he ride

thither, it strike he that he might possibly encounter Horace

D’Almayne, and he bethink he of his promise to Harry Coverdale, to

give up the acquaintance of the man whom he have so incautiously trust,

and who have abuse that trust by lead he into evil whenever an

opportunity present itself for so do.



yes! disagreeable as it be, perhaps even dangerous (for D’Almayne be

not a man to insult with impunity), he would redeem his pledge word--he

would show his gratitude to Coverdale. if D’Almayne be at the club,

he would cut he in a marked and unmistakeable manner! as these thought

be pass through his brain, he become aware of a young man, flashily

dress, and mount on a magnificent horse, who, as he pass, take off

his hat to he. confuse for the moment by the idea that it must be some

acquaintance whom he ought to recognise, he bow stiffly, whereupon the

horseman wheel his steed, and ride up to Lord Alfred’s side--



“I beg your Lordship’s pardon,” he begin, “but I wish to say a few word

to you. do not your Lordship remember I?”



“your behaviour towards I, Mr. Tirrett, be of a nature neither easily

to be forget, nor calculate to make I desirous of cultivate your

further acquaintance. I have the honour of wish you good morning.”



say this with the hauteur and dignity of the whole House of Peers

combine, Lord Alfred turn his head away from his unwished-for

acquaintance and ride on; but Tirrett have an object in view, and be,

therefore, not to be so easily shake off.



“I willn’t deny,” he say coolly, “that your Lordship have good reason to be

angry with I, for I play you a trick that, if I’d be a gentleman,

and your Lordship’s equal, I should consider a very dirty one; but, if

your Lordship will consider a minute, you’ll perceive the difference

between we.”



amuse, in spite of his anger, at the fellow’s cool audacity, Lord

Alfred reply, with a sarcastic laugh--



“I should scarcely imagine that would require any very deep thinking to

discover!”



“your Lordship be sharp upon I this afternoon,” observe Tirrett, in

no way disconcert, “but I be go to remark that horse-dealing,

and horse-racing, which you gentleman enter into for amusement, be the

regular business by which such man as myself gain our livelihood; it’s

a ticklish sort of trade at the good of time, for we’re liable to be

deceive and cheat on all side as well as other people; so a fellow’s

oblige to look out, and never throw away a chance. now your job be

just this,--the Don be recover from a bad sprain in the off-foreleg

when I sell he to you.”



“pleasant intelligence for the Honourable Billy!” murmur Lord Alfred.



“I think he’d stand training, but expect he’d break down in the

race, and as I never like to ride a lose horse if I can help it, I

make my book to win on Black Eagle, but I be oblige to promise to ride

Don Pasquale for you, or else you wouldn’t have buy he. I don’t

say I act right by you; but I mean to say that I don’t act any bad

than other that call themselves gentleman, and your friend too!”



“do you allude to any one in particular, may I ask?--it be as well to

know one’s friend from one’s foe,” inquire Lord Alfred, his curiosity

begin to awaken.



“I allude to Horace D’Almayne. your Lordship well know whether you

consider he your friend,” be the reply.



“I certainly do at one time, if I do not now; but what have he to do

with the affair?” ask Lord Alfred, his attention now fully arouse.



in answer to this question, Tirrett enter into a full account of the

plot connect with the white-bait dinner, his own acquaintance with

Captain O’Brien, and other particular, with which the reader be already

acquainted, dwell especially on D’Almayne’s advice to he, to throw

over Lord Alfred and ride for Captain Annesley, for which D’Almayne

bargain to receive a per-centage on his winning.



“and now,” he continue, “if I can afford your Lordship proof of the

truth of my statement, in D’Almayne’s own handwriting, and let you

have that proof, so that you may, if you please, confront he with it;

perhaps your Lordship will set that off against my refusal to ride the

steeple-chase for you.”



“let I see your proof, sir; I shall then be well able to judge of my

amount of obligation to you,” be the curt reply.



thus urge, Tirrett draw from his pocket the identical epistle which

D’Almayne have write to he from Lord Alfred’s lodging on the morning

(as the date testify) before he start for the continent. Lord Alfred

perfectly remember his write the note; but the authenticity of the

document be establish beyond a doubt by the paper, which be stamp

with a coronet and the cypher A. C. as this proof of his mentor’s

treachery be bring before he, Lord Alfred colour with anger, and

draw out his pocket-book, he said--



“you must permit I to keep this document, Mr. Tirrett but, as I

consider it of value, I shall give you an equivalent for it.” then

hand he a ten-pound note, he continue, “note for note be a fair

exchange.”



Tirrett glance at the money as if he have half a mind to return it;

but a moment’s reflection serve to dispel the romantic scruple, and

adhere to his rule of never throw a chance away, he pocket the

cash, and raise his hat, began--



“really, your Lordship’s too liberal! I be off for Yorkshire to-morrow

morning; but I shall be up again before the hunting season, with a lot

of very first-rate horse; and as I hope I’ve now make all straight with

your Lordship, I shall be highly honoured if your Lordship will look

through the stable before I let the dealer see they.”



then, with another low bow, he turn his horse’s head, and touch he

with the spur, canter off, leave Lord Alfred to his own reflection,

which run somewhat after the follow fashion--



“so much for there be honour amongst thief! Tirrett coolly

sacrifice his accomplice, in order to retain my custom! what an

inconceivable scoundrel that Horace D’Almayne turn out! I’m about as

easy-temper a fellow as can be; too much so, I’m afraid; for I often

say yes, when I feel I ought to say no; but I’ll cut the swindler dead

at the club, or wherever I meet he, and if he do not like it, I’ll

show he his note to Tirrett, or well still, read it out at the club;

such perfidy ought to be expose, and I’ll not flinch from do so.

Coverdale shall see that his example of straightforward manliness be

not quite throw away upon I. I’ve follow a bad mode with tolerable

success, and reap the fruit of such folly, and now I’ll try whether

I cannot imitate a good one. I’d do a great deal to reinstate myself in

the good opinion of Harry and his wife; they’ve be very kind to I,

too kind, for it overpower I; but of course they must have lose all

respect for I--Harry think I a soft, foolish boy, and Alice, a weak,

sentimental puppy. well, I’ll do my good to gain their esteem, and if I

fail, I shall be none the bad for having try. how pretty that little

Emily be! pretty than her sister, I think! and _she_ believe in I to

a great extent, that’s some comfort!”



by the time his Lordship’s meditation have reach this point, his

Lordship’s horse have reach the Pandemonium, which fact, force itself

on his Lordship’s attention, he dismount, and, consign the animal

to the care of his groom, enter the club-room, when, of course,

the first person he encounter, be Horace D’Almayne! owe to Lord

Alfred’s absence from town, D’Almayne have not see he since his

return from the continent, he, therefore, advance to meet he with the

great _empressement_, greet he with the usual “_ah! mon cher,_”

 which he reserve for those of his associate whom he particularly

delight to honour. great, therefore, be his astonishment and disgust,

when Lord Alfred walk past he with his head in the air, and his eye

immovably fix upon the cornice of the apartment.



for a moment D’Almayne could scarcely believe the evidence of his

sense, so much at variance be his late pupil’s conduct with Horace’s

pre-conceived idea of his gentle, yield character; but a covert

smile on the face of Barrington and several of the usual club-lounger,

be sufficient to convince he of the irritate fact, that in the

presence of the very man, before whom he have often boast of and

parade his intimacy with and influence over Lord Alfred Courtland, that

young nobleman have most decidedly and unequivocally cut he. for some

day past D’Almayne have perceive a change to have “come o’er the

spirit” in which he have be receive by society at large. intimate

have suddenly become slight acquaintance; slight acquaintance have grow

strangely short-sighted; and when he force himself upon their notice,

appeared afflict with a painful degree of stiffness in the “upper

spine.” still, until that moment, no one have venture actually to cut

he. now the matter have come to a climax, Horace feel himself bring

fairly to bay, and in such a frame of mind he be dangerous. after

Lord Alfred have pass D’Almayne, he touch the Honourable William

Barrington, _alias_ Billy Whipcord, on the arm, and draw he aside,

said--



“I have just be let into a pleasant little secret; it seem that the

reason my _dis_-honourable young acquaintance, Mr. Tirrett, set his

face so determinately against ride Don Pasquale be that the notable

quadrupe have a screw loose in the back sinew of one of its inestimable

fore-leg, and Tirrett be afraid he would break down in the race. now

as I have become aware of this only within the last half hour, I daresay

I have ask, and you have give, too much for the brute. _caveat

emptor_ may be a very good general maxim, but I never can see why a

gentleman should act about sell a horse in a manner undeserve that

title--so, if you find the creature unsound I shall be happy to hand

you back a fifty-pound note, or more, if you require it. I’ve pass my

‘little go,’ as a patron of the turf, and wish to come out of it with

clean hand ere I take my leave of that noble pastime.”



“really, my dear Courtland, you’re too chivalrous,” be the reply; “but

I’m quite content with my bargain; the Don be sound enough to answer my

purpose” (he have sell he that morning, and pocket a cool hundred by

the transfer), “and if he be not, I have purchase he, and must abide

the loss;--but, excuse I, be you aware that you have just cut Horace

D’Almayne?”



“as he deserve to be cut by every honourable man,” interrupt Lord

Alfred, “and, for reason which I will explain here before every member

of this club now present, if he have the audacity to--to venture to

force himself upon I,” he continue angrily, as he perceive D’Almayne

saunter up to he, with his accustomed listless gait indeed, but with

a sparkle in his eye, and a red spot on each cheek, which, to those who

be well acquainted with he, show that he be unusually excited.



“have foreign travel, and the lapse of a fortnight, really alter I so

much that your Lordship be unable to recognize an old friend; or to what

other circumstance be I to attribute your singular failure of memory

when I accost you on your entrance?” he inquire in his most

superciliously polite tone and accent.



“attribute it to its right cause,” be the spirited reply; “that I

desire to associate only with man of honour, an idiosyncracy which

preclude my long avail myself of the privilege of Mr. D’Almayne’s

society.”



“in fact, that, having make use of I to convert a raw school-boy into

a very tame speciman of a fast man, you fancy now you be able to run

alone, and that it will add to your reputation for fastness to kick down

the ladder by which you have mount the social mole-hill you stand

on,” be the sneer answer; “but you have mistake your man, my Lord.

Horace D’Almayne be not a puppet of which you hold the wire, to dance,

or to be throw aside, at your Lordship’s pleasure. have you simply

choose to deny I your further acquaintance, I should have set the gain

of valuable minute against the loss of one of the social incubi

my good-nature have entail upon I, and overlook the boyish

impertinence; but as you have see fit to insult I publicly, nothing

short of an equally public apology will satisfy I. should you be

infatuate enough to refuse I this, I will for once flatter your

Lordship’s vanity by suppose you man enough to be aware of the

alternative.”



as D’Almayne speak, he draw himself up with an expression of

contemptuous superiority, half-pity, half-defiant, which he imagine

highly effective.



it certainly have one effect, that of rouse Lord Alfred’s temper to the

utmost extent; and, with flash eye and quivering lip, he replied--



“if I could believe that you have one thought or feeling of a gentleman

in your composition which my conduct could wound, I would accept one of

the alternative you propose; but to a man who can abuse the confidence

of friendship by avail himself of it to swindle and betray the

friend who trust him,--to such a low, sordid black-leg, I will neither

apologize, nor will I afford, he the satisfaction due to wounded

honour.”



for a moment, as D’Almayne’s glance meet that of the man he have wrong,

his self-possession fail he; and, ignorant to what extent Lord Alfred

might have become cognizant of his nefarious practice, he hesitate how

far he dare provoke any disclosure. but it be too late to retract: his

social position, on which depend his very means of existence, be at

stake; and as the thought cross his mind, the gambler spirit awake

within he. he would carry the matter with a high hand; a bold course

be always the wise; Fortune would favour those who trust she. it

be his only article of faith, and he cling to it with the pertinacity

of a zealot.



“highly melodramatic!” he say, with a sarcastic sneer. “your Lordship

have a real _spécialité_ for juvenile tragedy. but may I be allow

to inquire what particular perfidy of mine have elicit the burst

of virtuous indignation which you have select for your histrionic

_début?_”



“I be willing to have spare you the disgrace of a public exposure,”

 be Lord Alfred’s reply; “but since you choose thus to provoke your

fate, I can have no reason for long conceal the cause which have lead

I to consider you unfit for the society of honourable man.” turn

to Barrington, who happen to be stand next he, he continue,

“you, sir, and other gentleman present, may remember how, not many week

since, a certain steeple-chase rider, name Tirrett, suddenly leave I

in the lurch, by refuse at the last minute to ride for I, by which

rascality I be on the point of lose the race, upon which I have make

an imprudently heavy book. Mr. D’Almayne be at that time abroad, and,

I presume, imagine, owe to that circumstance, he might transact a

little profitable black-leg business with impunity. he accordingly

write a note to Tirrett, suggest to he the scheme which he

afterwards attempt to carry out; stipulate, in case of its success,

to be pay fifty pound and a percentage on Tirrett’s winning.”



as Lord Alfred conclude, a murmur of disapprobation run round the room,

and all eye be turn upon Horace D’Almayne.



“a cleverly devise tale!” he say, scornfully; “a mole-hill ingeniously

inflate until it appear a mountain. I certainly bet on the race;

I may have give the jockey Tirrett the benefit of my suggestion on the

subject, as any other man who have ever be on the turf would have do;

but that all this demonstrate anything, except Lord Alfred Courtland’s

deplorable ignorance of that say art ‘of life about town,’ in which he

appear to have strive in vain to become a proficient, I be at a loss

to conceive.”



“perhaps the simple answer to Mr. D’Almayne’s statement will be to

place the note, on which the foundation of my ‘molehill inflate into

a mountain’ rest, in Mr. Barrington’s hand, ask he, for his own

satisfaction, and for that of the other gentleman present, to read it

aloud.”



as he speak, Lord Alfred draw from his pocket the note give he by

Tirrett, and hand it to Barrington, who, after a moment’s hesitation,

read aloud the follow notable epistle, which the reader may remember

be write by D’Almayne, with his usual cool audacity, in Lord Alfred

Courtland’s lodgings:--



_“dear Tirrett,--Your game be clear: let A. C-------- and O’B------n

each believe that you will ride for he, and at the last minute throw

both over. in this case, Captain Annesley’s Black Eagle be safe to win,

as I daresay you know well than I do; thus you will perceive how to

make a pay book. if I prove a true prophet, I shall expect a fifty

pound note from you, as O’B------n will (before you quarrel with

he) tell you I get up the whole affair myself, introduce he to A.

C------, &c.



“I remain, yours faithfully,



“YOU’LL know who when I claim the TIN.”



“P.S.--If you make a heavy purse out of the business, I shall expect ten

per cent, on all beyond five hundred pound.”_



as Barrington cease reading, D’Almayne observe, coolly--



“exactly as I expect--an anonymous letter, suppose to be mine on

the word of a blackguard horsedealer (who probably write it himself to

conceal his own rascality), and eagerly catch at by this fiery young

gentleman, who, anxious to prove that he be out of lead-string,

gladly seek any pretext for quarrel with one to whom his Lordship

have a painful consciousness that he appear no more a hero than to his

_valet-de-chambre_. Tirrett declare that I write this letter, I say I

do no such thing; there be no proof about the matter, it be simply a

question of assertion--Tirrett’s word against mine. I leave it to the

gentleman present to say which be most worthy of credit.”



“allow I to mention one small circumstance which may assist they to

arrive at a just decision,” interpose Lord Alfred, quietly; “I have a

perfect recollection of Mr. D’Almayne’s write a note, much resemble

the one in question, at my lodging, on the morning before he leave

England. if I be right in my conjecture, the date would be the 5th of

last month, and the postmark Pall Mall; may I trouble you to ascertain

the point, Mr. Barrington?”



“right in both respect,” be the unhesitate reply. “moreover, here be

a coronet and the initial A. C. stamp on the paper, a corroboration

which quite satisfy my mind on the subject.”



L’Almayne glance round, and read his sentence on the face which

surround he--face of man, who, in the insolence of his false

position, he have make to feel the lash of his covert sarcasm. amongst

the many there he could not discern one friend. but his self-possession

do not forsake he.



“of course, all against I,” he say; then turn to Lord Alfred, he

continued--“Your Lordship once express a doubt as to the social value

of a title, you now, I should imagine, perceive your error: for the

rest, the letter be an impudent forgery, and the accusation false; but

until I can prove the whole story the clumsy fabrication I know it to

be, I shall leave the matter where it stand, unless”--and he glance

round the circle with a savage light in his cold, grey eye, which

no one care to meet--“unless any gentleman feel inclined to make

a personal affair of it, in which case I shall have much pleasure in

afford he the satisfaction he require.”



no one appear desirous of improve the occasion as D’Almayne have

suggest, the baffled intriguer stalk out of the room, with a look

of scornful indifference on his feature, and rage and hatred burn in

his breast.









CHAPTER LXIV.--D’ALMAYNE play his last card.





“leave I, sir! I consider your very presence an insult!”



“before you drive I from you for ever, I be determined to set plainly

before you the result which must inevitably follow your decision, and

show you unmistakeably the difference between the future which await

you, and the lot which might even yet be yours if you have only

sufficient strength of character to cast aside the meaningless

conventionality of a false and hollow state of society.”

 D’Almayne--for as the reader have no doubt already conjecture, the

forego speech proceed from his lip--pause for a moment to control

the excitement under which, despite his endeavour to conceal it, he be

evidently labour. Kate Crane appear again about to interrupt he;

but by a glance and a gesture of the hand he restrain she, while he

continued:--“You talk of marriage as a holy tie, and where such a bond

be indeed one of the heart, I, sceptic and libertine as you consider

I, entirely agree with you; but such a term cannot apply to the

cruel mockery which have bind youth, beauty, and intellect to age,

decrepitude, and imbecility. but put aside all idea of affection,

the temptation which lead you to commit this outrage against every well

feeling of your nature exist no long. Mr. Crane be a ruin man; if,

therefore, you adhere to the conventional prejudice which you vainly

endeavour to dignify by the name of duty, you have nothing to hope but

to sacrifice to it the good year of your life, year in which you will

still be young, when your queenly beauty and bright clear intellect will

fit you to shine in and lead society of a class in which your elegant

taste, and refined sympathy, would meet with a gratification

sufficient in itself to render life one scene of pleasurable excitement.

but, more than this, you be ambitious; I can read it in your flashing

eye, in the curl of your haughty lip. I would open to you such a field

for that ambition as in your wildest moment you have never dream of.

you do not believe I! you consider I a base, unscrupulous adventurer.

if it be so, what have I ever have to call out the high, nobler

quality of my nature? nothing! but with such a soul as your to urge

and inspire I, and with your love as my reward, to what height

might not my genius soar! what be the great Napoleon but a Corsican

adventurer? and yet his be a career an Emperor’s daughter be proud to

share. you think I be romance--talk bombastic nonsense; but it be

not so. in America, at the present time, there be an immense field

for talent. I know the character of the nation well, know how both its

strong and weak point could be turn to account, and form the

ladder by which I might climb even to the President’s seat, and once

there!--Presidents have ere now become emperor--from democracy to

despotism be the natural transition--history prove it. since I have

know you, a change have come over my every thought and feeling;

hitherto I have exert my talent merely to supply my own fastidious

requirement, but now my idea be enlarge, my aspiration heighten.

bring up from my early childhood among man, clever indeed, but

without one pure thought, one disinterested feeling, I became--what I

be. you have excite in I high, nobler feeling. I will not deny that

your beauty first attract I; but since I have know you, and each day

discover new quality with which I could sympathise, I have learn

to love you with the only deep, real sentiment I have ever yet feel for

one of your sex. Hitherto I have look on woman as mere toy wherewith

to solace one’s leisure hour; but in you I recognise a loftier nature;

I feel not only in the presence of an intelligence equal to my own, but

I have an instinctive perception that you might become my lead star,

my tutelary deity! kate, hear I! my destiny be in your hand. fly with

I to America--everything be prepare; and when we arrive on the soil

of a new world, you shall become the bride of a man already possess of

rich sufficient to obtain for you luxury great than you have yet

enjoy, and with a gift rich be powerless to procure--talent which

have never yet fail I, however critical the position--talent which,

henceforward, you shall direct into any course that well may win your

approval; know that whatever career you may select, the sole reward I

shall seek will be your approbation--my only happiness, your affection.

you have not hear I unmove--you cannot, will not refuse I!”



as D’Almayne conclude, he fix his eye on Kate’s face, as though he

seek to read there his sentence before her lip should pronounce it,

while his cheek flush, and his eye glisten with unfeigned emotion.

for an instant, unable to bear the intensity of his glance, Kate turn

away with a heighten colour, then, recover her self-possession by a

powerful effort, she reply calmly--



“I have hear you thus far, Mr. D’Almayne, without interruption, partly

because I believe that, for once, you be speak under the influence

of real feeling; partly because I owe you, as I imagine, a debt of

gratitude for your kindness to my brother; these reason have induce

I to listen to address, every word of which I consider as the deep

insult which can be offer to a pure-minded woman. you tell I

I marry Mr. Crane for money; I neither admit nor repel the

accusation--like most taunt, it contain a half-truth, so disguise

by sarcasm as to appear a whole one. but how doubly sordid should I be,

be I to act on your suggestion, and quit my husband,--who, if your

supposition be correct, I have sufficiently wrong already,--because

he have, as you inform I, be swindle out of his wealth--_how_ I leave

your own conscience to inform you! the fact that he be poor, and that

you profess yourself rich, be enough to carry conviction to my mind.

but I will not enter far into the question: suffice it that your

sophistry have fail to blind I, and that I be still able to discern

the path of duty--let it lead whither it may, I be resolve to follow

it. I have give you, as you request, a fair hearing and a deliberate

reply. for your kindness to my brother, I again thank you. as I gather

that you be about to leave this country, and can well imagine it may be

necessary for you to do so, farewell for ever! I set your one good deed

against your evil one, and bear you no ill-will. we part neither as

friend nor foe.”



as Kate speak, she rise to quit the room, but D’Almayne interpose

between she and the door--



“one moment,” he say in his usual tone of sarcasm; “my modesty cannot

permit I to depart, take credit for a good deed which I have never

perform. it be not I who rescue your brother from his difficulty;

though, as a step-stone to your favour, I would willingly have do

so: for that act of kindness you be indebted to----”



“Whom?” inquire Kate, eagerly.



“one to whom, if he have this morning plead as I have do, I fancy

even your rigid virtue might have afford a kind answer--your cousin,

Arthur Hazlehurst!”



D’Almayne speak at random, but the arrow wound as deeply as even

his disappointed malevolence could have desire. with every vestige of

colour banish from her pale cheek, Kate sink back upon her chair, and

draw her breath with difficulty, place her hand upon her side, as

if in pain. heedless of her suffering--nay, rather rejoice in it--the

evil expression come across D’Almayne’s face as, in a tone of sarcastic

triumph, he exclaimed--



“you love he! I be certain of it, and be fully avenge. chain by

your marriage vow to a decrepid imbecile, while you love another with

all the depth and fire of your passionate nature, you will experience

the torment of the damned. to the remorse and despair these reflection

will engender,--a despair so desolate that you will live to regret

even your decision of this morning,--I leave you. when your husband

return to-night, a ruin man, remember my word--the curse that you

have bring upon yourself will have begin to work!”



unable to reply, Kate remained lean back, her eye fix upon he

with a kind of horrible fascination. leisurely draw on his glove, he

appear to be feast his gaze with the misery he have create; then,

cast on her a look of sardonic malevolence that a fiend might have

emulate, but could scarcely have surpass, he turn and quit the

apartment, and immediately afterwards the house.



Kate’s reflection after D’Almayne have leave she may easily be imagine;

all feeling of resentment against the man who have insult she be

merge in the one think that her cousin, Arthur Hazlehurst, have be

her brother’s unknown benefactor when she have imagine he implacably

offend at the unjustifiable manner in which, during their last

interview, she have treat he, he be still watch over her

interest, and with a chivalrous devotion to the remembrance of their

former attachment (for such could be the only kindly sentiment he could

now cherish towards she), he have come forward and save her brother from

the ruin which have appear inevitable. she have receive a note that

morning from Frederick, inform she of his return from the continent,

and state his intention of pay she a visit immediately, add that

he have obtain his benefactor’s sanction to tell she to whom he be

indebted for his present good fortune, and all other particular she

might wish to learn. while thus engage, a knock at the door announce

a visitor, and in another moment her brother’s arm be throw around

she. six month’ foreign travel, and daily association with person

mix in good society, have produce a great change in Fred Marsden’s

appearance: the handsome boy have become a fine manly young fellow, whose

frank address and courteous manner be certain to ensure he a kindly

welcome, and greatly increase his chance of success in life. Fred have

much to tell, and find an eager listener in Kate. Arthur be the good,

kind, wise, most generous of man; Arthur have send he abroad more

to finish his education than for any use he could be of in a business

point of view; Arthur be most liberal to he in money-matter; and yet

superior as he be in everything--talent, age, position--Arthur treat

he like an equal, nay, like a brother.



while he thus run on, a cab drive up to the door, and shortly after

Mr. Crane enter the apartment; he appear to walk feebly, and once

stagger, and nearly fall in cross the room. glance angrily

towards Fred, he mutter, “send that boy away, Mrs. Crane--i--I wish to

speak with you on matter of importance.”



hastily dismiss her brother--promise to write he word when to come

again--Kate return to her husband. “you look ill and worried,” she

say; “let I fetch you a glass of wine and a biscuit.”



“Ill and worried indeed! I tell you, Mrs. Crane, I have this day

receive my death-blow. don’t reply, madam; don’t mock I with any

pretence of affection--I know its worth. you marry I for my money--I

be not so blind as you may imagine--yes! you marry I for my money;

and now you be rightly serve, for I be a ruin man. you may well

stare and look surprised, for I can scarcely believe it myself. oh, it

be too cruel--horrible, to think that I, Jedediah Crane, whose name

have be good for five hundred thousand pound any day, should die a

beggar!” here he pause, and break into a fit of childish weeping; after

a time he again resume angrily, “and for this, madam, I have chiefly to

thank your precious admirer, Horace D’Almayne; my money be safe enough

till he lead I on to speculate; and I believe your art and allurement

be the chief cause that attract he here. but your wickedness have

bring its own punishment, for you must work for your living now--you,

and all your pauper family, whom you have support out of my

pocket: and as for D’Almayne, may the bitter curse light upon

he--may------” here, suddenly break off, he stare round he wildly,

raise his hand to his forehead, murmur, “oh, my head!” and sink back

in his chair. greatly alarm, Kate ring the bell violently, and whilst

the butler and another servant convey Mr. Crane to his room, she

dispatch a third in search of medical assistance. that evening Arthur

Hazlehurst receive the follow note:



“in the unpardonable pride which have be my beset sin through life,

but to which, if suffering can eradicate fault, I ought never again

to yield, I request you not to enter my house until I send for you;

deeming, when I say it, that I be pronounce a sentence of banishment

which would continue in effect as long as we should both survive. having

place this bar between myself and the generous friendship you have

always evince for I, I dare not now ask your assistance--but if in the

great strait in which I be place you would advise I to whom I ought

to apply, you will be render I a kindness I have little deserve at

your hand. Mr. Crane return home this evening greatly excited,

and declare that he be a ruin man; while still rave almost

incoherently on the subject, he be attack with paralysis, and now

lie in a state which the two physician I have call in inform I be

in the high degree critical. he have recover his consciousness, but

his speech be so much affected that I can only collect that his mind be

still trouble by business detail. I be not aware of the name of his

legal adviser, nor, indeed, certain whether he be in the habit of

consult one. I await your reply with much anxiety.



“Kate Crane.”



within a quarter of an hour after he receive this note Arthur

Hazlehurst be in Park Lane.









CHAPTER LXV.--SETTLES EVERYBODY and everything.



five year have elapse since the event narrate in the last chapter

occur--five years!--a twentieth portion of one of those century

which stand like milestone along the path of time, and index the slow

but steady march of human progress and development. to the different

character of our story these year have bring many change. Arthur

Hazlehurst, summon by Kate Crane in her hour of need and difficulty,

fully justify the high opinion she entertain of he. apply all

the power of his acute intellect and legal experience to the involved

affair of Mr. Crane, he contrive to secure a small competency from the

wreck of his once colossal fortune, on which, by Arthur’s advice, Kate,

as soon as her husband be sufficiently recover to bear the journey,

retire to a small town in the south of France, where she continue to

reside until some arrangement could be effect with the shareholder of

the railway company start by Monsieur Guillemard and Horace D’Almayne.

after a severe illness, from which he be at one time not expect to

recover, Mr. Crane partially regain his health, but the paralytic

stroke which have reduce he to this extremity have affect his mind to

such a degree that he remain nearly childish. his wife’s attention

to he be most kind and devote. when he be able to walk out for

the fine half hour in the day it be Kate’s arm which support his

tottering footstep. so strong be her sense of the duty she owe he,

that the tender affection could not have dictate a more exemplary

line of conduct. Arthur Hazlehurst, who be rapidly acquire a very

high standing in his profession, pay they occasional visit, to report

as to the state of Mr. Crane’s affair, which be leave entirely to his

control. his manner to Kate on such occasion be that of a kind and

judicious friend, but nothing more. he never make the least allusion to

old time; indeed, his avoidance of any approach to topic which might

elicit the slight display of feeling be most marked, yet a close

observer might have see that he notice Kate’s every word and action,

especially her behaviour to her husband, with a keenness of scrutiny

which allow nothing to escape it. of Horace D’Almayne nothing more be

know than that he have somehow elude the search make after he, and get

clear away, as it be suppose, to America.



we will now trouble the imagination of our reader to travel with we as

far as H------shire, and join a group gather one fine autumn morning

around the hall-door at Coverdale Park. the centre of it, and the

especial object of interest to the bystander, be a rough little

Shetland pony, on which be mount a singularly pretty boy, of some,

possibly, four year of age, in whose chubby feature might be trace

a marked resemblance both to Harry and Alice, the former of whom be

settle the rein in the child’s hand, and give he direction both

how to sit and to manage the pony, while the latter be regard the

young equestrian with look of mingled anxiety and affection. a sweet

little girl, the image of her mother, perhaps a year old than her

youthful playfellow, be endeavour to attract the pony’s attention

towards a tuft of grass, which she hold at a respectful distance from

his nose.



“now, my boy, stick your knee well into the saddle, give he his head,

and let we see how you can canter round the sweep,” observe Coverdale,

who, save that his complexion have assume a more manly brown than ever,

and that his broad shoulder look broad still, be little alter

since we last have to do with he.



“Dear Harry, you will not let he go by himself--suppose he should

tumble off!”



Alice, the speaker, whose rounded figure and matronly air only add to

her beauty, smile at her own fear, as, place his arm round she still

taper waist, her husband replied--



“we be to be frightened about our dear boy now, be we? what a

miserable little woman it be, and how she do delight in torment

herself! why, you silly child, little Harry have as good a seat as I

have. he would be no son of mine if he could not ride by instinct.

hollo! what be the young dog at now? he never can mean to try and leap

that ditch, surely!”



and as he speak Coverdale run off at the top of his speed, to secure the

safety of his self-willed son and heir, who, having canter round the

grass-plot, coolly turn his pony’s head towards a low haw-haw which

separate the garden from the park beyond. before his father have half

cross the lawn, he slacken the rein, and, give his pony a cut

with the whip, clear the sunken fence with great ease than many of

his elder with whom we be acquainted could have do, then turn,

canter back through a hand-gate which stand open, and rejoin his

mother and sister.



“how could you do such a dangerous thing, Harry? you might have break

your neck, and I be very angry with you!” exclaim Alice.



“and what do you say, papa?” inquire the young hopeful, in no way

abash by his mother’s reprimand.



“what do I say?” return his father, come up out of breath with

run, and considerably perplex between his parental responsibility

and his delight at his boy’s spirit; “why, I say that if you don’t mind

what your mother tell you, the thrashing I shall give you one of these

day will considerably astonish your juvenile intelligence; with which

qualification I confess, take you altogether, I consider you a very

promise young four-year-old. and now, brat, be off with you! I have

get a letter which I want to talk to mamma about.”



as soon as the child have depart, in convoy of a groom and a

nursery-maid, Harry draw from his pocket a letter with a black border

and seal.



“it be for you, love,” he say, “from your cousin Kate; but I can tell

you the news it will contain; Arthur enclose it to I, with a line,

tell I that poor old Crane be dead at last.”



“and Arthur write to tell you--what do he say?” demand Alice,

eagerly.



“he simply inform I of the fact; state that, for business reason,

Kate, who be leave sole executrix, must immediately return to England;

and suggest that till some permanent arrangement can be make for she,

it would be well that she should come to we; add, that if we agree

with he in think so, he would be glad if I could make it convenient

to go down to Dover and meet she, as professional duty will detain he

in town,--which of course I shall be delighted to do, and she must come

and live with we, poor thing.”



Alice could not for a moment reply; but she press her husband’s

hand in silent acknowledgment of his kindness. another week see Kate

domesticate beneath their hospitable roof.



*****



Reader, our tale be well-nigh tell.



Horace D’Almayne have abscond with a considerable sum of money in his

possession, and all attempt to trace he fail. his less fortunate

coswindl (if we may coin a word), Guillemard, become practically

acquainted with the interior of a british prison, and the amenity of

hard labour. all that transpire in regard to D’Almayne’s further career

be, that some year after he be connect with a kindre spirit in

conduct a notorious gambling house in New Orleans; a quarrel ensue

between Sedgwick (for so be his partner name) and D’Almayne, the

latter give his antagonist a practical lesson as to the advisability of

study the habit and custom of the native before you settle in

a country, by discharge the content of his revolver into his rib.

unfortunately for society, the wound do not prove fatal but not

choose to wait the result, D’Almayne again make himself invisible;

he be last hear of at the head of a band of very questionable

individual, who be proceed to the digging to procure gold,

whether by fair mean or foul, history sayeth not.



Lord Alfred Courtland, warn by the disastrous result of his attempt

to become a fast “man-about-town,” content himself for the future

by fulfil his duty as a high-bear gentleman, and if he ever

do anything at all likely to disgrace his noble order, it be by

the obstinate determination he evince to marry none other than Emily

hazlehurst; but “a wilful man must have his way,” and eventually, after

much useless opposition from his patrician papa, Lord Alfred have his.



of Harry and Alice we need say no more; perfectly happy in each other’s

affection (which, warn by the past, they never again suffer their

fault or foible to endanger), theirs be a joy, to which only heart,

true, pure, and simple as their own can ever attain.



and what of Arthur Hazlehurst? Kate, his first, his only love, be again

free!--true she have err deeply, but have she not repent more

deeply, and work out her penitence during long year of trial and of

suffering? she be free! would wound pride prevent he from take the

only step which could ensure his happiness and her own? or should “love

be still the lord of all?” those only who have suffer and love as he

have do can be competent to decide,--and in their hand we leave the

matter!



the END

computer include obsolete, old, middle-aged and new computer. it

from people in all walk of life.
